Leviathan

or, The Matter, Form, and Power of a Commonwealth, Ecclesiastical and Civil

by Thomas Hobbes

A Review and Conclusion

The Epistle Dedicatory

To my most honored friend, Mr. Francis Godolphin of Godolphin.

Honored Sir,

Your most worthy brother, Mr. Sidney Godolphin, while he lived, was kind enough to think my studies worth something — and, as you know, to back that good opinion with real acts of generosity, great in themselves and greater still for the worth of the man who gave them. There is no virtue that fits a man for the service of God or the service of his country, for civil society or for private friendship, that did not show plainly in everything he did — not as something acquired out of necessity, or put on for the occasion, but inborn, shining out of a generous nature. So in honor and gratitude to him, and with devotion to you, I humbly dedicate to you this discourse of mine on the commonwealth.

I don't know how the world will receive it, or how it may reflect on those who seem to favor it. The road I am walking is lined on one side by people fighting for too much liberty, and on the other by people fighting for too much authority, and it is hard to pass between those two points unwounded. Still, it seems to me that an effort to strengthen the civil power should not be condemned by the civil power itself; nor should private men, by attacking that effort, announce that they think such power is already too great.

Besides, I am not speaking of men, but in the abstract, of the seat of power — like those simple and impartial creatures in the Roman Capitol that defended the people inside with their noise, not because of who those people were, but because that is where they were. So I offend no one, I think, except those outside the walls, or anyone inside (if there is any such person) who favors them.

What may give the most offense are certain texts of Holy Scripture that I cite for purposes other than the ones they are ordinarily put to by others. But I have done this with due submission, and also — given my subject — out of necessity. For those texts are the outworks of the enemy: the forward fortifications from which they attack the civil power.

If, despite all this, you find my work generally condemned, you may excuse yourself by saying that I am a man who loves his own opinions and thinks everything he says is true; that I honored your brother, and honor you; and that on the strength of that, I have presumed — without your knowledge — to take the title of being, as I truly am,

Sir,

Your most humble and most obedient servant, Thomas Hobbes.

Paris, April 15/25, 1651.

Introduction

Nature — the art by which God has made and governs the world — can be imitated by the art of man, here as in so many other things, even to the point that man can make an artificial animal. After all, life is just a motion of limbs, set going from some central part inside. So why shouldn't we say that all automata — machines that move themselves by springs and wheels, the way a watch does — have an artificial life? For what is the heart, but a spring? What are the nerves, but so many strings? What are the joints, but so many wheels, giving motion to the whole body, just as the maker intended?

But art goes further still, imitating that rational and most excellent work of nature: man himself. For by art is created that great LEVIATHAN called a COMMONWEALTH, or STATE (in Latin, civitas), which is nothing other than an artificial man — though one of greater stature and strength than the natural man, for whose protection and defense it was built.

In this artificial man, sovereignty — the supreme power, whether held by a king or an assembly — is an artificial soul, giving life and motion to the whole body. The magistrates and other officers of justice and law enforcement are artificial joints. Reward and punishment, by which every joint and member is fastened to the seat of sovereignty and moved to do its duty, are the nerves, doing the same job they do in a natural body. The wealth and riches of all the individual members are its strength. Salus populi — the people's safety — is its business. Counselors, who supply it with everything it needs to know, are its memory. Equity and laws are an artificial reason and will. Concord is its health; sedition is its sickness; and civil war is its death. And lastly, the pacts and covenants — the binding agreements — by which the parts of this body politic were first made, fitted together, and united, resemble that Fiat, that "Let us make man," which God pronounced at the Creation.

To describe the nature of this artificial man, I will consider:

First, the matter it is made of, and its maker — both of which are man.

Secondly, how it is made, and by what covenants; what the rights and just power or authority of a sovereign are; and what preserves a commonwealth and what dissolves it.

Thirdly, what a Christian commonwealth is.

Lastly, what the Kingdom of Darkness is.

Concerning the first point, there is a saying that has been thrown around a lot lately: that wisdom is acquired not by reading books, but by reading men. Following that idea, certain people — who mostly can give no other proof of being wise — take great delight in showing off what they think they have read in other men, by passing unkind judgments on one another behind each other's backs.

But there is another saying, not well understood these days, by which they might learn to truly read one another, if they were willing to take the trouble. It is Nosce teipsum: Read yourself. This was never meant, as it is used now, to excuse the barbarous behavior of men in power toward their inferiors, or to encourage men of low rank to act insolently toward their betters. It was meant to teach us this: because one man's thoughts and passions are so similar to another's, whoever looks into himself — and considers what he is doing when he thinks, judges, reasons, hopes, fears, and so on, and on what grounds — will thereby read and know what the thoughts and passions of all other men are on similar occasions.

I mean the similarity of the passions themselves, which are the same in all men: desire, fear, hope, and the rest. I do not mean the similarity of the objects of the passions — the particular things desired, feared, hoped for, and so on. Those vary with each person's individual makeup and particular upbringing, and they are so easily hidden from us that the characters written on a man's heart — blotted and scrambled as they are by deceit, lying, faking, and false doctrines — are legible only to the One who searches hearts. It is true that men's actions sometimes reveal their designs. But to read those actions without comparing them to our own, and without weighing all the circumstances that might change the case, is to try to decipher a code without the key — and to be deceived most of the time, either by trusting too much or by trusting too little, depending on whether the reader is himself a good man or a bad one.

But even if a man could read another man perfectly by his actions, that would serve him only with his acquaintances, and they are few. Whoever is to govern a whole nation must read in himself not this or that particular man, but mankind. That is hard to do — harder than learning any language or science. Yet once I have set down my own reading in an orderly and clear way, the only work left to anyone else will be to consider whether he does not find the same things in himself. For this kind of doctrine admits no other demonstration.

Part I: Of Man

Chapter 1: Of Sense

I want to examine the thoughts of man — first one at a time, and afterward in trains, where each thought depends on the one before it.

Taken singly, every thought is a representation or appearance of some quality, or some other feature, of a body outside us — what we commonly call an OBJECT. The object works on the eyes, the ears, and the other parts of the body, and by working in different ways, it produces different appearances.

The origin of all of them is what we call SENSE. For there is no conception in a person's mind that was not first, in whole or in part, born in the organs of sense. Everything else derives from that original.

Knowing the natural cause of sense is not strictly necessary for the business now at hand, and I have written about it at length elsewhere. Still, to fill in each part of my present method, I will briefly lay it out here.

The cause of sense is the external body, or object, which presses on the organ proper to each sense — directly, as in taste and touch, or indirectly, as in seeing, hearing, and smelling. That pressure is carried inward, by way of the nerves and the other strings and membranes of the body, until it reaches the brain and the heart. There it causes a resistance, a counter-pressure — an endeavor of the heart to push back, to deliver itself. And because that effort presses outward, it seems to be some matter outside us.

This seeming, this appearance — call it the fancy — is exactly what people mean by sense. To the eye, it consists in a light, or a color with a shape. To the ear, in a sound. To the nostril, in an odor. To the tongue and palate, in a flavor. And to the rest of the body, in heat, cold, hardness, softness, and the other qualities we pick out by feeling.

Now, all these qualities we call "sensible" are, in the object that causes them, nothing but so many different motions of matter — motions by which the object presses on our organs in different ways. And in us, the ones being pressed, they are nothing but different motions either. For motion produces nothing but motion.

But how they appear to us is fancy — and the appearance is the same whether we are awake or dreaming. Press, rub, or strike your eye, and you will fancy you see a light. Press your ear, and it produces a ringing din. The bodies we actually see and hear do the very same thing — they produce these appearances by their own strong, though unobserved, action.

For if those colors and sounds really lived in the bodies, or objects, that cause them, they could never be separated from them. But they can be — we see it done. Mirrors and lenses sever the image from the thing; echoes, by reflection, sever the sound from its source. In those cases we know the thing we see is in one place, and the appearance is in another. And though at a certain distance the real, actual object seems to be clothed in the very fancy it begets in us, still the object is one thing, and the image or fancy is another.

So sense, in every case, is nothing else but original fancy — caused, as I have said, by the pressure, that is, the motion, of external things upon our eyes, ears, and the other organs appointed for it.

But the philosophy schools, throughout all the universities of Christendom, teach a different doctrine, grounded on certain texts of Aristotle. To explain vision, they say that the thing seen sends out, in every direction, a visible species — in plain English, a visible show, apparition, or aspect, a "being seen" — and that the receiving of this into the eye is seeing. To explain hearing, they say the thing heard sends out an audible species — that is, an audible aspect, or an audible "being seen" — which enters at the ear and makes hearing. And they go further: to explain understanding itself, they say the thing understood sends out an intelligible species — an intelligible "being seen" — which comes into the understanding and makes us understand.

I do not say this to disapprove of universities. But because I will have to speak later about their proper role in a commonwealth, I must show you along the way, at every opportunity, what things in them need to be amended. And among those things, the sheer frequency of insignificant speech — words that signify nothing — is one.

Chapter 2: Of Imagination

When a thing is lying still, it will lie still forever unless something else moves it. Nobody doubts that. But here is the twin truth: when a thing is in motion, it will stay in motion forever unless something else stops it. The reasoning is exactly the same — nothing can change itself — yet people find this one much harder to accept.

Why? Because people measure everything, not just other people but all other things, by themselves. After moving around, they feel pain and fatigue, so they assume everything else gets tired of motion too and seeks rest of its own accord. It rarely occurs to them that the desire for rest they feel in themselves might just be some other motion. That is how the Scholastics ended up teaching that heavy bodies fall downward out of an "appetite for rest" — a longing to preserve their nature in the place that suits them best. Which means they were ascribing appetite, and knowledge of what is good for one's own preservation, to lifeless objects. That is more than even human beings have. Absurd.

So: once a body is in motion, it moves forever unless something hinders it. And whatever hinders it cannot kill the motion in an instant, but only over time, by degrees. Watch the water: even after the wind dies down, the waves keep rolling for a long while afterward. The same thing happens with the motion made in the internal parts of a man when he sees, dreams, and so on. After the object is taken away, or the eye is shut, we still retain an image of the thing we saw — dimmer than when we actually saw it, but there.

This is what the Romans called IMAGINATION, from the image made in seeing — and they applied the word, somewhat loosely, to all the other senses as well. The Greeks called it fancy — phantasia — which means "appearance," a word that fits any of the senses equally well. Imagination, then, is nothing but DECAYING SENSE. It is found in human beings, and in many other living creatures, both sleeping and waking.

Memory

The decay of sense in a person who is awake is not the dying-out of the motion made in sensing. It is the obscuring of that motion — the way the light of the sun obscures the light of the stars. The stars give off their light just as much in the daytime as at night; but among the many impressions striking our eyes, ears, and other organs, only the strongest one registers. Since the sun's light dominates, the action of the stars does not affect us.

In the same way, when an object is removed from our sight, the impression it made in us remains. But other, more present objects come crowding in after it and work on us, so the imagination of the past thing grows obscured and weak — like a single voice in the noise of the day. It follows that the more time passes after seeing or sensing something, the weaker the imagination of it becomes. The continual change of the human body eventually destroys the very parts that were set in motion during sensing.

So distance in time and distance in space have one and the same effect on us. At a great distance in space, whatever we look at appears dim, the small details blur, and voices grow faint and indistinct. Just so, after a great distance in time, our imagination of the past is weak: of cities we once visited, we lose many particular streets; of events, many particular details.

This decaying sense goes by two names, depending on what we want to emphasize. When we mean the thing itself — the image, the imagination itself — we call it imagination, as I said before. But when we mean the decay, signaling that the sense is fading, old, and past, we call it MEMORY. So imagination and memory are one and the same thing, given different names for different purposes.

Much memory — memory of many things — is called EXPERIENCE.

Now, imagination is only of things that were previously perceived by sense, whether all at once or in parts at different times. This gives us two kinds. The first, imagining a whole object as it was presented to the senses, is SIMPLE imagination — as when you picture a man, or a horse, that you have seen before. The second is COMPOUND imagination — as when, from seeing a man at one time and a horse at another, we put together in our mind a centaur. The same thing happens when a man combines the image of his own person with the image of another man's deeds — when he imagines himself a Hercules or an Alexander, which happens often to people who read too many romance novels. That is compound imagination, and properly speaking, it is nothing but a fiction of the mind.

There are also other imaginations that arise in people while wide awake, caused by an unusually strong impression on the senses. Stare at the sun, and the impression leaves an image of the sun before your eyes long afterward. Concentrate long and intensely on geometrical figures, and even awake in the dark you will see images of lines and angles before your eyes. This kind of imagination has no particular name, because it is not the sort of thing people usually talk about.

Dreams

The imaginations of those who sleep are what we call DREAMS. And these too, like all other imaginations, were in the senses first — either whole or in pieces. Now, in sleep, the brain and nerves — the necessary organs of sense — are so numbed that external objects cannot easily move them. So in sleep, no imagination can occur, and therefore no dream, except what comes from the agitation of the inner parts of a man's own body. Those inner parts are connected to the brain and the other organs, and when they are disturbed, they keep the brain in motion — and so the imaginations formerly made there appear as if the man were awake.

But here is the difference. Since the organs of sense are numbed, no new object can come in to overpower and obscure those images with a stronger impression. So a dream is necessarily more vivid, in this silence of the senses, than our waking thoughts are. That is why it is so hard — many people think it impossible — to tell sense and dreaming apart with certainty.

For my own part, I am satisfied on this point, and here is my reasoning. In dreams I do not often or consistently think of the same persons, places, objects, and actions that I think of while awake. Nor do I remember as long a chain of coherent thoughts while dreaming as I do at other times. And while awake I often notice how absurd my dreams were, but I never dream about the absurdities of my waking thoughts. So I am confident that when I am awake, I know I am not dreaming — even though when I dream, I think I am awake.

Since dreams are caused by disturbances in some of the body's inner parts, different disturbances must produce different dreams. Lying cold breeds dreams of fear, and raises up the thought and image of some frightening object — for the motion runs both ways, from the brain to the inner parts and from the inner parts to the brain. Likewise, just as anger heats certain parts of the body when we are awake, so when we sleep, the overheating of those same parts causes anger, and raises up in the brain the imagination of an enemy. In the same way, natural affection, when we are awake, causes desire, and desire produces heat in certain other parts of the body — so too much heat in those parts while we sleep raises up in the brain the imagination of affection shown to us. In short, our dreams are our waking imaginations run in reverse: the motion, when we are awake, begins at one end; when we dream, at the other.

Apparitions and Visions

The hardest time to tell a dream from waking thoughts is when, by some accident, we fail to notice that we have slept. This happens easily to a man full of fearful thoughts, with a troubled conscience, who falls asleep without the usual signposts — going to bed, undressing — say, a man who nods off in a chair. Someone who deliberately and carefully settles himself down to sleep will, if some bizarre, extravagant imagination comes to him, have no trouble recognizing it as a dream.

Take the famous case of Marcus Brutus — the man whose life Julius Caesar spared, who was Caesar's favorite, and who murdered him anyway. The story goes that at Philippi, on the night before he gave battle to Augustus Caesar, Brutus saw a terrifying apparition. Historians usually report it as a vision. But consider the circumstances, and you can easily judge it to have been nothing but a short dream. Sitting in his tent, brooding, haunted by the horror of his rash act, slumbering in the cold — it was not hard for him to dream of exactly what frightened him most. And as the fear gradually woke him, the apparition would just as gradually have faded. Since he had no assurance that he had been asleep, he had no reason to think it a dream rather than a vision.

This is not a rare occurrence. Even people who are fully awake — if they are timid and superstitious, their heads full of frightening tales, alone in the dark — are subject to the same imaginations, and believe they see spirits and the ghosts of dead men walking in churchyards. What they see is either their own imagination, or else the trickery of certain persons who exploit such superstitious fear in order to pass through the night in disguise, to places they would rather not be known to visit.

From this ignorance — not knowing how to distinguish dreams and other strong imaginations from vision and real sense — arose the greater part of the religion of the pagans of the past, who worshipped satyrs, fauns, nymphs, and the like. And from the same ignorance comes the opinion that uneducated people hold today about fairies, ghosts, and goblins, and about the power of witches.

As for witches: I do not think their witchcraft is any real power. Yet I think they are justly punished — for the false belief that they can do such harm, joined with the intention to do it if they could. Their trade is closer to a new religion than to any craft or science. As for fairies and walking ghosts, I think the belief in them has been deliberately taught, or at least deliberately left unrefuted, in order to keep up the market for exorcisms, crosses, holy water, and other such inventions of "ghostly men" — the clergy.

Now, there is no doubt that God can produce supernatural apparitions. But it is no article of Christian faith that he does so as often as people fear such things — any more than they fear his stopping or changing the course of nature, which he can also do. Wicked men, under the cover of "God can do anything," are bold enough to say anything when it serves their purposes, even when they know it to be untrue. The wise man's part is to believe them no further than right reason makes what they say appear credible.

If this superstitious fear of spirits were swept away — and with it, fortune-telling from dreams, false prophecies, and the many other things that depend on them, by which crafty, ambitious people prey on the simple — men would be far better fitted than they are for civil obedience.

This ought to be the work of the universities. Instead, they nourish such doctrine. Not knowing what imagination or the senses actually are, they simply teach whatever they received. Some say that imaginations arise by themselves and have no cause. Others, that they arise most commonly from the will, and that good thoughts are blown ("inspired") into a man by God, and evil thoughts by the Devil — or that good thoughts are poured ("infused") into a man by God, and evil ones by the Devil. Some say the senses receive the "species" of things and deliver them to the "common sense," and the common sense hands them over to the fancy, and the fancy to the memory, and the memory to the judgment — like passing parcels from hand to hand, with many words and nothing understood.

Understanding

The imagination that is raised in a man — or in any other creature equipped with the faculty of imagining — by words or other voluntary signs, is what we generally call UNDERSTANDING. It is common to man and beast: a dog will, by habit, understand the call or the scolding of his master, and so will many other animals. But the understanding that is unique to man is the understanding not only of another's will, but of his conceptions and thoughts — grasped through the sequence and arrangement of the names of things into affirmations, negations, and other forms of speech. Of this kind of understanding I will speak later.

Chapter 3: The Train of Thoughts

By a train of thoughts — what I call the "consequence" of one thought from another — I mean the way thoughts follow each other in succession. To distinguish it from discourse in words, I call it MENTAL DISCOURSE: a conversation the mind has with itself.

When a person thinks about anything at all, the next thought is not as random as it seems. Thoughts do not just follow one another in any order whatsoever. Remember: we have no imagination that did not first come to us, in whole or in part, through the senses. In the same way, we never pass from one imagination to another unless the same transition happened before in our senses.

Here is the reason. All imaginations are motions inside us — leftovers of the motions made during sensation. And motions that followed one another during sensation stay linked together afterward. So when the first motion comes back and takes over, the second follows it, the way matter in motion hangs together — the way water on a smooth table is drawn in whichever direction your finger guides any one part of it.

But here is the catch: in sensation, the same perceived thing is sometimes followed by one thing and sometimes by another. So over time it becomes impossible to be certain what we will imagine next. Only one thing is certain: it will be something that followed the same thought before, at one time or another.

The Unguided Train of Thoughts

This train of thoughts, this mental discourse, comes in two kinds.

The first is unguided — without design, without steadiness. In this kind there is no passionate thought to govern and direct the thoughts that follow, no desire steering them toward some goal. The thoughts simply wander, and seem irrelevant to one another, as in a dream. These are typically the thoughts of people who are not only alone but also free of any pressing concern — though even then their thoughts are as busy as ever, just without harmony. It is like the sound an out-of-tune lute would make for anyone who strummed it, or a tuned one in the hands of someone who cannot play.

And yet even in this wild ranging of the mind, you can often trace its path and see how one thought depends on another. Here is an example. In a conversation about our present civil war, what could seem more out of place than the question someone actually asked: what was the value of a Roman penny? Yet to me the connection was perfectly clear. The thought of the war led to the thought of handing the King over to his enemies. That thought brought in the thought of the handing over of Christ. And that brought the thought of the thirty pieces of silver, the price of that betrayal — from which the malicious question followed easily enough. And all of this happened in an instant, for thought is quick.

The Regulated Train of Thoughts

The second kind is steadier, because it is regulated by some desire and design. The impression made by things we desire or fear is strong and lasting — or, if it fades for a while, it comes back quickly. Sometimes it is so strong that it interrupts and breaks our sleep.

From desire arises the thought of some means we have seen produce the thing we are aiming at. From that comes the thought of a means to that means, and so on, until we arrive at some starting point within our own power. And because the goal makes such a strong impression, it keeps coming back to mind — so if our thoughts begin to wander, they are quickly pulled back onto the path. One of the Seven Sages of Greece noticed this, and it led him to give people a precept that is now worn out with use: Respice finem — "look to the end." That is, in everything you do, keep your eye fixed on what you want, because that is what directs all your thoughts along the way to getting it.

The regulated train of thoughts is itself of two kinds. In the first, we imagine an effect and hunt for the causes or means that produce it. This kind is common to both humans and animals. In the second, we imagine some thing — anything at all — and hunt for all the possible effects it could produce. In other words, we imagine what we could do with it once we have it. Of this second kind I have never seen any sign except in human beings. It is a kind of curiosity that hardly belongs to the nature of any creature whose only passions are bodily ones, like hunger, thirst, lust, and anger.

In short, when the discourse of the mind is governed by design, it is nothing but seeking — the faculty of invention, which the Romans called sagacitas and solertia: a hunting out of the causes of some present or past effect, or of the effects of some present or past cause.

Remembrance

Sometimes a person looks for something he has lost. From the place and time where he first missed it, his mind runs backward, from place to place and time to time, to find where and when he last had it — that is, to find some definite, limited time and place from which to begin a methodical search. Then, from there, his thoughts run over those same places and times to discover what action or occasion might have made him lose it. This is what we call REMEMBRANCE, or calling to mind. The Romans called it reminiscentia — as if it were a re-counting, a going back over the ledger of our former actions.

Sometimes a person knows the definite area within which he must search, and then his thoughts sweep over every part of it — the way one would sweep a room to find a jewel, or the way a spaniel ranges across a field until it picks up a scent, or the way someone runs through the alphabet to start a rhyme.

Prudence

Sometimes a person wants to know the outcome of an action. Then he thinks of some similar action in the past and the outcomes that followed it, one after another, assuming that similar actions will be followed by similar outcomes. Someone who wants to foresee what will become of a criminal runs back over what he has seen follow the same crime before, in this order of thoughts: the crime, the officer, the prison, the judge, and the gallows. This kind of thinking is called foresight, and PRUDENCE, or providence — and sometimes wisdom. Though, since it is so hard to observe every circumstance, such guesswork is very prone to error. Still, this much is certain: the more experience of past things one person has compared to another, the more prudent he is, and the less often his expectations fail him.

Only the present exists in nature. Past things exist only in memory. And things to come have no existence at all — the future is nothing but a fiction of the mind, made by applying the consequences of past actions to present ones. The person with the most experience does this with the most confidence, but never with enough confidence to count as certainty. And though we call it prudence when the outcome matches our expectation, in its own nature it is only presumption. For genuine foresight of things to come — providence — belongs only to the one by whose will those things are to come. From him alone, and supernaturally, comes prophecy. The best natural prophet is the best guesser; and the best guesser is the one most practiced and studied in the matters he guesses at, for he has the most signs to guess by.

Signs

A SIGN is the prior event taken as evidence of the one that follows — or, in reverse, the following event taken as evidence of the prior one — whenever similar sequences have been observed before. And the more often they have been observed, the less uncertain the sign is. That is why the person with the most experience in any kind of business has the most signs by which to guess at the future, and so is the most prudent — so much more prudent than a newcomer to that business that no advantage of natural, off-the-cuff cleverness can make up the difference. Though perhaps many young men think otherwise.

Nevertheless, it is not prudence that separates man from beast. There are animals that, at a year old, observe more and pursue their own good more prudently than a ten-year-old child can.

Guessing About the Past

Just as prudence is a presumption about the future, drawn from the experience of the past, there is also a presumption about past things, drawn from other things that are likewise past. Someone who has seen the stages by which a flourishing state slid first into civil war and then into ruin will, at the sight of any other state's ruins, guess that the same kind of war and the same downward course happened there too. But this guess carries almost the same uncertainty as guesses about the future, since both rest on nothing but experience.

I can think of no other act of the human mind that is planted in us so naturally that it requires nothing for its exercise except being born human and living with the use of the five senses. The other faculties, which I will discuss shortly and which seem to belong to humans alone, are acquired and improved by study and effort. Most people learn them through instruction and discipline, and they all flow from the invention of words and speech. Beyond sense, thoughts, and the train of thoughts, the mind of man has no other motion at all — though with the help of speech and method, these same faculties can be raised to such a height that they distinguish humans from every other living creature.

Whatever we imagine is finite. Therefore there is no idea or conception of anything we call infinite. No one can hold in his mind an image of infinite size, or conceive the ends and bounds of the thing so named — we have no conception of the thing itself, only of our own inability. So the name of God is used not to make us conceive him — for he is incomprehensible, and his greatness and power are beyond conceiving — but so that we may honor him.

Also, because (as I said before) whatever we conceive was first perceived by sense, all at once or in parts, no one can have a thought representing anything that is not subject to sense. It follows that no one can conceive anything except as being in some place; as having some definite size; and as divisible into parts. Nor can anyone conceive that a thing is entirely in this place and entirely in another place at the same time, or that two or more things can occupy one and the same place at once. None of these things ever has been or ever can be presented to the senses. They are absurd phrases, accepted on trust — with no meaning at all — from deceived philosophers, and from Scholastics who are deceived, or deceiving.

Chapter 4: Of Speech

The Origin of Speech

The invention of printing, clever as it was, is no great achievement compared with the invention of letters. Who first discovered the use of letters, nobody knows. The man said to have brought them into Greece was Cadmus, son of Agenor, king of Phoenicia. A profitable invention it was — for preserving the memory of time past, and for connecting humankind, scattered as it is across so many distant regions of the earth. And a difficult one too, since it required careful observation of the various motions of the tongue, palate, lips, and the other organs of speech, in order to create that many distinct characters to record them.

But the noblest and most profitable invention of all was SPEECH itself — names and the ways of connecting them — by which people register their thoughts, recall them after they have passed, and declare them to one another for mutual benefit and conversation. Without speech, there would have been among human beings no commonwealth, no society, no contract, no peace — no more than among lions, bears, and wolves.

The first author of speech was God himself, who instructed Adam how to name the creatures presented to his sight. Scripture goes no further than that on the subject. But that much was enough to set Adam on the path of adding more names, as experience and the use of things gave him occasion, and of joining them together, bit by bit, so as to make himself understood. In time he could have gathered as much language as he found a use for — though hardly as rich a vocabulary as an orator or philosopher needs. I find nothing in Scripture, directly or by implication, suggesting that Adam was taught the names of all figures, numbers, measures, colors, sounds, imaginings, and relations — much less the names of words and speech themselves, like general, special, affirmative, negative, interrogative, optative, infinitive, all of which are useful — and least of all words like entity, intentionality, quiddity, and the other weighty terms of the Scholastic schools.

All this language, gathered and enlarged by Adam and his descendants, was lost again at the Tower of Babel, when God struck every man, for his rebellion, with forgetfulness of his former language. Forced by this to scatter into different parts of the world, people necessarily developed, little by little, the diversity of tongues we have now — in whatever ways need, the mother of all inventions, taught them. And over the course of time, languages everywhere grew richer.

The Uses of Speech

The general use of speech is to transfer our mental discourse into verbal discourse — to turn the train of our thoughts into a train of words. We do this for two benefits.

The first is to register the consequences of our thoughts. Thoughts tend to slip out of memory and put us to fresh labor; but they can be recalled again by the words we marked them with. So the first use of names is to serve as MARKS, or notes of remembrance.

The second comes when many people use the same words: then, by the connection and order of those words, they can signal to one another what they conceive or think about each matter, and also what they desire, fear, or feel. Used this way, words are called SIGNS.

The special uses of speech are these. First, to record what we discover by thinking to be the cause of anything, present or past, and what effects present or past things may produce — which, in sum, is the acquiring of arts and sciences. Second, to show others the knowledge we have attained — which is to counsel and teach one another. Third, to make our wills and purposes known to others, so that we may have one another's mutual help. Fourth, to please and delight ourselves and others by playing with our words, innocently, for pleasure or ornament.

The Abuses of Speech

To these four uses there correspond four abuses. First, when people register their thoughts wrongly because the meanings of their words keep shifting — so they record, as their conceptions, things they never actually conceived, and deceive themselves. Second, when they use words metaphorically — that is, in some sense other than the one the words were established for — and thereby deceive others. Third, when by words they declare something to be their will which is not. Fourth, when they use words to wound one another. Nature has armed living creatures — some with teeth, some with horns, some with hands — to hurt an enemy; so it is simply an abuse of speech to wound someone with the tongue. The one exception is a person we are obligated to govern; and then it is not wounding, but correcting and improving.

The way speech serves the remembrance of causes and effects consists in the imposing of names, and in the connecting of them.

Proper Names, Common Names, and Universals

Of names, some are PROPER, singular to one thing only — Peter, John, this man, this tree. And some are COMMON to many things — man, horse, tree. Each of these, though only one name, is nevertheless the name of many different particular things; and with respect to all of them taken together, it is called a UNIVERSAL. There is nothing in the world universal except names; for every one of the things named is individual and singular.

One universal name is imposed on many things because of their similarity in some quality or other feature. And whereas a proper name brings to mind one thing only, a universal recalls any one of those many.

Among universal names, some are of wider and some of narrower extent — the larger ones containing the smaller — and some are of equal extent, each containing the other. For example, the name body has a wider signification than the word man, and contains it; while the names man and rational are of equal extent, each comprehending the other. And we should notice here that by a "name" we do not always mean a single word, as in grammar; sometimes a name is a roundabout phrase of many words. All these words — he that in his actions observes the laws of his country — make up just one name, equivalent to the single word just.

By this imposing of names, some of wider and some of narrower signification, we turn the reckoning of consequences among things imagined in the mind into a reckoning of consequences among names. Take, for example, a man with no use of speech at all — someone born and remaining entirely deaf and mute. If you set a triangle before his eyes, with two right angles beside it (like the corners of a square), he may, by working it through, compare them and find that the three angles of that triangle equal the two right angles standing next to it. But show him another triangle, different in shape from the first, and he cannot know without fresh labor whether its three angles also equal the same.

A man who has the use of words is in a different position. When he observes that this equality of angles followed not from the length of the sides, nor from anything else particular to his triangle, but only from the sides being straight and the angles being three — and that this was all he meant by calling it a triangle — he will boldly conclude, universally, that this equality of angles holds in all triangles whatsoever, and will register his discovery in these general terms: every triangle has its three angles equal to two right angles. And so a consequence found in one particular case comes to be registered and remembered as a universal rule. It frees our mental reckoning from time and place; it spares us all labor of the mind except the first; and it makes what was found true here and now, true in all times and places.

Nowhere is the use of words in registering our thoughts so obvious as in numbering. A born fool who could never learn by heart the order of the number words — one, two, three — may notice every stroke of the clock, and nod along with it, or say one, one, one, but he can never know what hour it strikes. And it seems there was a time when those number names were not in use, and people had to apply the fingers of one or both hands to whatever they wanted to keep count of. That, it appears, is why our number words run to ten in every nation — and in some only to five — before beginning again. And someone who can count to ten, if he recites the numbers out of order, will lose himself and not know when he is done; much less will he be able to add and subtract and perform the other operations of arithmetic. So without words there is no possibility of reckoning numbers — much less of reckoning magnitudes, speeds, forces, and the other things whose calculation is necessary for humankind's existence and well-being.

When two names are joined together into a consequence, or affirmation — for instance, a man is a living creature, or if he is a man, he is a living creature — then if the second name, living creature, signifies everything the first name, man, signifies, the affirmation is TRUE; otherwise it is FALSE. For true and false are attributes of speech, not of things. Where there is no speech, there is neither truth nor falsehood. There may be ERROR — as when we expect something that will not happen, or suspect something that never did — but in neither case can a person be charged with untruth.

Since truth, then, consists in the right ordering of names in our affirmations, anyone who seeks precise truth must remember what every name he uses stands for, and place it accordingly. Otherwise he will find himself entangled in words like a bird in lime twigs: the more he struggles, the more stuck he becomes. That is why in geometry — the only science it has pleased God to bestow on mankind so far — men begin by settling the meanings of their words. This settling of meanings they call DEFINITIONS, and they place them at the beginning of their reckoning.

From this it is clear how necessary it is, for anyone who aspires to true knowledge, to examine the definitions of earlier authors — and either to correct them where they are carelessly set down, or to make his own. For the errors of definitions multiply as the reckoning proceeds, and lead men into absurdities which they eventually see but cannot escape without reckoning anew from the beginning, where the foundation of their errors lies. This is what happens to people who trust books: they do as those who add up many small sums into a larger one without checking whether the small sums were added correctly in the first place. When at last the error becomes visible, and they never think to distrust their starting points, they have no idea how to clear themselves. They waste their time fluttering over their books — like birds that came in by the chimney and, finding themselves shut in a room, flutter against the false light of a glass window, lacking the wit to consider how they got in.

So the first use of speech lies in the right definition of names, which is the acquisition of science. And the first abuse lies in wrong definitions, or in none at all; from this come all false and senseless doctrines. These make the men who take their instruction from the authority of books, rather than from their own thinking, as far below the condition of ordinary ignorant men as men equipped with true science are above it. For between true science and erroneous doctrine, ignorance sits in the middle. Natural sense and imagination are not subject to absurdity. Nature itself cannot err. And as people grow richer in language, they become wiser — or madder — than ordinary. Nor is it possible, without letters, for anyone to become either exceptionally wise or (unless his memory is damaged by disease or some defect of the organs) exceptionally foolish. For words are wise men's counters — they use them to calculate with, nothing more. But words are the money of fools, who value them by the authority of an Aristotle, a Cicero, a Thomas Aquinas, or any other doctor whatsoever — who was, after all, only a man.

What Can Be Named

Subject to names is whatever can enter into, or be considered in, an account — whatever can be added to something else to make a sum, or subtracted from it to leave a remainder. The Romans called accounts of money rationes, and accounting ratiocinatio; and what we call items in our bills and account books, they called nomina — that is, names. From this, it seems, they extended the word ratio to the faculty of reckoning in everything else. The Greeks have just one word, logos, for both speech and reason — not because they thought there was no speech without reason, but because there is no reasoning without speech. And the act of reasoning they called syllogism, which means summing up the consequences of one statement with another.

Because the same things can enter into an account for different features, their names are variously twisted and varied to show that diversity. This diversity of names can be reduced to four general headings.

First, a thing may enter into account as matter, or body — as living, sensible, rational, hot, cold, moved, at rest. With all such names, the word matter or body is understood; all these are names of matter.

Second, a thing may enter into account, or be considered, for some accident or quality we conceive to be in it — for being moved, for being so long, for being hot, and so on. Then, from the name of the thing itself, by a small change or twist, we make a name for the accident we are considering: for living we put into the account life; for moved, motion; for hot, heat; for long, length; and the like. All such names are names of the accidents and properties by which one body is distinguished from another. These are called ABSTRACT names — because they are severed, not from matter, but from the account of matter.

Third, we bring into account the properties of our own bodies, by which we make distinctions of another kind. When something is seen by us, we reckon not the thing itself but the sight, the color, the idea of it in the imagination. When something is heard, we reckon not the thing but only the hearing, or the sound, which is our imagination or conception of it through the ear. Such names are names of fancies — of our own imaginings.

Fourth, we bring into account, consider, and give names to names themselves, and to forms of speech. General, universal, special, equivocal are names of names. And affirmation, interrogation, command, narration, syllogism, sermon, oration, and many other such words are names of speeches.

Positive Names

And this is the whole variety of names POSITIVE — names put down to mark something that exists in nature, or that the human mind can feign: bodies that are, or may be conceived to be; or the properties of bodies that are, or may be feigned to be; or words and speech.

Negative Names and Their Uses

There are also other names, called NEGATIVE, which are notes signifying that a given word is not the name of the thing in question — words like nothing, no man, infinite, unteachable, three lacking four, and the like. These are nevertheless useful in reckoning, or in correcting a reckoning, and they call our past thoughts to mind, even though they are not the names of any thing — because they make us refuse to accept names that are not rightly used.

Meaningless Words

All other names are nothing but insignificant sounds, and they come in two sorts. The first sort: words that are new, and whose meaning has never been explained by any definition. The Scholastics and puzzled philosophers have coined these in abundance.

The second sort: when men weld together two names whose significations are contradictory and inconsistent — like the name an incorporeal body, or (which comes to the same thing) an incorporeal substance, and a great many more. For whenever an affirmation is false, the two names it is composed of, put together and made into one, signify nothing at all. For example, if it is a false affirmation to say a quadrangle is round, then the phrase round quadrangle signifies nothing; it is a mere sound. Likewise, if it is false to say that virtue can be poured, or blown up and down, then the phrases in-poured virtue and in-blown virtue are as absurd and meaningless as a round quadrangle. And so you will rarely meet a senseless, insignificant word that is not built out of some Latin or Greek names. A Frenchman seldom hears our Savior called by the name Parole, but often by the name Verbe — yet Verbe and Parole differ in nothing except that one is Latin and the other French.

Understanding

When a man, on hearing any speech, has the very thoughts which the words of that speech and their connection were established and arranged to signify, then he is said to UNDERSTAND it — understanding being nothing else but conception caused by speech. And therefore, if speech is peculiar to man (and as far as I know, it is), then understanding is peculiar to him as well. It follows that of absurd and false affirmations — when they are universal — there can be no understanding at all; though many people think they understand such statements, when all they are really doing is repeating the words softly to themselves, or turning them over in their minds.

What kinds of speech signify the desires, aversions, and passions of the human mind, and how those speeches are used and abused, I will discuss after I have dealt with the passions.

Unstable Names

The names of things that affect us — that is, things that please and displease us — have, in everyday conversation, an INCONSTANT signification, because not everyone is affected the same way by the same thing, nor is the same person affected the same way at all times. Since all names are imposed to signify our conceptions, and all our feelings are themselves conceptions, then when we conceive the same things differently, we can hardly avoid naming them differently. Even though the nature of the thing we conceive is the same, the diversity of our reception of it — owing to our different bodily constitutions and our prejudices of opinion — gives everything a tint of our different passions.

In reasoning, therefore, a man must be careful of words which, beyond signifying what we imagine of the thing's nature, also carry the disposition and interest of the speaker. The names of virtues and vices are like this. One man calls wisdom what another calls fear; one calls cruelty what another calls justice; one calls extravagance what another calls generosity; one calls seriousness what another calls stupidity; and so on. Names like these can never be true grounds for any reasoning. Neither can metaphors and figures of speech — but those are less dangerous, because they openly profess their inconstancy. The others do not.

Chapter 5: Of Reason and Science

What Reason Is

When a man reasons, he is doing nothing but conceiving a sum total from the addition of parts, or conceiving a remainder from the subtraction of one sum from another. If this is done with words, it is conceiving the consequence from the names of all the parts to the name of the whole; or from the names of the whole and one part, to the name of the other part. In some fields, such as numbers, men name other operations besides adding and subtracting — multiplying and dividing — but these are really the same thing. For multiplication is just the adding together of equal things, and division is just the subtracting of one thing as many times as we can.

These operations are not confined to numbers. They apply to anything at all that can be added together or taken one out of another. Arithmeticians teach how to add and subtract in numbers. Geometricians teach the same in lines, figures (solid and flat), angles, proportions, times, degrees of speed, force, power, and the like. Logicians teach the same in consequences of words: adding together two names to make an affirmation; two affirmations to make a syllogism; many syllogisms to make a demonstration; and from the sum, or conclusion of a syllogism, they subtract one proposition to find the other. Writers on politics add together agreements to find men's duties; and lawyers add laws and facts to find what is right and wrong in the actions of private persons. In sum: wherever there is room for addition and subtraction, there is room for reason. And where these have no place, reason has nothing at all to do.

Reason Defined

Out of all this we may define — that is to say, determine — what is meant by the word reason when we count it among the faculties of the mind. REASON, in this sense, is nothing but reckoning — that is, adding and subtracting — the consequences of general names agreed upon for the marking and signifying of our thoughts. I say marking them when we reckon by ourselves, and signifying when we demonstrate or prove our reckonings to other men.

Where Right Reason Is Found

In arithmetic, beginners must make mistakes, and even the professors themselves may often err and total things up wrongly. So too in every other subject of reasoning: the ablest, most attentive, and most practiced men may deceive themselves and draw false conclusions. Not that reason itself is not always right reason — it is, just as arithmetic is a certain and infallible art. But no one man's reason, and no group of men's reason, supplies the certainty — any more than an account is correctly totaled just because a great many people have unanimously approved it.

So when there is a dispute over an account, the parties must, by their own agreement, set up as right reason the reason of some arbitrator or judge, whose decision they will both accept. Otherwise their dispute must either come to blows or remain unsettled, for want of a right reason established by nature. And so it is in every debate of every kind. When men who think themselves wiser than everyone else clamor and demand "right reason" as judge, yet really want nothing more than for matters to be decided by no reason but their own, it is as intolerable in human society as it is in a card game, after trump has been declared, to insist on using as trump, on every hand, whatever suit you happen to hold the most of. For that is exactly what such men do: they want every passion of theirs, as it comes to hold sway in them, to be taken for right reason — and in their own disputes, no less. They betray their lack of right reason by the very claim they lay to it.

The Use of Reason

The use and end of reason is not to find the sum and truth of one or a few consequences far removed from the first definitions and settled meanings of names. It is to begin at those definitions and proceed from one consequence to the next. There can be no certainty about the final conclusion without certainty about all the affirmations and negations on which it was grounded and from which it was inferred.

Think of the head of a household settling his accounts. He adds the sums of all his bills of expense into one total — but never checks how each bill was added up by the people who submitted it, or what he is actually paying for. He gains no more by this than if he had accepted the whole account in one lump, trusting in the skill and honesty of every accountant. It is the same in reasoning about anything else: a man who takes up conclusions on the trust of authors, and does not work them out from the first items in every reckoning — which are the meanings of names settled by definitions — loses his labor. He does not know anything. He only believes.

Of Error and Absurdity

A man can reckon without words in particular cases — as when, at the sight of some one thing, we guess what probably came before it or what is likely to follow it. If what he thought likely to follow does not follow, or what he thought likely to have come before did not come before, this is called error, and even the most prudent men are subject to it.

But when we reason in words of general signification and land on a general inference that is false, then — though it is commonly called error — it is in fact an ABSURDITY, or senseless speech. For error is only a deception, a presuming that something has happened or will happen which, though it did not happen or will not, was at least not impossible. But when we make a general assertion, unless it is a true one, its very possibility is inconceivable. And words by which we conceive nothing but the sound are the ones we call absurd, insignificant, and nonsense. So if a man talked to me of a round quadrangle, or the accidents of bread in cheese, or immaterial substances, or a free subject, a free will, or anything "free" other than free from being hindered by opposition — I would not say he was in error. I would say his words were without meaning. That is to say: absurd.

I said before, in the second chapter, that man excels all other animals in this faculty: whenever he conceives anything whatsoever, he is inclined to inquire into its consequences, and what effects he could produce with it. Now I add another degree of that same excellence: by words, he can reduce the consequences he finds to general rules, called theorems or aphorisms. That is, he can reason — reckon — not only in numbers, but in everything else that can be added to or subtracted from something.

But this privilege is offset by another: the privilege of absurdity, to which no living creature is subject but man alone. And of men, none are more subject to it than those who profess philosophy. What Cicero says of them somewhere is entirely true: there is nothing so absurd that it cannot be found in the books of philosophers. The reason is obvious. Not one of them begins his reasoning from the definitions, or explanations, of the names he is going to use — a method that has been used only in geometry, whose conclusions have thereby been made indisputable.

The Causes of Absurdity

The first cause of absurd conclusions I ascribe to the lack of method: they do not begin their reasoning from definitions — that is, from settled meanings of their words — as if they could cast up an account without knowing the value of the number words one, two, and three.

Now, all bodies enter into our reckoning under various considerations, which I mentioned in the previous chapter; and since these considerations carry different names, various absurdities arise from confusing them and connecting their names into assertions where they do not fit. And therefore:

The second cause of absurd assertions I ascribe to giving the names of bodies to accidents, or of accidents to bodies. This is what people do when they say faith is "infused" or "inspired" — when nothing can be poured or breathed into anything but a body — or that extension is body, or that phantasms are spirits, and so on.

The third, to giving the names of the accidents of bodies outside us to the accidents of our own bodies — as they do who say the color is in the body, or the sound is in the air, and so on.

The fourth, to giving the names of bodies to names or speeches — as they do who say that there are things universal, or that a living creature is a genus, or "a general thing," and so on.

The fifth, to giving the names of accidents to names and speeches — as they do who say the nature of a thing is in its definition, or a man's command is his will, and the like.

The sixth, to the use of metaphors, tropes, and other rhetorical figures in place of proper words. It is perfectly acceptable in common speech to say, for example, the road goes, or leads, here or there, or the proverb says this or that — even though roads cannot go and proverbs cannot speak. But in reckoning, in the search for truth, such expressions cannot be admitted.

The seventh, to names that signify nothing, picked up and learned by rote from the Scholastics: "hypostatical," "transubstantiate," "consubstantiate," "eternal-now," and the rest of the Schoolmen's cant.

A man who can avoid these things will not easily fall into any absurdity — unless it be through the sheer length of a calculation, where he may forget what came before. For all men by nature reason alike, and reason well, when they have good principles. Who is so stupid as both to make a mistake in geometry and to persist in it after someone else has pointed out his error?

Science

From all this it appears that reason is not, like sense and memory, born with us; nor acquired by experience alone, as prudence is. It is attained by hard work: first, in the apt imposing of names; and second, in acquiring a good and orderly method of proceeding from the elements, which are names, to assertions made by connecting one name to another; and from there to syllogisms, which are the connections of one assertion to another — until we arrive at a knowledge of all the consequences of names belonging to the subject at hand. And that is what men call SCIENCE.

Whereas sense and memory are only knowledge of fact — of a thing past and unchangeable — science is the knowledge of consequences, of the dependence of one fact upon another. By it, from what we can do right now, we know how to do something else when we choose, or a similar thing at another time. Because once we see how a thing comes about, from what causes, and in what manner, then whenever similar causes come into our power, we see how to make them produce similar effects.

Children, then, are not endowed with reason at all until they have attained the use of speech. They are called reasonable creatures because of the evident possibility of their having the use of reason in time to come. And most men, though they have the use of reasoning a little way — as in counting to some degree — get little use out of it in everyday life. There they govern themselves, some better, some worse, according to their differences of experience, quickness of memory, and inclination toward various ends — but most of all according to good or bad fortune, and one another's mistakes. As for science — certain rules for their actions — they are so far from it that they do not even know what it is. Geometry they have taken for sorcery. And as for the other sciences, those who have never been taught their beginnings, and made some progress in them so as to see how they are acquired and generated, are in this respect like children who, having no idea where babies come from, are made to believe by the women that their brothers and sisters were not born but found in the garden.

Even so, those who have no science are in a better and nobler condition with their natural prudence than men who, by reasoning badly, or by trusting those who reason wrong, land on false and absurd general rules. For ignorance of causes and of rules does not lead men as far astray as relying on false rules — taking for causes of what they aim at things which are not causes of it at all, but rather causes of the contrary.

To conclude: the light of human minds is perspicuous words — but first trimmed and purged of ambiguity by exact definitions. Reason is the pace; the increase of science, the road; and the benefit of mankind, the destination. And on the contrary, metaphors and senseless, ambiguous words are like ignes fatui — will-o'-the-wisps. Reasoning upon them is wandering among innumerable absurdities; and their end is contention, and sedition, or contempt.

Prudence and Sapience, with Their Difference

As much experience is prudence, so much science is sapience. We usually have only the one name, wisdom, for them both; but the Romans always distinguished between prudentia and sapientia, ascribing the former to experience, the latter to science. To make the difference appear more clearly, suppose one man endowed with an excellent natural skill and dexterity in handling his weapons; and another who has added to that dexterity an acquired science of where he can strike his adversary, or be struck by him, in every possible posture or guard. The ability of the first would be to the ability of the second as prudence is to sapience: both useful, but the second infallible. But those who, trusting only to the authority of books, follow the blind blindly, are like the man who trusts the false rules of a fencing master and presumptuously ventures against an adversary who either kills him or disgraces him.

The Signs of Science

Of the signs of science, some are certain and infallible; some, uncertain. Certain, when the man who claims the science of anything can teach it — that is, demonstrate its truth clearly to another. Uncertain, when only some particular outcomes match his claim, and on many occasions things turn out as he says they must. The signs of prudence are all uncertain; for it is impossible to observe by experience, and remember, every circumstance that might change the outcome.

But in any business where a man has no infallible science to proceed by, to abandon his own natural judgment and be guided by general maxims read in authors — maxims subject to many exceptions — is a sign of folly, and is generally scorned under the name of pedantry. Even among those very men who, in the councils of the commonwealth, love to show off their reading in politics and history, very few do the same in their domestic affairs, where their own particular interest is at stake. They have prudence enough for their private business. But in public, they care more about the reputation of their own cleverness than about the success of anyone else's business.

Chapter 6: The Inner Beginnings of Voluntary Motion — Commonly Called the Passions — and the Speech That Expresses Them

Vital Motion and Voluntary Motion

Animals have two kinds of motion that belong to them alone. One is called vital motion: it begins at conception and continues without interruption through the whole of life. The circulation of the blood, the pulse, breathing, digestion, nutrition, excretion — these are vital motions, and they need no help from the imagination. The other kind is animal motion, otherwise called voluntary motion: walking, speaking, moving any of our limbs in whatever way we first picture in our minds.

I have already shown, in the first and second chapters, that sense is motion in the organs and inner parts of the body, caused by the action of the things we see and hear, and that imagination is nothing but the leftovers of that same motion, remaining after sense. And because walking, speaking, and other voluntary motions always depend on a prior thought of where, which way, and what, it is clear that the imagination is the first internal beginning of all voluntary motion.

Now, people who have never studied the matter cannot conceive of any motion at all when the thing moved is invisible, or when the space it moves through is too small to notice. But that does not mean such motions do not exist. However small a space may be, whatever moves across a larger space — of which that small one is a part — must move across the small one first.

These small beginnings of motion inside the human body, before they show themselves in walking, speaking, striking, and other visible actions, are commonly called ENDEAVOR — the tiny internal beginnings of motion.

Endeavor: Appetite, Desire, Hunger, Thirst, Aversion

When this endeavor is directed toward the thing that causes it, it is called APPETITE, or DESIRE. Desire is the general name; appetite is often reserved for the desire of food — namely hunger and thirst. And when the endeavor moves away from something, it is generally called AVERSION.

The words "appetite" and "aversion" come to us from the Latin, and both signify motions: one of approaching, the other of retreating. So do the Greek words for the same things, horme and aphorme. Nature herself often presses these truths on men — truths they later stumble over when they go looking for something beyond nature. The Scholastics, for instance, can find no actual motion at all in the mere appetite to walk or move. But since they must admit some motion is there, they call it "metaphorical motion" — which is simply absurd speech. Words may be metaphorical. Bodies and motions cannot.

What men desire, they are also said to LOVE; and they are said to HATE the things they have aversion for. So desire and love are the same thing, except that by desire we always mean the object is absent, and by love we usually mean it is present. Likewise, by aversion we signify the absence of the object, and by hate, its presence.

Of appetites and aversions, some are born with us: the appetite for food, the appetite for excretion and relief (which might more properly be called aversions, since they come from something men feel in their own bodies), and a few others — not many. The rest, the appetites for particular things, come from experience — from testing their effects on ourselves or watching their effects on other men. For things we do not know at all, or do not believe exist, we can have no further desire than the desire to taste and try them. But we feel aversion not only toward things we know have hurt us, but also toward things when we do not know whether they will hurt us or not.

Contempt

The things we neither desire nor hate, we are said to despise. CONTEMPT is nothing but an immobility, a stubbornness of the heart in resisting the action of certain things — and it comes either from the heart being already moved in another direction by more powerful objects, or from lack of experience of the things despised.

And because the makeup of a man's body is in constant change, it is impossible for all the same things to always cause in him the same appetites and aversions. Much less can all men agree in desiring almost any single object.

Good and Evil

But whatever a man desires, that is what he, for his part, calls good. Whatever he hates, he calls evil. And whatever he despises, he calls worthless and beneath notice. For these words — good, evil, contemptible — are always used in relation to the person using them. Nothing is simply and absolutely good or evil. There is no common rule of good and evil to be found in the nature of the objects themselves. The rule comes from the person of the man, where there is no commonwealth; or, in a commonwealth, from the person who represents it; or from an arbitrator or judge whom disagreeing men set up by consent, making his decision the rule.

Pulchrum and Turpe; Delightful and Profitable; Unpleasant and Unprofitable

Latin has two words whose meanings come close to good and evil but are not quite the same: pulchrum and turpe. The first signifies that which, by some visible sign, promises good; the second, that which promises evil. Our language has no names that general. For pulchrum we say, depending on the thing, fair, beautiful, handsome, gallant, honorable, comely, or lovely; for turpe we say foul, deformed, ugly, base, nauseating, and the like, as the subject requires. All these words, used properly, signify nothing but the look or appearance that promises good or evil.

So there are three kinds of good: good in the promise — that is pulchrum; good in effect, as the end desired — which is called jucundum, delightful; and good as the means — which is called utile, profitable. And there are just as many kinds of evil: evil in the promise is what they call turpe; evil in effect and end is molestum — unpleasant, troublesome; and evil in the means is inutile — unprofitable, hurtful.

Delight and Displeasure

In sense, as I have said before, what is really inside us is only motion, caused by the action of external objects — though it appears to the sight as light and color, to the ear as sound, to the nostril as odor, and so on. In the same way, when the action of that same object continues from the eyes, ears, and other organs to the heart, the real effect there is nothing but motion, or endeavor, which consists in appetite or aversion toward or away from the object. But the appearance, the felt sense of that motion, is what we call either DELIGHT or TROUBLE OF MIND.

Pleasure and Offense

This motion called appetite — and, in its appearance, delight and pleasure — seems to be a strengthening of the vital motion, a help to it. That is why the things that caused delight were quite properly called jucunda, from juvare, to help or fortify; and their contraries molesta, offensive, from hindering and troubling the vital motion.

PLEASURE, therefore (or delight), is the appearance or sense of good; and MOLESTATION or DISPLEASURE is the appearance or sense of evil. And so all appetite, desire, and love comes with some delight, more or less; and all hatred and aversion with more or less displeasure and offense.

Pleasures of Sense; Pleasures of the Mind; Joy, Pain, Grief

Of pleasures, or delights, some arise from the sense of an object that is present. These may be called pleasures of sense. (The word "sensual," as used by those who condemn such pleasures, has no place until there are laws.) Of this kind are all the fillings and emptyings of the body, and everything pleasant to sight, hearing, smell, taste, or touch.

Other pleasures arise from expectation — from foreseeing the end or consequence of things, whether those things please or displease in the sense itself. These are pleasures of the mind in the person who draws those consequences, and they are generally called JOY. In the same way, some displeasures are in the sense, and are called PAIN; others are in the expectation of consequences, and are called GRIEF.

These simple passions — appetite, desire, love, aversion, hate, joy, and grief — take different names for different reasons. First, when they follow one after another, they are named differently according to the opinion men have of their chances of getting what they desire. Secondly, from the object loved or hated. Thirdly, from considering many of them together. Fourthly, from the alternation or sequence itself.

Hope — Appetite combined with an opinion of attaining the thing is called HOPE.

Despair — The same, without that opinion, is DESPAIR.

Fear — Aversion, with the opinion that the object will hurt us, is FEAR.

Courage — The same, with hope of avoiding that hurt by resisting, is COURAGE.

Anger — Sudden courage is ANGER.

Confidence — Constant hope is CONFIDENCE in ourselves.

Diffidence — Constant despair is DIFFIDENCE in ourselves — a settled distrust of our own powers.

Indignation — Anger at great hurt done to another, when we believe it was done by injury, is INDIGNATION.

Benevolence — Desire of good for another is BENEVOLENCE, GOOD WILL, CHARITY. Directed at mankind in general, it is GOOD NATURE.

Covetousness — Desire of riches is COVETOUSNESS — a name always used as blame, because men competing for riches are displeased when others get them. The desire itself, though, deserves blame or approval according to the means by which the riches are sought.

Ambition — Desire of office or precedence is AMBITION — a name also used in the worse sense, for the reason just mentioned.

Pusillanimity — Desire of things that help our purposes only a little, and fear of things that hinder them only a little, is PUSILLANIMITY — small-mindedness.

Magnanimity — Contempt for little helps and little hindrances is MAGNANIMITY — greatness of mind.

Valor — Magnanimity in danger of death or wounds is VALOR, or FORTITUDE.

Liberality — Magnanimity in the use of riches is LIBERALITY.

Miserableness — Pusillanimity in the same is WRETCHEDNESS, MISERABLENESS, or PARSIMONY — depending on whether the speaker approves or disapproves.

Kindness — Love of persons for company and society is KINDNESS.

Natural Lust — Love of persons only for pleasing the senses is NATURAL LUST.

Luxury — Love of the same, acquired by rumination — that is, by imagining past pleasure — is LUXURY.

The Passion of Love; Jealousy — Love of one person singularly, with desire to be singularly loved in return, is THE PASSION OF LOVE. The same, with fear that the love is not mutual, is JEALOUSY.

Revengefulness — Desire to hurt another in order to make him condemn some act of his own is REVENGEFULNESS.

Curiosity — Desire to know why, and how, is CURIOSITY. No living creature has it but man — so man is distinguished from the other animals not only by his reason but by this singular passion as well. In the other animals, the appetite for food and the other pleasures of sense dominate, and crowd out any care for knowing causes. Curiosity is a lust of the mind that, by a persistence of delight in the continual and tireless generation of knowledge, outlasts the short intensity of any bodily pleasure.

Religion; Superstition; True Religion — Fear of invisible power, whether invented by the mind or imagined from stories that are publicly allowed, is RELIGION; from stories not allowed, SUPERSTITION. And when the imagined power really is such as we imagine it, TRUE RELIGION.

Panic Terror — Fear without any grasp of why or what is PANIC TERROR — named after the fables that make the god Pan its author. In truth, the person who first feels such fear always has some idea of the cause; the rest simply run because everyone else is running, each one assuming his neighbor knows why. That is why this passion strikes no one except in a crowd or multitude of people.
