Admiration — Joy from the sense of novelty is ADMIRATION — proper to man, because it stirs up the appetite for knowing the cause.

Glory; Vainglory — Joy arising from imagining one's own power and ability is that exultation of the mind called GLORYING. If it is grounded on the experience of one's own past actions, it is the same as confidence. But if it is grounded on the flattery of others, or merely supposed by the man himself for the delight he takes in its consequences, it is called VAINGLORY — vanity. The name fits, because a well-grounded confidence leads a man to attempt things, whereas merely supposed power does not, and is therefore rightly called vain.

Dejection — Grief from the opinion that one lacks power is called DEJECTION of mind.

The vainglory that consists in pretending or supposing abilities in ourselves that we know we do not have is most common in young men. It is fed by the histories and fictions of gallant heroes, and is often corrected by age and employment.

Sudden Glory; Laughter — SUDDEN GLORY is the passion that produces those grimaces we call LAUGHTER. It is caused either by some sudden act of our own that pleases us, or by spotting some defect in another person, by comparison with which we suddenly applaud ourselves. It strikes most often those who are conscious of having the fewest abilities themselves — people forced to stay in their own good graces by noticing the imperfections of other men. That is why a great deal of laughter at the defects of others is a sign of pusillanimity. One of the proper works of a great mind is to help others and free them from scorn, and to compare itself only with the most able.

Sudden Dejection; Weeping — On the contrary, SUDDEN DEJECTION is the passion that causes WEEPING. It is brought on by accidents that suddenly snatch away some intense hope, or some prop of a person's power. Those most subject to it are those who rely chiefly on external supports — such as women and children. So some weep for the loss of friends, others for friends' unkindness, others when reconciliation puts a sudden stop to their thoughts of revenge. But in all cases, both laughter and weeping are sudden motions, and habit wears them both away. No one laughs at an old joke, or weeps over an old calamity.

Shame; Blushing — Grief at the discovery of some defect in our ability is SHAME — the passion that reveals itself in BLUSHING. It consists in the awareness of something dishonorable. In young men it is a sign of the love of a good reputation, and commendable. In old men it is a sign of the same, but because it comes too late, not commendable.

Impudence — Contempt for good reputation is called IMPUDENCE.

Pity — Grief at the calamity of another is PITY, and it arises from imagining that the same calamity might befall ourselves. That is why it is also called COMPASSION, and, in the phrase of the present time, FELLOW-FEELING. And that is also why the best men have the least pity for calamity that comes from great wickedness — and why those who think themselves least exposed to a given calamity feel the least pity for it.

Cruelty — Contempt for, or insensitivity to, the calamity of others is what men call CRUELTY, and it comes from a sense of security about one's own fortune. For I do not believe it possible that any man takes pleasure in the great harms of other men with no end of his own in view.

Emulation; Envy — Grief at a competitor's success in wealth, honor, or some other good, joined with the endeavor to strain our own abilities to equal or surpass him, is called EMULATION. Joined instead with the endeavor to supplant or hinder the competitor, it is ENVY.

Deliberation

When appetites and aversions, hopes and fears about one and the same thing arise in a man's mind by turns — when the various good and evil consequences of doing or not doing the thing pass through his thoughts in succession, so that sometimes he has an appetite for it, sometimes an aversion from it, sometimes hope of managing it, sometimes despair or fear of attempting it — the whole sum of those desires, aversions, hopes, and fears, continued until the thing is either done or judged impossible, is what we call DELIBERATION.

It follows that there is no deliberation about things past, because they obviously cannot be changed; nor about things known or believed to be impossible, because men know, or think, that deliberating about them is pointless. But about impossible things we believe to be possible, we may deliberate — not knowing that it is in vain. And it is called deliberation because it is a putting an end to the liberty we had of doing the thing, or not doing it, according to our own appetite or aversion.

This alternating succession of appetites, aversions, hopes, and fears occurs no less in other living creatures than in man. Therefore beasts also deliberate.

Every deliberation is said to end when the thing deliberated about is either done or judged impossible — because until then we keep the liberty of doing it or not, according to our appetite or aversion.

The Will

In deliberation, the last appetite or aversion — the one immediately attached to the action or to the omission of it — is what we call the WILL: the act (not the faculty) of willing. And beasts that have deliberation must necessarily also have will.

The definition of the will commonly given by the Scholastics — that it is a "rational appetite" — is not a good one. If it were, there could be no voluntary act against reason, since a voluntary act is precisely one that proceeds from the will and from nothing else. But if instead of "rational appetite" we say "an appetite resulting from prior deliberation," then their definition becomes the same as mine. The will, therefore, is the last appetite in deliberating.

It is true that in everyday talk we say a man once had a will to do a thing that he nevertheless refrained from doing. But that, properly speaking, was only an inclination — and an inclination makes no action voluntary, because the action depends not on it but on the last inclination or appetite. If the intervening appetites made an action voluntary, then by the same logic all the intervening aversions would make the same action involuntary — and one and the same action would be both voluntary and involuntary.

From this it is clear that voluntary actions include not only those that begin in covetousness, ambition, lust, or other appetites for the thing proposed, but also those that begin in aversion, or in fear of the consequences of leaving it undone.

The Forms of Speech for the Passions

The forms of speech that express the passions are partly the same as those that express our thoughts, and partly different. First, and generally, all passions can be expressed in the indicative: I love, I fear, I rejoice, I deliberate, I will, I command. But some passions have particular expressions of their own — expressions which, nonetheless, are not affirmations, unless they happen to serve some further inference beyond the passion they spring from.

Deliberation is expressed in the subjunctive — the form of speech proper for stating suppositions and their consequences, as in: if this is done, then this will follow. It differs from the language of reasoning only in this: reasoning works in general terms, while deliberation is mostly about particulars.

The language of desire and aversion is the imperative: do this, do not do that. When the person addressed is obliged to do or not do the thing, this is command; otherwise it is prayer, or else counsel.

The language of vainglory, of indignation, of pity, and of revengefulness is the optative — the language of wishing. But for the desire to know there is a special expression called the interrogative: What is it? When will it happen? How is it done? Why so?

Other language of the passions I find none. Cursing, swearing, reviling, and the like do not signify as speech at all — they are just the actions of a tongue that has gotten used to them.

These forms of speech, I say, are expressions, or voluntary indications, of our passions. But they are not certain signs of them, because men may use them at will, whether they feel the passions or not. The best signs of a passion actually present are found in the face, the motions of the body, the actions, and the ends or aims we otherwise know the man to have.

Apparent Good and Evil

In deliberation, the appetites and aversions are raised by foreseeing the good and evil consequences and sequels of the action we are deliberating about. So the good or evil effect of the action depends on foreseeing a long chain of consequences — and very seldom is any man able to see to the end of it. But as far as a man does see: if the good in those consequences outweighs the evil, the whole chain is what writers call apparent or seeming good; and when the evil outweighs the good, the whole is apparent or seeming evil. So the man who has, by experience or reason, the longest and surest view of the consequences deliberates best himself, and is able, when he chooses, to give the best counsel to others.

Happiness

Continual success in obtaining the things a man desires from time to time — continual prospering, in other words — is what men call FELICITY: happiness. I mean the happiness of this life. For there is no such thing as perpetual tranquility of mind while we live here, because life itself is nothing but motion, and can no more be without desire and without fear than it can be without sense. What kind of happiness God has prepared for those who devoutly honor him, a man will know no sooner than he enjoys it — those are joys as incomprehensible now as the Scholastics' phrase "beatifical vision" is unintelligible.

Praise and Magnification

The form of speech by which men signify their opinion of the goodness of anything is PRAISE. The form by which they signify its power and greatness is MAGNIFYING. And the form by which they signify their opinion of a man's happiness the Greeks called makarismos — calling someone blessed — for which we have no word in our language.

And this much is sufficient, for the present purpose, to have said about the passions.

Chapter 7: The Ends, or Conclusions, of Discourse

Every train of discourse driven by the desire for knowledge comes, at last, to an end — either because you find what you were looking for, or because you give up the search. And wherever the chain of discourse gets interrupted, there is an end of it, at least for that time.

Judgment, or Final Verdict; Doubt

If the discourse is purely mental — the mind talking to itself — it consists of thoughts swinging back and forth: the thing will happen, the thing will not happen; it has been, it has not been. So wherever you break off the chain of a man's thinking, you leave him stranded in a presumption one way or the other: it will be, or it will not be; it has been, or it has not been. All of this is OPINION.

Notice the parallel. The back-and-forth of desires, when we deliberate about good and evil, has its exact counterpart in the back-and-forth of opinions, when we inquire into the truth about past and future. And just as the last desire in deliberation is called the will, so the last opinion in the search for the truth of past and future is called the JUDGMENT — the resolute and final verdict of the person doing the reasoning. And just as the whole chain of alternating desires on the question of good or bad is called deliberation, so the whole chain of alternating opinions on the question of true or false is called DOUBT.

No discourse whatsoever can end in absolute knowledge of fact, whether past or still to come. Knowledge of fact is, at its origin, sense — and ever afterward, memory. And knowledge of consequence, which I said before is called science, is not absolute but conditional. No man can know by discourse that this or that is, has been, or will be — that would be knowing absolutely. He can only know that if this is, then that is; if this has been, then that has been; if this shall be, then that shall be. That is knowing conditionally. And even then, it is not knowledge of the consequence of one thing to another, but of one name of a thing to another name of the same thing.

Science, Opinion, Conscience

So when discourse is put into speech, and begins with the definitions of words, and proceeds by connecting them into general affirmations, and connecting those in turn into syllogisms, the end — the final sum — is called the conclusion. And the thought of the mind it signifies is that conditional knowledge, that knowledge of the consequences of words, which is commonly called science. But if the first ground of such discourse is not definitions, or if the definitions are not rightly joined together into syllogisms, then the end or conclusion is once again opinion — an opinion about the truth of something said, even though it is sometimes said in absurd and senseless words that no one could possibly understand.

When two or more men know one and the same fact, they are said to be CONSCIOUS of it to one another — which is as much as to say they know it together. And because such men make the best witnesses of each other's deeds, or of a third man's, it always has been and always will be considered a very evil act for any man to speak against his conscience, or to corrupt or force another man to do so. That is why the plea of conscience has been listened to so carefully in every age.

Afterward, men took the same word and used it metaphorically, for the knowledge of their own secret deeds and secret thoughts. Hence the rhetorical saying that the conscience is a thousand witnesses.

And last of all, men passionately in love with their own new opinions — however absurd — and stubbornly determined to maintain them, gave those opinions, too, the revered name of conscience. As if to make it seem unlawful ever to change them or to speak against them. And so they pretend to know their opinions are true, when the most they actually know is that they think so.

Belief and Faith

When a man's discourse does not begin at definitions, it begins in one of two other places. Either it begins at some contemplation of his own — and then it is still called opinion. Or it begins at the saying of someone else, whose ability to know the truth, and whose honesty in not deceiving, he does not doubt. In that case the discourse is not so much about the thing as about the person. And the resolution of it is called BELIEF and FAITH: faith in the man; belief both of the man and of the truth of what he says.

So in belief there are two opinions at once: one about the saying, the other about the man's virtue. To have faith in a man, to trust him, to believe him — these all mean the same thing, namely an opinion of the man's truthfulness. But to believe what is said means only an opinion of the truth of the saying itself.

Now here is something worth observing. The phrase "I believe in" — like the Latin credo in, and the Greek pisteuo eis — is never used except in the writings of theologians. In all other writing, men say instead: I believe him; I have faith in him; I rely on him — in Latin, credo illi and fido illi; in Greek, pisteuo auto. And this peculiarity of the ecclesiastical use of the word has stirred up many disputes about the right object of the Christian faith.

But "believing in," as the words are used in the Creed, means not trust in the person, but confession and acknowledgment of the doctrine. For it is not only Christians — men of every kind believe in God in the sense of holding everything they hear him say to be true, whether they understand it or not. And that is all the faith and trust that can possibly be placed in any person whatsoever. But they do not all believe the doctrine of the Creed.

From this we may draw a conclusion. Whenever we believe a saying to be true — any saying at all — on the basis of arguments taken not from the thing itself, nor from the principles of natural reason, but from the authority and good opinion we have of the man who said it, then the speaker is the person we believe in, the person we trust, the person whose word we take. He is the object of our faith, and the honor done in believing is done to him alone.

It follows that when we believe the Scriptures are the word of God, having no immediate revelation from God himself, our belief, faith, and trust is in the Church — whose word we take, and rest content with. And those who believe what a prophet tells them in the name of God are taking the word of the prophet: they do honor to him, and it is him they trust and believe concerning the truth of what he reports — whether he is a true prophet or a false one.

And the same goes for all other history. If I refused to believe everything the historians have written about the glorious deeds of Alexander or Caesar, I don't think the ghost of Alexander or Caesar would have any just cause to be offended — nor would anyone else, except the historian. If Livy says the gods once made a cow speak, and we don't believe it, we are not distrusting God; we are distrusting Livy.

So the matter is plain: whatever we believe on no other grounds than the authority of men and their writings — whether those men were sent from God or not — is faith in men only.

Chapter 8: The Virtues Commonly Called Intellectual, and Their Opposite Defects

Intellectual Virtue Defined

Virtue, in every kind of subject, is something valued for standing out. It exists by comparison. If everyone had every quality in equal measure, nothing would be prized in anyone.

By INTELLECTUAL VIRTUES, we always mean those abilities of the mind that people praise, value, and wish they had themselves. They usually go under the name of a good wit — though the word "wit" is also used to single out one particular ability from the rest.

Wit: Natural or Acquired

These virtues come in two sorts: natural and acquired.

By "natural," I do not mean what a man has from birth. What we are born with is nothing but sense, and in sense men differ so little from one another — and from brute beasts — that it cannot be counted among the virtues. I mean instead the wit that is gained by use and experience alone, without method, training, or instruction.

This NATURAL WIT consists mainly of two things: quickness of imagining — that is, a swift movement from one thought to the next — and steady direction toward some chosen goal.

The opposite, a slow imagination, makes the defect of mind commonly called DULLNESS, stupidity, and sometimes other names that signify slowness of motion, or difficulty in being moved at all.

Good Wit, or Fancy; Good Judgment; Discretion

This difference in quickness comes from the difference in men's passions. People love and dislike different things, so their thoughts run in different directions, and they hold onto and notice different things as they pass through the imagination.

Now, when thoughts follow one another, there are really only a few things to notice about them: how they are alike, how they are unlike, what they are good for, or how they serve a particular purpose.

Those who notice the likenesses between things — especially likenesses that others rarely spot — are said to have a good wit, by which, in this case, we mean a good FANCY: a creative quickness of imagination.

Those who notice the differences and dissimilarities — which is called distinguishing, discerning, and judging between thing and thing — are said, when such discerning is not easy, to have a good JUDGMENT. And in matters of conversation and business in particular, where you must read the times, the places, and the people, this virtue is called DISCRETION.

The first of these, fancy without the help of judgment, is not praised as a virtue. But the second — judgment, and discretion — is praised for its own sake, with or without any fancy to assist it.

Besides the discretion about times, places, and persons that a good fancy requires, something else is needed too: a frequent steering of one's thoughts toward their end — that is, toward some use to be made of them. A man who does this will find himself easily supplied with comparisons that please, not only because they illustrate his speech and decorate it with new and fitting metaphors, but because of the sheer rarity of their invention.

But without steadiness and direction toward some end, a great fancy is one kind of madness. You see it in people who, entering into any conversation, get snatched away from their point by every thought that crosses their mind, into so many digressions, and such long ones, and so many parentheses, that they completely lose themselves. I know of no particular name for this kind of folly. Its cause is sometimes lack of experience, which makes a thing seem new and remarkable to one man that seems ordinary to everyone else; sometimes it is small-mindedness, which makes something seem important to him that other men consider a trifle. And whatever seems new or important — and therefore worth telling — pulls a man, step by step, away from the road his talk was supposed to travel.

In a good poem, whether epic or dramatic, and likewise in sonnets, epigrams, and other pieces, both judgment and fancy are required — but the fancy must be the more prominent, because such works please by their extravagance. They simply must not offend through indiscretion.

In a good history, judgment must be the more prominent, because the goodness of a history lies in its method, its truth, and its choice of the actions most worth knowing. Fancy has no place there except in dressing up the style.

In speeches of praise, and in attacks and invectives, fancy dominates, because the goal is not truth but honoring or dishonoring someone — which is done through noble or vile comparisons. Judgment only supplies the circumstances that make an action praiseworthy or blameworthy.

In exhortations and in courtroom pleadings, judgment or fancy is most needed depending on whether truth or disguise serves the purpose at hand.

In demonstration, in counsel, and in every rigorous search for truth, judgment does everything — except that sometimes the understanding needs to be opened up by an apt comparison, and then there is that much use for fancy. But metaphors, in this case, are utterly excluded. Since metaphors openly profess deceit, to admit them into counsel or reasoning would be obvious folly.

And in any discourse whatsoever, if a lack of discretion shows, the whole performance will be taken as a sign of poor wit, no matter how extravagant the fancy. And the reverse holds too: where the discretion is obvious, the discourse will never be taken for witless, however ordinary the fancy.

The secret thoughts of a man range over everything — holy, profane, clean, obscene, grave, and frivolous — without shame or blame. Spoken discourse cannot do that; it can go only as far as judgment approves of the time, the place, and the company. An anatomist or a physician may speak or write his professional opinion about unclean things, because his purpose is not to please but to be useful. But for another man to publish his extravagant and entertaining fancies about the same subjects is like a man who has been tumbled in the mud coming to present himself in good company. The difference is nothing but a lack of discretion.

Likewise, in openly relaxed moods and among familiar company, a man may play with the sounds and double meanings of words, and often with flashes of extraordinary fancy. But in a sermon, or in public, or before strangers, or before people we ought to respect, there is no jingling wordplay that will not be counted as folly. And again, the only difference is the lack of discretion.

So where wit is lacking, it is not fancy that is missing, but discretion. Judgment without fancy is wit; fancy without judgment is not.

Prudence

When a man with some project in hand runs his thoughts over a multitude of things and observes how they contribute to that project — or what project they might contribute to — and when his observations are neither easy nor common, this wit of his is called prudence. It depends on a great deal of experience, and on memory of similar things and their consequences in the past.

In prudence there is not as much difference between men as there is in their fancies and judgments. The experience of men of equal age is not very unequal in quantity; it simply lies in different fields, since everyone has his own private business. Governing a household well and governing a kingdom well are not different degrees of prudence — they are different sorts of business, just as painting a miniature and painting a portrait at life size or larger are not different degrees of art. A plain farmer is more prudent in the affairs of his own house than a privy counselor is in the affairs of another man.

Craft

Add to prudence the use of unjust or dishonest means — the kind that fear or poverty usually suggests to men — and you get that crooked wisdom called CRAFT, which is a sign of small-mindedness. For magnanimity, greatness of mind, is precisely the contempt for unjust or dishonest shortcuts.

And what the Romans called versutia — in English, "shifting" — is the trick of escaping a present danger or difficulty by getting into a bigger one, as when a man robs one person to pay another. It is just a shorter-sighted craft. The name comes from versura, which means borrowing money at interest to pay the interest you already owe.

Acquired Wit

As for acquired wit — I mean wit acquired by method and instruction — there is only one kind: reason. Reason is grounded in the right use of speech, and it produces the sciences. But I have already spoken of reason and science in the fifth and sixth chapters.

The causes of these differences in wit lie in the passions, and the differences in the passions come partly from men's different bodily constitutions and partly from their different upbringings. If the difference came from the makeup of the brain and the organs of sense, exterior or interior, men would differ as much in their sight and hearing and other senses as they do in their fancies and discretions. So the difference comes from the passions — which vary not only with men's physical temperaments but also with their differences of custom and education.

The passions that most of all cause differences of wit are, principally, a greater or lesser desire for power, for riches, for knowledge, and for honor. And all of these can be reduced to the first — the desire for power. For riches, knowledge, and honor are just different kinds of power.

Giddiness; Madness

So a man who has no great passion for any of these things — who is, as people say, indifferent — may well be a good man in the sense that he gives no offense, but he cannot possibly have either a great fancy or much judgment. For the thoughts are to the desires what scouts and spies are to an army: they range abroad and find the way to the things desired. All steadiness of the mind's motion, and all its quickness, comes from that.

To have no desires is to be dead. To have weak passions is dullness. To have passions spread indifferently across everything is GIDDINESS and distraction. And to have stronger, more vehement passions for something than is ordinarily seen in other men — that is what men call MADNESS.

Of madness there are almost as many kinds as there are passions. Sometimes the extraordinary and extravagant passion comes from a bad constitution of the body's organs, or from damage done to them; and sometimes the damage and disorder of the organs is caused by the vehemence or long persistence of the passion itself. But in both cases the madness is of one and the same nature.

The passion whose violence or persistence produces madness is either great vainglory — commonly called pride and self-conceit — or great dejection of mind.

Rage

Pride makes a man prone to anger, and the excess of anger is the madness called RAGE and FURY. And so it happens that an excessive desire for revenge, once it becomes habitual, injures the organs and turns into rage. Excessive love, joined with jealousy, also becomes rage. An excessive opinion of oneself — as divinely inspired, or as wise, learned, handsome, and so on — becomes distraction and giddiness; the same opinion joined with envy becomes rage. And a vehement conviction of the truth of anything, when contradicted by others — rage.

Melancholy

Dejection makes a man prone to groundless fears, which is the madness commonly called MELANCHOLY. It too shows itself in various ways: in haunting lonely places and graves, in superstitious behavior, in fearing some particular thing — one man one thing, another man another.

In sum, all passions that produce strange and unusual behavior go by the general name of madness. As for the particular kinds of madness, anyone who cared to take the trouble could enroll a legion of them. And if the excesses are madness, there is no doubt that the passions themselves, when they tend toward evil, are degrees of the same thing.

Take an example. The effect of folly in people possessed by an opinion of being divinely inspired may not always show itself in any one man through some wildly extravagant action. But when many of them conspire together, the rage of the whole multitude is visible enough. What greater proof of madness could there be than to clamor against, strike, and throw stones at our best friends? Yet a multitude like that will do something even worse: they will clamor against, fight, and destroy the very people who, all their lives before, protected them and kept them safe from injury. And if this is madness in the multitude, it is the same madness in every individual man.

Think of it this way. In the middle of the sea, a man hears no sound from the patch of water right beside him — yet he can be quite sure that patch contributes as much to the roaring of the sea as any other patch of the same size. In the same way, though we may notice no great disturbance in one or two individual men, we can be quite sure their individual passions are parts of the seditious roaring of a troubled nation.

And even if nothing else betrayed their madness, the very act of claiming such inspiration for themselves is proof enough. If a man in Bedlam — the madhouse — entertained you with perfectly sober conversation, and as you took your leave you asked who he was, so you could repay his courtesy another time, and he told you he was God the Father, I think you would need to wait for no extravagant action to be sure of his madness.

This opinion of inspiration, commonly called the "private spirit," very often begins with a man's lucky discovery of an error that everyone else holds. Not knowing — or not remembering — by what chain of reasoning he arrived at this singular truth (as he thinks it is, though it is often an untruth he has stumbled onto), he immediately admires himself as someone in the special grace of God Almighty, who has revealed this to him supernaturally by his Spirit.

Again, that madness is nothing other than passion showing itself too plainly can be gathered from the effects of wine, which are the same as the effects of disordered organs. The variety of behavior in men who have drunk too much is exactly the variety found in madmen: some raging, some loving, some laughing — all extravagantly, each according to whichever passion dominates in him. For all the wine does is strip away dissimulation, and take from them the sight of how deformed their passions look.

For I believe the most sober of men, when they walk alone with nothing to occupy or discipline the mind, would be unwilling for the vanity and extravagance of their thoughts in those moments to be publicly seen. Which is a confession that passions, left unguided, are for the most part sheer madness.

The world's opinions about the cause of madness, in ancient times and in later ones, have been two. Some derived madness from the passions. Others derived it from demons or spirits, good or bad, which they believed could enter a man, possess him, and move his organs in the strange and uncouth way that madmen move. The first sort, accordingly, called such men madmen. The second sort called them sometimes demoniacs (that is, possessed by spirits), sometimes energumeni (that is, agitated or moved by spirits). And in Italy today they are called not only pazzi, madmen, but also spiritati — men possessed.

There was once a great crowd gathered at Abdera, a Greek city, for a performance of the tragedy of Andromeda, on an extremely hot day. A great many of the spectators fell into fevers, and the heat and the tragedy together produced this odd result: they did nothing but recite iambic verses full of the names of Perseus and Andromeda. The madness, along with the fever, was cured by the coming of winter — and this madness was thought to come from the passion imprinted on them by the tragedy.

Likewise, a fit of madness once swept through another Greek city, seizing only the young women, and caused many of them to hang themselves. Most people at the time thought it the work of the Devil. But one man suspected that this contempt for life might come from some passion of the mind, and reckoned that the young women did not hold their honor in the same contempt. So he advised the magistrates to strip the bodies of those who hanged themselves and let them hang out naked. This, the story says, cured the madness.

But on the other side, those same Greeks often attributed madness to the operation of the Eumenides, the Furies — and sometimes of Ceres, Phoebus, and other gods. So much did men credit phantasms that they took them for living aerial bodies, and gave them the general name of spirits. And as the Romans held the same opinion as the Greeks, so did the Jews. They called madmen prophets, or, depending on whether they thought the spirits good or bad, demoniacs. Some of them called both prophets and demoniacs madmen, and some called the same man both demoniac and madman.

For the pagans of the past, this is no surprise, since diseases and health, vices and virtues, and many natural accidents were named and worshipped by them as demons — so that by "demon" a man might understand a fever as easily as a devil. But for the Jews to hold such an opinion is somewhat strange. For neither Moses nor Abraham claimed to prophesy by possession of a spirit; they prophesied from the voice of God, or by a vision or dream. Nor is there anything in their law, moral or ceremonial, by which they were taught that there was any such thing as inspiration-by-possession, or possession at all.

When God is said (Numbers 11:25) to take from the spirit that was in Moses and give it to the seventy elders, the Spirit of God — if we take it for the substance of God — is not something that can be divided up. What the Scriptures mean by the Spirit of God in a man is the man's own spirit, inclined to godliness. And where it is said (Exodus 28:3), "whom I have filled with the spirit of wisdom to make garments for Aaron," it does not mean a spirit inserted into them that can sew garments; it means the wisdom of their own spirits in that kind of work.

In the same sense, the spirit of a man, when it produces unclean actions, is ordinarily called an unclean spirit; and so with other "spirits" — not always, but whenever the virtue or vice in question is extraordinary and conspicuous. Nor did the other prophets of the Old Testament claim possession-style inspiration, or claim that God spoke in them. God spoke to them — by voice, vision, or dream. And the "burden of the Lord" was not possession; it was command.

How then could the Jews fall into this opinion of possession? I can imagine no reason except the one common to all mankind: the lack of curiosity to search out natural causes, and the habit of placing happiness in acquiring the coarse pleasures of the senses and whatever leads most directly to them. For when people see some strange and unusual ability or defect in a man's mind, and cannot also see what cause it might naturally come from, they can hardly believe it natural. And if not natural, it must, they think, be supernatural. And then what can it be, but that either God or the Devil is in him?

That is how it came about that when our Savior (Mark 3:21) was pressed by the multitude, those of his own household suspected he was mad, and went out to restrain him — while the scribes said he had Beelzebub, and that this was how he cast out devils: as if the greater madman overawed the lesser. And it is how (John 10:20) some said, "He has a devil, and is mad," while others, holding him for a prophet, said, "These are not the words of one who has a devil." So too in the Old Testament: the man who came to anoint Jehu (2 Kings 9:11) was a prophet, but some of the company asked Jehu, "What did that madman come for?"

In sum, it is clear that whoever behaved in an extraordinary way was thought by the Jews to be possessed by either a good or an evil spirit — except by the Sadducees, who erred so far in the other direction as not to believe there were any spirits at all (which comes very near to outright atheism), and who thereby, perhaps, only provoked others all the more to call such men demoniacs rather than madmen.

But why, then, does our Savior go about curing them as if they were possessed, and not as if they were mad? I can give no other kind of answer than the one given to those who press Scripture the same way against the motion of the Earth. The Scripture was written to show men the kingdom of God, and to prepare their minds to become his obedient subjects — leaving the world, and the philosophy of the world, to the disputation of men, for the exercise of their natural reason. Whether it is the Earth's motion or the Sun's that makes day and night, and whether the wild actions of men come from passion or from the Devil (so long as we do not worship him), makes no difference at all to our obedience and subjection to God Almighty — which is the thing the Scripture was written for.

As for our Savior speaking to the disease as if to a person: that is the usual manner of all who cure by words alone, as Christ did (and as enchanters pretend to do, whether they speak to a devil or not). Is not Christ also said (Matthew 8:26) to have rebuked the winds? Is he not said (Luke 4:39) to rebuke a fever? Yet that does not prove a fever is a devil.

And where many of these devils are said to confess Christ, there is no need to interpret those passages as anything more than that those madmen confessed him. And where our Savior (Matthew 12:43) speaks of an unclean spirit that goes out of a man, wanders through dry places seeking rest and finding none, and returns into the same man with seven other spirits worse than himself — that is plainly a parable, alluding to a man who makes a small effort to quit his lusts, is defeated by their strength, and ends up seven times worse than he was.

So I see nothing at all in Scripture that requires us to believe demoniacs were anything other than madmen.

Insignificant Speech

There is yet another fault in the discourses of some men which may also be numbered among the kinds of madness: the abuse of words I spoke of earlier, in the fifth chapter, under the name of absurdity. This is when men speak words which, put together, have no signification at all. Some fall into it by misunderstanding words they have received and now repeat by rote; others do it deliberately, intending to deceive through obscurity.

This affliction strikes no one but those who traffic in questions about incomprehensible matters, like the Scholastics, or in questions of abstruse philosophy. The common run of men seldom speak insignificantly — and are therefore counted idiots by those distinguished persons. But to be sure their words have nothing in the mind corresponding to them, we need some examples. If any man requires one, let him take a Scholastic into his hands and see whether he can translate any single chapter on any difficult point — the Trinity, the Deity, the nature of Christ, transubstantiation, free will, and so on — into any modern language so as to make it intelligible, or into any tolerable Latin of the kind familiar to those who lived when Latin was a living, common tongue.

What is the meaning of these words: "The first cause does not necessarily inflow anything into the second, by force of the essential subordination of the second causes, by which it may help it to work"? That is the translation of the title of the sixth chapter of Suárez's first book, Of the Concourse, Motion, and Help of God. When men write whole volumes of such stuff, are they not mad — or trying to make others so?

And take in particular the question of transubstantiation. After certain words have been spoken, there are men who say that the white-ness, the round-ness, the magni-tude, the quali-ty, the corruptibili-ty — all of them incorporeal, and so on — go out of the wafer and into the body of our blessed Savior. Do they not make those nesses, tudes, and ties into so many spirits possessing his body? For by "spirits" they always mean things that, while incorporeal, can nevertheless be moved from one place to another.

So this kind of absurdity may rightly be counted among the many sorts of madness. And all the time these men spend guided by the clear thoughts of their worldly ambition — when they refrain from disputing and writing this way — is nothing but their lucid intervals.

And so much for the intellectual virtues and defects.

Chapter 9: The Different Branches of Knowledge

There are two kinds of KNOWLEDGE. One is knowledge of fact. The other is knowledge of the consequence of one statement to another.

The first kind is nothing more than sense and memory, and it is absolute knowledge — as when we see something happening, or remember seeing it done. This is the knowledge required of a witness.

The second kind is called science, and it is conditional — as when we know that if the figure shown is a circle, then any straight line through the center will divide it into two equal parts. This is the knowledge required of a philosopher — that is, of anyone who claims to reason.

The record of knowledge of fact is called HISTORY, and it comes in two sorts. One is natural history: the history of facts, or effects of nature, that do not depend on human will — histories of metals, plants, animals, regions, and the like. The other is civil history: the history of the voluntary actions of people living in commonwealths.

The records of science are the books that contain demonstrations of how one statement follows from another. These are commonly called books of philosophy, and they come in many sorts, depending on the variety of their subject matter. They may be divided as I have divided them in the following table.

SCIENCE — that is, knowledge of consequences, which is also called PHILOSOPHY:

A. Consequences from the properties of natural bodies — which is called NATURAL PHILOSOPHY.

   1. Consequences from the properties common to all natural bodies, which are quantity and motion.
      a. Consequences from quantity and motion in the abstract — indeterminate, not yet tied to any particular thing. Because these are the principles, the first foundation of philosophy, this is called philosophia prima:
         FIRST PHILOSOPHY
      b. Consequences from motion and quantity determined — pinned down to particular cases.
         1) Consequences from quantity and motion determined by figure and by number — the mathematical sciences:
            GEOMETRY
            ARITHMETIC
         2) Consequences from the motion and quantity of particular kinds of bodies.
            a) Consequences from the motion and quantity of the great parts of the world, such as the earth and the stars — cosmography:
               ASTRONOMY
               GEOGRAPHY
            b) Consequences from the motion of particular kinds and shapes of bodies — mechanics, the doctrine of weight — the science of:
               ENGINEERS
               ARCHITECTURE
               NAVIGATION
   2. PHYSICS — consequences from qualities.
      a. Consequences from the qualities of transient bodies, the kind that sometimes appear and sometimes vanish:
         METEOROLOGY
      b. Consequences from the qualities of permanent bodies.
         1) Consequences from the qualities of the stars.
            a) Consequences from the light of the stars. From this, together with the motion of the sun, is made the science of:
               SCIOGRAPHY — the science of shadows, used for telling time
            b) Consequences from the influence of the stars:
               ASTROLOGY
         2) Consequences from the qualities of the liquid bodies that fill the space between the stars — such as the air, or the ethereal substance.
         3) Consequences from the qualities of earthly bodies.
            a) Consequences from the parts of the earth that have no sense.
               1] Consequences from the qualities of minerals, such as stones, metals, and so on.
               2] Consequences from the qualities of plants.
            b) Consequences from the qualities of animals.
               1] Consequences from the qualities of animals in general.
                  a] Consequences from vision:
                     OPTICS
                  b] Consequences from sounds:
                     MUSIC
                  c] Consequences from the rest of the senses.
               2] Consequences from the qualities of human beings in particular.
                  a] Consequences from the passions of men:
                     ETHICS
                  b] Consequences from speech.
                     i) In magnifying, vilifying, and the like:
                        POETRY
                     ii) In persuading:
                        RHETORIC
                     iii) In reasoning:
                        LOGIC
                     iv) In contracting — in making agreements — the science of:
                        JUST and UNJUST

B. Consequences from the properties of political bodies — which is called POLITICS, or CIVIL PHILOSOPHY.

   1. Consequences from the institution of commonwealths, concerning the rights and duties of the body politic, or sovereign.
   2. Consequences from the same, concerning the duties and rights of the subjects.

Chapter 10: Of Power, Worth, Dignity, Honor, and Worthiness

Power

The POWER of a man, taken in the most general sense, is his present means to obtain some future apparent good. Power is either original or instrumental.

Natural power is the excellence of body or mind: exceptional strength, good looks, prudence, skill in the arts, eloquence, generosity, noble birth. Instrumental powers are the ones acquired through those natural gifts, or through luck, and they serve as means and instruments to acquire still more — riches, reputation, friends, and the secret working of God, which men call good luck. For power, in this respect, behaves like fame: it grows as it goes. Or like a falling object, which picks up speed the farther it travels.

The greatest of human powers is the kind compounded out of the powers of many men, united by consent in one person — natural or artificial — who has the use of all their powers at his command. That is the power of a commonwealth. Or it may depend on the will of each individual member, like the power of a faction, or of several factions in league. So to have servants is power. To have friends is power. They are strengths united.

Riches joined with generosity are power, because they buy friends and servants. Riches without generosity are not — because in that case wealth does not defend its owner; it just exposes him to envy, like prey.

A reputation for power is itself power, because it attracts the loyalty of people who need protection.

So is a reputation for loving one's country — what we call popularity — and for the same reason.

In fact, any quality that makes a man loved or feared by many people, or even the reputation of such a quality, is power, because it is a means of getting the help and service of many.

Success is power, because it builds a reputation for wisdom or good fortune, which makes men either fear you or rely on you.

Friendliness in those who already hold power increases their power, because it wins love.

A reputation for prudence in the conduct of peace or war is power, because we hand over the government of ourselves more willingly to prudent men than to others.

Noble birth is power — not everywhere, but only in those commonwealths where nobility carries privileges. The power consists in the privileges.

Eloquence is power, because it looks like prudence.

Good looks are power, because beauty is a promise of good things to come, and it recommends men to the favor of women and strangers.

The sciences are only a small power, because science is not eminent — it does not stand out, so nobody gives any one man credit for it. And it exists in only a few people, and even in them, only about a few subjects. For science has this peculiar nature: no one can recognize that it exists at all unless he has already mastered a good share of it himself.

Arts of public use — fortification, the making of engines and other instruments of war — are power, because they contribute to defense and victory. And though the true mother of these arts is science, namely mathematics, they get credited to the craftsman who brings them into the light. With the common crowd, the midwife passes for the mother.

Worth

The value, or WORTH, of a man is — as with everything else — his price: that is, however much would be given for the use of his power. So it is not absolute, but depends on the need and judgment of someone else. An able commander of soldiers commands a great price in wartime, or when war threatens — but not in peace. A learned and honest judge is worth a great deal in peacetime, but less in war. And with men, as with everything else, it is not the seller who sets the price, but the buyer. Let a man rate himself at the highest value he can — and most men do — his true value is still no more than what others judge it to be.

The way we show the value we place on one another is what is commonly called honoring and dishonoring. To value a man at a high rate is to honor him; at a low rate, to dishonor him. But "high" and "low" here have to be measured against the rate each man sets on himself.

Dignity

The public worth of a man — the value placed on him by the commonwealth — is what men commonly call DIGNITY. And this valuation by the commonwealth shows itself in offices of command, judgeships, public employment, or in names and titles introduced to mark such value.

To Honor and Dishonor

To ask another for help of any kind is to HONOR him, because it is a sign that we believe he has the power to help; and the harder the help asked for, the greater the honor.

To obey is to honor, because nobody obeys someone they think has no power to help or hurt them. And so, to disobey is to dishonor.

To give great gifts to a man is to honor him, because it is buying protection and acknowledging power. To give small gifts is to dishonor, because that is mere charity, and it signals an opinion that he needs only small assistance. To work hard at promoting another's good, and to flatter him, is to honor — a sign that we seek his protection or aid. To neglect him is to dishonor.

To give way, or yield place to another in any advantage, is to honor — an admission of greater power. To grab precedence for yourself is to dishonor.

To show any sign of love or fear of another is to honor, for both to love and to fear is to value. To despise someone, or to love or fear him less than he expects, is to dishonor — it is undervaluing.

To praise a man, to magnify him, to call him happy, is to honor him, because nothing is valued but goodness, power, and happiness. To mock, revile, or pity him is to dishonor.

To speak to another with consideration, to appear before him with decency and humility, is to honor him — these are signs of fear of offending. To speak to him rashly, or to do anything obscene, sloppy, or impudent in his presence, is to dishonor.

To believe, to trust, to rely on another is to honor him — a sign of confidence in his virtue and power. To distrust or disbelieve him is to dishonor.

To listen to a man's counsel, or to his talk of any kind, is to honor — a sign that we think him wise, or eloquent, or witty. To fall asleep, walk out, or chat while he speaks is to dishonor.

To do for another those things he takes for signs of honor, or that law or custom makes signs of honor, is to honor him; because in approving the honor done by others, we acknowledge the power that others acknowledge. To refuse to do them is to dishonor.

To agree with a man's opinion is to honor him — a sign of approving his judgment and wisdom. To dissent is to dishonor, and amounts to accusing him of error — and if you dissent from him on many things, of folly.

To imitate is to honor, for imitation is the most emphatic form of approval. To imitate a man's enemy is to dishonor him.

To honor those whom another honors is to honor him — a sign of approval of his judgment. To honor his enemies is to dishonor him.

To employ a man in counsel, or in difficult undertakings, is to honor him — a sign of confidence in his wisdom or other power. To deny such employment to those who seek it is to dishonor.

All these ways of honoring are natural, and they hold both inside and outside commonwealths. But inside commonwealths, where the holder of supreme authority can make anything he pleases stand for a sign of honor, there are other honors as well.

A sovereign honors a subject with any title, office, employment, or action whatsoever that he himself chooses to count as a sign of his will to honor him.

The king of Persia honored Mordecai when he ordered him led through the streets in the king's robes, on one of the king's horses, with a crown on his head and a prince walking ahead of him proclaiming, "Thus shall it be done to him whom the king will honor." And yet another king of Persia — or the same one on another occasion — granted a man who asked, as reward for some great service, permission to wear one of the king's robes. Permission granted, said the king — but he must wear it as the king's fool. And that turned it into dishonor. So much for civil honor: things like magistracies, offices, titles, and in some places painted coats of arms. Men honor those who have them, as carrying so many signs of favor in the commonwealth — and that favor is power.

Honorable is whatever possession, action, or quality serves as evidence and sign of power.

And therefore to be honored, loved, or feared by many is honorable, as evidence of power. To be honored by few or none is dishonorable.

Good fortune, if it lasts, is honorable, as a sign of the favor of God. Bad fortune and losses are dishonorable. Riches are honorable, for they are power. Poverty is dishonorable. Magnanimity, generosity, hope, courage, confidence are honorable, for they spring from an awareness of one's own power. Small-mindedness, stinginess, fear, and self-distrust are dishonorable.

Deciding promptly — settling on what to do in good time — is honorable, because it shows contempt for small difficulties and dangers. Indecision is dishonorable, as a sign of overvaluing little obstacles and little advantages. For when a man has weighed his options as long as time allows and still cannot decide, the difference between them must be slight; so if he fails to decide, he is overvaluing trifles — which is small-mindedness.

All actions and speeches that come, or seem to come, from much experience, science, discretion, or wit are honorable, for all these are powers. Actions or words that come from error, ignorance, or folly are dishonorable.

Gravity — a serious manner — is honorable insofar as it seems to come from a mind occupied with something else, because being occupied is a sign of power. But if it seems to come from a deliberate effort to look grave, it is dishonorable. The gravity of the first kind is like the steadiness of a ship loaded with merchandise; the second, like the steadiness of a ship ballasted with sand and other junk.

To be conspicuous — that is, to be widely known for wealth, office, great deeds, or any eminent good — is honorable, as a sign of the power for which one is conspicuous. Obscurity, by contrast, is dishonorable.

To be descended from famous parents is honorable, because such men more easily inherit the connections and friends of their ancestors. To be descended from obscure parentage is, by contrast, dishonorable.

Actions that spring from fairness, even at a cost to oneself, are honorable, as signs of magnanimity — and magnanimity is a sign of power. By contrast, craft, shifty dealing, and disregard of fairness are dishonorable.

And here is the uncomfortable part: it makes no difference to honor whether an action is just or unjust, so long as it is great and difficult — and therefore a sign of much power. For honor consists only in the opinion of power. That is why the ancient pagans did not think they were dishonoring the gods, but greatly honoring them, when they put them in their poems committing rapes, thefts, and other great but unjust or unclean acts. Nothing about Jupiter is celebrated so much as his adulteries; nothing about Mercury so much as his frauds and thefts. In one of Homer's hymns, the highest praise given to Mercury is this: that born in the morning, he had invented music by noon, and before nightfall had stolen the cattle of Apollo away from his herdsmen.

Among men, too, until great commonwealths were established, it was considered no dishonor to be a pirate or a highway robber — rather a lawful trade, not only among the Greeks but among all other nations, as the histories of ancient times make plain. And to this day, in this part of the world, private duels are honorable, and always will be, however unlawful — until the day honor is established for those who refuse a challenge, and disgrace for those who issue one. For duels are often genuine effects of courage, and the basis of courage is always strength or skill, which are power. Though for the most part they are the effects of rash talk, and of the fear of dishonor in one or both of the fighters — who, having committed themselves by their own rashness, are driven into the dueling ground to avoid disgrace.

Coats of arms and hereditary heraldry are honorable where they carry eminent privileges; otherwise not. For their power consists either in those privileges, or in riches, or in some other thing equally honored in other men. This kind of honor, commonly called gentry, descends from the ancient Germans. No such thing was ever known where German customs were unknown, and it is not in use anywhere today where Germans have not settled. The ancient Greek commanders, when they went to war, painted their shields with whatever designs they pleased — so much so that an unpainted shield was a sign of poverty and of a common soldier. But they did not pass these designs down as inheritance. The Romans handed down the marks of their families, but those were the portraits of their ancestors, not emblems. Among the peoples of Asia, Africa, and America there is not, and never was, any such thing. The Germans alone had that custom, and from them it spread into England, France, Spain, and Italy, when in great numbers they either fought as allies of the Romans or carved out their own conquests in these western parts of the world.

For Germany was anciently — like all other countries at their beginnings — divided among countless little lords, or masters of families, who waged continual war on one another. These masters, chiefly so that they could be recognized by their followers when they were covered in armor, and partly for ornament, painted their armor, or their shield, or their coat with the picture of some beast or other device, and also fixed some prominent, visible mark on the crest of their helmets. This ornament of arms and crest descended by inheritance to their children — to the eldest unchanged, and to the rest with some mark of difference, whatever the old master (in German, the Here-alt — the source of our word "herald") thought fit. When many such families joined together into a greater monarchy, this duty of the herald, to keep the coats of arms distinct, became a separate private office. The descendants of those lords are the great and ancient gentry, whose arms mostly display living creatures noted for courage and plunder, or castles, battlements, belts, weapons, bars, palisades, and other emblems of war — for in those days nothing was honored but military virtue. Later on, not only kings but popular commonwealths granted various coats of arms to those who marched off to war or returned from it, as encouragement or reward for their service. All of this an observant reader can find in those ancient Greek and Latin histories that mention the German nation and its customs.

Titles of Honor

Titles of honor, such as duke, count, marquis, and baron, are honorable, as signifying the value set on their holders by the sovereign power of the commonwealth. These titles were in old times titles of office and command, derived some from the Romans, some from the Germans and French. Dukes — in Latin duces — were generals in war. Counts — comites, "companions" — were those who kept the general company out of friendship, and were left behind to govern and defend conquered and pacified territory. Marquises — marchiones — were counts who governed the marches, the borderlands of the empire. These titles of duke, count, and marquis came into the empire around the time of Constantine the Great, from the customs of the German military. But baron seems to have been a title of the Gauls, meaning a great man — the kind of men the kings or princes employed in war around their persons. It seems to derive from vir — Latin for "man" — becoming ber and bar, which meant in the language of the Gauls what vir meant in Latin; thence to bero and baro, so that such men were called berones, and afterward barones, and in Spanish varones. Anyone who wants the origins of titles of honor in more detail can find them, as I found these, in Mr. Selden's most excellent treatise on the subject. In the course of time, these offices of honor — through political trouble, and for reasons of good and peaceful government — were turned into mere titles, serving mostly to mark the precedence, place, and rank of subjects in the commonwealth. Men were made dukes, counts, marquises, and barons of places where they had neither property nor command, and still other titles were invented for the same purpose.

Worthiness and Fitness

WORTHINESS is a different thing from the worth or value of a man, and different too from his merit or desert. It consists in a particular power or ability for the thing he is said to be worthy of — and that particular ability is usually called FITNESS, or aptitude.

For the worthiest man to be a commander, a judge, or to hold any other office, is the one best equipped with the qualities required to do the job well; and the worthiest of riches is the one with the qualities most needed for using riches well. Lacking any of these qualities, a man may still be a worthy man — valuable for something else. Then again, a man may be worthy of riches, office, and employment and yet have no claim to receive them ahead of anyone else, and so cannot be said to merit or deserve them. For merit presupposes a right — that the thing deserved is owed by promise. Of that I shall say more later, when I come to speak of contracts.

Chapter 11: The Difference of Manners

What "Manners" Means Here

By MANNERS, I do not mean politeness — how one person should greet another, or whether a man should rinse his mouth or pick his teeth in company, and other points of the small morals. I mean those qualities of mankind that concern our living together in peace and unity.

To see what those qualities are, start here: the happiness of this life does not consist in the repose of a satisfied mind. There is no Finis Ultimus — final goal — and no Summum Bonum — greatest good — of the kind described in the books of the old moral philosophers. A man whose desires have come to an end can no more live than a man whose senses and imagination have come to a standstill. Happiness is a continual progress of desire from one object to another, where getting the first thing is only the road to getting the next.

Why? Because the object of a man's desire is not to enjoy something once, for one instant of time, but to secure forever the way to his future desires. So the voluntary actions and inclinations of all men aim not only at obtaining a contented life, but at making it secure. Men differ only in how they go about it — partly because passions differ from person to person, and partly because each person has different knowledge, or different opinions, about which causes produce the effect he wants.

A Restless Desire for Power, in All Mankind

So in the first place, I put forward as a general inclination of all mankind a perpetual and restless desire of power after power, that ceaseth only in death.

The cause of this is not always that a man hopes for a more intense delight than he has already attained, or that he cannot be content with a moderate power. It is that he cannot secure the power and means to live well that he has right now without acquiring more. That is why kings, whose power is greatest, turn their efforts to securing it — at home by laws, abroad by wars. And when that is done, a new desire takes its place: in some, fame from new conquests; in others, ease and sensual pleasure; in others, admiration, or being flattered for excellence in some art or other ability of the mind.

Competition Breeds Conflict

Competition for riches, honor, command, or any other power inclines men toward contention, enmity, and war — because the way for one competitor to get what he desires is to kill, subdue, supplant, or repel the other. Competition for praise, in particular, inclines men toward reverence for antiquity. Men compete with the living, not the dead — so they credit the dead with more than they deserve, in order to dim the glory of their living rivals.

Love of Ease Makes Men Obey a Common Power

Desire for ease and sensual pleasure disposes men to obey a common power, because such desires make a man give up the protection he might otherwise have to win by his own industry and labor.

So Does Fear of Death or Wounds

Fear of death and wounds disposes men the same way, and for the same reason. On the other hand, needy and hardy men who are not content with their present condition — along with all men ambitious for military command — are inclined to keep the causes of war alive, and to stir up trouble and sedition. There is no military honor except through war, and no better hope of mending a bad game than forcing a new shuffle of the cards.

And So Does Love of Knowledge and the Arts of Peace

Desire for knowledge, and for the arts of peace, inclines men to obey a common power. Such a desire contains a desire for leisure — and therefore for protection by some power other than one's own.

Love of Praise Inspires Praiseworthy Action

Desire for praise disposes men to praiseworthy actions — the kind that please the people whose judgment they value; for when we despise a man, we despise his praise as well. Desire for fame after death does the same. True, after death there is no sense of the praise given us on earth — those joys are either swallowed up in the unspeakable joys of heaven or extinguished in the extreme torments of hell. Yet such fame is not pointless. Men take a present delight in foreseeing it, and in foreseeing the benefit that may pass from it to their descendants. They do not see that benefit now, but they imagine it — and whatever is pleasure to the senses is pleasure in the imagination too.

Hatred, from Benefits Too Great to Repay

To receive from someone we consider our equal greater benefits than we can ever hope to repay disposes us to counterfeit love — and to real, secret hatred. It puts a man in the position of a desperate debtor who avoids the sight of his creditor and quietly wishes him somewhere he will never be seen again. Benefits oblige; obligation is bondage; and bondage to an equal is hateful.

But to receive benefits from someone we acknowledge as our superior inclines us to love, because the obligation is no new humiliation; and cheerful acceptance (which men call gratitude) is such an honor done to the giver that it generally counts as repayment. Receiving benefits from an equal, or even an inferior, also disposes us to love — as long as there is hope of repaying them. For then the receiver intends the obligation as one of mutual aid and service, and from that comes a competition over who shall out-give the other: the noblest and most profitable contest possible, in which the winner is pleased with his victory, and the loser gets his revenge by admitting it.

And from Knowing You Deserve to Be Hated

To have done a man more harm than you can make up for, or are willing to, inclines you to hate the one you have hurt. For you must expect either his revenge or his forgiveness — and both are hateful.

Fear Makes Men Strike First

Fear of oppression disposes a man to strike first, or to seek safety by joining with others. There is no other way a man can secure his life and liberty.

So Does Distrust of One's Own Cleverness

Men who distrust their own cleverness do better in times of tumult and sedition than men who suppose themselves wise or crafty. The crafty love to consult; the others, fearing to be outmaneuvered, strike first. And in sedition, where men are always on the edge of battle, holding together and using every advantage of force is a better strategy than anything cleverness can produce.

Vanity Leads to Empty Undertakings

Vain men — those who delight in imagining themselves gallant without being conscious of any real ability in themselves — are inclined only to show, not to action. For when danger or difficulty appears, they can expect nothing but to have their insufficiency exposed.

Vain men who estimate their ability from the flattery of other men, or from the luck of some past success, without any solid ground of hope from true knowledge of themselves, are inclined to rush in — and then, as danger or difficulty approaches, to retreat if they can. For seeing no way to safety, they would rather risk their honor, which can be patched up with an excuse, than their lives, for which no patch is sufficient.

Ambition Comes from a High Opinion of One's Own Wisdom

Men who hold a strong opinion of their own wisdom in matters of government are disposed to ambition, because without public employment in council or office, the honor of their wisdom is lost. That is why eloquent speakers are inclined to ambition: eloquence looks like wisdom, both to themselves and to others.

Indecision Comes from Overvaluing Small Things

Pusillanimity disposes men to indecision, and so to losing the occasions and best opportunities for action. For when men have deliberated right up to the moment of action, and it is still not clear which course is best, that is a sign that the motives on either side are not very different. To fail to decide then is to lose the occasion by weighing trifles — which is pusillanimity.

Frugality (though a virtue in poor men) makes a man unfit to achieve actions that require the strength of many men at once. For it starves their effort, which has to be nourished and kept vigorous by reward.

Trusting Others, from Not Knowing the Marks of Wisdom and Kindness

Eloquence, joined with flattery, disposes men to trust those who have it — for eloquence looks like wisdom, and flattery looks like kindness. Add military reputation, and men are disposed to attach themselves, and subject themselves, to those who have all three. The first two give them assurance against danger from the man himself; the third gives them assurance against danger from others.

And from Ignorance of Causes

Lack of science — that is, ignorance of causes — disposes a man, or rather forces him, to rely on the advice and authority of others. For every man who cares about the truth must, if he does not rely on his own judgment, rely on the opinion of someone else — someone he thinks wiser than himself, and sees no reason to suspect of deceiving him.

And from Lack of Understanding

Ignorance of what words signify — which is lack of understanding — disposes men to take on trust not only truths they do not know, but errors too, and worse, the nonsense of those they trust. For neither error nor nonsense can be detected without a perfect understanding of words.

From the same source it comes that men give different names to one and the same thing, according to the difference of their own passions. Those who approve of a private opinion call it opinion; those who dislike it call it heresy — and yet heresy means nothing more than private opinion, with a stronger tincture of anger in it.

From the same source it also comes that men cannot distinguish, without study and real understanding, between one action of many men and many actions of one multitude — between, for example, the single action of all the senators of Rome in killing Catiline, and the many actions of a number of senators in killing Caesar. And so they are disposed to take for the action of the people what is really a multitude of actions done by a multitude of men — led on, perhaps, by the persuasion of one.

Clinging to Custom, from Ignorance of the Nature of Right and Wrong

Ignorance of the causes and original constitution of right, equity, law, and justice disposes a man to make custom and example the rule of his actions: to think a thing unjust because it has been the custom to punish it, and just because he can produce an example of it going unpunished and approved — or, as the lawyers (who alone use this false measure of justice) barbarously call it, a precedent. He is like little children, who have no other rule of good and bad manners than the corrections they receive from their parents and teachers. Except that children stay constant to their rule, and grown men do not. Grown strong and stubborn, men appeal from custom to reason and from reason to custom as it serves their turn — abandoning custom when their interest requires it, and setting themselves against reason whenever reason is against them.

That is why the doctrine of right and wrong is perpetually disputed, both by the pen and by the sword — while the doctrine of lines and figures is not. For in that subject, men do not care what the truth is, since it crosses no man's ambition, profit, or lust. I have no doubt that if the proposition that the three angles of a triangle are equal to two angles of a square had been contrary to any man's right of dominion, or to the interest of men who have dominion, that doctrine would have been — if not disputed — then suppressed by the burning of every book of geometry, as far as the man it concerned was able.

Following Private Men, from Ignorance of the Causes of Peace

Ignorance of remote causes disposes men to attribute all events to the causes that are immediate and instrumental — for these are the only causes they perceive. That is why, in every country, men aggrieved by payments to the public discharge their anger on the publicans — the tax farmers, collectors, and other officers of the public revenue — and attach themselves to anyone who finds fault with the public government. Then, having committed themselves beyond hope of justification, they end up attacking the supreme authority itself, for fear of punishment, or for shame at accepting a pardon.

Credulity, from Ignorance of Nature

Ignorance of natural causes disposes a man to credulity — to believing, many times, in impossibilities. For he knows nothing to say they might not be true, being unable to detect the impossibility. And credulity, because men love to be listened to in company, disposes them to lying. So ignorance by itself, without any malice, can make a man both believe lies and tell them — and sometimes invent them too.

Curiosity Comes from Anxiety About the Future

Anxiety about the future disposes men to inquire into the causes of things, because knowing the causes makes men better able to arrange the present to their best advantage.

Natural Religion Comes from the Same Source

Curiosity — the love of knowing causes — draws a man from considering an effect to seeking its cause, and then the cause of that cause, until of necessity he comes at last to this thought: there must be some cause that has no earlier cause, but is eternal. And that is what men call God. So it is impossible to inquire deeply into natural causes without being inclined to believe there is one eternal God — even though no one can have an idea of him in the mind that matches his nature.

Think of a man born blind, who hears others talk of warming themselves by a fire and is then brought to warm himself at one. He can easily conceive, and assure himself, that there is something there which men call fire, and which causes the heat he feels — but he cannot imagine what it is like, or have an idea of it in his mind such as sighted men have. In the same way, from the visible things of this world and their admirable order, a man may conceive that there is a cause of them all, which men call God — and yet have no idea or image of him in his mind.

And those who make little or no inquiry into the natural causes of things are still moved by the fear that grows out of that very ignorance — fear of whatever it is that has the power to do them great good or great harm. That fear inclines them to suppose, and invent for themselves, various kinds of invisible powers; to stand in awe of their own imaginations; to call on those powers in times of distress, and to give them thanks when expected good fortune arrives — making the creatures of their own imagination into their gods. By this means, from the endless variety of imagination, men have created in the world endless sorts of gods. And this fear of invisible things is the natural seed of what everyone calls religion in himself — and superstition in those who worship or fear that power differently than he does.

This seed of religion has been noticed by many. And some of those who noticed it have been inclined to nourish it, dress it up, and shape it into laws — adding to it, out of their own invention, whatever opinions about the causes of future events they thought would best enable them to govern others, and to make the greatest use of their own powers.

Chapter 12: Of Religion

Religion Exists Only in Humans

Since there are no signs or fruits of religion anywhere but in human beings, there is no reason to doubt that the seed of religion is also found only in human beings. It must consist in some quality peculiar to us — or at least in some quality we possess to an eminent degree — that is not found in other living creatures.

First Seed: The Desire to Know Causes

First, it is peculiar to human nature to be inquisitive about the causes of the events we see. Some people ask more, some less, but everyone asks at least enough to be curious about the causes of their own good and bad fortune.

Second: Everything That Begins Must Have a Cause
