Secondly, when we see anything that has a beginning, we naturally think it must have had a cause — something that determined it to begin exactly when it did, rather than sooner or later.

Third: Watching How One Thing Follows Another

Thirdly, consider the difference between us and the animals. A beast's whole happiness is its daily food, its comfort, and its appetites. It has little or no foresight about the future, because it does not observe or remember the order, sequence, and dependence of the things it sees. But man observes how one event is produced by another, and remembers what came before and what came after. And when he cannot be sure of the true causes of things — for the causes of good and bad fortune are mostly invisible — he supposes causes for them anyway: either causes his own imagination suggests, or causes he takes on the authority of other men, people he believes are his friends and wiser than himself.

The Natural Cause of Religion: Anxiety About the Future

The first two of these traits produce anxiety. Once a man is convinced that everything that has happened, or will happen, has a cause, it becomes impossible for him — constantly striving as he does to protect himself from the evils he fears and to obtain the goods he desires — not to be in perpetual worry about the future.

So every man, and especially the over-cautious man, is in a condition like that of Prometheus. The name Prometheus, translated, means "the prudent man." And Prometheus was chained to Mount Caucasus, a place with a vast view, where an eagle fed on his liver, devouring by day exactly as much as grew back at night. In the same way, the man who looks too far ahead, anxious about the future, has his heart gnawed at all day long by fear of death, poverty, or some other calamity. He gets no rest, no pause from his anxiety, except in sleep.

Which Makes Men Fear Invisible Powers

This perpetual fear, which accompanies mankind whenever we are ignorant of causes — as if we were standing in the dark — must have some object. And when there is nothing visible to blame for our good or bad fortune, we blame some invisible power or agent.

Perhaps this is what some of the old poets meant when they said that the gods were first created by human fear. Said of the gods — that is, of the many gods of the pagans — this is very true.

But the acknowledgment of one God — eternal, infinite, and omnipotent — can be derived more easily from the desire to know the causes of natural bodies and their various powers and operations than from fear of what might happen to us in the future. For take a man who, from some effect he sees, reasons back to its next and immediate cause, and from there to the cause of that cause, plunging himself deep into the pursuit of causes. He will arrive at last at this conclusion: that there must be — as even the heathen philosophers admitted — one First Mover. That is, one first and eternal cause of all things. And that is what people mean by the name God.

And he reaches it without ever thinking about his own fortune. Worry about one's fortune does the opposite: it inclines a man toward fear, and it distracts him from searching out the causes of other things — and so it gives occasion for inventing as many gods as there are men to invent them.

And Imagine Them to Be Bodiless

As for the matter, or substance, of these imagined invisible agents: natural thinking could arrive at no other idea than that they were made of the same stuff as the human soul — and that the human soul was made of the same stuff as what appears in a dream to someone sleeping, or in a mirror to someone awake. Not knowing that such apparitions are nothing but creations of the imagination, men took them to be real, external substances, and so called them ghosts — as the Romans called them imagines and umbrae, "images" and "shades" — and thought them spirits, that is, thin airy bodies. They imagined the invisible agents they feared to be like these ghosts, except able to appear and vanish at will.

But the opinion that such spirits were incorporeal — without bodies, immaterial — could never enter any man's mind by nature. Men can certainly put together words with contradictory meanings, like "spirit" and "incorporeal," but they can never form a mental image of anything answering to them. That is why men who, by their own reflection, arrive at the acknowledgment of one infinite, omnipotent, and eternal God prefer to confess that he is incomprehensible and above their understanding, rather than to define his nature as "incorporeal spirit" and then have to confess that their definition is unintelligible. Or if they do give him such a title, they do it not dogmatically — not intending to make the divine nature understood — but piously, to honor him with attributes as remote as possible from the grossness of visible bodies.

But Not Know How These Powers Cause Anything

Next, consider how men think these invisible agents work their effects — that is, what immediate causes they use to bring things about. Men who do not know what causing actually is (which is to say, almost all men) have no rule to guess by except this: observing and remembering what they have seen come before a similar effect on some earlier occasion — without seeing any dependence or connection at all between the earlier event and the later one. And so from similar things in the past, they expect similar things to come, and they hope for good or bad luck, superstitiously, from things that play no part whatsoever in causing it.

So the Athenians, facing their war at Lepanto, demanded another Phormio — a general who had won for them there before. The faction of Pompey, for their war in Africa, demanded another Scipio. And others have done the same on countless occasions since. In the same way men attribute their fortune to a bystander, to a lucky or unlucky place, to words spoken aloud — especially if the name of God is among them, as in charming and conjuring, the liturgy of witches. They go so far as to believe such words have the power to turn a stone into bread, bread into a man, or anything into anything.

Honoring Them the Way They Honor Men

Thirdly, as for the worship that men naturally offer to invisible powers: it can be nothing other than the same expressions of reverence they would use toward men. Gifts. Petitions. Thanks. Bowing the body. Respectful forms of address. Sober behavior. Carefully prepared words. Swearing oaths — that is, assuring one another of promises — by invoking them. Beyond that, reason suggests nothing. It leaves men either to stop there, or, for further ceremonies, to rely on those they believe wiser than themselves.

And Crediting Them With Every Extraordinary Event

Lastly, as for how these invisible powers reveal to men the things that are going to happen — especially their good or bad fortune in general, or their success in some particular undertaking — men are naturally at a loss. All they can do is guess at the future from the past, which makes them very apt not only to take chance occurrences, after one or two coincidences, as omens of the same outcome forever after, but also to believe similar predictions from other men, once they have formed a good opinion of them.

Four Things: The Natural Seeds of Religion

In these four things, then — opinion of ghosts, ignorance of secondary causes, devotion toward what men fear, and the taking of chance things for omens — consists the natural seed of religion. And because of the different imaginations, judgments, and passions of different people, this seed has grown up into ceremonies so varied that the ceremonies used by one man are, for the most part, ridiculous to another.

Cultivated Into Different Religions

For these seeds have been cultivated by two sorts of men. The first sort nourished and ordered them according to their own invention. The second sort did so by God's commandment and direction. But both sorts did it with the same purpose: to make the men who relied on them more apt for obedience, laws, peace, charity, and civil society.

So the religion of the first sort is part of human politics, and teaches part of the duty that earthly kings require of their subjects. The religion of the second sort is divine politics, and contains precepts for those who have yielded themselves as subjects in the kingdom of God. Of the first sort were all the founders of commonwealths and the lawgivers of the pagans. Of the second sort were Abraham, Moses, and our blessed Savior, through whom the laws of the kingdom of God have come down to us.

The Absurd Opinions of the Pagans

As for the part of religion that consists in opinions about the nature of invisible powers: there is almost nothing that has a name that the pagans did not, in one place or another, consider a god or a devil, or that their poets did not imagine to be animated, inhabited, or possessed by some spirit or other.

The unformed matter of the world was a god, under the name of Chaos.

The sky, the ocean, the planets, fire, earth, and the winds were so many gods.

Men, women, a bird, a crocodile, a calf, a dog, a snake, an onion, a leek — all deified. Beyond that, they filled almost every place with spirits called demons: the plains with Pan and his satyrs; the woods with fauns and nymphs; the sea with tritons and still more nymphs; every river and fountain with a ghost bearing its name, plus nymphs; every house with its lares, or household spirits; every man with his genius — his personal guardian spirit; hell with ghosts and spiritual officers like Charon, Cerberus, and the Furies; and the nighttime everywhere with larvae and lemures — ghosts of the dead — and a whole kingdom of fairies and bogeymen.

They also ascribed divinity, and built temples, to mere accidents and qualities: Time, Night, Day, Peace, Concord, Love, Contention, Virtue, Honor, Health, Rust, Fever, and the like. When they prayed for or against these things, they prayed to them — as if there were ghosts with those names hovering over their heads, dropping down or withholding the good or evil being prayed for or against.

They invoked their own wit under the name of the Muses; their own ignorance under the name of Fortune; their own lust under the name of Cupid; their own rage under the name of the Furies; their own private parts under the name of Priapus. And they blamed what happened in their sleep on incubi and succubi — demons who supposedly visited by night. In short, there was nothing a poet could introduce as a character in his poem that they did not make either a god or a devil.

The same authors of pagan religion noticed the second ground of religion — men's ignorance of causes, and their consequent readiness to credit their fortune to causes with no apparent connection to it at all. They seized the opportunity to foist upon that ignorance, in place of secondary causes, a kind of second-tier, ministerial gods: ascribing the cause of fertility to Venus; the cause of the arts to Apollo; of subtlety and craft to Mercury; of tempests and storms to Aeolus; and so on, other effects to other gods — until among the heathen there were almost as many gods as there were lines of business.

To the worship men naturally thought fitting for their gods — offerings, prayers, thanks, and the rest already named — these same pagan legislators added images, in both painting and sculpture, so that the more ignorant sort (that is to say, most people, the general run of them), thinking the gods these images represented were really contained in them, housed within them as it were, might stand in that much more fear of them. And they endowed these gods with lands, houses, officers, and revenues, set apart from all other human uses — that is, consecrated, made holy to those idols: caverns, groves, woods, mountains, and whole islands.

They attributed to these gods not only shapes — some human, some animal, some monstrous — but also the faculties and passions of men and beasts: sense, speech, sex, lust, reproduction. And not only reproduction by mixing with one another to propagate the race of gods, but also by mixing with men and women, begetting half-breed gods who were mere lodgers in heaven, like Bacchus, Hercules, and others. Besides these: anger, revenge, and the other passions of living creatures, and the actions that flow from them — fraud, theft, adultery, sodomy, and any vice that can be taken as an effect of power or a cause of pleasure; all those vices, in short, which among men are considered to be against the law rather than against honor.

Lastly, consider predictions of the future, which are naturally nothing but conjectures from past experience — and supernaturally, divine revelation. To these the same authors of pagan religion, partly on the strength of pretended experience, partly on pretended revelation, added innumerable other superstitious methods of divination, and made men believe they could discover their fortunes:

Sometimes in the ambiguous or senseless answers of the priests at Delphi, Delos, Ammon, and other famous oracles — answers made ambiguous by design, so that the oracle would be right either way, or made absurd by the intoxicating vapors of the place, which are very common in sulfurous caverns.

Sometimes in the leaves of the Sibyls — prophetesses whose prophecies, like perhaps those of Nostradamus (for the fragments now surviving seem to be inventions of later times), filled certain books held in high repute in the days of the Roman Republic.

Sometimes in the meaningless speeches of madmen, who were supposed to be possessed by a divine spirit — a possession they called enthusiasm, literally "a god within." These kinds of fortune-telling were accounted theomancy, or prophecy.

Sometimes in the position of the stars at a person's birth, which was called horoscopy and considered a branch of judicial astrology.

Sometimes in one's own hopes and fears, called thumomancy — divination by one's own spirit — or presage.

Sometimes in the predictions of witches who pretended to consult the dead — which is called necromancy, conjuring, and witchcraft, and is really nothing but sleight of hand and accomplices working a con.

Sometimes in the chance flight or feeding of birds, called augury.

Sometimes in the entrails of a sacrificed beast, which was aruspicina.

Sometimes in dreams.

Sometimes in the croaking of ravens or the chattering of birds.

Sometimes in the lines of the face, which was called metoposcopy; or by palmistry, in the lines of the hand; or in chance words overheard, called omina — omens.

Sometimes in monsters or unusual events — eclipses, comets, rare meteors, earthquakes, floods, deformed births, and the like — which they called portenta and ostenta, because they thought these things portended, or foreshowed, some great calamity to come.

Sometimes in pure lottery: heads or tails; counting holes in a sieve; dipping at random into the verses of Homer and Virgil; and innumerable other such empty notions.

So easily are men drawn to believe anything, by men who have gained their trust and who know how to take hold of their fear and their ignorance with gentleness and skill.

What the Founders of Pagan Religion Were After

And so the first founders and legislators of commonwealths among the pagans, whose aim was simply to keep the people in obedience and peace, took care everywhere to do three things.

First, to imprint on people's minds the belief that the precepts they gave concerning religion came not from their own devising but from the dictates of some god or other spirit — or else that the lawgivers themselves were beings of a higher nature than mere mortals — so that their laws might be the more easily accepted. So Numa Pompilius claimed to have received the ceremonies he instituted among the Romans from the nymph Egeria. The first king and founder of the kingdom of Peru claimed that he and his wife were children of the Sun. And Muhammad, to establish his new religion, claimed to have conferences with the Holy Ghost in the form of a dove.

Second, they took care to make it believed that the same things which were forbidden by the laws were also displeasing to the gods.

Third, to prescribe ceremonies, supplications, sacrifices, and festivals by which the anger of the gods could supposedly be appeased — and to teach that failure in war, great epidemics of sickness, earthquakes, and every man's private misery all came from the anger of the gods, and that the gods' anger came from neglecting their worship, or from forgetting or botching some required point of the ceremonies. And though among the ancient Romans a man was not forbidden to deny what the poets had written about the pains and pleasures after this life — indeed, several men of great authority and gravity in that state openly mocked such things in their speeches — still, the belief in them was always more encouraged than the contrary.

By these and similar institutions, they achieved their goal, which was the peace of the commonwealth. The common people, blaming their misfortunes on neglect or error in the ceremonies, or on their own disobedience to the laws, were that much less inclined to mutiny against their governors. And being entertained with the pomp and pastime of festivals and public games held in honor of the gods, they needed nothing but bread to keep them from discontent, grumbling, and uprising against the state. That is why the Romans, who had conquered the greatest part of the known world, had no scruple about tolerating any religion whatsoever in the city of Rome itself — unless it contained something incompatible with their civil government. Nor do we read of any religion being forbidden there except that of the Jews, who, being the peculiar kingdom of God, thought it unlawful to acknowledge subjection to any mortal king or state whatsoever. And so you see how the religion of the pagans was a part of their policy.

True Religion: The Laws of God's Kingdom Are the Same

But where God himself planted religion by supernatural revelation, he also made for himself a peculiar kingdom, and gave laws — not only of behavior toward himself, but of behavior toward one another. And so in the kingdom of God, policy and civil laws are a part of religion, and the distinction between temporal and spiritual dominion has no place there.

It is true that God is king of all the earth. Yet he may still be king of a peculiar, chosen nation. There is no more contradiction in that than in a general who commands the whole army also keeping a particular regiment or company of his own. God is king of all the earth by his power; but of his chosen people he is king by covenant. I will speak more fully of the kingdom of God, both by nature and by covenant, in a later part of this discourse, where I have assigned it its own place.

Why Religions Change

Given how religion is propagated, it is not hard to understand the causes of its dissolution back into its first seeds, or principles — which are nothing but an opinion of a deity, and of powers invisible and supernatural. That opinion can never be entirely rooted out of human nature; new religions can always be made to spring from it again, cultivated by men who have the reputation for such work.

For all organized religion is founded, at first, on the faith that a multitude places in some one person — someone they believe to be not only a wise man who labors for their happiness, but a holy man, to whom God himself condescends to declare his will supernaturally. It follows necessarily that when the people who govern a religion come to have their wisdom, their sincerity, or their love suspected — or when they cannot show any plausible token of divine revelation — the religion they want to uphold will be suspected too, and (without fear of the civil sword) contradicted and rejected.

Demanding Belief in Impossibilities

What destroys the reputation for wisdom, in anyone who founds a religion or adds to one already founded, is requiring belief in contradictions. Both halves of a contradiction cannot possibly be true. To demand belief in both is therefore evidence of ignorance — which exposes the author on that point, and discredits him in everything else he puts forward as supernatural revelation. For a man may indeed receive revelation of many things above natural reason, but of nothing against it.

Living Contrary to the Religion They Preach

What destroys the reputation for sincerity is doing or saying things that look like signs that the men who require others to believe do not believe it themselves. All such doings and sayings are therefore called scandalous — stumbling blocks that make men fall on the road of religion: injustice, cruelty, profanity, greed, and luxurious living. After all, who can believe that a man who routinely commits actions springing from any of these roots really believes there is any invisible power to be feared — the same power he uses to frighten other men over lesser faults?

What destroys the reputation for love is being caught pursuing private ends: when the belief they require of others leads, or seems to lead, to the acquisition of power, riches, rank, or secure pleasure for themselves alone, or for themselves especially. For whatever men reap personal benefit from, they are presumed to do for their own sakes and not for love of others.

The Lack of Miracles

Lastly, the only testimony men can give of a divine calling is the working of miracles, or true prophecy (which is also a miracle), or extraordinary success. So when points of religion received from men who did perform such miracles are supplemented with additions from men who cannot certify their calling with any miracle, those additions win no more belief than whatever the custom and laws of the place where the believers were raised have worked into them. For just as in natural matters men of judgment require natural signs and arguments, so in supernatural matters they require supernatural signs — which are miracles — before they consent inwardly, and from their hearts.

All these causes of the weakening of men's faith appear plainly in the following examples.

First, we have the example of the children of Israel. When Moses — who had certified his calling to them by miracles and by leading them successfully out of Egypt — was absent a mere forty days, they revolted from the worship of the true God he had commended to them, and setting up a golden calf as their god (Exodus 32:1-2), they relapsed into the idolatry of the very Egyptians from whom they had so recently been delivered. And again: after Moses, Aaron, Joshua, and the whole generation that had seen the great works of God in Israel were dead, another generation arose and served Baal (Judges 2:11). So when miracles failed, faith failed too.

Again: when the sons of Samuel, appointed by their father as judges in Beersheba, took bribes and judged unjustly (1 Samuel 8:3), the people of Israel refused to have God as their king any longer in any way different from how he was king of all other peoples — and so they cried out to Samuel to choose them a king after the manner of the nations. So when justice failed, faith failed too — to the point that they deposed their God from reigning over them.

And when the Christian religion was being planted, the oracles fell silent in every part of the Roman Empire, and the number of Christians grew wonderfully every day and in every place through the preaching of the apostles and evangelists. A great part of that success may reasonably be attributed to the contempt into which the pagan priests of that time had brought themselves by their uncleanness, their greed, and their scheming among princes.

The religion of the Church of Rome, likewise, was abolished in England and many other parts of Christendom partly for the same cause — for the failing of virtue in the pastors makes faith fail in the people — and partly from the importing of Aristotle's philosophy and doctrine into religion by the Scholastics. From that source arose so many contradictions and absurdities that the clergy earned a reputation both for ignorance and for fraudulent intentions, and inclined the people to revolt against them: either against the will of their own princes, as in France and Holland, or with their princes' consent, as in England.

Lastly, among the points the Church of Rome has declared necessary for salvation, there are so many that work so plainly to the advantage of the Pope and of his spiritual subjects living in the territories of other Christian princes, that if it were not for the mutual rivalry of those princes, they could exclude all foreign authority without war or trouble, as easily as it has been excluded in England.

For who fails to see whose benefit it serves to have it believed that a king does not hold his authority from Christ unless a bishop crowns him? That a king, if he is a priest, cannot marry? That whether a prince was born in lawful marriage must be judged by authority from Rome? That subjects may be freed from their allegiance if the court of Rome judges their king a heretic? That a king — like Chilperic of France — may be deposed by a pope (as he was by Pope Zachary) for no cause at all, and his kingdom given to one of his own subjects? That the clergy and the religious orders, in whatever country, shall be exempt from their king's jurisdiction in criminal cases? Or who fails to see who profits from the fees for private Masses, and the proceeds of Purgatory — along with other signs of private interest, enough to deaden the most lively faith, if (as I said) the civil magistrate and custom did not prop it up more than any opinion people hold of the sanctity, wisdom, or honesty of their teachers?

So I may attribute all the changes of religion in the world to one and the same cause: unpleasing priests. And not only among Catholics, but even in that church which has prided itself most on reformation.

Chapter 13: The Natural Condition of Mankind — Their Happiness and Their Misery

Nature has made men so equal in the faculties of body and mind that, although you can sometimes find one man who is obviously stronger in body or quicker in mind than another, still, when you add everything up, the difference between man and man is not so great that one of them can claim any advantage for himself that another cannot claim just as well. Consider strength of body: the weakest man has strength enough to kill the strongest, either by a secret plot or by joining forces with others who are in the same danger he is.

As for the faculties of the mind, I find an even greater equality among men than the equality of strength. (I am setting aside the arts built on words, and especially the skill of proceeding by general and infallible rules called science, which very few people have, and only in a few subjects — because it is not a natural faculty we are born with, nor is it picked up, the way prudence is, while we are busy attending to something else.) For prudence is nothing but experience, and equal time bestows experience equally on all men in whatever things they equally apply themselves to.

What may make such equality hard to believe is simply each man's vain conceit of his own wisdom. Nearly every man thinks he has more of it than the common crowd — that is, more than everyone except himself and a few others whom he approves of, either because they are famous or because they agree with him. For such is the nature of men: however readily they admit that many others are more clever, more eloquent, or more learned, they will hardly believe that many are as wise as they are. Each man sees his own wit up close and other men's at a distance. But this proves that men are equal on this point, not unequal. For ordinarily there is no greater sign that a thing is equally distributed than that every man is contented with his share.

Equality Breeds Mistrust

From this equality of ability arises an equality of hope in attaining our ends. And therefore, if any two men desire the same thing, which nevertheless they cannot both enjoy, they become enemies; and on the way to their end — which is chiefly their own survival, and sometimes only their pleasure — they try to destroy or subdue one another. This is how it comes to pass that where an invader has nothing more to fear than another man's individual strength, then if one man plants, sows, builds, or holds a good piece of ground, others can be expected to come prepared with united forces to dispossess him — to strip him not only of the fruit of his labor, but of his life or liberty as well. And the invader, in turn, stands in the same danger from someone else.

Mistrust Breeds War

Given this mutual mistrust, there is no way for any man to secure himself so reasonable as striking first: that is, using force or cunning to master the persons of all the men he can, for as long as it takes until he sees no other power big enough to endanger him. This is no more than his own survival requires, and it is generally allowed.

There is more. Some men take pleasure in contemplating their own power through acts of conquest, and they push those conquests further than their security requires. And because of them, others — who would otherwise be glad to live at ease within modest bounds — would not be able to survive for long by standing only on the defensive. They too must increase their power by invading. And so, since enlarging one's dominion over other men is necessary to a man's survival, it has to be allowed him.

Again, men get no pleasure (but on the contrary, a great deal of grief) from keeping company where there is no power able to overawe them all. For every man expects his companion to value him at the same rate he sets on himself; and at every sign of contempt or undervaluing, he naturally tries — as far as he dares, which among men with no common power to keep them quiet is far enough to make them destroy each other — to extort a higher value from those who slight him, by harming them; and from everyone else, by the example.

So in the nature of man we find three principal causes of quarrel: first, competition; second, distrust; third, glory.

The first makes men invade for gain; the second, for safety; and the third, for reputation. The first use violence to make themselves masters of other men's persons, wives, children, and cattle; the second, to defend them; the third, over trifles — a word, a smile, a different opinion, or any other sign of being undervalued, whether aimed directly at them in person or reflected onto their relatives, their friends, their nation, their profession, or their name.

Outside Civil States, There Is Always a War of Every One Against Every One

This makes one thing clear: whenever men live without a common power to keep them all in check, they are in the condition called war — and not just any war, but a war of every man against every man. For WAR consists not in battle only, or in the act of fighting, but in a stretch of time in which the will to fight is sufficiently known. The notion of time, then, belongs to the nature of war just as it belongs to the nature of weather. Foul weather does not lie in a shower or two of rain, but in a tendency toward rain over many days together. In the same way, the nature of war consists not in actual fighting, but in the known disposition to fight, during all the time there is no assurance to the contrary. All other time is PEACE.

The Miseries of Such a War

Whatever follows from a time of war, where every man is enemy to every man, also follows from a time when men live with no other security than what their own strength and their own ingenuity can supply. In such a condition there is no place for industry, because the fruit of it is uncertain — and consequently no farming of the earth; no navigation, and no use of the goods that can be imported by sea; no comfortable building; no machines for moving and hauling things that require much force; no knowledge of the face of the earth; no reckoning of time; no arts; no letters; no society; and, worst of all, continual fear and danger of violent death — and the life of man: solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short.

To someone who has not weighed these things carefully, it may seem strange that nature should pull men apart this way and make them apt to invade and destroy one another. Such a person, not trusting this inference drawn from the passions, may want to see it confirmed by experience. Then let him consider his own behavior. When he takes a journey, he arms himself and tries to travel in good company. When he goes to sleep, he locks his doors. Even inside his own house, he locks his chests. And he does all this knowing that there are laws and armed public officers ready to avenge any wrong done to him. What opinion does he have of his fellow subjects, when he rides armed? Of his fellow citizens, when he locks his doors? Of his own children and servants, when he locks his chests? Doesn't he accuse mankind by his actions just as much as I do by my words?

But neither of us is accusing man's nature in this. The desires and other passions of man are in themselves no sin. Neither are the actions that proceed from those passions — until men know a law that forbids them. And they cannot know such laws until laws are made; and no law can be made until men have agreed on the person who shall make it.

It may perhaps be thought that no such time or condition of war ever existed, and I believe it was never universal over the whole world. But there are many places where people live that way now. The indigenous peoples in many parts of America — at least as the reports that reach us describe them — have, apart from the government of small families whose harmony depends on natural appetite, no government at all, and live to this day in the brutish manner I described above. In any case, we can see what kind of life there would be where there was no common power to fear, from the kind of life that men who have previously lived under a peaceful government sink into during a civil war.

But even if there had never been a time when particular men were in a condition of war against one another, still, in all times, kings and persons of sovereign authority — because of their independence — are in continual jealousies, in the state and posture of gladiators: weapons pointed, eyes fixed on one another. That is, their forts, garrisons, and guns stand on the frontiers of their kingdoms, and their spies work continually among their neighbors. That is a posture of war. But because by this posture they uphold the industry of their subjects, it does not produce the misery that goes along with the liberty of individual men.

In Such a War, Nothing Is Unjust

From this war of every man against every man, something else follows: that nothing can be unjust. The notions of right and wrong, justice and injustice, have no place there. Where there is no common power, there is no law; where there is no law, there is no injustice. Force and fraud are in war the two cardinal virtues. Justice and injustice are not faculties of either the body or the mind. If they were, they could exist in a man who was alone in the world, just as his senses and passions can. They are qualities that relate to men in society, not in solitude.

It also follows from the same condition that there is no ownership, no dominion, no mine and thine distinct — but only that to be every man's that he can get, and for as long as he can keep it.

And this is the bad condition that man is actually placed in by mere nature — though with a possibility of climbing out of it, a possibility that lies partly in the passions, partly in his reason.

The Passions That Incline Men to Peace

The passions that incline men to peace are fear of death; desire of the things necessary for comfortable living; and a hope of obtaining them by their industry. And reason suggests convenient articles of peace on which men may be drawn to agreement. These articles are the ones otherwise called the laws of nature — of which I shall speak more particularly in the two following chapters.

Chapter 14: The First and Second Laws of Nature, and Contracts

What the Right of Nature Is

The RIGHT OF NATURE, which writers commonly call jus naturale — the natural right — is the liberty each man has to use his own power, as he himself wills, for the preservation of his own nature; that is to say, of his own life. And consequently, it is the liberty of doing anything which, in his own judgment and reason, he conceives to be the aptest means to that end.

What Liberty Is

By LIBERTY is understood, according to the proper meaning of the word, the absence of external impediments. Such impediments may often take away part of a man's power to do what he would; but they cannot prevent him from using the power left to him according to what his judgment and reason dictate.

What a Law of Nature Is

A LAW OF NATURE (lex naturalis — natural law) is a precept, or general rule, discovered by reason, which forbids a man to do anything that is destructive of his life, or that takes away his means of preserving it; and forbids him to neglect anything by which he thinks his life may best be preserved.

Those who write on this subject tend to confuse jus and lex — right and law. But the two ought to be distinguished. A RIGHT consists in the liberty to do something or to hold back from it; whereas a LAW determines the matter and binds you to one or the other. So law and right differ as much as obligation and liberty — which, in one and the same matter, cannot coexist.

By Nature, Every Man Has a Right to Everything

Now, the condition of man, as I declared in the previous chapter, is a condition of war of every one against every one. In that condition, every man is governed by his own reason, and there is nothing he might use that could not be a help to him in preserving his life against his enemies. It follows that in such a condition, every man has a right to everything — even to one another's body. And therefore, as long as this natural right of every man to everything endures, no man — however strong or wise he may be — can have any security of living out the time that nature ordinarily allows men to live.

The Fundamental Law of Nature

And consequently it is a precept, a general rule of reason: that every man ought to seek peace, as far as he has hope of obtaining it; and when he cannot obtain it, that he may seek and use all the helps and advantages of war.

The first branch of this rule contains the first and fundamental law of nature, which is: seek peace, and follow it. The second branch contains the sum of the right of nature, which is: by all means we can, defend ourselves.

The Second Law of Nature

From this fundamental law of nature, which commands men to seek peace, this second law is derived: that a man be willing, when others are willing too, to lay down this right to all things — as far as he thinks it necessary for peace and for his own defense — and be contented with as much liberty against other men as he would allow other men against himself.

For as long as every man holds on to this right of doing anything he likes, all men remain in the condition of war. But if other men will not lay down their right along with him, then there is no reason for anyone to strip himself of his own. That would be to offer himself up as prey — which no man is bound to do — rather than to dispose himself to peace.

This is that law of the Gospel: whatever you require that others should do to you, do that to them. And it is that law of all men: quod tibi fieri non vis, alteri ne feceris — what you do not want done to you, do not do to another.

What It Means to Lay Down a Right

To lay down a man's right to anything is to strip himself of the liberty of hindering another from the benefit of that other man's own right to the same thing. For he who renounces or passes away his right does not give any other man a right which that man did not have before — because there is nothing to which every man did not already have a right by nature. He merely stands out of the way, so that the other may enjoy his own original right without hindrance from him — though not without hindrance from everyone else. So the benefit that comes to one man from another man's giving up his right is simply this much: fewer obstacles to the use of the right he had all along.

Renouncing and Transferring a Right; Obligation, Duty, Injustice

A right is laid aside either by simply renouncing it, or by transferring it to another. By simply RENOUNCING it, when the man does not care who receives the benefit of it. By TRANSFERRING it, when he intends the benefit for some particular person or persons.

And when a man has in either way abandoned or granted away his right, he is then said to be OBLIGED, or BOUND, not to hinder those to whom the right was granted or abandoned from enjoying the benefit of it. He ought not — it is his DUTY not — to make void that voluntary act of his own. And such hindrance is INJUSTICE, and INJURY, as being sine jure — without right — the right having been renounced or transferred beforehand.

So injury, or injustice, in the controversies of the world is somewhat like what the Scholastics, in their disputations, call absurdity. For just as it is called an absurdity to contradict what one maintained at the beginning, so in the world it is called injustice and injury to voluntarily undo what one had voluntarily done from the beginning.

The way a man either simply renounces or transfers his right is by a declaration, or signification — some voluntary and sufficient sign or signs — that he does so renounce or transfer, or has done so, to the one who accepts it. These signs are either words only, or actions only, or — as happens most often — both words and actions. And these same signs are the BONDS by which men are bound and obliged. Bonds that have their strength not from their own nature — for nothing is more easily broken than a man's word — but from fear of some evil consequence upon breaking them.

Not All Rights Can Be Given Away

Whenever a man transfers his right or renounces it, he does so either in exchange for some right reciprocally transferred to himself, or for some other good he hopes to gain by it. For it is a voluntary act, and the object of every man's voluntary acts is some good to himself.

And therefore there are some rights which no man can be understood — by any words or other signs — to have abandoned or transferred. First, a man cannot lay down the right of resisting those who assault him by force to take away his life; because he cannot be understood to aim at any good to himself by doing so. The same may be said of wounds, and chains, and imprisonment — both because there is no benefit in enduring such things, as there is in patiently allowing another to be wounded or imprisoned, and also because when a man sees men proceed against him by violence, he cannot tell whether they intend his death or not.

And lastly, the motive and end for which this renouncing and transferring of right is introduced is nothing else but the security of a man's person — his life, and his means of preserving life in a way that does not make him weary of it. And therefore, if a man by words or other signs seems to strip himself of the very end for which those signs were intended, he is not to be understood as if he meant it, or as if it were his will. He is to be understood as ignorant of how such words and actions would be interpreted.

What a Contract Is

The mutual transferring of right is what men call CONTRACT.

There is a difference between transferring the right to a thing and transferring — that is, delivering — the thing itself. For the thing may be delivered together with the transfer of the right, as in buying and selling with cash in hand, or in the exchange of goods or lands; or it may be delivered some time afterward.

What a Covenant Is

Again, one of the contractors may deliver the thing contracted for on his part, and leave the other to perform his part at some fixed time afterward, trusting him in the meantime. In that case, the contract on the trusting side is called a PACT, or COVENANT. Or both parties may contract now to perform later. In these cases, the one who is to perform in the time to come, being trusted, is said — when he performs — to be keeping his promise, or keeping faith; and if he voluntarily fails to perform, that is a violation of faith.

Free Gift

When the transferring of right is not mutual, but one party transfers in the hope of gaining friendship or service from another, or from that person's friends; or in hope of gaining a reputation for charity or magnanimity; or to free his mind from the pain of compassion; or in hope of reward in heaven — this is not contract, but GIFT, FREE GIFT, GRACE. These words all signify one and the same thing.

Express Signs of Contract

Signs of contract are either express or by inference. Express signs are words spoken with understanding of what they signify. Such words are either of the present or past tense — "I give," "I grant," "I have given," "I have granted," "I will that this be yours" — or of the future: "I will give," "I will grant." Words of the future are called a promise.

Signs of Contract by Inference

Signs by inference are sometimes the consequence of words, sometimes the consequence of silence, sometimes the consequence of actions, sometimes the consequence of refraining from an action. In general, a sign by inference of any contract is whatever sufficiently demonstrates the will of the contractor.

A Free Gift Passes Only by Words of the Present or Past

Words alone, if they are of the time to come and contain a bare promise, are an insufficient sign of a free gift, and therefore not binding. For if they are of the time to come — "Tomorrow I will give" — they are a sign that I have not given yet, and consequently that my right is not transferred, but remains mine until I transfer it by some other act. But if the words are of the present or past — "I have given," or "I do give, to be delivered tomorrow" — then my tomorrow's right is given away today, and that by the force of the words alone, even with no other evidence of my will.

There is a great difference in meaning between these two phrases: volo hoc tuum esse cras and cras dabo — that is, between "I will that this be yours tomorrow" and "I will give it to you tomorrow." The phrase "I will" in the first expression signifies an act of the will in the present; in the second, it signifies a promise of an act of the will to come. So the first words, being of the present, transfer a future right; the second, being of the future, transfer nothing.

But if there are other signs of the will to transfer a right besides words, then even though the gift is free, the right may pass by words of the future. For instance, if a man sets a prize for whoever comes first to the end of a race, the gift is free; and though the words are of the future, the right passes. For if he did not want his words understood that way, he should not have let the runners run.

In Contracts, Words of Past, Present, and Future All Bind

In contracts, the right passes not only where the words are of the present or past, but also where they are of the future. For all contract is mutual transfer, or exchange, of right; and therefore he who promises only — because he has already received the benefit for which he promises — is to be understood as intending the right to pass. For unless he had been content to have his words so understood, the other party would not have performed his part first. For this reason, in buying and selling and other acts of contract, a promise is equivalent to a covenant, and is therefore binding.

What Merit Is

He that performs first, in the case of a contract, is said to MERIT what he is to receive by the other's performance; and he has it as his due. Also, when a prize is offered to many, to be given only to the winner, or money is thrown into a crowd to be enjoyed by those who catch it — though this is a free gift, yet to win it, or to catch it, is to merit it, and to have it as DUE. For the right is transferred in the offering of the prize and in the throwing down of the money; it is simply not determined to whom until the outcome of the contest decides it.

But between these two sorts of merit there is this difference: in contract, I merit by virtue of my own power and the contractor's need; but in the case of free gift, I am enabled to merit only by the generosity of the giver. In contract, I merit that the contractor should part with his right; in the case of gift, I do not merit that the giver should part with his right, but only that when he has parted with it, it should be mine rather than another's.

And this, I think, is the meaning of that distinction of the Scholastics between meritum congrui and meritum condigni — merit by fitness and merit by worthiness. God Almighty has promised paradise to those men (blinkered as they are by carnal desires) who can walk through this world according to the precepts and limits he has prescribed; so they say that he who walks that way shall merit paradise ex congruo — by fitness. But because no man can demand a right to it by his own righteousness, or by any other power in himself, but only by the free grace of God, they say no man can merit paradise ex condigno — by worthiness. This, I say, I think is the meaning of that distinction. But since these disputers do not agree on the meaning of their own technical terms any longer than it serves their purposes, I will not affirm anything about what they mean. I say only this: when a gift is given indefinitely, as a prize to be contended for, he that wins it merits it, and may claim the prize as due.

When Covenants of Mutual Trust Are Invalid

If a covenant is made in which neither party performs immediately, but they trust one another, then in the condition of mere nature — which is a condition of war of every man against every man — upon any reasonable suspicion, it is void. But if there is a common power set over them both, with right and force sufficient to compel performance, it is not void.

For he that performs first has no assurance the other will perform afterward; because the bonds of words are too weak to bridle men's ambition, greed, anger, and other passions without the fear of some coercive power — and in the condition of mere nature, where all men are equal and each is judge of the justness of his own fears, no such power can possibly be supposed. And therefore he who performs first only betrays himself to his enemy — contrary to the right, which he can never abandon, of defending his life and his means of living.

But in a civil state, where a power is set up to constrain those who would otherwise violate their faith, that fear is no longer reasonable. And for that reason, he who by the covenant is to perform first is obliged to do so.

The cause of fear that makes such a covenant invalid must always be something arising after the covenant is made — some new fact, or other sign of the will not to perform. Otherwise it cannot make the covenant void. For whatever could not stop a man from promising ought not to be admitted as a hindrance to performing.

The Right to the End Contains the Right to the Means

He that transfers any right transfers, as far as lies in his power, the means of enjoying it. He that sells land is understood to transfer the grass and whatever grows upon it; nor can he that sells a mill divert the stream that drives it. And they that give a man the right of government in sovereignty are understood to give him the right of levying money to maintain soldiers, and of appointing magistrates for the administration of justice.

No Covenants with Animals

To make a covenant with brute beasts is impossible. Since they do not understand our speech, they do not understand or accept any transfer of right, nor can they transfer any right to another; and without mutual acceptance, there is no covenant.

None with God, Except by Special Revelation

To make a covenant with God is impossible, except through the mediation of those God speaks to — either by supernatural revelation, or by his lieutenants who govern under him and in his name. For otherwise we cannot know whether our covenants are accepted or not. And therefore those who vow anything contrary to any law of nature vow in vain, since it would be unjust to pay such a vow. And if the thing vowed is commanded by the law of nature, then it is not the vow that binds them, but the law.

Covenants Only Concern the Possible and the Future

The matter, or subject, of a covenant is always something that falls under deliberation — for to covenant is an act of the will, that is to say, an act, and the last act, of deliberating. It is therefore always understood to be something to come, and something judged possible for the one covenanting to perform.

And therefore, to promise what is known to be impossible is no covenant. But if something thought possible beforehand proves impossible afterward, the covenant is valid and binding — though not to the thing itself, yet to its value; or if that too is impossible, then to the honest, unfeigned endeavor of performing as much as is possible. For to more than that, no man can be obliged.

How Covenants Are Dissolved

Men are freed of their covenants in two ways: by performing, or by being forgiven. For performance is the natural end of obligation; and forgiveness is the restoring of liberty, being a transfer back of that right in which the obligation consisted.

Covenants Extorted by Fear Are Valid

Covenants entered into out of fear, in the condition of mere nature, are binding. For example, if I covenant to pay a ransom, or perform a service for my life, to an enemy, I am bound by it. For it is a contract in which one party receives the benefit of life, and the other is to receive money or service for it; and consequently, where no other law forbids the performance — as none does in the condition of mere nature — the covenant is valid.

Therefore prisoners of war, if trusted with the payment of their ransom, are obliged to pay it. And if a weaker prince makes a disadvantageous peace with a stronger one out of fear, he is bound to keep it — unless, as was said before, some new and just cause of fear arises to renew the war. And even within commonwealths, if I am forced to buy my life from a thief by promising him money, I am bound to pay it, until the civil law discharges me. For whatever I may lawfully do without obligation, I may lawfully covenant to do through fear; and what I lawfully covenant, I cannot lawfully break.

An Earlier Covenant Voids a Later One

A former covenant makes a later one void. For a man that has passed away his right to one man today does not have it to pass to another man tomorrow; and therefore the later promise passes no right, and is null.

A Covenant Not to Defend Yourself Is Void

A covenant not to defend myself from force by force is always void. For, as I have shown before, no man can transfer or lay down his right to save himself from death, wounds, and imprisonment — the avoiding of which is the only purpose of laying down any right at all. Therefore the promise of not resisting force transfers no right in any covenant, and is not binding. For though a man may covenant, "Unless I do such-and-such, kill me," he cannot covenant, "Unless I do such-and-such, I will not resist you when you come to kill me." For man by nature chooses the lesser evil — the danger of death in resisting — rather than the greater, which is certain and present death in not resisting.

And all men grant this to be true, as shown by the fact that they lead criminals to execution and to prison with armed guards, even though those criminals consented to the very law by which they are condemned.

No Man Is Obliged to Accuse Himself

A covenant to accuse oneself, without assurance of pardon, is likewise invalid. For in the condition of nature, where every man is judge, there is no place for accusation; and in the civil state, accusation is followed by punishment — which, being force, a man is not obliged not to resist.

The same is true of accusing those whose condemnation would plunge a man into misery — a father, a wife, a benefactor. For the testimony of such an accuser, if not willingly given, is presumed to be corrupted by nature, and therefore not to be received; and where a man's testimony is not to be credited, he is not bound to give it.

Accusations extracted by torture, likewise, are not to be counted as testimony. For torture is to be used only as a means of conjecture — a glimmer of light in the further examination and search for truth. What a man confesses in that case tends toward the relief of the one being tortured, not toward informing the torturers, and therefore ought not to have the credit of sufficient testimony. For whether he delivers himself by a true accusation or a false one, he does it by the right of preserving his own life.

The Purpose and Form of an Oath

The force of words being, as I have noted before, too weak to hold men to the performance of their covenants, there are in man's nature only two imaginable helps to strengthen it. These are either a fear of the consequence of breaking their word, or a glory, a pride, in appearing not to need to break it. This second is a generosity too rarely found to be counted on — especially among the pursuers of wealth, command, or sensual pleasure, which is to say the greatest part of mankind. The passion to be relied upon is fear.

Fear has two very general objects: one is the power of invisible spirits; the other is the power of the men one would offend by breaking faith. Of these two, though the first is the greater power, the fear of the second is commonly the greater fear. The fear of the first is, in every man, his own religion — which has a place in the nature of man before civil society exists. The second does not; at least, not place enough to keep men to their promises, because in the condition of mere nature, the inequality of power is revealed only by the outcome of battle.

So before the time of civil society, or during its interruption by war, there is nothing that can strengthen a covenant of peace against the temptations of greed, ambition, lust, or any other strong desire, except the fear of that invisible power which every man worships as God and fears as an avenger of his treachery. All that can be done, therefore, between two men not subject to civil power, is to put one another to swear by the God each fears. This swearing, or OATH, is a form of speech, added to a promise, by which the one who promises signifies that unless he performs, he renounces the mercy of his God, or calls down God's vengeance on himself. Such was the heathen form: "Let Jupiter kill me, as I kill this beast." So is our form: "I shall do thus and thus, so help me God." And each man adds the rites and ceremonies of his own religion, so that the fear of breaking faith may be the greater.

No Oath Except by God

From this it appears that an oath taken according to any other form or rite than that of the man who swears it is in vain, and is no oath; and that there is no swearing by anything which the swearer does not believe to be God. For though men have sometimes sworn by their kings, out of fear or flattery, they meant it to be understood that they were attributing divine honor to them. And swearing by God unnecessarily is merely profaning his name; while swearing by other things, as men do in everyday talk, is not swearing at all, but an impious habit picked up from too much vehemence in talking.

An Oath Adds Nothing to the Obligation

It appears also that the oath adds nothing to the obligation. For a covenant, if lawful, binds in the sight of God without the oath just as much as with it; and if unlawful, it does not bind at all — even though it is confirmed with an oath.

Chapter 15: The Other Laws of Nature

The Third Law of Nature: Justice

From the law of nature that obliges us to transfer to others those rights which, if we kept them, would block the peace of mankind, a third law follows. It is this: that men perform the covenants they make. Without this law, covenants are pointless — nothing but empty words. Every man would still hold his right to everything, and we would still be in the condition of war.

What Justice and Injustice Are

This law of nature is the fountain and origin of JUSTICE. Where no covenant has gone before, no right has been transferred, and every man has a right to everything — so no action can be unjust. But once a covenant is made, breaking it is unjust. The definition of injustice is exactly this, and nothing more: the not performing of a covenant. And whatever is not unjust, is just.

Justice and Property Begin with the Commonwealth

But remember what I said in the last chapter: covenants of mutual trust are invalid wherever either side has reasonable fear that the other will not perform. So although justice originates in the making of covenants, there can be no actual injustice until that fear is removed — and as long as men remain in the natural condition of war, it cannot be removed. Before the words "just" and "unjust" can have any place, there must be some coercive power that compels all men equally to keep their covenants, through the terror of a punishment greater than any benefit they could expect from breaking them. That same power must also secure the property men acquire by mutual contract, as compensation for the universal right they give up. And no such power exists before a commonwealth is erected.

You can draw the same conclusion from the standard definition of justice taught in the universities: "Justice is the constant will of giving to every man his own." Where there is no "own" — no property — there is no injustice. And where no coercive power has been erected — where there is no commonwealth — there is no property, because all men have a right to all things. Therefore where there is no commonwealth, nothing is unjust.

So the nature of justice consists in the keeping of valid covenants. But covenants only become valid when a civil power is constituted that is strong enough to compel men to keep them. And that is also the moment property begins.

Justice Is Not Contrary to Reason

The fool has said in his heart: there is no such thing as justice. Sometimes he says it with his tongue too, and argues for it seriously. His argument goes like this. Every man's survival and contentment are entrusted to his own care. So there can be no reason why a man may not do whatever he thinks will get him there. Therefore making or not making covenants, keeping or not keeping them, is not against reason — so long as it serves his benefit.

Notice what the fool is not denying. He does not deny that covenants exist, that they are sometimes broken and sometimes kept, or that breaking them is called injustice and keeping them justice. What he questions is whether injustice — setting aside the fear of God, for the same fool has said in his heart there is no God — might not sometimes square with the reason that tells every man to pursue his own good. Especially, he says, when the payoff is big enough to put a man beyond the reach not only of other men's scorn and insults, but of their power. The kingdom of God is gotten by violence, he says — but what if it could be gotten by unjust violence? Would it be against reason to take it that way, when it is impossible to be hurt by doing so? And if it is not against reason, it is not against justice — or else justice cannot be approved as good.

From this kind of reasoning, successful wickedness has come to be called virtue. There are men who condemn breaking faith in every other case, yet allow it when the prize is a kingdom. Even the pagans who believed that Saturn was deposed by his own son Jupiter still believed that this same Jupiter was the avenger of injustice. There is something similar in a piece of law in Coke's commentaries on Littleton — the great English law book — where he says that if the rightful heir to the crown stands convicted of treason, the crown shall nevertheless descend to him, and eo instante — at that very instant — the conviction becomes void. From examples like these, a man will be strongly tempted to conclude: when the heir apparent of a kingdom kills the man who holds the throne — even his own father — you may call it injustice or whatever name you please, but it can never be against reason, since all the voluntary actions of men aim at their own benefit, and the most reasonable actions are the ones that best serve their ends.

This reasoning sounds clever. It is nevertheless false.

Here is why. The question is not about mutual promises where neither side has any security of performance — where no civil power stands over the parties — because such promises are not covenants at all. The question arises only where one party has already performed, or where there is a power that can make him perform. Then we ask: is it against reason — that is, against the benefit of the other party — to perform, or not? And I say it is not against reason.

To show this, consider two things. First: when a man does something that, by everything that can be foreseen and reckoned on, tends to his own destruction, the act is not made reasonable or wise just because some accident he could not have expected happens to turn it to his benefit. Lucky is not the same as smart.

Second: in a condition of war, where every man is an enemy to every man for lack of a common power to keep them all in check, no man can hope to defend himself from destruction by his own strength or wit alone. He needs allies — confederates — and in any confederation, each member expects the same defense from the alliance that everyone else does. So a man who declares that he thinks it reasonable to cheat the people who help him can, in reason, expect no means of safety beyond his own single power. The man who breaks his covenant — and thereby declares that he thinks he may reasonably do so — cannot be received into any society that unites for peace and defense, except by the mistake of those who let him in. And once in, he cannot be kept there unless they fail to see the danger of their mistake. A man cannot reasonably count on other people's errors as the means of his security. So if he is abandoned or cast out of society, he perishes; and if he survives in society, it is only by the errors of other men — errors he could neither foresee nor count on. Either way, his policy works against the reason of his own preservation. Everyone who refrains from destroying him does so only out of ignorance of what is good for themselves.

As for the fool's example of gaining the secure and everlasting happiness of heaven by any means whatever — that is frivolous. There is only one imaginable way to gain it, and it is not breaking covenants but keeping them.

And as for the other example, gaining sovereignty by rebellion: it is obvious that even if it succeeds, it cannot reasonably be expected to succeed — the contrary is far more likely. And whoever gains power that way teaches others to gain it from him the same way. So the attempt is against reason. Justice, therefore — that is, the keeping of covenant — is a rule of reason that forbids us to do anything destructive to our life. And consequently it is a law of nature.

Some people go further still. They will not have the law of nature be the set of rules that preserve man's life on earth, but rules for attaining eternal happiness after death — and they think breaking a covenant may sometimes serve that end, and so be just and reasonable. These are the people who think it a work of religious merit to kill, depose, or rebel against the sovereign power set over them by their own consent. But there is no natural knowledge of man's condition after death, much less of any reward then waiting for breach of faith. There is only a belief, grounded on other men's saying that they know it supernaturally — or that they know people, who knew people, who knew other people, who knew it supernaturally. So breach of faith cannot be called a precept of reason or of nature.

Covenants Are Not Cancelled by the Vices of the Person They Are Made With

Others accept the keeping of faith as a law of nature, but make exceptions for certain persons — heretics, say, or people who habitually fail to keep their own covenants. This too is against reason. If some fault in a man were enough to discharge a covenant once made, the same fault should, in reason, have been enough to prevent the making of it.

The Justice of Men and the Justice of Actions

The words "just" and "unjust" mean one thing when applied to men, and another when applied to actions. Applied to men, they signify whether a man's manners — his whole way of life — conform to reason. Applied to actions, they signify whether particular actions conform to reason, regardless of the man's character.

A just man, then, is one who takes all the care he can that his actions be just; an unjust man is one who neglects that care. In our language such men are more often called righteous and unrighteous than just and unjust, but the meaning is the same. A righteous man does not lose that title by one or a few unjust actions that spring from sudden passion or from mistaking things or persons. Nor does an unrighteous man lose his character because of actions he does, or holds back from doing, out of fear — because his will is shaped not by justice but by the apparent benefit of the act.

What gives human actions the flavor of justice is a certain nobility or gallantness of courage — rarely found — by which a man scorns to owe the contentment of his life to fraud or broken promises. This justice of manners is what is meant when justice is called a virtue, and injustice a vice.

The justice of actions, by contrast, does not make men "just" but merely guiltless; and the injustice of actions — which is also called injury — makes them merely guilty.

Justice of Manners and Justice of Actions

Again: injustice of manners is the disposition or readiness to do injury. It is injustice before it ever proceeds to action, and without supposing any individual person wronged. But the injustice of an action — an injury — does suppose an individual person wronged: namely, the person to whom the covenant was made. That is why the injury is often received by one man while the damage lands on another. Suppose a master commands his servant to give money to a stranger, and the servant fails to do it. The injury is done to the master, whom the servant had covenanted to obey; the damage falls on the stranger, to whom the servant had no obligation, and whom he therefore could not injure. So too in commonwealths: private men may forgive one another their debts, but not robberies or other violence that damages them. Withholding a debt is an injury to themselves; robbery and violence are injuries to the person of the commonwealth.

Nothing Done to a Man with His Own Consent Can Be an Injury

Whatever is done to a man in accordance with his own will, signified to the doer, is no injury to him. If the doer had not given away, by some earlier covenant, his original right to do as he pleases, then there is no breach of covenant — and so no injury. And if he had, then the other man's signified will to have the thing done releases that covenant — and so, again, no injury.

Commutative and Distributive Justice

Writers divide the justice of actions into commutative and distributive. The first, they say, consists in arithmetical proportion; the second in geometrical proportion. So they place commutative justice in the equal value of the things contracted for, and distributive justice in distributing equal benefit to men of equal merit — as if it were injustice to sell something for more than you paid for it, or to give a man more than he merits.

This is wrong. The value of everything contracted for is measured by the appetite of the contractors, and so the just value is whatever they are content to give. And merit — apart from the kind created by covenant, where performance on one side merits performance on the other, which falls under commutative justice, not distributive — is not owed by justice at all. It is rewarded by grace only. So this distinction, in the sense in which it is usually expounded, is not right.

To speak properly: commutative justice is the justice of a contractor — the performance of covenant in buying and selling, hiring and renting out, lending and borrowing, exchanging, bartering, and other acts of contract.

And distributive justice is the justice of an arbitrator — the act of defining what is just. If the arbitrator performs the trust placed in him by those who made him arbitrator, he is said to distribute to every man his own. This is indeed just distribution, and may be called (though improperly) distributive justice. More properly it is called equity — which is itself a law of nature, as I will show in its place.

The Fourth Law of Nature: Gratitude

As justice depends on a prior covenant, so gratitude depends on prior grace — that is, on a prior free gift. It is the fourth law of nature, and may be put in this form: that a man who receives benefit from another out of mere grace should endeavor that the giver have no reasonable cause to regret his good will. No man gives except with the intention of some good to himself, because giving is voluntary, and the object of every voluntary act is some good to the doer. If men see that their generosity will be wasted, there will be no beginning of benevolence or trust — and therefore no mutual help, and no reconciliation of one man to another. Men would remain in the condition of war, which is contrary to the first and fundamental law of nature, the one that commands men to seek peace. The breach of this law is called ingratitude, and it has the same relation to grace that injustice has to obligation by covenant.

The Fifth Law: Mutual Accommodation, or Complaisance

A fifth law of nature is COMPLAISANCE — that is: that every man strive to accommodate himself to the rest. To understand this, consider that men differ in their fitness for society, owing to the diversity of their affections — much like stones brought together for building. A stone that is so rough and irregular in shape that it takes more room from the others than it fills itself, and so hard that it cannot easily be smoothed, hinders the building, and the builders throw it away as useless and troublesome. Just so, a man whose roughness of nature drives him to hold on to things that are superfluous to him and necessary to others, and whose stubborn passions cannot be corrected, is to be left out of society, or cast out of it, as a burden to it. Every man is supposed — not only by right but by necessity of nature — to do all he can to obtain what is necessary for his preservation. So whoever sets himself against that, for the sake of things he does not need, is guilty of the war that must follow, and acts contrary to the fundamental law of nature, which commands us to seek peace. Those who observe this law may be called SOCIABLE (the Latins call them commodi — agreeable, accommodating); those who break it: stubborn, unsociable, perverse, intractable.

The Sixth Law: Readiness to Pardon

A sixth law of nature is this: that, given assurance about the future, a man ought to pardon the past offenses of those who repent and desire it. PARDON is nothing but the granting of peace. Granted to those who persist in their hostility, it is not peace but fear. But refused to those who give assurance for the future, it is the sign of an aversion to peace — and therefore contrary to the law of nature.

The Seventh Law: In Revenge, Look Only to the Good to Come

A seventh: that in revenges — that is, in paying back evil for evil — men look not at the greatness of the evil past, but at the greatness of the good to follow. This forbids us to inflict punishment with any other purpose than the correction of the offender or the guidance of others. The law follows from the one before it, which commands pardon upon security for the future. Besides, revenge that pays no regard to example or to future benefit is mere triumphing and glorying in another's hurt, aiming at no end at all — for the end is always something still to come. Glorying to no end is vanity, and contrary to reason; and hurting without reason tends to introduce war, which is against the law of nature. Its common name is cruelty.

The Eighth Law: Against Insult

Because all signs of hatred or contempt provoke men to fight — so much so that most men would rather risk their lives than go unavenged — we may set down in the eighth place this precept for a law of nature: that no man declare hatred or contempt of another, by deed, word, look, or gesture. The breach of this law is commonly called contumely — insult.

The Ninth Law: Against Pride

The question of who is the better man has no place in the condition of mere nature, where, as I showed earlier, all men are equal. The inequality that exists now was introduced by the civil laws. I know that Aristotle, in the first book of his Politics, lays down as a foundation of his doctrine that some men are by nature more worthy to command — meaning the wiser sort, such as he took himself to be on account of his philosophy — and others to serve, meaning those with strong bodies who were not philosophers like him. As if master and servant were introduced not by the consent of men, but by difference of wit! That is against reason, and against experience too. Very few men are so foolish that they would not rather govern themselves than be governed by others. And when the wise-in-their-own-eyes contend by force with those who distrust their own wisdom, the wise do not always win — or often — or almost ever.

If nature has made men equal, then, that equality must be acknowledged. And if nature has made men unequal, the equality must still be admitted — because men who think themselves equal will not enter into conditions of peace except on equal terms. So for the ninth law of nature I set down this: that every man acknowledge another as his equal by nature. The breach of this precept is pride.

The Tenth Law: Against Arrogance

On this law depends another: that at the entrance into conditions of peace, no man require to reserve for himself any right which he is not content to have reserved to every one of the rest. Just as it is necessary for all men who seek peace to lay down certain rights of nature — that is, to give up the liberty of doing whatever they please — so it is necessary for human life to retain some rights: the right to govern their own bodies; to enjoy air, water, movement, roads to travel from place to place; and everything else without which a man cannot live, or cannot live well. If, when peace is being made, men demand for themselves what they would not grant to others, they act contrary to the previous law, which commands the acknowledgment of natural equality, and therefore against the law of nature. The observers of this law are the people we call modest; the breakers, arrogant. The Greeks call the violation of this law pleonexia — the desire for more than one's share.

The Eleventh Law: Equity
