Also, if a man is trusted to judge between man and man, it is a precept of the law of nature that he deal equally between them. Without that, the disputes of men can be settled only by war. A judge who plays favorites does everything in his power to scare men away from using judges and arbitrators at all — and so, against the fundamental law of nature, he is a cause of war.

The observance of this law — giving to each man, by equal distribution, what in reason belongs to him — is called EQUITY, and, as I said before, distributive justice. Its violation is called acception of persons — in Greek, prosopolepsia: favoritism, judging by the face rather than the case.

The Twelfth Law: Equal Use of Common Things

From this follows another law: that things which cannot be divided be enjoyed in common, if they can be — without limit, if the quantity of the thing permits; otherwise in proportion to the number of those who have a right to it. Any other arrangement makes the distribution unequal, and contrary to equity.

The Thirteenth Law: Of Lots

But some things can neither be divided nor enjoyed in common. In that case the law of nature that prescribes equity requires that the entire right — or else, if the use is to alternate, the first possession — be determined by lot. For equal distribution belongs to the law of nature, and no other means of equal distribution can be imagined.

The Fourteenth Law: Of Birthright and First Possession

There are two sorts of lots: arbitrary and natural. An arbitrary lot is one agreed on by the competitors. A natural lot is either primogeniture — birthright of the firstborn; the Greek word is kleronomia, which means "given by lot" — or first seizure.

So things that cannot be enjoyed in common or divided ought to be awarded to the first possessor, and in some cases to the firstborn, as acquired by lot.

The Fifteenth Law: Of Mediators

It is also a law of nature that all men who mediate peace be allowed safe conduct. For the law that commands peace as the end commands intercession as the means; and the means to intercession is safe conduct.

The Sixteenth Law: Submission to Arbitration

However willing men may be to observe these laws, questions may still arise about a man's action. First, was it done or not done? Second, if done, was it against the law or not? The first is called a question of fact; the second a question of right. Unless the parties to the question mutually covenant to stand by the decision of another, they are as far from peace as ever. This other person, to whose decision they submit, is called an ARBITRATOR. So it belongs to the law of nature that those in a dispute submit their right to the judgment of an arbitrator.

The Seventeenth Law: No Man Is His Own Judge

Since every man is presumed to do all things for his own benefit, no man is a fit arbitrator in his own cause. And even if he were perfectly fit, equity grants equal benefit to each party — so if one of them is admitted as judge, the other must be admitted too, and the dispute, which is the cause of war, remains. That is against the law of nature.

The Eighteenth Law: No Judge Who Has a Stake in the Outcome

For the same reason, no man ought to be accepted as arbitrator in any cause if the victory of one party visibly brings him greater profit, honor, or pleasure than the victory of the other. He has taken a bribe — an unavoidable bribe, perhaps, but a bribe nonetheless — and no man can be obliged to trust him. So again the dispute, and the condition of war, remain — contrary to the law of nature.

The Nineteenth Law: Of Witnesses

And in a dispute over fact, since the judge has no reason to credit one party more than the other, he must (if there is no other evidence) give credit to a third witness — or a third and a fourth, or more. Otherwise the question stays undecided and is left to force, contrary to the law of nature.

These are the laws of nature that dictate peace as the means of preserving men in multitudes — the ones that concern the doctrine of civil society. There are other things that tend to the destruction of particular men — drunkenness, and all the other forms of intemperance — which may therefore also be counted among the things the law of nature forbids. But they are not necessary to mention here, and not relevant enough to this place.

A Rule for Testing the Laws of Nature Easily

This may seem too subtle a chain of deductions for all men to take notice of — most men being too busy getting food, and the rest too careless to understand. But so that no man can plead ignorance, the laws have been compressed into one easy sum, intelligible to even the humblest capacity. It is this: Do not that to another, which thou wouldest not have done to thyself. This rule shows a man that, to learn the laws of nature, he need do only one thing. When he is weighing the actions of other men against his own, and theirs seem too heavy, let him put their actions into the other pan of the scale and his own into theirs — so that his own passions and self-love add nothing to the weight. Then there is not one of these laws of nature that will not appear to him perfectly reasonable.

The Laws of Nature Always Bind in Conscience — but in Action, Only When It Is Safe

The laws of nature oblige in foro interno — in the internal court, the court of conscience. That is, they bind a man to desire that they take effect. But in foro externo — in the external court, the court of action — they do not always bind. A man who was modest and tractable, and kept all his promises, in a time and place where no one else did, would only make himself prey to others and bring about his own certain ruin — contrary to the foundation of all the laws of nature, which aim at nature's preservation. And on the other side, a man who has security that others will observe those laws toward him, but does not observe them himself, is seeking not peace but war — and consequently the destruction of his nature by violence.

And whatever laws bind in foro interno can be broken not only by an act contrary to the law, but also by an act in accordance with it — in the case where a man believes it to be contrary. For although his action follows the law, his purpose was against the law; and where the obligation is in foro interno, that is a breach.

The Laws of Nature Are Eternal

The laws of nature are immutable and eternal. Injustice, ingratitude, arrogance, pride, iniquity, favoritism, and the rest can never be made lawful. For it can never be that war preserves life and peace destroys it.

And Yet Easy

These same laws are easy to observe, because they oblige only to a desire and an endeavor — I mean an unfeigned and constant endeavor. Since they require nothing but the endeavor, whoever endeavors to fulfill them, fulfills them. And he that fulfills the law is just.

The Science of These Laws Is the True Moral Philosophy

The science of these laws is the true and only moral philosophy. For moral philosophy is nothing other than the science of what is good and evil in the conversation and society of mankind. Good and evil are names that signify our desires and aversions, which differ as men's temperaments, customs, and doctrines differ. Different men disagree not only in their judgments about what is pleasant or unpleasant to taste, smell, hearing, touch, and sight, but about what conforms or fails to conform to reason in the actions of common life. Indeed, the same man at different times differs from himself: at one time he praises — that is, calls good — what at another time he condemns and calls evil. From this arise disputes, controversies, and at last war.

So as long as a man is in the condition of mere nature, which is a condition of war, private appetite is the measure of good and evil. And yet all men agree on this much: that peace is good. And therefore the way or means of peace — which, as I have shown, are justice, gratitude, modesty, equity, mercy, and the rest of the laws of nature — are good too; that is to say, moral virtues, and their contrary vices, evil. Now the science of virtue and vice is moral philosophy; and therefore the true doctrine of the laws of nature is the true moral philosophy.

The writers on moral philosophy acknowledge the same virtues and vices. But they do not see what their goodness consists in, nor that they come to be praised as the means of peaceful, sociable, and comfortable living. So they locate virtue in a moderate middle degree of the passions — as if it were not the cause, but the degree, of daring that made courage; as if it were not the cause, but the size, of a gift that made generosity.

These dictates of reason men usually call by the name of laws — but improperly. They are only conclusions, or theorems, about what conduces to men's preservation and defense; whereas law, properly speaking, is the word of one who by right has command over others. Yet if we consider these same theorems as delivered in the word of God, who by right commands all things — then they are properly called laws.

Chapter 16: Persons, Authors, and Things Personated

What a Person Is

A PERSON is one whose words or actions are considered either as his own, or as representing the words or actions of another man — or of any other thing to which they are attributed, whether truly or by fiction.

Natural and Artificial Persons

When the words and actions are considered as his own, he is called a NATURAL PERSON. When they are considered as representing the words and actions of another, he is a feigned or ARTIFICIAL PERSON.

Where the Word "Person" Comes From

The word "person" is Latin. Where we use it, the Greeks had prosopon, which means the face — just as persona in Latin means the disguise, the outward appearance of a man, counterfeited on the stage; and sometimes, more particularly, the part of the costume that disguises the face: a mask or visor. From the stage, the word was carried over to any representer of speech and action, in courtrooms as well as theaters. A person, then, is the same thing as an actor — both on the stage and in everyday life. To PERSONATE is to act, or to represent, either yourself or someone else; and he who acts another is said to bear his person, or to act in his name. Cicero uses the word in this sense when he says, "Unus sustineo tres personas: mei, adversarii, et judicis — I bear three persons: my own, my adversary's, and the judge's." A man in this role goes by different names on different occasions: a representer or representative, a lieutenant, a vicar, an attorney, a deputy, a procurator, an actor, and the like.

Actor, Author, Authority

Among artificial persons, some have their words and actions owned by those they represent. In that case the person is the ACTOR — the one who acts — and the one who owns his words and actions is the AUTHOR; and the actor acts by authority. For the same relation that, when we speak of goods and possessions, is called ownership (in Latin dominus, in Greek kurios — owner, master), is called authorship when we speak of actions. And just as the right of possession is called dominion, so the right of doing any action is called AUTHORITY. By authority, then, is always understood a right of doing some act; and done by authority means done by commission or license from the one whose right it is.

Covenants Made by Authority Bind the Author

From this it follows that when the actor makes a covenant by authority, he binds the author by it, no less than if the author had made it himself, and no less subjects him to all its consequences. So everything said earlier (in chapter 14) about the nature of covenants between man and man in their natural capacity is true also when covenants are made by their actors, representers, or procurators who have authority from them — as far as their commission extends, but no further.

And therefore anyone who makes a covenant with the actor, or representer, without knowing what authority he actually has, does so at his own peril. For no man is obliged by a covenant of which he is not the author — nor, consequently, by a covenant made against, or beyond, the authority he gave.

But Not the Actor

When the actor does something against the law of nature by command of the author, and he is obliged by a former covenant to obey him, then it is not he but the author who breaks the law of nature. For though the action is against the law of nature, it is not his action. On the contrary: to refuse to do it would be against the law of nature that forbids breaking covenants.

The Authority Must Be Shown

And he who makes a covenant with the author through the mediation of the actor, not knowing what authority the actor has but taking him at his word, is no longer obliged if that authority is not made evident to him on demand. For a covenant made with the author is not valid without the author's own counter-assurance. But if the man making the covenant knew in advance that he could expect no assurance beyond the actor's word, then the covenant is valid — because in that case the actor makes himself the author. And so, just as the covenant obliges the author and not the actor when the authority is evident, it obliges the actor alone when the authority is faked; for then there is no author but himself.

Inanimate Things Can Be Personated

There are few things that cannot be represented by fiction. Inanimate things — a church, a hospital, a bridge — may be personated by a rector, a master, or an overseer. But inanimate things cannot be authors, and therefore cannot give authority to their actors. Still, the actors may have authority to maintain such things, given to them by their owners or governors. And for this reason, such things cannot be personated until there is some state of civil government.

Those Without the Use of Reason

Likewise children, fools, and madmen who have no use of reason may be personated by guardians or curators. But they can be authors, during that time, of no action done by them — not unless, when they recover the use of reason, they judge that action reasonable. Yet during the period of folly, whoever has the right of governing them may give authority to the guardian. But this, again, has no place except in a civil state; for before such a state exists, there is no dominion of persons.

False Gods

An idol, a mere figment of the brain, may be personated — as the gods of the heathen were. Officers appointed by the state personated them; and the gods held possessions, goods, and rights which men from time to time dedicated and consecrated to them. But idols cannot be authors, for an idol is nothing. The authority came from the state. And therefore, before the introduction of civil government, the gods of the heathen could not be personated.

The True God

The true God may be personated. And he was: first, by Moses, who governed the Israelites — they were not his people, but God's people — not in his own name, with "Hoc dicit Moses — Thus says Moses," but in God's name, with "Hoc dicit Dominus — Thus says the Lord." Secondly, by the Son of man, his own Son, our Blessed Savior Jesus Christ, who came to bring back the Jews and bring all nations into the kingdom of his Father — not as from himself, but as one sent from his Father. And thirdly, by the Holy Ghost, the Comforter, speaking and working in the Apostles — which Holy Ghost was a Comforter who came not of himself, but was sent by them both, and proceeded from them both.

How a Multitude of Men Becomes One Person

A multitude of men is made one person when they are represented by one man, or one person — provided it is done with the consent of every single individual in that multitude. For it is the unity of the representer, not the unity of the represented, that makes the person one. And it is the representer who bears the person — and only one person. Unity in a multitude cannot be understood in any other way.

Every One of Them Is an Author

And because the multitude is naturally not one but many, its members cannot be understood as one author, but as many authors, of everything their representative says or does in their name. Every man gives their common representer authority from himself in particular, and owns all the actions the representer performs — if they give him authority without limit. Otherwise, when they limit him in what he shall represent them in, and how far, none of them owns more than what they gave him commission to act. Without that one representer, there is no "one action of many men" — only the many actions of one multitude.

A Representative May Be Many Men, Made One by Majority of Voices

If the representative consists of many men, the voice of the greater number must be taken as the voice of them all. For if the lesser number pronounces, say, in the affirmative, and the greater in the negative, there will be more than enough negatives to cancel the affirmatives; and so the surplus of negatives, standing uncontradicted, is the only voice the representative has.

Representatives with an Even Number Are Often Useless

A representative of an even number — especially when the number is not large, so that the contradictory voices are frequently equal — is for that reason frequently mute and incapable of action. Yet in some cases, contradictory voices equal in number can still settle a question. In condemning or absolving, for instance, an equal split of votes absolves precisely by failing to condemn; but it does not, on the contrary, condemn by failing to absolve. For when a case has been heard, not to condemn is to absolve; but to say that not absolving is condemning is not true. The same holds in deliberating whether to carry out an execution at once or to defer it to another time: when the voices are equal, not decreeing execution is a decree of delay.

The Negative Voice

Or suppose the number is odd — three or more, whether men or assemblies — and each one has, by a negative voice, the authority to cancel the effect of all the affirmative voices of the rest. This number is no representative at all. Because of the diversity of men's opinions and interests, it becomes — often, and in matters of the greatest consequence — a mute person, unfit for many things, and above all unfit for the government of a multitude, especially in time of war.

Of authors there are two sorts. The first is the author simply so called, whom I defined above: the one who owns the action of another simply. The second is the one who owns an action or covenant of another conditionally — that is, he undertakes to do it himself if the other does not do it at, or before, a certain time. These conditional authors are generally called SURETIES — in Latin, fidejussores and sponsores; for debt in particular, praedes; and for appearance before a judge or magistrate, vades.

Part II: Of Commonwealth

Chapter 17: The Causes, Generation, and Definition of a Commonwealth

The Purpose of a Commonwealth: Personal Security

Why would men — who naturally love liberty, and naturally love power over others — ever agree to put themselves under restraint, the kind of restraint we see them living under in commonwealths? The final cause, the end, the whole design of it, is foresight: they can see that this is how they preserve their own lives and win a more contented life in the bargain. In other words, this is how they get themselves out of that miserable condition of war which, as I have shown, follows necessarily from the natural passions of men whenever there is no visible power to keep them all in awe — no power to bind them, through fear of punishment, to keep their covenants and observe those laws of nature I set down in chapters fourteen and fifteen.

Security Cannot Come from the Law of Nature Alone

For the laws of nature — justice, fairness, modesty, mercy, and, summing them all up, do to others as you would have done to you — are, by themselves, contrary to our natural passions. Without the terror of some power to make them be observed, they stand no chance against the passions that pull us toward partiality, pride, revenge, and the rest. And covenants, without the sword, are but words, with no strength to secure a man at all.

So despite the laws of nature — which everyone keeps when he is willing to keep them, which is to say, when he can do so safely — if no power has been set up, or none great enough for our security, then every man will rely, and may lawfully rely, on his own strength and cunning to guard himself against everyone else. And so it has been. In every place where men lived in small families, robbing and plundering one another was a trade. Far from being considered a violation of the law of nature, the greater the plunder a man took, the greater his honor. The only laws men observed in that business were the laws of honor: spare cruelty, leave men their lives and their farming tools. And what small families did then, cities and kingdoms — which are only larger families — do now. For their own security they enlarge their dominions on every pretext of danger, fear of invasion, or aid that might be given to invaders. They work as hard as they can to subdue or weaken their neighbors by open force and secret schemes; and because they have no other safeguard, they do it justly — and are remembered for it in later ages with honor.

Nor from Joining a Few Men or Families Together

Nor does joining together in small numbers give men this security. When numbers are small, a small addition on one side or the other makes such a large difference in strength that it is enough to decide the victory — and that very fact invites invasion. The multitude big enough to trust for our security is not fixed at any particular number. It is measured against the enemy we fear, and it is big enough when the enemy's advantage is not so visible and so obvious that it would decide the outcome of a war and so tempt him to attack.

Nor from a Great Multitude, Unless Directed by One Judgment

And let the multitude be as great as you please: if each man's actions are directed by his own private judgment and his own private appetites, they can expect no defense and no protection from it — not against a common enemy, and not against the injuries they do one another. Because they are divided in their opinions about how their strength should best be used, they do not help each other; they hinder each other, and through mutual opposition they grind their strength down to nothing. The result is that they are easily subdued by a very few who do agree together — and when there is no common enemy, they make war on each other over their private interests.

Here is the proof. If we could suppose that a great multitude of men would agree to observe justice and the other laws of nature without a common power to keep them all in awe, then we might as well suppose all mankind would do the same. And in that case there would be no civil government or commonwealth at all — and no need for one — because there would be peace without subjection.

And the Direction Must Be Continual

Nor is it enough — for the security men want to last their whole lives — that they be governed and directed by one judgment for a limited time only, as for one battle or one war. They may win a victory through their united effort against a foreign enemy. But afterward, when they have no common enemy left, or when the man one faction calls an enemy another faction calls a friend, the difference in their interests must necessarily dissolve them, and they fall back into a war among themselves.

Why Certain Creatures, Without Reason or Speech, Live in Society Without Any Coercive Power

It is true that certain living creatures, such as bees and ants, live sociably with one another — which is why Aristotle counts them among the political creatures — and yet they have no direction except their own particular judgments and appetites, and no speech by which one of them can tell another what it thinks would serve the common benefit. So someone may well want to know why mankind cannot do the same. To which I answer:

First, men are continually in competition for honor and dignity, and these creatures are not. Among men, therefore, that competition breeds envy and hatred, and finally war. Among them, it does not.

Second, among these creatures the common good does not differ from the private good. Each one, being naturally inclined to its own private benefit, serves the common benefit at the same time. But man — whose joy consists in comparing himself with other men — can relish nothing but what makes him stand out.

Third, these creatures, not having the use of reason as man does, do not see, and do not think they see, any fault in how their common business is managed. Whereas among men there are a great many who think themselves wiser and abler to govern the public than the rest. These men strive to reform and innovate, one this way, another that way — and so they drag the commonwealth into distraction and civil war.

Fourth, these creatures, though they have some use of voice to make their desires and feelings known to one another, lack that art of words by which some men can present good in the likeness of evil, and evil in the likeness of good — and inflate or shrink the apparent size of either — unsettling men and disturbing their peace whenever it pleases them.

Fifth, irrational creatures cannot tell the difference between injury and damage — between being wronged and merely being hurt. So as long as they are at ease, they take no offense at their fellows. Whereas man is most troublesome precisely when he is most at ease, for that is when he loves to show off his wisdom and second-guess the actions of those who govern the commonwealth.

Lastly, the agreement of these creatures is natural. The agreement of men is by covenant only — which is artificial. So it is no wonder that something more than covenant is required to make their agreement constant and lasting: a common power, to keep them in awe and to direct their actions toward the common benefit.

The Generation of a Commonwealth

There is only one way to erect such a common power — one able to defend them from invasion by foreigners and from the injuries they do one another, and so to secure them well enough that by their own industry, and by the fruits of the earth, they may feed themselves and live contentedly. That way is this: to confer all their power and strength upon one man, or upon one assembly of men, that can reduce all their wills, by majority voice, to one will.

This means appointing one man, or one assembly of men, to bear their person — to be their representative. And it means that every one of them owns and acknowledges himself to be the author of whatever the bearer of their person does, or causes to be done, in everything that concerns the common peace and safety. Every man submits his will to that man's will, and his judgment to that man's judgment.

This is more than consent. It is more than concord. It is a real unity of them all in one and the same person, made by covenant of every man with every man — as if every man said to every man: "I authorize and give up my right of governing myself to this man, or to this assembly of men, on this condition: that you give up your right to him too, and authorize all his actions in the same way."

When this is done, the multitude so united in one person is called a COMMONWEALTH — in Latin, CIVITAS. This is the generation of that great LEVIATHAN, or rather (to speak more reverently) of that Mortal God, to which we owe, under the Immortal God, our peace and defense. For by this authority, given to him by every individual man in the commonwealth, he has so much power and strength conferred on him that the terror of it enables him to shape the wills of them all — toward peace at home, and mutual aid against their enemies abroad.

The Definition of a Commonwealth

In him consists the essence of the commonwealth, which, to define it, is this: one person, of whose acts a great multitude, by mutual covenants with one another, have every one made themselves the author, so that he may use the strength and means of them all, as he thinks expedient, for their peace and common defense.

Sovereign and Subject Defined

The one who bears this person is called the SOVEREIGN, and is said to have sovereign power. Everyone besides him is his SUBJECT.

There are two ways of attaining this sovereign power. One is by natural force: as when a man makes his children submit themselves, and their children, to his government, because he can destroy them if they refuse; or when by war a man subdues his enemies to his will, granting them their lives on that condition. The other is when men agree among themselves to submit to some man, or assembly of men, voluntarily, trusting that he will protect them against all others.

This second kind may be called a political commonwealth, or a commonwealth by institution — by agreement. The first kind is a commonwealth by acquisition — by conquest. And I shall speak first of a commonwealth by agreement.

Chapter 18: The Rights of Sovereigns by Institution

What It Means to Institute a Commonwealth

A commonwealth is said to be INSTITUTED — established by the agreement of the people themselves, rather than by conquest — when a multitude of men agree and covenant, every one with every one, as follows: that whatever man or assembly of men the major part chooses to bear the person of them all — that is, to be their representative — every one of them, the man who voted for it and the man who voted against it alike, shall authorize all the actions and judgments of that man or assembly, exactly as if they were his own. And the purpose of all this is to live peaceably among themselves, and to be protected against other men.

What Follows from This Institution

From this institution of a commonwealth are derived all the rights and powers of the man, or assembly of men, on whom the sovereign power is conferred by the consent of the assembled people.

1. The Subjects Cannot Change the Form of Government

First, because they covenant, it is to be understood that they are not bound by any former covenant to anything that contradicts this one. Consequently, those who have already instituted a commonwealth — being bound by covenant to own the actions and judgments of one sovereign — cannot lawfully make a new covenant among themselves to obey anyone else, in anything whatsoever, without the sovereign's permission.

So the subjects of a monarch cannot, without his leave, cast off monarchy and return to the confusion of a disunited multitude; nor can they transfer their person from the man who bears it to another man, or to another assembly. For they are bound, every man to every man, to own — and to be counted author of — everything that the man who is already their sovereign shall do, and judge fit to be done. If even one man dissented, all the rest would be breaking the covenant they made with that man, which is injustice. Besides, each of them has given the sovereignty to the one who bears their person; so if they depose him, they take from him what is his own — and that, again, is injustice.

What's more, if a man who attempts to depose his sovereign is killed or punished by him for the attempt, he is the author of his own punishment, since by the institution he is the author of everything his sovereign does. And because it is injustice for a man to do anything for which he may be punished by his own authority, he is unjust on that ground as well.

Some men have excused their disobedience to their sovereign by claiming a new covenant — made not with men, but with God. This too is unjust. For there is no covenant with God except through the mediation of somebody who represents God's person; and nobody does that but God's lieutenant, who holds the sovereignty under God. But this pretense of a covenant with God is so obvious a lie, even in the pretenders' own consciences, that it is the act not merely of an unjust disposition, but of a vile and cowardly one.

2. Sovereign Power Cannot Be Forfeited

Secondly, because the right of bearing the person of them all is given to the man they make sovereign by a covenant made only between subject and subject — not by any covenant between him and any of them — there can be no breach of covenant on the sovereign's part. And consequently none of his subjects can be freed from subjection by any pretense that he has forfeited his power.

That the man who is made sovereign makes no covenant with his subjects beforehand is plain to see. Either he would have to make it with the whole multitude as one party to the covenant, or he would have to make a separate covenant with every individual man. With the whole multitude as one party, it is impossible — because before the institution they are not yet one person. And if he makes as many separate covenants as there are men, those covenants become void the moment he has the sovereignty; for whatever act any one of them might point to as a breach is the act both of the sovereign and of all the rest, since it was done in the person, and by the right, of every one of them in particular.

Besides, if any one or more of them claim the sovereign broke the covenant made at his institution, and others — or even one other subject, or the sovereign alone — claim there was no such breach, there is in this case no judge to decide the controversy. So the question returns to the sword; and every man recovers the right of protecting himself by his own strength — contrary to the very design they had in the institution. It is therefore pointless to grant sovereignty by way of a prior covenant.

The opinion that any monarch receives his power by covenant — that is, on condition — comes from failing to understand this easy truth: covenants, being nothing but words and breath, have no force to oblige, contain, constrain, or protect any man except what they get from the public sword — that is, from the untied hands of the man or assembly that holds the sovereignty, whose actions are vouched for by them all, and performed by the strength of them all, united in him.

But when an assembly of men is made sovereign, nobody imagines any such covenant to have passed at the institution. No man is so dull as to say, for example, that the people of Rome made a covenant with the Romans to hold the sovereignty on such-and-such conditions — and that if the conditions were not met, the Romans might lawfully depose the Roman people. That men fail to see that the reasoning is the same in a monarchy as in a popular government comes from the ambition of some, who are kinder to government by an assembly — in which they may hope to take part — than to monarchy, which they have no hope of ever enjoying.

3. No One Can Justly Protest Against the Institution of the Sovereign Declared by the Major Part

Thirdly, because the major part has, by consenting voices, declared a sovereign, the man who dissented must now consent with the rest. That is, he must be content to own all the actions the sovereign shall do — or else justly be destroyed by the rest. For if he voluntarily entered the congregation of those who were assembled, he sufficiently declared by that act his will to stand by whatever the major part should ordain — and therefore tacitly covenanted to do so. So if he refuses to stand by it, or protests against any of their decrees, he acts contrary to his covenant, and therefore unjustly. And whether he was part of the congregation or not, and whether his consent was asked or not, he must either submit to their decrees or be left in the condition of war he was in before — in which anyone at all might destroy him without injustice.

4. The Sovereign's Actions Cannot Be Justly Accused by the Subject

Fourthly, because by this institution every subject is author of all the actions and judgments of the instituted sovereign, it follows that whatever the sovereign does can be no injury to any of his subjects, nor ought he to be accused of injustice by any of them. For a man who does something by authority from another does no injury, in doing it, to the one by whose authority he acts. But by this institution of a commonwealth, every particular man is author of everything the sovereign does. So a man who complains of injury from his sovereign is complaining of something he himself is the author of; and therefore he ought to accuse no one but himself — no, and not even himself, because to do injury to one's self is impossible.

It is true that those who have sovereign power may commit iniquity — moral wrong. But they cannot commit injustice, or injury in the proper meaning of the word.

5. Whatever the Sovereign Does Is Unpunishable by the Subject

Fifthly, and as a consequence of the last point, no man who has sovereign power can justly be put to death, or punished in any other manner, by his subjects. For seeing every subject is author of the actions of his sovereign, he would be punishing another for actions committed by himself.

6. The Sovereign Is Judge of What Is Necessary for the Peace and Defense of His Subjects

And because the end of this institution is the peace and defense of them all, and whoever has a right to the end has a right to the means, it belongs by right to whatever man or assembly holds the sovereignty to be judge both of the means of peace and defense, and of the hindrances and disturbances to them; and to do whatever he shall think necessary to be done — both in advance, to preserve peace and security by preventing discord at home and hostility from abroad, and, when peace and security are lost, to recover them. And therefore:

And Judge of What Doctrines Are Fit to Be Taught

Sixthly, it is annexed to the sovereignty to be judge of which opinions and doctrines work against peace, and which promote it; and consequently, on what occasions, how far, and in what matters men are to be trusted when speaking to multitudes of people; and who shall examine the doctrines of all books before they are published. For the actions of men proceed from their opinions; and in the good governing of opinions consists the good governing of men's actions, with a view to their peace and concord.

And though in matters of doctrine nothing ought to be regarded but the truth, this is not in conflict with regulating doctrine for the sake of peace. For a doctrine in conflict with peace can no more be true than peace and concord can be against the law of nature.

It is true that in a commonwealth where false doctrines have, through the negligence or clumsiness of governors and teachers, become generally accepted over time, the contrary truths may be generally offensive. Yet even the most sudden and rough bursting-in of a new truth never breaks the peace — it only sometimes wakes the war. For men governed so loosely that they dare take up arms to defend or introduce an opinion are still at war; their condition is not peace, but only a cease-fire out of fear of one another; they live, as it were, perpetually on the edge of the battlefield. It belongs, therefore, to the holder of the sovereign power to be judge — or to appoint all the judges — of opinions and doctrines, as a thing necessary to peace, in order to prevent discord and civil war.

7. The Right of Making Rules, So That Every Subject Knows What Is His Own

Seventhly, annexed to the sovereignty is the whole power of prescribing the rules by which every man may know what goods he may enjoy, and what actions he may do, without being harassed by any of his fellow subjects. And this is what men call PROPERTY. For before the constitution of sovereign power, as I have already shown, all men had a right to all things — which necessarily causes war. So this property, being necessary to peace and depending on sovereign power, is the act of that power, made for the sake of public peace. These rules of property (or meum and tuum — mine and thine), and of good, evil, lawful, and unlawful in the actions of subjects, are the CIVIL LAWS — that is, the laws of each commonwealth in particular; though the name "civil law" is now restricted to the ancient civil laws of the city of Rome — for Rome being the head of a great part of the world, her laws in those days were, in these parts, the civil law.

8. To Him Also Belongs the Right of All Judicature and the Deciding of Controversies

Eighthly, annexed to the sovereignty is the right of judicature — that is, of hearing and deciding all controversies that may arise concerning law, whether civil or natural, or concerning fact. For without the deciding of controversies there is no protection of one subject against the injuries of another; the laws concerning mine and thine are in vain; and there remains to every man, from his natural and necessary appetite for his own preservation, the right of protecting himself by his private strength — which is the condition of war, and contrary to the end for which every commonwealth is instituted.

9. And of Making War and Peace as He Shall Think Best

Ninthly, annexed to the sovereignty is the right of making war and peace with other nations and commonwealths — that is, of judging when it serves the public good, and how great the forces to be assembled, armed, and paid for that end should be, and of levying money on the subjects to cover the expense. For the power by which the people are to be defended consists in their armies; and the strength of an army consists in the union of their strength under one command — which command the instituted sovereign therefore has, because the command of the militia, even without any other institution, makes the man who holds it sovereign. And therefore, whoever may be made general of an army, the holder of the sovereign power is always generalissimo — the supreme commander.

10. And of Choosing All Counselors and Ministers, Both of Peace and War

Tenthly, annexed to the sovereignty is the choosing of all counselors, ministers, magistrates, and officers, both in peace and in war. For since the sovereign is charged with the end — the common peace and defense — he is understood to have the power to use whatever means he shall think most fit to discharge that duty.

11. And of Rewarding and Punishing — at His Discretion, Where No Former Law Has Set the Measure

Eleventhly, to the sovereign is committed the power of rewarding with riches or with honor, and of punishing with bodily or monetary punishment, or with public disgrace, every subject — according to the law he has previously made; or, if no law has been made, according to what he shall judge will most encourage men to serve the commonwealth, or deter them from doing it disservice.

12. And of Honor and Rank

Lastly, consider what value men are naturally apt to set on themselves, what respect they expect from others, and how little they value other men. From all this there continually arise among them rivalry, quarrels, factions, and at last war — to the destroying of one another, and the weakening of their strength against a common enemy. So it is necessary that there be laws of honor, and a public scale of the worth of men who have deserved well of the commonwealth, or are able to deserve well of it; and that there be force in the hands of someone or other to put those laws into execution. But it has already been shown that not only the whole militia — the armed forces of the commonwealth — but also the judging of all controversies, is annexed to the sovereignty. To the sovereign, therefore, it also belongs to give titles of honor, and to appoint what rank of place and dignity each man shall hold, and what signs of respect, in public or private meetings, they shall give to one another.

These Rights Are Indivisible

These are the rights that make up the essence of sovereignty; and they are the marks by which a man may discern in what man, or assembly of men, the sovereign power is placed and resides. For these rights are incommunicable and inseparable. The power to coin money, to dispose of the estates and persons of underage heirs, to have pre-emption — the right of first purchase — in markets, and all other prerogatives granted by statute, may be transferred away by the sovereign, and the power to protect his subjects still retained. But if he transfers the militia, he retains the judicature in vain, for want of the power to execute the laws. If he grants away the power of raising money, the militia is in vain. If he gives away the government of doctrines, men will be frightened into rebellion by the fear of spirits. So if we consider any one of these rights, we shall quickly see that holding on to all the rest will produce no effect in preserving peace and justice — the end for which all commonwealths are instituted.

It is of this division that it is said, "A kingdom divided in itself cannot stand." For unless this division comes first, division into opposing armies can never happen. If the opinion had not first taken hold of the greatest part of England that these powers were divided between the King, the Lords, and the House of Commons, the people would never have been divided, and fallen into this civil war — first between those who disagreed about politics, and afterward between the dissenters about the liberty of religion. And the war has so instructed men on this point of sovereign right that there are few now in England who do not see that these rights are inseparable. So it will be generally acknowledged at the next return of peace — and so it will continue, until their miseries are forgotten, and no longer, unless the common people are taught better than they have been so far.

And No Grant Can Pass Them Away Without a Direct Renouncing of the Sovereign Power

And because these are essential and inseparable rights, it follows necessarily that whatever the words by which any of them may seem to be granted away, the grant is void — unless the sovereign power itself is renounced in direct terms, and the name of sovereign is no longer given by the grantees to the man who granted them. For when he has granted away all he can, if we grant back the sovereignty, everything is restored, as inseparably annexed to it.

The Power and Honor of Subjects Vanish in the Presence of the Sovereign Power

Since this great authority is indivisible, and inseparably annexed to the sovereignty, there is little ground for the opinion of those who say of sovereign kings that though they are singulis majores — of greater power than each of their subjects taken one by one — they are nonetheless universis minores — of less power than all of them together. For if by "all together" they do not mean the collective body as one person, then "all together" and "every one" signify the same thing, and the statement is absurd. But if by "all together" they understand them as one person — which person the sovereign bears — then the power of all together is the same as the sovereign's power, and again the statement is absurd. This absurdity they see well enough when the sovereignty rests in an assembly of the people; in a monarch they fail to see it — and yet the power of sovereignty is the same wherever it is placed.

And as with the power, so also the honor of the sovereign ought to be greater than that of any one of his subjects, or of all of them together. For the sovereignty is the fountain of honor. The dignities of lord, earl, duke, and prince are its creatures. As servants are equal in the presence of their master, and without any honor at all, so are subjects in the presence of the sovereign. And though they shine, some more, some less, when they are out of his sight, in his presence they shine no more than the stars in the presence of the sun.

Sovereign Power Is Not as Harmful as the Lack of It — and the Harm Comes Mostly from Not Readily Submitting to a Lesser Evil

But here a man may object that the condition of subjects is very miserable, exposed as they are to the lusts and other irregular passions of the man, or men, who hold such unlimited power in their hands. Commonly, those who live under a monarch think it is the fault of monarchy; and those who live under the government of democracy, or some other sovereign assembly, attribute all the inconvenience to that form of commonwealth — when in fact the power is the same in all forms, provided the forms are complete enough to protect them.

Such objectors fail to consider that the estate of man can never be without some inconvenience or other; and that the greatest inconvenience that can possibly happen to the people in general, under any form of government, is scarcely noticeable next to the miseries and horrible calamities that accompany a civil war — or that dissolute condition of masterless men, without subjection to laws, and without a coercive power to tie their hands from plunder and revenge.

Nor do they consider that the heaviest pressure from sovereign governors comes not from any delight or profit the governors can expect from damaging or weakening their subjects — for in their subjects' vigor consists their own strength — but from the subjects themselves: who, contributing unwillingly to their own defense, make it necessary for their governors to draw from them whatever they can in time of peace, so that they may have the means, in any emergency or sudden need, to resist their enemies, or to gain advantage over them.

For all men are by nature equipped with remarkable magnifying glasses — their passions and their self-love — through which every little payment looks like a great grievance. But they lack the telescopes — namely, moral and civil science — to see, far off, the miseries that hang over them, and that cannot be avoided without such payments.

Chapter 19: The Several Kinds of Commonwealth by Agreement, and the Succession to Sovereign Power

There Are Only Three Forms of Commonwealth

The difference between commonwealths lies in the difference of the sovereign — the person who represents each and every one of the multitude. And since sovereignty rests either in one man or in an assembly of more than one; and since into that assembly either every man has the right to enter, or not every man but only certain men distinguished from the rest; it is clear that there can be only three kinds of commonwealth. For the representative must be either one man or more than one; and if more, then it is an assembly of all, or of only a part.

When the representative is one man, the commonwealth is a MONARCHY. When it is an assembly of all who are willing to come together, it is a DEMOCRACY, or popular commonwealth. When it is an assembly of only a part, it is called an ARISTOCRACY.

There can be no other kind of commonwealth. For either one, or more, or all must hold the sovereign power — which I have shown to be indivisible — entire.

Tyranny and Oligarchy Are Just Other Names for Monarchy and Aristocracy

There are other names for government in the histories and the books of policy — tyranny, for instance, and oligarchy. But these are not the names of other forms of government. They are the names of the same forms, disliked. People who are discontented under a monarchy call it tyranny; people displeased with an aristocracy call it oligarchy; and likewise, people who feel aggrieved under a democracy call it anarchy — which means the absence of government. Yet I do not think anyone believes that the absence of government is some new kind of government. And by the same reasoning, no one should believe that a government is one kind when they like it, and another kind when they dislike it or feel oppressed by the governors.

Subordinate Representatives Are Dangerous

Clearly, men in a state of absolute liberty may, if they please, give authority to one man to represent every one of them, just as readily as they may give such authority to any assembly of men whatsoever. They may therefore subject themselves, if they think it good, to a monarch just as absolutely as to any other representative.

It follows that where a sovereign power has already been set up, there can be no other representative of the same people, except for certain particular purposes, limited by the sovereign. To allow otherwise would be to erect two sovereigns, and to have every man's person represented by two actors who, by opposing each other, must divide the power which — if men want to live in peace — is indivisible. That would reduce the multitude back into the condition of war, contrary to the very purpose for which all sovereignty is instituted.

So just as it would be absurd for a sovereign assembly to invite the people of its dominion to send up deputies with power to make known their advice and desires, and then to treat those deputies, rather than itself, as the absolute representative of the people — so it is equally absurd to think the same thing in a monarchy. And I do not know how so obvious a truth has lately been so little noticed: that in a certain monarchy, a man who held the sovereignty by a descent of six hundred years, who alone was called sovereign, who received the title of Majesty from every one of his subjects, and who was unquestionably taken by them for their king, was nevertheless never considered their representative — while that name passed without contradiction as the title of the men whom the people, at his command, sent up to carry their petitions and give him (if he permitted it) their advice.

Let this serve as a warning to those who are the true and absolute representatives of a people: instruct men in the nature of that office, and be careful how you admit any other general representation on any occasion whatsoever, if you mean to discharge the trust committed to you.

Comparing Monarchy with Sovereign Assemblies

The difference between these three kinds of commonwealth does not lie in a difference of power, but in a difference of convenience — that is, in their aptitude to produce the peace and security of the people, which is the purpose for which they were instituted. Comparing monarchy with the other two, we may observe the following.

First: whoever bears the person of the people, or belongs to the assembly that bears it, also bears his own natural person. And though he may be careful, in his political person, to advance the common interest, he is just as careful — or more so — to advance the private good of himself, his family, his relatives, and his friends. For the most part, if the public interest happens to cross the private, he prefers the private; for the passions of men are commonly more powerful than their reason. From this it follows that wherever the public and private interest are most closely united, there the public interest is most advanced. Now in a monarchy, the private interest is the same as the public. The riches, power, and honor of a monarch arise only from the riches, strength, and reputation of his subjects. For no king can be rich, or glorious, or secure, whose subjects are poor, or contemptible, or too weak — through want or division — to fight a war against their enemies. Whereas in a democracy or an aristocracy, the public prosperity does not add as much to the private fortune of a man who is corrupt or ambitious as a treacherous piece of advice often does, or a betrayal, or a civil war.

Second: a monarch receives counsel from whomever he pleases, whenever and wherever he pleases. He can hear the opinions of men genuinely versed in the matter he is deliberating, of whatever rank or quality, as long before the time of action and with as much secrecy as he wants. But when a sovereign assembly needs counsel, no one is admitted except those who have had a right to be there from the beginning — and these, for the most part, are men more versed in acquiring wealth than in acquiring knowledge. They give their advice in long speeches, which may and commonly do stir men up to action, but do not govern them in it. For the understanding is never enlightened by the flame of the passions — only dazzled. Nor is there any place or time in which an assembly can receive counsel in secrecy, because of its own multitude.

Third: the resolutions of a monarch are subject to no inconstancy except that of human nature. But in assemblies, besides the inconstancy of nature, there arises an inconstancy from the number. The absence of a few who would have kept a resolution firm once it was taken — which may happen through overconfidence, negligence, or private obstacles — or the diligent attendance of a few of the contrary opinion, undoes today everything that was concluded yesterday.

Fourth: a monarch cannot disagree with himself out of envy or self-interest. An assembly can — and to such a height that it may produce a civil war.

Fifth: there is this inconvenience in monarchy, that any subject may be stripped of everything he possesses by the power of one man, for the enriching of a favorite or a flatterer. I confess this is a great and unavoidable inconvenience. But the same thing can just as easily happen where the sovereign power is in an assembly. For their power is the same; they are just as subject to evil counsel, and just as easily seduced by orators, as a monarch is by flatterers; and becoming one another's flatterers, they serve one another's greed and ambition by turns. Moreover, the favorites of an assembly are many, and their relatives far more numerous than any monarch's. Besides, there is no favorite of a monarch who cannot help his friends as well as hurt his enemies. But orators — that is to say, the favorites of sovereign assemblies — though they have great power to hurt, have little power to save. For to accuse requires less eloquence than to excuse (such is man's nature), and condemnation looks more like justice than absolution does.

Sixth: it is an inconvenience in monarchy that the sovereignty may descend upon an infant, or upon someone who cannot tell good from evil. The inconvenience consists in this: that the use of his power must be in the hands of another man, or of some assembly of men, who govern by his right and in his name, as curators and protectors of his person and his authority. But to say there is inconvenience in putting the use of the sovereign power into the hands of a man, or an assembly of men, is to say that all government is more inconvenient than confusion and civil war. So whatever danger can be claimed here must arise from the rivalry of those who may become competitors for an office of such great honor and profit.

To see that this inconvenience does not come from the form of government we call monarchy, consider the possibilities. Either the previous monarch appointed who should have the guardianship of his infant successor — expressly, by testament, or tacitly, by not overriding the accepted custom in that case. Then such inconvenience, if it happens, is to be blamed not on the monarchy but on the ambition and injustice of the subjects — which is the same in every kind of government where the people are not well instructed in their duty and in the rights of sovereignty. Or else the previous monarch made no arrangement at all for such guardianship. In that case the law of nature has provided this sufficient rule: that the guardianship shall be in the man who has, by nature, the most interest in preserving the authority of the infant, and who can gain the least by his death or diminishment. For since every man by nature seeks his own benefit and advancement, to put an infant into the power of those who can advance themselves by destroying or damaging him is not guardianship — it is treachery. So with sufficient provision made against every just quarrel about the government under a child, if any contention arises that disturbs the public peace, it is not to be attributed to the form of monarchy, but to the ambition of subjects and their ignorance of their duty.

On the other side, there is no great commonwealth whose sovereignty rests in a great assembly that is not — when it comes to deliberations of peace and war, and the making of laws — in the very same condition as if the government were in a child. For just as a child lacks the judgment to dissent from the counsel given him, and so is forced to take the advice of those to whom he is entrusted, so an assembly lacks the liberty to dissent from the counsel of the majority, whether that counsel is good or bad. And just as a child needs a tutor or protector to preserve his person and authority, so also, in great commonwealths, the sovereign assembly, in all great dangers and troubles, needs Custodes Libertatis — guardians of liberty — that is, dictators, or protectors of its authority. These are nothing less than temporary monarchs, to whom the assembly may commit the entire exercise of its power for a time — and at the end of that time, assemblies have been stripped of that power by their dictators more often than infant kings have been by their protectors, regents, or any other tutors.

Though the kinds of sovereignty are, as I have now shown, only three — monarchy, where one man has it; democracy, where the general assembly of subjects has it; and aristocracy, where it rests in an assembly of certain persons named or otherwise distinguished from the rest — still, anyone who considers the particular commonwealths that have existed and do exist in the world may not find it easy to reduce them all to three, and may be inclined to think there are other forms, made by mixing these together. For example: elective kingdoms, where kings hold the sovereign power for a time only; or kingdoms in which the king's power is limited — governments which most writers nevertheless call monarchies. Likewise, if a popular or aristocratic commonwealth subdues an enemy country and governs it by a president, procurator, or other magistrate, this may seem at first sight to be a democratic or aristocratic government. But it is not. Elective kings are not sovereigns but ministers of the sovereign; limited kings are not sovereigns but ministers of those who hold the sovereign power; and provinces subject to the democracy or aristocracy of another commonwealth are not governed democratically or aristocratically, but monarchically.

First, consider an elective king, whose power is limited to his lifetime, as it is in many places in Christendom today, or to certain years or months, like the dictator's power among the Romans. If he has the right to appoint his successor, he is no longer elective but hereditary. But if he has no power to elect his successor, then either there is some other known man or assembly that can elect a new one after his death — or else the commonwealth dies and dissolves with him, and returns to the condition of war. If it is known who has the power to confer the sovereignty after his death, then it is also known that the sovereignty was in them all along. For no one has the right to give what he does not have the right to possess and keep for himself, if he thinks fit. But if there is no one who can confer the sovereignty after the death of the man first elected, then he has the power — indeed, he is obliged by the law of nature — to provide for a successor, to keep those who trusted him with the government from relapsing into the miserable condition of civil war. And consequently, he was, when elected, an absolute sovereign.

Second, a king whose power is limited is not superior to the one — man or assembly — that has the power to limit it. And he that is not superior is not supreme; that is to say, not sovereign. The sovereignty, therefore, was always in the assembly that had the right to limit him, and the government was consequently not a monarchy, but either a democracy or an aristocracy. So it was in ancient Sparta, where the kings had the privilege of leading the armies, but the sovereignty was in the Ephori — the board of magistrates who held the real power.

Third, when the Roman people governed the land of Judea, for example, by a president, Judea was not therefore a democracy — for the people of Judea were not governed by any assembly into which any of them had the right to enter. Nor was it an aristocracy — for they were not governed by any assembly into which any man could enter by their election. They were governed by one person. And though that person, with respect to the people of Rome, was an assembly of the people — a democracy — yet with respect to the people of Judea, who had no right whatsoever to participate in the government, it was a monarch. For where people are governed by an assembly chosen by themselves out of their own number, the government is called a democracy or an aristocracy; but when they are governed by an assembly not of their own choosing, it is a monarchy — not of one man over another man, but of one people over another people.

The Right of Succession

In all these forms of government, the material is mortal. Not only monarchs but whole assemblies die. So for the preservation of peace among men, it is necessary that, just as order was taken for an artificial man, order should also be taken for an artificial eternity of life. Without it, men governed by an assembly would return to the condition of war in every generation, and men governed by one man would return to it as soon as their governor died. This artificial eternity is what men call the right of succession.

There is no perfect form of government where the disposing of the succession is not in the hands of the present sovereign. For if it lies in any other particular man or private assembly, it lies in a person who is a subject, and the sovereign may take it back at his pleasure — so the right is really in himself. And if it lies in no particular man, but is left to a fresh choice, then the commonwealth is dissolved when the sovereign dies, and the right belongs to whoever can seize it — contrary to the intention of those who instituted the commonwealth for their perpetual security, not a temporary one.

In a democracy, the whole assembly cannot fail unless the multitude to be governed fails. Questions about the right of succession therefore have no place at all in that form of government.

In an aristocracy, when any member of the assembly dies, the election of another into his place belongs to the assembly, as the sovereign to whom the choosing of all counselors and officers belongs. For whatever the representative does as actor, every one of the subjects does as author. And though the sovereign assembly may give others the power to elect new men to fill its ranks, the election is still made by its authority — and by that same authority it may be recalled whenever the public requires it.

The Present Monarch Has the Right to Dispose of the Succession

The greatest difficulty about the right of succession arises in monarchy. The difficulty comes from this: at first sight it is not obvious who is to appoint the successor, nor, in many cases, whom he has appointed. Both questions require more careful reasoning than most men are accustomed to use.

As to the question of who shall appoint the successor of a monarch who has the sovereign authority — that is, a monarch who owns the sovereign power, not one who merely uses it, as elective kings and princes do — we must consider this: either the man in possession has the right to dispose of the succession, or else that right falls back to the dissolved multitude. For the death of the man who owns the sovereign power leaves the multitude with no sovereign at all — that is, with no representative in whom they are united and through whom they are capable of doing any single action whatsoever. They are therefore incapable of electing any new monarch. Every man would have an equal right to submit himself to whomever he thinks best able to protect him — or, if he can, to protect himself by his own sword. And that is a return to confusion, and to the condition of a war of every man against every man, contrary to the end for which monarchy was first instituted. It is therefore clear that, by the institution of monarchy, the disposing of the successor is always left to the judgment and will of the present possessor.

And as for the question that may sometimes arise — whom the monarch in possession has designated to succeed to and inherit his power — it is settled by his express words and testament, or by other tacit signs sufficient to show his will.

Succession Passes by Express Words

By express words, or testament: when the monarch declares it in his lifetime, viva voce — by spoken word — or in writing, as the first emperors of Rome declared who should be their heirs. For the word "heir" does not by itself mean a man's children or nearest relatives, but whomever a man declares, in any way, that he wants to succeed him in his estate. If, therefore, a monarch declares expressly, by word or writing, that a certain man shall be his heir, then that man is invested with the right of being monarch immediately upon the death of his predecessor.

Or by Letting a Custom Stand

But where testament and express words are lacking, other natural signs of the will are to be followed. One of these is custom. Where the custom is that the next of kin absolutely succeeds, there the next of kin has the right to the succession — for if the will of the man in possession had been otherwise, he could easily have declared it in his lifetime. Likewise, where the custom is that the next of the male line succeeds, there the right of succession is in the next male relative, for the same reason. And the same holds if the custom were to advance the female line. For whatever custom a man could override with a word, and does not, is a natural sign that he wants that custom to stand.

Or by Presumption of Natural Affection

But where neither custom nor testament has gone before, it is to be understood: first, that a monarch's will is for the government to remain monarchical, because he has approved that form of government in his own case. Second, that a child of his own, male or female, is to be preferred before anyone else — because men are presumed to be more inclined by nature to advance their own children than the children of other men; and of their own children, a male rather than a female, because men, in his view, are naturally fitter than women for actions of labor and danger. Third, where his own children fail, a brother rather than a stranger — and so on, always the nearer in blood rather than the more remote, because it is always presumed that the nearer in kin is the nearer in affection; and it is evident that a man always receives, by reflection, the most honor from the greatness of his nearest kindred.

Disposing of the Succession, Even to a King of Another Nation, Is Not Unlawful

But if it is lawful for a monarch to dispose of the succession by words of contract or by testament, someone may object to a great inconvenience: he may sell or give his right of governing to a stranger. And because strangers — that is, men not used to living under the same government, and not speaking the same language — commonly undervalue one another, this may turn to the oppression of his subjects. That is indeed a great inconvenience. But it does not come necessarily from being subject to a stranger's government; it comes from the unskillfulness of governors who are ignorant of the true rules of politics. That is why the Romans, when they had subdued many nations, made their government digestible by removing that grievance as far as they thought necessary: they gave not only the privileges but the very name of Romans, sometimes to whole nations, and sometimes to the leading men of every nation they conquered, and took many of them into the Senate and into offices of responsibility, even in the city of Rome itself.

And this is what our most wise king, King James, aimed at in working for the union of his two realms of England and Scotland. Had he obtained it, it would in all likelihood have prevented the civil wars that now make both those kingdoms miserable. It is therefore no injury to the people for a monarch to dispose of the succession by will — though through the fault of many princes, it has sometimes proved inconvenient. And there is this further argument for its lawfulness: whatever inconvenience can come from giving a kingdom to a stranger can also come from marrying among strangers in such a way that the right of succession may descend upon them — and yet everyone counts that as lawful.

Chapter 20: Parental Dominion and Despotic Dominion

A commonwealth by acquisition — by conquest — is one where the sovereign power is acquired by force. And it is acquired by force when men, singly or many together by majority vote, authorize all the actions of the man or assembly that holds their lives and liberty in his power, and do so out of fear of death or chains.

How It Differs from a Commonwealth by Agreement

This kind of dominion, or sovereignty, differs from sovereignty by agreement in only one respect. Men who choose their sovereign by agreement do it out of fear of one another, not fear of the man they are installing. In conquest, they subject themselves to the very man they are afraid of.

In both cases, notice, they do it out of fear. That point deserves the attention of those who hold that all covenants made out of fear of death or violence are void. If that were true, no one in any kind of commonwealth could be obligated to obey at all.

It is true that in a commonwealth, once it is set up — whether by agreement or by conquest — promises made out of fear of death or violence are not covenants, and do not bind, when the thing promised is against the laws. But the reason is not that the promise was made out of fear. The reason is that the person promising had no right to the thing he promised. And when he could lawfully perform his promise and chooses not to, what releases him is not any invalidity in the covenant — it is the sentence of the sovereign. Otherwise we would have a contradiction: a man would promise lawfully and break his promise unlawfully. But when the sovereign — who is the actor here — acquits him, then he is acquitted by the very person who extorted the promise, as the author of his release.

The Rights of Sovereignty Are the Same in Both

But the rights and consequences of sovereignty are the same in both. The conquering sovereign's power cannot be transferred to another without his consent. He cannot forfeit it. He cannot be accused of injury by any of his subjects. He cannot be punished by them. He is the judge of what is necessary for peace, and the judge of doctrines. He is the sole lawmaker, and the supreme judge of disputes, and of the times and occasions of war and peace. It belongs to him to choose magistrates, counselors, commanders, and all other officers and ministers, and to decide rewards and punishments, honor and rank. The reasons for all of this are the same ones I gave in the previous chapter for the identical rights and consequences of sovereignty by agreement.

Parental Dominion: Acquired Not by Begetting, but by Consent

Dominion is acquired in two ways: by generation and by conquest.

The right of dominion by generation is the right a parent has over his children, and it is called PARENTAL dominion (Hobbes' term: paternal). But it does not actually come from the begetting — as if the parent had dominion over the child simply because he produced him. It comes from the child's consent, either expressed outright or declared by other sufficient signs.

Here is why begetting cannot be the source. As to generation, God ordained a helper for man, and there are always two people who are equally parents. So dominion over the child should belong equally to both, and the child should be equally subject to both — which is impossible, for no one can obey two masters.

Some have assigned the dominion to the man alone, on the grounds that he is the more excellent sex. They miscalculate. There is not always such a difference of strength or judgment between the man and the woman that the right could be settled without a fight.

In commonwealths, this dispute is decided by the civil law, and for the most part — though not always — the ruling favors the father. But that is only because commonwealths have mostly been founded by fathers of families, not by mothers. The question before us, though, concerns the pure state of nature, where there are presumed to be no laws of marriage and no laws for the raising of children — nothing but the law of nature and the natural inclination of the sexes toward each other and toward their children.

In this state of pure nature, either the parents settle the dominion over the child between themselves by contract, or they do not settle it at all. If they settle it, the right passes according to the contract. We find in history that the Amazons made exactly such a contract with the men of neighboring countries, to whom they went to have children: any male child would be sent back to the father, but the females would stay with them. So the dominion over the females rested with the mother.

Or by Raising the Child

If there is no contract, the dominion belongs to the mother. For in the state of pure nature, where there are no marriage laws, no one can know who the father is unless the mother declares it. The right of dominion over the child therefore depends on her will, and is consequently hers.

Consider it another way. The infant is first of all in the mother's power, so that she may either nourish it or abandon it. If she nourishes it, the child owes its life to her, and is therefore bound to obey her rather than anyone else — and so the dominion over it is hers. But if she abandons it and someone else finds it and nourishes it, the dominion belongs to the one who nourishes it. For the child ought to obey the person who preserves it. Preservation of life is the whole purpose for which one man becomes subject to another, so every man is presumed to promise obedience to the one who has the power to save or destroy him.

Or by One Parent's Prior Subjection to the Other

If the mother is the father's subject, the child is in the father's power. And if the father is the mother's subject — as when a sovereign queen marries one of her own subjects — the child is subject to the mother, because the father is her subject too.

If a man and a woman who are monarchs of two separate kingdoms have a child, and they make a contract about who shall have dominion over him, the right passes by the contract. If they make no contract, the dominion follows the dominion of the place where the child lives — for the sovereign of each country has dominion over everyone who resides in it.

Whoever has dominion over the child has dominion also over the child's children, and over their children's children. For he that has dominion over a man's person has dominion over everything that is his. Without that, dominion would be a mere title, with no effect.

Succession Follows the Rules of Possession

The right of succession to parental dominion proceeds in the same way as the right of succession to a monarchy — which I have already covered sufficiently in the previous chapter.

Despotic Dominion: How It Is Acquired

Dominion acquired by conquest, or victory in war, is what some writers call DESPOTIC, from the Greek despotes — a lord or master. It is the dominion of a master over his servant.

This dominion is acquired by the victor when the vanquished, to avoid the death-blow hanging over him, covenants — either in express words or by other sufficient signs of his will — that so long as his life and the liberty of his body are allowed him, the victor shall have the use of them at his pleasure.
