Only after such a covenant is made is the vanquished a SERVANT — not before. By the word servant (whether it comes from servire, to serve, or from servare, to save — a question I leave to the grammarians) I do not mean a captive kept in prison or in chains until his captor, or whoever bought him from his captor, decides what to do with him. Such men — commonly called slaves — have no obligation at all. They may break their chains or their prison, and may kill their master or carry him off captive, with perfect justice. A servant is something different: a man who, having been captured, is allowed his bodily freedom, and who, upon his promise not to run away and not to do violence to his master, is trusted by him.

Not the Victory, but the Consent of the Vanquished

So it is not the victory that gives the right of dominion over the vanquished — it is his own covenant. He is not obligated because he was conquered, that is, beaten and captured or put to flight. He is obligated because he comes in and submits to the victor.

Nor is the victor obligated to spare an enemy who surrenders without any promise of his life. Yielding at discretion obligates the victor no longer than his own discretion shall see fit.

What men do when they ask for what we now call quarter (the Greeks called it zogria — "taking alive") is to dodge the immediate fury of the victor by submitting, and to bargain for their lives with ransom or service. So a man who has been given quarter has not had his life given to him — only deferred until further deliberation. He has not yielded on condition of keeping his life; he has yielded at discretion. Only then is his life secure, and his service owed, when the victor has trusted him with his bodily freedom. For slaves who work in prisons or in shackles do not work out of duty, but to avoid the cruelty of their taskmasters.

The master of a servant is master also of all he has, and may demand the use of it — his goods, his labor, his servants, and his children — as often as he sees fit. For the servant holds his life from his master by the covenant of obedience: that is, by owning and authorizing whatever the master shall do. And if the master kills him, or throws him into chains, or otherwise punishes him for refusing to obey, the servant is himself the author of that punishment, and cannot accuse the master of injury.

In sum: the rights and consequences of both parental and despotic dominion are exactly the same as those of a sovereign by agreement, and for the same reasons — the reasons laid out in the previous chapter. So consider a man who is monarch of several nations, holding sovereignty in one by the agreement of the assembled people, and in another by conquest — that is, by the submission of each individual to avoid death or chains. If he demands more from the conquered nation than from the other, on the grounds of his title by conquest, that is an act of ignorance of the rights of sovereignty. For the sovereign is equally absolute over both — or else there is no sovereignty at all, and then every man may lawfully protect himself, if he can, with his own sword. Which is the condition of war.

The Difference Between a Family and a Kingdom

From all this it appears that a great family, if it is not part of some commonwealth, is in itself — as far as the rights of sovereignty go — a little monarchy. This holds whether the family consists of a man and his children, or a man and his servants, or a man and his children and servants together; the father or master is the sovereign.

And yet a family is not properly a commonwealth, unless it has such power — by its own numbers or by other advantages — that it cannot be subdued without the hazard of war. For where a group of men is plainly too weak to defend itself even united, each member may use his own reason in time of danger to save his own life, by flight or by submission to the enemy, as he thinks best — just as a very small company of soldiers, surprised by an army, may throw down their weapons and ask for quarter, or run, rather than be put to the sword.

And that shall suffice for what I find, by speculation and deduction, concerning sovereign rights — derived from the nature, the needs, and the purposes of men in setting up commonwealths and placing themselves under monarchs or assemblies entrusted with power enough to protect them.

The Right of Monarchy from Scripture

Now let us consider what Scripture teaches on the same point.

To Moses, the children of Israel say this: "Speak thou to us, and we will hear thee; but let not God speak to us, lest we die" (Exodus 20:19). That is absolute obedience to Moses.

Concerning the right of kings, God himself, by the mouth of Samuel, says: "This shall be the right of the king you will have to reign over you. He shall take your sons, and set them to drive his chariots, and to be his horsemen, and to run before his chariots; and to gather in his harvest; and to make his engines of war, and instruments of his chariots. And he shall take your daughters to make perfumes, to be his cooks and bakers. He shall take your fields, your vineyards, and your olive groves, and give them to his servants. He shall take the tithe of your grain and wine, and give it to the men of his chamber, and to his other servants. He shall take your menservants and your maidservants, and the choice of your youth, and employ them in his business. He shall take the tithe of your flocks; and you shall be his servants" (1 Samuel 8:11-17). That is absolute power, and it is summed up in the last words: you shall be his servants.

Again, when the people heard what power their king was to have, they consented to it anyway, saying: "We will be as all other nations, and our king shall judge our causes, and go before us, to conduct our wars" (1 Samuel 8:19-20). Here is confirmed the right that sovereigns have both to the armed forces and to all judicature — which together contain as absolute a power as one man can possibly transfer to another.

Again, the prayer of King Solomon to God was this: "Give to thy servant understanding, to judge thy people, and to discern between good and evil" (1 Kings 3:9). It belongs to the sovereign, then, to be judge, and to prescribe the rules for telling good from evil. Those rules are laws — and therefore the legislative power is in him.

Saul sought the life of David. Yet when it was in David's power to kill Saul, and his servants would gladly have done it, David forbade them, saying: "God forbid I should do such an act against my Lord, the anointed of God" (1 Samuel 24:9).

For the obedience of servants, St. Paul says: "Servants, obey your masters in all things" (Colossians 3:20), and "Children, obey your parents in all things" (verse 22). That is simple obedience in those who are subject to parental or despotic dominion.

Again: "The Scribes and Pharisees sit in Moses' chair, and therefore all that they shall bid you observe, that observe and do" (Matthew 23:2-3). There again is simple obedience. And St. Paul: "Warn them that they subject themselves to princes, and to those that are in authority, and obey them" (Titus 3:2). This obedience is also simple.

Lastly, our Savior himself acknowledges that men ought to pay whatever taxes kings impose — "Give to Caesar that which is Caesar's" — and he paid such taxes himself. He acknowledged too that the king's word is sufficient to take anything from any subject when there is need, and that the king is the judge of that need. For he himself, as King of the Jews, commanded his disciples to take the donkey and its colt to carry him into Jerusalem: "Go into the village over against you, and you shall find a she-ass tied, and her colt with her; untie them, and bring them to me. And if any man ask you what you mean by it, say, the Lord hath need of them; and they will let them go" (Matthew 21:2-3). The disciples will not ask whether his need is a sufficient title, nor whether he is the judge of that need. They simply acquiesce in the will of the Lord.

To these passages we may add the one from Genesis: "You shall be as gods, knowing good and evil" (Genesis 3:5), and verse 11: "Who told thee that thou wast naked? Hast thou eaten of the tree, of which I commanded thee thou shouldest not eat?"

The judging of good and evil was forbidden under the name of the fruit of the tree of knowledge, as a trial of Adam's obedience. The Devil, to inflame the ambition of the woman — to whom that fruit already looked beautiful — told her that by tasting it, they would be as gods, knowing good and evil. And so, having both eaten, they did indeed take upon themselves God's office, which is the judging of good and evil. But they acquired no new ability to tell the two apart correctly.

As for the line that having eaten, they saw they were naked — no one has ever read that passage to mean they had been blind before and could not see their own skin. The meaning is plain: that was the moment they first judged their nakedness (in which it was God's will to create them) to be indecent. And by being ashamed of it, they silently passed judgment on God himself. Whereupon God says, "Hast thou eaten...?" — as if to say: do you, who owe me obedience, take it upon yourself to judge my commandments? By this it is clearly signified — though allegorically — that the commands of those who have the right to command are not to be censured or disputed by their subjects.

Sovereign Power Ought to Be Absolute in Every Commonwealth

So it appears plainly, to my understanding, from both reason and Scripture, that the sovereign power — whether placed in one man, as in monarchy, or in one assembly of men, as in popular and aristocratic commonwealths — is as great as men can possibly be imagined to make it.

People may imagine many evil consequences flowing from so unlimited a power. But the consequences of the lack of it — perpetual war of every man against his neighbor — are far worse. The condition of man in this life will never be free of inconveniences. But no commonwealth suffers any great inconvenience except what proceeds from the subjects' disobedience, and their breach of the very covenants from which the commonwealth had its being. And whoever thinks sovereign power too great, and seeks to make it less, must subject himself to a power that can limit it — which is to say, to a greater one.

The strongest objection is the objection from practice: men ask where and when such power has ever been acknowledged by subjects. But one may ask them in return: when, or where, has there ever been a kingdom long free from sedition and civil war? In those nations whose commonwealths have lived long, and have been destroyed only by foreign war, the subjects never disputed the sovereign power at all. But in any case, an argument from the practice of men — men who have never sifted the question to the bottom, who have never weighed with exact reason the causes and nature of commonwealths, and who suffer daily the miseries that flow from their ignorance of them — is no argument at all. Even if everyone in the world built their houses on sand, it would not follow that houses ought to be built that way.

The skill of making and maintaining commonwealths consists in certain rules, as arithmetic and geometry do — not, like tennis, in practice alone. And these rules are ones that poor men have not had the leisure to find out, and that men who have had the leisure have so far lacked the curiosity, or the method, to discover.

Chapter 21: The Liberty of Subjects

What Liberty Is

Liberty, or FREEDOM, properly means the absence of opposition — and by opposition I mean external impediments to motion. The word applies just as well to irrational and inanimate creatures as to rational ones. Whatever is tied down or hemmed in, so that it can move only within a certain space, and that space is fixed by the opposition of some external body — we say it does not have the liberty to go further.

The same goes for all living creatures while they are imprisoned or restrained by walls or chains. The same goes for water while it is held in by banks or vessels, when otherwise it would spread itself into a larger space. We say they are not at liberty to move as they would without those external impediments.

But when the obstacle to motion lies in the constitution of the thing itself, we do not say it lacks the liberty to move; we say it lacks the power. A stone lying still, or a man fastened to his bed by sickness, is not unfree. He is unable.

What It Means to Be Free

According to this proper and generally accepted meaning of the word, A FREE MAN is he that, in those things which by his strength and wit he is able to do, is not hindered from doing what he has a will to do.

But when the words "free" and "liberty" are applied to anything but bodies, they are being misused. What is not subject to motion is not subject to impediment. When we say, for example, "the way is free," we do not mean any liberty of the road, but of the people who walk it without being stopped. When we say a gift is free, we do not mean any liberty of the gift, but of the giver, who was not bound by any law or covenant to give it. When we speak freely, it is not the liberty of the voice or the pronunciation, but of the man, whom no law has obliged to speak otherwise than he did.

And finally, from the phrase "free will" no liberty of the will, desire, or inclination can be inferred — only the liberty of the man, which consists in this: that he finds no stop in doing what he has the will, desire, or inclination to do.

Fear and Liberty Are Consistent

Fear and liberty go together perfectly well. When a man throws his goods into the sea for fear the ship will sink, he does it entirely willingly, and he could refuse to do it if he wanted. It is therefore the action of a man who was free. So too a man sometimes pays his debt only for fear of prison; since nobody stopped him from keeping the money, that was the action of a man at liberty. And in general, all the actions men do in commonwealths out of fear of the law are actions the doers had the liberty not to do.

Liberty and Necessity Are Consistent

Liberty and necessity go together too. Watch the water: it has not only the liberty but the necessity of flowing down its channel. The same is true of the actions men do voluntarily. Because they proceed from the man's will, they proceed from liberty. And yet because every act of a man's will, and every desire and inclination, proceeds from some cause, and that cause from another cause, in a continual chain whose first link is in the hand of God — the first of all causes — they proceed from necessity. To anyone who could see the connection of all those causes, the necessity of all men's voluntary actions would be plainly visible.

And so God, who sees and arranges all things, sees also that the liberty of man in doing what he will is accompanied by the necessity of doing what God wills — no more and no less. Men may do many things that God does not command, and of which He is therefore not the author; but they can have no passion or appetite for anything unless God's will is the cause of that appetite. If His will did not guarantee the necessity of man's will, and consequently of everything that depends on man's will, then the liberty of men would contradict and obstruct the omnipotence and liberty of God. And that is enough, for our present purpose, about natural liberty — the only thing that is properly called liberty.

Artificial Chains: The Civil Laws

But just as men, to attain peace and preserve themselves by it, have made an artificial man, which we call a commonwealth, so they have also made artificial chains, called civil laws. By their mutual covenants, they have fastened these chains at one end to the lips of the man or assembly to whom they have given the sovereign power, and at the other end to their own ears. These bonds are weak by their own nature; yet they can be made to hold — not because they are hard to break, but because breaking them is dangerous.

The Liberty of Subjects Lies in What the Laws Leave Unregulated

It is only in relation to these bonds that I am now to speak of the liberty of subjects. No commonwealth in the world has rules regulating all the actions and words of men — such a thing is impossible. It follows necessarily that in every kind of action the laws have passed over in silence, men have the liberty to do what their own reason suggests is most profitable to themselves.

For if we take liberty in its proper sense — bodily liberty, freedom from chains and prison — it would be quite absurd for men to clamor, as they do, for a liberty they so obviously enjoy. And if we take liberty to mean exemption from the laws, it is no less absurd for men to demand, as they do, the very liberty by which all other men may become masters of their lives. Yet absurd as it is, that is exactly what they demand — not realizing that laws have no power to protect them without a sword in the hands of a man, or men, to see those laws carried out.

The liberty of a subject, therefore, lies only in those things which the sovereign, in regulating men's actions, has left unregulated: the liberty to buy and sell and otherwise make contracts with one another; to choose where they live, what they eat, and how they make their living; to raise their children as they think fit; and so on.

The Subject's Liberty Is Consistent with the Sovereign's Unlimited Power

Still, we must not imagine that this kind of liberty abolishes or limits the sovereign's power of life and death. For I have already shown that nothing the sovereign representative does to a subject, on any pretext whatsoever, can properly be called injustice or injury — because every subject is the author of every act the sovereign does. The sovereign never lacks the right to anything, except insofar as he himself is a subject of God, and bound by that to observe the laws of nature.

And so it can happen, and often does happen in commonwealths, that a subject is put to death by command of the sovereign power, and yet neither party wrongs the other — as when Jephthah caused his daughter to be sacrificed. In that case and others like it, the one who dies had the liberty to do the action for which he is nevertheless put to death without injury.

The same holds for a sovereign prince who puts an innocent subject to death. The action is against the law of nature, because it is contrary to equity — as the killing of Uriah by David was. Yet it was not an injury to Uriah, but to God. Not to Uriah, because Uriah himself had given David the right to do as he pleased. But to God, because David was God's subject, and the law of nature forbade him all iniquity. David himself, when he repented of the deed, plainly confirmed this distinction, saying, "Against you only have I sinned."

In the same way, when the people of Athens banished the most powerful men of their commonwealth for ten years at a time, they did not think they were committing any injustice — and yet they never even asked what crime the man had committed, only what harm he might do. Indeed, they would command the banishment of they knew not whom. Every citizen brought his oyster shell into the marketplace with the name of the man he wanted banished written on it, without actually accusing him of anything. Sometimes they banished an Aristides, precisely because of his reputation for justice; sometimes a vulgar comedian like Hyperbolus, simply as a joke. And yet no one can say that the sovereign people of Athens lacked the right to banish them, or that an Athenian lacked the liberty to joke, or to be just.

The Liberty the Ancient Writers Praise Is the Liberty of Commonwealths, Not of Private Men

The liberty that is mentioned so often and so honorably in the histories and philosophy of the ancient Greeks and Romans — and in the writings and talk of those who have gotten all their political learning from them — is not the liberty of particular men. It is the liberty of the commonwealth. And it is exactly the same liberty every man would have if there were no civil laws and no commonwealth at all. Its effects are the same, too.

For just as among masterless men there is perpetual war of every man against his neighbor — no inheritance to pass to a son or expect from a father, no property in goods or lands, no security, but a full and absolute liberty in every particular man — so in states and commonwealths that do not depend on one another, every commonwealth (not every man) has an absolute liberty to do whatever it judges — that is, whatever the man or assembly that represents it judges — most conducive to its benefit. But along with that, commonwealths live in the condition of perpetual war, forever on the edge of battle, with their frontiers armed and cannons planted against their neighbors all around.

The Athenians and the Romans were free — that is, they were free commonwealths. Not that any particular man had the liberty to resist his own representative, but that their representative had the liberty to resist or invade other peoples. To this day, the word LIBERTAS — liberty — is written in huge letters on the turrets of the city of Lucca. Yet no man can conclude from this that a particular man has any more liberty there, or any more exemption from service to the commonwealth, than he would have in Constantinople. Whether a commonwealth is a monarchy or a popular state, the freedom is still the same.

But it is an easy thing for men to be deceived by the attractive name of liberty, and — for lack of the judgment to tell the difference — to mistake for their private inheritance and birthright what is in fact the right of the public only. And when that same error is backed by the authority of men famous for their writings on the subject, it is no wonder it produces sedition and the overthrow of governments.

In these western parts of the world, we have been taught to take our opinions about the founding and the rights of commonwealths from Aristotle, Cicero, and other Greeks and Romans. Living under popular states, these men did not derive those rights from the principles of nature. They copied them into their books from the practice of their own commonwealths, which were popular — just as grammarians describe the rules of language from the speech of their time, or the rules of poetry from the poems of Homer and Virgil.

And because the Athenians were taught (to keep them from wanting to change their government) that they were free men, and that everyone living under a monarchy was a slave, Aristotle wrote it down in his Politics (book 6, chapter 2): "In democracy, liberty is to be supposed: for it is commonly held that no man is free in any other government." And like Aristotle, so Cicero and other writers grounded their civil doctrine on the opinions of the Romans, who were taught to hate monarchy — at first by the men who, having deposed their sovereign, divided the sovereignty of Rome among themselves, and afterward by their successors.

By reading these Greek and Latin authors, men have acquired from childhood a habit — under a false show of liberty — of favoring uprisings, of insolently second-guessing the actions of their sovereigns, and then of second-guessing those second-guessers, with so much spilling of blood that I think I can truly say: nothing was ever so dearly bought as these western parts have bought the learning of the Greek and Latin tongues.

How to Measure the True Liberty of a Subject

Now we come to the particulars of the true liberty of a subject — that is, the things a subject may refuse to do, without injustice, even when the sovereign commands them. To find them, we must consider what rights we give away when we make a commonwealth — or, which is the same thing, what liberty we deny ourselves by owning all the actions, without exception, of the man or assembly we make our sovereign.

For our obligation and our liberty both lie in the act of our submission, and must therefore be deduced from it. No man has any obligation that does not arise from some act of his own, for all men equally are by nature free. And since such deductions must be drawn either from the express words "I authorize all his actions," or from the intention of the man who submits himself to the sovereign's power (an intention which is to be understood from the purpose for which he submits), the obligation and liberty of the subject must be derived either from those words (or their equivalent), or else from the purpose for which sovereignty was instituted: namely, peace among the subjects themselves, and defense against a common enemy.

Subjects May Defend Their Own Bodies, Even Against Those Who Lawfully Attack Them

First, then. Sovereignty by agreement is created by covenant of every one to every one; sovereignty by conquest, by covenant of the vanquished to the victor, or of child to parent. So it is plain that every subject retains liberty in all those things whose right cannot be transferred by covenant. I showed in chapter 14 that covenants not to defend one's own body are void. Therefore:

They Are Not Bound to Hurt Themselves

If the sovereign commands a man — even one justly condemned — to kill, wound, or maim himself; or not to resist those who attack him; or to go without food, air, medicine, or anything else he cannot live without; that man has the liberty to disobey.

If a man is interrogated by the sovereign, or by the sovereign's authority, about a crime he himself committed, he is not bound to confess it without assurance of a pardon — because, as I showed in the same chapter, no man can be obliged by covenant to accuse himself.

Again, the consent of a subject to sovereign power is contained in these words: "I authorize, or take upon me, all his actions." There is no restriction at all in those words of the subject's own former natural liberty. By permitting the sovereign to kill me, I am not bound to kill myself when he commands it. It is one thing to say, "Kill me, or my fellow, if you please"; it is quite another to say, "I will kill myself, or my fellow." It follows, therefore, that:

No man is bound by the words themselves either to kill himself or to kill any other man. Consequently, whatever obligation a man may sometimes have, on the sovereign's command, to carry out a dangerous or dishonorable office, depends not on the words of our submission but on the intention behind it — which is to be understood from its purpose. So the rule is this: when our refusal to obey would frustrate the purpose for which sovereignty was established, then there is no liberty to refuse. Otherwise, there is.

Nor to Fight in War, Unless They Volunteer

On this ground, a man commanded as a soldier to fight against the enemy may in many cases refuse without injustice — even though his sovereign has right enough to punish his refusal with death. For instance, when he provides a capable soldier to take his place: in that case he has not deserted the service of the commonwealth. And allowance must be made for natural timidity — not only in women, of whom no such dangerous duty is expected, but also in men of womanish courage. When armies fight, there is running away on one side or both; yet when men do it not out of treachery but out of fear, they are judged to have acted not unjustly, but dishonorably. For the same reason, avoiding battle is not injustice — it is cowardice.

But the man who enrolls himself as a soldier, or takes enlistment money, throws away the excuse of a timid nature. He is obliged not only to go into battle but also not to run from it without his captain's leave. And when the defense of the commonwealth requires, all at once, the help of everyone able to bear arms, then everyone is obliged — because otherwise the founding of the commonwealth, which they lack the purpose or the courage to preserve, was for nothing.

No man has the liberty to resist the sword of the commonwealth in defense of another man, guilty or innocent — because such a liberty strips the sovereign of the means of protecting us, and so destroys the very essence of government. But suppose a great many men together have already resisted the sovereign power unjustly, or committed some capital crime for which every one of them expects death. Do they then have the liberty to band together and assist and defend one another? Certainly they do. For they are only defending their lives — which the guilty man may do just as well as the innocent. There was indeed injustice in the first breach of their duty; but their taking up arms afterward, though it is done to maintain what they have done, is no new unjust act. And if it is done only to defend their persons, it is not unjust at all. The offer of a pardon, however, takes away the plea of self-defense from those it is offered to, and makes it unlawful for them to keep assisting or defending the rest.

The Greatest Liberty of Subjects Depends on the Silence of the Law

As for other liberties, they depend on the silence of the law. Where the sovereign has prescribed no rule, the subject has the liberty to act or to hold back, at his own discretion. This kind of liberty is therefore greater in some places and lesser in others, greater in some times and lesser in others, according to what those who hold the sovereignty think most convenient. For example, there was a time in England when a man could enter his own land by force and throw out anyone wrongfully occupying it. But in later times, that liberty of forcible entry was taken away by a statute made by the king in Parliament. And in some places of the world men have the liberty of many wives; in other places, no such liberty is allowed.

If a subject has a dispute with his sovereign — about a debt, or the right of possession of lands or goods, or about any service required of him, or about any penalty, bodily or financial, grounded on a previous law — he has the same liberty to sue for his right as if it were a dispute with another subject, and before judges appointed by the sovereign. For since the sovereign is making his demand by force of a previous law, and not by virtue of his power, he thereby declares that he requires no more than what that law shows to be due. The lawsuit is therefore not contrary to the will of the sovereign, and consequently the subject has the liberty to demand a hearing of his case, and a sentence according to that law. But if the sovereign demands or takes anything on the claim of his power, then no legal action lies. For everything done by him by virtue of his power is done by the authority of every subject — and so a man who brings an action against the sovereign brings it against himself.

If a monarch or sovereign assembly grants a liberty to all or any of his subjects, and while that grant stands he is unable to provide for their safety, the grant is void — unless he directly renounces the sovereignty or transfers it to another. For he could have openly and in plain terms renounced or transferred it, if that had been his will, and he did not. So we must understand that it was not his will, and that the grant came from ignorance of the conflict between such a liberty and the sovereign power. The sovereignty is therefore still retained, and with it all the powers necessary to exercise it: the power of war and peace, of the courts, of appointing officers and counselors, of levying money, and the rest named in chapter 18.

When Subjects Are Released from Obedience to Their Sovereign

The obligation of subjects to the sovereign is understood to last as long, and no longer, than the power lasts by which he is able to protect them. For the right men have by nature to protect themselves, when no one else can protect them, can be given up by no covenant.

The sovereignty is the soul of the commonwealth; once it has departed from the body, the limbs no longer receive their motion from it. The end of obedience is protection — and wherever a man sees protection, whether in his own sword or in another's, nature directs his obedience to it, and his effort to maintain it. And though sovereignty, in the intention of those who make it, is immortal, it is by its own nature not only subject to violent death by foreign war, but carries within it, from the very moment of its founding, through the ignorance and passions of men, many seeds of a natural mortality by internal discord.

Captivity

If a subject is taken prisoner in war, or his person or his means of living are within the guards of the enemy, and his life and bodily liberty are given back to him on condition that he become subject to the victor, he has the liberty to accept the condition. And having accepted it, he is the subject of the one who took him — because he had no other way to preserve himself. The case is the same if he is detained on the same terms in a foreign country. But if a man is held in prison or in chains, or is not trusted with the liberty of his own body, he cannot be understood to be bound by covenant to subjection. He may therefore escape, if he can, by any means whatsoever.

If the Sovereign Renounces the Government for Himself and His Heirs

If a monarch gives up the sovereignty both for himself and for his heirs, his subjects return to the absolute liberty of nature. For though nature may declare who his sons are and who his nearest kin are, it depends on his own will (as was said in the previous chapter) who shall be his heir. If he will have no heir, there is no sovereignty and no subjection. The case is the same if he dies without known relatives and without declaring an heir — for then no heir can be known, and consequently no subjection is due.

Banishment

If the sovereign banishes his subject, then during the banishment he is not a subject. But a man sent abroad on a mission, or given leave to travel, is still a subject — though by contract between sovereigns, not by virtue of the covenant of subjection. For whoever enters another's dominion is subject to all its laws, unless he has a privilege by the friendship of the sovereigns, or by special license.

If the Sovereign Becomes Subject to Another

If a monarch defeated in war surrenders himself as subject to the victor, his subjects are released from their former obligation and become obliged to the victor. But if he is held prisoner, or does not have the liberty of his own body, he is not understood to have given away the right of sovereignty. His subjects are therefore obliged to obey the magistrates he previously appointed, who govern not in their own name but in his. For since his right remains, the only question is one of administration — that is, of the magistrates and officers. And if he has no means of naming them, he is presumed to approve those he himself appointed before.

Chapter 22: Of Subordinate Systems — Political and Private

The Different Kinds of Systems of People

Having spoken of the generation, form, and power of a commonwealth, the order of my subject brings me next to its parts. And first to systems, which resemble the similar parts — the muscles — of a natural body. By SYSTEMS I mean any number of men joined together in one interest or one business. Some of these are regular, and some irregular. Regular systems are those in which one man, or one assembly of men, is constituted representative of the whole number. All the rest are irregular.

Of regular systems, some are absolute and independent, subject to none but their own representative. Only commonwealths are of this kind, and of those I have spoken already in the five preceding chapters. The others are dependent — that is, subordinate to some sovereign power, to which every member, and the representative too, is subject.

Of subordinate systems, some are political and some private. Political systems — otherwise called bodies politic, or persons in law — are those made by authority from the sovereign power of the commonwealth. Private systems are those that subjects set up among themselves, or that are constituted by authority from a stranger. For no authority derived from a foreign power is public within the dominion of another; there, it is private.

And of private systems, some are lawful, some unlawful. Lawful are those the commonwealth allows; all others are unlawful. Irregular systems — those with no representative at all — consist only in a gathering of people. If such a gathering is not forbidden by the commonwealth and not made with evil design — like the flow of people to markets, or to public shows, or for any other harmless purpose — it is lawful. But when the intention is evil, or (if the number is considerable) unknown, it is unlawful.

In All Political Bodies, the Power of the Representative Is Limited

In bodies politic, the power of the representative is always limited; and what prescribes the limits is the sovereign power. For unlimited power is absolute sovereignty, and the sovereign in every commonwealth is the absolute representative of all the subjects. No one else, therefore, can be representative of any part of them except so far as the sovereign gives leave. And to give a body politic of subjects leave to have an absolute representative, for all intents and purposes, would be to abandon the government of that much of the commonwealth, and to divide the dominion — contrary to the people's peace and defense. The sovereign cannot be understood to do that by any grant which does not plainly and directly discharge them of their subjection. For when one reading of his words contradicts what he clearly intends, those words are not signs of his will; they are signs of error and miscalculation — to which all mankind is far too prone.

The bounds of the power given to the representative of a body politic are to be learned from two things: one, their writ, or letters from the sovereign; the other, the law of the commonwealth.

By Letters Patent

In the founding or acquisition of a commonwealth, which is independent, no writing is needed, because the power of its representative has no bounds except those set out by the unwritten law of nature. But in subordinate bodies, the necessary limitations — concerning their business, their times, and their places — are so various that they can neither be remembered without letters nor taken notice of unless those letters are patent: open letters, which can be read out to everyone, and sealed or certified with the seals or other permanent signs of the sovereign authority.

And by the Laws

And because such limitation is not always easy, or even possible, to set down in writing, the ordinary laws, common to all subjects, must determine what the representative may lawfully do in every case where the letters themselves are silent. And therefore:

When the Representative Is One Man, His Unauthorized Acts Are His Alone

In a body politic, if the representative is one man, then whatever he does in the person of the body that is not warranted by his letters or by the laws is his own act — not the act of the body, nor of any member of it besides himself. For beyond what his letters or the laws allow, he represents no one's person but his own. But what he does within those bounds is the act of every member. For everyone is author of the sovereign's acts, since the sovereign is their unlimited representative; and the act of a man who does not stray from the sovereign's letters is the act of the sovereign — so every member of the body is author of it.

When It Is an Assembly, the Act Belongs Only to Those Who Voted for It

But if the representative is an assembly, then whatever the assembly decrees without warrant from their letters or the laws is the act of the assembly — the body politic — and the act of every man by whose vote the decree was made. It is not the act of any man who, being present, voted against it, nor of any man who was absent, unless he voted by proxy. It is the act of the assembly because it was voted by the majority; and if it is a crime, the assembly may be punished as far as it is capable of punishment — by dissolution, or by forfeiture of its letters (which to such artificial and fictitious bodies is capital punishment), or, if the assembly has a common stock in which none of the innocent members has property, by a fine. For nature has exempted all bodies politic from bodily penalties. But those who did not give their vote are innocent, because the assembly cannot represent any man in things unwarranted by its letters, and so they are not implicated by its votes.

When the Representative Is One Man, He Alone Is Liable for Money Borrowed or Owed — Not the Members

If the person of the body politic rests in one man, and he borrows money from a stranger — that is, from someone not of the same body (for no letters need limit borrowing, since limiting the lending is left to men's own inclination) — the debt is the representative's. For if his letters gave him authority to make the members pay what he borrows, he would by consequence have sovereignty over them. In that case the grant would either be void, as proceeding from error — a failing common to human nature, and an insufficient sign of the granter's will — or, if the sovereign stands by it, then the representative is sovereign, and falls outside the present question, which concerns subordinate bodies only. No member, therefore, is obliged to pay the debt so borrowed; only the representative himself. For the lender, being a stranger to the letters and to the constitution of the body, counts as his debtors only those who are actually engaged; and since the representative can engage himself and no one else, the lender has him alone for debtor. He must pay it out of the common stock, if there is one, or, if there is none, out of his own estate.

If he comes into debt by contract, or by a fine, the case is the same.

When It Is an Assembly, Only Those Who Assented Are Liable

But when the representative is an assembly and the debt is owed to a stranger, then all those, and only those, are responsible for the debt who gave their votes to the borrowing, or to the contract that made it due, or to the act for which the fine was imposed. For every one of them, in voting, engaged himself for the payment. He who is author of the borrowing is obliged to pay even the whole debt — though once any one of them pays it, the rest are discharged.

If the Debt Is to a Member of the Assembly, Only the Body Is Obliged

But if the debt is owed to one of the assembly itself, then only the assembly is obliged to pay it, out of their common stock, if they have one. For having liberty of vote, if he voted that the money should be borrowed, he voted that it should be paid back. If he voted against the borrowing, or was absent, then by lending the money he votes for the borrowing after all, contradicting his earlier vote; he is bound by the later one, and becomes both borrower and lender. Consequently he cannot demand payment from any particular man, but only from the common treasury. And if that fails, he has no remedy and no complaint except against himself — who, knowing the acts of the assembly and its means to pay, and being under no compulsion, nevertheless lent his money through his own folly.

Protesting the Decrees of Political Bodies Is Sometimes Lawful — But Against the Sovereign Power, Never

From all this it is clear that in political bodies which are subordinate and subject to a sovereign power, it is sometimes not only lawful but prudent for a particular man to protest openly against the decrees of the representative assembly, and to have his dissent registered, or witnessed — because otherwise he may be obliged to pay debts contracted, and answer for crimes committed, by other men. But in a sovereign assembly that liberty is taken away: first, because he who protests there denies the assembly's sovereignty; and second, because whatever the sovereign power commands is, as far as the subject is concerned (though not always in the sight of God), justified by the command itself — for of every such command, every subject is the author.

Political Bodies for the Government of a Province, Colony, or Town

The variety of bodies politic is almost infinite. They are distinguished not only by the different kinds of business for which they are constituted — and the diversity there is beyond description — but also by their times, places, and numbers, which are subject to many limitations. As to their business, some are ordained for government. First, the government of a province may be committed to an assembly of men, in which all resolutions depend on the votes of the majority. That assembly is then a body politic, and its power is limited by its commission. The word "province" signifies a charge, or care of business, which the man whose business it is commits to another to administer for him and under him. So when one commonwealth contains several countries with laws distinct from one another, or far distant in place, and the administration of government is committed to different persons, those countries where the sovereign does not reside, but governs by commission, are called provinces.

But of a province governed by an assembly residing in the province itself, there are few examples. The Romans, who held sovereignty over many provinces, always governed them by presidents and praetors — not by assemblies, the way they governed the city of Rome and the adjacent territories. Likewise, when colonies went out from England to plant Virginia and the Summer Islands — Bermuda — though their government here at home was committed to assemblies in London, those assemblies never committed the government under them to any assembly there. They sent each plantation one governor. For though every man, where he can be present, naturally desires to participate in government, yet where men cannot be present, they are by nature also inclined to commit the government of their common interest to a monarchical rather than a popular form. You can see the same thing in men with great private estates: when they are unwilling to take the pains of managing their own business, they would rather trust one servant than an assembly of their friends or servants.

But however it stands in fact, we may suppose the government of a province or colony committed to an assembly. And when it is, what I have to say here is this: whatever debt that assembly contracts, and whatever unlawful act it decrees, is the act only of those who assented, not of any who dissented or were absent — for the reasons already given. Also, an assembly residing outside the bounds of the colony it governs cannot exercise any power over the persons or goods of any member of the colony, to seize them for debt or any other duty, in any place outside the colony itself; it has no jurisdiction or authority elsewhere, and is left to whatever remedy the law of that place allows it. And though the assembly has the right to impose a fine on any of its members who break the laws it makes, it has no right to enforce that fine outside the colony itself. What is said here of the rights of an assembly governing a province or colony applies equally to an assembly governing a town, a university, a college, a church, or any other government over the persons of men.

And generally, in all bodies politic, if any particular member thinks himself injured by the body itself, the hearing of his case belongs to the sovereign, and to those whom the sovereign has appointed — or shall appoint — as judges of such cases. It does not belong to the body itself. For in this case the whole body is merely his fellow subject. In a sovereign assembly it is otherwise: for there, if the sovereign is not the judge, even in his own cause, there can be no judge at all.

Political Bodies for the Ordering of Trade

In a body politic formed for the ordering of foreign trade, the most suitable representative is an assembly of all the members — that is, an assembly arranged so that every man who ventures his money may be present, if he chooses, at all the deliberations and resolutions of the body. To see why, consider the purpose for which merchants — who are free to buy and sell, export and import, each at his own discretion — nevertheless bind themselves together into one corporation.

It is true that few merchants can fill a whole ship with the goods they buy at home for export, or with what they buy abroad to bring home; so they need to join together in one society, where every man may either share in the gains in proportion to his stake, or take his own goods and sell what he transports or imports at whatever prices he sees fit. But that alone is no body politic, for there is no common representative to bind them to any law beyond what binds every other subject. No — the purpose of their incorporating is to make their gains greater, and that is done two ways: by being the only buyer and the only seller, both at home and abroad. So to grant a company of merchants the status of a corporation, a body politic, is to grant them a double monopoly: one as sole buyers, the other as sole sellers. For when a company is incorporated for trade with a particular foreign country, they alone export the goods marketable in that country — which makes them the sole buyer at home and the sole seller abroad. At home there is only one buyer, and abroad only one seller: both profitable to the merchant, because he thereby buys at home at lower rates and sells abroad at higher. And abroad there is only one buyer of foreign goods, and only one who sells them at home: both, again, profitable to the investors.

Of this double monopoly, one half is disadvantageous to the people at home, the other to foreigners. For at home, by their sole right of exportation, they set whatever price they please on the farming and handiwork of the people; and by their sole right of importation, whatever price they please on all the foreign goods the people need — both bad for the people. Conversely, by being sole seller of native goods abroad and sole buyer of foreign goods on the spot, they raise the price of the one and beat down the price of the other, to the foreigner's disadvantage. For where only one sells, the goods are dearer; and where only one buys, cheaper. Such corporations, therefore, are nothing but monopolies. Yet they would be very profitable for a commonwealth if they were bound into one body in foreign markets only, and left at liberty at home — every man free to buy and sell at whatever price he could.

The purpose of these bodies of merchants, then, is not any common benefit to the whole body (which in this case has no common stock except what is deducted from the members' individual stakes for building, buying, supplying, and manning ships), but the particular gain of each individual investor. It is reasonable, therefore, that every one of them be acquainted with the management of his own money — that is, that every one be a member of the assembly that has the power to order it, and be acquainted with its accounts. And so the representative of such a body must be an assembly at which every member may be present at the consultations, if he wishes.

If a body politic of merchants contracts a debt to a stranger by the act of their representative assembly, every member is liable by himself for the whole. For a stranger can take no notice of their private rules; he regards them simply as so many particular men, each obliged for the whole payment, until payment by any one of them discharges all the rest. But if the debt is owed to one of the company, the creditor is debtor for the whole to himself, and so cannot demand his debt from any particular man — only from the common stock, if there is one.

If the commonwealth imposes a tax on the body, it is understood to be laid on every member in proportion to his particular stake in the company. For in this case there is no other common stock than what is made up of their individual investments.

If a fine is laid on the body for some unlawful act, only those are liable by whose votes the act was decreed, or by whose assistance it was carried out; for in none of the rest is there any crime except belonging to the body — and if that were a crime, it is not theirs, because the body was ordained by the authority of the commonwealth.

If one of the members is in debt to the body, the body may sue him; but his goods cannot be seized, nor his person imprisoned, by the body's own authority — only by the authority of the commonwealth. For if they could do it by their own authority, they could by their own authority give judgment that the debt is due — which would be to make them judges in their own cause.

A Political Body for Giving Counsel to the Sovereign

These bodies made for the government of men, or of trade, are either perpetual, or limited to a time prescribed in writing. But there are also bodies whose duration is limited only by the nature of their business. For example, suppose a sovereign monarch, or a sovereign assembly, thinks fit to command the towns and other parts of the territory to send him their deputies, to inform him of the condition and needs of the subjects, or to advise with him on the making of good laws, or for any other purpose — dealing with them as with one person representing the whole country. Such deputies, having a place and time of meeting assigned to them, are, in that place and at that time, a body politic representing every subject of that dominion — but only for such matters as are put before them by the man or assembly that summoned them by the sovereign authority. And when it is declared that nothing more shall be proposed or debated by them, the body is dissolved. For if they were the absolute representative of the people, they would be the sovereign assembly — and then there would be two sovereign assemblies, two sovereigns, over the same people, which cannot stand with their peace. Therefore, once there is a sovereignty, there can be no absolute representation of the people except by it. As for the limits of how far such a body shall represent the whole people, those are set out in the writing by which they were summoned. For the people cannot choose their deputies for any other purpose than the one expressed in the writing directed to them by their sovereign.

A Regular and Lawful Private Body: the Family

Private bodies that are regular and lawful are those constituted without letters or any other written authority beyond the laws common to all other subjects. Because they are united under one representative person, they count as regular. Of this kind are all families, in which the father, or master, orders the whole household. He obliges his children and servants as far as the law permits, but no further; for none of them is bound to obey in actions the law has forbidden. In all other actions, for as long as they are under domestic government, they are subject to their fathers and masters as to their immediate sovereigns. For the father and the master, having been absolute sovereigns in their own families before commonwealths were instituted, lose afterward no more of their authority than the law of the commonwealth takes from them.

Regular but Unlawful Private Bodies

Private bodies that are regular but unlawful are those that unite themselves under one representative person without any public authority at all. Such are the corporations of beggars, thieves, and gypsies, formed the better to order their trade of begging and stealing; and the corporations of men who, by authority from some foreign person, unite themselves within another's dominion, for the easier spreading of doctrines and for building a party against the power of the commonwealth.

Irregular Systems, Such as Private Leagues

Irregular systems are, in their nature, nothing but leagues — or sometimes the mere gathering of people without union for any particular design, not bound by any obligation of one to another, but arising only from a similarity of wills and inclinations. They become lawful or unlawful according to the lawfulness or unlawfulness of each particular man's design in them; and his design is to be judged by the occasion.

Leagues of subjects — leagues being commonly made for mutual defense — are, within a commonwealth (which is itself no more than a league of all the subjects together), for the most part unnecessary; they smell of unlawful design, and are for that cause unlawful, and commonly go by the name of factions, or conspiracies. For a league is a connection of men by covenants; and if no power is given to any one man or assembly to compel performance (as in the condition of mere nature), it remains valid only so long as no just cause of distrust arises. Leagues between commonwealths, over whom there is no human power established to keep them all in awe, are therefore not only lawful but profitable for as long as they last. But leagues of the subjects of one and the same commonwealth — where every man can obtain his right by means of the sovereign power — are unnecessary for maintaining peace and justice, and, if their design is evil or unknown to the commonwealth, unlawful. For all banding together of strength by private men is, if for evil intent, unjust; if for an intent unknown, dangerous to the public, and unjustly concealed.

Secret Cabals

If the sovereign power rests in a great assembly, and a number of men who are part of that assembly consult apart, without authority, to contrive the guidance of the rest — that is a faction, an unlawful conspiracy, a fraudulent seduction of the assembly for their own particular interest. But if a man whose private interest is to be debated and judged in the assembly makes as many friends there as he can, there is no injustice in him; for in this case he is no part of the assembly. And even if he hires such friends with money (unless there is an express law against it), it is still not injustice. For sometimes — such are men's manners — justice cannot be had without money; and every man may think his own cause just until it has been heard and judged.

Feuds of Private Families

In every commonwealth, if a private man keeps more servants than the management of his estate and his lawful employment for them require, it is faction, and unlawful. For having the protection of the commonwealth, he has no need of private force to defend him. And whereas in nations not thoroughly civilized, numerous families have lived in continual hostility and invaded one another with private force, it is plain enough that either they acted unjustly, or else they had no commonwealth at all.

Factions for Government

And as with factions for kindred, so with factions for the government of religion — Papists, Protestants, and so on — or of the state, like the patricians and plebeians of old Rome, or the aristocratic and democratic factions of old Greece: all are unjust, being contrary to the peace and safety of the people, and a taking of the sword out of the hand of the sovereign.

A gathering of people is an irregular system, whose lawfulness or unlawfulness depends on the occasion and on the number of those assembled. If the occasion is lawful and evident, the gathering is lawful — like the usual meeting of men at church, or at a public show, in the usual numbers. But if the numbers are extraordinarily great, the occasion is not evident; and consequently anyone who cannot give a particular and good account of his being among them is to be judged conscious of an unlawful and tumultuous design. It may be lawful for a thousand men to join in a petition to be delivered to a judge or magistrate; yet if a thousand men come to present it, that is a tumultuous assembly — because one or two are all that purpose requires. But in such cases it is not some set number that makes the assembly unlawful; it is a number too great for the officers then present to suppress and bring to justice.

When an unusual number of men assemble against a man whom they accuse, the assembly is an unlawful tumult, since they could deliver their accusation to the magistrate by a few, or by one man. Such was the case of St. Paul at Ephesus, where Demetrius, with a great number of other men, brought two of Paul's companions before the magistrate, crying out with one voice, "Great is Diana of the Ephesians!" — which was their way of demanding justice against them, for teaching the people a doctrine that was against both their religion and their trade. The occasion, considering the laws of that people, was a just one; yet their assembly was judged unlawful, and the magistrate rebuked them for it in these words (Acts 19:40): "If Demetrius and the other workmen can accuse any man of anything, there are pleas, and deputies; let them accuse one another. And if you have any other thing to demand, your case may be judged in an assembly lawfully called. For we are in danger of being accused for this day's sedition, because there is no cause by which any man can give a reason for this gathering of people." Here he calls an assembly of which men can give no just account a sedition — one they could not answer for.

And this is all I shall say concerning systems and assemblies of people, which may be compared, as I said, to the similar parts of a man's body: those that are lawful, to the muscles; those that are unlawful, to wens, boils, and abscesses, bred by the unnatural gathering of evil humors.

Chapter 23: The Public Ministers of Sovereign Power

In the last chapter I spoke of the similar parts of a commonwealth — the systems and bodies within it. In this one I will speak of its organic parts: the organs and joints of the artificial man, which are its public ministers.

Who Counts as a Public Minister

A PUBLIC MINISTER is a person whom the sovereign — whether a monarch or an assembly — employs in some business, with authority to represent, in that employment, the person of the commonwealth.

Now, every man or assembly that holds sovereignty represents two persons — or, as the more common phrase puts it, has two capacities. One is natural, the other political. A monarch bears not only the person of the commonwealth but also the person of a man; a sovereign assembly bears not only the person of the commonwealth but also the person of the assembly itself.

This distinction matters, because those who serve the sovereign in his natural capacity are not public ministers. Only those who serve him in the administration of the public business are. So the ushers, sergeants, and other officers who wait on an assembly for no purpose except the convenience of the men assembled — in an aristocracy or a democracy — are not public ministers. Neither are the stewards, chamberlains, treasurers, or any other officers of a monarch's household. They serve the man, not the state.

Ministers for General Administration

Among public ministers, some are given charge of a general administration — either of the whole dominion or of a part of it.

Of the whole: as when the predecessor of an infant king commits the entire administration of the kingdom, during the boy's minority, to a Protector or Regent. In that case every subject is obliged to obey — as far as the ordinances the Protector makes and the commands he gives are issued in the king's name and are not inconsistent with the king's sovereign power.

Of a part, or province: as when a monarch or a sovereign assembly gives the general charge of some territory to a governor, lieutenant, prefect, or viceroy. Here too, everyone in that province is obliged to obey everything he does in the name of the sovereign, so long as it is not incompatible with the sovereign's right.

For such Protectors, viceroys, and governors have no right of their own at all — only what depends on the sovereign's will. And no commission given to them can ever be interpreted as a declaration of intent to transfer the sovereignty itself, unless it says so in express and unmistakable words. This kind of public minister resembles the nerves and tendons that move the various limbs of a natural body.

Ministers for Special Administration: First, the Economy

Other ministers have a special administration — charge of some particular business, either at home or abroad.

At home, first, there is the economy of the commonwealth. Those who have authority concerning the treasury — to collect, receive, pay out, or audit the tributes, taxes, rents, fines, and whatever else counts as public revenue — are public ministers. Ministers, because they serve the representative person and can do nothing against his command or without his authority. Public, because they serve him in his political capacity.

Second, those who have authority concerning the military — the custody of arms, forts, and ports; the levying, paying, or commanding of soldiers; or providing anything necessary for war by land or sea — are public ministers. But a soldier without command, though he fights for the commonwealth, does not on that account represent its person — because there is no one for him to represent it to. Everyone who holds command represents the commonwealth only to those he commands.

Ministers for the Instruction of the People

Those who have authority to teach the people their duty to the sovereign power — or to license others to teach it — and to instruct them in the knowledge of what is just and unjust, so as to make them more fit to live in godliness and in peace among themselves, and to resist the public enemy, are also public ministers. Ministers, because they do this not by their own authority but by another's. Public, because they do it — or should do it — by no authority but the sovereign's.

Only the monarch, or the sovereign assembly, has immediate authority from God to teach and instruct the people. No one but the sovereign receives his power Dei Gratia simply — that is, from the favor of God alone. Everyone else receives his from the favor and providence of God and of his sovereign: in a monarchy, Dei Gratia et Regis — "by the grace of God and the king" — or Dei Providentia et Voluntate Regis — "by God's providence and the king's will."

Ministers for Judicature

Those who are given jurisdiction are also public ministers. In their seats of justice they represent the person of the sovereign, and their sentence is his sentence. For, as I have already shown, all judicature is essentially attached to the sovereignty; every other judge is therefore only a minister of him, or them, that holds the sovereign power.

Controversies come in two kinds — controversies of fact and controversies of law — and so judgments do too: some of fact, some of law. Consequently, in the same controversy there may be two judges, one of fact and one of law.

In both kinds of controversy, a dispute may arise between the party being judged and the judge himself. Since both are subjects of the sovereign, equity requires that this dispute be judged by men agreed on by the consent of both — for no man can be a judge in his own cause. But the sovereign is already agreed on as judge by them both, so the sovereign must either hear the cause and decide it himself, or appoint as judge someone both parties agree on.

This agreement can be understood to happen in several ways. First, if the defendant is allowed to object to any judges whose interest makes him suspect them — as for the complainant, he has already chosen his own judge by bringing the case — then the judges he does not object to are judges he himself has agreed on. Second, if he appeals to some other judge, he can appeal no further; his appeal is his choice. Third, if he appeals to the sovereign himself, and the sovereign — in person, or through delegates the parties agree on — gives sentence, that sentence is final. For the defendant has been judged by his own judges; that is to say, by himself.

Having considered these properties of just and rational judicature, I cannot resist pointing out the excellent constitution of the courts of justice established in England for both Common Pleas and Public Pleas. By Common Pleas I mean those where both complainant and defendant are subjects; by Public Pleas — also called Pleas of the Crown — those where the complainant is the sovereign.

There were two orders of men: Lords and Commons. The Lords had this privilege: in all capital crimes, their judges were none but Lords — as many of them as chose to be present. Since this was always acknowledged as a privilege of favor, their judges were never anyone but those they had themselves desired. And in all controversies, every subject — and the Lords too, in civil cases — had for judges men of the county where the matter in dispute lay. Against these the accused could make his objections, until at last twelve men were agreed on without objection, and by those twelve he was judged. So, having his own judges, the party could allege nothing against the finality of the sentence.

These public persons, with authority from the sovereign power either to instruct or to judge the people, are members of the commonwealth that may fitly be compared to the organs of voice in a natural body.

Ministers for Execution

Public ministers also include all those who have authority from the sovereign to carry out the execution of judgments given; to publish the sovereign's commands; to suppress riots; to apprehend and imprison criminals; and to do other acts that tend to preserve the peace. Every act they do by such authority is the act of the commonwealth, and their service answers to that of the hands in a natural body.

Public ministers abroad are those who represent the person of their own sovereign to foreign states: ambassadors, messengers, agents, and heralds, sent by public authority and on public business.

But those sent only by the authority of some private faction of a troubled state, even if they are received, are neither public nor private ministers of the commonwealth — because none of their actions have the commonwealth for author. Likewise, an ambassador sent from one prince to another to offer congratulations or condolences, or to attend a ceremony, is a private person: the authority may be public, but the business is private, belonging to the prince in his natural capacity.

And if a man is sent secretly into another country to scout out its plans and its strength — a spy — then although both the authority and the business are public, he is only a private minister, because there is no one abroad who can take notice of any person in him except his own. Yet he is still a minister of the commonwealth, and may be compared to an eye in the natural body. And those appointed to receive the petitions and other information of the people — who are, so to speak, the public ear — are public ministers, and represent their sovereign in that office.

Counselors with No Employment but Advice Are Not Public Ministers

A counselor — or a council of state, if we consider it as having no authority of judicature or command, but only the job of giving advice to the sovereign when asked, or offering it unasked — is not a public person. The reason: the advice is addressed to the sovereign alone, and the sovereign's person cannot be represented to him by someone else in his own presence.

But a body of counselors is never without some other authority, either of judicature or of immediate administration. In a monarchy, they represent the monarch when delivering his commands to the public ministers. In a democracy, the council or senate presents the result of its deliberations to the people as a council — but when it appoints judges, or hears cases, or gives audience to ambassadors, it acts in the quality of a minister of the people. And in an aristocracy, the council of state is the sovereign assembly itself, and gives counsel to no one but itself.

Chapter 24: The Nutrition and Procreation of a Commonwealth

A Commonwealth Is Nourished by the Commodities of Sea and Land

The NUTRITION of a commonwealth consists in these things: plenty, meaning an abundance of the materials that sustain life; the distribution of those materials; their CONCOCTION — that is, their preparation, their digestion into a usable form; and, once prepared, their conveyance through convenient channels to public use.

As for plenty, nature itself sets the limit. The materials of life are those commodities which, from the two breasts of our common mother — the land and the sea — God either gives freely to mankind, or sells to us for the price of our labor.

The matter of this nourishment consists of animals, plants, and minerals, and God has laid them freely before us, on or near the face of the earth. Nothing more is required than the labor and industry of gathering them in. So plenty depends — next to God's favor — purely on the labor and industry of men.

This matter, commonly called commodities, is partly native and partly foreign. Native commodities are those available within the territory of the commonwealth; foreign ones are imported from outside. And because no territory under the dominion of a single commonwealth — unless it is enormous — produces everything needed for the maintenance and motion of the whole body, and because few territories fail to produce something beyond their own needs, the surplus commodities at home stop being surplus at all. They become the means of supplying what is lacking at home, by importing what can be had abroad — whether by exchange, by just war, or by labor. For a man's labor, too, is a commodity that can be exchanged for benefit, as much as any other thing. Indeed, there have been commonwealths with no more territory than they needed to live on that nevertheless not only maintained but increased their power — partly by the labor of carrying trade from one place to another, and partly by selling manufactured goods made from materials brought in from elsewhere.

And by the Right of Distributing Them

The distribution of the materials of this nourishment is the establishment of mine, and thine, and his — in one word, PROPERTY — and in every kind of commonwealth it belongs to the sovereign power. For where there is no commonwealth, there is (as I have already shown) a perpetual war of every man against his neighbor, in which everything belongs to whoever can grab it and keep it by force. That is neither property nor common ownership; it is uncertainty. This is so obvious that even Cicero — a passionate defender of liberty — attributed all property to the civil law, in a public court. "Let the civil law be once abandoned," he said, "or even carelessly guarded — not to say oppressed — and there is nothing any man can be sure of receiving from his ancestors, or of leaving to his children." And again: "Take away the civil law, and no man knows what is his own and what belongs to another."

Property, then, is an effect of the commonwealth — property begins with the commonwealth. And since the commonwealth can do nothing except through the person who represents it, distribution is the act of the sovereign alone, and it consists in laws, which no one can make who does not hold the sovereign power. The ancients understood this well. They called law Nomos — which means "distribution" — and they defined justice as distributing to every man his own.

All Private Land Comes Originally from the Sovereign's Distribution

In this distribution, the first law concerns the division of the land itself. The sovereign assigns every man a portion according to what he — and not any subject, or any number of subjects — judges consistent with equity and the common good. The children of Israel were a commonwealth in the wilderness, but they lacked the commodities of the earth until they became masters of the Promised Land. That land was afterward divided among them, not at their own discretion, but at the discretion of Eleazar the priest and Joshua their general. There were twelve tribes — thirteen, once the tribe of Joseph was subdivided — yet they made only twelve portions of land, ordained no land at all for the tribe of Levi, and assigned the Levites a tenth of the whole harvest instead. The division, in other words, was arbitrary: it followed the judgment of those in authority, not some pre-existing right.

And though a people who take possession of a land by war do not always exterminate the former inhabitants, as the Jews did, but leave many or most or all of them their estates, it is still clear that they hold those estates afterward as grants from the victor's distribution — just as the people of England held everything they had from William the Conqueror.

A Subject's Property Shuts Out Other Subjects, Not the Sovereign

From all this we can conclude what a subject's property in his land actually amounts to: a right to exclude every other subject from using it — not a right to exclude his sovereign, whether that sovereign is an assembly or a monarch. For the sovereign — that is, the commonwealth, whose person he bears — is understood to do nothing except for the sake of the common peace and security, and this distribution of lands is to be understood as done for the same end. It follows that any distribution the sovereign makes that damages the common peace and security is contrary to the will of every subject who entrusted his peace and safety to the sovereign's discretion and conscience, and so by the will of every one of them it is to be counted void.

It is true that a sovereign monarch, or the greater part of a sovereign assembly, may order many things done in pursuit of their passions, against their own consciences — and that is a breach of trust and of the law of nature. But this is not enough to authorize any subject to make war on his sovereign, or even to accuse him of injustice, or to speak evil of him in any way. The subjects authorized all his actions, and in granting him the sovereign power they made those actions their own. In what cases the commands of sovereigns are contrary to equity and the law of nature is a question I will take up later, in another place.

The Public Cannot Be Put on a Diet

In the distribution of land, one might imagine the commonwealth itself taking a portion — possessing it and improving it through its representative — and that this portion could be made large enough to cover the whole cost of the common peace and defense. And that would be perfectly true, if you could imagine a representative free of human passions and weaknesses. But human nature being what it is, setting aside public land, or any fixed revenue for the commonwealth, is pointless. It tends toward the dissolution of government, back into the condition of mere nature and war, the moment the sovereign power falls into the hands of a monarch or an assembly that is either too careless with money or too reckless in committing the public stock to a long or costly war.

Commonwealths can endure no diet. Their expenses are not limited by their own appetite but by external accidents — and by the appetites of their neighbors. So the public riches cannot be capped at any limit other than what emergencies happen to require. In England, the Conqueror reserved various lands for his own use (besides forests and hunting chases, kept for his recreation or for preserving the woods), and he reserved various services on the land he gave his subjects. Yet it seems these were reserved not for his maintenance in his public capacity but in his natural one — for he and his successors, despite all those reserves, laid arbitrary taxes on all their subjects' land whenever they judged it necessary.
