And if those public lands and services were intended as a sufficient maintenance for the commonwealth, the plan ran contrary to its own purpose: the taxes that followed prove the revenue was insufficient, and the recent state of the Crown's revenue proves it was subject to being sold off and whittled away. It is therefore pointless to assign a portion to the commonwealth — a commonwealth which may sell it or give it away, and which does sell it and give it away whenever its representative does so.

Where and in What Goods to Trade Abroad Depends on the Sovereign Too

Just as the distribution of lands at home belongs to the sovereign, so does the assignment of where, and in what commodities, the subject may trade abroad. For if that were left to the discretion of private persons, some of them would be drawn by profit both to supply the enemy with the means to hurt the commonwealth, and to hurt it themselves by importing things that please men's appetites but are nonetheless harmful — or at least useless — to them. And therefore it belongs to the commonwealth (that is, to the sovereign only) to approve or disapprove both the places and the goods of foreign trade.

The Laws of Transferring Property Also Belong to the Sovereign

Further: it is not enough for the sustenance of a commonwealth that every man have property in a portion of land, or in a few commodities, or a natural property in some useful skill — for there is hardly a skill in the world that is not necessary to the existence, or at least the well-being, of almost every individual man. So it is necessary that men distribute what they can spare, and transfer their property in it to one another, by exchange and mutual contract. And therefore it belongs to the commonwealth — that is to say, to the sovereign — to appoint the manner in which all kinds of contract between subjects (buying, selling, exchanging, borrowing, lending, leasing, and hiring) are to be made, and by what words and signs they shall be understood as valid. As for the matter and distribution of the nourishment to the several members of the commonwealth, this much — considering the scale of the whole work — is sufficient.

Money: The Blood of the Commonwealth

By concoction I mean this: reducing all the commodities that are not consumed right away, but stored up as nourishment for the future, into something of equal value — and something so portable that it does not hinder a man's movement from place to place, so that wherever he goes, he can buy whatever nourishment that place affords. And this something is nothing other than gold, and silver, and money. Gold and silver, as it happens, are highly valued in almost every country of the world, which makes them a convenient measure of the value of all other things between nations. And money — of whatever material, so long as it is coined by the sovereign of a commonwealth — is a sufficient measure of the value of all things between the subjects of that commonwealth.

By means of these measures, all commodities, movable and immovable, are made to accompany a man wherever he goes, inside or outside his ordinary place of residence. Money passes from man to man within the commonwealth, and goes round and round, nourishing every part of it as it circulates. In this way concoction is, so to speak, the sanguification of the commonwealth — its blood-making. For natural blood is made the same way, from the fruits of the earth, and as it circulates it nourishes every member of the body of man along its route.

And because silver and gold get their value from the material itself, they enjoy two privileges. First, their value cannot be altered by the power of one commonwealth, or even a few, since they are a common measure of the commodities of all places — whereas base money can easily be raised or lowered in value. Secondly, they give commonwealths the power to move, and to stretch out their arms, when need requires, into foreign countries — supplying not only private subjects who travel, but whole armies with provisions. But coin that is valued not for its material but only for the stamp of the place that minted it cannot endure a change of air. It has its effect at home only — and even there it is subject to changes in the law, which can shrink its value, often to the loss of the very people who hold it.

The Conduits That Carry Money to Public Use

The conduits and channels by which money is conveyed to public use are of two sorts: one that carries it into the public coffers, and another that issues it out again for public payments. Of the first sort are collectors, receivers, and treasurers. Of the second are the treasurers again, together with the officers appointed to pay the various public and private ministers. And in this, too, the artificial man keeps up his resemblance to the natural one. The veins of the natural body receive blood from all the parts of the body and carry it to the heart; there the blood is made vital, and the heart, through the arteries, sends it out again to enliven all the members and enable them to move.

Colonies: The Children of a Commonwealth

The PROCREATION, or children, of a commonwealth are what we call plantations, or colonies: groups of men sent out from the commonwealth under a conductor or governor, to inhabit a foreign country — either one previously empty of inhabitants, or one emptied at the time, by war.

When a colony is settled, one of two things happens. Either the colonists become a commonwealth of their own, discharged from their subjection to the sovereign who sent them — as many commonwealths of ancient times did. In that case the commonwealth they came from was called their metropolis, or mother city, and it requires no more of them than fathers require of the children they emancipate and set free from the family's government: namely, honor and friendship. Or else they remain united to their metropolis, as the colonies of the Roman people did — and then they are not commonwealths themselves, but provinces, parts of the commonwealth that sent them out. So the rights of colonies (apart from honor, and alliance with their metropolis) depend entirely on their license, or charter — the letters by which their sovereign authorized them to plant.

Chapter 25: On Counsel

What Counsel Is

Nothing shows more plainly how misleading it is to judge the nature of things by the ordinary, inconstant use of words than the confusion between counsel and command. The trouble comes from grammar: both are spoken in the imperative mood. "Do this" is what a man says when he commands — but it is also what he says when he gives counsel, and when he exhorts. And yet few people fail to see that these are very different things, or fail to tell them apart, once they notice who is speaking, to whom the speech is directed, and on what occasion.

But when readers meet these phrases in books, and either cannot or will not stop to consider the circumstances, they sometimes mistake the precepts of counselors for the precepts of commanders, and sometimes the reverse — whichever reading best suits the conclusion they want to draw or the action they want to approve. To prevent those mistakes, and to give the terms commanding, counseling, and exhorting their proper and distinct meanings, I define them as follows.

The Difference Between Command and Counsel

COMMAND is when a man says "Do this" or "Do not do this" without expecting any reason for it beyond the will of the one who says it. From this it follows plainly that he who commands is aiming at his own benefit. The only reason behind his command is his own will — and the proper object of every man's will is some good to himself.

COUNSEL is when a man says "Do this" or "Do not do this" and draws his reasons from the benefit that the action will bring to the person he is speaking to. From this it is evident that he who gives counsel claims to be aiming — whatever he actually intends — at the good of the one he advises.

So here is the first great difference between counsel and command: command is directed to the benefit of the one who gives it; counsel, to the benefit of the one who receives it.

From this comes a second difference. A man may be obliged to do what he is commanded — as when he has covenanted to obey. But he can never be obliged to do what he is counseled, because the harm of ignoring counsel falls on himself alone. And if he should covenant to follow it, then the counsel has changed its nature: it has become a command.

A third difference: no man can claim a right to be another man's counselor. He cannot claim that being heard benefits himself — that is not what counsel is for — and to demand the right to counsel someone is really to announce a desire to learn that person's plans, or to gain some other good for oneself, which (as I said before) is the proper object of every man's will.

One more thing belongs to the nature of counsel: whoever asks for it cannot, in fairness, accuse or punish the counsel he receives, whatever it turns out to be. To ask counsel of another man is to give him permission to advise whatever he thinks best. So a man who gives counsel to his sovereign — whether a monarch or an assembly — when the sovereign asks for it, cannot in fairness be punished for it, whether his advice agrees with the opinion of the majority or not, so long as it speaks to the question under debate. Indeed, if the sense of an assembly could be known before the debate was finished, the assembly should neither ask for nor accept any further counsel — for the sense of the assembly is the resolution of the debate and the end of all deliberation. The general rule is this: whoever demands counsel is the author of it, and therefore cannot punish it; and what the sovereign cannot do, no one else can. But if one subject counsels another to do something against the law, that counsel is punishable by the commonwealth — whether it came from bad intention or from mere ignorance — because ignorance of the law is no excuse where every man is bound to know the laws he lives under.

What Exhortation and Dehortation Are

EXHORTATION and DEHORTATION are counsel delivered with visible signs that the giver passionately wants it followed — or, to put it more briefly, counsel vehemently pressed. (Exhortation urges you toward an action; dehortation urges you away from it.) A man who exhorts does not carefully work out the consequences of what he advises, or bind himself to the rigor of true reasoning. He spurs the man he counsels toward action — just as the man who dehorts scares him away from it. So such speakers pitch their reasons to the common passions and opinions of their audience, and they reach for similes, metaphors, examples, and the other tools of oratory to persuade their hearers that following the advice would be useful, honorable, or just.

Several things follow from this. First: exhortation and dehortation are directed to the good of the speaker, not of the one being advised — which is contrary to the duty of a counselor, who by the very definition of counsel ought to regard not his own benefit but the benefit of the man he advises. And that the exhorter is serving his own benefit is plain enough from his long and vehement urging, and from the artful, calculated style of it. Nobody asked him for any of that. Since it springs from his own motives, it is directed chiefly to his own advantage — and only accidentally, or not at all, to the good of the person being counseled.

Second: exhortation and dehortation have a use only where a man is speaking to a crowd. When speech is addressed to one person, that person can interrupt and examine the reasons more rigorously than a multitude can — a multitude is simply too many people to enter into dispute and dialogue with someone addressing them all at once.

Third: those who exhort and dehort when they have been asked for counsel are corrupt counselors — bribed, as it were, by their own interest. However good their advice may happen to be, the man who gives it this way is no more a good counselor than a judge who hands down a just sentence for a bribe is a just judge. But where a man may lawfully command — a father in his family, a general in his army — his exhortations and dehortations are not only lawful but necessary and praiseworthy. Only then they are no longer counsels; they are commands. And when such commands order harsh and unpleasant labor, necessity sometimes — and humanity always — requires that they be sweetened in the delivery: framed with encouragement, in the tone and phrasing of counsel rather than the harsher language of command.

We can take examples of the difference between command and counsel from the forms of speech that express them in Holy Scripture. "Have no other gods but me. Make no graven image. Do not take God's name in vain. Keep the Sabbath holy. Honor your parents. Do not kill. Do not steal" — these are commands, because the reason we are to obey them is drawn from the will of God our King, whom we are obliged to obey. But these words — "Sell all you have, give it to the poor, and follow me" — are counsel, because the reason for doing so is drawn from our own benefit: namely, that we shall have "treasure in heaven." Again, "Go into the village across from you, and you will find a donkey tied, and her colt; untie her and bring her to me" is a command, for the reason behind the act is drawn from the will of the Master. But "Repent, and be baptized in the name of Jesus" is counsel — because doing so brings no benefit to God Almighty, who will still be King however we rebel, but only to ourselves, who have no other way of escaping the punishment hanging over us for our sins.

What Makes a Good or Bad Counselor

We have now derived the difference between counsel and command from the nature of counsel itself — which consists in laying out the benefit or harm that may come to the person counseled, through the necessary or probable consequences of the proposed action. From that same nature we can also derive the difference between fit and unfit counselors.

Experience is nothing but the memory of consequences observed in similar actions before, and counsel is nothing but the speech by which that experience is made known to another. So the virtues and defects of counsel are the same as the intellectual virtues and defects. To the person of a commonwealth, counselors serve in the place of memory and mental discourse. But here the resemblance between the commonwealth and a natural man breaks down in one respect of great importance. A natural man receives his experience from the natural objects of sense, which act on him without any passion or interest of their own. Whereas those who give counsel to the representative person of a commonwealth may have — and often do have — private aims and passions of their own, which render their counsels always suspect and many times unfaithful. So we may set down as the first condition of a good counselor: that his ends and interests not be inconsistent with the ends and interests of the one he counsels.

Second. The office of a counselor, when an action comes under deliberation, is to make its consequences plain — to inform the one he counsels truly and clearly. So he ought to deliver his advice in the form of speech that makes the truth appear most evident: with the firmest reasoning, in language exact and proper, and as briefly as the evidence allows. Therefore rash and unproven inferences (such as those fetched only from examples or from the authority of books — which are witnesses of fact or of opinion, not arguments about what is good or evil), obscure, confused, and ambiguous expressions, and all metaphorical speech designed to stir up passion, are repugnant to the office of a counselor. Such reasoning and such language are useful only for deceiving a man, or for steering him toward someone else's ends instead of his own.

Third. The ability to counsel comes from experience and long study, and no man can be presumed to have experience in everything that the administration of a great commonwealth requires. So no man is presumed to be a good counselor except in business he has not only handled extensively but has also thought long and hard about. The business of a commonwealth is to keep the people safe at home and defend them against foreign invasion. That requires great knowledge of human nature, of the rights of government, and of the nature of equity, law, justice, and honor — none of which can be gained without study. It also requires knowledge of the strength, resources, and geography of one's own country and its neighbors, and of the inclinations and designs of every nation that might do them harm — and that is not gained without much experience. Not just the whole of these subjects, but every single particular of them, demands the age and observation of a man in years, and more than ordinary study. The kind of wit required for counsel, as I have said before, is judgment; and men differ in judgment according to their different educations — some trained in one kind of study or business, some in another. Where infallible rules exist for doing a thing — as in machines and buildings, where the rules of geometry govern — all the experience in the world cannot equal the counsel of the man who has learned or discovered the rule. Where there is no such rule, the man with the most experience in that particular kind of business has the best judgment of it, and is the best counselor.

Fourth. To counsel a commonwealth in any business that touches another commonwealth, it is necessary to know the intelligence reports and dispatches that come from there, and all the records of treaties and other transactions of state between the two — which no one can do except those whom the representative sees fit to admit. From which we may see that people who have not been called to counsel have no good counsel to push forward in such cases.

Fifth. Supposing the number of counselors equal, a man is better counseled by hearing them separately than in an assembly — and for many reasons.

First, hearing them apart, you get the advice of every man. In an assembly, many of them deliver their advice with an aye or a no, or with their hands or feet, moved not by their own judgment but by the eloquence of another — or by fear of displeasing some speaker, or the whole assembly, by contradicting — or by fear of appearing slower-witted than those who applauded the contrary opinion.

Second, in any assembly of many, there cannot fail to be some whose interests run contrary to the public's. Those interests make them passionate; passion makes them eloquent; and eloquence draws others into the same advice. The passions of men, which are moderate when separate — like the heat of a single ember — when assembled are like many embers that inflame one another (especially when they fan each other with orations), to the point of setting the commonwealth on fire under the pretense of counseling it.

Third, hearing every man apart, you can examine — when there is need — the truth or probability of his reasons, and the grounds of the advice he gives, by frequent interruptions and objections. That cannot be done in an assembly, where on every difficult question a man is more likely to be stunned and dazzled by the variety of talk about it than informed of the course he ought to take. Besides, no assembly of many is ever called together for advice without someone in it ambitious to be thought eloquent and learned in politics — someone who gives his advice with an eye not to the business at hand but to the applause for his motley oration, stitched together from the multicolored threads and shreds of other authors. That is, at best, an irrelevance that eats up the time of serious consultation — and in the private way of counseling apart, it is easily avoided.

Fourth, in deliberations that ought to be kept secret — and public business gives many occasions for those — the counsels of many, and especially of assemblies, are dangerous. That is why great assemblies are forced to hand such affairs over to smaller numbers, and to the persons most experienced and most trusted.

To conclude: who approves of taking counsel from a great assembly of counselors so heartily that he would wish for, or even accept, their services when the question is marrying off his children, disposing of his lands, governing his household, or managing his private estate — especially if some among them do not wish him well?

A man who does his business with the help of many prudent counselors, consulting each one separately in his own area of expertise, does it best — like a man playing tennis with skilled partners, each placed at his proper station. He does next best who uses his own judgment alone, like a man who plays with no partner at all. But the man who is carried up and down to his business by an organized council — one that cannot move except by a plurality of agreeing opinions, and whose decisions are commonly slowed (out of envy or self-interest) by the dissenting faction — does it worst of all. He is like a man carried to the ball by players who may be good, but who carry him in a wheelbarrow or some other contraption: heavy in itself, dragged back by the clashing judgments and efforts of those pushing it, and all the more so the more hands are set to it — and worst of all when one or more among them wants him to lose.

It is true that many eyes see more than one. But that does not apply to many counselors — only to the case where the final decision rests with one man. Otherwise, because many eyes see the same thing along different lines of sight, and are apt to glance sideways toward their private benefit, those who do not wish to miss their mark, though they look about with two eyes, never aim with more than one. And so no great popular commonwealth was ever held together except by a foreign enemy that united it, or by the reputation of some one eminent man among them, or by the secret counsel of a few, or by the mutual fear of evenly matched factions — never by the open consultations of the assembly. As for very small commonwealths, popular or monarchical, no human wisdom can hold them up any longer than the jealousy of their powerful neighbors lasts.

Chapter 26: Of Civil Laws

What Civil Law Is

By CIVIL LAWS I mean the laws that men are bound to observe not because they are members of this or that commonwealth in particular, but because they are members of a commonwealth at all. Knowledge of particular laws belongs to those who make the study of their own countries' laws their profession; knowledge of civil law in general belongs to anyone. The ancient law of Rome was called their civil law, from the word civitas — which means a commonwealth. The countries that once lived under the Roman Empire and were governed by that law still keep whatever parts of it they see fit, and they call that portion "the Civil Law" to distinguish it from the rest of their own civil laws. But that is not what I intend to discuss here. My design is not to show what is law here or there, but what law is — as Plato, Aristotle, Cicero, and various others have done without ever taking up the legal profession.

First, it is obvious that law in general is not counsel but command. And not the command of just any man to just any man, but only the command of one whose command is addressed to someone formerly obliged to obey him. Civil law adds only one thing: the name of the person commanding, which is the persona civitatis — the person of the commonwealth.

With that in mind, I define civil law in this manner: CIVIL LAW is, to every subject, those rules which the commonwealth has commanded him — by word, writing, or other sufficient sign of its will — to use for the distinction of right and wrong; that is to say, of what is contrary to the rule and what is not.

There is nothing in this definition that is not evident at first sight. Everyone can see that some laws are addressed to all subjects in general, some to particular provinces, some to particular occupations, and some to particular men; each is law to everyone the command is directed at, and to no one else. Everyone can see that laws are the rules of just and unjust — nothing being counted unjust that is not contrary to some law. Likewise, that none can make laws but the commonwealth, because our subjection is to the commonwealth alone; and that commands must be signified by sufficient signs, because otherwise a man has no way of knowing how to obey them. Whatever can be deduced from this definition by necessary consequence, then, ought to be acknowledged as truth. Here is what I deduce from it.

The Sovereign Is the Legislator

1. In every commonwealth, the legislator is the sovereign and no one else — whether the sovereign is one man, as in a monarchy, or one assembly of men, as in a democracy or aristocracy. For the legislator is the one who makes the law, and only the commonwealth prescribes and commands the observance of the rules we call law. Therefore the commonwealth is the legislator. But the commonwealth is not a person, and has no capacity to do anything except through its representative — that is, the sovereign. Therefore the sovereign is the sole legislator. For the same reason, no one can repeal a law once made except the sovereign, because a law is repealed only by another law that forbids it to be put into execution.

And Is Not Subject to the Civil Laws

2. The sovereign of a commonwealth, whether an assembly or one man, is not subject to the civil laws. Since he has the power to make and repeal laws, he can free himself from any subjection whenever he pleases — by repealing the laws that trouble him and making new ones — and consequently he was free all along. The man who can be free whenever he wants to be is free already. Nor is it possible for any person to be bound to himself; whoever can bind can release, and so whoever is bound only to himself is not bound at all.

Custom Becomes Law by the Sovereign's Consent, Not by Length of Time

3. When long use acquires the authority of a law, it is not the length of time that makes the authority but the will of the sovereign, signified by his silence — for silence is sometimes a sign of consent. And custom remains law only so long as the sovereign stays silent about it. So if the sovereign has a question of right grounded not on his present will but on laws made long ago, the passage of time takes nothing away from his right, and the question must be judged by equity. After all, plenty of unjust actions and unjust sentences go unchallenged for longer than any man can remember. Our own lawyers agree that no customs count as law except reasonable ones, and that evil customs ought to be abolished. But the judgment of what is reasonable, and of what is to be abolished, belongs to the one who makes the law — the sovereign assembly or monarch.

The Law of Nature and the Civil Law Contain Each Other

4. The law of nature and the civil law contain each other, and they are of equal extent. Here is why. The laws of nature — which consist of equity, justice, gratitude, and the other moral virtues that depend on these — are not, in the condition of mere nature, properly laws at all, but qualities that dispose men to peace and obedience: conclusions, or theorems, as I said at the end of Chapter 15. Once a commonwealth is established, then they are actually laws — and not before — because then they are the commands of the commonwealth, and therefore civil laws as well; for it is the sovereign power that obliges men to obey them. When private men fall into disputes, someone must declare what equity is, what justice is, and what moral virtue is, and make those declarations binding; and that requires the ordinances of sovereign power, with punishments ordained for those who break them. Those ordinances are therefore part of the civil law. The law of nature, then, is part of the civil law in every commonwealth in the world.

The reverse is also true: the civil law is part of the dictates of nature. For justice — that is, performance of covenant, and giving every man his own — is a dictate of the law of nature. But every subject in a commonwealth has covenanted to obey the civil law: either with his fellow subjects, as when men assemble to make a common representative, or with the representative himself, one by one, as when men subdued by the sword promise obedience so that they may receive their lives. Therefore obedience to the civil law is part of the law of nature too. Civil law and natural law are not different kinds of law but different parts of law: one part, being written, is called civil; the other, unwritten, natural. The right of nature, however — man's natural liberty — may be cut back and restrained by the civil law. Indeed, the whole purpose of making laws is nothing but such restraint, without which there cannot possibly be any peace. Law was brought into the world for one thing only: to limit the natural liberty of particular men in such a way that they do not hurt one another but assist one another, and join together against a common enemy. These are the artificial chains, called civil laws; and the greatest liberty of subjects still depends on the silence of the law.

Provincial Laws Are Made by the Sovereign Power, Not by Custom

5. If the sovereign of one commonwealth subdues a people who have lived under other written laws, and afterward governs them by those same laws, those laws are still the civil laws of the victor, not of the vanquished commonwealth. For the legislator is not the one by whose authority the laws were first made, but the one by whose authority they continue to be laws now. So where the dominion of a commonwealth contains diverse provinces, and in those provinces a diversity of laws — commonly called the customs of each province — we are not to suppose that such customs have their force merely from length of time. They were anciently laws, written or otherwise made known, as the constitutions and statutes of the sovereigns of those days; and they are laws now, not by virtue of the prescription of time, but by the constitutions of their present sovereigns. But if an unwritten law is generally observed in all the provinces of a dominion, and no iniquity appears in its use, that law can be nothing but a law of nature, obliging all mankind equally.

Some Foolish Opinions of Lawyers About the Making of Laws

6. Since all laws, written and unwritten, receive their authority and force from the will of the commonwealth — that is, from the will of the representative, which in a monarchy is the monarch and in other commonwealths the sovereign assembly — a man may well wonder where certain opinions come from, found in the books of eminent lawyers in several commonwealths, which directly or by consequence make the legislative power depend on private men or subordinate judges.

For example: "The common law has no controller but Parliament." That is true only where a Parliament holds the sovereign power and can be assembled and dissolved by nothing but its own discretion. For if anyone else has the right to dissolve it, that person also has the right to control it — and consequently to control its controllings. And if no one has that right, then the controller of laws is not Parlamentum — the Parliament — but Rex in Parlamento — the King in Parliament. And where a Parliament is sovereign: even if it should assemble ever so many men, and ever so wise, from the countries subject to it, for whatever cause, no one will believe that such a gathering has thereby acquired legislative power for itself.

Another example: "The two arms of a commonwealth are force and justice; the first is in the King, the other deposited in the hands of Parliament." As if a commonwealth could hold together where the force is in any hand that justice has no authority to command and govern.

7. That law can never be against reason, our lawyers all agree; and they agree that the law is not the letter — that is, not every construction that can be put on the words — but what accords with the intention of the legislator. All true. But the question is: whose reason shall be received as law? It cannot be any private reason, for then there would be as much contradiction in the laws as there is among the Scholastics. Nor can it be — as Sir Edward Coke would have it (in his commentary on Littleton, Book 2, chapter 6, folio 97b) — an "artificial perfection of reason, gotten by long study, observation, and experience" (such as his own, he means). For long study may just as well increase and entrench erroneous opinions; and when men build on false foundations, the more they build, the greater the ruin. Of those who study and observe with equal time and equal diligence, the reasonings and resolutions are, and must remain, discordant. So it is not that juris prudentia — the wisdom of subordinate judges — that makes law, but the reason of this our artificial man, the commonwealth, and his command. And since the commonwealth, in its representative, is but one person, contradiction cannot easily arise in the laws; and when it does, the same reason is able to remove it, by interpretation or by alteration. In all courts of justice it is the sovereign — the person of the commonwealth — who judges. The subordinate judge ought to keep in view the reason that moved his sovereign to make the law, so that his sentence conforms to it; then his sentence is his sovereign's sentence. Otherwise it is his own, and an unjust one.

A Law Made but Not Made Known Is No Law

8. From the fact that law is a command, and that a command consists in a declaration or manifestation — by voice, writing, or some other sufficient sign — of the will of the one who commands, we may understand this: the command of the commonwealth is law only to those who have the means to take notice of it. Over born fools, children, and madmen there is no law, any more than over brute beasts; nor are they capable of the title of just or unjust, because they never had the power to make any covenant or to understand its consequences, and so they never took it upon themselves to authorize the actions of any sovereign, as those who make themselves a commonwealth must do. And just as nature or accident has taken from some men the notice of all laws in general, so too any man from whom some accident — not arising from his own fault — has taken away the means of taking notice of a particular law is excused if he does not observe it. Properly speaking, that law is no law to him. It is therefore necessary to consider here what arguments and signs are sufficient for knowing what the law is — that is to say, what the will of the sovereign is — in monarchies and in other forms of government alike.

All Unwritten Laws Are Laws of Nature

First: if a law obliges all subjects without exception, and is not written or otherwise published in places where they can take notice of it, it is a law of nature. For whatever men are to recognize as law not on other men's word but each from his own reason must be agreeable to the reason of all men — and no law fits that description but the law of nature. The laws of nature therefore need no publishing and no proclamation, since they are contained in this one sentence, approved by all the world: Do not that to another, which thou thinkest unreasonable to be done by another to thyself.

Second: if a law obliges only some category of men, or one particular man, and is not written or published by word, it is likewise a law of nature, known by the same arguments and signs that distinguish those in that condition from other subjects. For any law that is not written, or somehow published by the one who makes it law, can be known in no way except by the reason of the one who is to obey it — and such a law is therefore not only civil but natural. For example, if the sovereign employs a public minister without written instructions about what to do, that minister is obliged to take the dictates of reason as his instructions. If the sovereign makes him a judge, the judge is to take notice that his sentence ought to follow the reason of his sovereign — and since that reason is always understood to be equity, he is bound to it by the law of nature. If an ambassador, then in everything not contained in his written instructions, he is to take as his instruction whatever reason dictates to be most conducive to his sovereign's interest. And so it goes for all other ministers of the sovereignty, public and private. All these instructions of natural reason may be gathered under one name: fidelity, which is a branch of natural justice.

The law of nature excepted, it belongs to the essence of all other laws to be made known to every man who shall be obliged to obey them — by word, by writing, or by some other act known to proceed from the sovereign authority. For the will of another cannot be understood except by his own word or act, or by conjecture drawn from his aim and purpose; and in the person of the commonwealth, that purpose must always be presumed consonant with equity and reason. In ancient times, before letters were in common use, laws were often put into verse, so that uneducated people, taking pleasure in singing or reciting them, could keep them more easily in memory. For the same reason Solomon advises a man to bind the ten commandments upon his ten fingers (Proverbs 7:3). And concerning the law that Moses gave the people of Israel at the renewing of the covenant, he tells them to teach it to their children, talking of it both at home and on the road, at lying down and at rising up (Deuteronomy 11:19); to write it on the posts and doors of their houses; and to assemble the people — man, woman, and child — to hear it read (Deuteronomy 31:12).

Nothing Is Law Where the Legislator Cannot Be Known

Nor is it enough that the law be written and published; there must also be clear signs that it proceeds from the will of the sovereign. For private men, when they have force enough — or think they have — to secure their unjust designs and carry them safely to their ambitious ends, may publish whatever they please as law, without the legislative authority or against it. What is required, then, is not only a declaration of the law, but also sufficient signs of its author and its authority. The author, or legislator, is presumed in every commonwealth to be evident, because he is the sovereign — constituted by the consent of everyone, and so presumed by everyone to be sufficiently known. It is true that men are generally so heedless, and so secure, that once the memory of the first constitution of their commonwealth wears out, they no longer consider by whose power they are defended against their enemies, by whose power their industry is protected, and by whose power they are righted when injury is done to them. But since no man who actually considers the matter can question it, no excuse can be drawn from ignorance of where the sovereignty is placed. And it is a dictate of natural reason — and consequently an evident law of nature — that no man ought to weaken the very power whose protection he has himself demanded, or knowingly accepted, against others. Therefore no man can have any doubt about who is sovereign except by his own fault, whatever evil men may suggest. The real difficulty lies in the evidence of the authority derived from him; and removing that difficulty depends on knowledge of the public registers, public counsels, public ministers, and public seals, by which all laws are sufficiently verified.

The Difference Between Verifying and Authorizing

Verified, I say — not authorized. For the verification is only the testimony and record, not the authority of the law, which consists in the command of the sovereign alone.

The Law Verified by the Subordinate Judge

If, then, a man has a question of injury that depends on the law of nature — that is to say, on common equity — the sentence of the judge who is commissioned with authority to hear such causes is a sufficient verification of the law of nature in that individual case. The advice of someone who makes the study of the law his profession is useful for avoiding disputes; but it is only advice. It is the judge who must tell men what is law, upon the hearing of the controversy.

By the Public Registers

But when the question is of an injury or a crime under a written law, every man can — by recourse to the registers, himself or through others — be sufficiently informed, before he does the injury or commits the crime, whether it is an injury or not. Indeed, he ought to do so: when a man doubts whether the act he is about to do is just or unjust, and can inform himself if he wishes, going ahead anyway is unlawful. Likewise, a man who supposes himself injured in a case settled by the written law — which he can see and consider, himself or through others — does unjustly if he complains before he consults the law; he reveals a disposition to harass other men rather than to demand his own right.

By Letters Patent and the Public Seal

If the question is of obedience to a public officer, then to have seen his commission with the public seal and heard it read — or to have had the means of being informed of it, had one wished — is a sufficient verification of his authority. For every man is obliged to do his best endeavor to inform himself of all written laws that may concern his own future actions.

The Interpretation of the Law Depends on the Sovereign Power

The legislator known, and the laws sufficiently published — whether in writing or by the light of nature — there is still one very material circumstance wanting to make them obligatory. For the nature of a law lies not in its letter but in its intent, or meaning — that is to say, in the authentic interpretation of the law, which is the sense of the legislator. Therefore the interpretation of all laws depends on the sovereign authority; and the interpreters can be none but those whom the sovereign — to whom alone the subject owes obedience — shall appoint. Otherwise, by the craft of an interpreter, the law may be made to bear a sense contrary to that of the sovereign, and by that means the interpreter becomes the legislator.

All Laws Need Interpretation

All laws, written and unwritten, need interpretation. The unwritten law of nature is easy enough for those who use their natural reason without partiality or passion, and it therefore leaves its violators without excuse. But considering that there are very few men — perhaps none — who are not in some cases blinded by self-love or some other passion, it has become of all laws the most obscure, and consequently it has the greatest need of able interpreters. Written laws, if short, are easily misinterpreted through the various meanings of a word or two; if long, they are all the more obscure through the various meanings of many words. So no written law, delivered in few words or many, can be well understood without a perfect understanding of the final causes for which the law was made — and the knowledge of those final causes is in the legislator. To him, therefore, no knot in the law can be insoluble: either he finds the ends of the thread and unties it that way, or he makes whatever ends he pleases by the legislative power — as Alexander did with his sword and the Gordian knot — which no other interpreter can do.

The Authentic Interpretation of Law Is Not That of Writers

The interpretation of the laws of nature, in a commonwealth, does not depend on books of moral philosophy. The authority of writers, without the authority of the commonwealth, does not make their opinions law, however true those opinions may be. What I have written in this very treatise concerning the moral virtues, and their necessity for procuring and maintaining peace, though it is evident truth, is not law on that account; it is law because in every commonwealth in the world it is part of the civil law. Naturally reasonable it may be — but it is by the sovereign power that it is law. Otherwise it would be a great error to call the laws of nature "unwritten law" at all, when we see so many volumes published about them, and in those volumes so many contradictions of one another, and of themselves.

The Interpreter of the Law Is the Judge, Giving Sentence Viva Voce — Aloud, in Court — in Every Particular Case

The interpretation of the law of nature is the sentence of the judge who is constituted by the sovereign authority to hear and determine the controversies that depend on it; and it consists in applying the law to the case at hand. For in the act of judging, the judge does no more than consider whether the demand of the party is consonant with natural reason and equity; and the sentence he gives is therefore the interpretation of the law of nature. That interpretation is authentic — not because it is his private sentence, but because he gives it by the authority of the sovereign, by which it becomes the sovereign's sentence, and law, for that time, to the parties pleading.

A Judge's Ruling Does Not Bind Him, or Any Other Judge, to Rule the Same Way in Similar Cases Forever After

But no judge, subordinate or sovereign, is immune to error in a judgment of equity. So if, in a later case of the same kind, he finds it more consistent with equity to give the contrary ruling, he is obliged to do so. No man's error becomes his own law, or obliges him to persist in it. And for the same reason, it does not become law for other judges either, even if they have sworn to follow it.

Granted: when a wrong ruling is given by the sovereign's authority, and the sovereign knows of it and allows it, then in laws that are changeable it amounts to the making of a new law — for cases in which every little circumstance is the same. But in laws that cannot change, such as the laws of nature, wrong rulings are no laws at all: not to the same judge, not to other judges, not in similar cases, ever after. Princes succeed one another; one judge passes and another comes; indeed, heaven and earth shall pass away, but not one tittle of the law of nature shall pass — for it is the eternal law of God. Therefore all the rulings of all the judges who have ever lived cannot, even taken together, make a law contrary to natural equity. Nor can the examples of former judges justify an unreasonable ruling, or excuse the present judge from the work of studying what equity requires in the case before him, from the principles of his own natural reason.

Take an example. It is against the law of nature to punish the innocent — and an innocent man is one who clears himself in court and is acknowledged innocent by the judge. Now suppose a man is accused of a capital crime. Seeing the power and malice of some enemy, and how often judges are corrupt and partial, he runs away for fear of how it might turn out. Later he is captured and brought to a lawful trial, where he shows convincingly that he was not guilty of the crime, and is acquitted of it — and is nevertheless condemned to forfeit his goods. This is a plain condemnation of an innocent man. So I say there is no place in the world where this can count as an interpretation of a law of nature, or be made law by the rulings of earlier judges who did the same thing. The judge who first ruled that way ruled unjustly, and no injustice can be a pattern of judgment for the judges who come after.

A written law may forbid innocent men to flee, and they may be punished for fleeing. But to treat flight from fear of injury as a presumption of guilt, after a man has already been judicially absolved of the crime, is contrary to the very nature of a presumption — which has no place once judgment has been given. Yet a great lawyer sets exactly this down as the common law of England. "If a man," he says, "that is innocent, be accused of felony, and for fear flees for the same; although he judicially acquits himself of the felony, yet if it be found that he fled for the felony, he shall, notwithstanding his innocence, forfeit all his goods, chattels, debts, and duties. For as to the forfeiture of them, the law will admit no proof against the presumption in law, grounded upon his flight."

Look at what is being said here. An innocent man, judicially acquitted, despite his innocence — and when no written law forbade him to flee — is condemned, after his acquittal, on a mere presumption in law, to lose everything he owns. If the law grounds on his flight a presumption of the deed itself — and the deed was capital — then the sentence should have been capital. If the presumption was not of the deed, then why should he lose his goods? This, therefore, is no law of England; and the condemnation rests not on a presumption of law, but on the presumption of the judges.

It is also against law to say that no proof shall be admitted against a presumption of law. All judges, sovereign and subordinate, who refuse to hear proof, refuse to do justice. Even if the sentence happens to be just, judges who condemn without hearing the proofs offered are unjust judges, and their presumption is nothing but prejudice — which no man ought to carry with him to the seat of justice, whatever precedent judgments or examples he claims to be following. There are other matters of this kind in which men's judgments have been warped by trusting to precedents; but this much is enough to show that although a judge's sentence is law to the party pleading before him, it is no law to any judge who succeeds him in that office.

Likewise, when the question concerns the meaning of written laws, the interpreter is not whoever writes a commentary on them. Commentaries are usually more open to quibbling than the text itself, and so they need further commentaries — and there is no end to that kind of interpretation. So unless there is an interpreter authorized by the sovereign, from whom the subordinate judges may not depart, the interpreter can be none other than the ordinary judges, exactly as in cases of unwritten law; and their sentences are to be taken as law by the parties pleading — in that particular case — but they do not bind other judges to rule the same way in similar cases. A judge can err even in interpreting written laws; but no error of a subordinate judge can change the law, which is the general sentence of the sovereign.

The Difference Between the Letter and the Meaning of the Law

With written laws, men commonly distinguish between the letter of the law and its sentence — its meaning. If by the letter is meant whatever can be squeezed out of the bare words, the distinction is a good one. For the meanings of almost all words, either in themselves or in their metaphorical uses, are ambiguous, and a clever argument can stretch them into many senses — but there is only one sense of the law. If, however, by the letter is meant the literal sense, then the letter and the sentence — the intention — of the law are one and the same. For the literal sense is the sense the legislator intended the letter of the law to convey; and the intention of the legislator is always presumed to be equity. For a judge to think otherwise of the sovereign would be a serious insult.

So if the words of the law do not fully authorize a reasonable sentence, the judge ought to fill the gap with the law of nature; or, if the case is difficult, postpone judgment until he has received fuller authority. For example: a written law ordains that whoever is thrust out of his house by force shall be restored to it by force. Now it happens that a man carelessly leaves his house empty, and on returning is kept out of it by force — a case for which no specific law was ordained. It is evident that this case is contained in the same law; otherwise the man has no remedy at all, and that, we must suppose, is against the intention of the legislator.

Again, the words of the law command the judge to rule according to the evidence. A man is falsely accused of a deed that the judge himself saw done — by someone else, not by the accused. In this case the judge should neither follow the letter of the law into condemning the innocent, nor give sentence against the evidence of the witnesses, since the letter of the law runs the other way. Instead he should arrange with the sovereign for another judge to be appointed, and serve as a witness himself. So the awkward results that follow from the bare words of a written law may lead a judge to the intention of the law, and so to a better interpretation of it — but no awkward result can justify a sentence against the law. For a judge of right and wrong is not a judge of what is convenient or inconvenient to the commonwealth.

The Abilities Required in a Judge

The abilities required in a good interpreter of the law — that is, in a good judge — are not the same as those of an advocate, namely the study of the laws. A judge ought to take notice of the facts from nothing but the witnesses; and in the same way he ought to take notice of the law from nothing but the statutes and constitutions of the sovereign, cited in the pleadings, or declared to him by those who have authority from the sovereign power to declare them. He has no need to work out in advance what his judgment will be: what he shall say about the facts will be given to him by witnesses, and what he shall say on points of law will come from those who present it in their pleadings and interpret it, with authority, on the spot.

The Lords of Parliament in England were judges, and the most difficult cases have been heard and decided by them; yet few of them were deeply versed in the study of the laws, and fewer still had practiced law as a profession. And although they consulted with lawyers appointed to attend for that very purpose, the Lords alone had the authority to give sentence. In the same way, in ordinary trials of right, twelve men of the common people are the judges, and they give sentence not only on the facts but on the right itself, pronouncing simply for the plaintiff or for the defendant. That is, they are judges not only of fact but also of right; and in a criminal case they determine not only whether the deed was done or not done, but whether it was murder, homicide, felony, assault, and the like — which are determinations of law. But because they are not expected to know the law on their own, someone with authority is there to inform them of it in the particular case they are to judge. And yet if they do not rule according to what he tells them, they face no penalty for it — unless it can be shown that they ruled against their consciences, or had been corrupted by a bribe.

The things that make a good judge, or a good interpreter of the laws, are these. First, a right understanding of that principal law of nature called equity — which depends not on reading other men's writings, but on the goodness of a man's own natural reason and his own reflection, and is therefore presumed to be strongest in those who have had the most leisure, and the most inclination, to reflect on it. Second, contempt for unnecessary riches and promotions. Third, the ability, in judging, to strip himself of all fear, anger, hatred, love, and compassion. Fourth and last, patience to hear, diligent attention in hearing, and the memory to retain, digest, and apply what he has heard.

Divisions of Law

The classification and division of the laws has been done in different ways, according to the different methods of the men who have written about them. It is a matter that depends not on nature but on the writer's purpose, and it serves each man's own method. In the Institutes of Justinian, we find seven sorts of civil laws:

1. The edicts, constitutions, and epistles of the prince — that is, of the emperor — because the whole power of the people resided in him. The proclamations of the kings of England are like these.

2. The decrees of the whole people of Rome (including the senate), when the question was put to them by the senate. These were laws, at first, by virtue of the sovereign power residing in the people; and those of them that the emperors did not repeal remained laws by the imperial authority. For all binding laws are understood to be laws by the authority of whoever has the power to repeal them. Somewhat like these laws are the Acts of Parliament in England.

3. The decrees of the common people (excluding the senate), when the question was put to them by the tribune of the people. Those of them not repealed by the emperors remained laws by the imperial authority. Like these were the orders of the House of Commons in England.

4. Senatus consulta — the orders of the senate. When the people of Rome grew so numerous that it became inconvenient to assemble them, the emperor thought it fitting that men should consult the senate instead of the people. These bear some resemblance to the acts of council.

5. The edicts of praetors and, in some cases, of the aediles — like the chief justices in the courts of England.

6. Responsa prudentum — the answers of the learned: the rulings and opinions of those lawyers whom the emperor gave authority to interpret the law, and to give answers to those who came to them for legal advice. Judges were obliged by the emperor's constitutions to observe those answers in giving judgment; and they would be like the reports of adjudged cases in England, if other judges were bound by the law of England to observe them. For the judges of the common law of England are not, properly speaking, judges, but juris consulti — legal advisers — of whom the real judges, who are either the Lords or twelve men of the county, are to ask advice on points of law.

7. Also unwritten customs, which by their own nature are an imitation of law. By the tacit consent of the emperor, provided they are not contrary to the law of nature, these are genuinely laws.

Another division of laws is into natural and positive. Natural laws are those that have been laws from all eternity. They are called not only natural but also moral laws, consisting in the moral virtues — justice, equity, and all the habits of mind that lead to peace and charity — of which I have already spoken in the fourteenth and fifteenth chapters.

Positive laws are those that have not existed from eternity, but were made laws by the will of those who held sovereign power over others; and they are either written, or made known to men by some other sign of the will of their legislator.

Another Division of Law

Again, of positive laws, some are human and some divine. And of human positive laws, some are distributive and some penal. Distributive laws are those that determine the rights of subjects, declaring to every man the terms on which he acquires and holds property in lands or goods, and a right or liberty of action; and these speak to all the subjects. Penal laws are those that declare what penalty shall be inflicted on those who violate the law; and these speak to the ministers and officers appointed to carry out the punishment. For although everyone ought to be informed in advance of the punishments ordained for transgression, the command is not addressed to the offender — who cannot be expected to punish himself faithfully — but to the public ministers appointed to see the penalty executed. These penal laws are for the most part written together with the distributive laws, and are sometimes called judgments. For all laws are general judgments, or sentences, of the legislator; and likewise every particular judgment is a law to the man whose case is judged.

How Divine Positive Law Is Made Known to Be Law

Divine positive laws (for natural laws, being eternal and universal, are all divine) are those commandments of God which are not from all eternity, nor addressed universally to all men, but only to a certain people or to certain persons — and which are declared to be God's laws by those whom God has authorized to declare them. But how can this human authority to declare God's positive laws be known? God may command a man, in a supernatural way, to deliver laws to other men. But it is of the essence of law that the person to be bound by it be assured of the authority of the one who declares it — and we cannot naturally perceive that authority to come from God. So two questions arise. How can a man, without a supernatural revelation of his own, be assured of the revelation received by the declarer? And how can he be bound to obey those laws?

To the first question — how a man can be assured of another's revelation without a revelation made particularly to himself — the answer is that it is evidently impossible. A man may be moved to believe in such a revelation by the miracles he sees the declarer perform, or by the extraordinary holiness of his life, or his extraordinary wisdom, or the extraordinary success of his actions — all of which are marks of God's extraordinary favor. But they are not assured evidence of special revelation. Miracles are marvelous works; but what is marvelous to one man may not be marvelous to another. Holiness can be faked; and the visible successes of this world are most often the work of God through natural and ordinary causes. So no man can infallibly know, by natural reason, that another man has had a supernatural revelation of God's will. He can have only a belief — and each man's belief will be firmer or weaker as the signs of it appear greater or smaller.

The second question — how he can be bound to obey such laws — is not so hard. If the law declared is not against the law of nature (which is undoubtedly God's law), and he undertakes to obey it, then he is bound by his own act. Bound, I say, to obey it — but not bound to believe it. For men's beliefs and inward thoughts are not subject to commands; they answer only to the working of God, ordinary or extraordinary. Faith in a supernatural law is not a fulfilling of it but only an assenting to it — not a duty we offer to God, but a gift God freely gives to whom he pleases. Just so, unbelief is not a breach of any of God's laws, but a rejection of them all — except the laws of nature.

What I am saying here will be made still clearer by the examples and testimonies on this point in holy Scripture. The covenant God made with Abraham — in a supernatural manner — was this: "This is the covenant which thou shalt observe between me and thee and thy seed after thee" (Genesis 17:10). Abraham's descendants did not receive this revelation; they did not yet exist. Yet they are a party to the covenant, and bound to obey whatever Abraham declared to them as God's law. And they could not be bound except through the obedience they owed to their parents — who, when they are subject to no other earthly power, as in Abraham's case, hold sovereign power over their children and servants. Again, where God says to Abraham, "In thee shall all nations of the earth be blessed: for I know thou wilt command thy children, and thy house after thee, to keep the way of the Lord, and to observe righteousness and judgment," it is plain that the obedience of his family, who had no revelation of their own, depended on their prior obligation to obey their sovereign.

At Mount Sinai, only Moses went up to God; the people were forbidden to approach, on pain of death — and yet they were bound to obey everything Moses declared to them as God's law. On what ground, except this submission of their own: "Speak thou to us, and we will hear thee; but let not God speak to us, lest we die"? These two passages make it sufficiently clear that in a commonwealth, a subject who has no certain and assured revelation made particularly to himself concerning the will of God is to obey, as God's will, the command of the commonwealth. For if men were free to take their own dreams and fancies — or the dreams and fancies of private men — for God's commandments, scarcely two men would agree on what God's commandments are; and yet, out of regard for those fancies, every man would despise the commandments of the commonwealth.

I conclude, therefore, that in all things not contrary to the moral law — that is to say, to the law of nature — all subjects are bound to obey as divine law whatever is declared to be so by the laws of the commonwealth. This is evident to anyone's reason. Whatever is not against the law of nature may be made law in the name of those who hold the sovereign power; and there is no reason men should be any less obliged by it when it is propounded in the name of God. Besides, there is no place in the world where men are permitted to claim commandments of God other than those the commonwealth declares to be such. Christian states punish those who revolt from the Christian religion; and all other states punish those who set up any religion their laws forbid. And in whatever is not regulated by the commonwealth, it is equity — which is the law of nature, and therefore an eternal law of God — that every man equally enjoy his liberty.

Another Division of Laws

There is also another distinction of laws: into fundamental and not fundamental. I confess I could never discover, in any author, what a fundamental law is supposed to mean. Nevertheless, one may very reasonably distinguish laws in that manner.

What a Fundamental Law Is

A FUNDAMENTAL LAW, in every commonwealth, is one which, if it is taken away, the commonwealth fails and is utterly dissolved — like a building whose foundation is destroyed. A fundamental law, therefore, is the law by which subjects are bound to uphold whatever power is given to the sovereign, whether a monarch or a sovereign assembly, without which the commonwealth cannot stand: such as the power of war and peace, of judicature, of choosing officers, and of doing whatever the sovereign judges necessary for the public good. A law is not fundamental when repealing it does not drag the dissolution of the commonwealth along with it — such as the laws concerning disputes between one subject and another. So much for the division of laws.

The Difference Between Law and Right

I find the terms lex civilis and jus civile — civil law and civil right — used interchangeably for the same thing, even in the most learned authors; and they ought not to be. For right is liberty — namely, the liberty the civil law leaves us. But civil law is an obligation, and takes from us the liberty the law of nature gave us. Nature gave every man a right to secure himself by his own strength, and to strike a suspected neighbor first, as a precaution; the civil law takes away that liberty in every case where the law's protection can safely be waited for. So lex and jus — law and right — differ as much as obligation and liberty.

And Between a Law and a Charter

Likewise, laws and charters are taken indiscriminately for the same thing. Yet charters are donations of the sovereign — not laws, but exemptions from law. The language of a law is jubeo, injungo — "I command and enjoin." The language of a charter is dedi, concessi — "I have given, I have granted." And what is given or granted to a man is not forced upon him by a law. A law may be made to bind all the subjects of a commonwealth; a liberty, or charter, applies only to one man, or to some one part of the people. For to say that all the people of a commonwealth have liberty in some case whatsoever is only to say that, in such a case, no law has been made — or that, having been made, it has now been repealed.

Chapter 27: Of Crimes, Excuses, and Extenuations

What Sin Is

A SIN is not only a violation of a law, but also any contempt for the lawgiver. For such contempt is a breach of all his laws at once. So sin can consist not only in committing an act, or speaking words the laws forbid, or failing to do what the law commands, but also in the intention or purpose to break the law. The mere purpose to break the law is already a degree of contempt toward the one whose job it is to see the law enforced.

But notice the line here. To take pleasure in the mere imagination of possessing another man's goods, servants, or wife — with no intention of taking them from him by force or fraud — is no breach of the law that says "Thou shalt not covet." Nor is it a sin to take pleasure in imagining or dreaming of the death of a man from whose life you expect nothing but damage and grief. The sin begins when you resolve to put some act in execution that tends toward it. For to be pleased by the fiction of something that would please you if it were real is a passion so built into the nature of man — and of every other living creature — that to make it a sin would be to make a sin of being human. Thinking this through has made me consider those people too severe, both on themselves and on others, who maintain that the first stirrings of the mind are sins, even when checked by the fear of God. Though I confess it is safer to err on that side than on the other.

What a Crime Is

A CRIME is a sin that consists in committing — by deed or word — what the law forbids, or omitting what it has commanded. So every crime is a sin; but not every sin is a crime. To intend to steal or kill is a sin, even if it never shows itself in word or deed: God, who sees the thoughts of man, can lay it to his charge. But until it appears in something done or said, from which a human judge can argue the intention, it does not have the name of crime.

The Greeks observed this distinction in their words: hamartema on one side, and egklema or aitia on the other. The first (translated "sin") means any swerving from the law whatsoever; the latter two (translated "crime") mean only that kind of sin for which one man may accuse another. For intentions that never appear in any outward act, there is no place for human accusation. The Latins did the same: peccatum, which is sin, covers every kind of deviation from the law; but crimen — a word they derive from cerno, "to perceive" — means only such sins as can be made to appear before a judge, and therefore are not mere intentions.

Where There Is No Civil Law, There Is No Crime

From this relation of sin to law, and of crime to civil law, several things follow.

First: where law ceases, sin ceases. But because the law of nature is eternal, violation of covenants, ingratitude, arrogance, and all acts contrary to any moral virtue can never cease to be sins.

Second: where the civil law ceases, crimes cease. For when no law remains but the law of nature, there is no place for accusation — every man is then his own judge, accused only by his own conscience and cleared by the uprightness of his own intention. So when his intention is right, his act is no sin; when it is not, his act is a sin — but not a crime.

Third: when the sovereign power ceases, crime ceases too. For where there is no such power, there is no protection to be had from the law, and so everyone may protect himself by his own strength. No man, in setting up a sovereign power, can be supposed to have given away the right of preserving his own body — the very thing all sovereignty was established to secure. But this holds only for those who did not themselves help to destroy the power that protected them. For that was a crime from the beginning.

Ignorance of the Law of Nature Excuses No One

The source of every crime is some defect of the understanding, or some error in reasoning, or some sudden force of the passions. The defect in understanding is ignorance; in reasoning, erroneous opinion.

Ignorance, in turn, comes in three sorts: ignorance of the law, of the sovereign, and of the penalty.

Ignorance of the law of nature excuses no one, because every man who has reached the use of reason is supposed to know that he ought not do to another what he would not have done to himself. So wherever a man goes, if he does anything contrary to that law, it is a crime. If a man comes here from the Indies and persuades people to receive a new religion, or teaches them anything that tends toward disobedience of this country's laws — however sincerely convinced he is of the truth of what he teaches — he commits a crime, and may justly be punished for it. Not only because his doctrine is false, but because he is doing what he would never approve in another: namely, someone traveling from here to there and trying to change the religion in his country. But ignorance of the civil law does excuse a man in a foreign country, until that law is declared to him — because until then, no civil law binds him.

Ignorance of the Civil Law Sometimes Excuses

Likewise, if the civil law of a man's own country has not been declared clearly enough for him to know it if he wishes, and the action is not against the law of nature, his ignorance is a good excuse. In all other cases, ignorance of the civil law excuses nothing.

Ignorance of the Sovereign Does Not Excuse

Ignorance of the sovereign power in the place where a man ordinarily lives does not excuse him; he ought to take notice of the power by which he has been protected there.

Ignorance of the Penalty Does Not Excuse

Ignorance of the penalty, where the law has been declared, excuses no one. For in breaking the law — which without fear of penalty would not be a law at all, just empty words — he takes on the penalty even if he does not know what it is. Whoever voluntarily does any action accepts all the known consequences of it; and punishment is a known consequence of breaking the laws in every commonwealth. If the punishment is already fixed by law, he is subject to that; if not, he is subject to arbitrary punishment. And this is only reasonable: he who does injury with no limit but his own will should suffer punishment with no limit but the will of him whose law he violated.

Punishments Declared Before the Fact Excuse from Greater Punishments After It

But when a penalty is attached to the crime in the law itself, or has usually been inflicted in similar cases, the offender is excused from any greater penalty. For a punishment known in advance, if it is not great enough to deter men from the action, is an invitation to it. When men compare the benefit of their injustice with the harm of their punishment, by necessity of nature they choose what looks best for themselves. So when they are punished more than the law had previously fixed, or more than others were punished for the same crime, it is the law that tempted and deceived them.

Nothing Can Be Made a Crime by a Law Made After the Fact

No law made after an act is done can make that act a crime. If the act is against the law of nature, then the law existed before the act; and a positive law cannot be taken notice of before it is made, and therefore cannot bind. But when the law forbidding an act was made before the act was done, the man who does it is liable to a penalty ordained afterward — provided no lesser penalty had been made known before, either in writing or by example — for the reason just given.

False Principles of Right and Wrong as Causes of Crime

From defect in reasoning — that is, from error — men are prone to violate the laws in three ways.

First, by presuming false principles. Men observe how, in all places and all ages, unjust actions have been authorized by the force and victories of those who committed them; how powerful men break through the cobweb laws of their country while the weaker sort, and those who failed in their schemes, are counted the only criminals. From this they adopt principles and grounds of reasoning such as: "Justice is just an empty word. Whatever a man can get by his own effort and risk is his own. The practice of all nations cannot be unjust. The examples of former times are good arguments for doing the same again" — and many more of that kind. Grant these, and no act can be a crime in itself; it must be made one not by the law, but by the success of those who commit it. The same act becomes virtuous or vicious as Fortune pleases — so that what Marius makes a crime, Sulla makes meritorious, and Caesar (the same laws still standing) turns back into a crime again, to the perpetual disturbance of the peace of the commonwealth.

False Teachers Misinterpreting the Law of Nature

Second, by false teachers, who either misinterpret the law of nature so that it contradicts the civil law, or teach as laws their own doctrines, or traditions of former times, that are inconsistent with the duty of a subject.

False Inferences from True Principles, by Teachers

Third, by erroneous inferences from true principles. This commonly happens to men who are hasty and rash in concluding and deciding what to do — the kind who have a great opinion of their own understanding and believe that matters of this sort require no time or study, only common experience and a good natural wit, which no man thinks himself short of. Yet the knowledge of right and wrong, which is no less difficult, is something no one would claim without great and long study.

Of these defects in reasoning, none can excuse a crime (though some may extenuate one) in any man who claims to manage his own private business — much less in those who take on a public office. For they claim to possess the very reason whose absence they would plead as their excuse.

The Passions as Causes of Crime

Of the passions that most often cause crime, one is vainglory — a foolish overrating of one's own worth, as if differences in worth were the effect of one's wit, or riches, or blood, or some other natural quality, rather than depending on the will of those who hold the sovereign authority. From this comes a presumption that the punishments ordained by the laws, and extended generally to all subjects, ought not to be inflicted on them with the same rigor with which they are inflicted on poor, obscure, simple men — the people lumped together under the name of "the vulgar."

Presumption of Riches

So it commonly happens that those who measure their value by the greatness of their wealth venture on crimes, hoping to escape punishment by corrupting public justice, or by buying a pardon with money or other rewards.

And of Friends

And those who have a multitude of powerful relatives, and popular men who have gained a reputation among the multitude, take courage to violate the laws from the hope of overpowering the very authority whose job it is to enforce them.

And of Wisdom

And those who have a great and false opinion of their own wisdom take it upon themselves to criticize the actions and question the authority of those who govern, and to unsettle the laws with their public talk, so that nothing shall count as a crime except what their own designs require to be one. These same men are also prone to all the crimes that consist in craft and in deceiving their neighbors, because they think their schemes are too subtle to be detected.

These, I say, are the effects of a false presumption of their own wisdom. For of those who are the first movers in the disturbance of a commonwealth — which can never happen without a civil war — very few live long enough to see their new designs established. The benefit of their crimes goes to posterity, and to the very people who would least have wished it: which proves they were not as wise as they thought. And those who deceive in the hope of not being noticed usually deceive themselves — the darkness they believe they hide in being nothing but their own blindness. They are no wiser than children who think everything is hidden when they cover their own eyes.

And in general, all vainglorious men (unless they are also timid) are subject to anger, being more prone than others to read contempt into the ordinary liberties of conversation. And there are few crimes that anger cannot produce.
