Hatred, Lust, Ambition, and Covetousness as Causes of Crime

As for the passions of hate, lust, ambition, and covetousness — what crimes they tend to produce is so obvious to every man's experience and understanding that nothing needs to be said of them, except this: they are infirmities so fastened to the nature of man, and of all other living creatures, that their effects cannot be prevented except by an extraordinary use of reason, or a constant severity in punishing them.

In the things men hate, they find a continual and unavoidable irritation; so either a man's patience must be endless, or he must relieve himself by removing the power of whatever torments him. The first is difficult; the second is often impossible without some violation of the law. Ambition and covetousness are passions that press on a man perpetually, while reason is not perpetually present to resist them — so whenever the hope of impunity appears, their effects follow. And as for lust, what it lacks in duration it makes up in vehemence, which is enough to outweigh the apprehension of any mild or uncertain punishment.

Fear Is Sometimes a Cause of Crime, When the Danger Is Neither Present Nor Bodily

Of all the passions, the one that least inclines men to break the laws is fear. Indeed — setting aside some generous natures — fear is the only thing that makes men keep the laws when breaking them promises profit or pleasure. And yet in many cases a crime may be committed through fear.

For not every fear justifies the action it produces. Only the fear of bodily hurt — what we call bodily fear — and only when a man can see no way to be delivered from it except by the action. A man is attacked, fears immediate death, and sees no escape except by wounding his attacker: if he wounds him to death, this is no crime. For no man, at the making of a commonwealth, is supposed to have abandoned the defense of his life or limbs where the law cannot arrive in time to help him.

But to kill a man because I conclude from his actions or his threats that he will kill me when he can — when I have the time and means to demand protection from the sovereign power — is a crime.

Again: a man receives insulting words, or some petty injuries (the kind for which the lawmakers assigned no punishment, and which they thought no man with the use of reason should even take notice of), and he is afraid that unless he avenges them he will fall into contempt, and so be exposed to the same injuries from others. To avoid this he breaks the law, protecting himself for the future by the terror of his private revenge. This is a crime. For the hurt is not bodily but imaginary — and (though in this corner of the world a custom begun not many years ago, among young and vain men, has made it feel real) it is so slight that a gallant man, sure of his own courage, cannot be bothered to notice it.

A man may also stand in fear of spirits — through his own superstition, or through giving too much credit to other men who tell him of strange dreams and visions — and so be made to believe these spirits will hurt him for doing or not doing various things, when doing or not doing them is contrary to the laws. What is done or omitted on these grounds is not excused by this fear; it is a crime. For as I showed back in the second chapter, dreams are naturally nothing but the imaginings that remain in sleep after the impressions our senses received while awake; and when men, by some accident, are not sure they have been asleep, dreams seem to be real visions. So the man who presumes to break the law on the strength of his own dream, or another man's, or a pretended vision, or any other fancy about the power of invisible spirits beyond what the commonwealth permits, is abandoning the law of nature — which is a certain offense — to follow the imagery of his own or some other private man's brain. He can never know whether it means anything or nothing, nor whether the man who reports his dream is telling the truth or lying. And if every private man were given leave to do this (as they all must be, by the law of nature, if any one of them is), no law could be made to hold, and every commonwealth would dissolve.

Crimes Are Not Equal

From these different sources of crime, it is already clear that not all crimes are of the same weight, as the Stoics of old maintained. There is room not only for EXCUSE, by which what seemed a crime is proved to be none at all, but also for EXTENUATION, by which a crime that seemed great is made less. For though all crimes equally deserve the name of injustice — just as every deviation from a straight line is equally crookedness, which the Stoics rightly observed — it does not follow that all crimes are equally unjust, any more than that all crooked lines are equally crooked. Failing to see this, the Stoics held it as great a crime to kill a hen against the law as to kill one's father.

Total Excuses

The only thing that totally excuses an act, and strips it of the nature of a crime, is whatever at the same time takes away the obligation of the law. For once an act is committed against the law, if the one who committed it is bound by that law, it can be nothing other than a crime.

The lack of means to know the law totally excuses: a law a man has no means to inform himself of is not binding on him. But lack of diligence in inquiring shall not count as lack of means. Nor shall any man who claims reason enough to manage his own affairs be supposed to lack the means to know the laws of nature, because they are known by the very reason he claims to have. Only children and madmen are excused from offenses against the natural law.

Where a man is a captive, or in the power of the enemy — and he is in the power of the enemy when his person or his means of living is — and this is through no fault of his own, the obligation of the law ceases. He must obey the enemy or die, and so such obedience is no crime. For no man is obliged, when the protection of the law fails, not to protect himself by the best means he can.

If a man is compelled by the terror of immediate death to do an act against the law, he is totally excused, because no law can oblige a man to abandon his own preservation. And even supposing such a law were binding, a man would reason like this: "If I do not do it, I die now; if I do it, I die later; so by doing it, I gain time of life." Nature itself compels him to the act.

When a man lacks food or some other thing necessary for his life, and cannot preserve himself in any way except by some act against the law — as when, in a great famine, he takes by force or stealth the food he cannot obtain for money or by charity, or when, in defense of his life, he snatches away another man's sword — he is totally excused, for the reason just given.

Excuses Against the Author

Again, acts done against the law by the authority of another are excused, by that authority, as against the author — for no man ought to accuse, in another who is merely his instrument, an act that is his own. But the act is not thereby excused against any third person injured by it; for in the violation of the law, both the author and the actor are criminals.

From this it follows that when the man or assembly that holds the sovereign power commands a man to do something contrary to a former law, the doing of it is totally excused. The sovereign ought not condemn it, since he is its author; and what cannot justly be condemned by the sovereign cannot justly be punished by anyone else. Besides, when the sovereign commands something to be done against his own former law, the command, as to that particular act, is an abrogation of the law.

If the man or assembly holding the sovereign power disclaims some right essential to sovereignty, and a subject thereby acquires some liberty inconsistent with the sovereign power — that is, with the very existence of a commonwealth — then if the subject refuses to obey a command contrary to that granted liberty, it is nevertheless a sin, and contrary to the duty of a subject. He ought to take notice of what is inconsistent with sovereignty, because sovereignty was erected by his own consent and for his own defense; and he ought to recognize that such a liberty was granted only through ignorance of its evil consequences. But if he not only disobeys, but also resists a public official in the execution of the command, then it is a crime — because he could have had his wrong righted, without any breach of the peace, by complaint.

The degrees of crime are measured on several scales: first, by the malignity of the source or cause; second, by the contagion of the example; third, by the mischief of the effect; and fourth, by the circumstances of times, places, and persons.

Presumption of Power Aggravates

The same act against the law is a greater crime if it proceeds from a presumption of strength, riches, or friends to resist those who must enforce the law, than if it proceeds from a hope of not being discovered, or of escaping by flight. For presuming on impunity by force is a root from which springs, at all times and under every temptation, a contempt of all laws; whereas in the latter case, the apprehension of danger that makes a man flee makes him more obedient for the future. A crime we know to be a crime is greater than the same crime committed under a false belief that it is lawful. For he who commits it against his own conscience is presuming on his force or other power, which encourages him to do it again; but he who does it in error, once the error is shown to him, conforms to the law.

Evil Teachers Extenuate

He whose error proceeds from the authority of a teacher, or of a publicly authorized interpreter of the law, is not as much at fault as he whose error proceeds from stubbornly following his own principles and reasoning. For what is taught by one who teaches by public authority, the commonwealth teaches; and it carries a resemblance of law until the same authority overrules it. In all crimes that do not contain a denial of the sovereign power, and are not against an evident law, this excuses totally. But he who grounds his actions on his own private judgment ought to stand or fall according to whether that judgment was right or wrong.

Examples of Impunity Extenuate

The same act is a greater crime if it has been consistently punished in other men than if there have been many prior examples of impunity. For those examples are so many hopes of impunity given by the sovereign himself; and since he who supplies a man with such a hope and presumption of mercy — encouraging him to offend — has his own part in the offense, he cannot reasonably charge the offender with the whole of it.

Premeditation Aggravates

A crime arising from a sudden passion is not as great as the same crime arising from long deliberation. In the first case there is room for extenuation in the common infirmity of human nature. But he who acts with premeditation has looked around carefully; he has cast his eye on the law, on the punishment, and on the consequences for human society — and in committing the crime, he has held all of these in contempt and ranked them below his own appetite. Yet no suddenness of passion is enough for a total excuse. For all the time between first knowing the law and committing the act shall be counted as time for deliberation, because a man ought, by meditating on the law, to correct the irregularity of his passions.

Where the law is publicly and diligently read and explained before all the people, an act against it is a greater crime than where men are left without such instruction — left to inquire after the law with difficulty, uncertainty, and interruption of their work, and to be informed by private men. In the latter case, part of the fault falls on common infirmity; but in the former there is plain negligence, which carries some contempt of the sovereign power.

Tacit Approval by the Sovereign Extenuates

Acts that the law expressly condemns, but that the lawmaker by other clear signs of his will tacitly approves, are lesser crimes than the same acts condemned both by the law and by the lawmaker. For since the will of the lawmaker is a law, in this case two contradictory laws appear — which would excuse totally, if men were bound to take notice of the sovereign's approval by any signs other than what his commands express. But because there are punishments attached not only to breaking his law, but also to keeping it, he is in part a cause of the transgression, and so cannot reasonably charge the whole crime to the offender.

Take an example. The law condemns duels, and makes the punishment capital. Yet on the other side, the man who refuses a duel is exposed to contempt and scorn, with no remedy — and is sometimes judged by the sovereign himself unworthy of any command or promotion in war. If, on that account, he accepts the duel — considering that all men lawfully try to win the good opinion of those who hold the sovereign power — he ought not in reason to be punished rigorously, since part of the fault may be laid on the punisher. I say this not because I wish for liberty of private revenge, or any other kind of disobedience, but to urge a care in governors not to wink at anything sideways which they directly forbid. The examples princes set, for those who watch them, are and always have been more powerful in governing men's actions than the laws themselves. And though it is our duty to do not what they do but what they say, that duty will never be performed until it pleases God to give men an extraordinary and supernatural grace to follow that precept.

Comparing Crimes by Their Effects

Again, if we compare crimes by the mischief of their effects: first, the same act is greater when its damage reaches many than when it hurts only a few. And so, when an act hurts not only in the present but also — by example — in the future, it is a greater crime than one that hurts only in the present. The former is a fertile crime, multiplying to the hurt of many; the latter is barren.

To maintain doctrines contrary to the religion established in the commonwealth is a greater fault in an authorized preacher than in a private person. So is living profanely or unchastely, or doing any irreligious act whatsoever. Likewise, for a professor of the law to maintain any point, or do any act, that tends to weaken the sovereign power is a greater crime than in another man. So too with a man whose reputation for wisdom is such that his counsels are followed and his actions imitated by many: his act against the law is a greater crime than the same act in another. For such men do not merely commit a crime — they teach it as law to everyone else. And in general, all crimes are made greater by the scandal they give: by becoming stumbling blocks to the weak, who look not so much at the road they walk as at the light that other men carry before them.

Laesae Majestas

Also, acts of hostility against the present state of the commonwealth are greater crimes than the same acts done to private men, for the damage extends to everyone. Such are betraying the strongholds of the commonwealth, or revealing its secrets to an enemy; all attempts on the representative of the commonwealth, whether a monarch or an assembly; and all endeavors, by word or deed, to diminish its authority, either now or in the succession. The Latins call these crimes crimina laesae majestatis — crimes of injured majesty — and they consist in a design or act contrary to a fundamental law.

Bribery and False Testimony

Likewise, crimes that make judgments worthless are greater crimes than injuries done to one or a few persons. To take money to give false judgment, or false testimony, is a greater crime than to cheat a man out of the same sum, or even a greater one, by other means. For not only is the man wronged who falls by such judgments, but all judgments are made useless, and the door is opened to force and private revenge.

Embezzlement

Also, robbery and embezzlement of the public treasury or revenues is a greater crime than robbing or defrauding a private man, because to rob the public is to rob many at once.

Counterfeiting Authority

So too the counterfeit usurpation of public office, and the counterfeiting of public seals or public coin, are greater crimes than counterfeiting a private man's person or his seal, because the fraud spreads its damage to many.

Crimes Against Private Men, Compared

Of acts against the law done to private men, the greater crime is the one whose damage, in the common opinion of mankind, is felt most keenly. And therefore:

To kill against the law is a greater crime than any other injury that leaves life preserved.

To kill with torment is greater than simply to kill.

Mutilation of a limb is greater than robbing a man of his goods.

Robbing a man of his goods by terror of death or wounds is greater than by secret theft.

Secret theft is greater than taking by consent fraudulently obtained.

Violation of chastity by force is greater than by seduction.

And of a married woman, greater than of an unmarried one.

For all these things are commonly valued this way — though some men feel a given offense more, and some less. But the law looks not to the particular person, but to the general inclination of mankind.

That is why the offense men take at insults — in words or gestures — when they produce no harm beyond the momentary grief of the man insulted, has been ignored in the laws of the Greeks, the Romans, and other commonwealths both ancient and modern. They supposed the true cause of such grief to lie not in the insult (which gets no grip on men conscious of their own virtue) but in the small-mindedness of the man who is offended by it.

A crime against a private man is also much aggravated by the person, the time, and the place. To kill one's parent is a greater crime than to kill anyone else, for the parent ought to have the honor of a sovereign — though he has surrendered his power to the civil law — because he held it originally by nature. And to rob a poor man is a greater crime than to rob a rich man, because to the poor man it is a damage felt far more keenly.

And a crime committed at a time or place appointed for worship is greater than the same crime committed at another time or place, for it proceeds from a greater contempt of the law.

Many other cases of aggravation and extenuation could be added; but from those I have set down, it is easy for anyone to take the measure of any other crime proposed.

What Public Crimes Are

Lastly: because in almost all crimes an injury is done not only to some private man but also to the commonwealth, the same crime is called a PUBLIC CRIME when the accusation is brought in the name of the commonwealth, and a private crime when brought in the name of a private man. The pleas are named accordingly: public pleas — judicia publica, or pleas of the Crown — and private pleas. So in an accusation of murder, if the accuser is a private man, the plea is a private plea; if the accuser is the sovereign, the plea is a public plea.

Chapter 28: Punishments and Rewards

The Definition of Punishment

A PUNISHMENT is an evil inflicted by public authority on a person who has done — or failed to do — something that the same authority has judged to be a violation of the law, for this purpose: that the wills of men may thereby be better disposed to obedience.

Where the Right to Punish Comes From

Before I draw anything out of this definition, there is a question of great importance that has to be answered first: by what door did the right, or authority, to punish in any case come in?

Here is the puzzle. From everything said before, no man can be supposed bound by covenant not to resist violence. It follows that no man can be understood to have given anyone else a right to lay violent hands on his person. In the making of a commonwealth, every man gives away the right of defending others — but not the right of defending himself. He also obligates himself to assist the one who holds the sovereignty in punishing others — but not in punishing himself. And covenanting to assist the sovereign in hurting another person does not give the sovereign a right to punish, unless the one making the covenant had a right to do that hurt himself. So it is plain: the right which the commonwealth — that is, the man or assembly that represents it — has to punish is not grounded on any concession or gift from the subjects.

But I have also shown earlier that before the institution of the commonwealth, every man had a right to everything, and a right to do whatever he thought necessary for his own preservation — subduing, hurting, or killing any man to that end. And this is the foundation of the right of punishing that is exercised in every commonwealth. The subjects did not give the sovereign that right. Rather, by laying down their own rights, they strengthened him to use his — as he should think fit — for the preservation of them all. The right was not given to him; it was left to him, and left to him alone. And except for the limits set on him by natural law, it remains as entire as it was in the condition of mere nature, in the war of every man against his neighbor.

Private Injuries and Revenges Are Not Punishments

From the definition of punishment, I draw the following conclusions. First: neither private revenge nor injuries done by private men can properly be called punishment, because they do not proceed from public authority.

Nor Is Being Passed Over for Favor

Second: to be neglected and passed over by public favor is not a punishment, because no new evil is thereby inflicted on anyone. The man is simply left in the condition he was in before.

Nor Pain Inflicted Without a Public Hearing

Third: an evil inflicted by public authority without a prior public condemnation is not to be called punishment, but a hostile act — because the deed for which a man is punished must first be judged, by public authority, to be a violation of the law.

Nor Pain Inflicted by a Usurped Power

Fourth: an evil inflicted by usurped power, or by judges without authority from the sovereign, is not punishment but an act of hostility. The acts of a usurped power do not have the condemned person for their author, and therefore they are not acts of public authority.

Nor Pain Inflicted Without Regard to Future Good

Fifth: any evil inflicted without the intention — or even the possibility — of disposing the offender, or other men by his example, to obey the laws, is not punishment but an act of hostility. Without that end in view, no hurt done to anyone deserves the name. Punishment exists only for the correction of the offender or the guidance of others — never for revenge.

Natural Bad Consequences Are Not Punishments

Sixth: nature itself attaches harmful consequences to certain actions — as when a man assaulting another is himself killed or wounded, or when he falls sick from doing some unlawful act. With respect to God, who is the author of nature, such hurt may be said to be inflicted, and so may be called a divine punishment. But it is not contained under the name of punishment with respect to men, because it is not inflicted by the authority of man.

Hurt Less Than the Benefit of the Crime Is Not Punishment

Seventh: if the harm inflicted is less than the benefit or satisfaction that naturally follows from the crime committed, that harm does not fall within the definition. It is the price, or the purchase, of a crime rather than its punishment. Here is why: it is of the nature of punishment to have for its end the disposing of men to obey the law. If the penalty is less than the benefit of breaking the law, it does not achieve that end — it works the opposite effect. The man simply pays for his crime and counts it a bargain.

Where the Penalty Is Fixed by Law, Anything Beyond It Is Not Punishment but Hostility

Eighth: if a punishment is determined and prescribed in the law itself, and after the crime is committed a greater punishment is inflicted, the excess is not punishment but an act of hostility. For the aim of punishment is not revenge, but terror — and the terror of an unknown great punishment is taken away the moment a lesser one is declared. The unexpected addition is no part of the punishment.

But where the law fixes no punishment at all, whatever is inflicted has the nature of punishment. A man who sets about violating a law in which no penalty is specified expects an indeterminate — that is to say, an arbitrary — punishment.

Hurt Inflicted for a Deed Done Before the Law Existed Is Not Punishment

Ninth: harm inflicted for a deed done before there was any law forbidding it is not punishment, but an act of hostility. Before the law, there is no violation of the law. Punishment presupposes a deed judged to have been a violation of the law. Therefore harm inflicted for a deed done before the law was made is not punishment, but an act of hostility.

The Representative of the Commonwealth Cannot Be Punished

Tenth: hurt inflicted on the representative of the commonwealth is not punishment, but an act of hostility. It is of the nature of punishment to be inflicted by public authority — and public authority is the authority of the representative alone.

Hurt Done to Rebels Is Done by Right of War, Not by Way of Punishment

Lastly: harm inflicted on a declared enemy does not fall under the name of punishment at all. Consider why. Enemies either were never subject to the law, and therefore cannot violate it; or, having been subject to it, they profess to be subject no longer, and by consequence deny that they can violate it. So all the harms that can be done to them must be taken as acts of hostility — and in declared hostility, every infliction of evil is lawful.

From this it follows that if a subject deliberately and knowingly denies, by deed or word, the authority of the representative of the commonwealth, he may lawfully be made to suffer whatever the representative wills — whatever penalty may have been previously laid down for treason. In denying his subjection, he denies the punishment the law has ordained, and therefore he suffers as an enemy of the commonwealth: that is, according to the will of the representative. The punishments set down in the law are for subjects, not for enemies — and enemies are exactly what such men are, who, having been subjects by their own act, deliberately revolt and deny the sovereign power.

The first and most general division of punishments is into divine and human. Of divine punishment I shall have occasion to speak in a more convenient place later on.

Human punishments are those inflicted by the command of man, and they are either corporal, or pecuniary, or ignominy, or imprisonment, or exile, or a mixture of these.

Corporal Punishments

Corporal punishment is that which is inflicted on the body directly, according to the intention of the one inflicting it: lashes, or wounds, or the deprivation of bodily pleasures that were lawfully enjoyed before.

Capital Punishment

Of these, some are capital, some less than capital. Capital punishment is the infliction of death — either simply, or with torment. Less than capital are lashes, wounds, chains, and any other bodily pain not in its own nature deadly. For if death follows a punishment but was not in the intention of the one inflicting it, the punishment is not to be counted capital, even though the harm proves fatal through some accident that could not be foreseen. In that case death is not inflicted, but hastened.

Pecuniary punishment consists not only in taking away a sum of money, but also lands or any other goods that are usually bought and sold for money. And here a distinction matters. If the law that ordains such a penalty was made with the design of collecting money from those who break it, then it is not properly a punishment at all, but the price of a privilege — an exemption from the law, which does not absolutely forbid the act, but forbids it only to those who cannot afford to pay. The exception is where the law in question is a law of nature, or part of religion; in that case the payment is not an exemption from the law but a violation of it. Where a law exacts a monetary fine from those who take the name of God in vain, paying the fine is not the price of a license to swear — it is the punishment for breaking a law that admits no dispensation. Likewise, if the law requires a sum of money to be paid to the person who was injured, this is merely compensation for the hurt done to him. It cancels the accusation of the injured party, not the crime of the offender.

Ignominy

Ignominy is the infliction of some evil the commonwealth has made dishonorable, or the deprivation of some good the commonwealth has made honorable. Some things are honorable by nature — the effects of courage, magnanimity, strength, wisdom, and other abilities of body and mind. Other things are made honorable by the commonwealth — badges, titles, offices, or any other distinctive mark of the sovereign's favor. The first kind, though they may fail through nature or accident, cannot be taken away by a law, so the loss of them is not punishment. But the second kind can be taken away by the same public authority that made them honorable, and stripping them is properly punishment: degrading condemned men of their badges, titles, and offices, or declaring them incapable of holding the like in the future.

Imprisonment

Imprisonment is when a man is deprived of liberty by public authority, and it can happen for two different ends. One is the safe custody of a man who stands accused; the other is the inflicting of pain on a man who stands condemned.

The first is not punishment, because no man is supposed to be punished before he has been judicially heard and declared guilty. Therefore whatever hurt a man is made to suffer by bonds or restraint before his case is heard — over and above what is necessary to assure his custody — is against the law of nature. But the second is punishment, because it is an evil inflicted by public authority for something the same authority has judged a violation of the law.

Under the word imprisonment I include every restraint of motion caused by an external obstacle — whether it is a building, which goes by the general name of a prison; or an island, as when men are said to be confined to one; or a place where men are set to forced labor, as in ancient times men were condemned to the quarries and in our times to the galleys; or a chain, or any other such impediment.

Exile

Exile — banishment — is when a man is condemned for a crime to depart from the dominion of the commonwealth, or from a certain part of it, and not to return, either for a fixed time or forever. In its own nature, without other circumstances added, it does not seem to be a punishment at all, but rather an escape — a public command to avoid punishment by running away. Cicero says no such punishment was ever ordained in the city of Rome; he calls exile a refuge for men in danger. For if a banished man is still permitted to enjoy his goods and the income from his lands, the mere change of air is no punishment. Nor does it serve the benefit of the commonwealth for which all punishments are ordained — that is, the forming of men's wills toward observing the law. Often it works to the commonwealth's damage. A banished man is a lawful enemy of the commonwealth that banished him, being no longer a member of it. But if he is also stripped of his lands or goods, then the punishment lies not in the exile, but is to be counted among pecuniary punishments.

Punishing Innocent Subjects Is Against the Law of Nature

All punishments of innocent subjects, whether great or small, are against the law of nature. Punishment is only for violation of the law; therefore there can be no punishment of the innocent.

To punish an innocent subject is a violation, first, of that law of nature which forbids all men, in their revenges, to look at anything but some future good — for no good can come to the commonwealth by punishing the innocent. Second, of the law that forbids ingratitude: since all sovereign power is originally given by the consent of every one of the subjects so that they may be protected as long as they are obedient, the punishment of the innocent is a returning of evil for good. And third, of the law that commands equity — that is, the equal distribution of justice — which is plainly not observed in punishing the innocent.

But Harm Done to Innocents in War Is Another Matter

But inflicting any evil whatsoever on an innocent man who is not a subject — if it is done for the benefit of the commonwealth and without violating any previous covenant — is no breach of the law of nature. All men who are not subjects are either enemies, or else they have ceased to be enemies through some prior covenant. Against enemies whom the commonwealth judges capable of doing it harm, it is lawful by the original right of nature to make war. And in war the sword does not judge; the victor makes no distinction between guilty and innocent as to what is past, and shows no other mercy than what serves the good of his own people.

On this ground, vengeance against subjects who deliberately deny the authority of the established commonwealth is lawfully extended not only to the fathers, but to the third and fourth generation not yet in being — and consequently innocent of the deed for which they are afflicted. The nature of this offense consists in the renouncing of subjection, which is a relapse into the condition of war, commonly called rebellion. Those who offend in this way suffer not as subjects, but as enemies. For rebellion is simply war renewed.

Reward Is Either Salary or Grace

REWARD is either by gift or by contract. When by contract, it is called salary or wages — benefit owed for service performed or promised. When by gift, it is benefit proceeding from the grace of those who bestow it, to encourage men to serve them, or to enable men to do so.

So when the sovereign of a commonwealth attaches a salary to a public office, the man who receives it is bound in justice to perform that office. Otherwise — when there is no salary — he is bound only in honor, to acknowledgment and an effort to repay the favor. It is true that men have no lawful remedy when they are commanded to abandon their private business and serve the public without reward or salary. But they are not bound to such service by the law of nature, nor by the institution of the commonwealth, unless the service cannot be done any other way — because it is presumed the sovereign may make use of all their means. So much is this so that the most common soldier may demand the wages of his service in war as a debt.

Benefits Bestowed Out of Fear Are Not Rewards

The benefits a sovereign bestows on a subject out of fear of some power and ability that subject has to hurt the commonwealth are not properly rewards. They are not salaries, because in this case no contract is supposed — every man is already obliged not to do the commonwealth disservice. Nor are they graces, because they are extorted by fear, which ought never to touch the sovereign power. They are rather sacrifices, which the sovereign — considered in his natural person, not in the person of the commonwealth — makes to appease the discontent of someone he thinks more powerful than himself. And they encourage not obedience, but the opposite: the continuation and escalation of further extortion.

Salaries Fixed and Variable

Some salaries are fixed and proceed from the public treasury; others are uncertain and variable, proceeding from the execution of the office for which the salary is established. The second kind is in some cases harmful to the commonwealth — as in the administration of justice. For where the income of judges and the officers of a court arises from the multitude of cases brought before them, two inconveniences must follow. One is the nourishing of lawsuits, for the more suits, the greater the income. The other, which depends on the first, is contention over jurisdiction — each court drawing to itself as many cases as it can. In offices of execution these inconveniences do not arise, because such officers cannot increase their own employment by any effort of their own.

And this shall suffice for the nature of punishment and reward — which are, as it were, the nerves and tendons that move the limbs and joints of a commonwealth.

So far I have set forth the nature of man, whose pride and other passions have compelled him to submit himself to government, together with the great power of his governor — whom I compared to the Leviathan, taking that comparison from the last two verses of the forty-first chapter of Job, where God, having set forth the great power of the Leviathan, calls him King of the Proud. "There is nothing," says he, "on earth to be compared with him. He is made so as not to be afraid. He sees every high thing below him, and is king of all the children of pride." (Job 41:33-34)

But because he is mortal and subject to decay, as all other earthly creatures are; and because there is something in heaven — though not on earth — that he must stand in fear of, and whose laws he ought to obey: I shall in the next chapters speak of his diseases and the causes of his mortality, and of what laws of nature he is bound to obey.

Chapter 29: The Things That Weaken a Commonwealth, or Tend to Dissolve It

The Dissolution of Commonwealths Comes from Defective Founding

Nothing that mortals make can be immortal. Still, if men actually had the use of the reason they claim to have, their commonwealths could at least be secured against dying of internal diseases — those seeds of natural mortality I spoke of before. By the nature of their founding, they are designed to live as long as mankind itself, or as long as the laws of nature, or as long as justice itself — the thing that gives them life. So when a commonwealth comes apart, not through outside violence but through internal disorder, the fault does not lie in men as the material; it lies in men as the makers and arrangers of it.

Here is what happens. Men eventually grow weary of the irregular jostling and hacking at one another, and they desire with all their hearts to fit themselves together into one firm and lasting building. But they lack two things: the art of making good laws to square their actions by, and the humility and patience to let the rough, awkward edges of their present greatness be chiseled off. So without the help of a very able architect, they cannot be assembled into anything but a rickety structure — one that barely lasts out their own lifetime, and is certain to fall on the heads of their children.

Among the infirmities of a commonwealth, then, I will count first those that arise from a defective founding. These resemble the diseases of a natural body that come from defective procreation.

Lack of Absolute Power

Here is one such defect: a man, in order to obtain a kingdom, sometimes settles for less power than the peace and defense of the commonwealth actually require. The result is this. When the power he set aside has to be picked up again for the public safety, taking it back looks like an unjust act — and that appearance disposes great numbers of men, whenever the occasion presents itself, to rebel. It is exactly like the bodies of children begotten by diseased parents: they are subject either to early death, or to purging the bad quality inherited from their flawed conception by breaking out in boils and scabs.

And when kings deny themselves some necessary power like this, it is not always out of ignorance of what their office requires — though sometimes it is. Often it is out of a hope of taking the power back whenever they please. In this they reason badly. Because the people who will hold them to their promises will be propped up against them by foreign commonwealths, which, for the good of their own subjects, let slip few chances to weaken their neighbors. That is how Thomas Becket, Archbishop of Canterbury, was supported against Henry the Second by the Pope — the subjection of churchmen to the commonwealth having been waived by William the Conqueror at his coronation, when he swore an oath not to infringe the liberty of the Church. And that is how the barons — whose power William Rufus had inflated, to win their help in shifting the succession from his elder brother to himself, to a degree incompatible with sovereign power — were backed by the French in their rebellion against King John.

Nor does this happen only in monarchy. The official style of the ancient Roman commonwealth was "The Senate and People of Rome" — and neither senate nor people claimed the whole power. This first produced the seditions of Tiberius Gracchus, Caius Gracchus, Lucius Saturninus, and others; and afterward the wars between the senate and the people under Marius and Sulla, and again under Pompey and Caesar — ending in the extinction of their democracy and the setting up of monarchy.

The people of Athens bound themselves on only one single point: that no man, on pain of death, should propose renewing the war for the island of Salamis. And yet because of that one restriction, they would have had an enemy permanently stationed at the very gates of their city — if Solon had not spread the word that he was mad, and then, in the gestures and costume of a madman, proposed the war in verse to the people who crowded around him. Such is the damage, and such are the tricks, that every commonwealth is forced into when its power is limited even a little.

Private Judgment of Good and Evil

In the second place, I observe the diseases of a commonwealth that come from the poison of seditious doctrines. One of these is: "Every private man is the judge of good and evil actions."

This is true in the state of nature, where there are no civil laws. It is also true under civil government, in cases the law has not settled. But otherwise it is plain that the measure of good and evil actions is the civil law, and the judge is the legislator — who is always the representative of the commonwealth. From this false doctrine, men are inclined to debate with themselves and dispute the commands of the commonwealth, and then to obey or disobey them as their private judgments see fit. And by that, the commonwealth is distracted and weakened.

The Erroneous Conscience

Another doctrine repugnant to civil society is: "Whatever a man does against his conscience is sin." It depends on the same presumption — making oneself the judge of good and evil. For a man's conscience and his judgment are the same thing, and just as the judgment can be wrong, so can the conscience.

So yes: a man subject to no civil law sins in everything he does against his conscience, because he has no other rule to follow but his own reason. But it is not so for the man who lives in a commonwealth. There, the law is the public conscience — the one he has already agreed to be guided by. Otherwise, in such diversity as there is of private consciences — which are nothing but private opinions; the most their owners actually know is that they think so — the commonwealth would inevitably be torn apart, and no man would dare obey the sovereign power any further than it seemed good in his own eyes.

The Pretense of Inspiration

It has also commonly been taught that "faith and holiness are not attained by study and reason, but by supernatural inspiration, or infusion." Grant that, and I do not see why any man should give a reason for his faith; or why every Christian should not also be a prophet; or why any man should take the law of his country, rather than his own inspiration, as the rule of his conduct. And so we fall back into the same fault: taking it on ourselves to judge of good and evil — or making judges of it out of those private men who claim to be supernaturally inspired — to the dissolution of all civil government.

Faith comes by hearing, and hearing comes by those circumstances that bring us into the presence of the people who speak to us. Those circumstances are all arranged by God Almighty — and yet they are not supernatural; it is only that the number of them working together in every effect is too great to observe. Faith and holiness are, indeed, not very common; but they are not miracles. They are brought about by education, discipline, correction, and other natural means, by which God works them in his chosen, at whatever time he thinks fit.

And these three opinions, so destructive to peace and government, have in this part of the world come chiefly from the tongues and pens of unlearned theologians, who, stringing together the words of Holy Scripture in ways that do not agree with reason, do all they can to make men think that holiness and natural reason cannot stand together.

Making the Sovereign Subject to the Civil Laws

A fourth opinion repugnant to the nature of a commonwealth is this: "The holder of the sovereign power is subject to the civil laws."

It is true that sovereigns are all subject to the laws of nature, because those laws are divine and cannot be repealed by any man or any commonwealth. But to the laws that the sovereign himself makes — that is, that the commonwealth makes — he is not subject. To be subject to the laws is to be subject to the commonwealth, that is, to the sovereign representative, that is, to himself; and that is not subjection, but freedom from the laws. And here is why the error matters: because it sets the laws above the sovereign, it also sets a judge above him, and a power to punish him — which is to make a new sovereign. And then, by the same reasoning, a third sovereign to punish the second; and so on forever, to the confusion and dissolution of the commonwealth.

Giving Subjects Absolute Property

A fifth doctrine that tends to dissolve a commonwealth is: "Every private man has an absolute property in his goods — a property that excludes even the right of the sovereign."

Every man does indeed have a property that excludes the right of every other subject. But he has it only from the sovereign power; without that power's protection, every other man would have an equal right to the same things. If the right of the sovereign is also shut out, then he cannot perform the office they put him into — which is to defend them both from foreign enemies and from each other's injuries — and consequently there is no longer a commonwealth.

And if the property of subjects does not exclude the sovereign representative's right to their goods, much less does it exclude his right to their offices of judging and enforcement, in which they represent the sovereign himself.

Dividing the Sovereign Power

There is a sixth doctrine, plainly and directly against the very essence of a commonwealth, and it is this: "The sovereign power may be divided." For what is dividing the power of a commonwealth, other than dissolving it? Powers divided destroy each other. As I showed before, these rights are incommunicable and inseparable. And for these doctrines men are chiefly indebted to certain professors of the law, who labor to make the laws depend on their own learning rather than on the legislative power.

Imitating Neighboring Nations

Along with false doctrine, the example of a different form of government in a neighboring nation often disposes men to alter the form already established. That is how the people of the Jews were stirred up to reject God and demand that the prophet Samuel give them a king "after the manner of the nations." That is how the lesser cities of Greece were continually convulsed by the seditions of aristocratic and democratic factions — one party in almost every commonwealth wanting to imitate the Spartans, the other the Athenians. And I have no doubt that many men were content to watch the recent troubles in England out of imitation of the Low Countries — supposing that all it took to grow rich was to change, as the Dutch had done, the form of their government.

For man's nature is, of itself, prone to crave novelty. So when men are provoked toward it by the nearness of neighbors who have grown rich by it, it is almost impossible for them not to embrace the agitators who urge them to change. They love the first beginnings of the disorder even while they are tormented by its continuance — like hot-blooded men who, having caught the itch, tear at themselves with their own nails until they can no longer stand the pain.

Imitating the Greeks and Romans

As for rebellion against monarchy in particular, one of its most frequent causes is the reading of the political books and histories of the ancient Greeks and Romans. From these books, young men — and everyone else who lacks the antidote of solid reason — receive a strong and delightful impression of the great exploits of war achieved by the commanders of those armies, and along with it a pleasing notion of everything else those nations did. They imagine that all that prosperity came not from the rivalry of particular men, but from the virtue of their popular form of government — never considering the constant seditions and civil wars produced by the defects of that very system.

From the reading of such books, I say, men have taken it upon themselves to kill their kings — because the Greek and Latin writers, in their books and discourses on politics, make it lawful and even praiseworthy for any man to do so, provided that before he does it, he first calls his king a tyrant. For they do not say that regicide — the killing of a king — is lawful; they say that tyrannicide — the killing of a tyrant — is lawful. (And a tyrant, remember, is nothing but a monarch disliked.) From the same books, those who live under a monarch form the opinion that subjects in a popular commonwealth enjoy liberty, while in a monarchy they are all slaves. Notice: I say those who live under a monarchy form that opinion — not those who actually live under popular government, for they find no such thing.

In sum, I cannot imagine anything more damaging to a monarchy than allowing such books to be publicly read without immediately applying the correctives of careful teachers, fit to draw out their venom. And that venom I will not hesitate to compare to the bite of a mad dog — a disease the physicians call hydrophobia, or fear of water. The man so bitten suffers a continual torment of thirst, and yet he recoils from water, as if the poison were laboring to turn him into a dog. In just the same way, when a monarchy has once been bitten to the quick by those democratic writers who never stop snarling at that form of government, what it needs most is a strong monarch — and yet, out of a kind of tyrannophobia, a fear of being strongly governed, the people abhor him when they have him.

Just as there have been doctors who hold that there are three souls in a man, so there are also those who think there can be more souls — that is, more sovereigns — than one in a commonwealth. They set up a "supremacy" against the sovereignty; canons against laws; a "ghostly" authority against the civil. They work on men's minds with words and distinctions that in themselves signify nothing, but betray by their very obscurity that there walks abroad — invisibly, some think — another kingdom, a kind of kingdom of fairies, in the dark.

Now it is plain that the civil power and the power of the commonwealth are the same thing; and that supremacy, and the power of making canons and granting church offices, implies a commonwealth. It follows that where one man is sovereign and another supreme — where one can make laws and another can make canons — there must be two commonwealths over the very same subjects. That is a kingdom divided against itself, and it cannot stand. For all the empty distinction between "temporal" and "ghostly," they are still two kingdoms, and every subject is subject to two masters. The ghostly power claims the right to declare what is sin, and so by consequence claims to declare what is law — sin being nothing but the transgression of the law. The civil power, meanwhile, claims to declare what is law. So every subject must obey two masters, each insisting that his commands be treated as law — which is impossible. Or, if there is really only one kingdom, then either the civil power, which is the power of the commonwealth, must be subordinate to the ghostly, or the ghostly must be subordinate to the temporal — in which case there is no supremacy but the temporal.

When these two powers oppose each other, then, the commonwealth cannot help but stand in great danger of civil war and dissolution. The civil authority, being more visible, and standing in the clearer light of natural reason, cannot fail to draw to itself, in every age, a very considerable part of the people. And the spiritual authority, though it stands in the darkness of Scholastic distinctions and hard words, can never lack a party big enough to trouble a commonwealth, and sometimes to destroy it — because the fear of darkness and ghosts is greater than other fears.

And this is a disease that may quite fitly be compared to epilepsy, or the falling sickness — which the Jews took to be a kind of possession by spirits — in the natural body. In that disease, an unnatural spirit, or wind, in the head obstructs the roots of the nerves. Shaking them violently, it takes away the motion they should naturally receive from the power of the soul in the brain, and so causes violent and irregular motions in the limbs — what men call convulsions — so that the person seized by it falls down, sometimes into the water and sometimes into the fire, like a man deprived of his senses. So too in the body politic. When the spiritual power moves the members of a commonwealth by the terror of punishments and the hope of rewards — which are its nerves — in ways other than the civil power, which is the soul of the commonwealth, would move them, and suffocates the people with strange and hard words, it either overwhelms the commonwealth with oppression, or throws it into the fire of a civil war.

Mixed Government

Sometimes, even in purely civil government, there is more than one soul. This happens when the power of raising money (which is the nutritive faculty) depends on a general assembly; the power of military conduct and command (which is the motive faculty) depends on one man; and the power of making laws (which is the rational faculty) depends on the chance agreement not only of those two but of a third party as well. This endangers the commonwealth — sometimes for lack of consent to good laws, but most often for lack of the nourishment necessary to its life and motion.

Few people see it, but such a government is not government at all; it is the division of the commonwealth into three factions. Men call it "mixed monarchy," but the truth is that it is not one independent commonwealth but three independent factions — not one representative person, but three. In the kingdom of God there may be three persons who are independent without any breach of unity in the God who reigns; but where men reign — men subject to a diversity of opinions — it cannot be so. So if the king bears the person of the people, and the general assembly also bears the person of the people, and another assembly bears the person of a part of the people, they are not one person and one sovereign, but three persons and three sovereigns.

To what disease in the natural body of man I can exactly compare this deformity of a commonwealth, I do not know. But I once saw a man who had another man growing out of his side, with a head, arms, chest, and stomach of his own. If he had had yet another man growing out of his other side, the comparison would have been exact.

Lack of Money

So far I have named the diseases of a commonwealth that pose the greatest and most immediate danger. There are others, not so great, that are nevertheless worth observing.

First: the difficulty of raising money for the necessary uses of the commonwealth, especially as war approaches. This difficulty arises from the opinion every subject holds that he has a property in his lands and goods that excludes the sovereign's right to the use of them. The result is this. The sovereign power, which foresees the necessities and dangers of the commonwealth — finding the flow of money to the public treasury blocked by the tight grip of the people — ought to extend itself to meet and prevent those dangers at their beginnings. Instead it contracts itself as long as it can; and when it can contract no longer, it wrestles with the people through legal stratagems to obtain little sums, which do not suffice; until at last it is forced to break open the way violently for an immediate supply, or perish. Driven often to these extremities, it finally either brings the people back to their proper temper, or the commonwealth must die.

We may compare this distemper very aptly to an ague — a malarial fever. The fleshy parts grow congealed, or are blocked by venomous matter, so that the veins, which by their natural course empty into the heart, are not supplied from the arteries as they should be. First comes a cold contraction and trembling of the limbs; then a hot, violent effort of the heart to force a passage for the blood. Until it can do that, it makes do with the small refreshment of cooling things, until — if nature is strong enough — it finally breaks the resistance of the obstructed parts and sweats out the venom. If nature is too weak, the patient dies.

Monopolies, and the Abuses of Tax-Farmers

Again, there is sometimes in a commonwealth a disease resembling pleurisy. That is when the treasure of the commonwealth, flowing out of its proper channels, pools in excessive abundance in one or a few private men, through monopolies or through the farming of the public revenues — just as the blood in a pleurisy, getting into the membrane of the chest, breeds an inflammation there, attended by fever and painful stabbing.

Popular Men

The popularity of a powerful subject is also a dangerous disease, unless the commonwealth has very good security for his loyalty. The people, who ought to receive their motion from the authority of the sovereign, are drawn away from their obedience to the laws by the flattery and reputation of an ambitious man, to follow someone of whose virtues and designs they know nothing. This is generally more dangerous in a popular government than in a monarchy, because an army can easily be made to believe that it is the people. That is how Julius Caesar — set up by the people against the senate — having won the affections of his army, made himself master of senate and people alike. And this maneuver of popular and ambitious men is plain rebellion, and may be compared to the effects of witchcraft.

The Excessive Size of a Town; the Multitude of Corporations

Another infirmity of a commonwealth is the immoderate size of a single town, when it can supply, out of its own limits, the manpower and the funding of a great army. So too the great number of corporations, which are like so many lesser commonwealths in the bowels of a greater one — like worms in the entrails of a natural man.

The Liberty of Disputing Against the Sovereign Power

To these we may add the liberty of disputing against absolute power, taken by self-styled experts in political prudence. Bred mostly in the dregs of the people, yet animated by false doctrines, they are perpetually meddling with the fundamental laws, to the harassment of the commonwealth — like the little worms physicians call ascarides.

We may further add the insatiable appetite — the bulimia — of enlarging dominion, with the often incurable wounds taken from the enemy in the process; and the wens — those useless lumps of growth — of conquests never united with the body, which are many times a burden, and lost with less danger than they are kept; and likewise the lethargy of ease, and the consumption brought on by riot and extravagant spending.

The Dissolution of the Commonwealth

Lastly: when, in a war foreign or internal, the enemies win a final victory — so that the forces of the commonwealth no longer keep the field, and there is no further protection for subjects in their loyalty — then the commonwealth is DISSOLVED, and every man is at liberty to protect himself by whatever means his own discretion suggests.

For the sovereign is the public soul, giving life and motion to the commonwealth; and when that soul expires, the members are no more governed by it than the carcass of a man is governed by his departed — though immortal — soul. The right of a sovereign monarch cannot be extinguished by anyone else's act; but the obligation of the members can be. A man who lacks protection may seek it anywhere; and when he has found it, he is obliged — without the fraudulent pretense of having submitted merely out of fear — to protect his protector for as long as he is able. But when the power of a sovereign assembly is once suppressed, its right perishes utterly, because the assembly itself is extinct — and so there is no possibility for that sovereignty to ever re-enter.

Chapter 30: The Office of the Sovereign Representative

Securing the Good of the People

The OFFICE of the sovereign — whether a monarch or an assembly — consists in the end for which he was trusted with sovereign power: namely, securing the safety of the people. To this he is obliged by the law of nature, and he must render an account of it to God, the author of that law, and to nobody else. This is the old maxim salus populi — "the safety of the people" is the supreme law. But by safety I do not mean bare survival. I mean also all the other contentments of life that every man, by lawful industry, and without danger or harm to the commonwealth, can acquire for himself.

By Instruction and Laws

And this is meant to be done not by care applied to individuals — beyond protecting them from injuries when they complain — but by a general providence: by public instruction, in both doctrine and example, and by the making and executing of good laws, which individual persons can then apply to their own cases.

A Sovereign Must Not Give Up Any Essential Right of Sovereignty — Nor Leave the People Untaught About the Grounds of Those Rights

Now, if the essential rights of sovereignty (the ones spelled out back in chapter 18) are taken away, the commonwealth is dissolved by it, and every man returns to the condition and calamity of a war with every other man — which is the greatest evil that can happen in this life. So it is the office of the sovereign to keep those rights entire. And consequently it is against his duty, first, to transfer any of them to someone else, or to lay any of them aside. For he that deserts the means deserts the end. And he deserts the means who, being the sovereign, acknowledges himself subject to the civil laws; or renounces the power of supreme judicature; or the power of making war or peace by his own authority; or of judging what the commonwealth needs; or of levying money and soldiers when, and in whatever amounts, his own conscience judges necessary; or of appointing officers and ministers of both war and peace; or of appointing teachers, and examining which doctrines support — and which undermine — the defense, peace, and good of the people.

Secondly, it is against his duty to let the people remain ignorant or misinformed about the grounds and reasons of those essential rights. Because an ignorant people is easily seduced and drawn into resisting him, just when the commonwealth needs those rights used and exercised.

And the grounds of these rights especially need to be diligently and truly taught, because they cannot be maintained by any civil law or by the terror of legal punishment. A civil law forbidding rebellion (and all resistance to the essential rights of sovereignty is rebellion) does not bind as a civil law at all. It binds only by virtue of the law of nature, which forbids breaking faith — and if men do not know that natural obligation, they cannot know the right of any law the sovereign makes. As for the punishment, they take it for nothing more than an act of hostility — and when they think they have strength enough, they will try to avoid it by acts of hostility of their own.

The Objection That There Are No Principles of Reason for Absolute Sovereignty

I have heard some people say that justice is just a word without substance, and that whatever a man can acquire for himself by force or cunning — not only in the condition of war, but inside a commonwealth too — is his own. I have already shown this to be false. In the same spirit, there are also people who maintain that there are no grounds, no principles of reason, to support those essential rights that make sovereignty absolute. If there were, they argue, somebody somewhere would have discovered them by now — whereas, as far as we can see, there has never yet been a commonwealth where those rights were acknowledged or claimed in full.

They argue exactly as badly as if the indigenous peoples of the Americas were to deny that there are any grounds or principles of reason for building a house that lasts as long as its materials — because they had never yet seen one so well built. Time and industry produce new knowledge every day. The art of building well was derived from principles of reason, observed by industrious men who had long studied the nature of materials and the different effects of shape and proportion — and that happened long after mankind had begun (poorly) to build. In the same way, long after men first began to set up commonwealths — imperfect ones, apt to relapse into disorder — principles of reason may be found out, by industrious meditation, to make their constitutions last (except by external violence) forever. And these are the kind of principles I have set down in this discourse.

Whether the men who today have the governing of mankind notice these principles and make use of them, or neglect them, concerns my own personal interest, at this point, very little. But suppose these principles of mine are not principles of reason after all. I am still sure they are principles backed by the authority of Scripture — as I will make clear when I come to speak of the kingdom of God, administered by Moses over the Jews, his peculiar people by covenant.

The Objection That Ordinary People Cannot Understand Them

But then they say: even if the principles are right, common people lack the capacity to be made to understand them. I would be delighted if the rich and powerful subjects of a kingdom, and those accounted the most learned, were no less incapable than the common people are. Everyone knows that the obstructions to this kind of doctrine come not so much from the difficulty of the matter as from the interests of those who are supposed to learn it. Powerful men can hardly digest anything that sets up a power to bridle their passions; and learned men can hardly digest anything that exposes their errors and thereby lessens their authority. Whereas the minds of common people — unless they are tainted by dependence on the powerful, or scribbled over with the opinions of their teachers — are like clean paper, fit to receive whatever public authority chooses to imprint on them.

Shall whole nations be brought to accept the great mysteries of the Christian religion, which are above reason — shall millions of men be made to believe that the same body can be in countless places at one and the same time, which is against reason — and yet men cannot, by teaching and preaching protected by law, get people to receive a doctrine so consonant with reason that any unprejudiced man needs only to hear it to learn it? I conclude, therefore, that in instructing the people in the essential rights of sovereignty (which are the natural and fundamental laws), there is no difficulty — so long as a sovereign keeps his power entire — except what proceeds from his own fault, or the fault of those he trusts with the administration of the commonwealth. And consequently it is his duty to have the people instructed in them. And not only his duty, but his benefit too, and his security against the danger that rebellion poses to his own natural person.

The People Are to Be Taught Not to Crave a Change of Government

To descend to particulars: the people are to be taught, first, that they ought not to fall in love with any form of government they see in neighboring nations more than with their own — and that whatever present prosperity they see in nations governed differently, they should not desire change. For the prosperity of a people ruled by an aristocratic or democratic assembly does not come from the aristocracy or the democracy, but from the obedience and concord of the subjects. Nor do people flourish in a monarchy because one man has the right to rule them, but because they obey him. Take away the obedience (and with it the concord of the people) in any kind of state, and they will not only fail to flourish — in a short time they will be dissolved.

And those who set out, by disobedience, merely to reform the commonwealth will find that they destroy it instead — like the foolish daughters of Peleus in the fable, who, wanting to renew the youth of their decrepit father, took Medea's advice, cut him in pieces, and boiled him with strange herbs, but did not make a new man of him. This desire for change is like a breach of the first of God's commandments. For there God says, Non habebis deos alienos — thou shalt not have the gods of other nations; and in another place, concerning kings, he says that they are gods.

Nor to Attach Themselves to Popular Men Against the Sovereign

Secondly, they are to be taught that they ought not to be so carried away by admiration for the virtue of any fellow subject — however high he stands, however conspicuously he shines in the commonwealth — nor for any assembly (except the sovereign assembly itself), as to give them any obedience or honor that belongs to the sovereign alone, whom such men, in their particular stations, merely represent. Nor should the people receive any influence from such men except what is conveyed through them from the sovereign authority. For a sovereign cannot be imagined to love his people as he ought if he is not jealous of them, but lets them be seduced from their loyalty by the flattery of popular men — as they have often been, not only secretly but openly, with preachers proclaiming the marriage between the demagogue and the people in facie ecclesiae — before the face of the church, in full public view — and publishing it in the open streets. This may fitly be compared to a violation of the second of the Ten Commandments.

Nor to Dispute the Sovereign Power

Thirdly, and as a consequence of this, they ought to be informed how great a fault it is to speak evil of the sovereign representative (whether one man or an assembly of men), or to argue and dispute his power, or in any way to use his name irreverently — by which he may be brought into contempt with his people, and their obedience (on which the safety of the commonwealth rests) loosened. This is the doctrine the third commandment points to, by resemblance.

Set Days Apart for Learning Their Duty

Fourthly: people cannot be taught all this — nor, once taught, remember it — nor, after one generation has passed, even know in whom the sovereign power is placed — unless certain times are set apart from their ordinary labor, in which they can attend to those appointed to instruct them. So it is necessary that such times be fixed, when they can assemble together and (after prayers and praises given to God, the sovereign of sovereigns) hear their duties told to them, and the positive laws that generally concern them all read and explained, and be reminded of the authority that makes those rules laws.

To this end the Jews had a sabbath every seventh day, in which the law was read and expounded — and in the solemnity of which they were reminded that their king was God; that having created the world in six days, he rested the seventh; and that by their resting from labor on that day, they remembered that this God was their king, who redeemed them from their slavish and painful labor in Egypt, and gave them a time, after they had rejoiced in God, to take joy in themselves too, by lawful recreation. So the first table of the commandments is spent entirely in setting down the sum of God's absolute power — not only as God, but as king, by special covenant, of the Jews. And it may therefore give light to those who hold sovereign power by the consent of men, showing them what doctrine they ought to teach their subjects.

And to Honor Their Parents

And because the first instruction of children depends on the care of their parents, it is necessary that children be obedient to them while under their tuition — and not only then, but afterwards too (as gratitude requires), acknowledging the benefit of their education by external signs of honor. To this end they are to be taught that originally the father of every man was also his sovereign lord, with power of life and death over him; and that when the fathers of families instituted a commonwealth and resigned that absolute power, it was never intended that they should lose the honor due to them for their children's upbringing. Giving up that honor was not necessary to the institution of sovereign power — and besides, there would be no reason for any man to want children, or to take the trouble to feed and instruct them, if he were afterwards to get no more benefit from them than from strangers. And this accords with the fifth commandment.

And to Avoid Doing Injury

Again, every sovereign ought to cause justice to be taught — and since justice consists in taking from no man what is his, that amounts to teaching men not to deprive their neighbors, by violence or fraud, of anything that the sovereign authority has made theirs. Of the things a man holds as his own, the dearest are his own life and limbs; next in degree (for most men) come those that concern married affection; and after them, riches and the means of living. So the people are to be taught to abstain from violence against one another's persons through private revenge; from violation of marital honor; and from forcible robbery and sneaking theft of one another's goods. For the same purpose, it is necessary to show them the evil consequences of false judgment through the corruption of judges or witnesses — for that erases the distinction between mine and thine, and justice becomes of no effect. All of this is intimated in the sixth, seventh, eighth, and ninth commandments.

And to Do All This Sincerely, From the Heart

Lastly, they are to be taught that not only the unjust deeds, but the designs and intentions to do them — even when something happens to prevent them — are injustice; for injustice consists in the depravity of the will as well as in the irregularity of the act. This is the intention of the tenth commandment, and the sum of the second table — which all reduces to this one commandment of mutual charity: "Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself"; just as the sum of the first table reduces to the love of God, whom the Jews had then newly received as their king.

The Use of the Universities

As for the means and channels by which the people can receive this instruction, we should ask: by what means did opinions so contrary to the peace of mankind, resting on weak and false principles, nevertheless become so deeply rooted in them? I mean the opinions I specified in the previous chapter: that men should judge what is lawful and unlawful not by the law itself but by their own private judgment; that subjects sin in obeying the commands of the commonwealth unless they themselves have first judged those commands lawful; that their property in their riches excludes the dominion the commonwealth has over the same; that it is lawful for subjects to kill those they call tyrants; that the sovereign power may be divided; and the like.

These opinions get instilled into the people in the following way. Those whom necessity or greed keeps attentive to their trades and labor, and those, on the other side, whom abundance or laziness carries after their sensual pleasures — and these two sorts of men make up the greatest part of mankind — are diverted from the deep meditation that learning the truth requires, not only in matters of natural justice but in every other science. So they receive their notions of duty chiefly from divines in the pulpit, and partly from those neighbors and familiar acquaintances who, having a knack for ready and plausible talk, seem wiser and better learned in cases of law and conscience than they are themselves. And the divines, and the others who make a show of learning, derive their knowledge from the universities and the schools of law, or from books published by men eminent in those schools and universities. It is therefore obvious that the instruction of the people depends wholly on the right teaching of youth in the universities.

But (some may say) are not the universities of England learned enough already to do that? Or is it you who would undertake to teach the universities? Hard questions. To the first, I do not hesitate to answer: until toward the latter end of Henry the Eighth, the power of the Pope was always upheld against the power of the commonwealth principally by the universities; and the fact that the doctrines maintained against the sovereign power of the king by so many preachers, and by so many lawyers and others who had their education there, is argument enough that even if the universities were not the authors of those false doctrines, they did not know how to plant the true ones. In such a contradiction of opinions, it is quite certain that they have not been sufficiently instructed; and it is no wonder if they still retain a relish of that subtle liquor against the civil authority with which they were first seasoned. But to the second question, it is neither fitting nor needful for me to say yes or no — for anyone who sees what I am doing can easily perceive what I think.

The safety of the people further requires, from him or them that hold the sovereign power, that justice be equally administered to all degrees of people: that the rich and mighty, as well as poor and obscure persons, may be righted of the injuries done to them — so that the great have no greater hope of impunity when they do violence, dishonor, or any injury to those of meaner sort, than when one of the meaner sort does the like to one of them. For in this consists equity, and to equity, as a precept of the law of nature, a sovereign is as much subject as the lowest of his people. All breaches of the law are offenses against the commonwealth; but some are also offenses against private persons. Those that concern the commonwealth only may be pardoned without any breach of equity — for every man may pardon what is done against himself, at his own discretion. But an offense against a private man cannot, in equity, be pardoned without the consent of the injured party, or reasonable satisfaction.

The inequality of subjects proceeds from the acts of sovereign power, and therefore has no more place in the presence of the sovereign — that is, in a court of justice — than the inequality between kings and their subjects has in the presence of the King of kings. The honor of great persons is to be valued for their beneficence and for the aid they give to men of lower rank — or not at all.
