And the violence, oppression, and injuries they commit are not extenuated but aggravated by the greatness of their persons, because they have the least need to commit them. The consequences of partiality toward the great unfold like this: impunity makes insolence; insolence, hatred; and hatred, an endeavor to pull down all oppressing and insulting greatness — even at the cost of ruining the commonwealth.

Equal Taxes

To equal justice belongs also the equal imposition of taxes. And this equality depends not on the equality of riches, but on the equality of the debt that every man owes to the commonwealth for his defense. It is not enough for a man to labor for the maintenance of his life; he must also fight, if need be, to secure his labor. Men must either do as the Jews did after their return from captivity, when they rebuilt the Temple — build with one hand and hold the sword in the other — or else they must hire others to fight for them. For the taxes laid on the people by the sovereign power are nothing else but the wages due to those who hold the public sword, to defend private men in the exercise of their various trades and callings.

Now, since the benefit everyone receives from this is the enjoyment of life — which is equally dear to poor and rich — the debt a poor man owes to those who defend his life is the same a rich man owes for the defense of his. The only difference is that the rich, who have the service of the poor, may be debtors not only for their own persons but for many more. All things considered, then, the equality of taxation consists in the equality of what is consumed, rather than in the riches of the persons who consume it. For what reason is there that a man who labors much and, saving the fruits of his labor, consumes little, should be more heavily charged than one who lives idly, earns little, and spends everything he gets — when the one receives no more protection from the commonwealth than the other? But when taxes are laid on the things men consume, every man pays equally for what he uses, and the commonwealth is not defrauded by the luxurious waste of private men.

Public Charity

And whereas many men become unable to maintain themselves by their labor through unavoidable accident, they ought not to be left to the charity of private persons, but to be provided for — as far as the necessities of nature require — by the laws of the commonwealth. For just as it is uncharitable in any man to neglect those who cannot help themselves, so it is uncharitable in the sovereign of a commonwealth to expose them to the hazard of such uncertain charity.

Preventing Idleness

But for those who have strong bodies, the case is different. They are to be made to work; and to remove the excuse of not finding employment, there ought to be laws that encourage every kind of industry — navigation, agriculture, fishing, and all kinds of manufacture that require labor. And if the multitude of poor but able-bodied people keeps increasing, they are to be transplanted into countries not sufficiently inhabited — where, nevertheless, they are not to exterminate the people they find there, but to oblige them to live closer together: not ranging over a great deal of ground to snatch what they find, but courting each little plot with skill and labor, to draw their sustenance from it in due season. And when the whole world is overcharged with inhabitants, then the last remedy of all is war — which provides for every man, by victory or by death.

What Makes a Good Law

To the care of the sovereign belongs the making of good laws. But what is a good law? By a good law I do not mean a just law — for no law can be unjust. The law is made by the sovereign power, and everything done by that power is warranted and owned by every one of the people; and what every man wills in this way, no man can call unjust. It is with the laws of a commonwealth as with the rules of a game: whatever the players all agree on is injustice to none of them. A good law is one that is needful for the good of the people, and at the same time clear.

Laws That Are Necessary

For the use of laws (which are only rules backed by authority) is not to bind the people from all voluntary actions, but to direct them and keep them in such motion that they do not hurt themselves through their own impetuous desires, rashness, or indiscretion — just as hedges are set, not to stop travelers, but to keep them on the road. So a law that is not needful lacks the true end of a law, and is not good. A law might be thought good when it benefits the sovereign even though it is not necessary for the people — but it is not so. For the good of the sovereign and of the people cannot be separated. It is a weak sovereign that has weak subjects; and it is a weak people whose sovereign lacks the power to rule them at his will. Unnecessary laws are not good laws, but traps for money — which, where the right of sovereign power is acknowledged, are superfluous; and where it is not acknowledged, insufficient to defend the people.

Laws That Are Clear

The clarity of a law consists not so much in the words of the law itself as in a declaration of the causes and motives for which it was made. That is what shows us the meaning of the legislator; and once the meaning of the legislator is known, the law is more easily understood in few words than in many. For all words are subject to ambiguity, and so multiplying words in the body of the law is multiplying ambiguity. Besides, excessive wordiness seems to imply that whoever can dodge the words is outside the reach of the law. And this is the cause of many unnecessary lawsuits. When I consider how short the laws of ancient times were, and how by degrees they grew ever longer, I seem to see a contest between the drafters of the law and the pleaders of the law — the former seeking to hem in the latter, and the latter seeking to slip out of their fences — and the pleaders have won. It therefore belongs to the office of a legislator (which in all commonwealths is the supreme representative, whether one man or an assembly) to make the reason clear for which the law was made, and to keep the body of the law itself as short as possible, in the most proper and significant terms that can be found.

Punishments

It also belongs to the office of the sovereign to make a right application of punishments and rewards. And since the end of punishing is not revenge or the venting of anger, but correction — of the offender, or of others through his example — the severest punishments should be inflicted for those crimes that are most dangerous to the public: those that proceed from malice against the established government; those that spring from contempt of justice; those that provoke indignation in the multitude; and those which, left unpunished, seem authorized — as when they are committed by the sons, servants, or favorites of men in authority. For indignation carries men not only against the actors and authors of injustice, but against every power that looks likely to protect them — as in the case of Tarquin, who, for the insolent act of one of his sons, was driven out of Rome, and the monarchy itself dissolved with him.

But crimes of weakness — those that proceed from great provocation, from great fear, great need, or from ignorance of whether the act is a great crime or not — often leave room for lenity without prejudice to the commonwealth; and lenity, when there is room for it, is required by the law of nature. It is the punishment of the leaders and teachers of an uprising — not of the poor, seduced people — that profits the commonwealth by example. To be severe with the common people is to punish an ignorance that may in great part be laid at the sovereign's own door, since it was his fault that they were no better instructed.

Rewards

In the same way, it belongs to the office and duty of the sovereign always to apply his rewards so that benefit to the commonwealth arises from them — for that is their use and end. And this is done when those who have served the commonwealth well are recompensed — with as little expense of the common treasury as possible — well enough that others are encouraged both to serve it as faithfully as they can and to study the arts that will enable them to serve it better.

But to buy off a popular, ambitious subject with money or promotion, so that he will be quiet and stop making bad impressions on the minds of the people — that has nothing of the nature of reward in it (for reward is ordained for past service, not for disservice), nor is it a sign of gratitude, but of fear; and it tends not to the benefit but to the damage of the public. It is a contest with ambition like that of Hercules with the monster Hydra: the beast had many heads, and for every one cut off, three grew up in its place. In just the same way, when the stubbornness of one popular man is overcome with reward, many more arise — following the example — to do the same mischief in hope of the same payoff. And like every kind of manufacture, malice multiplies by being marketable. A civil war may sometimes be deferred by such methods, but the danger keeps growing greater, and the public ruin more assured. It is therefore against the duty of the sovereign, to whom the public safety is committed, to reward those who aspire to greatness by disturbing the peace of their country — rather than opposing the beginnings of such men, at a little danger now, instead of at a much greater danger later.

Counselors

Another business of the sovereign is to choose good counselors — I mean men whose advice he takes in the government of the commonwealth. The word counsel — consilium, corrupted from considium — has a broad meaning, and covers all assemblies of men who sit together, not only to deliberate about what is to be done in the future, but also to judge of facts past and of law for the present. I take it here in the first sense only. And in this sense there is no choosing of counsel in a democracy or an aristocracy, because the persons counseling are members of the person counseled. The choice of counselors, then, belongs properly to monarchy — in which the sovereign who does not try to choose, in every field, the most able men, fails to discharge his office as he ought.

The most able counselors are those who have the least hope of benefit from giving evil counsel, and the most knowledge of what conduces to the peace and defense of the commonwealth. It is a hard matter to know who expects benefit from public troubles; but the signs that justify suspicion include the soothing of the people in their unreasonable or irremediable grievances, by men whose estates are not sufficient to cover their accustomed expenses — something easily observed by anyone whose business it is to know it. But to know who has the most knowledge of public affairs is harder still; and those who do know such men have that much less need of them. For to know who knows the rules of almost any art is itself a great degree of knowledge of that art, since no man can be assured of the truth of another's rules unless he is first taught to understand them. The best signs of knowledge of any art are much practice in it and consistently good effects from it.

Good counsel comes neither by lot nor by inheritance; and therefore there is no more reason to expect good advice from the rich or noble in matters of state than in drawing up the dimensions of a fortress — unless we are to think there is no method needed in the study of politics (as there is in the study of geometry), but only spectating. Which is not so. Politics is the harder study of the two. In these parts of Europe it has been taken as the right of certain persons to have a place in the highest council of state by inheritance. This derives from the conquests of the ancient Germans, in which many absolute lords, joining together to conquer other nations, would not enter the confederacy without privileges that would mark the difference, in time to come, between their posterity and the posterity of their subjects. Those privileges are inconsistent with sovereign power; by the favor of the sovereign, they may seem to keep them; but if they contend for them as their right, they must by degrees let them go, and end with no more honor than naturally adheres to their abilities.

And however able the counselors may be in any affair, the benefit of their counsel is greater when each gives his advice, and the reasons for it, separately, than when they do it in an assembly by way of speeches; and greater when they have prepared in advance than when they speak on the spur of the moment — both because they have more time to survey the consequences of the action, and because they are less liable to be carried into contradiction by envy, rivalry, or the other passions that arise from difference of opinion.

The best counsel, in matters that concern not other nations but only the ease and benefit the subjects may enjoy from laws that look only inward, is to be taken from the general reports and complaints of the people of each province, who know their own wants best — and who therefore, when they demand nothing that derogates from the essential rights of sovereignty, ought to be diligently listened to. For without those essential rights, as I have said often enough, the commonwealth cannot subsist at all.

Commanders

A commander-in-chief of an army, if he is not popular, will be neither loved nor feared by his army as he ought to be, and consequently cannot perform his office with good success. He must therefore be industrious, valiant, affable, generous, and fortunate, so that he gains a reputation both for competence and for loving his soldiers. This is popularity; it breeds in the soldiers both the desire and the courage to recommend themselves to his favor, and it protects the severity of the general when he must punish mutinous or negligent soldiers. But this love of soldiers, if no assurance is given of the commander's fidelity, is a dangerous thing to sovereign power — especially when that power is in the hands of an assembly that is not popular. It therefore belongs to the safety of the people that those to whom the sovereign commits his armies be both good leaders and faithful subjects.

But when the sovereign himself is popular — that is, revered and beloved by his people — there is no danger at all from the popularity of a subject. For soldiers are never so generally unjust as to side with their captain against their sovereign, however much they love him, when they love not only the sovereign's person but also his cause. That is why those who have at any time suppressed the power of their lawful sovereign by violence have always, before they could settle into his place, been put to the trouble of contriving titles for themselves — to save the people from the shame of accepting them. To have a known right to sovereign power is so popular a quality that the man who has it needs nothing more, on his own part, to turn the hearts of his subjects to him, than to be seen able to govern his own household absolutely; and nothing more, on the part of his enemies, than the disbanding of their armies. For the greatest and most active part of mankind has never yet been well contented with the present.

Concerning the offices of one sovereign to another, which are comprehended in the law commonly called the law of nations, I need say nothing here — because the law of nations and the law of nature are the same thing. Every sovereign has the same right, in procuring the safety of his people, that any individual man has in procuring the safety of his own body. And the same law that dictates to men who have no civil government what they ought to do, and what to avoid, with regard to one another, dictates the same to commonwealths — that is, to the consciences of sovereign princes and sovereign assemblies. For there is no court of natural justice except in the conscience alone, where not man but God reigns — whose laws, such of them as oblige all mankind, are natural in respect of God as the author of nature, and are laws in respect of the same God as King of kings. But of the kingdom of God, as King of kings and as king also of a peculiar people, I shall speak in the rest of this discourse.

Chapter 31: The Kingdom of God by Nature

The Scope of the Chapters That Follow

I have already proved, in everything written so far, the following: that the condition of mere nature — that is, of absolute liberty, the condition of people who are neither sovereigns nor subjects — is anarchy and a condition of war. That the precepts by which men are guided to avoid that condition are the laws of nature. That a commonwealth without sovereign power is just a word without substance, and cannot stand. And that subjects owe their sovereigns simple obedience in everything that does not conflict with the laws of God.

Only one thing is still missing for a complete knowledge of civil duty: knowing what those laws of God are. Without that, a man cannot tell, when the civil power commands him to do something, whether the command is contrary to God's law or not. And so he either offends the Divine Majesty through too much civil obedience, or, out of fear of offending God, breaks the commandments of the commonwealth. To steer clear of both these rocks, it is necessary to know what the divine laws are. And since the knowledge of all law depends on the knowledge of the sovereign power, I will say something in what follows about the KINGDOM OF GOD.

Who Counts as a Subject in the Kingdom of God

"God is King, let the earth rejoice," says the Psalmist (Psalm 96:1). And again, "God is King though the nations be angry; and he that sitteth on the cherubims, though the earth be moved" (Psalm 98:1). Whether men like it or not, they are always subject to the divine power. By denying God's existence or his providence, men may shake off their ease — but not their yoke.

But to call this power of God a "kingdom" — a power that extends not only to man but to beasts, plants, and lifeless bodies — is only a metaphorical use of the word. For only he properly reigns who governs his subjects by his word: by promising rewards to those who obey it, and by threatening punishment to those who do not. So the subjects in the kingdom of God are not inanimate bodies, and not irrational creatures, because they understand no precepts as his. Nor atheists. Nor those who do not believe that God takes any care of the actions of mankind — because these people acknowledge no word as his, and have no hope of his rewards or fear of his threats. Those, then, who believe there is a God who governs the world, who has given precepts and set out rewards and punishments for mankind — those are God's subjects. All the rest are to be understood as enemies.

God's Threefold Word: Reason, Revelation, Prophecy

To rule by words requires that the words be plainly made known; otherwise they are not laws. For it belongs to the nature of law to be sufficiently and clearly promulgated — published in a way that takes away the excuse of ignorance. In the laws of men there is only one kind of promulgation: proclamation by the voice of man. But God declares his laws three ways: by the dictates of natural reason, by revelation, and by the voice of some man to whom he gives credibility with everyone else through the working of miracles.

From this arises a triple word of God — rational, sensible, and prophetic — and to these correspond three kinds of hearing: right reason, supernatural sense, and faith. As for supernatural sense, which consists in revelation or inspiration, no universal laws have ever been given that way, because God does not speak in that manner except to particular persons, and he says different things to different men.

God's Twofold Kingdom: Natural and Prophetic

From the difference between the other two kinds of God's word — the rational and the prophetic — we may attribute to God a twofold kingdom: natural and prophetic. Natural, in which he governs as many of mankind as acknowledge his providence, through the natural dictates of right reason. And prophetic, in which, having chosen out one particular nation (the Jews) as his subjects, he governed them — and them alone — not only by natural reason, but by positive laws, which he gave them through the mouths of his holy prophets. It is the natural kingdom of God I intend to speak of in this chapter.

God's Right to Rule Comes from His Omnipotence

The right of nature by which God reigns over men, and punishes those who break his laws, is derived not from his having created them — as if he required obedience out of gratitude for his benefits — but from his irresistible power.

I have shown earlier how sovereign right arises from pact. To show how the same right can arise from nature, I need only show in what case it is never given up. By nature, all men had a right to all things — which means every man had a right to reign over all the rest. But because this right could not be obtained by force, it concerned the safety of everyone to lay that right aside, and to set up men with sovereign authority, by common consent, to rule and defend them. Yet if there had been any man with irresistible power, there would have been no reason why he should not, by that power, have ruled and defended both himself and them, according to his own discretion.

To those whose power is irresistible, then, dominion over all men attaches naturally, by the sheer excellence of that power. And consequently it is from that power that the kingdom over men, and the right of afflicting men at his pleasure, belongs naturally to God Almighty — not as Creator, not as gracious, but as omnipotent. And though punishment is due for sin only (because by that word we understand affliction for sin), the right of afflicting is not always derived from men's sin, but from God's power.

Sin Is Not the Cause of All Suffering

Why do evil men often prosper, while good men suffer adversity? The ancients disputed this question endlessly, and it is the same as our question here: by what right does God hand out the prosperities and adversities of this life? It is so difficult a question that it has shaken faith in divine providence — not only among ordinary people, but among philosophers, and, what is more, among the saints. "How good," says David, "is the God of Israel to those that are upright in heart; and yet my feet were almost gone, my treadings had well-nigh slipped; for I was grieved at the wicked, when I saw the ungodly in such prosperity." And Job — how earnestly he argues with God over the many afflictions he suffered despite his righteousness!

In Job's case, the question is decided by God himself — and not with arguments drawn from Job's sin, but from his own power. Job's friends reasoned backward from his affliction to his sin; Job defended himself with the knowledge of his own innocence. Then God himself takes up the matter. He justifies the affliction with arguments drawn from his power — such as "Where were you when I laid the foundations of the earth?" and the like — and in doing so he both approved Job's innocence and rebuked the erroneous doctrine of his friends.

Our Savior's words about the man born blind agree with this doctrine: "Neither has this man sinned, nor his parents; but that the works of God might be made manifest in him." It is true that Scripture says death entered the world by sin — meaning that if Adam had never sinned, he would never have died, never suffered any separation of his soul from his body. But it does not follow that God could not justly have afflicted him even if he had never sinned — just as God afflicts other living creatures, which cannot sin at all.

The Divine Laws

Having spoken of the right of God's sovereignty as grounded only on nature, we must consider next what the divine laws are — the dictates of natural reason. These laws concern either the natural duties of one man to another, or the honor naturally due to our divine sovereign. The first are the same laws of nature I have already discussed in chapters 14 and 15 of this treatise: equity, justice, mercy, humility, and the rest of the moral virtues. What remains, then, is to consider what precepts natural reason alone — without any other word of God — dictates to men concerning the honor and worship of the Divine Majesty.

What Honor and Worship Are

Honor consists in the inward thought and opinion of another's power and goodness. So to honor God is to think as highly of his power and goodness as we possibly can. The external signs of that opinion, appearing in men's words and actions, are called WORSHIP — which is one part of what the Latins understood by the word cultus.

For cultus properly and consistently means the labor a man spends on something with the purpose of getting some benefit from it. Now, the things we get benefit from are of two kinds. Either they are subject to us, and the profit they yield follows our labor as a natural effect; or they are not subject to us, and answer our labor according to their own wills. In the first sense, the labor spent on the earth is called culture — cultivation — and the education of children is a culture of their minds. In the second sense — where men's wills must be won to our purpose not by force, but by pleasing them — it means as much as courting: winning favor by good offices, by praise, by acknowledging their power, and by whatever else is pleasing to those from whom we hope for some benefit. And this, properly, is worship. In this sense Publicola is understood as a worshipper of the people, and cultus Dei as the worship of God.

The Signs of Honor

From internal honor — the opinion of power and goodness — arise three passions: love, which refers to goodness; and hope and fear, which relate to power. And there are three parts of external worship: praise, magnifying, and blessing. The subject of praise is goodness; the subject of magnifying and blessing is power, and its effect, happiness. Praise and magnifying can be expressed both by words and by actions: by words, when we say a man is good or great; by actions, when we thank him for his generosity and obey his power. The opinion of another's happiness can only be expressed by words.

Natural and Arbitrary Worship

Some signs of honor, both in attributes and in actions, are naturally so: among attributes, "good," "just," "generous," and the like; among actions, prayers, thanks, and obedience. Others are signs of honor by institution or by the custom of men — honorable in some times and places, dishonorable in others, and indifferent in still others. Such are the gestures used in greeting, prayer, and thanksgiving, which different times and places use differently. The first kind is natural worship; the second, arbitrary worship.

Commanded and Free Worship

And arbitrary worship comes in two varieties: sometimes it is commanded, sometimes voluntary. Commanded, when it is what the one being worshipped requires; free, when it is what the worshipper thinks fit. When worship is commanded, the worship lies not in the words or gestures, but in the obedience. But when it is free, the worship consists in the opinion of the onlookers. For if the words or actions by which we intend honor seem ridiculous to them, or insulting, then they are not worship at all — because a sign is not a sign to the one who gives it, but to the one it is made to: that is, to the spectator.

Public and Private Worship

Again, there is public worship and private worship. Public worship is the worship a commonwealth performs as one person. Private worship is what a private person exhibits. Public worship, with respect to the whole commonwealth, is free; but with respect to particular men, it is not. Private worship is free when done in secret; but done in the sight of the multitude, it is never without some restraint — either from the laws or from the opinion of men — and that is contrary to the nature of liberty.

The Purpose of Worship

Among men, the purpose of worship is power. For when a man sees another being worshipped, he supposes him powerful, and is the readier to obey him — which makes his power greater still. But God has no ends. The worship we give him proceeds from our duty, and is directed — as far as our capacity allows — by those rules of honor that reason dictates the weak should perform toward more powerful men: in hope of benefit, in fear of damage, or in thankfulness for good already received from them.

The Attributes of Divine Honor

So that we may know what worship of God the light of nature teaches us, I will begin with his attributes.

First, it is obvious that we ought to attribute existence to him. For no man can have the will to honor something he thinks has no being at all.

Secondly, those philosophers who said that the world, or the soul of the world, was God spoke unworthily of him — in fact, they denied his existence. For by "God" we understand the cause of the world; and to say the world is God is to say it has no cause — that is, that there is no God.

Thirdly, to say the world was not created but is eternal is to deny there is a God — since whatever is eternal has no cause.

Fourthly, those who attribute ease to God (as they imagine they do), and so take from him the care of mankind, take from him his honor. For it removes men's love and fear of him — and that is the root of honor.

Fifthly, in things that signify greatness and power, to say God is finite is not to honor him. For it is no sign of a will to honor God to attribute less to him than we can; and finite is less than we can, because to anything finite it is easy to add more.

Therefore, to attribute shape to him is not honor, for all shape is finite.

Nor to say we conceive, or imagine, or have an idea of him in our mind — for whatever we conceive is finite.

Nor to attribute parts to him, or totality — which are attributes only of finite things.

Nor to say he is in this place or that — for whatever is in a place is bounded and finite.

Nor that he moves, or rests — for both of these attributes assign him a place.

Nor that there is more than one God — because that implies they are all finite, for there cannot be more than one infinite.

Nor should we ascribe to him (except metaphorically — meaning not the passion but its effect) passions that partake of grief, such as repentance, anger, or mercy; or of want, such as appetite, hope, or desire; or any passive faculty at all. For passion is power limited by something else.

And therefore when we ascribe a will to God, it is not to be understood as a man's will — a rational appetite — but as the power by which he effects everything.

Likewise when we attribute to him sight and other acts of sense, or knowledge and understanding — which in us are nothing but a tumult of the mind, stirred up by external things pressing on the organs of a man's body. There is no such thing in God; and since these things depend on natural causes, they cannot be attributed to him.

Whoever will attribute to God nothing but what natural reason warrants must use either negative attributes, such as infinite, eternal, incomprehensible; or superlatives, such as Most High, Most Great, and the like; or indefinite ones, such as good, just, holy, Creator. And he must use them not as though he meant to declare what God is — for that would be to confine him within the limits of our imagination — but to declare how much we admire him, and how ready we would be to obey him. That is a sign of humility, and of a will to honor him as much as we can. For there is only one name that signifies our conception of his nature, and that is I AM; and only one name of his relation to us, and that is God — in which is contained Father, King, and Lord.

Actions That Are Signs of Divine Honor

Concerning the actions of divine worship, the most general precept of reason is this: that they be signs of the intention to honor God.

Such as, first, prayers. For it was not the carvers, when they made images, who were thought to make them gods — it was the people who prayed to them.

Secondly, thanksgiving — which differs from prayer in divine worship only in this, that prayers come before the benefit and thanks come after it. The purpose of both is the same: to acknowledge God as the author of all benefits, past and future alike.

Thirdly, gifts — that is, sacrifices and offerings — are signs of honor, provided they are of the best; for they are thanksgivings.

Fourthly, to swear by none but God is naturally a sign of honor. For it is a confession that God alone knows the heart, and that no man's cleverness or strength can protect him against God's vengeance on the perjured.

Fifthly, it is part of rational worship to speak carefully of God; for it shows fear of him, and fear is a confession of his power. From this it follows that the name of God is not to be used rashly and to no purpose — for that is as much as to use it in vain. And it is to no purpose unless it is by way of an oath, by order of the commonwealth, to make judgments certain; or between commonwealths, to avoid war.

It also follows that disputing about God's nature is contrary to his honor. For in this natural kingdom of God, it is supposed there is no way to know anything except by natural reason — that is, from the principles of natural science. And those principles are so far from teaching us anything about God's nature that they cannot even teach us our own nature, or the nature of the smallest living creature. So when men dispute about the attributes of God from the principles of natural reason, they only dishonor him. For in the attributes we give to God, what matters is not the signification of philosophical truth, but the signification of pious intention — the intention to do him the greatest honor we are able. From neglecting this consideration have come whole volumes of disputation about the nature of God, which tend not to his honor, but to the honor of our own wits and learning — and which are nothing but inconsiderate and vain abuses of his sacred name.

Sixthly, in prayers, thanksgivings, offerings, and sacrifices, natural reason dictates that each one should be, of its kind, the best, and the most significant of honor. For example, prayers and thanksgiving should be made in words and phrases that are not hasty, not flippant, not vulgar, but beautiful and well composed — for otherwise we do not give God as much honor as we can. The heathens were absurd to worship images as gods; but their doing it in verse, and with music of both voice and instruments, was reasonable. So too, that the beasts they sacrificed, and the gifts they offered, and their actions in worshipping were full of submission, and commemorated benefits received — that was according to reason, since it proceeded from an intention to honor him.

Seventhly, reason directs us to worship God not only in secret, but also — and especially — in public, in the sight of men. For without that, the thing most acceptable in honor is lost: leading others to honor him.

Lastly, obedience to his laws — which in this case means the laws of nature — is the greatest worship of all. For just as obedience is more acceptable to God than sacrifice, so to make light of his commandments is the greatest of all insults. And these are the laws of divine worship that natural reason dictates to private men.

Public Worship Must Be Uniform

But since a commonwealth is one person, it ought to exhibit one worship to God. And it does this when it commands that worship to be exhibited by private men publicly. This is PUBLIC WORSHIP, and its defining property is uniformity. For actions done differently by different men cannot be called a public worship. And therefore, where many sorts of worship are allowed, proceeding from the different religions of private men, it cannot be said that there is any public worship — or that the commonwealth has any religion at all.

All Attributes Depend on the Civil Laws

And because words — and consequently the attributes of God — get their signification by the agreement and constitution of men, those attributes are to be held significant of honor that men intend to be so. And whatever may be done by the wills of particular men, where there is no law but reason, may be done by the will of the commonwealth, through civil laws. And because a commonwealth has no will, and makes no laws, except those made by the will of the man or assembly that holds the sovereign power, it follows that the attributes which the sovereign ordains for signs of honor in the worship of God ought to be taken and used as such by private men in their public worship.

But Not All Actions

Not all actions, however, are signs by convention. Some are naturally signs of honor, others of contempt. The latter — the things men are ashamed to do in the sight of those they revere — can never be made part of divine worship by any human power. The former — decent, modest, humble behavior — can never be separated from it. But there is an infinite number of actions and gestures of an indifferent nature; and those of them that the commonwealth ordains to be publicly and universally in use, as signs of honor and part of God's worship, are to be taken and used as such by the subjects. As for what Scripture says — "It is better to obey God than men" — that applies in the kingdom of God by pact, not by nature.

Natural Punishments

Having spoken thus briefly of the natural kingdom of God and his natural laws, I will add to this chapter only a short declaration of his natural punishments.

There is no action of man in this life that is not the beginning of a chain of consequences so long that no human foresight is high enough to see to the end of it. In this chain, pleasing and unpleasing events are linked together — in such a way that whoever does anything for his pleasure must commit himself to suffer all the pains attached to it. These pains are the natural punishments of those actions that begin more harm than good. And so it comes to pass that intemperance is naturally punished with diseases; rashness, with mishaps; injustice, with the violence of enemies; pride, with ruin; cowardice, with oppression; the negligent government of princes, with rebellion; and rebellion, with slaughter. For since punishments follow the breach of laws, natural punishments must naturally follow the breach of the laws of nature — and so they follow them as their natural effects, not arbitrary ones.

The Conclusion of the Second Part

And so much for the constitution, nature, and right of sovereigns, and for the duty of subjects, derived from the principles of natural reason.

Now, when I consider how different this doctrine is from the practice of most of the world — especially of these Western parts, which have received their moral learning from Rome and Athens — and how much depth of moral philosophy is required in those who administer the sovereign power, I am at the point of believing this labor of mine as useless as the Commonwealth of Plato. For Plato too was of the opinion that the disorders of state, and the change of governments by civil war, can never be taken away until sovereigns become philosophers.

But then I consider again: that the science of natural justice is the only science necessary for sovereigns and their principal ministers; that they need not be burdened with the mathematical sciences (as Plato would have them), beyond encouraging the study of them by good laws; and that neither Plato nor any other philosopher so far has put in order, and sufficiently or probably proved, all the theorems of moral doctrine, so that men might learn from them both how to govern and how to obey. And then I recover some hope: that one time or other, this writing of mine may fall into the hands of a sovereign who will consider it for himself — for it is short, and I think clear — without the help of any interested or envious interpreter; and who, by the exercise of entire sovereignty, in protecting the public teaching of it, will convert this truth of speculation into the utility of practice.

Part III: Of a Christian Commonwealth

Chapter 32: The Principles of Christian Politics

The Word of God, Delivered by Prophets, Is the Main Principle of Christian Politics

Up to this point, I have derived the rights of sovereign power and the duty of subjects from the principles of nature alone — principles that experience has shown to be true, or that agreement about the use of words has made true. That is, I have argued from the nature of human beings as we know it by experience, and from definitions of the words essential to all political reasoning, definitions everyone accepts.

But the subject I take up next is the nature and rights of a CHRISTIAN COMMONWEALTH, and much of that depends on supernatural revelations of the will of God. So the foundation of my argument from here on must be not only the natural word of God, but also the prophetic word.

This does not mean we are to renounce our senses and our experience — or natural reason, which is itself the undoubted word of God. These are the talents God has placed in our hands to trade with until our blessed Savior comes again. They are not to be folded up in the napkin of an unquestioning faith, like the servant in the parable who buried his master's money; they are to be invested — spent in the pursuit of justice, peace, and true religion.

It is true that many things in God's word are above reason. That is, they can neither be demonstrated nor refuted by natural reason. But nothing in it is contrary to reason. And when something seems to be, the fault lies either in our clumsy interpretation or in our faulty reasoning.

So when something written there is too hard for our examination, we are told to take our understanding captive to the words, and not to wear ourselves out trying to extract a philosophical truth, by logic, from mysteries that cannot be comprehended and fall under no rule of natural science. The mysteries of our religion are like medicine for the sick: pills that cure when swallowed whole, but when chewed, are mostly thrown back up without doing any good.

What It Means to Take the Understanding Captive

But this captivity of our understanding does not mean submitting our intellect to the opinion of some other man. It means submitting our will to obedience, where obedience is due.

Here is why. Sense, memory, understanding, reason, and opinion are not in our power to change. They are always, and necessarily, whatever the things we see, hear, and consider suggest to us. They are not the effects of our will — our will is the effect of them. We take our understanding and reason captive, then, when we refrain from contradiction; when we speak as we are commanded to speak by lawful authority; and when we live accordingly. In short, it is trust, and faith placed in the one who speaks — even when the mind can form no idea at all from the words spoken.

How God Speaks to Men

When God speaks to a man, it must be either immediately — directly — or through another man, to whom God had previously spoken directly himself.

How God speaks to a man directly may be well enough understood by those he has spoken to. But how that should be understood by anyone else is hard, if not impossible, to know. If a man claims to me that God has spoken to him supernaturally and directly, and I doubt it, I cannot easily see what argument he could produce that would oblige me to believe him. It is true that if he is my sovereign, he can oblige me to obedience — meaning I may not declare, by act or word, that I do not believe him. But he cannot oblige me to think anything other than what my reason persuades me of. And if someone with no such authority over me makes the same claim, there is nothing that compels either belief or obedience.

Consider what such claims actually amount to. To say God has spoken to him in the Holy Scripture is not to say God has spoken to him directly, but through the prophets, or the apostles, or the church — the same way God speaks to every other Christian. To say God has spoken to him in a dream is no more than to say he dreamed that God spoke to him — which carries no force with anyone who knows that dreams are mostly natural, and can spring from earlier thoughts. And dreams of that particular kind tend to spring from self-conceit, foolish arrogance, and a false opinion of one's own godliness or virtue — the belief that one has earned the favor of an extraordinary revelation. To say he has seen a vision, or heard a voice, is to say he dreamed somewhere between sleeping and waking; that is exactly how a man often mistakes his dream for a vision, having failed to notice that he had dozed off. To say he speaks by supernatural inspiration is to say he feels a burning desire to speak, or holds some strong opinion of himself, for which he can give no natural and sufficient reason.

So although God Almighty certainly can speak to a man by dreams, visions, voices, and inspiration, he obliges no one to believe that he has done so to the man who claims it. That man, being a man, may err — and, what is more, may lie.

By What Marks Prophets Are Known

How, then, can a person to whom God has never revealed his will directly (except by way of natural reason) know when to obey, and when not to obey, the word of God delivered by someone who says he is a prophet?

Scripture itself shows the problem. Of the 400 prophets the king of Israel consulted about his war against Ramoth Gilead, only Micaiah was a true one (1 Kings 22). And the prophet sent to prophesy against the altar Jeroboam had set up — a true prophet, shown to be sent from God by two miracles performed in his presence — was nevertheless deceived by another, older prophet, who persuaded him, as if speaking from the mouth of God, to eat and drink with him (1 Kings 13). If one prophet can deceive another, what certainty is there of knowing the will of God by any way other than reason?

To this I answer, out of the Holy Scripture itself, that there are two marks by which — together, not separately — a true prophet is to be known. One is the working of miracles. The other is teaching no other religion than the one already established. Separately, I say, neither one is sufficient.

"If a prophet rise among you, or a dreamer of dreams, and shall claim to do a miracle, and the miracle comes to pass; if he says, Let us follow strange gods, which you have not known, you shall not listen to him... But that prophet and dreamer of dreams shall be put to death, because he has spoken to make you revolt from the Lord your God" (Deuteronomy 13:1-5).

Two things should be observed in those words. First: God will not have miracles alone serve as proof of a prophet's calling. As the third verse says, they serve instead as a test of the constancy of our adherence to God himself. After all, the works of the Egyptian sorcerers, though not as great as those of Moses, were still great miracles. Second: however great the miracle may be, if it tends to stir up revolt against the king, or against whoever governs by the king's authority, the man who performs it is to be regarded as nothing other than someone sent to test their allegiance. For in this passage, the words "revolt from the Lord your God" are equivalent to "revolt from your king" — because Israel had made God their king by pact at the foot of Mount Sinai, and God ruled them through Moses alone; only Moses spoke with God, and from time to time he declared God's commandments to the people.

Our Savior Christ did the same. After he had brought his disciples to acknowledge him as the Messiah — that is, as God's anointed, the king the Jewish nation expected daily but refused when he came — he did not fail to warn them of the danger of miracles. "There shall arise," he says, "false Christs, and false prophets, and they shall do great wonders and miracles, even to the seducing (if it were possible) of the very elect" (Matthew 24:24). Which shows that false prophets may have the power of miracles — and yet we are not to take their doctrine for God's word. St. Paul goes further, telling the Galatians that "if he himself, or an angel from heaven, preach another gospel to them than the one he had preached, let him be accursed" (Galatians 1:8). That gospel was that Christ was king. So by these words, all preaching against the power of the established king stands accursed by St. Paul — for his speech was addressed to people who, through his preaching, had already received Jesus as the Christ, that is, as king of the Jews.

The Marks of a Prophet Under the Old Law: Miracles, and Doctrine That Conforms to the Law

Just as miracles without preaching the doctrine God has established prove nothing, so preaching the true doctrine without working miracles is an insufficient argument for direct revelation. If a man who teaches no false doctrine claims to be a prophet without showing any miracle, his claim deserves no special regard. This is clear from Deuteronomy: "If you say in your heart, How shall we know that the word of the prophet is not what the Lord has spoken? When the prophet has spoken in the name of the Lord something that does not come to pass, that is the word the Lord has not spoken; the prophet has spoken it out of the pride of his own heart. Do not fear him" (Deuteronomy 18:21-22).

But here a man may ask again: when the prophet has foretold a thing, how shall we know whether it will come to pass or not? He may foretell it as something to arrive after a long stretch of time — longer than a human life — or indefinitely, as something that will happen sooner or later. In that case, this mark of a prophet is useless. Therefore the miracles that oblige us to believe a prophet must be confirmed by an immediate event, or one not long delayed.

So it is plain: teaching the religion God has established, and showing a present miracle, joined together, were the only marks by which Scripture would have a true prophet — that is, direct revelation — acknowledged. Neither of them alone is sufficient to oblige any other man to heed what he says.

When Miracles Cease, Prophets Cease, and Scripture Takes Their Place

Since miracles have now ceased, we have no sign left by which to recognize the claimed revelations or inspirations of any private man, and no obligation to give ear to any doctrine beyond what conforms to the Holy Scriptures. Since the time of our Savior, the Scriptures supply the place of all other prophecy. From them, by wise and careful reckoning, all the rules and precepts necessary to know our duty to God and man can easily be deduced — without enthusiasm, and without supernatural inspiration.

And this Scripture is the source from which I will take the principles of my argument concerning the rights of those who are the supreme governors of Christian commonwealths on earth, and the duty of Christian subjects toward their sovereigns. To that end, I shall speak in the next chapter of the books, the writers, the scope, and the authority of the Bible.

Chapter 33: The Number, Antiquity, Scope, Authority, and Interpreters of the Books of Holy Scripture

The Books of Holy Scripture

By the books of Holy Scripture we mean those books that ought to be the CANON — that is to say, the rules of Christian life. And because every rule of life that men are bound in conscience to observe is a law, the question of Scripture is really the question of what counts as law throughout all Christendom, both natural and civil. Scripture does not spell out which laws every Christian king shall set up in his own dominions; but it does determine which laws he shall not set up. And since I have already proved that sovereigns in their own dominions are the sole legislators, it follows that the only books that are canonical — that is, the only books that are law — in any nation are the ones established as such by the sovereign authority.

It is true that God is the sovereign of all sovereigns. So when he speaks to any subject, that subject ought to obey him, whatever any earthly power commands to the contrary. But the question is not whether to obey God. The question is when and what God has said. And subjects who have received no supernatural revelation cannot know that except by natural reason — the same reason that guided them, for the sake of peace and justice, to obey the authority of their commonwealths, that is, of their lawful sovereigns.

Following that obligation, I can acknowledge no books of the Old Testament as Holy Scripture except those that the authority of the Church of England has commanded us to acknowledge. Which books these are is well enough known without a catalogue here. They are the same ones acknowledged by St. Jerome, who counts the rest as Apocrypha — namely the Wisdom of Solomon, Ecclesiasticus, Judith, Tobias, the first and second books of Maccabees (though he had seen the first in Hebrew), and the third and fourth books of Esdras.

Of the canonical books, Josephus — a learned Jew who wrote in the time of the Emperor Domitian — counts twenty-two, making the number match the letters of the Hebrew alphabet. St. Jerome arrives at the same count, though the two of them divide the books differently. Josephus numbers five books of Moses, thirteen of prophets who wrote the history of their own times (how that squares with the prophets' writings contained in the Bible, we shall see later), and four of hymns and moral precepts. St. Jerome counts five books of Moses, eight of prophets, and nine of other holy writings, which he calls the Hagiographa — the "sacred writings." The Septuagint — the seventy learned Jewish scholars sent for by Ptolemy, King of Egypt, to translate the Jewish law from Hebrew into Greek — have left us no other books as Holy Scripture in the Greek language than the very ones received by the Church of England.

As for the books of the New Testament, they are acknowledged as canon equally by all Christian churches, and by every sect of Christians that admits any books at all as canonical.

Their Antiquity

Who originally wrote the various books of Holy Scripture has never been established by sufficient testimony from other history — and testimony is the only proof there is for a matter of fact. Nor can it be settled by arguments of natural reason, because reason serves only to establish truths of consequence, not truths of fact. So the light that must guide us in this question has to be the light held out to us by the books themselves. And though that light does not show us the writer of every book, it is still useful: it can tell us the time in which the books were written.

The Pentateuch Was Not Written by Moses

Start with the Pentateuch. The fact that these are called the five Books of Moses is not enough to prove that Moses wrote them — no more than the titles "the Book of Joshua," "the Book of Judges," "the Book of Ruth," and "the Books of the Kings" prove that those books were written by Joshua, by the judges, by Ruth, and by the kings. In book titles, the subject is named as often as the writer. "The History of Livy" names the writer; but "the History of Scanderbeg" is named for its subject.

Now consider what we read in the last chapter of Deuteronomy, verse 6, concerning the tomb of Moses: "no man knoweth of his sepulcher to this day" — that is, to the day those words were written. Plainly, then, those words were written after his burial. It would be a strange reading indeed to say that Moses spoke of his own tomb — even by prophecy — declaring that it had not been found up to a day on which he was still alive.

Perhaps someone will object that only the last chapter was written by another hand, not the whole Pentateuch. So consider what we find in the Book of Genesis (Genesis 12:6): "And Abraham passed through the land to the place of Sichem, unto the plain of Moreh, and the Canaanite was then in the land." These must be the words of someone writing at a time when the Canaanite was no longer in the land — and therefore not the words of Moses, who died before he ever entered it. Likewise, in Numbers 21:14, the writer cites another, more ancient book, titled The Book of the Wars of the Lord, in which were recorded the acts of Moses at the Red Sea and at the brook of Arnon. It is sufficiently evident, then, that the five Books of Moses were written after his time — though how long after is not so clear.

But even though Moses did not compile those books entirely, and not in the form we now have them, he did write everything that he is there said to have written. For example: the Volume of the Law, which is contained, it seems, in chapter 11 of Deuteronomy and the chapters following, up to chapter 27 — the same law that was commanded to be written on stones at their entry into the land of Canaan (Deuteronomy 31:9). This Moses himself wrote and delivered to the priests and elders of Israel, to be read every seventh year to all Israel when they assembled at the Feast of Tabernacles. This is the law God commanded their kings to copy from the priests and Levites — once they had established that form of government — and to lay beside the Ark (Deuteronomy 31:26). And it is the same law that, after being lost, was found again long afterward by Hilkiah and sent to King Josiah, who had it read to the people and so renewed the covenant between God and them (2 Kings 22:8; 23:1-3).

The Book of Joshua Was Written After His Time

That the Book of Joshua was also written long after the time of Joshua can be gathered from many passages in the book itself. Joshua had set up twelve stones in the middle of the Jordan as a monument of their crossing (Joshua 4:9), and of these the writer says, "They are there unto this day" (Joshua 5:9) — and "unto this day" is a phrase that signifies a time past, beyond living memory. In the same way, when the Lord said that he had rolled the reproach of Egypt off the people, the writer says, "The place is called Gilgal unto this day" — which would have been an odd thing to say in Joshua's own lifetime. So also with the name of the Valley of Achor, named for the trouble that Achan stirred up in the camp (Joshua 7:26): the writer says the name "remaineth unto this day," which must therefore be long after the time of Joshua. There are many other arguments of this kind, such as Joshua 8:29, 13:13, 14:14, and 15:63.

The Books of Judges and Ruth Were Written Long After — Indeed After the Captivity

The same is plain from similar arguments in the Book of Judges — chapters 1:21, 1:26, 6:24, 10:4, 15:19, 17:6 — and in Ruth 1:1. But the clearest of all is Judges 18:30, where it is said that Jonathan "and his sonnes were Priests to the Tribe of Dan, untill the day of the captivity of the land."

The Same for the Books of Samuel

That the Books of Samuel were likewise written after his own time, there are arguments of the same kind: 1 Samuel 5:5, 7:13, 7:15, 27:6, and 30:25, where, after David had ruled that those who guarded the supplies should share the spoils equally with those who fought, the writer says, "He made it a Statute and an Ordinance to Israel to this day." Again (2 Samuel 6:4), when David — displeased that the Lord had struck down Uzzah for putting out his hand to steady the Ark — called the place Perez-Uzzah, the writer says it is called so "to this day." The writing of that book, then, must come long after the time of the events; that is, long after the time of David.

The Books of the Kings and the Chronicles

As for the two Books of the Kings and the two Books of the Chronicles: besides the passages mentioning monuments that, as the writer says, remained until his own days — such as 1 Kings 9:13, 9:21, 10:12, 12:19; 2 Kings 2:22, 8:22, 10:27, 14:7, 16:6, 17:23, 17:34, 17:41; 1 Chronicles 4:41, 5:26 — there is a sufficient argument that they were written after the captivity in Babylon: their history is carried right up to that time. For the deeds recorded in a register are always more ancient than any book that mentions and quotes that register — and these books do exactly that in many places, referring the reader to the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah, to the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel, to the books of the prophet Samuel, of the prophet Nathan, of the prophet Ahijah, to the Vision of Jehdo, and to the books of the prophet Serveiah and of the prophet Addo.

Ezra and Nehemiah

The Books of Esdras — that is, Ezra — and Nehemiah were certainly written after the return from captivity, because they contain the return itself, the rebuilding of the walls and houses of Jerusalem, the renewal of the covenant, and the reordering of their government.

Esther

The history of Queen Esther belongs to the time of the captivity; the writer, therefore, must have lived at that time or after it.

Job

The Book of Job carries no mark of the time it was written. And though it is clear enough (Ezekiel 14:14; James 5:11) that Job was no invented person, the book itself seems to be not a history but a treatise on a question much disputed in ancient times: why wicked men have often prospered in this world while good men have suffered. The strongest evidence is this: from the beginning of the book to the third verse of the third chapter, where Job's complaint begins, the Hebrew is in prose (as St. Jerome testifies); from there to the sixth verse of the last chapter, it is in hexameter verse; and the rest of that final chapter is prose again. So the whole dispute is in verse, and the prose is added only as a preface at the beginning and an epilogue at the end. And verse is not the usual style of people in great pain, like Job, or of people who come to comfort them, like his friends — but it was common in philosophy, especially moral philosophy, in ancient times.

The Psalter

The Psalms were written for the most part by David, for the use of the choir. To these were added some songs of Moses and other holy men, and some written after the return from the captivity — such as Psalm 137 and Psalm 126. From this it is plain that the Psalter was compiled, and put into the form it now has, after the return of the Jews from Babylon.

The Proverbs

The Proverbs are a collection of wise and godly sayings — partly Solomon's, partly those of Agur the son of Jakeh, and partly those of the mother of King Lemuel. It is hardly likely that Solomon collected them rather than Agur or Lemuel's mother. The sayings are theirs; but the collecting and compiling of them into this one book was the work of some other godly man who lived after them all.

Ecclesiastes and the Song of Songs

The Books of Ecclesiastes and the Canticles — the Song of Songs — contain nothing that was not Solomon's, except the titles or inscriptions. For "The Words of the Preacher, the Son of David, King in Jerusalem," and "the Song of Songs, which is Solomon's," seem to have been added for the sake of distinguishing the books later, when the books of Scripture were gathered into one body of the law — so that not only the doctrine but the authors too would be on record.

The Prophets

Of the prophets, the most ancient are Zephaniah, Jonah, Amos, Hosea, Isaiah, and Micah, who lived in the time of Amaziah and Azariah — also called Uzziah — kings of Judah. But the Book of Jonah is not properly a record of his prophecy — his whole prophecy comes down to these few words: "Fourty dayes and Ninivy shall be destroyed" — but rather a history, a narrative, of his stubbornness in disputing God's commands. So it is hardly likely that Jonah is its author, since he is its subject. The Book of Amos, on the other hand, is his prophecy.

Jeremiah, Obadiah, Nahum, and Habakkuk prophesied in the time of Josiah.

Ezekiel, Daniel, Haggai, and Zechariah prophesied during the captivity.

When Joel and Malachi prophesied is not evident from their writings. But considering the inscriptions, or titles, of their books, this much is plain enough: the whole Scripture of the Old Testament was set forth in the form we now have it after the return of the Jews from their captivity in Babylon, and before the time of Ptolemy Philadelphus, who had it translated into Greek by the seventy men sent to him out of Judea for that purpose.

And if the books of the Apocrypha — which the Church commends to us not as canonical, but as profitable for our instruction — may be believed on this point, then Scripture was set forth in its present form by Esdras, that is, Ezra. This appears from what he himself says in the second book (2 Esdras 14:21-22 and following), where, speaking to God, he says: "Thy law is burnt; therefore no man knoweth the things which thou hast done, or the works that are to begin. But if I have found Grace before thee, send down the holy Spirit into me, and I shall write all that hath been done in the world, since the beginning, which were written in thy Law, that men may find thy path, and that they which will live in the later days, may live." And at verse 45: "And it came to passe when the forty dayes were fulfilled, that the Highest spake, saying, 'The first that thou hast written, publish openly, that the worthy and unworthy may read it; but keep the seventy last, that thou mayst deliver them onely to such as be wise among the people.'" And so much for the time when the books of the Old Testament were written.

The New Testament

The writers of the New Testament all lived within less than a generation after Christ's ascension, and all of them had seen our Savior or been his disciples — except St. Paul and St. Luke. Consequently, whatever they wrote is as ancient as the time of the apostles themselves. But the time when the books of the New Testament were received and acknowledged by the Church as their writing is not nearly so ancient. For just as the books of the Old Testament come down to us from no earlier a time than that of Ezra — who, by the direction of God's Spirit, restored them when they were lost — so the books of the New Testament, of which the copies were few and could not easily all be in any one private man's hands, cannot be traced back to any earlier time than when the governors of the Church collected, approved, and recommended them to us as the writings of those apostles and disciples whose names they bear.

The first complete list of all the books of both the Old and New Testaments appears in the Canons of the Apostles, supposedly collected by Clement, the first Bishop of Rome after St. Peter. But because that attribution is only supposed, and questioned by many, the Council of Laodicea is the first we know of that recommended the Bible to the Christian churches of the day as the writings of the prophets and apostles — and that council was held in the year 364 after Christ.

By that time, ambition had so far gotten the better of the great doctors of the Church that they no longer regarded emperors, even Christian ones, as the shepherds of the people, but as sheep — and non-Christian emperors as wolves. They worked to pass off their doctrine not as counsel and information, the way preachers should, but as laws, the way absolute governors do; and they thought pious any fraud that tended to make people more obedient to Christian doctrine. Yet even so, I am persuaded they did not falsify the Scriptures. The copies of the New Testament books were in the hands of the churchmen alone — and if they had intended to corrupt them, they would surely have made them more favorable to their own power over Christian princes and civil sovereignty than they actually are. I see no reason, therefore, to doubt that the Old and New Testaments, as we now have them, are the true registers of the things done and said by the prophets and apostles.

And the same may be true of some of the books called Apocrypha — if they were left out of the canon not because their doctrine conflicts with the rest, but only because they are not found in Hebrew. For after the conquest of Asia by Alexander the Great, there were few learned Jews who were not fluent in Greek. The seventy interpreters who turned the Bible into Greek were all Hebrews; and we still have the works of Philo and Josephus, both Jews, written by them eloquently in Greek. But in the end, it is not the writer that makes a book canonical — it is the authority of the Church.

Their Scope

Although these books were written by many different men, it is plain that the writers were all filled with one and the same Spirit, because they all work together toward one and the same end: setting forth the rights of the kingdom of God — Father, Son, and Holy Ghost.

The Book of Genesis traces the genealogy of God's people from the creation of the world to the going into Egypt. The other four books of Moses contain the election of God as their king and the laws he prescribed for their government. The books of Joshua, Judges, Ruth, and Samuel, up to the time of Saul, describe the acts of God's people until the time they cast off God's yoke and demanded a king, after the manner of their neighbor nations. The rest of the Old Testament history traces the succession of the line of David down to the captivity — the line from which would spring the restorer of the kingdom of God, our blessed Savior, God the Son, whose coming was foretold in the books of the prophets. After him, the evangelists wrote his life and actions and his claim to the kingdom during the time he lived on earth. And lastly, the Acts and the Epistles of the apostles declare the coming of God the Holy Ghost, and the authority he left with the apostles and their successors, for the direction of the Jews and the invitation of the Gentiles.

In sum: the histories and prophecies of the Old Testament, and the Gospels and Epistles of the New, have all had one and the same scope — to convert men to the obedience of God: first, in Moses and the priests; second, in the man Christ; and third, in the apostles and the successors to apostolic power. For these three, at different times, represented the person of God: Moses and his successors, the high priests and kings of Judah, in the Old Testament; Christ himself, during the time he lived on earth; and the apostles and their successors, from the day of Pentecost (when the Holy Ghost descended on them) to this day.

The Question of the Authority of the Scriptures, Properly Stated

There is a question much disputed among the various sects of the Christian religion: from where do the Scriptures derive their authority? The question is sometimes put in other terms — how do we know them to be the word of God? or, why do we believe them to be so? And the difficulty of answering it comes mainly from the sloppy wording of the question itself.

For it is believed on all hands that the first and original author of the Scriptures is God; so that is not the point in dispute. Again, it is obvious that no one can know they are God's word — though all true Christians believe it — except those to whom God himself has revealed it supernaturally; so the question is wrongly framed as a question of our knowledge. Lastly, if the question is framed as one of our belief, then — since some are moved to believe for one reason and others for other reasons — no single general answer can serve for them all.

The question, truly stated, is this: by what authority are they made law?

Their Authority and Interpretation

So far as the Scriptures do not differ from the laws of nature, there is no doubt that they are the law of God, and they carry their authority with them, legible to every man who has the use of natural reason. But this is no other authority than that of all other moral doctrine consonant with reason — whose dictates are laws not made, but eternal.

If they are made law by God himself, then they are of the nature of written law, and written laws are laws only to those to whom God has published them sufficiently — so sufficiently that no man can excuse himself by saying he did not know they were his.

Therefore, a man to whom God has not supernaturally revealed that these writings are his, nor that those who published them were sent by him, is not obliged to obey them by any authority except the authority of him whose commands already have the force of laws — that is to say, by no other authority than that of the commonwealth, residing in the sovereign, who alone has the legislative power.

Consider the alternative. If it is not the legislative authority of the commonwealth that gives the Scriptures the force of laws, it must be some other authority derived from God, either private or public.

If private, it obliges only the particular man to whom God has been pleased to reveal it. For suppose every man were obliged to accept as God's law whatever particular men, claiming private inspiration or revelation, chose to press upon him — and consider how many men there are who, out of pride and ignorance, take their own dreams, wild fancies, and madness for testimonies of God's Spirit, or who, out of ambition, claim such divine testimony falsely and against their own consciences. It would be impossible for any divine law to be acknowledged at all.

If public, then it is the authority either of the commonwealth or of the Church. But the Church, if it is one person, is the same thing as a commonwealth of Christians — called a commonwealth because it consists of men united in one person, their sovereign, and called a Church because it consists of Christian men united in one Christian sovereign. And if the Church is not one person, then it has no authority at all. It can neither command nor perform any action whatsoever; it is incapable of having any power or right to anything; it has no will, no reason, no voice — for all these qualities belong to a person. So if the whole number of Christians is not contained in one commonwealth, they are not one person, and there is no universal Church with any authority over them; and therefore the Scriptures are not made laws by a universal Church. And if Christendom is one commonwealth, then all Christian monarchs and states are private persons, subject to being judged, deposed, and punished by a universal sovereign of all Christendom.

So the question of the authority of the Scriptures comes down to this: whether Christian kings, and the sovereign assemblies in Christian commonwealths, are absolute in their own territories, immediately under God — or subject to one Vicar of Christ, set over the universal Church, to be judged, condemned, deposed, and put to death as he judges expedient or necessary for the common good.

This question cannot be resolved without a more particular consideration of the kingdom of God — and from the kingdom of God we must also judge the authority to interpret Scripture. For whoever has a lawful power over any writing, to make it law, has also the power to approve or disapprove any interpretation of it.

Chapter 34: What "Spirit," "Angel," and "Inspiration" Mean in the Books of Holy Scripture

How "Body" and "Spirit" Are Used in Scripture

The foundation of all true reasoning is the constant meaning of words. And in the doctrine that follows, that meaning does not depend (as in natural science) on the will of the writer, nor (as in everyday conversation) on common usage, but on the sense the words carry in Scripture. So before I go any further, I need to determine, out of the Bible itself, the meaning of certain words whose ambiguity could make whatever I build on them obscure or open to dispute. I will begin with the words BODY and SPIRIT — which in the language of the Scholastics are called "corporeal substance" and "incorporeal substance."

The word "body," in its most general sense, means that which fills or occupies some definite space, or imagined place; something that does not depend on our imagination, but is a real part of what we call the universe. For since the universe is the sum of all bodies, there is no real part of it that is not also a body, and nothing is properly a body that is not also part of that sum of all bodies, the universe. And because bodies are subject to change — that is, to appearing differently to the senses of living creatures — a body is also called a substance, meaning a subject of various properties: sometimes moving, sometimes standing still; seeming to our senses sometimes hot, sometimes cold; now one color, smell, taste, or sound, now another. This diversity of seeming, produced by the different ways bodies act on the organs of our senses, we attribute to alterations in the bodies that act on us, and we call these their "accidents" — their changing properties. Taken this way, "substance" and "body" mean the same thing. And therefore "incorporeal substance" is a pair of words that, when joined together, destroy each other — as if a man were to say "an incorporeal body."

But ordinary people do not call the whole universe "body" — only those parts of it they can detect by touch, resisting their force, or by sight, blocking their view of what lies beyond. So in common speech, air and airy substances are usually not counted as bodies. Instead, whenever people notice their effects, they call them wind, or breath, or (because the Latin word for these is spiritus) spirits — as when they call the airy substance that gives life and motion to the body of a living creature its "vital and animal spirits." As for those idols of the brain that show us bodies where no bodies are — in a mirror, in a dream, or to a fevered brain awake — they are (as the Apostle says of all idols generally) nothing. Nothing at all, I say, in the place where they seem to be; and in the brain itself, nothing but commotion, arising either from the action of external objects or from the disordered agitation of our organs of sense. People too busy to search into the causes of these things do not know, on their own, what to call them. So they are easily persuaded by those whose learning they revere: some to call them bodies, and to think them made of air compacted by supernatural power, because sight judges them to be solid; others to call them spirits, because touch finds nothing in the place where they appear to resist the fingers. So the proper meaning of "spirit" in common speech is either a subtle, fluid, invisible body, or else a ghost — some other idol or phantasm of the imagination. Beyond that, the word has many metaphorical meanings. Sometimes it stands for a disposition or inclination of the mind: the disposition to contradict what other men say is "a spirit of contradiction"; a disposition to uncleanness, "an unclean spirit"; perverseness, "a froward spirit"; sullenness, "a dumb spirit"; and an inclination to godliness and God's service, "the Spirit of God." Sometimes it stands for some exceptional ability, or extraordinary passion, or disease of the mind — as when great wisdom is called "the spirit of wisdom," and madmen are said to be "possessed with a spirit."

I find no other meaning of "spirit" anywhere. And where none of these meanings can make sense of the word in Scripture, the passage simply lies beyond human understanding, and our faith in it consists not in our opinion but in our submission — as in all the places where God is said to be a spirit, or where "the Spirit of God" means God himself. For the nature of God is incomprehensible: we understand nothing of what he is, only that he is. And therefore the attributes we give him are not meant to tell one another what he is, nor to express our opinion of his nature, but to express our desire to honor him with the names we count most honorable among ourselves.

First: "Spirit of God" Sometimes Means a Wind, or Breath
