Genesis 1:2 — "The Spirit of God moved upon the face of the waters." Here, if "the Spirit of God" meant God himself, then motion would be attributed to God, and consequently place — and motion and place are intelligible only of bodies, not of "incorporeal substances." In that case the passage is beyond our understanding; for we can conceive of nothing that moves without changing place, or that has no dimension, and whatever has dimension is body. But the meaning of these words is best understood from a similar passage, Genesis 8:1. When the earth was covered with water, as in the beginning, and God intended to make the waters recede and uncover the dry land again, he uses similar words: "I will bring my Spirit upon the earth, and the waters shall be diminished." There, "Spirit" means a wind — that is, air, or spirit, in motion — which might well be called (as in the earlier passage) the Spirit of God, because it was God's work.

Second: For Extraordinary Gifts of Understanding

Genesis 41:38 — Pharaoh calls the wisdom of Joseph "the Spirit of God." Joseph having advised him to find a wise and discreet man and set him over the land of Egypt, Pharaoh says: "Can we find such a man as this, in whom is the Spirit of God?" And in Exodus 28:3, God says, "You shall speak to all who are wise-hearted, whom I have filled with the spirit of wisdom, to make Aaron garments, to consecrate him." Here extraordinary skill — even though only in making garments — is called the Spirit of God, because it is the gift of God. The same usage appears again in Exodus 31:3-6 and 35:31. And in Isaiah 11:2-3, the prophet, speaking of the Messiah, says: "The Spirit of the Lord shall abide upon him, the spirit of wisdom and understanding, the spirit of counsel and fortitude, and the spirit of the fear of the Lord." Plainly this does not mean so many ghosts, but so many eminent graces that God would give him.

Third: For Extraordinary Passions

In the Book of Judges, an extraordinary zeal and courage in defense of God's people is called the Spirit of God — as when it stirred Othniel, Gideon, Jephthah, and Samson to deliver them from servitude (Judges 3:10, 6:34, 11:29, 13:25, 14:6, 19). And of Saul, on the news of the Ammonites' outrage against the men of Jabesh-Gilead, it is said (1 Samuel 11:6) that "the Spirit of God came upon Saul, and his anger" (or, as the Latin has it, his fury) "was kindled greatly." It is not likely that a ghost is meant here, but an extraordinary zeal to punish the cruelty of the Ammonites. In the same way, the Spirit of God that came upon Saul when he was among the prophets praising God with songs and music (1 Samuel 19:20) should be understood not as a ghost, but as an unexpected and sudden zeal to join them in their devotions.

Fourth: For the Gift of Prediction by Dreams and Visions

The false prophet Zedekiah says to Micaiah (1 Kings 22:24), "Which way did the Spirit of the Lord go from me to speak to you?" This cannot be understood of a ghost; for Micaiah had declared the outcome of the battle to the kings of Israel and Judah as something seen in a vision, not as something a spirit spoke inside him.

In the same way it appears throughout the books of the prophets that although they spoke "by the Spirit of God" — that is, by a special grace of prediction — their knowledge of the future came not from a ghost within them, but from some supernatural dream or vision.

Fifth: For Life

Genesis 2:7 says, "God made man of the dust of the earth, and breathed into his nostrils (spiraculum vitae — the breath of life) the breath of life, and man became a living soul." There, the breath of life breathed in by God means nothing more than that God gave him life. And Job 27:3, "as long as the Spirit of God is in my nostrils," means nothing more than "as long as I live." So in Ezekiel 1:20, "the spirit of life was in the wheels" is equivalent to "the wheels were alive." And in Ezekiel 2:30, "the spirit entered into me, and set me on my feet" means "I recovered my vital strength" — not that any ghost or incorporeal substance entered into his body and possessed it.

Sixth: For Subordination to Authority

In Numbers 11:17, God says, "I will take of the spirit which is upon you, and will put it upon them, and they shall bear the burden of the people with you" — meaning the seventy elders. Then two of the seventy are said to have prophesied in the camp; some complained about it, and Joshua asked Moses to forbid them, which Moses would not do. From this it appears that Joshua did not know they had received authority to prophesy — and that they prophesied according to the mind of Moses; that is, by a spirit, or authority, subordinate to his own.

In the same sense we read (Deuteronomy 34:9) that "Joshua was full of the spirit of wisdom, because Moses had laid his hands upon him" — that is, because Moses had ordained him to carry forward the work he himself had begun (bringing God's people into the promised land) but, cut short by death, could not finish.

In the same sense it is said (Romans 8:9), "If any man does not have the Spirit of Christ, he is none of his" — meaning not the ghost of Christ, but submission to his doctrine. So also 1 John 4:2: "By this you shall know the Spirit of God: every spirit that confesses that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh is of God." What is meant is the spirit of unfeigned Christianity — submission to the central article of Christian faith, that Jesus is the Christ — which cannot be interpreted as a ghost.

Likewise the words of Luke 4:1, "And Jesus, full of the Holy Ghost" (that is, as Matthew 4:1 and Mark 1:12 express it, "of the Holy Spirit"), may be understood as zeal to do the work for which he was sent by God the Father. But to interpret it as a ghost is to say that God himself (for so our Savior was) was filled with God — which is improper and meaningless. How we ever came to translate "spirits" by the word "ghosts" — a word that signifies nothing, in heaven or on earth, except the imaginary inhabitants of man's brain — I will not examine. But this I do say: the word "spirit" in the text means no such thing. It means either, properly, a real substance, or, metaphorically, some extraordinary ability or passion of the mind or of the body.

Seventh: For Airy Bodies

The disciples of Christ, seeing him walk on the sea (Matthew 14:26 and Mark 6:49), supposed him to be a spirit — meaning by that an airy body, not a phantasm. For it is said that they all saw him, which cannot be true of delusions of the brain (those are not common to many people at once, the way visible bodies are, but private to each, because everyone's imagination differs); it can be true only of bodies. The same applies where the same apostles took him for a spirit (Luke 24:3, 7). So also in Acts 12:15: when St. Peter was delivered out of prison and no one would believe it, and the servant girl said he was at the door, they said, "It is his angel." By that they must have meant a corporeal substance — or else we must say the disciples themselves followed the common opinion of both Jews and Gentiles that some such apparitions were not imaginary but real, things that did not need a man's imagination in order to exist. The Jews called these "spirits" and "angels," good or bad — just as the Greeks called the same things "demons." And some such apparitions may indeed be real and substantial: that is, subtle bodies, which God can form by the same power by which he formed all things, and can use as ministers and messengers — that is to say, angels — to declare his will and carry it out when he pleases, in an extraordinary and supernatural way. But once he has formed them, they are substances endowed with dimension; they take up room and can be moved from place to place, which is the distinctive mark of bodies. Therefore they are not incorporeal ghosts — that is, ghosts that are in no place; that is, that are nowhere; that is, that seeming to be something, are nothing. But if "corporeal" is taken in the most common sense, for substances perceptible by our external senses, then an "incorporeal substance" is something not imaginary but real: namely, a thin, invisible substance, but one that has the same dimensions found in grosser bodies.

What an Angel Is

The name ANGEL signifies, in general, a messenger — and most often, a messenger of God. And "a messenger of God" means anything that makes known his extraordinary presence: that is, an extraordinary manifestation of his power, especially by a dream or vision.

Concerning the creation of angels, Scripture delivers nothing at all. That they are spirits is often repeated; but by the name of "spirit" is meant — in Scripture and in common speech alike, among Jews and Gentiles alike — sometimes thin bodies, like the air, the wind, or the vital and animal spirits of living creatures; and sometimes the images that rise in the imagination in dreams and visions, which are not real substances but accidents of the brain. Yet when God raises such images supernaturally, to signify his will, it is not improper to call them God's messengers — that is to say, his angels.

The Gentiles commonly mistook the imagery of the brain for things really existing outside them, independent of imagination, and out of those images they built their opinions of demons, good and evil — which, because they seemed to exist on their own, they called substances, and because they could not feel them with their hands, incorporeal. In just the same way the Jews, on the same grounds — with nothing in the Old Testament compelling them to it — held the general opinion (the sect of the Sadducees excepted) that those apparitions, which it pleased God sometimes to produce in men's imaginations for his own service (and which he therefore called his angels), were substances independent of the imagination: permanent creatures of God. Those they thought favorable to them they esteemed the angels of God; those they thought would hurt them they called evil angels, or evil spirits — such as the spirit of Python, and the spirits of madmen, of lunatics, and of epileptics. For they considered anyone troubled with such diseases to be possessed by demons.

But if we examine the places in the Old Testament where angels are mentioned, we will find that in most of them nothing can be understood by the word "angel" except some image raised, supernaturally, in the imagination, to signify the presence of God in the performance of some supernatural work. And therefore in the remaining places, where the angels' nature is not spelled out, it may be understood the same way.

For we read in Genesis 16 that the same apparition is called not only an angel but God: what verse 7 calls "the angel of the Lord" says to Hagar in verse 10, "I will multiply your descendants exceedingly" — that is, it speaks in the person of God. And this apparition was not even a visible figure, but a voice. From this it is plain that "angel" there signifies nothing but God himself, who caused Hagar, supernaturally, to apprehend a supernatural voice testifying to God's special presence there. Why, then, should the angels that appeared to Lot — who are called "men" in Genesis 19:13, and whom Lot, though they were two, addresses (verse 18) as one, and that one as God (for the words are, "Lot said to them, Oh, not so, my Lord") — not be understood as images of men supernaturally formed in the imagination, just as before, by "angel," a fancied voice was understood? When the angel called to Abraham from heaven to stay his hand from slaying Isaac (Genesis 22:11), there was no apparition at all, only a voice — which was nevertheless properly enough called a messenger, or angel, of God, because it declared God's will supernaturally; and this spares us the trouble of supposing any permanent ghosts. The angels Jacob saw on the ladder of heaven (Genesis 28:12) were a vision of his sleep — therefore only imagination, and a dream; yet because they were supernatural, and signs of God's special presence, those apparitions are not improperly called angels. The same is to be understood in Genesis 31:11, where Jacob says, "The angel of the Lord appeared to me in my sleep." For an apparition made to a man in his sleep is what all men call a dream, whether the dream is natural or supernatural. And what Jacob there calls an angel was God himself; for the same angel says (verse 13), "I am the God of Bethel."

So too in Exodus 14:9: the angel that went before the army of Israel to the Red Sea, and then moved behind it, is (verse 19) the Lord himself. And he appeared not in the form of a beautiful man, but by day in the form of a pillar of cloud and by night in the form of a pillar of fire; and yet this pillar was the entire apparition — the whole angel promised to Moses (Exodus 14:9) to guide the army. For this cloudy pillar is said to have descended, and stood at the door of the Tabernacle, and talked with Moses.

There you see motion and speech — the things commonly attributed to angels — attributed to a cloud, because the cloud served as a sign of God's presence; and it was no less an angel than if it had had the form of a man, or of a child of the greatest imaginable beauty, or wings, as angels are usually painted, for the false instruction of common people. For it is not their shape but their use that makes them angels: and their use is to be signs of God's presence in supernatural operations. So when Moses (Exodus 33:14) asked God to go along with the camp, as he had always done before the making of the golden calf, God did not answer "I will go," nor "I will send an angel in my place," but this: "My presence shall go with you."

To list every place in the Old Testament where the name "angel" occurs would take too long. So, to cover them all at once, I say this: there is no text in that part of the Old Testament which the Church of England holds canonical from which we can conclude that there is, or ever has been created, any permanent thing — understood by the name of spirit or angel — that does not have quantity; that cannot be divided by the understanding, that is, considered in parts, so that one part is in one place and the next part in the next place; and, in sum, that is not (taking "body" for whatever is something, or somewhere) corporeal. In every such place, the sense will bear the interpretation of "angel" as "messenger" — just as John the Baptist is called an angel, and Christ the Angel of the Covenant; and just as (by the same analogy) the dove and the fiery tongues, being signs of God's special presence, might also be called angels. We do find in Daniel two names of angels, Gabriel and Michael. But it is clear from the text itself (Daniel 12:1) that by Michael is meant Christ — not as an angel, but as a prince; and that Gabriel (like the similar apparitions made to other holy men in their sleep) was nothing but a supernatural phantasm, by which it seemed to Daniel, in his dream, that two saints were talking, and one of them said to the other, "Gabriel, let us make this man understand his vision." For God has no need to distinguish his celestial servants by names, which are useful only to the short memories of mortals. Nor is there any place in the New Testament from which it can be proved that angels — except where the word stands for those men whom God has made the messengers and ministers of his word or works — are things both permanent and incorporeal. That they are permanent may be gathered from the words of our Savior himself (Matthew 25:41), where he says it shall be said to the wicked on the last day, "Go, you cursed, into everlasting fire, prepared for the Devil and his angels." This passage is clear evidence for the permanence of evil angels (unless we suppose that "the Devil and his angels" may be understood as the Church's adversaries and their ministers) — but then it is incompatible with their immateriality, because everlasting fire is no punishment to substances incapable of suffering, as all incorporeal things would be. Angels, therefore, are not proved incorporeal from that text. Likewise where St. Paul says (1 Corinthians 6:3), "Do you not know that we shall judge the angels?" and 2 Peter 2:4, "For if God did not spare the angels that sinned, but cast them down into hell," and Jude 1:6, "And the angels that did not keep their first estate, but left their own habitation, he has reserved in everlasting chains under darkness until the judgment of the last day" — though these prove the permanence of angelic nature, they also confirm its materiality. And Matthew 22:30: "In the resurrection men neither marry nor are given in marriage, but are as the angels of God in heaven." But in the resurrection men will be permanent and not incorporeal; so, therefore, are the angels.

There are various other passages from which the same conclusion can be drawn. To anyone who understands what the words "substance" and "incorporeal" signify — taking "incorporeal" to mean not a subtle body, but no body at all — they imply a contradiction: so that to say an angel or spirit is, in that sense, an incorporeal substance is in effect to say there is no angel or spirit at all. Considering, therefore, what the word "angel" signifies in the Old Testament, and the nature of dreams and visions that come to men in the ordinary course of nature, I was inclined to this opinion: that angels were nothing but supernatural apparitions of the imagination, raised by the special and extraordinary operation of God, to make his presence and his commandments known to mankind, and chiefly to his own people. But the many passages of the New Testament, and our Savior's own words — in texts where there is no suspicion that the Scripture has been corrupted — have extorted from my feeble reason an acknowledgment and belief that there are also angels that are substantial and permanent. But to believe they are in no place — that is to say, nowhere; that is to say, nothing — as those who insist they are incorporeal (though indirectly) do say, cannot be proved from Scripture.

What Inspiration Is

On the meaning of the word "spirit" depends the meaning of the word INSPIRATION. Taken properly, inspiration is nothing but the blowing into a man of some thin and subtle air or wind, the way a man fills a bladder with his breath. Or, if spirits are not corporeal but exist only in the imagination, then it is the blowing in of a phantasm — which is improper to say, and impossible, for phantasms do not exist; they only seem to be something. The word, therefore, is used in Scripture only metaphorically. When it is said (Genesis 2:7) that God inspired into man the breath of life, nothing more is meant than that God gave him vital motion. We are not to think that God first made a living breath and then blew it into Adam after he was made — whether that breath was real or only apparent — but only, as Acts 17:25 puts it, "that he gave him life and breath": that is, made him a living creature. And when it is said (2 Timothy 3:16) that "all Scripture is given by inspiration from God" — speaking there of the Scripture of the Old Testament — it is an easy metaphor, signifying that God inclined the spirit, or mind, of those writers to write what would be useful for teaching, reproving, correcting, and instructing men in the way of righteous living. But where St. Peter (2 Peter 1:21) says that "prophecy came not in old time by the will of man, but holy men of God spoke as they were moved by the Holy Spirit," by "the Holy Spirit" is meant the voice of God in a dream or supernatural vision — which is not inspiration. Nor, when our Savior breathed on his disciples and said, "Receive the Holy Spirit," was that breath the Spirit; it was a sign of the spiritual graces he gave to them. And though it is said of many, and of our Savior himself, that he was "full of the Holy Spirit," that fullness is not to be understood as an infusion of the substance of God, but as an accumulation of his gifts — such as the gift of holiness of life, of tongues, and the like — whether attained supernaturally or by study and hard work; for in every case they are the gifts of God. So likewise where God says (Joel 2:28), "I will pour out my Spirit upon all flesh, and your sons and your daughters shall prophesy, your old men shall dream dreams, and your young men shall see visions," we are not to take this in the literal sense, as if his Spirit were like water, subject to pouring out or pouring in; rather, it is as if God had promised to give them prophetic dreams and visions. For the strict use of the word "infused," in speaking of the graces of God, is an abuse of the word; those graces are virtues, not bodies to be carried here and there and poured into men as into barrels.

In the same way, to take "inspiration" in its literal sense — to say that good spirits entered into men to make them prophesy, or evil spirits into those who became frenzied, lunatic, or epileptic — is not to take the word as Scripture uses it. For "the Spirit" there stands for the power of God, working by causes unknown to us. So too the wind (Acts 2:2) that is said to fill the house where the apostles were assembled on the day of Pentecost is not to be understood as the Holy Spirit, which is the Deity itself, but as an external sign of God's special working on their hearts — working to produce in them the internal graces and holy virtues he thought necessary for the performance of their apostleship.

Chapter 35: What Scripture Means by "Kingdom of God," "Holy," "Sacred," and "Sacrament"

Theologians Take "Kingdom of God" Metaphorically — But Scripture Takes It Literally

When theologians write about the kingdom of God — especially in sermons and devotional books — they almost always mean one of two things. Usually they mean eternal happiness after this life, up in the highest heaven, which they also call the kingdom of glory. Sometimes they mean the down payment on that happiness — sanctification — which they call the kingdom of grace. What they never mean is a monarchy: that is, the sovereign power of God over actual subjects, acquired by those subjects' own consent. And yet that is the proper meaning of the word kingdom.

I find the opposite. In most places where Scripture uses the phrase, the KINGDOM OF GOD means a kingdom properly so called — a real one, established by the votes of the people of Israel in a special way. They chose God as their king by a covenant made with him, and God, for his part, promised them possession of the land of Canaan. Only rarely is the phrase used metaphorically, and then it means dominion over sin — and that only in the New Testament. The metaphor works because every subject will have that kind of dominion in the kingdom of God, without taking anything away from the sovereign.

From the very creation, God did not only reign over all mankind naturally, by his might. He also had special subjects, whom he commanded by a voice, the way one man speaks to another. That is how he reigned over Adam, commanding him not to eat from the tree of the knowledge of good and evil. When Adam disobeyed — when he tasted the fruit and took it upon himself to play God, judging between good and evil not by his Creator's commandment but by his own sense of things — his punishment was to lose the state of eternal life in which God had first created him. Afterward God punished Adam's descendants for their vices with a universal flood, sparing only eight persons. And in those eight, at that time, the kingdom of God consisted.

Where the Kingdom of God Began

After this, it pleased God to speak to Abraham and to make a covenant with him (Genesis 17:7-8) in these words: "I will establish my covenant between me and thee, and thy seed after thee in their generations, for an everlasting covenant, to be a God to thee, and to thy seed after thee; and I will give unto thee, and to thy seed after thee, the land wherein thou art a stranger, all the land of Canaan, for an everlasting possession." And as a memorial and a token of this covenant, God ordained (verse 11) the sacrament of circumcision.

This is what is called the Old Covenant, or Old Testament. It contains a contract between God and Abraham, by which Abraham binds himself and his descendants, in a special way, to be subject to God's positive law — the law God explicitly lays down. To the moral law he was bound already, as by an oath of allegiance. And though the name of king is not yet given to God here, nor the name of kingdom to Abraham and his seed, the thing itself is the same: an institution, by pact, of God's special sovereignty over the descendants of Abraham. When Moses renewed this same covenant at Mount Sinai, it was expressly called a peculiar kingdom of God over the Jews. And it is of Abraham — not of Moses — that St. Paul says (Romans 4:11) that he is "the father of the faithful." That is, the father of those who are loyal, and do not violate the allegiance they swore to God — sworn then by circumcision, and afterward, in the New Covenant, by baptism.

The Kingdom of God Is, Properly, His Civil Sovereignty Over a Special People, by Pact

This covenant was renewed by Moses at the foot of Mount Sinai (Exodus 19:5), where the Lord commands Moses to speak to the people this way: "If you will obey my voice indeed, and keep my covenant, then ye shall be a peculiar people to me, for all the earth is mine; and ye shall be unto me a sacerdotal kingdom, and an holy nation."

Take that phrase "peculiar people." The Latin Vulgate has peculium de cunctis populis — "a private treasure out of all the peoples." The English translation made at the start of King James's reign has "a peculiar treasure unto me above all nations," and the Geneva French has "the most precious jewel of all nations." But the truest translation is the first one, and St. Paul himself confirms it (Titus 2:14) when, alluding to this very passage, he says that our blessed Savior "gave himself for us, that he might purify us to himself, a peculiar (that is, an extraordinary) people." The Greek word there is periousios, which is commonly set against the word epiousios. Epiousios means ordinary, everyday — as in the Lord's Prayer, where it describes our "daily" bread. Periousios means the opposite: something extra, stored up, enjoyed in a special manner — what the Latins call a peculium, a private treasure. And this reading is confirmed by the reason God gives immediately afterward, when he adds, "For all the earth is mine." It is as if he had said: "All the nations of the world are mine — but not the way you are mine. They are all mine by reason of my power. But you shall be mine by your own consent and covenant." That is something added on top of his ordinary title to all nations.

The same text confirms the point again, in express words: "Ye shall be to me a sacerdotal kingdom, and an holy nation." The Vulgate has regnum sacerdotale — a priestly kingdom — which matches the translation of 1 Peter 2:9, sacerdotium regale, a royal priesthood. It also matches the institution itself, under which no man could enter the Sanctum Sanctorum — the Holy of Holies — which is to say, no man could inquire God's will directly from God himself, except the High Priest alone. The English translation mentioned above, following Geneva, has "a kingdom of priests." Either that means the succession of one High Priest after another, or it contradicts both St. Peter and the actual practice of the high priesthood. For there was never anyone but the High Priest alone whose job it was to inform the people of God's will; and no assembly of priests was ever allowed into the Sanctum Sanctorum.

The title "holy nation" confirms the same thing. For holy means what belongs to God by special right, not by general right. All the earth, as the text says, is God's; but all the earth is not called holy. Only that is holy which is set apart for his special service, as the nation of the Jews was. So this one passage is evidence enough that by the kingdom of God is properly meant a commonwealth — instituted, by the consent of those who were to be its subjects, for their civil government and the regulation of their behavior: not only toward God their king, but toward one another in matters of justice, and toward other nations in both peace and war. It was, properly, a kingdom in which God was king, and in which the High Priest, after the death of Moses, was to be his sole viceroy or lieutenant.

But many other passages clearly prove the same thing. First (1 Samuel 8:7): when the elders of Israel, fed up with the corruption of Samuel's sons, demanded a king, Samuel was displeased and prayed to the Lord. And the Lord answered him: "Hearken unto the voice of the people, for they have not rejected thee, but they have rejected me, that I should not reign over them." From this it is evident that God himself was their king at that time, and that Samuel did not command the people — he only delivered to them what God appointed him to deliver, from time to time.

Again (1 Samuel 12:12), Samuel says to the people: "When ye saw that Nahash, king of the children of Ammon, came against you, ye said unto me, Nay, but a king shall reign over us — when the Lord your God was your king." It is plain that God was their king, and governed the civil state of their commonwealth.

And after the Israelites had rejected God, the prophets foretold his restoration. Isaiah (24:23): "Then the moon shall be confounded, and the sun ashamed, when the Lord of Hosts shall reign in Mount Zion, and in Jerusalem" — where he speaks expressly of God's reign in Zion and Jerusalem; that is, on earth. And Micah (4:7): "And the Lord shall reign over them in Mount Zion" — and Mount Zion is in Jerusalem, on the earth. And Ezekiel (20:33): "As I live, saith the Lord God, surely with a mighty hand, and a stretched out arm, and with fury poured out, I will rule over you"; and (verse 37) "I will cause you to pass under the rod, and I will bring you into the bond of the covenant." That is: I will reign over you, and make you stand to the covenant you made with me through Moses — the covenant you broke in your rebellion against me in the days of Samuel, when you elected another king.

And in the New Testament, the angel Gabriel says of our Savior (Luke 1:32-33): "He shall be great, and be called the Son of the Most High, and the Lord shall give him the throne of his father David; and he shall reign over the house of Jacob for ever; and of his kingdom there shall be no end." This too is a kingdom on earth. It was for claiming this kingdom, as an enemy of Caesar, that he was put to death. The title on his cross was "Jesus of Nazareth, King of the Jews." He was crowned, in mockery, with a crown of thorns. And for proclaiming him, it is said of the disciples (Acts 17:7) "that they did all of them contrary to the decrees of Caesar, saying there was another king, one Jesus."

The kingdom of God, therefore, is a real kingdom, not a metaphorical one — and it is taken that way not only in the Old Testament but in the New. When we say, "For thine is the kingdom, the power, and the glory," we mean God's kingdom by force of our covenant, not by the right of God's power. A kingdom of the second sort God always has, so it would be pointless to pray "Thy kingdom come" — unless we mean the restoration of that kingdom of God which Christ will bring, the kingdom that was interrupted when the Israelites revolted and elected Saul. Nor would it have made sense to announce "The kingdom of heaven is at hand," or to pray "Thy kingdom come," if that kingdom had still been standing.

There are so many other passages confirming this interpretation that it would be a wonder no more notice is taken of it — except that it sheds too much light on the right of Christian kings to govern the church, and certain people have noticed that. That is why, instead of "a sacerdotal kingdom," they translate "a kingdom of priests" — by the same logic they might translate St. Peter's "royal priesthood" into "a priesthood of kings." And where the text has "a peculiar people," they put "a precious jewel" or "treasure" — by which standard a man might as well call a general's special regiment, or his own company, the general's precious jewel, or his treasure.

In short: the kingdom of God is a civil kingdom. It consisted, first, in the obligation of the people of Israel to the laws Moses would bring them from Mount Sinai — and which afterward the High Priest of the day was to deliver to them from before the cherubim in the Sanctum Sanctorum. This kingdom was cast off in the election of Saul. The prophets foretold it would be restored by Christ. Its restoration is what we pray for daily when we say in the Lord's Prayer, "Thy kingdom come." Its rightful claim is what we acknowledge when we add, "For thine is the kingdom, the power, and the glory, for ever and ever, Amen." Its proclamation was the preaching of the apostles; and men are prepared for it by the teachers of the Gospel. To embrace that Gospel — that is, to promise obedience to God's government — is to be in the kingdom of grace, because God has freely (gratis) given to such people the power to be subjects — that is, children — of God hereafter, when Christ comes in majesty to judge the world and actually to govern his own people. And that is what is called the kingdom of glory.

If the kingdom of God (also called the kingdom of heaven, for the gloriousness and astonishing height of that throne) were not a kingdom that God exercised on earth, through his lieutenants or vicars who deliver his commandments to the people — then there would never have been so much contention and war over the question of who it is through whom God speaks to us. No priest would have troubled himself with "spiritual jurisdiction," and no king would have bothered to deny it to him.

From this literal interpretation of the kingdom of God arises the true interpretation of the word HOLY. It is the word which, in God's kingdom, answers to what men in their own kingdoms call public, or the king's.

The king of any country is the public person, the representative of all his subjects. And God, the king of Israel, was the Holy One of Israel. The nation that is subject to one earthly sovereign is the nation of that sovereign — that is, of the public person. So the Jews, who were God's nation, were called (Exodus 19:6) "a holy nation." By holy is always understood either God himself, or that which is God's own property; just as by public is always meant either the person of the commonwealth itself, or something that belongs to the commonwealth in such a way that no private person can claim any property in it.

That is why the Sabbath — God's day — is a holy day; the Temple — God's house — a holy house; sacrifices, tithes, and offerings — God's tribute — holy duties; priests, prophets, and anointed kings under Christ — God's ministers — holy men; the celestial ministering spirits — God's messengers — holy angels; and so on. Wherever the word holy is taken properly, there is always something implied about ownership, acquired by consent. When we say "Hallowed be thy name," we are simply praying to God for grace to keep the first commandment: to have no other gods but him. Mankind is God's nation by right of ownership — but only the Jews were a holy nation. Why? Because they became his property by covenant.

What "Sacred" Means

The word profane is usually used in Scripture to mean the same as common; and so their opposites — holy and proper, one's own — must, in the kingdom of God, mean the same as each other too. Figuratively, it is true, men are also called holy who led such godly lives that they seemed to have abandoned all worldly ambitions and devoted themselves entirely to God. But in the proper sense, that which is made holy by God's appropriating it or setting it apart for his own use is said to be sanctified by God — like the seventh day in the fourth commandment, or like the elect in the New Testament, who were said to be sanctified when they were endowed with the spirit of godliness. And that which is made holy by the dedication of men — given to God so that it may be used only in his public service — is called SACRED, and is said to be consecrated: temples and other houses of public prayer, and their utensils; priests and ministers; victims, offerings, and the physical materials of the sacraments.

Degrees of Holiness

There are degrees of holiness. For of the things set apart for the service of God, some may be set apart again, for a nearer and more special service. The whole nation of the Israelites was a people holy to God; yet within Israel, the tribe of Levi was a holy tribe; among the Levites, the priests were holier still; and among the priests, the High Priest was the most holy. In the same way, the land of Judea was the Holy Land; but the holy city where God was to be worshipped was more holy; the Temple more holy than the city; and the Sanctum Sanctorum more holy than the rest of the Temple.

Sacrament

A SACRAMENT is the separation of some visible thing from common use, and its consecration to God's service, as a sign — either of our admission into the kingdom of God, to be counted among his peculiar people, or of the commemoration of that admission. In the Old Testament, the sign of admission was circumcision; in the New Testament, baptism. The commemoration of it in the Old Testament was the eating, at a fixed yearly time, of the paschal lamb, which reminded the people of the night they were delivered from their bondage in Egypt. In the New Testament it is the celebration of the Lord's Supper, which reminds us of our deliverance from the bondage of sin by our blessed Savior's death on the cross.

The sacraments of admission are used only once, because a person needs to be admitted only once. But because we need to be reminded often of our deliverance and of our allegiance, the sacraments of commemoration must be repeated. These are the principal sacraments — they are, as it were, the solemn oaths we swear of our allegiance. There are other consecrations that may be called sacraments, insofar as the word implies nothing more than consecration to God's service. But insofar as the word implies an oath, or promise of allegiance to God, there were no others in the Old Testament but circumcision and the Passover; nor are there any others in the New Testament but baptism and the Lord's Supper.

Chapter 36: The Word of God, and Prophets

What "Word" Means

When Scripture speaks of the word of God, or the word of man, it does not mean a part of speech — what grammarians call a noun or a verb, or any single sound that signifies nothing until it is strung together with other words. It means a complete speech or discourse, by which the speaker affirms, denies, commands, promises, threatens, wishes, or asks. In this sense the right Latin term is not vocabulum — a single word — but sermo (in Greek, logos): a speech, a discourse, a saying.

Both the Words Spoken by God and the Words Concerning God Are Called God's Word in Scripture

Notice, too, that "the word of God" (or of man) can point in two different directions. Sometimes it points to the speaker — the words that God has spoken, or that a man has spoken. In this sense, when we say "the Gospel of St. Matthew," we mean that Matthew wrote it. But sometimes it points to the subject. When we read in the Bible of "the words of the days of the kings of Israel, or Judah," the meaning is that the acts done in those days were the subject of those words.

And in the Greek of the Scriptures, which keeps many Hebrew turns of phrase, "the word of God" very often means not what is spoken by God, but what is spoken concerning God and his government — that is, the doctrine of religion. So much so that logos Theou — the word of God — and theologia — theology — come to the same thing: the doctrine we usually call divinity. The following passages make this plain.

In Acts 13:46, "Then Paul and Barnabas waxed bold, and said, It was necessary that the word of God should first have been spoken to you, but seeing you put it from you, and judge yourselves unworthy of everlasting life, lo, we turn to the Gentiles." What is here called the word of God was the doctrine of the Christian religion, as the context before it makes evident. And in Acts 5:20, an angel tells the apostles, "Go stand and speak in the Temple, all the words of this life." By "the words of this life" is meant the doctrine of the Gospel — as is clear from what they actually did in the Temple, expressed in the last verse of that same chapter: "Daily in the Temple, and in every house, they ceased not to teach and preach Christ Jesus." Plainly, Jesus Christ was the subject of this "word of life" — or, what is the same thing, the subject of the words of that eternal life which our Savior offered them.

Likewise in Acts 15:7 the word of God is called the word of the Gospel, because it contains the doctrine of the kingdom of Christ. The same word is called in Romans 10:8-9 the word of faith — that is, as the passage itself spells out, the doctrine of Christ come, and raised from the dead. Again, Matthew 13:19: "When anyone heareth the word of the kingdom" — that is, the doctrine of the kingdom taught by Christ. And the same word is said in Acts 12:24 "to grow and to be multiplied" — which is easy to understand of the gospel doctrine, but hard and strange to understand of the voice or speech of God. In exactly the same sense, "the doctrine of devils" (1 Timothy 4:1) does not mean words spoken by any devil, but the doctrine of the pagans concerning demons, and those phantasms which they worshipped as gods.

Considering these two senses of the WORD OF GOD as Scripture uses it, one thing is clear: in the second sense — where it means the doctrine of the Christian religion — the whole of Scripture is the word of God. In the first sense, it is not. For example, the words "I am the Lord thy God," and so on to the end of the Ten Commandments, were spoken by God to Moses; but the preface — "God spake these words and said" — must be understood as the words of the man who wrote the holy history.

The word of God taken as that which God has spoken is itself understood sometimes properly, sometimes metaphorically. Properly: the words he has spoken to his prophets. Metaphorically: his wisdom, power, and eternal decree in making the world. In this sense those fiats of Genesis 1 — "Let there be light," "Let there be a firmament," "Let us make man" — are the word of God. In the same sense it is said in John 1:3, "All things were made by it, and without it was nothing made that was made." And Hebrews 1:3, "He upholdeth all things by the word of his power" — that is, by the power of his word; that is, by his power. And Hebrews 11:3, "The worlds were framed by the word of God" — and many other places to the same effect. The Romans used the word the same way: their name Fate properly signifies "the word spoken," and carries this very sense.

Second, for the Effect of His Word

Secondly, "the word of God" is sometimes put for the effect of his word — that is, for the thing itself which his word affirmed, commanded, threatened, or promised. So in Psalm 105:19, Joseph is said to have been kept in prison "till his word was come": that is, until the thing came to pass which he had foretold to Pharaoh's butler (Genesis 40:13) concerning his restoration to office. "His word was come" means the thing itself had come to pass. So also in 1 Kings 18:36, Elijah says to God, "I have done all these thy words," instead of "I have done all these things at thy word," or command. And Jeremiah 17:15, "Where is the word of the Lord?" is put for "Where is the evil he threatened?" And Ezekiel 12:28, "There shall none of my words be prolonged any more" — where by "words" are understood those things which God promised to his people. And in the New Testament, Matthew 24:35: "Heaven and earth shall pass away, but my words shall not pass away" — that is, there is nothing I have promised or foretold that shall not come to pass.

It is in this sense that St. John the Evangelist — and, I think, St. John only — calls our Savior himself, as he was in the flesh, the word of God. As in John 1:14, "the Word was made flesh": that is to say, the word, or promise, that Christ should come into the world. He "in the beginning was with God" — that is, it was in the purpose of God the Father to send God the Son into the world, to light men's way to eternal life; but that purpose was not put into execution, not actually made flesh, until then. So our Savior is there called "the Word" not because he was the promise, but because he was the thing promised.

Those who seize on this passage to call him "the Verb of God" only make the text more obscure. They might as well call him the Noun of God. By "noun," as by "verb," men understand nothing but a part of speech — a voice, a sound — that neither affirms, nor denies, nor commands, nor promises; it is no substance at all, bodily or spiritual, and therefore cannot be said to be either God or man. Our Savior is both.

This Word, which St. John in his Gospel says was with God, is in his first Epistle (verse 1) called "the Word of life," and in verse 2, "the eternal life, which was with the Father." So he can be called the Word in no other sense than that in which he is called eternal life — that is, "he that hath procured us eternal life" by his coming in the flesh. So also in Revelation 19:13, where the apostle, describing Christ clothed in a garment dipped in blood, says his name is "the Word of God." This is to be understood as if he had said his name was "He that was come according to the purpose of God from the beginning, and according to his word and promises delivered by the prophets." There is nothing here about the incarnation of a word, but about the incarnation of God the Son — called the Word because his incarnation was the performance of the promise. In just the same way the Holy Ghost is called "the Promise" (Acts 1:4; Luke 24:49).

Third, for the Words of Reason and Equity

There are also places in Scripture where "the word of God" signifies words that agree with reason and equity, even though they were spoken neither by a prophet nor by a holy man. Pharaoh Necho was an idolater; yet his words to the good King Josiah, sent by messengers to advise him not to oppose his march against Carchemish, are said to have proceeded from the mouth of God — and Josiah, not listening to them, was killed in the battle. You can read it in 2 Chronicles 35:21-23. It is true that the same story, as told in the first book of Esdras, has not Pharaoh but Jeremiah speaking these words to Josiah from the mouth of the Lord. But we are to credit the canonical Scripture, whatever may be written in the Apocrypha.

The word of God is likewise to be taken for the dictates of reason and equity whenever Scripture says that word is written in man's heart — as in Psalm 36:31, Jeremiah 31:33, Deuteronomy 30:11 and 14, and many similar places.

The Different Meanings of the Word "Prophet"

The name PROPHET signifies in Scripture sometimes a prolocutor — a spokesman, one who speaks from God to man, or from man to God. Sometimes it signifies a praedictor — a foreteller of things to come. And sometimes it signifies one who speaks incoherently, like a man out of his mind.

It is most frequently used in the sense of speaking from God to the people. In this sense Moses, Samuel, Elijah, Isaiah, Jeremiah, and others were prophets. In this sense the High Priest was a prophet, for he alone went into the Sanctum Sanctorum — the Holy of Holies — to inquire of God, and was to declare God's answer to the people. That is why, when Caiaphas said it was expedient that one man should die for the people, St. John says (John 11:51) that "he spake not this of himself, but being High Priest that year, he prophesied that one man should die for the nation." Those who taught the people in Christian congregations are likewise said to prophesy (1 Corinthians 14:3). And it is in this same sense that God says to Moses concerning Aaron (Exodus 4:16), "He shall be thy spokesman to the people; and he shall be to thee a mouth, and thou shalt be to him instead of God." What is "spokesman" here is in Exodus 7:1 rendered "prophet": "See," says God, "I have made thee a god to Pharaoh, and Aaron thy brother shall be thy prophet."

In the sense of speaking from man to God, Abraham is called a prophet in Genesis 20:7, where God speaks to Abimelech in a dream: "Now therefore restore the man his wife, for he is a prophet, and shall pray for thee." From this we may also gather that the name of prophet may be given, not improperly, to those in Christian churches whose calling is to say public prayers for the congregation.

In the same sense, the prophets who came down from the high place — the hill of God — with a psaltery, a tabret, a pipe, and a harp (1 Samuel 10:5-6), with Saul among them (verse 10), are said to prophesy: they were praising God, publicly, in that manner. In the same sense Miriam is called a prophetess (Exodus 15:20). And it is to be taken the same way in 1 Corinthians 11:4-5, where St. Paul speaks of "every man that prayeth or prophesieth with his head covered" and "every woman that prayeth or prophesieth with her head uncovered." Prophecy in that place means nothing more than praising God in psalms and holy songs — which women might do in church, though it was not lawful for them to speak to the congregation.

And it is in this signification that the poets of the pagans, who composed hymns and other poems in honor of their gods, were called vates — prophets — as anyone versed in the books of the gentiles knows well, and as is evident in Titus 1:12, where St. Paul says of the Cretans that "a prophet of their own said, they were liars." Not that St. Paul held their poets to be prophets; he merely acknowledges that the word "prophet" was commonly used for those who celebrated the honor of God in verse.

Predicting the Future Is Not Always Prophecy

When prophecy means prediction — the foretelling of future contingents — then the name covered not only those who were God's spokesmen and foretold to others what God had foretold to them, but also all those impostors who pretend, by the help of familiar spirits, or by superstitious divination from past events and false causes, to foretell similar events in time to come. Of these (as I have already shown in chapter 12 of this discourse) there are many kinds — men who gain more reputation for prophecy among the common sort by one chance event that can be twisted to their purpose than they can ever lose by any number of failures.

Prophecy is not an art; nor, when it is taken for prediction, is it a permanent vocation. It is an extraordinary and temporary employment from God — most often of good men, but sometimes also of the wicked. The woman of Endor, who is said to have had a familiar spirit and by it to have raised a phantasm of Samuel and foretold Saul his death, was not on that account a prophetess. She possessed no science by which she could raise such a phantasm; nor does it appear that God commanded the raising of it. God only guided that imposture to be a means of Saul's terror and discouragement — and, in consequence, of the defeat by which he fell.

As for incoherent speech: among the gentiles it was taken for one sort of prophecy, because the prophets of their oracles — intoxicated by a spirit, or vapor, from the cave of the Pythian oracle at Delphi — were for the time genuinely mad, and spoke like madmen. Out of their loose words a meaning could be made to fit any event whatsoever — the way all bodies are said to be made of materia prima, first matter, which fits anything because it is nothing in particular. And I find incoherent speech taken for prophecy in Scripture as well (1 Samuel 18:10): "And the evil spirit came upon Saul, and he prophesied in the midst of the house."

How God Has Spoken to the Prophets

Among so many significations of the word "prophet" in Scripture, the most frequent is this: one to whom God speaks immediately — directly — telling him what he is to say from God to some other man, or to the people. And here a question naturally arises: in what manner does God speak to such a prophet? Can it properly be said (some may ask) that God has voice and language, when it cannot properly be said that he has a tongue, or other organs, as a man does?

The prophet David argues, "Shall he that made the eye, not see? Or he that made the ear, not hear?" But this kind of speech is meant, not (as usually supposed) to describe God's nature, but to declare our intention to honor him. To see and to hear are honorable attributes, and may be given to God to declare — as far as our capacity can conceive it — his almighty power. But if the argument were taken in its strict and proper sense, then from God's making of all the parts of man's body one could conclude that he also uses them as we use them — and many of those uses are so unseemly that to attribute them to him would be the greatest insult in the world.

Therefore, when we read that God spoke to men immediately, we are to understand it of whatever way it may be by which God makes them understand his will. And the ways by which he does this are many, and to be sought only in Holy Scripture — where, although it is often said simply that God spoke to this or that person without declaring how, there are also many places that record the signs by which the prophets were to recognize his presence and his command. From these we may understand how he spoke to many of the rest.

To the Extraordinary Prophets of the Old Testament He Spoke by Dreams or Visions

In what manner God spoke to Adam and Eve, and to Cain, and to Noah, is not expressed; nor how he spoke to Abraham, until Abraham had come out of his own country to Sichem in the land of Canaan. Then (Genesis 12:7) God is said to have appeared to him. So there is one way by which God made his presence manifest: by an apparition, or vision. And again (Genesis 15:1), "The word of the Lord came to Abraham in a vision" — that is to say, something appeared as a sign of God's presence, as God's messenger, to speak to him.

Again, the Lord appeared to Abraham (Genesis 18:1) by an apparition of three angels; and to Abimelech (Genesis 20:3) in a dream; to Lot (Genesis 19:1) by an apparition of two angels; and to Hagar (Genesis 21:17) by the apparition of one angel; and to Abraham again (Genesis 22:11) by the apparition of a voice from heaven; and to Isaac in the night (Genesis 26:24) — that is, in his sleep, or by dream; and to Jacob in a dream (Genesis 28:12) — that is to say, as the text itself puts it, "Jacob dreamed that he saw a ladder"; and again (Genesis 32:1) in a vision of angels; and to Moses (Exodus 3:2) in the apparition of a flame of fire out of the midst of a bush. And after the time of Moses — wherever the Old Testament expresses the manner in which God spoke immediately to a man — he spoke always by a vision or by a dream: so to Gideon, Samuel, Elijah, Elisha, Isaiah, Ezekiel, and the rest of the prophets. And often in the New Testament too: to Joseph, to St. Peter, to St. Paul, and to St. John the Evangelist in the Apocalypse.

Only to Moses did he speak in a more extraordinary manner, on Mount Sinai and in the Tabernacle; and to the High Priest, in the Tabernacle and in the Holy of Holies of the Temple. Moses, and after him the High Priests, were prophets of a more eminent place and degree in God's favor; and God himself declares in express words that to other prophets he spoke in dreams and visions, but to his servant Moses in the way a man speaks to his friend. The words are these (Numbers 12:6-8): "If there be a prophet among you, I the Lord will make myself known to him in a vision, and will speak unto him in a dream. My servant Moses is not so, who is faithful in all my house; with him I will speak mouth to mouth, even apparently, not in dark speeches; and the similitude of the Lord shall he behold." And Exodus 33:11: "The Lord spake to Moses face to face, as a man speaketh to his friend."

And yet even this speaking of God to Moses was by the mediation of an angel, or angels — as appears expressly from Acts 7:35 and 53, and from Galatians 3:19 — and was therefore a vision, though a clearer vision than was given to other prophets. Consistent with this, when God says (Deuteronomy 13:1), "If there arise amongst you a prophet, or dreamer of dreams," the second phrase is only an interpretation of the first. And in Joel 2:28 — "Your sons and your daughters shall prophesy; your old men shall dream dreams, and your young men shall see visions" — the word "prophesy" is again expounded by dream and vision. It was in the same manner that God spoke to Solomon, promising him wisdom, riches, and honor; for the text says (1 Kings 3:15), "And Solomon awoke, and behold, it was a dream."

So, in general, the extraordinary prophets of the Old Testament took notice of the word of God in no other way than from their dreams or visions — that is to say, from the imaginations they had in their sleep, or in an ecstasy. In every true prophet these imaginations were supernatural; in false prophets they were either natural, or faked.

The same prophets were nevertheless said to speak "by the Spirit" — as in Zechariah 7:12, where the prophet, speaking of the Jews, says, "They made their hearts hard as adamant, lest they should hear the law, and the words which the Lord of Hosts hath sent in his Spirit by the former prophets." From this it is clear that speaking by the Spirit, or by inspiration, was not some particular manner of God's speaking, distinct from vision — not when those said to speak by the Spirit were extraordinary prophets, who for every new message needed a particular new commission, which is to say a new dream or vision.

To the Supreme Prophets of Perpetual Calling, God Spoke from the Mercy Seat, in a Manner Not Expressed in Scripture

Of prophets who held the office by a perpetual calling in the Old Testament, some were supreme and some subordinate. Supreme was first Moses, and after him the High Priests, each for his own time, as long as the priesthood was royal. And after the people of the Jews had rejected God, that he should no more reign over them, those kings who submitted themselves to God's government were also his chief prophets, and the High Priest's office became ministerial — an office of service. When God was to be consulted, the priests put on the holy vestments and inquired of the Lord as the king commanded them, and they were removed from office when the king thought fit.

For King Saul (1 Samuel 13:9) commanded the burnt offering to be brought; and (1 Samuel 14:18) he commands the priest to bring the Ark near him — and then (verse 19) to let it alone, because he saw an advantage over his enemies. And in the same chapter, Saul asks counsel of God. In like manner King David, after his anointing, though before he had possession of the kingdom, is said to "enquire of the Lord" (1 Samuel 23:2) whether he should fight the Philistines at Keilah; and (verse 10) David commands the priest to bring him the ephod, to inquire whether he should stay in Keilah or not. And King Solomon (1 Kings 2:27) took the priesthood from Abiathar and gave it (verse 35) to Zadok.

Therefore Moses, and the High Priests, and the pious kings — who inquired of God on all extraordinary occasions how they were to conduct themselves, or what outcome they should expect — were all sovereign prophets. But in what manner God spoke to them is not manifest. To say that when Moses went up to God on Mount Sinai it was a dream, or a vision, such as other prophets had, contradicts the distinction God himself drew between Moses and all other prophets (Numbers 12:6-8). To say God spoke or appeared as he is in his own nature is to deny his infiniteness, invisibility, and incomprehensibility. To say he spoke by inspiration, or by the infusion of the Holy Spirit — taking "Holy Spirit" to mean the Deity itself — is to make Moses equal with Christ, in whom alone (as St. Paul says, Colossians 2:9) the Godhead dwells bodily. And finally, to say he spoke by the Holy Spirit in the sense of the graces or gifts of the Holy Spirit is to attribute nothing supernatural to Moses at all; for God disposes men to piety, justice, mercy, truth, faith, and every sort of virtue, moral and intellectual, by doctrine, by example, and by many natural and ordinary occasions.

And as none of these ways can be applied to God's speaking to Moses at Mount Sinai, so none of them can be applied to his speaking to the High Priests from the Mercy Seat. Therefore, in what manner God spoke to those sovereign prophets of the Old Testament, whose office it was to inquire of him, is simply not intelligible. In the time of the New Testament there was no sovereign prophet but our Savior — who was both the God that spoke and the prophet to whom he spoke.

To Subordinate Prophets of Perpetual Calling, God Spoke by the Spirit

As for subordinate prophets of perpetual calling, I find no passage proving that God spoke to them supernaturally — only in the way he naturally inclines men to piety, to belief, to righteousness, and to the other virtues, as he does all other Christian men. That way works through constitution, instruction, education, and the occasions and invitations men have toward Christian virtue; yet it is truly attributed to the operation of the Spirit of God, or Holy Spirit (which in our language we call the Holy Ghost). For there is no good inclination that is not of the operation of God. But these operations are not always supernatural. So when a prophet is said to speak in the Spirit, or by the Spirit of God, we are to understand no more than this: that he speaks according to God's will as declared by the supreme prophet. For the most common acceptation of the word "spirit" is a man's intention, mind, or disposition.

In the time of Moses there were seventy men, besides himself, who prophesied in the camp of the Israelites. In what manner God spoke to them is declared in Numbers 11:25: "The Lord came down in a cloud, and spake unto Moses, and took of the Spirit that was upon him, and gave it to the seventy elders. And it came to pass, when the Spirit rested upon them, they prophesied, and did not cease."

From this, two things are manifest. First, that their prophesying to the people was subservient and subordinate to the prophesying of Moses; for God took of the Spirit of Moses to put upon them, so that they prophesied as Moses would have them — otherwise they would not have been allowed to prophesy at all. Indeed, a complaint was made against them to Moses (verse 27), and Joshua wanted Moses to forbid them — which he did not do, but said to Joshua, "Be not jealous on my behalf."

Second, that the Spirit of God in that place signifies nothing but the mind and disposition to obey and assist Moses in the administration of the government. For if it meant they had the substantial Spirit of God — the divine nature itself — breathed into them, then they had it in no less measure than Christ himself, in whom alone the Spirit of God dwelt bodily. It is meant, therefore, of the gift and grace of God that guided them to cooperate with Moses — from whom their spirit was derived. And it appears (verse 16) that they were men whom Moses himself was to appoint as elders and officers of the people; for the words are, "Gather unto me seventy men, whom thou knowest to be elders and officers of the people" — where "thou knowest" is the same as "thou appointest," or "hast appointed to be such." For we are told earlier (Exodus 18) that Moses, following the counsel of Jethro his father-in-law, appointed judges and officers over the people, men who feared God; and out of these came those seventy, whom God, by putting Moses' spirit upon them, inclined to aid Moses in the administration of the kingdom.

It is in this same sense that the Spirit of God is said (1 Samuel 16:13-14), immediately upon the anointing of David, to have come upon David and departed from Saul: God giving his graces to the man he chose to govern his people, and taking them away from the man he rejected. So "the Spirit" means an inclination to God's service — not any supernatural revelation.

God Sometimes Also Spoke by Lots

God also spoke many times by the outcome of lots — lots ordered by those he had put in authority over his people. So we read that God revealed, by the lots which Saul caused to be drawn (1 Samuel 14:43), the fault Jonathan had committed in eating a honeycomb, contrary to the oath the people had taken. And (Joshua 18:10) God divided the land of Canaan among the Israelites by "the lots that Joshua did cast before the Lord in Shiloh." In the same manner, it seems, God exposed the crime of Achan (Joshua 7:16 and following). These, then, are the ways by which God declared his will in the Old Testament.

And he used all the same ways in the New Testament. To the Virgin Mary, by a vision of an angel; to Joseph, in a dream; to Paul, on the road to Damascus, in a vision of our Savior; to Peter, in the vision of a sheet let down from heaven with all sorts of flesh, of clean and unclean beasts — and, in prison, by the vision of an angel; to all the apostles and writers of the New Testament, by the graces of his Spirit; and to the apostles again, at the choosing of Matthias in the place of Judas Iscariot, by lot.

Every Man Ought to Examine the Probability of a Claimed Prophet's Calling

All prophecy, then, supposes a vision or a dream (and these two, when they are natural, are the same thing), or some special gift of God so rarely observed in mankind that it is a thing to marvel at where it does appear. And such gifts, like the most extraordinary dreams and visions, may proceed from God not only by his supernatural and immediate operation, but also by his natural operation, through the mediation of second causes. So reason and judgment are needed to discern between natural and supernatural gifts, and between natural and supernatural visions or dreams.

Consequently, men need to be very circumspect and wary about obeying the voice of a man who, claiming to be a prophet, requires us to obey God in the way that he, in God's name, tells us is the way to happiness. For he who pretends to teach men the way to such great felicity pretends to govern them — that is, to rule and reign over them — which is a thing all men naturally desire, and which is therefore rightly suspected of ambition and imposture. Such a claim ought to be examined and tested by every man before he yields obedience to it — unless he has yielded that obedience already, in the institution of a commonwealth, as when the prophet is the civil sovereign, or is authorized by the civil sovereign.

If this examination of prophets and spirits were not permitted to every one of the people, it would have been pointless to set out the marks by which every man might distinguish between those he ought to follow and those he ought not. Such marks are set out — in Deuteronomy 13:1 and following, to know a prophet by, and in 1 John 4:1 and following, to know a spirit by. And seeing there is so much prophesying in the Old Testament, and so much preaching against prophets in the New, and ordinarily a far greater number of false prophets than of true ones, everyone must beware of obeying their directions — at his own peril.

First, that there were many more false prophets than true appears from this: when Ahab consulted four hundred prophets (1 Kings 22), they were all lying impostors, except the one man Micaiah. And a little before the time of the Captivity, the prophets were liars generally. "The prophets," says the Lord through Jeremiah (14:14), "prophesy lies in my name. I sent them not, neither have I commanded them, nor spake unto them; they prophesy to you a false vision, a thing of naught, and the deceit of their heart." So far did it go that God commanded the people, by the mouth of the prophet Jeremiah (23:16), not to obey them: "Thus saith the Lord of Hosts, hearken not unto the words of the prophets, that prophesy to you. They make you vain; they speak a vision of their own heart, and not out of the mouth of the Lord."

All Prophecy but the Sovereign Prophet's Is to Be Examined by Every Subject

In the time of the Old Testament, then, there were quarrels among the visionary prophets — one contesting with another, demanding, "When did the Spirit depart from me, to go to thee?" as between Micaiah and the rest of the four hundred — and prophets flatly calling one another liars (as in Jeremiah 14:14). And there are just such controversies in the New Testament's terms today, among the "spiritual" prophets of our own time. Every man, then as now, is bound to make use of his natural reason, and to apply to all prophecy the rules God has given us for discerning the true from the false.

Of these rules, in the Old Testament there were two: first, that the doctrine conform to what Moses, the sovereign prophet, had taught them; and second, the miraculous power of foretelling what God would bring to pass — both together, as I have already shown from Deuteronomy 13:1 and following. In the New Testament there was one mark only: the preaching of this doctrine, that JESUS IS THE CHRIST — that is, the King of the Jews promised in the Old Testament. Whoever denied that article was a false prophet, whatever miracles he might seem to work; and he who taught it was a true prophet.

For St. John (1 John 4:2 and following), speaking expressly of the means to examine spirits, whether they are of God or not, after telling them that false prophets would arise, says this: "Hereby know ye the Spirit of God. Every spirit that confesseth that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh, is of God" — that is, is approved and allowed as a prophet of God. Not that he is therefore a godly man, or one of the elect, merely because he confesses, professes, or preaches Jesus to be the Christ — but he is an avowed prophet. For God sometimes speaks by prophets whose persons he has not accepted: as he did by Balaam, and as he foretold Saul of his death by the witch of Endor. And again, in the next verse: "Every spirit that confesseth not that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh, is not of Christ. And this is the spirit of Antichrist." So the rule is complete on both sides: he is a true prophet who preaches the Messiah already come, in the person of Jesus; and he is a false one who denies him come, and looks for him in some future impostor who will falsely claim that honor — the impostor whom the apostle there properly calls Antichrist.

Every man therefore ought to consider who is the sovereign prophet — that is, who is God's viceregent on earth, holding, next under God, the authority to govern Christian men — and to take for his rule the doctrine which that sovereign prophet has, in the name of God, commanded to be taught; and by that rule to examine and test the truth of whatever doctrines pretended prophets, with miracles or without, shall at any time put forward. If he finds a doctrine contrary to that rule, he should do as those men did who came to Moses and complained that there were some prophesying in the camp whose authority to do so they doubted — leave it to the sovereign, as they left it to Moses, to uphold or to forbid them, as he shall see cause. If the sovereign disavows them, obey their voice no more; if he approves them, obey them as men to whom God has given a part of the spirit of their sovereign.

For when Christian men do not take their Christian sovereign for God's prophet, one of two things must follow. Either they must take their own dreams for the prophecy they mean to be governed by, and the swelling of their own hearts for the Spirit of God; or they must let themselves be led by some foreign prince, or by some of their fellow subjects — men who can bewitch them into rebellion by slandering the government, with no miracle to confirm their calling beyond the occasional extraordinary success, and impunity. And by this means, destroying all laws, both divine and human, they reduce all order, all government, and all society to the first chaos of violence and civil war.

Chapter 37: Of Miracles, and Their Use

A Miracle Is a Work That Causes Wonder

By miracles we mean the admirable works of God — which is why they are also called wonders. And because they are usually performed to signal what God commands, in situations where people would otherwise doubt (following their own private natural reasoning) what he has commanded and what he has not, Scripture commonly calls them signs — in the same sense the Latins called them ostenta and portenta, words meaning "showings" and "foreshadowings" of what the Almighty is about to bring to pass.

A Miracle Must Be Rare, with No Known Natural Cause

So to understand what a miracle is, we first have to understand what kinds of works make people wonder and call them admirable. There are only two things that make anyone wonder at an event. The first is that it is strange — that is, nothing like it has ever been produced, or only very rarely. The second is that when it is produced, we cannot imagine it was done by natural means, but only by the immediate hand of God. But the moment we see some possible natural cause for a thing, however rarely the like has been done, we stop wondering. And if the like has been done often, however impossible it is to imagine a natural means for it, we likewise stop wondering, and no longer count it a miracle.

So if a horse or a cow were to speak, that would be a miracle — because the thing is strange, and a natural cause is hard to imagine. The same goes for seeing some strange deviation of nature produce a living creature of an entirely new shape. But when a man, or any other animal, fathers offspring like himself, that is no miracle — even though we understand how it happens no better than the other case — simply because it is usual. In the same way, if a man were transformed into a stone or a pillar, it would be a miracle, because it is strange. But if a piece of wood is changed into stone, that is no miracle, because we see it often. And yet we know no more by what operation of God the one is brought to pass than the other.

The first rainbow ever seen in the world was a miracle, because it was the first — and therefore strange — and it served as a sign from God, placed in heaven to assure his people that the world would never again be universally destroyed by water. But today, because rainbows are frequent, they are not miracles — not to those who know their natural causes, and not to those who don't. Likewise, there are many rare works produced by human skill and craft. But when we know they were made by men — because then we also know the means by which they were made — we do not count them as miracles, since they were not wrought by the immediate hand of God but through the mediation of human industry.

What Seems a Miracle to One Man May Not to Another

Furthermore, since admiration and wonder depend on the knowledge and experience a person happens to have — some more, some less — it follows that the same thing may be a miracle to one man and not to another. That is why ignorant and superstitious people make great wonders out of works that other people don't marvel at in the least, knowing they proceed from nature (which is not the immediate, but the ordinary work of God). Eclipses of the sun and moon have been taken for supernatural works by the common people — even while there were others who, from their natural causes, could predict the very hour they would arrive. Or take the man who, by conspiracy and secret information-gathering, learns the private doings of some ignorant, unwary person, and then tells him what he did in times past. To the victim it seems a miraculous thing. But among wise and wary men, "miracles" like that are not so easily performed.

The Purpose of Miracles

Again, it belongs to the very nature of a miracle that it be wrought to procure credit for God's messengers, ministers, and prophets — so that people may know they are called, sent, and employed by God, and so be more inclined to obey them. That is why the creation of the world, and after it the destruction of every living creature in the universal flood, are not usually called miracles, admirable works though they were: they were not done to procure credit for any prophet or other minister of God. For however admirable a work may be, the wonder does not lie in the fact that it could be done — people naturally believe the Almighty can do all things — but in the fact that he does it at the prayer or word of a man.

The works of God in Egypt, done by the hand of Moses, were properly miracles, because they were done with the intention of making the people of Israel believe that Moses came to them not from any scheme of his own self-interest, but as one sent from God. After God had commanded him to deliver the Israelites from Egyptian bondage, Moses said (Exodus 4:1), "They will not believe me, but will say, the Lord has not appeared to me." So God gave him power to turn the rod in his hand into a serpent and back into a rod again; and by putting his hand into his bosom, to make it leprous, and by pulling it out again, to make it whole — all to make the children of Israel believe (as it says in verse 5) that the God of their fathers had appeared to him. And if that were not enough, God gave him power to turn their waters into blood. And when he had done these miracles before the people, it is said (verse 31) that "they believed him." Nevertheless, out of fear of Pharaoh, they did not yet dare to obey him. So the other works, done to plague Pharaoh and the Egyptians, all tended to make the Israelites believe in Moses — and were properly miracles.

In the same way, if we consider all the miracles done by the hand of Moses and all the rest of the prophets up to the Captivity, and those of our Savior and his apostles afterward, we will find that their purpose was always to create or confirm belief that these men did not come on their own initiative, but were sent by God.

We can observe something further in Scripture: the purpose of miracles was to produce belief not universally, in all men, elect and reprobate alike, but in the elect only — that is, in those whom God had determined should become his subjects. The miraculous plagues of Egypt were not aimed at converting Pharaoh; God had told Moses beforehand that he would harden Pharaoh's heart so that he would not let the people go. And when Pharaoh finally did let them go, it was not the miracles that persuaded him — it was the plagues that forced him. So too with our Savior. It is written (Matthew 13:58) that he did not perform many miracles in his own country because of their unbelief; and in Mark 6:5, instead of "he did not perform many," it reads "he could perform none." This was not because he lacked the power — to say that would be blasphemy against God. Nor was it because the purpose of miracles was something other than converting unbelievers to Christ; the purpose of all the miracles of Moses, the prophets, our Savior, and his apostles was to add men to the Church. It was because the purpose of their miracles was to add to the Church not all men, but those who should be saved — that is, those whom God had elected. Since our Savior was sent from his Father, he could not use his power to convert those whom his Father had rejected.
