As for the commentators on this passage of St. Mark who say that his phrase "he could not" stands for "he would not" — they do so without a single example in the Greek language (where "would not" is sometimes put for "could not" in inanimate things that have no will, but "could not" for "would not," never). And by doing so they lay a stumbling block before weak Christians, as if Christ could do miracles only among the credulous.

The Definition of a Miracle

From what I have set down here about the nature and use of a miracle, we may define it this way: a MIRACLE is a work of God (apart from his operation by the way of nature, ordained in the creation), done to make manifest to his elect the mission of an extraordinary minister for their salvation.

From this definition we may draw two conclusions. First: in all miracles, the work done is not the effect of any power in the prophet, because it is the effect of the immediate hand of God — that is to say, God has done it without using the prophet as a subordinate cause.

Second: no devil, angel, or other created spirit can do a miracle. For such a being would have to do it either by virtue of some natural science, or by incantation — that is, by the power of words. If enchanters do it by a power of their own, independent of God, then there is some power that does not proceed from God — which all men deny. And if they do it by a power given to them, then the work is not from the immediate hand of God, but natural — and consequently no miracle.

There are some texts of Scripture that seem to attribute the power of working wonders — equal to some of those immediate miracles wrought by God himself — to certain arts of magic and incantation. For example, we read that after the rod of Moses, cast on the ground, became a serpent, "the magicians of Egypt did the like by their enchantments" (Exodus 7:11); and that after Moses had turned the waters of the Egyptian streams, rivers, ponds, and pools into blood, "the magicians of Egypt did so likewise, with their enchantments" (Exodus 7:22); and that after Moses had, by the power of God, brought frogs upon the land, "the magicians also did so with their enchantments, and brought up frogs upon the land of Egypt" (Exodus 8:7). Wouldn't a man be apt to attribute miracles to enchantments — that is, to the efficacy of the sound of words — and think it well proved from these and similar passages? And yet there is no passage of Scripture anywhere that tells us what an enchantment actually is. If, therefore, enchantment is not what many take it to be — a working of strange effects by spells and words — but rather imposture and delusion, wrought by ordinary means, and so far from supernatural that the impostors need no study even of natural causes, but only the ordinary ignorance, stupidity, and superstition of mankind to pull it off — then those texts that seem to support the power of magic, witchcraft, and enchantment must have a different sense than the one they seem to carry at first sight.

Men Are Easily Deceived by False Miracles

For it is plain enough that words have no effect except on those who understand them — and then their only effect is to signify the intentions or passions of the speaker, and thereby produce hope, fear, or other passions or ideas in the hearer. So when a rod seems to become a serpent, or water seems to become blood, or any other miracle seems to be done by enchantment — if it is not for the edification of God's people, then it is not the rod, nor the water, nor anything else that has been enchanted. That is to say, the thing worked upon by the words is not the object. It is the spectator. The whole "miracle" consists in this: the enchanter has deceived a man. And that is no miracle at all. It is a very easy thing to do.

For such is the ignorance and proneness to error of all men generally — and especially of those who know little about natural causes, or about the nature and interests of other men — that they can be taken in by countless easy tricks. Before it was known that there is a science of the course of the stars, imagine what reputation for miraculous power a man might have gained by telling people, "At this hour, on this day, the sun will go dark." A juggler, by his handling of cups and other trinkets, would be thought to do his wonders by the power of the devil at the very least, if his act were not common practice today. A man who has trained himself to speak by drawing in his breath (the kind of men the ancients called ventriloqui — "belly-speakers"), making the weakness of his voice seem to come not from the weak push of his speech organs but from far away, can make a great many people believe it is a voice from heaven, saying whatever he pleases to tell them. And for a crafty man who has dug into the secrets and private confessions that one person ordinarily makes to another about his past actions and adventures, it is no hard matter to repeat them back — and yet there are many who, by exactly such means, gain the reputation of being conjurers.

It would take too long to catalogue all the kinds of men the Greeks called thaumaturgi — workers of wonders — and yet everything they do, they do by their own simple dexterity. And if we look at the impostures wrought by confederacy — by accomplices working together — there is nothing, however impossible to do, that is impossible to be believed. Two men conspiring, one to seem lame and the other to cure him with a charm, will deceive many. But many men conspiring — one to seem lame, another to "cure" him, and all the rest to bear witness — will deceive many more.

Precautions Against the Imposture of Miracles

Given this readiness of mankind to give over-hasty belief to pretended miracles, there can be no better caution — and I think no other — than the one God has prescribed: first through Moses, as I said in the previous chapter, at the beginning of Deuteronomy 13 and the end of Deuteronomy 18. We are to take no one for a prophet who teaches any other religion than the one established by God's lieutenant (which at that time was Moses); nor anyone — even if he teaches the same religion — whose prediction we do not see come to pass. So Moses in his time, and Aaron and his successors in theirs, and the sovereign governor of God's people, next under God himself — that is, the head of the Church in every age — are to be consulted about what doctrine has been established, before we give credit to a pretended miracle or prophet.

And once that is done, when men claim something is a miracle, we must both see it done and use every possible means to consider whether it was really done — and not only that, but whether it is the kind of thing no man could do by his natural power, so that it requires the immediate hand of God. And in this too we must have recourse to God's lieutenant, to whom we have submitted our private judgments in all doubtful cases. For example: suppose a man claims that after certain words are spoken over a piece of bread, God has instantly made it not bread, but a god, or a man, or both — and yet it still looks exactly as much like bread as it ever did. There is no reason for anyone to think this has really been done, nor consequently to fear the man who claims it, until he inquires of God, through his vicar or lieutenant, whether it has been done or not. If the lieutenant says no, then what Moses said follows (Deuteronomy 18:22): "he has spoken it presumptuously; you shall not fear him." If the lieutenant says it is done, then the man is not to contradict it.

Likewise, if we do not see a miracle but only hear tell of one, we are to consult the lawful Church — that is, its lawful head — about how far to credit those who report it. And this is chiefly the situation of people who, in these days, live under Christian sovereigns. For miracles have now ceased: in these times I do not know of one man who ever saw any wondrous work, done by the charm or word or prayer of a man, that anyone with even a moderate share of reason would think supernatural. The question now is no longer whether what we see done is a miracle, or whether the miracle we hear or read of was a real work and not merely the act of a tongue or pen — but, in plain terms, whether the report is true or a lie.

In that question, we are not to make our own private reason or conscience the judge, but the public reason — that is, the reason of God's supreme lieutenant. And in fact we have made him judge already, if we have given him sovereign power to do everything necessary for our peace and defense. A private man always has the liberty — because thought is free — to believe or not believe in his heart those acts that have been put forward as miracles. He may weigh what benefit would flow, from people's belief in them, to those who claim or promote them, and from that conjecture whether they are miracles or lies. But in forming that judgment, we are not to renounce our senses and experience, nor our natural reason — that undoubted word of God. When it comes to public confession of the faith, however, private reason must submit to the public reason — that is, to God's lieutenant. But who this lieutenant of God and head of the Church is, we shall consider in its proper place hereafter.

Chapter 38: What Scripture Means by Eternal Life, Hell, Salvation, the World to Come, and Redemption

Civil society depends on justice, and justice depends on the power of life and death — along with the lesser rewards and punishments — which resides in those who hold the sovereignty of the commonwealth. It is therefore impossible for a commonwealth to stand where anyone other than the sovereign has the power to give rewards greater than life, or to inflict punishments greater than death. Now, eternal life is a greater reward than this present life, and eternal torment is a greater punishment than natural death. So for everyone who wants to avoid the calamities of confusion and civil war by obeying authority, it is well worth considering carefully what Holy Scripture means by eternal life and eternal torment; for what offenses, committed against whom, men are to be eternally tormented; and for what actions they are to obtain eternal life.

Where Adam Would Have Lived Forever, Had He Not Sinned: The Earthly Paradise

First, we find that Adam was created in such a condition of life that, had he not broken God's commandment, he would have enjoyed it in the paradise of Eden everlastingly. For the tree of life was there, and he was allowed to eat from it for as long as he refrained from eating from the tree of knowledge of good and evil, which was forbidden to him. And so, as soon as he had eaten from it, God thrust him out of paradise, "lest he put forth his hand, and take also of the tree of life, and live forever" (Genesis 3:22).

From this it seems to me — with submission, nevertheless, in this and in all questions whose resolution depends on Scripture, to the interpretation of the Bible authorized by the commonwealth whose subject I am — that if Adam had not sinned, he would have had an eternal life on earth, and that mortality entered into him and his descendants through his first sin. Not that actual death entered then; for in that case Adam could never have had children, whereas in fact he lived long afterward and saw numerous descendants before he died. But where it is said, "In the day that you eat of it, you shall surely die," the meaning must be his mortality — the certainty of death.

Since eternal life was lost by Adam's forfeiture in committing sin, whoever was to cancel that forfeiture would thereby recover that life again. Now, Jesus Christ has satisfied for the sins of all who believe in him, and has therefore recovered for all believers that ETERNAL LIFE which was lost by the sin of Adam. And it is in this sense that St. Paul's comparison holds (Romans 5:18-19): "As by the offense of one, judgment came upon all men to condemnation, even so by the righteousness of one, the free gift came upon all men to justification of life." The same point is delivered even more clearly in these words (1 Corinthians 15:21-22): "For since by man came death, by man came also the resurrection of the dead. For as in Adam all die, even so in Christ shall all be made alive."

Texts on Where Believers Will Enjoy Eternal Life

Concerning the place where men shall enjoy that eternal life which Christ has obtained for them, the texts just cited seem to put it on earth. For if, as in Adam, all die — that is, all have forfeited paradise and eternal life on earth — even so, in Christ, all shall be made alive; then all men shall be made to live on earth, for otherwise the comparison would not hold.

The Psalmist seems to agree (Psalm 133:3): "Upon Zion God commanded the blessing, even life for evermore" — and Zion is in Jerusalem, on earth. So does St. John (Revelation 2:7): "To him that overcomes I will give to eat of the tree of life, which is in the midst of the paradise of God." That was the tree of Adam's eternal life — and his life was to have been on earth. The same seems confirmed again by St. John (Revelation 21:2), where he says, "I John saw the Holy City, New Jerusalem, coming down from God out of heaven, prepared as a bride adorned for her husband" — and again at verse 10 to the same effect. As if he were saying: the new Jerusalem, the paradise of God, at the second coming of Christ, will come down to God's people from heaven; they will not go up to it from earth.

This matches exactly what the two men in white clothing — that is, the two angels — said to the apostles as they watched Christ ascend (Acts 1:11): "This same Jesus, who is taken up from you into heaven, shall so come, as you have seen him go up into heaven." Which sounds as if they had said: he will come down to govern them, under his Father, eternally here — not take them up to govern them in heaven. And this agrees with the restoration of the kingdom of God instituted under Moses, which was a political government of the Jews on earth.

Again, our Savior's saying (Matthew 22:30) "that in the resurrection they neither marry, nor are given in marriage, but are as the angels of God in heaven" is a description of an eternal life resembling the one we lost in Adam, on the point of marriage. For since Adam and Eve, had they not sinned, would have lived on earth eternally in their individual persons, it is obvious they would not have gone on procreating forever. If immortals reproduced the way mankind does now, the earth in a short time could not have offered them so much as a place to stand. The Jews who asked our Savior whose wife the woman who had married many brothers would be in the resurrection did not understand the consequences of immortality: that there will be no generation, and consequently no marriage — any more than there is marriage or generation among the angels.

The comparison between the eternal life Adam lost and the eternal life our Savior recovered by his victory over death holds in this point too: just as Adam lost eternal life by his sin and yet lived on for a time afterward, so the faithful Christian has recovered eternal life through Christ's passion, even though he dies a natural death and remains dead for a time — namely, until the resurrection. For just as death is reckoned from Adam's condemnation, not from its execution, so life is reckoned from the absolution, not from the resurrection, of those who are elected in Christ.

Ascension into Heaven

That the place where men are to live eternally after the resurrection is the heavens — meaning by heaven those parts of the world most remote from earth, where the stars are, or above the stars in another, higher heaven called caelum empyreum, the "empyrean heaven" (of which there is no mention in Scripture, nor any ground in reason) — is not easily drawn from any text I can find.

By the kingdom of heaven is meant the kingdom of the King who dwells in heaven; and his kingdom was the people of Israel, whom he ruled through the prophets, his lieutenants — first Moses, and after him Eleazar and the sovereign priests — until in the days of Samuel they rebelled and demanded a mortal man for their king, after the manner of other nations. And when our Savior Christ, through the preaching of his ministers, shall have persuaded the Jews to return and called the Gentiles to his obedience, then there shall be a new kingdom of heaven — because our King will then be God, whose throne is heaven — without any necessity, evident in Scripture, that man shall ascend to his happiness any higher than God's footstool, the earth.

On the contrary, we find it written (John 3:13) that "no man has ascended into heaven, but he that came down from heaven, even the Son of man, that is in heaven." And I observe in passing that these words are not, like the ones immediately before them, the words of our Savior, but of St. John himself — for Christ was at that time not in heaven, but on earth. The same is said of David (Acts 2:34), where St. Peter, in order to prove the ascension of Christ, takes the words of the Psalmist (Psalm 16:10), "You will not leave my soul in hell, nor suffer your Holy One to see corruption," and says they were spoken not of David but of Christ — and to prove it, adds this reason: "For David is not ascended into heaven."

But to this a man may easily answer: though their bodies were not to ascend until the general day of judgment, their souls were in heaven as soon as they departed from their bodies. And this seems to be confirmed by the words of our Savior (Luke 20:37-38), who, proving the resurrection out of the words of Moses, says this: "That the dead are raised, even Moses showed, at the bush, when he calls the Lord the God of Abraham, and the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob. For he is not a God of the dead, but of the living; for they all live to him." But if these words are to be understood only of the immortality of the soul, they do not prove at all what our Savior intended to prove — which was the resurrection of the body, that is to say, the immortality of the man. Therefore our Savior means that those patriarchs were immortal not by some property that follows from the essence and nature of mankind, but by the will of God, who was pleased, of his mere grace, to bestow eternal life on the faithful. And though at that time the patriarchs and many other faithful men were dead, yet — as the text says — they lived to God; that is, they were written in the Book of Life with those who were absolved of their sins and ordained to eternal life at the resurrection.

That the soul of man is by its own nature eternal, a living creature independent of the body — or that any mere man is immortal otherwise than by the resurrection on the last day (Enoch and Elijah excepted) — is a doctrine not apparent in Scripture. The whole of chapter 14 of Job, which is the speech not of his friends but of Job himself, is a complaint about this mortality of our nature — and yet no contradiction of immortality at the resurrection. "There is hope of a tree," he says at verse 7, "if it be cast down. Though the root of it grows old, and the stock of it dies in the ground, yet when it scents the water it will bud, and bring forth boughs like a plant. But man dies, and wastes away; yes, man gives up the ghost, and where is he?" And at verse 12: "man lies down, and rises not, till the heavens be no more."

But when is it that the heavens shall be no more? St. Peter tells us: at the general resurrection. For in his second epistle, chapter 3, verse 7, he says that "the heavens and the earth that are now, are reserved unto fire against the day of judgment, and perdition of ungodly men"; and at verse 12, "looking for, and hastening to the coming of God, wherein the heavens shall be on fire, and shall be dissolved, and the elements shall melt with fervent heat. Nevertheless, we according to the promise look for new heavens, and a new earth, wherein dwells righteousness." So when Job says man does not rise until the heavens are no more, it is the same as if he had said: the immortal life (and soul and life in Scripture usually signify the same thing) does not begin in man until the resurrection and day of judgment, and its cause is not his specific nature and generation, but the promise. For St. Peter does not say, "We look for new heavens and a new earth from nature," but "from promise."

Lastly: it has already been proved, from many clear places of Scripture, in chapter 35 of this book, that the kingdom of God is a civil commonwealth, in which God himself is sovereign — by virtue first of the Old Covenant and then of the New — and in which he reigns by his vicar, or lieutenant. Those same places therefore also prove that after the second coming of our Savior in his majesty and glory, to reign actually and eternally, the kingdom of God is to be on earth. But because this doctrine, though proved out of places of Scripture neither few nor obscure, will strike most men as a novelty, I do no more than propose it. I maintain nothing in this, or in any other paradox of religion. I am waiting for the end of that dispute of the sword — not yet decided among my countrymen — concerning the authority by which all sorts of doctrine are to be approved or rejected, and whose commands, in speech and in writing, whatever the opinions of private men, must be obeyed by everyone who means to be protected by their laws. For the points of doctrine concerning the kingdom of God have so great an influence on the kingdom of man that they are not to be determined by anyone except those who, under God, hold the sovereign power.

The Place, After Judgment, of Those Who Were Never in the Kingdom of God, or Who Were Cast Out of It

Just as the kingdom of God and eternal life have their place on earth, so also, by the Scripture, do God's enemies and their torments after judgment. The name of the place where all men remain until the resurrection — those who were buried, or swallowed up by the earth — is usually given in Scripture by words that signify under ground; which the Latins generally read as infernus and inferni, and the Greeks as Hades, that is to say, a place where men cannot see. It includes the grave as well as any other, deeper place.

But as for the place of the damned after the resurrection, neither the Old Testament nor the New fixes it by any marker of location — only by the company. It shall be where those wicked men were whom God, in former times, destroyed from the face of the earth in extraordinary and miraculous ways. For example, the damned are said to be in inferno, in Tartarus, or in the bottomless pit, because Korah, Dathan, and Abiram were swallowed up alive into the earth. Not that the writers of Scripture would have us believe there could be, in the globe of the earth — which is not only finite but, compared to the height of the stars, of no considerable size at all — a pit without a bottom, a hole of infinite depth, such as the Greeks in their demonology (that is, their doctrine concerning demons), and the Romans after them, called Tartarus. Of it Virgil says:

    Bis patet in praeceps, tantum tenditque sub umbras,
    Quantus ad aethereum coeli suspectus Olympum

— "it gapes sheer downward into the shadows twice as far as the upward gaze from earth to heavenly Olympus." The proportion of earth to heaven simply cannot bear such a thing. Rather, we should believe the damned are there, indefinitely, where those men are on whom God inflicted that exemplary punishment.

The Congregation of Giants

Again: those mighty men of the earth who lived in the time of Noah, before the flood — whom the Greeks called heroes and the Scripture giants, and who, both agree, were begotten by copulation of the children of God with the children of men — were destroyed for their wicked lives by the general deluge. The place of the damned is therefore also sometimes marked out by the company of those dead giants. So Proverbs 21:16: "The man that wanders out of the way of understanding shall remain in the congregation of the giants." And Job 26:5: "Behold the giants groan under water, and they that dwell with them." Here the place of the damned is under the water. And Isaiah 14:9: "Hell is troubled how to meet thee" — that is, the king of Babylon — "and will displace the giants for thee." Here again the place of the damned, if the sense is literal, is under water.

The Lake of Fire

Thirdly: because the cities of Sodom and Gomorrah were consumed for their wickedness, by the extraordinary wrath of God, with fire and brimstone, and the country around them turned with them into a stinking, bituminous lake, the place of the damned is sometimes expressed by fire, and by a fiery lake. So in the Apocalypse (Revelation 21:8): "But the fearful, unbelieving, and abominable, and murderers, and whoremongers, and sorcerers, and idolaters, and all liars, shall have their part in the lake that burns with fire and brimstone; which is the second death."

So it is plain that hell fire, which is expressed here by metaphor from the real fire of Sodom, does not signify any particular kind or place of torment, but is to be taken indefinitely, for destruction — as in chapter 20, verse 14 (Revelation 20:14), where it is said that "Death and Hell were cast into the lake of fire"; that is to say, they were abolished and destroyed. As if to say: after the day of judgment there shall be no more dying, and no more going into hell — that is, no more going to Hades (from which word, perhaps, our word hell is derived) — which is the same as saying: no more dying.

Utter Darkness

Fourthly: from the plague of darkness inflicted on the Egyptians — of which it is written (Exodus 10:23), "They saw not one another, neither rose any man from his place for three days; but all the children of Israel had light in their dwellings" — the place of the wicked after judgment is called utter darkness, or, as it stands in the original, darkness without. So it is expressed in Matthew 22:13, where the king commands his servants "to bind hand and foot the man that had not on his wedding garment, and to cast him out," eis to skotos to exoteron — into the external darkness, the darkness without. Though this is translated "utter darkness," the phrase does not signify how great the darkness is, but where it is to be: namely, outside the dwelling of God's elect.

Gehenna and Tophet

Lastly: there was a place near Jerusalem called the Valley of the Children of Hinnom. In one part of it, called Tophet, the Jews had committed the most grievous idolatry, sacrificing their children to the idol Moloch; there too God had afflicted his enemies with the most grievous punishments; and there Josiah had burned the priests of Moloch upon their own altars, as appears at length in 2 Kings 23. Afterward the place served to receive the filth and garbage carried out of the city, and fires were made there from time to time, to purify the air and take away the stench of rotting flesh. From this abominable place the Jews ever after called the place of the damned by the name Gehenna, or Valley of Hinnom. And this Gehenna is the word that is now usually translated HELL; and from the fires burning there from time to time, we get the notion of everlasting and unquenchable fire.

The Literal Sense of Scripture Concerning Hell

Now, no one interprets Scripture to mean that after the day of judgment the wicked are all to be eternally punished in the Valley of Hinnom; or that they shall rise again only to live forever underground or underwater; or that after the resurrection they shall never see one another again, or never stir from one place to another. It follows, it seems to me, quite necessarily, that what is said about hell fire is spoken metaphorically — and that there is therefore a proper sense to be sought out (for every metaphor has some real ground that can be expressed in proper words), both for the place of hell and for the nature of hellish torment and the tormentors.

Satan and Devil Are Not Proper Names but Titles

First, as for the tormentors: we have their nature and properties exactly and properly delivered in their names — the Enemy, or Satan; the Accuser, or Diabolus; the Destroyer, or Abaddon. These significant names — Satan, Devil, Abaddon — do not set before us any individual person, as proper names do, but only an office or quality. They are therefore appellatives — common nouns, titles — and ought not to have been left untranslated, as they are in the Latin and modern Bibles. Left untranslated, they look like the proper names of demons, and men are the more easily seduced into believing the doctrine of devils — which in those days was the religion of the Gentiles, and contrary to the religion of Moses and of Christ.

And because by the Enemy, the Accuser, and the Destroyer is meant the enemy of those who shall be in the kingdom of God, it follows that if the kingdom of God after the resurrection is on earth (as I have shown from Scripture, in the previous chapter, it seems to be), then the Enemy and his kingdom must be on earth too. So it was, after all, in the time before the Jews deposed God: God's kingdom was in Palestine, and the nations round about were the kingdoms of the Enemy. And consequently, by Satan is meant any earthly enemy of the Church.

The Torments of Hell

The torments of hell are expressed sometimes by "weeping and gnashing of teeth," as in Matthew 8:12; sometimes by "the worm of conscience," as in Isaiah 66:24 and Mark 9:44, 46, 48; sometimes by fire, as in the place just quoted — "where the worm dies not, and the fire is not quenched" — and many places besides; sometimes by "shame and contempt," as in Daniel 12:2: "And many of them that sleep in the dust of the earth shall awake; some to everlasting life, and some to shame and everlasting contempt." All these places designate, metaphorically, a grief and discontent of mind at the sight of that eternal happiness in others which they themselves, through their own unbelief and disobedience, have lost. And because such happiness in others cannot be felt except by comparison with one's own actual miseries, it follows that the damned are to suffer such bodily pains and calamities as fall upon those who not only live under evil and cruel governors, but have for their enemy the eternal King of the saints, God Almighty.

And among these bodily pains is to be reckoned, for every one of the wicked, a SECOND DEATH. For though Scripture is clear about a universal resurrection, we nowhere read that any of the reprobate is promised an eternal life. St. Paul, answering the question of what bodies men shall rise with (1 Corinthians 15:42-43), says that "the body is sown in corruption, and is raised in incorruption; it is sown in dishonor, it is raised in glory; it is sown in weakness, it is raised in power." But glory and power cannot be applied to the bodies of the wicked. Nor can the name of second death be applied to those who can never die more than once. And although in metaphorical speech an everlasting calamitous life may be called an everlasting death, it cannot reasonably be understood as a second death.

The fire prepared for the wicked is an everlasting fire — that is to say, the condition in which no man can be free of torture, of body and mind alike, after the resurrection, shall endure forever. In that sense the fire shall be unquenchable, and the torments everlasting. But it cannot be inferred from this that anyone cast into that fire, or tormented with those torments, shall endure and withstand them in such a way as to be eternally burnt and tortured and yet never be destroyed, never die. And though there are many places that affirm everlasting fire and torments — into which men may be cast, one after another, successively, forever — I find none that affirm there shall be an eternal life in that fire for any individual person. On the contrary, they affirm an everlasting death, which is the second death (Revelation 20:13-14): "For after Death and the Grave shall have delivered up the dead which were in them, and every man be judged according to his works, Death and the Grave shall also be cast into the lake of fire. This is the second death." From which it is evident that there is to be a second death for everyone who shall be condemned at the day of judgment — after which he shall die no more.

The Joys of Eternal Life and Salvation Are the Same Thing; Salvation from Sin and from Misery Are One

The joys of eternal life are all comprehended in Scripture under the name of SALVATION, or being saved. To be saved is to be made secure — either against particular evils, or absolutely, against all evil, including want, sickness, and death itself. And because man was created in an immortal condition, not subject to corruption, and consequently subject to nothing that tends to the dissolution of his nature, and fell from that happiness by the sin of Adam, it follows that to be saved from sin is to be saved from all the evil and calamities that sin has brought upon us. And therefore, in Holy Scripture, remission of sin and salvation from death and misery are the same thing — as appears from the words of our Savior who, having cured a man sick with palsy by saying (Matthew 9:2), "Son, be of good cheer, your sins are forgiven you," and knowing the scribes took it for blasphemy that a man should claim to forgive sins, asked them (verse 5) "whether it were easier to say, Your sins are forgiven you, or, Arise and walk" — signifying that, as far as saving the sick man went, it was all one to say "Your sins are forgiven" and "Arise and walk," and that he used the first form of speech only to show that he had the power to forgive sins. And it is evident in reason, besides: since death and misery were the punishments of sin, the discharge of sin must also be a discharge of death and misery. That is salvation absolute — the salvation the faithful are to enjoy after the day of judgment, by the power and favor of Jesus Christ, who for that reason is called our SAVIOR.

Concerning particular salvations, such as are meant in 1 Samuel 14:39, "as the Lord lives that saves Israel" — that is, saves them from their temporary enemies — and in 2 Samuel 22:4, "You are my Savior, you save me from violence," and in 2 Kings 13:5, "God gave the Israelites a Savior, and so they were delivered from the hand of the Assyrians," and the like, I need say nothing; for there is neither difficulty nor any vested interest to corrupt the interpretation of texts of that kind.

The Place of Eternal Salvation

But concerning the general salvation, because it must take place in the kingdom of heaven, there is great difficulty about the place. On one side: by kingdom — which is an estate ordained by men for their perpetual security against enemies and want — it would seem that this salvation should be on earth. For what salvation sets before us is a glorious reign of our King, by conquest, not a safety by escape. So wherever we look for salvation, we must also look for triumph; and before triumph, victory; and before victory, battle — which can hardly be supposed to happen in heaven. But however good this reasoning may be, I will not trust to it without very clear places of Scripture.

The state of salvation is described at length in Isaiah 33:20-24:

"Look upon Zion, the city of our solemnities; your eyes shall see Jerusalem a quiet habitation, a tabernacle that shall not be taken down; not one of its stakes shall ever be removed, neither shall any of its cords be broken.

But there the glorious Lord will be to us a place of broad rivers and streams, where no galley with oars shall go, neither shall gallant ship pass by.

For the Lord is our judge, the Lord is our lawgiver, the Lord is our king; he will save us.

Your riggings are loosed; they could not strengthen their mast; they could not spread the sail. Then is the prey of a great spoil divided; the lame take the prey.

And the inhabitant shall not say, I am sick; the people that dwell therein shall be forgiven their iniquity."

In these words we have the place from which salvation is to proceed: "Jerusalem, a quiet habitation." Its eternity: "a tabernacle that shall not be taken down," and so on. Its Savior: "the Lord, their judge, their lawgiver, their king — he will save us." The salvation itself: "the Lord shall be to them as a broad moat of swift waters," and so on. The condition of their enemies: their riggings loose, their masts weak, the lame taking their spoils. The condition of the saved: "the inhabitant shall not say, I am sick." And lastly, all of it is comprehended in the forgiveness of sin: "the people that dwell therein shall be forgiven their iniquity." From this it is evident that salvation shall be on earth, at the time when God shall reign in Jerusalem, at the second coming of Christ; and that from Jerusalem shall proceed the salvation of the Gentiles who shall be received into God's kingdom.

This is declared even more expressly by the same prophet (Isaiah 66:20-21): "And they" — that is, the Gentiles who held any Jew in bondage — "shall bring all your brethren, for an offering to the Lord, out of all nations, upon horses, and in chariots, and in litters, and upon mules, and upon swift beasts, to my holy mountain, Jerusalem, says the Lord, as the children of Israel bring an offering in a clean vessel into the house of the Lord. And I will also take of them for priests and for Levites, says the Lord." From this it is manifest that the chief seat of God's kingdom — the place from which the salvation of us who were Gentiles shall proceed — shall be Jerusalem.

The same is confirmed by our Savior in his conversation with the woman of Samaria about the place of God's worship. He tells her (John 4:22) that the Samaritans worship they know not what, but the Jews worship what they know: "For salvation is of the Jews" — ex Judaeis, that is, it begins at the Jews. As if he were saying: you worship God, but you do not know by whom he will save you, as we do, who know it shall be one of the tribe of Judah — a Jew, not a Samaritan. And that is why the woman, not at all beside the point, answered him, "We know the Messiah shall come." So what our Savior says — salvation is from the Jews — is the same as what Paul says (Romans 1:16-17): "The Gospel is the power of God to salvation to every one that believes; to the Jew first, and also to the Greek. For in it the righteousness of God is revealed from faith to faith" — from the faith of the Jew, to the faith of the Gentile.

In the same sense the prophet Joel, describing the day of judgment (Joel 2:30-31) — that God would "show wonders in heaven and in earth, blood, and fire, and pillars of smoke. The sun shall be turned to darkness, and the moon into blood, before the great and terrible day of the Lord come" — adds at verse 32: "and it shall come to pass, that whoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved. For in Mount Zion, and in Jerusalem shall be salvation." And Obadiah, verse 17, says the same: "Upon Mount Zion shall be deliverance; and there shall be holiness, and the house of Jacob shall possess their possessions" — that is, the possessions of the heathen, which he spells out in the following verses: the Mount of Esau, the land of the Philistines, the fields of Ephraim, of Samaria, Gilead, and the cities of the south; and he concludes with these words: "the kingdom shall be the Lord's."

All these places stand for salvation, and the kingdom of God after the day of judgment, on earth. On the other side, I have found no text that can plausibly be made to prove any ascension of the saints into heaven — that is to say, into any caelum empyreum or other ethereal region — except that it is called the kingdom of heaven. And it may have that name because God, who was King of the Jews, governed them by his commands, sent to Moses by angels from heaven, to bring them back to obedience, and shall send him from there again, to rule both them and all other faithful men, from the day of judgment, everlastingly. Or because the throne of this our great King is in heaven, while the earth is but his footstool. But that the subjects of God should have any place as high as his throne, or higher than his footstool — that hardly seems suitable to the dignity of a king, nor can I find any clear text for it in Holy Scripture.

From what has been said about the kingdom of God and about salvation, it is not hard to interpret what is meant by the WORLD TO COME. There are three worlds mentioned in Scripture: the old world, the present world, and the world to come. Of the first, St. Peter speaks (2 Peter 2:5): "If God spared not the old world, but saved Noah the eighth person, a preacher of righteousness, bringing the flood upon the world of the ungodly," and so on. So the first world ran from Adam to the general flood. Of the present world, our Savior speaks (John 18:36): "My kingdom is not of this world" — for he came only to teach men the way of salvation, and to renew the kingdom of his Father by his doctrine. Of the world to come, St. Peter speaks (2 Peter 3:13): "Nevertheless we, according to his promise, look for new heavens and a new earth." This is that world in which Christ, coming down from heaven in the clouds, with great power and glory, shall send his angels and gather together his elect from the four winds and from the uttermost parts of the earth, and from then on reign over them, under his Father, everlastingly.

Redemption

The salvation of a sinner presupposes a prior REDEMPTION. For whoever is once guilty of sin is liable to the penalty for it, and must pay — or someone else must pay for him — whatever ransom the offended party, who has him in his power, shall require. And since the person offended is Almighty God, in whose power are all things, the ransom to be paid before salvation can be acquired is whatever ransom God has been pleased to require.

By this ransom is not meant a satisfaction for sin equivalent to the offense — something no sinner could ever make for himself, nor any righteous man for another. The damage a man does to another man he can make amends for by restitution or compensation; but sin cannot be taken away by compensation, for that would make the liberty to sin a thing for sale. But sins may be pardoned to the repentant — either freely, or upon such penalty as God is pleased to accept. What God usually accepted in the Old Testament was some sacrifice or offering. To forgive sin is not an act of injustice, even where the punishment has been threatened. Even among men, though the promise of good binds the promiser, threats — that is to say, promises of evil — do not bind them; much less shall they bind God, who is infinitely more merciful than men.

Our Savior Christ, therefore, in redeeming us, did not satisfy for the sins of men in the sense that his death, by its own virtue, could make it unjust in God to punish sinners with eternal death. Rather, he made that sacrifice and offering of himself, at his first coming, which God was pleased to require for the salvation, at his second coming, of all who in the meantime should repent and believe in him. And though this act of our redemption is not always called a sacrifice and offering in Scripture, but sometimes a price, by price we are not to understand anything by whose value he could claim a right to a pardon for us from his offended Father — but only that price which God the Father was pleased, in mercy, to demand.

Chapter 39: What the Word "Church" Means in Scripture

Church: The Lord's House

The word "church" — in Greek, ecclesia — means several different things in the books of Holy Scripture. Sometimes, though not often, it means God's house: a temple where Christians gather to perform their holy duties in public. That is the sense in "Let your women keep silence in the churches" (1 Corinthians 14:34). But even there the word is used metaphorically, standing for the congregation gathered inside the building. Since then it has come to be used for the building itself, to distinguish the temples of Christians from the temples of idolaters.

The Temple of Jerusalem was God's house, the house of prayer; and in the same way, any building that Christians dedicate to the worship of Christ is Christ's house. That is why the Greek Fathers called such a building Kuriake — "the Lord's house" — and from that word, in our own language, came "kirk," and then "church."

What Ecclesia Properly Means

When "church" does not mean a building, it means what ecclesia meant in the Greek commonwealths: a congregation, an assembly of citizens called together to hear the magistrate speak to them. In the Roman commonwealth the same thing was called a concio, and the man who spoke was called ecclesiastes or concionator — the speaker, the addresser of assemblies. When the citizens were called together by lawful authority, as in (Acts 19:39), the gathering was ecclesia legitima — a lawful church, in Greek ennomos ecclesia. But when they were stirred up by riotous and seditious shouting, it was a confused church — ecclesia sugkechumene.

Sometimes the word is used for all the people who have the right to belong to the congregation, even when they are not actually assembled — that is, for the whole multitude of Christians, however widely scattered they may be. That is the sense in which "Saul made havoc of the church" (Acts 8:3), and the sense in which Christ is called the head of the church. Sometimes it means a particular group of Christians, as in "Salute the church that is in his house" (Colossians 4:15). Sometimes it means the elect only, as in "a glorious church, without spot or wrinkle, holy and without blemish" (Ephesians 5:27) — which refers to the church triumphant, the church that is to come. And sometimes it means a congregation actually assembled, made up of people who profess Christianity, whether their profession is sincere or counterfeit. That is how it is understood in (Matthew 18:17): "Tell it to the church, and if he neglect to hear the church, let him be to thee as a Gentile or publican."

In What Sense the Church Is One Person; the Church Defined

Only in this last sense can the church be considered one person — one body that can be said to have the power to will, to pronounce, to command, to be obeyed, to make laws, or to perform any other action at all. Here is why. Without authority from a lawful congregation, whatever act is done by a crowd of people is the particular act of each individual who was present and lent a hand to it. It is not the act of them all together, as one body. Still less is it the act of those who were absent, or of those who were present but did not want it done.

According to this sense, I define a CHURCH to be: a company of men professing Christian religion, united in the person of one sovereign, at whose command they ought to assemble, and without whose authority they ought not to assemble.

And because in every commonwealth an assembly that gathers without warrant from the civil sovereign is unlawful, it follows that a church assembled in any commonwealth that has forbidden it to assemble is an unlawful assembly.

A Christian Commonwealth and a Church Are One and the Same

It also follows that there is no universal church on earth that all Christians are bound to obey — because there is no power on earth to which all the commonwealths of the world are subject. There are Christians living under many different princes and states, but each of those Christians is a subject of the commonwealth he himself belongs to, and so he cannot be subject to the commands of any other person.

Therefore a church capable of acting — capable of commanding, judging, absolving, condemning, or doing anything else — is the same thing as a civil commonwealth made up of Christians. It is called a civil state because its subjects are men, and a church because its subjects are Christians. Temporal and spiritual government are but two words brought into the world to make men see double and mistake their lawful sovereign.

It is true that after the resurrection the bodies of the faithful will be not only spiritual but eternal. In this life, however, they are coarse and corruptible. So in this life there is no government of either state or religion except temporal government; and no doctrine may lawfully be taught to any subject if the governor of both state and religion forbids it to be taught. And that governor must be one. Otherwise faction and civil war must follow in the commonwealth — between church and state; between the party of the spiritual and the party of the temporal; between the sword of justice and the shield of faith; and, worse than all of these, a war inside every Christian's own breast, between the Christian and the man.

The doctors of the church are called pastors. So are civil sovereigns. But if pastors are not subordinated one to another, so that there is one chief pastor, men will be taught contrary doctrines — both of which may be false, and one of which must be. Who that one chief pastor is, according to the law of nature, has already been shown: it is the civil sovereign. And to whom Scripture has assigned that office, we shall see in the chapters that follow.

Chapter 40: The Rights of the Kingdom of God — in Abraham, Moses, the High Priests, and the Kings of Judah

Abraham's Sovereign Rights

The father of the faithful, and the first member of the kingdom of God by covenant, was Abraham. The covenant was made with him first. In it, he bound himself — and his descendants after him — to acknowledge and obey the commands of God. Not just the commands he could discover for himself by the light of nature, as moral laws, but also whatever commands God should deliver to him in a special way, by dreams and visions.

Think about why that distinction matters. As far as the moral law was concerned, Abraham and his family were already obligated to obey it. There was no need to purchase their obedience with the promise of the land of Canaan. No contract could add anything to, or strengthen, the obligation by which they — and all people everywhere — were already naturally bound to obey God Almighty. So the covenant Abraham made with God came down to this: to accept as the commandment of God whatever was commanded to him in God's name, in a dream or vision, and to deliver it to his family, and make sure they observed it.

Abraham Alone Ordered the Religion of His Own People

In this contract between God and Abraham, we can observe three points of major consequence for the government of God's people.

First: when this covenant was made, God spoke only to Abraham. He made no contract with any member of Abraham's family or any of his descendants — except in the sense that their wills (which are the essence of every covenant) were already wrapped up in the will of Abraham before the contract was made. Abraham, in other words, was assumed to have lawful power to make his people perform everything he covenanted on their behalf. Scripture says exactly this (Genesis 18:18-19): "All the nations of the earth shall be blessed in him, for I know him, that he will command his children and his household after him, and they shall keep the way of the Lord."

From this we can draw our first conclusion: those to whom God has not spoken directly are to receive the positive commandments of God from their sovereign — just as Abraham's family and descendants received them from Abraham, their father, their lord, and their civil sovereign. And consequently, in every commonwealth, those who have no supernatural revelation to the contrary ought to obey the laws of their own sovereign in the external acts and profession of religion. As for the inward thoughts and beliefs of men — which human governors cannot take notice of, for God alone knows the heart — these are not voluntary. They are not the effect of laws, but of the unrevealed will and power of God, and so they fall under no obligation at all.

No Claim of "Private Spirit" Against Abraham's Religion

From the same contract follows a second point. Suppose one of Abraham's subjects claimed a private vision, or spirit, or some other revelation from God, to support a doctrine Abraham had forbidden. It was not unlawful for Abraham to punish that person — and to punish anyone who followed or sided with such a pretender. And consequently, it is lawful now for the sovereign to punish any man who sets up his "private spirit" against the laws. For the sovereign holds the same place in the commonwealth that Abraham held in his own family.

Abraham the Sole Judge and Interpreter of What God Said

And from the same contract arises a third point: just as no one but Abraham in his family could take notice of what was, or was not, the word of God, so no one but the sovereign in a Christian commonwealth can do so. God spoke only to Abraham, and Abraham alone was in a position to know what God said and to interpret it to his family. Therefore those who hold the place of Abraham in a commonwealth are the only interpreters of what God has spoken.

What Moses' Authority Rested On

The same covenant was renewed with Isaac, and afterwards with Jacob — but then no more, until the Israelites were freed from the Egyptians and arrived at the foot of Mount Sinai. There it was renewed by Moses (as I said before, in chapter 35), in such a way that from that time forward the Israelites became the peculiar kingdom of God, whose lieutenant was Moses for his own lifetime, with the succession to that office settled on Aaron and his heirs after him — to be to God a priestly kingdom forever.

By this constitution, a kingdom is acquired to God. But notice a difficulty. Moses had no authority to govern the Israelites as a successor to Abraham's right, because he could not claim it by inheritance. So it does not yet appear that the people were obligated to take him for God's lieutenant any longer than they believed that God actually spoke to him. His authority — despite the covenant they had made with God — still depended entirely on the opinion they held of his holiness, of the reality of his conversations with God, and of the truth of his miracles. And if that opinion changed, they were no longer obligated to accept anything he proposed to them in God's name as the law of God.

We have to ask, then: what other ground was there for their obligation to obey him? It could not be the commandment of God itself, because God did not speak to them directly — only through the mediation of Moses himself. Our Savior says of himself (John 5:31), "If I bear witness of myself, my witness is not true." If that holds for Christ, then much less should Moses' testimony about himself be accepted — especially when he was claiming kingly power over God's people. His authority, therefore, like the authority of all other princes, must be grounded on the consent of the people and their promise to obey him.

And so it was. For the people (Exodus 20:18), "when they saw the thunderings, and the lightnings, and the noise of the trumpet, and the mountain smoking, moved back and stood far off. And they said to Moses: speak thou with us, and we will hear; but let not God speak with us, lest we die." Here was their promise of obedience. By this promise they obligated themselves to obey whatever he delivered to them as the commandment of God.

Moses Was Sovereign of the Jews (Under God) All His Lifetime, Though Aaron Held the Priesthood

Now, the covenant did establish a priestly kingdom — that is, a kingdom hereditary to Aaron. But that must be understood of the succession after Moses was dead. Whoever orders and establishes the political system, as first founder of a commonwealth — whether monarchy, aristocracy, or democracy — must necessarily have sovereign power over the people the whole time he is doing it. And that Moses held that power his whole lifetime is plainly affirmed in Scripture.

First, in the text just cited: the people promised obedience to him, not to Aaron.

Second (Exodus 24:1-2): "And God said unto Moses, Come up unto the Lord, thou, and Aaron, Nadab and Abihu, and seventy of the elders of Israel. And Moses alone shall come near the Lord, but they shall not come nigh, neither shall the people go up with him." This makes it plain that Moses — who alone was called up to God, not Aaron, not the other priests, not the seventy elders, not the people, who were forbidden to come up — was alone the one who represented the person of God to the Israelites. That is to say, he was their sole sovereign under God. It is true that afterwards (verse 9) we read: "Then went up Moses, and Aaron, Nadab, and Abihu, and seventy of the elders of Israel, and they saw the God of Israel, and there was under his feet, as it were, a paved work of a sapphire stone," and so on. But this did not happen until after Moses had been with God before, and had already brought down to the people the words God had spoken to him. He alone went up on the people's business; the others, like the nobles of his retinue, were admitted purely as an honor, to a special grace not allowed to the people — which was (as the following verse shows) to see God and live: "God laid not his hand upon them; they saw God, and did eat and drink" — that is, they lived — but they carried no commandment from him to the people.

Third: everywhere the text says "The Lord spoke unto Moses" — in all matters of government, and also in the ordering of the ceremonies of religion contained in chapters 25 through 31 of Exodus and throughout Leviticus. To Aaron, rarely. Indeed, the calf that Aaron made, Moses threw into the fire.

Fourth: the question of Aaron's authority — raised when he and Miriam mutinied against Moses — was judged by God himself in Moses' favor (Numbers 12).

And likewise in the dispute between Moses and the people, when Korah, Dathan, and Abiram, and two hundred and fifty princes of the assembly "gathered themselves together against Moses, and against Aaron" (Numbers 16:3), "and said unto them, Ye take too much upon you, seeing all the congregation are holy, every one of them, and the Lord is amongst them; why lift you up yourselves above the congregation of the Lord?" God caused the earth to swallow Korah, Dathan, and Abiram alive, with their wives and children, and consumed those two hundred and fifty princes with fire.

So: not Aaron, not the people, not any aristocracy of the chief princes of the people — Moses alone had, next under God, the sovereignty over the Israelites. And not only in matters of civil policy, but in religion too. For only Moses spoke with God, and therefore only Moses could tell the people what it was that God required of them. No man, on pain of death, could be so presumptuous as to approach the mountain where God talked with Moses. "Thou shalt set bounds to the people round about," says the Lord (Exodus 19:12), "and say, Take heed to yourselves that you go not up into the mount, or touch the border of it; whosoever toucheth the mount shall surely be put to death." And again (verse 21): "Get down, charge the people, lest they break through unto the Lord to gaze."

From all this we may conclude: whoever in a Christian commonwealth holds the place of Moses is the sole messenger of God and interpreter of his commandments. Accordingly, no man ought, in interpreting Scripture, to go beyond the bounds set by his own sovereign. For the Scriptures — since God now speaks in them — are Mount Sinai, and its boundaries are the laws of those who represent God's person on earth. To look upon the Scriptures, and behold in them the wondrous works of God, and learn to fear him — that is allowed. But to interpret them — that is, to pry into what God says to the man he has appointed to govern under him, and to make ourselves judges of whether he governs as God commands him or not — is to transgress the bounds God has set us, and to gaze upon God irreverently.

All Spirits Were Subordinate to the Spirit of Moses

In Moses' time there was no prophet, and no claimant to the spirit of God, except those Moses had approved and authorized. There were in his time only seventy men who are said to prophesy by the spirit of God, and all seventy were of Moses' own choosing. Concerning them, God says to Moses (Numbers 11:16): "Gather to me seventy of the elders of Israel, whom thou knowest to be the elders of the people." To these God imparted his spirit — but it was not a different spirit from the spirit of Moses. For the text says (verse 25): "God came down in a cloud, and took of the spirit that was upon Moses, and gave it to the seventy elders."

Now, as I showed before (chapter 36), by "spirit" is understood the mind. So the meaning of the passage is simply this: God endowed them with a mind conformable and subordinate to the mind of Moses, so that they might prophesy — that is, speak to the people in God's name — in such a way as to promote, as ministers of Moses and by his authority, doctrine that agreed with Moses' doctrine. They were only ministers. And when two of them prophesied in the camp, it was considered a new and unlawful thing; as verses 27 and 28 of the same chapter show, they were accused of it, and Joshua advised Moses to forbid them — not realizing that it was by Moses' own spirit that they prophesied. From which it is plain that no subject ought to claim prophecy, or the spirit, in opposition to the doctrine established by the man whom God has set in the place of Moses.

After Moses, Sovereignty Belonged to the High Priest

When Aaron was dead, and after him Moses, the kingdom — being a priestly kingdom — descended, by virtue of the covenant, to Aaron's son Eleazar, the high priest. And God declared him sovereign, next under himself, at the very moment he appointed Joshua general of the army. For God says expressly concerning Joshua (Numbers 27:21): "He shall stand before Eleazar the priest, who shall ask counsel for him before the Lord; at his word shall they go out, and at his word they shall come in, both he, and all the children of Israel with him." The supreme power of making war and peace, then, was in the priest. The supreme power of judicature belonged to the high priest as well: the Book of the Law was in the priests' keeping, and the priests and Levites alone were the subordinate judges in civil cases, as appears in Deuteronomy 17:8-10. And as for the manner of God's worship, no one ever doubted that the high priest, until the time of Saul, had the supreme authority over it.

Therefore the civil and the ecclesiastical power were both joined together in one and the same person — the high priest. And they ought to be so joined in whoever governs by divine right; that is, by authority that comes immediately from God.

Sovereign Power Between Joshua and Saul

After the death of Joshua, until the time of Saul, the Book of Judges notes repeatedly "that there was in those days no king in Israel" — sometimes adding that "every man did that which was right in his own eyes." By "there was no king" we should understand "there was no sovereign power" in Israel. And so it was — if we consider the actual exercise of such power. For after the deaths of Joshua and Eleazar, "there arose another generation" (Judges 2:10) "that knew not the Lord, nor the works which he had done for Israel, but did evil in the sight of the Lord, and served Baalim." The Jews had that quality St. Paul notes — they "look for a sign" — not only before they would submit to the government of Moses, but even after they had bound themselves by their submission. Yet signs and miracles had as their purpose to produce faith, not to keep men from violating faith once given; for to keep faith, men are already obligated by the law of nature.

But if we consider not the exercise of governing but the right of it, the sovereign power was still in the high priest. So whatever obedience was given to any of the Judges — men chosen by God in an extraordinary way, to rescue his rebellious subjects from the hands of their enemies — cannot be used as an argument against the right of the high priest to sovereign power in all matters, both of policy and of religion. Neither the Judges nor Samuel himself had an ordinary calling to government, but an extraordinary one; and the Israelites obeyed them not out of duty, but out of reverence for their evident favor with God, displayed in their wisdom, their courage, or their success. Up to this point, then, the right of regulating the state and the right of regulating religion were inseparable.

The Rights of the Kings of Israel

After the Judges came kings. And whereas before, all authority — in religion and in policy alike — had been in the high priest, now it was all in the king. For the sovereignty over the people, which had rested not only on God's divine power but also on the particular pact the Israelites made with God, belonged next under him to the high priest, as his viceregent on earth. And that sovereignty was cast off by the people, with the consent of God himself. When they said to Samuel (1 Samuel 8:5), "make us a king to judge us, like all the nations," they were declaring that they would no longer be governed by the commands laid on them by the priest in the name of God, but by a man who would command them the same way all other nations were commanded. So in stripping the high priest of royal authority, they deposed that peculiar government of God. And yet God consented to it, saying to Samuel (verse 7): "Hearken unto the voice of the people, in all that they shall say unto thee; for they have not rejected thee, but they have rejected me, that I should not reign over them."

Having rejected God, in whose right the priests had governed, the people left the priests no authority at all except whatever the king was pleased to allow them — and that was more or less, depending on whether the kings were good or evil. As for the government of civil affairs, it was obviously all in the king's hands. In the same chapter (verse 20) the people say they want to be like all the nations: their king shall be their judge, and go before them, and fight their battles. In other words, he shall have the whole authority, in peace and in war alike. And that authority contained the ordering of religion within it — because in those days there was no other word of God by which to regulate religion than the Law of Moses, which was their civil law.

The kings' actual conduct confirms this. We read (1 Kings 2:27) that Solomon "thrust out Abiathar from being priest before the Lord." So he had authority over the high priest, exactly as over any other subject — which is a great mark of supremacy in religion. We read (1 Kings 8) that Solomon dedicated the Temple, that he blessed the people, and that he himself, in person, made that excellent prayer now used in the consecration of all churches and houses of prayer — another great mark of supremacy in religion. Again, we read (2 Kings 22) that when a question arose about the Book of the Law found in the Temple, it was not the high priest who decided it; Josiah sent both him and others to inquire about it from Huldah the prophetess — another mark of supremacy in religion. Lastly, we read (1 Chronicles 26:30) that David made Hashabiah and his brothers, Hebronites, officers of Israel on the west side of Jordan, "in all business of the Lord, and in the service of the king"; and likewise (verse 32) that he made other Hebronites "rulers over the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half tribe of Manasseh" — the rest of Israel, who lived beyond Jordan — "for every matter pertaining to God, and affairs of the king." Is that not full power, both "temporal" and "spiritual" — as those who would like to divide it call the two halves?

To conclude: from the first institution of God's kingdom down to the Captivity, the supremacy in religion was in the same hand as the civil sovereignty. And after the election of Saul, the office of the priests was not magisterial but ministerial — they served; they did not rule.

In Practice, the Kings' Supremacy in Religion Did Not Match Their Right

Now, although the government of policy and religion was joined — first in the high priests, and afterwards in the kings — as far as the right was concerned, the same holy history shows that the people never understood it that way. A great part of them — probably the greatest part — gave sufficient credit to the fame of Moses, or to the conversations between God and the priests, only so long as they saw great miracles, or (what is equivalent to a miracle) great abilities or great success in the undertakings of their governors. So whenever their governors displeased them, they seized the occasion — blaming sometimes the policy, sometimes the religion — to change the government or revolt from their obedience whenever they pleased. And from this came, time after time, the civil troubles, divisions, and calamities of the nation.

For example: after the deaths of Eleazar and Joshua, the next generation — which had not seen the wonders of God, and was left to its own weak reason — did not consider itself bound by the covenant of a priestly kingdom. They paid no further attention to the commandment of the priest, nor to any law of Moses, but every man did what was right in his own eyes. In civil affairs they obeyed whatever men they thought, from time to time, were able to deliver them from the neighboring nations that oppressed them. And they consulted not God (as they ought to have done), but whatever men or women they guessed to be prophets by their predictions of things to come. Even with an idol in their chapel, if they had a Levite for their chaplain, they reckoned they were worshipping the God of Israel.

And later, when they demanded a king after the manner of the nations, it was not with any design of abandoning the worship of God their King. Despairing of justice from the sons of Samuel, they wanted a king to judge them in civil matters — but not a king they would allow to change the religion they believed had been handed down to them by Moses. So they always kept a pretext in reserve — either justice or religion — to release themselves from their obedience whenever they had hope of prevailing. Samuel was displeased with the people for desiring a king (for God was their king already, and Samuel had only an authority under him); yet Samuel himself, when Saul failed to follow his counsel by destroying Agag as God had commanded, anointed another king — namely David — to take the succession away from Saul's heirs. Rehoboam was no idolater; but when the people thought him an oppressor, that civil pretext carried ten tribes away from him to Jeroboam, who was an idolater. And throughout the whole history of the kings, of Judah and of Israel alike, there were always prophets rebuking the kings for transgressing the religion — and sometimes for errors of state as well. Jehoshaphat was reproved by the prophet Jehu for aiding the king of Israel against the Syrians (2 Chronicles 19:2); Hezekiah was reproved by Isaiah for showing his treasures to the ambassadors of Babylon.

All of this shows that although the power of both state and religion was in the kings, none of them exercised it uncontested — except those who were popular for their own natural abilities or their good fortune. So from the practice of those times, no argument can be drawn that the right of supremacy in religion was not in the kings. Unless, that is, we place it in the prophets — and conclude that because Hezekiah, praying to the Lord before the cherubim, was not answered from there and then, but afterwards by the prophet Isaiah, Isaiah was therefore the supreme head of the Church. Or that because Josiah consulted Huldah the prophetess about the Book of the Law, neither he nor the high priest but Huldah the prophetess held the supreme authority in matters of religion. Which, I think, is not the opinion of any theologian.

After the Captivity, the Jews Had No Settled Commonwealth

During the Captivity, the Jews had no commonwealth at all. After their return, though they renewed their covenant with God, no promise of obedience was made — not to Esdras, not to anyone else. And shortly afterwards they became subjects of the Greeks, from whose customs and demonology, and from the doctrine of the Cabalists, their religion became badly corrupted. The result is such confusion, in state and religion alike, that nothing can be gathered from that period concerning the supremacy in either.

And therefore, as far as the Old Testament is concerned, we may conclude this: whoever held the sovereignty of the commonwealth among the Jews also held the supreme authority in the matter of God's external worship, and represented God's person — that is, the person of God the Father. Though he was not called by the name of Father until the time when God sent into the world his Son, Jesus Christ, to redeem mankind from their sins and bring them into his everlasting kingdom, to be saved forevermore. Of which we are to speak in the chapter that follows.

Chapter 41: The Office of Our Blessed Savior

The Three Parts of the Office of Christ

We find in Holy Scripture three parts of the office of the Messiah. The first is that of a REDEEMER, or savior. The second is that of a pastor, counselor, or teacher — that is, of a prophet sent from God to convert those whom God has elected to salvation. The third is that of a king — an eternal king, but under his Father, as Moses and the high priests were in their several times.

And to these three parts correspond three different times. Our redemption he accomplished at his first coming, by the sacrifice in which he offered himself up for our sins on the cross. Our conversion he worked partly then, in his own person, and partly works now, through his ministers — and he will continue working it until his coming again. And after his coming again shall begin his glorious reign over his elect, which is to last eternally.

His Office as Redeemer

To the office of a redeemer — that is, of one who pays the ransom of sin, and that ransom is death — it belongs that he was sacrificed, and thereby bore upon his own head, and carried away from us, our iniquities, in the manner God had required. Not that the death of one man, even a man without sin, can satisfy for the offenses of all men by the strict rigor of justice. It satisfies through the mercy of God, who ordained such sacrifices for sin as he was pleased, in his mercy, to accept.

Under the old law (as we may read in Leviticus 16), the Lord required that once every year an atonement be made for the sins of all Israel, priests and people alike. To perform it, Aaron alone was to sacrifice a young bull for himself and the priests; and for the rest of the people, he was to receive from them two young goats. One of these he was to sacrifice. But the other was the SCAPEGOAT: Aaron was to lay his hands on its head, and by confessing the iniquities of the people, lay them all on that head; and then, by some suitable man, he was to have the goat led into the wilderness, there to escape — and carry away with it the iniquities of the people.

As the sacrifice of the one goat was a sufficient price for the ransom of all Israel — sufficient because acceptable to God — so the death of the Messiah is a sufficient price for the sins of all mankind, because no more was required. Our Savior Christ's sufferings seem to be prefigured here as clearly as in the offering of Isaac, or in any other type — prophetic foreshadowing — of him in the Old Testament. He was both the sacrificed goat and the scapegoat. "He was oppressed, and he was afflicted; he opened not his mouth; he was brought as a lamb to the slaughter, and as a sheep is dumb before the shearer, so he opened not his mouth" (Isaiah 53:7) — there he is the sacrificed goat. "He has borne our griefs, and carried our sorrows" (verse 4); and again, "the Lord has laid on him the iniquities of us all" (verse 6) — and so he is the scapegoat. "He was cut off from the land of the living for the transgression of my people" (verse 8) — there again he is the sacrificed goat. And once more, "he shall bear their sins" (verse 11) — there he is the scapegoat. Thus the Lamb of God is equivalent to both of those goats: sacrificed, in that he died; and escaping, in his resurrection — raised at the fitting moment by his Father, and removed from the dwelling place of men in his ascension.

Christ's Kingdom Is Not of This World

Now, a redeemer has no title to the thing he redeems before the redemption is complete and the ransom paid; and this ransom was the death of the redeemer himself. So it is plain that our Savior, as man, was not king of those he redeemed before he suffered death — that is, during the time he lived bodily on the earth. I say he was not then king in the present, by virtue of the pact which the faithful make with him in baptism. Nevertheless, by renewing their pact with God in baptism, they were obliged to obey him as king — under his Father — whenever he should be pleased to take the kingdom upon him.

And this is exactly what our Savior himself says expressly: "My kingdom is not of this world" (John 18:36). Now Scripture speaks of only two worlds: this one, which stands until the day of judgment — which is therefore also called the last day — and the one that shall be a new heaven and a new earth. So the kingdom of Christ is not to begin until the general resurrection. That is what our Savior means when he says, "The Son of man shall come in the glory of his Father, with his angels; and then he shall reward every man according to his works" (Matthew 16:27). To reward every man according to his works is to exercise the office of a king; and this is not to happen until he comes in the glory of his Father, with his angels.

When our Savior says, "The scribes and Pharisees sit in Moses' seat; all therefore whatsoever they bid you do, that observe and do" (Matthew 23:2), he declares plainly that he assigns kingly power, for that time, not to himself but to them. He does the same where he says, "Who made me a judge or a divider over you?" (Luke 12:14), and "I came not to judge the world, but to save the world" (John 12:47).

And yet our Savior did come into this world so that he might be a king, and a judge, in the world to come. For he was the Messiah — that is, the Christ; that is, the anointed priest and the sovereign prophet of God. He was to have, in other words, all the power that was in Moses the prophet, in the high priests who succeeded Moses, and in the kings who succeeded the priests. And St. John says expressly, "The Father judges no man, but has committed all judgment to the Son" (John 5:22). This does not contradict the other passage, "I came not to judge the world" — for that one is spoken of the present world, the other of the world to come. So also with what is said of the second coming of Christ: "You that have followed me in the regeneration, when the Son of man shall sit on the throne of his glory, you shall also sit on twelve thrones, judging the twelve tribes of Israel" (Matthew 19:28).

The Purpose of Christ's Coming Was to Renew the Covenant of the Kingdom of God, and to Persuade the Elect to Embrace It — the Second Part of His Office

If, then, Christ had no kingdom in this world while he was on earth, what was the purpose of his first coming? It was to restore to God, by a new covenant, the kingdom which was his by the old covenant, but had been cut off by the rebellion of the Israelites in the election of Saul.
