To do this, he was to preach to them that he was the Messiah — that is, the king promised to them by the prophets — and to offer himself as a sacrifice for the sins of those who would, by faith, submit themselves to him; and, if the nation as a whole should refuse him, to call to his obedience such of the Gentiles as would believe in him.

So there are two parts of our Savior's office during his stay on earth: one, to proclaim himself the Christ; the other, by teaching and by working miracles, to persuade men — and prepare them — to live in such a way as to be worthy of the immortality that believers were to enjoy when he comes in majesty to take possession of his Father's kingdom. That is why he himself often calls the time of his preaching the REGENERATION. The regeneration is not properly a kingdom, and so it gave no one a warrant to deny obedience to the magistrates who then ruled — for he commanded his followers to obey those who sat in Moses' chair, and to pay tribute to Caesar. It was only an earnest — a down payment — of the kingdom of God that was to come, given to those to whom God had given the grace to be his disciples and to believe in him. For this reason the godly are said to be already in the kingdom of grace, as people already naturalized in that heavenly kingdom.

The Preaching of Christ Was Not Contrary to the Law of the Jews, Nor of Caesar

So far, then, there is nothing done or taught by Christ that tends to diminish the civil rights of the Jews or of Caesar. As for the commonwealth that then existed among the Jews: both those who ruled and those who were governed were all expecting the Messiah and the kingdom of God — which they could not have done if their laws had forbidden him, when he came, to manifest and declare himself. Since he did nothing but go about proving himself, by preaching and miracles, to be that very Messiah, he did nothing in this against their laws. The kingdom he claimed was to be in another world. He taught all men to obey, in the meantime, those who sat in Moses' seat. He allowed them to give Caesar his tribute, and he refused to take it upon himself to be a judge. How then could his words or actions be seditious, or tend to the overthrow of their civil government at the time?

But God had determined Christ's sacrifice as the means of bringing his elect back to their formerly covenanted obedience; and as the instrument for bringing this about, he made use of their malice and ingratitude. Nor was any of it contrary to the laws of Caesar. For though Pilate himself, to gratify the Jews, delivered him up to be crucified, yet before he did so, he pronounced openly that he found no fault in him. And as the title of his condemnation, Pilate put not what the Jews demanded — "that he pretended to be king" — but simply, "that he was King of the Jews"; and despite their clamor, he refused to alter it, saying, "What I have written, I have written."

The Third Part of His Office: To Be King — Under His Father — of the Elect

As for the third part of his office, which was to be king, I have already shown that his kingdom is not to begin until the resurrection. But then he shall be king — not only as God, in which sense he is king already, and ever shall be, of all the earth, by virtue of his omnipotence; but also, in a special way, king of his own elect, by virtue of the pact they make with him in their baptism. That is why our Savior says (Matthew 19:28) that his apostles shall sit upon twelve thrones, judging the twelve tribes of Israel, "when the Son of man shall sit on the throne of his glory" — signifying that he shall reign then in his human nature. And again: "The Son of man shall come in the glory of his Father, with his angels, and then he shall reward every man according to his works" (Matthew 16:27). We may read the same in Mark 13:26 and 14:26, and — more expressly as to the time — in Luke 22:29-30: "I appoint unto you a kingdom, as my Father has appointed to me, that you may eat and drink at my table in my kingdom, and sit on thrones judging the twelve tribes of Israel." From all this it is plain that the kingdom of Christ, appointed to him by his Father, is not to be until the Son of man comes in glory and makes his apostles judges of the twelve tribes of Israel.

But here a man may ask: since there is no marriage in the kingdom of heaven, shall men really eat and drink there? What kind of eating is meant in this place? Our Savior expounds it himself: "Labor not for the meat which perishes, but for that meat which endures unto everlasting life, which the Son of man shall give you" (John 6:27). So eating at Christ's table means eating of the tree of life — that is to say, enjoying immortality in the kingdom of the Son of man. From these passages, and many more, it is evident that our Savior's kingdom is to be exercised by him in his human nature.

Christ's Authority in the Kingdom of God Is Subordinate to His Father

Again, he is to be king then in no other way than as subordinate — a viceregent of God the Father, as Moses was in the wilderness, as the high priests were before the reign of Saul, and as the kings were after it. For it is one of the prophecies concerning Christ that he should be like Moses in his office: "I will raise them up a Prophet from among their brethren, like unto thee, and will put my words into his mouth," says the Lord (Deuteronomy 18:18).

And this likeness to Moses shows plainly in the actions of our Savior himself while he lived on earth. As Moses chose twelve princes of the tribes to govern under him, so our Savior chose twelve apostles, who shall sit on twelve thrones and judge the twelve tribes of Israel. As Moses authorized seventy elders to receive the Spirit of God and to prophesy to the people — that is, as I have said before, to speak to them in the name of God — so our Savior also ordained seventy disciples to preach his kingdom and salvation to all nations. And as Moses, when a complaint was brought to him against certain of the seventy who prophesied in the camp of Israel, justified them in it, as serving under his government, so our Savior, when St. John complained to him of a certain man who cast out devils in his name, justified the man, saying, "Forbid him not, for he that is not against us is on our side" (Luke 9:50).

Again, our Savior resembled Moses in instituting sacraments — both the sacrament of admission into the kingdom of God, and the sacrament of commemoration of his deliverance of his elect from their miserable condition.

As the children of Israel had, for the sacrament of their reception into the kingdom of God before the time of Moses, the rite of circumcision — a rite that was omitted in the wilderness, and restored as soon as they came into the promised land — so also the Jews, before the coming of our Savior, had a rite of baptizing: washing with water all those Gentiles who embraced the God of Israel. St. John the Baptist used this rite in receiving all those who gave their names to the Christ, whom he preached as already come into the world; and our Savior instituted the same rite as a sacrament to be taken by all who believed in him.

Where the rite of baptism first came from is not formally stated in Scripture. But it may plausibly be thought an imitation of the law of Moses concerning leprosy, by which a leprous man was commanded to be kept out of the camp of Israel for a certain time; after which, being judged clean by the priest, he was admitted into the camp again after a solemn washing. This may well be a type of the washing in baptism, in which men cleansed of the leprosy of sin by faith are received into the Church with the solemnity of baptism. There is another conjecture, drawn from the ceremonies of the pagans of the past, in a case that rarely happens: when a man who was thought dead chanced to recover, other men were reluctant to keep company with him — as they would be reluctant to keep company with a ghost — unless he were received back into the number of men by washing, just as newborn children were washed from the uncleanness of their birth, which was a kind of new birth. This Greek ceremony, in the days when Judaea was under the dominion of Alexander and the Greeks who succeeded him, may plausibly enough have crept into the religion of the Jews. But since it is not likely our Savior would countenance a heathen rite, it is most likely that baptism proceeded from the legal ceremony of washing after leprosy.

As for the other sacrament — the eating of the paschal lamb — it is manifestly imitated in the sacrament of the Lord's Supper, in which the breaking of the bread and the pouring out of the wine keep in memory our deliverance from the misery of sin by Christ's passion, just as the eating of the paschal lamb kept in memory the deliverance of the Jews out of the bondage of Egypt.

Since the authority of Moses, then, was only subordinate — he was but a lieutenant to God — it follows that Christ, whose authority as man was to be like that of Moses, was no more than subordinate to the authority of his Father. The same is signified even more expressly by his teaching us to pray, "Our Father, thy kingdom come," and "For thine is the kingdom, the power, and the glory"; by the saying that he "shall come in the glory of his Father"; by what St. Paul says — "then comes the end, when he shall have delivered up the kingdom to God, even the Father" (1 Corinthians 15:24) — and by many other most express passages.

One and the Same God Is the Person Represented by Moses and by Christ

Our Savior, therefore, both in teaching and in reigning, represents — as Moses did — the person of God; the God who from that time forward, but not before, is called the Father. And being still one and the same substance, God is one person as represented by Moses, and another person as represented by his Son, the Christ. For since "person" is a term relative to a representer, a plurality of representers necessarily brings with it a plurality of persons — even though the substance represented is one and the same.

Chapter 42: Of Ecclesiastical Power

To understand ECCLESIASTICAL POWER — what it is, and who holds it — we have to divide the time since our Savior's ascension into two periods: one before kings and men holding sovereign civil power converted to Christianity, the other after their conversion. For it was a long time after the ascension before any king or civil sovereign embraced the Christian religion and publicly allowed it to be taught.

The Holy Spirit That Fell on the Apostles

For the time in between, it is plain where ecclesiastical power resided: in the apostles; and after them, in those whom the apostles ordained to preach the gospel — to convert men to Christianity and to direct the converted in the way of salvation; and after these, the power was handed on again to others, whom they in turn ordained. This was done by the laying on of hands upon those ordained, which signified the giving of the Holy Spirit, the Spirit of God, to those they made ministers of God, to advance his kingdom. So the laying on of hands was nothing else but the seal of their commission to preach Christ and teach his doctrine; and the giving of the Holy Spirit by that ceremony of laying on hands was an imitation of what Moses did. For Moses used the same ceremony with his minister Joshua, as we read: "And Joshua the son of Nun was full of the spirit of wisdom; for Moses had laid his hands upon him" (Deuteronomy 34:9). Our Savior, then, gave his Spirit to the apostles between his resurrection and his ascension: first by breathing on them and saying, "Receive ye the Holy Spirit" (John 20:22); and after his ascension, by sending down upon them "a mighty wind, and cloven tongues of fire" (Acts 2:2-3) — not by the laying on of hands, just as God laid no hands on Moses. And the apostles afterward transmitted that same Spirit by the laying on of hands, as Moses did to Joshua. So it is clear from this in whom ecclesiastical power continually remained in those first times, when there was no Christian commonwealth: namely, in those who received it from the apostles by the successive laying on of hands.

The Trinity

Here we have the person of God borne now a third time. For as Moses and the high priests were God's representative in the Old Testament, and our Savior himself, as man, was during his stay on earth, so the Holy Spirit — that is to say, the apostles and their successors in the office of preaching and teaching, who had received the Holy Spirit — have represented him ever since. But a person (as I showed back in chapter 16) is the one who is represented, as often as he is represented. And therefore God, who has been represented — that is, personated — three times, may properly enough be said to be three persons, even though neither the word "person" nor the word "Trinity" is ever applied to him in the Bible. St. John does indeed say, "There are three that bear witness in heaven: the Father, the Word, and the Holy Spirit; and these three are one" (1 John 5:7). But this does not disagree with three persons in the proper meaning of the word "person" — which is, that which is represented by another — rather, it fits exactly. For God the Father, as represented by Moses, is one person; as represented by his Son, another person; and as represented by the apostles, and by the teachers who taught by authority derived from them, a third person. And yet every person here is the person of one and the same God.

But someone may ask here: what was it that these three bore witness to? St. John tells us (verse 11): they bear witness that "God has given us eternal life in his Son." And if it should be asked where that testimony shows itself, the answer is easy. He testified to it by the miracles he worked — first through Moses, secondly through his Son himself, and lastly through his apostles, who had received the Holy Spirit. All of these, each in their own time, represented the person of God, and either prophesied of Jesus Christ or preached him. As for the apostles, it was the very mark of apostleship, in the twelve first and great apostles, to bear witness to his resurrection — as appears expressly where St. Peter, when a new apostle was to be chosen in place of Judas Iscariot, uses these words: "Of these men which have companied with us all the time that the Lord Jesus went in and out amongst us, beginning at the baptism of John, unto that same day that he was taken up from us, must one be ordained to be a witness with us of his resurrection" (Acts 1:21-22). Those words interpret the "bearing of witness" mentioned by St. John.

In the same place St. John mentions another trinity of witnesses — on earth. For he says, "There are three that bear witness in earth: the Spirit, and the water, and the blood; and these three agree in one" (verse 8). That is to say: the graces of God's Spirit, and the two sacraments, baptism and the Lord's Supper, which all agree in one testimony — to assure the consciences of believers of eternal life. Of this testimony he says, "He that believeth on the Son of man hath the witness in himself" (verse 10). In this trinity on earth, the unity is not a unity of the thing itself, for the Spirit, the water, and the blood are not the same substance, though they give the same testimony. But in the trinity of heaven, the persons are the persons of one and the same God, though represented on three different times and occasions.

To sum up: the doctrine of the Trinity, as far as it can be gathered directly from Scripture, is in substance this — that God, who is always one and the same, was the person represented by Moses; the person represented by his Son incarnate; and the person represented by the apostles. As represented by the apostles, the Holy Spirit by which they spoke is God. As represented by his Son, who was God and man, the Son is that God. As represented by Moses and the high priests, the Father — that is to say, the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ — is that God. And from this we can gather the reason why the names Father, Son, and Holy Spirit, as signifying the Godhead, are never used in the Old Testament. For they are persons — that is, they take their names from representing — and that could not happen until different men had represented God's person in ruling, or in directing under him.

Thus we see how ecclesiastical power was left by our Savior to the apostles, and how — so that they might exercise that power the better — they were endowed with the Holy Spirit. That is why the Spirit is sometimes called in the New Testament Paracletus — an assister, one called in for help — though it is commonly translated "Comforter." Let us now consider the power itself: what it was, and over whom.

Ecclesiastical Power Is Only the Power to Teach

Cardinal Bellarmine, in his third general Controversy, has handled a great many questions concerning the ecclesiastical power of the Pope of Rome, and he begins with this one: whether it ought to be monarchical, aristocratic, or democratic. All those kinds of power are sovereign and coercive. So if it should turn out that our Savior left them no coercive power at all, but only a power to proclaim the kingdom of Christ and to persuade men to submit to it; and, by precepts and good counsel, to teach those who have submitted what they must do to be received into the kingdom of God when it comes; and that the apostles and the other ministers of the gospel are our schoolmasters and not our commanders, their precepts not laws but wholesome counsel — then that whole dispute was in vain.

An Argument for This: The Power of Christ Himself

I have already shown (in the last chapter) that the kingdom of Christ is not of this world. Therefore neither can his ministers — unless they are kings — require obedience in his name. For if the supreme King does not exercise his royal power in this world, by what authority can obedience be required toward his officers? "As my Father sent me," says our Savior, "so send I you." But our Savior was sent to persuade the Jews to return to the kingdom of his Father, and to invite the Gentiles to receive it — not to reign in majesty, not even as his Father's lieutenant, until the day of judgment.

From the Name "Regeneration"

The time between the ascension and the general resurrection is called not a reigning but a regeneration — that is, a preparation of men for the second and glorious coming of Christ at the day of judgment. This appears in the words of our Savior — "You that have followed me in the regeneration, when the Son of man shall sit in the throne of his glory, you shall also sit upon twelve thrones" (Matthew 19:28) — and in the words of St. Paul: "Having your feet shod with the preparation of the gospel of peace" (Ephesians 6:15).

From Its Comparison to Fishing, Leaven, and Seed

Our Savior also compares this work to fishing — that is, to winning men to obedience not by coercion and punishment, but by persuasion. That is why he did not tell his apostles he would make them so many Nimrods, hunters of men, but fishers of men. It is compared also to leaven, to the sowing of seed, and to the multiplication of a grain of mustard seed. All of these images exclude compulsion; and consequently, in this time, there can be no actual reigning. The work of Christ's ministers is evangelization — that is, a proclamation of Christ and a preparation for his second coming, just as the evangelization of John the Baptist was a preparation for his first.

From the Nature of Faith

Again, the office of Christ's ministers in this world is to make men believe and have faith in Christ. But faith has no relation to compulsion or command, and no dependence on them at all. It rests only on certainty, or on the probability of arguments drawn from reason, or from things men believe already. Therefore the ministers of Christ in this world have no power, by that title, to punish any man for not believing, or for contradicting what they say. By the title of Christ's ministers, I say, they have no such power. But if they hold sovereign civil power by political institution, then they may indeed lawfully punish any contradiction of their laws whatsoever. And St. Paul, speaking of himself and of the other preachers of the gospel of that time, says in express words: "We have no dominion over your faith, but are helpers of your joy" (2 Corinthians 1:24).

From the Authority Christ Has Left to Civil Rulers

Another argument that the ministers of Christ in this present world have no right of commanding can be drawn from the lawful authority Christ has left to all princes, Christian and infidel alike. St. Paul says, "Children, obey your parents in all things, for this is well pleasing to the Lord" (Colossians 3:20). And again: "Servants, obey in all things your masters according to the flesh, not with eye-service, as men-pleasers, but in singleness of heart, as fearing the Lord" (verse 22). This is spoken to men whose masters were infidels — and yet they are told to obey them in all things. Again, concerning obedience to princes, exhorting us to "be subject to the higher powers," he says "that all power is ordained of God," and that we ought to be subject to them not only for fear of incurring their wrath, "but also for conscience' sake" (Romans 13:1-6). And St. Peter: "Submit yourselves to every ordinance of man, for the Lord's sake, whether it be to the king, as supreme, or unto governors, as to them that are sent by him for the punishment of evildoers, and for the praise of them that do well; for so is the will of God" (1 Peter 2:13-15). And St. Paul again: "Put men in mind to be subject to principalities and powers, and to obey magistrates" (Titus 3:1). These princes and powers, of whom St. Peter and St. Paul here speak, were all infidels. How much more, then, are we to obey those Christians whom God has ordained to hold sovereign power over us? How can we be obliged to do anything contrary to the command of the king, or other sovereign representative of the commonwealth of which we are members, and from whom we look for protection? It is therefore plain that Christ has not left his ministers in this world any authority to command other men — unless they are also endowed with civil authority.

What Christians May Do to Avoid Persecution

But someone may object: what if a king, or a senate, or some other sovereign person forbids us to believe in Christ? To this I answer that such forbidding has no effect, because belief and unbelief never follow men's commands. Faith is a gift of God, which man can neither give nor take away by promises of reward or threats of torture.

And if it is asked further — what if our lawful prince commands us to say with our tongue that we do not believe? Must we obey such a command? Profession with the tongue is only an external thing — no more than any other gesture by which we signify our obedience. A Christian who holds firmly in his heart the faith of Christ has, in that case, the same liberty the prophet Elisha allowed to Naaman the Syrian. Naaman was converted in his heart to the God of Israel, for he says: "Thy servant will henceforth offer neither burnt offering nor sacrifice unto other gods, but unto the Lord. In this thing the Lord pardon thy servant: that when my master goeth into the house of Rimmon to worship there, and he leaneth on my hand, and I bow myself in the house of Rimmon — when I bow myself in the house of Rimmon, the Lord pardon thy servant in this thing" (2 Kings 5:17). The prophet approved this, and told him, "Go in peace." Here Naaman believed in his heart; but by bowing before the idol Rimmon, he denied the true God in effect just as much as if he had done it with his lips.

But then what shall we answer to our Savior's saying, "Whosoever denies me before men, I will deny him before my Father which is in heaven"? We may say this: whatever a subject is compelled to do in obedience to his sovereign — as Naaman was — doing it not from his own mind but in deference to the laws of his country, that action is not his, but his sovereign's. Nor is it he who in that case denies Christ before men, but his governor, and the law of his country.

If any man accuses this doctrine of being contrary to true and sincere Christianity, I put a question to him. Suppose there were a subject in some Christian commonwealth who was inwardly, in his heart, of the Muslim religion, and his sovereign commanded him, on pain of death, to attend the divine service of the Christian church. Does he think that man is obliged in conscience to suffer death for that cause, rather than obey the command of his lawful prince? If he says the man ought rather to suffer death, then he authorizes every private man to disobey his prince in defense of his religion, true or false. If he says the man ought to obey, then he is allowing to himself what he denies to another — contrary to the words of our Savior, "Whatsoever you would that men should do unto you, that do ye unto them," and contrary to the law of nature, which is the indubitable, everlasting law of God: "Do not that to another, which thou wouldest not have done to thyself."

Martyrs

But what then shall we say of all the martyrs we read of in the history of the Church? Did they throw their lives away needlessly? To answer this, we have to distinguish among the persons who have been put to death for that cause. Some of them had received a calling to preach and to profess the kingdom of Christ openly; others had no such calling, and nothing more was required of them than their own faith.

The former sort, if they were put to death for bearing witness to this point — that Jesus Christ is risen from the dead — were true martyrs. For a MARTYR is (to give the word its true definition) a witness of the resurrection of Jesus the Messiah; and no one can be such a witness except those who lived alongside him on earth and saw him after he was risen. For a witness must have seen what he testifies to, or else his testimony is no good. That none but such can properly be called martyrs of Christ is plain from the words of St. Peter: "Wherefore of these men which have companied with us all the time that the Lord Jesus went in and out amongst us, beginning from the baptism of John unto that same day he was taken up from us, must one be ordained to be a martyr (that is, a witness) with us of his resurrection" (Acts 1:21-22). Notice here: the one who is to be a witness of the truth of the resurrection of Christ — that is to say, of the truth of this fundamental article of the Christian religion, that Jesus was the Christ — must be some disciple who lived with him, and saw him before and after his resurrection; and consequently must be one of his original disciples. Those who came later can witness no more than that their predecessors said it. They are witnesses only of other men's testimony — second martyrs, so to speak: martyrs of Christ's witnesses.

A man who, in order to maintain every doctrine he himself draws out of the history of our Savior's life, or out of the Acts or the Epistles of the apostles — or which he believes on the authority of some private man — will oppose the laws and authority of the civil state, is very far from being a martyr of Christ, or even a martyr of his martyrs. There is one article only that earns a man so honorable a name when he dies for it, and that article is this: JESUS IS THE CHRIST — that is to say, the one who has redeemed us, and who shall come again to give us salvation and eternal life in his glorious kingdom. To die for every tenet that serves the ambition or the profit of the clergy is not required. Nor is it the death of the witness that makes the martyr, but the testimony itself; for the word means nothing else but a man who bears witness, whether he is put to death for his testimony or not.

Also, a man who has not been sent to preach this fundamental article, but takes it upon himself by his private authority — even though he is a witness, and consequently a martyr, whether a primary martyr of Christ or a secondary martyr of his apostles, disciples, or their successors — is not obliged to suffer death for that cause. Not being called to it, it is not required at his hands; nor should he complain if he loses the reward he expects from those who never set him to work. No one, therefore, can be a martyr of either the first or the second degree who has no warrant to preach Christ come in the flesh — that is to say, no one but those who are sent to the conversion of unbelievers. For no man is a witness to him who already believes, and therefore needs no witness — only to those who deny it, or doubt it, or have not heard it. Christ sent his apostles and his seventy disciples with authority to preach; he did not send everyone who believed. And he sent them to unbelievers: "I send you," he says, "as sheep amongst wolves" — not as sheep to other sheep.

The Argument from the Terms of Their Commission

Lastly, the points of their commission, as they are expressly set down in the gospel, contain — not one of them — any authority over the congregation.

To Preach

First, we have it (Matthew 10) that the twelve apostles were sent "to the lost sheep of the house of Israel" and commanded to preach "that the kingdom of God was at hand." Now preaching, in the original, is the act a crier, herald, or other officer performs when he publicly proclaims a king. But a crier has no right to command anyone. Likewise the seventy disciples are sent out "as laborers, not as lords of the harvest" (Luke 10:2), and are told to say, "The kingdom of God is come nigh unto you" (verse 9). And the kingdom meant here is not the kingdom of grace but the kingdom of glory; for they are commanded to pronounce it as a threat against the cities that will not receive them — that it shall be more tolerable on that day for Sodom than for such a city (verse 11). And our Savior tells his disciples, who were seeking priority of place, that their office was to minister, "even as the Son of man came not to be ministered unto, but to minister" (Matthew 20:28). Preachers, therefore, have not magisterial power but ministerial — they serve; they do not rule. "Be not called masters," says our Savior, "for one is your master, even Christ" (Matthew 23:10).

And to Teach

Another point of their commission is to teach all nations — as it stands in Matthew 28:19, or as St. Mark has it, "Go into all the world, and preach the gospel to every creature" (Mark 16:15). Teaching and preaching, then, are the same thing. For those who proclaim the coming of a king must also make known by what right he comes, if they expect men to submit themselves to him. So St. Paul did with the Jews of Thessalonica, when "three Sabbath days he reasoned with them out of the Scriptures, opening and alleging that Christ must needs have suffered, and risen again from the dead, and that this Jesus is Christ." But to teach out of the Old Testament that Jesus was the Christ — that is to say, the king — and that he rose from the dead, is not to say that men, once they believe it, are bound to obey those who tell them so against the laws and commands of their sovereigns. It is to say that they will do wisely to await the coming of Christ hereafter in patience and faith, with obedience to their present magistrates.

To Baptize

Another point of their commission is to baptize, "in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost." What is baptism? Dipping into water. But what is it to dip a man into water in the name of anything? The meaning of these words of baptism is this. He that is baptized is dipped, or washed, as a sign of becoming a new man and a loyal subject: to that God whose person was represented in old time by Moses and the high priests, when he reigned over the Jews; and to Jesus Christ his Son, God and man, who has redeemed us, and who shall, in his human nature, represent his Father's person in his eternal kingdom after the resurrection; and as a sign that he acknowledges the doctrine of the apostles — who, assisted by the Spirit of the Father and of the Son, were left as guides to bring us into that kingdom — to be the only sure way there. This is our promise in baptism. And the authority of earthly sovereigns is not to be put down until the day of judgment — for that is expressly affirmed by St. Paul, where he says: "As in Adam all die, so in Christ shall all be made alive. But every man in his own order: Christ the firstfruits, afterward they that are Christ's at his coming; then cometh the end, when he shall have delivered up the kingdom to God, even the Father, when he shall have put down all rule, and all authority and power" (1 Corinthians 15:22-24). So it is plain that in baptism we do not set up over ourselves another authority by which our external actions are to be governed in this life; we promise to take the doctrine of the apostles as our guide on the way to eternal life.

And to Forgive and Retain Sins

The power of remitting and retaining sins — called also the power of loosing and binding, and sometimes the KEYS OF THE KINGDOM OF HEAVEN — is a consequence of the authority to baptize, or to refuse baptism. For baptism is the sacrament of allegiance of those who are to be received into the kingdom of God — that is to say, into eternal life; that is to say, into the remission of sin. For as eternal life was lost by the committing of men's sins, so it is recovered by their remission. The end of baptism is the remission of sins. That is why St. Peter, when those converted by his sermon on the day of Pentecost asked him what they were to do, advised them to "repent, and be baptized in the name of Jesus, for the remission of sins." And therefore, since to baptize is to declare a man's reception into God's kingdom, and to refuse baptism is to declare his exclusion, it follows that the power to declare men cast out of it, or retained in it, was given to the same apostles, and to their substitutes and successors. And so, after our Savior had breathed upon them, saying, "Receive the Holy Ghost" (John 20:22), he adds in the next verse: "Whose soever sins ye remit, they are remitted unto them; and whose soever sins ye retain, they are retained."

These words do not grant an authority to forgive or retain sins simply and absolutely, as God forgives or retains them — God, who knows the heart of man and the truth of his penitence and conversion. They grant it conditionally: to the penitent. And this forgiveness, or absolution, in case the absolved man's repentance is feigned, is by that very fact made void — without any further act or sentence by the absolver — and has no effect at all toward salvation, but on the contrary toward the aggravation of his sin. Therefore the apostles and their successors are to follow only the outward marks of repentance. Where those marks appear, they have no authority to deny absolution; where they do not appear, they have no authority to absolve. The same is to be observed in baptism: the apostles had no power to deny baptism to a converted Jew or Gentile, nor to grant it to the unrepentant.

But since no man can discern the truth of another man's repentance further than by external marks, drawn from his words and actions — which are subject to hypocrisy — another question arises: who is constituted judge of those marks? And this question our Savior himself decides: "If thy brother shall trespass against thee," he says, "go and tell him his fault between thee and him alone; if he shall hear thee, thou hast gained thy brother. But if he will not hear thee, then take with thee one or two more. And if he shall neglect to hear them, tell it unto the church; let him be unto thee as a heathen man, and a publican" (Matthew 18:15-17). From this it is plain that the judgment concerning the truth of repentance belonged not to any one man, but to the Church — that is, to the assembly of the faithful, or to those who have authority to be their representative. But besides the judgment, the pronouncing of the sentence is also necessary; and this always belonged to the apostle, or to some pastor of the church, as its spokesman. Of this our Savior speaks in verse 18: "Whatsoever ye shall bind on earth shall be bound in heaven; and whatsoever ye shall loose on earth shall be loosed in heaven."

And the practice of St. Paul conforms to this, where he says: "For I verily, as absent in body, but present in spirit, have determined already, as though I were present, concerning him that hath so done this deed: in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, when ye are gathered together, and my spirit, with the power of our Lord Jesus Christ, to deliver such a one to Satan" (1 Corinthians 5:3-5) — that is to say, to cast him out of the Church, as a man whose sins are not forgiven. Paul here pronounces the sentence; but the assembly was first to hear the cause (for St. Paul was absent), and consequently to condemn him. But in the same chapter the judgment in such a case is attributed even more expressly to the assembly: "But now I have written unto you not to keep company, if any man that is called a brother be a fornicator, etc. — with such a one, no, not to eat. For what have I to do to judge them that are without? Do not ye judge them that are within?" (verses 11-12). The sentence by which a man was put out of the Church, then, was pronounced by the apostle or pastor; but the judgment on the merits of the case lay with the Church — that is to say (these being the times before the conversion of kings and of men holding sovereign authority in the commonwealth), with the assembly of the Christians dwelling in the same city. In Corinth, for instance, with the assembly of the Christians of Corinth.

Excommunication

This part of the power of the keys, by which men were thrust out of the kingdom of God, is what is called EXCOMMUNICATION; and to excommunicate is, in the original, aposunagogon poiein — to cast out of the synagogue, that is, out of the place of divine service. The word is drawn from the custom of the Jews of casting out of their synagogues anyone they considered contagious in conduct or in doctrine — just as lepers, by the law of Moses, were separated from the congregation of Israel until the priest should pronounce them clean.

The Use of Excommunication Without Civil Power

While excommunication was not yet reinforced with civil power, its whole use and effect was this: those who were not excommunicated were to avoid the company of those who were. It was not enough to regard them as heathens who had never been Christians, for with heathens one might eat and drink — which with excommunicated persons one might not do. This appears in the words of St. Paul (1 Corinthians 5:9-10), where he tells them he had formerly forbidden them to keep company with fornicators; but because that could not be done without going out of the world altogether, he restricts the ban to such fornicators and otherwise vicious persons as were of the brethren — "with such a one," he says, they ought not to keep company, "no, not to eat." And this is no more than what our Savior says: "Let him be to thee as a heathen, and as a publican" (Matthew 18:17). For publicans — tax farmers, the collectors of the commonwealth's revenue — were so hated and detested by the Jews who had to pay them that "publican" and "sinner" were taken among them for the same thing; so much so that when our Savior accepted the invitation of Zacchaeus, a publican, it was held against him as a crime, even though his purpose was to convert him. And therefore, when our Savior added "publican" to "heathen," he was forbidding them to eat with an excommunicated man.

As for keeping excommunicated men out of their synagogues, or places of assembly, the Church had no power to do it except the power of whoever owned the place, whether Christian or heathen. And because all places are, by right, in the dominion of the commonwealth, the man who had been excommunicated, no less than the man who had never been baptized, might enter them by commission from the civil magistrate — as Paul, before his conversion, entered the synagogues at Damascus (Acts 9:2) to arrest Christians, men and women, and carry them bound to Jerusalem, by commission from the high priest.

It Has No Effect on an Apostate

From this it appears that upon a Christian who turned apostate, in a place where the civil power persecuted the Church, or declined to assist it, excommunication carried nothing — neither damage in this world, nor terror. No terror, because of their unbelief; no damage, because casting them out simply returned them to the favor of the world, and in the world to come they would be no worse off than those who had never believed. The damage fell rather on the Church: casting them out provoked them to a freer exercise of their malice.

Only on the Faithful

Excommunication, therefore, had its effect only on those who believed that Jesus Christ was to come again in glory, to reign over and to judge both the living and the dead, and that he would refuse entrance into his kingdom to those whose sins were retained — that is, to those who had been excommunicated by the Church. And that is why St. Paul calls excommunication a delivering of the excommunicated person to Satan: for outside the kingdom of Christ, all other kingdoms after the judgment are comprehended in the kingdom of Satan. This is what the faithful stood in fear of, so long as they stood excommunicated — that is to say, in a state in which their sins were not forgiven. From this we can understand that excommunication, in the time when the Christian religion was not authorized by the civil power, was used only for the correction of conduct, not of errors of opinion. For it is a punishment that no one could feel except those who believed in, and expected, our Savior's coming again to judge the world; and those who so believed needed no other opinion — only uprightness of life — to be saved.

For What Faults Excommunication Applies

Excommunication applies to injustice — as in Matthew 18: if your brother offends you, tell him privately; then with witnesses; lastly, tell the church; and then, if he still does not obey, "let him be to thee as a heathen man, and a publican." And excommunication applies to a scandalous life, as in 1 Corinthians 5:11: "If any man that is called a brother be a fornicator, or covetous, or an idolater, or a drunkard, or an extortioner — with such a one ye are not to eat." But for excommunicating a man who held this foundation — that Jesus was the Christ — over a difference of opinion on other points, by which that foundation was not destroyed, there appears no authority in Scripture, and no example in the apostles.

There is, indeed, a text in St. Paul that seems to say the contrary: "A man that is a heretic, after the first and second admonition, reject" (Titus 3:10). For a HERETIC is one who, though a member of the Church, nevertheless teaches some private opinion the Church has forbidden; and such a man, St. Paul advises Titus, after a first and second admonition, to reject. But "to reject," in this place, is not to excommunicate the man. It is to give up admonishing him, to let him alone, to set aside disputing with him, as one who can only be convinced by himself. The same apostle says, "Foolish and unlearned questions avoid" (2 Timothy 2:23); and the word "avoid" in this place and "reject" in the other are the same word in the original, paraitou — but foolish questions may be set aside without excommunication. And again, "Avoid foolish questions" (Titus 3:9), where the original word, periistaso — set them by — is equivalent to the former word, "reject."

There is no other passage that can be drawn, with even a show of plausibility, to countenance the casting out of the Church of faithful men — men who believed the foundation, and differed only in some singular structure of their own built on top of it, proceeding perhaps from a good and pious conscience. On the contrary, all the passages that command the avoiding of such disputes are written as a lesson to pastors (such as Timothy and Titus were): not to make new articles of faith by settling every small controversy, which burdens men with a needless load on their consciences, or provokes them to break the union of the Church. And this lesson the apostles themselves observed well. St. Peter and St. Paul, though their controversy was great (as we may read in Galatians 2:11), did not cast one another out of the Church. Nevertheless, even in the apostles' time there were other pastors who did not observe it — such as Diotrephes (3 John 9), who cast out of the church men whom St. John himself thought fit to be received into it, out of the pride he took in preeminence. So early had vainglory and ambition found their way into the Church of Christ.

Who Can Be Excommunicated

For a man to be liable to excommunication at all, several conditions have to be met. First, he must be a member of some community — that is, of some lawful assembly, that is, of some Christian church that has the power to judge the matter for which he is to be excommunicated. Where there is no community, there can be no excommunication; and where there is no power to judge, there can be no power to pass sentence.

From this it follows that one church cannot excommunicate another. Either the two churches have equal power to excommunicate each other — in which case excommunication is not discipline, not an act of authority, but schism and the breakdown of charity — or one church is subordinate to the other in such a way that the two speak with a single voice, in which case they are really one church, and the part that gets excommunicated is no longer a church at all, but a scattered crowd of individual persons.

And because the sentence of excommunication amounts to advice not to keep company with the excommunicated person, not even to eat with him, the sentence has no effect if the one excommunicated is a sovereign prince or assembly. All subjects are bound by the law of nature to be in the company and presence of their own sovereign whenever he requires it. They cannot lawfully expel him from any place in his own dominion, sacred or ordinary; they cannot leave his dominion without his permission; and least of all, if he invites them to that honor, can they refuse to eat with him. As for other princes and states: since they are not parts of one and the same congregation, they need no sentence to keep them away from the excommunicated state. The very institution of a commonwealth, which unites many men into one community, also separates one community from another. So excommunication is not needed for keeping kings and states apart; it has no effect beyond what lies in the nature of politics itself — unless its effect is to goad princes into making war on one another.

Nor does the excommunication of a Christian subject have any effect, so long as he obeys the laws of his own sovereign, whether that sovereign is Christian or heathen. For if he believes that "Jesus is the Christ, he has the Spirit of God" (1 John 4:1), "and God dwells in him, and he in God" (1 John 4:15). But a man who has the Spirit of God, who dwells in God, in whom God dwells, can receive no harm from the excommunication of men. Therefore, whoever believes Jesus to be the Christ is free from all the dangers threatened against the excommunicated. And whoever does not believe it is no Christian in the first place. So a true and sincere Christian is not liable to excommunication. Neither is a professed Christian, until his hypocrisy shows in his behavior — that is, until his conduct goes against the law of his sovereign, which is the rule of behavior, and which Christ and his apostles have commanded us to obey. The church cannot judge a man's morals except by his external actions, and external actions can never be unlawful except when they break the law of the commonwealth.

If a man's father, or mother, or master is excommunicated, the children are still not forbidden to keep them company or to eat with them. To forbid that would, in most cases, be to oblige them not to eat at all, since they have no other way to get food — and it would authorize them to disobey their parents and masters, contrary to the teaching of the apostles.

In sum: the power of excommunication cannot be stretched any further than the purpose for which the apostles and pastors of the church hold their commission from our Savior — which is not to rule by command and coercion, but to teach and to direct men on the road to salvation in the world to come. A master of any science may give up on a student who stubbornly refuses to practice his rules; but he cannot accuse the student of injustice, because the student was never bound to obey him. In the same way, a teacher of Christian doctrine may abandon disciples who stubbornly persist in an unchristian life; but he cannot say they have wronged him, because they are not obliged to obey him. To a teacher who complains that way, one may apply God's answer to Samuel in a similar case: "They have not rejected thee, but me" (1 Samuel 8).

Excommunication, then, when it lacks the backing of the civil power — as it does whenever a Christian state or prince is excommunicated by some foreign authority — is without effect, and consequently ought to be without terror. The name fulmen excommunicationis — the thunderbolt of excommunication — came from a fantasy of the Bishop of Rome who first used it: that he was King of Kings, just as the pagans made Jupiter king of the gods and gave him, in their poems and pictures, a thunderbolt to subdue and punish any giants who dared deny his power. That fantasy rested on two errors. The first: that the kingdom of Christ is of this world — contrary to our Savior's own words, "My kingdom is not of this world." The second: that the Pope is Christ's vicar not only over his own subjects, but over all the Christians of the world — for which there is no basis in Scripture, and the contrary will be proved in its proper place.

Who Interpreted the Scriptures Before Civil Sovereigns Became Christian

When St. Paul came to Thessalonica, where there was a synagogue of the Jews, he, "as his manner was, went in unto them, and three Sabbath days reasoned with them out of the Scriptures, opening and alleging that Christ must needs have suffered and risen again from the dead, and that this Jesus whom he preached was the Christ" (Acts 17:2-3). The Scriptures mentioned here were the Scriptures of the Jews — the Old Testament. The men to whom he had to prove that Jesus was the Christ, risen from the dead, were also Jews, and they already believed those Scriptures were the word of God. The result: some of them believed (verse 4), and some did not (verse 5).

Why is that? They all believed the Scripture. Why did they not all believe alike — some accepting, others rejecting, the interpretation of St. Paul who cited it, each man interpreting it for himself? Here is the reason. St. Paul came to them with no legal commission, in the manner of a man who would not command but persuade. And persuade he must, either by miracles, as Moses did with the Israelites in Egypt, so that they might see his authority in God's works; or by reasoning from the Scripture they already accepted, so that they might see the truth of his doctrine in God's word. But whoever persuades by reasoning from written principles makes the person he is speaking to the judge — both of what those principles mean, and of how strong his inferences from them are. If these Jews of Thessalonica were not the judges of what St. Paul alleged out of Scripture, who else was? If St. Paul was the judge, why did he need to quote any passages at all to prove his doctrine? It would have been enough to say: I find it so in Scripture — that is, in your laws — of which I am the interpreter, sent by Christ. Therefore there was no interpreter of Scripture whose interpretation the Jews of Thessalonica were bound to accept. Every one of them was free to believe or not believe, according to whether the citations seemed to him to fit, or not to fit, the meaning of the passages cited.

And this holds generally, in every case in the world: whoever offers a proof makes the person he addresses the judge of that proof. As for the Jews in particular, they were bound by express words (Deuteronomy 17) to accept the resolution of all hard questions from the priests and judges of Israel then in office. But that applies to the Jews who were not yet converted.

For the conversion of the Gentiles, there was no point in citing Scriptures they did not believe. So the apostles labored by reason to refute their idolatry; and that done, they worked to persuade them to faith in Christ by their testimony of his life and resurrection. So at that stage there could not yet be any controversy about the authority to interpret Scripture — since no man, while still an unbeliever, was obliged to follow anyone's interpretation of any Scripture except his own sovereign's interpretation of the laws of his country.

Now consider the conversion itself, and ask what there was in it that could create such an obligation. Men were converted to nothing other than belief in what the apostles preached. And the apostles preached nothing but this: that Jesus was the Christ — that is, the King who was to save them and reign over them eternally in the world to come — and consequently that he was not dead but risen from the dead, gone up into heaven, and coming again one day to judge the world (which would also rise again to be judged), and to reward every man according to his works. None of them preached that he himself, or any other apostle, was an interpreter of Scripture whose interpretation everyone who became a Christian ought to take for law. To interpret the laws is part of administering a present kingdom — and the apostles had no present kingdom. They prayed, as all pastors have prayed ever since, "Thy kingdom come"; and they urged their converts to obey the pagan princes they then lived under.

The New Testament had not yet been published as one body. Each evangelist was the interpreter of his own gospel; each apostle, of his own epistle. And as for the Old Testament, our Savior himself said to the Jews, "Search the Scriptures; for in them ye think ye have eternal life, and they are they that testify of me" (John 5:39). If he had not meant for them to interpret the Scriptures themselves, he would not have told them to draw from them the proof that he was the Christ. He would either have interpreted the Scriptures himself, or referred them to the interpretation of the priests.

When a difficulty arose, the apostles and elders of the church assembled and settled what should be preached and taught, and how they should interpret the Scriptures to the people — but they did not take from the people the liberty to read the Scriptures and interpret them for themselves. The apostles sent various letters to the churches, and other writings for their instruction — which would have been pointless if the people had not been allowed to interpret them, that is, to consider what they meant. And as it was in the apostles' time, so it had to remain until there were pastors who could authorize an interpreter whose interpretation would generally be accepted as final. And that could not happen until kings were pastors, or pastors kings.

The Power to Make Scripture Law

There are two senses in which a writing may be called canonical. Canon means a rule; and a rule is a precept by which a man is guided and directed in any action whatsoever. Such precepts, even when given by a teacher to his student, or by a counselor to his friend, with no power to compel obedience, are still canons — because they are rules. But when they are given by someone whom the receiver is bound to obey, then those canons are not only rules but laws. The question here, therefore, is about the power to make the Scriptures — which are the rules of Christian faith — into laws.

The Ten Commandments

The first part of Scripture to become law was the Ten Commandments, written on two tablets of stone, delivered by God himself to Moses, and made known to the people by Moses. Before that time there was no written law of God. Having not yet chosen any people to be his peculiar kingdom, God had given men no law but the law of nature — that is, the precepts of natural reason, written in every man's own heart.

Of these two tablets, the first contains the law of sovereignty:

1. That they should not obey or honor the gods of other nations — "Non habebis Deos alienos coram me," that is, "Thou shalt not have for gods the gods that other nations worship, but only me." By this they were forbidden to obey or honor, as their king and governor, any god other than the one who spoke to them then through Moses, and afterward through the High Priest.

2. That they should not make any image to represent him. That is, they were not to pick out for themselves, in heaven or on earth, any representative of their own imagining, but were to obey Moses and Aaron, whom God had appointed to that office.

3. That they should not take the name of God in vain. That is, they should not speak rashly of their King, nor dispute his right, nor question the commissions of Moses and Aaron, his lieutenants.

4. That every seventh day they should abstain from their ordinary labor and spend that time doing him public honor.

The second tablet contains the duty of one man toward another: to honor parents; not to kill; not to commit adultery; not to steal; not to corrupt judgment by false witness; and finally, not even to plan in their hearts any injury to one another.

The question now is: who gave these written tablets the binding force of laws? There is no doubt that they were made laws by God himself. But a law does not bind anyone — is not law to anyone — except those who acknowledge it as the act of the sovereign. So how could the people of Israel, who were forbidden to approach the mountain to hear what God said to Moses, be obliged to obey all the laws Moses set before them? Some of those laws were indeed laws of nature — all of the second tablet — and so were to be acknowledged as God's laws not by the Israelites alone, but by all people. But for the laws peculiar to the Israelites, those of the first tablet, the question stands — except for this: immediately after the laws were proposed, the people had bound themselves to obey Moses, in these words: "Speak thou to us, and we will hear thee; but let not God speak to us, lest we die" (Exodus 20:19). It was therefore Moses alone at that time — and after him the High Priest, whom God (through Moses) declared should administer this his peculiar kingdom — who had the power on earth to make that short Scripture, the Ten Commandments, law in the commonwealth of Israel. But Moses, and Aaron, and the succeeding High Priests were the civil sovereigns. So up to this point, the canonizing of Scripture — the making of Scripture into law — belonged to the civil sovereign.

The Judicial and Levitical Law

The judicial law — the laws God prescribed to the magistrates of Israel as the rule for administering justice, and for the sentences and judgments they were to pronounce in disputes between man and man — and the Levitical law — the rule God prescribed concerning the rites and ceremonies of the priests and Levites — were all delivered to the people by Moses only; and so they too became laws by virtue of that same promise of obedience to Moses. Whether those laws were written down at the time, or simply dictated to the people by Moses by word of mouth after his forty days with God on the mountain, the text does not say. But they were all positive laws, equivalent to holy Scripture, and made canonical by Moses, the civil sovereign.

The Second Law

After the Israelites had come into the plains of Moab opposite Jericho, ready to enter the promised land, Moses added various other laws to the earlier ones. These are therefore called Deuteronomy — that is, the second laws. They are, as it is written, "the words of a covenant which the Lord commanded Moses to make with the children of Israel, besides the covenant which he made with them in Horeb" (Deuteronomy 29:1). For after explaining the earlier laws at the beginning of the book of Deuteronomy, he adds others, beginning at chapter 12 and continuing to the end of chapter 26 of the same book. This law the people were commanded to write upon great plastered stones at their crossing of the Jordan (Deuteronomy 27:1). This law was also written by Moses himself in a book and delivered into the hands of the priests and the elders of Israel (Deuteronomy 31:9), with the command (verse 26) that it be placed at the side of the Ark — for in the Ark itself there was nothing but the Ten Commandments. This was the law Moses commanded the kings of Israel to keep a copy of (Deuteronomy 17:18). And this is the law which, after being lost for a long time, was found again in the Temple in the days of Josiah, and by his authority received as the law of God. But note: both Moses at the writing of it, and Josiah at the recovery of it, held the civil sovereignty. So thus far, the power of making Scripture canonical lay with the civil sovereign.

Besides this book of the Law, no other book was received among the Jews as the law of God from the time of Moses until after the Captivity. The prophets, with few exceptions, lived during the Captivity itself, and the rest only a little before it; and so far were their prophecies from being generally received as laws that the prophets themselves were persecuted — partly by false prophets, and partly by kings whom those false prophets had seduced. And even this book, which Josiah confirmed as the law of God, was lost — along with all the history of the works of God — in the Captivity and the sack of Jerusalem, as appears from 2 Esdras 14:21: "Thy Law is burnt; therefore no man knoweth the things that are done of thee, or the works that shall begin." And before the Captivity, between the time the Law was first lost (which Scripture does not mention, but which was probably the time of Rehoboam, when Shishak king of Egypt carried off the treasures of the Temple — 1 Kings 14:26) and the time of Josiah, when it was found again, the Jews had no written word of God at all. They governed themselves by their own discretion, or by the direction of whoever each of them took for a prophet.

When the Old Testament Became Canon

From all this we may infer that the Scriptures of the Old Testament as we have them today were not canonical — were not a law to the Jews — until the renewal of their covenant with God at their return from the Captivity, and the restoration of their commonwealth under Esdras. From that time forward they were counted the law of the Jews, and as such were translated into Greek by seventy elders of Judaea, placed in the library of Ptolemy at Alexandria, and approved as the word of God. Now since Esdras was the High Priest, and the High Priest was their civil sovereign, it is plain that the Scriptures were never made laws except by the sovereign civil power.

The New Testament Began to Be Canon Under Christian Sovereigns

From the writings of the Fathers who lived before the Christian religion was received and authorized by the Emperor Constantine, we can see that the books we now have of the New Testament were held by the Christians of that age — except a few, whose small numbers led the rest to be called the Catholic Church, and the others heretics — to be the dictates of the Holy Spirit, and consequently the canon, or rule, of faith. Such was the reverence and esteem they had for their teachers — and in general, the reverence disciples bear toward their first masters, in every kind of doctrine they receive from them, is no small thing. So there is no doubt that when St. Paul wrote to the churches he had converted, or any other apostle or disciple of Christ wrote to those who had already embraced Christ, the recipients received those writings as the true Christian doctrine. But in that age it was not the power and authority of the teacher that made men receive the writings — it was the faith of the hearer. It was not the apostles who made their own writings canonical; every convert made them canonical to himself.

But the question here is not what any Christian made a law or canon to himself — which he might reject again by the same right he received it. The question is what was made a canon to them in such a way that they could not, without injustice, act contrary to it. That the New Testament should be canonical in this sense — a law — in any place where the law of the commonwealth had not made it so, is contrary to the very nature of a law. For a law, as has already been shown, is the command of that man or assembly to whom we have given sovereign authority to make such rules for the direction of our actions as he thinks fit, and to punish us when we do anything contrary to them. When any other man offers us rules the sovereign ruler has not prescribed, those rules are nothing but counsel and advice — which the person counseled may refuse to follow without injustice, whether the advice is good or bad; and which, when it is contrary to the laws already established, he cannot follow without injustice, no matter how good he thinks it is.

I say he cannot in that case follow it in his actions, nor in his conversation with other men — though he may, without blame, believe his private teachers, wish he had the liberty to practice their advice, and wish it were publicly received as law. For internal faith is by its own nature invisible, and consequently exempt from all human jurisdiction; but the words and actions that proceed from it, when they are breaches of our civil obedience, are injustice before both God and man.

Since, then, our Savior has denied that his kingdom is in this world; since he has said he came not to judge but to save the world — he has not subjected us to any laws other than the laws of the commonwealth. That is: the Jews to the law of Moses (which he says he came not to destroy but to fulfill — Matthew 5), other nations to the laws of their various sovereigns, and all men to the laws of nature. The keeping of these laws both he himself and his apostles recommended to us in their teaching as a necessary condition of being admitted by him, at the last day, into his eternal kingdom, where there will be protection and life everlasting. Since, then, our Savior and his apostles left us no new laws to bind us in this world, but new doctrine to prepare us for the next, the books of the New Testament — which contain that doctrine — were not, until obedience to them was commanded by those God has given power on earth to be legislators, obligatory canons, that is, laws. They were only good and safe advice for the direction of sinners on the road to salvation, which every man might take or refuse at his own peril, without injustice.

Consider, too, the commission our Savior Christ gave to his apostles and disciples: to proclaim his kingdom — not present, but to come; to teach all nations; to baptize those who believed; to enter the houses of those who received them; and where they were not received, to shake the dust off their feet against that house — but not to call down fire from heaven to destroy them, and not to compel them to obedience by the sword. In all of this there is nothing of power; only persuasion. He sent them out as sheep among wolves, not as kings to their subjects. They had no commission to make laws, but to obey, and to teach obedience to, laws already made. Consequently they could not make their writings obligatory canons without the help of the sovereign civil power. And therefore the Scripture of the New Testament is law only where the lawful civil power has made it law. And there, the king or sovereign makes it a law to himself as well — by which he subjects himself not to the teacher or apostle who converted him, but to God himself and his Son Jesus Christ, as immediately as the apostles did.

Could Church Councils Make Scripture Law?

What might seem to give the New Testament the force of law, for those who had embraced Christian doctrine in the times and places of persecution, is the decrees the Christians made among themselves in their synods. We read the formula of the council of the apostles, the elders, and the whole church (Acts 15:28), in this style: "It seemed good to the Holy Ghost, and to us, to lay upon you no greater burden than these necessary things," and so on — a style that seems to signify a power to lay a burden on those who had received their doctrine. And "to lay a burden on someone" sounds like the same thing as "to oblige" — so the acts of that council, one might think, were laws to the Christians of that time.

Nevertheless, they were no more laws than these other precepts are: "Repent"; "Be baptized"; "Keep the commandments"; "Believe the gospel"; "Come unto me"; "Sell all that thou hast, give it to the poor"; "Follow me." These are not commands but invitations — callings of men to Christianity — like that of Isaiah 55:1: "Ho, every man that thirsteth, come ye to the waters; come, and buy wine and milk without money."

For first, the apostles' power was no different from our Savior's own: to invite men to embrace the kingdom of God — a kingdom which they themselves acknowledged to be not present, but to come. And those who have no kingdom can make no laws. And second, if the acts of their council were laws, then they could not be disobeyed without sin. But we read nowhere that those who rejected the doctrine of Christ sinned in doing so — only that they died in their sins; that is, that their sins against the laws they already owed obedience to were not pardoned. And those laws were the laws of nature and the civil laws of the state, to which every Christian man had submitted himself by pact. The "burden" the apostles could lay on those they had converted is therefore not to be understood as laws, but as conditions — conditions proposed to those who sought salvation, which they might accept or refuse at their own peril, without committing any new sin, though not without the hazard of being condemned and shut out of the kingdom of God for their sins past. That is why St. John does not say of unbelievers that the wrath of God shall come upon them, but that "the wrath of God remaineth upon them"; not that they shall be condemned, but that "they are condemned already" (John 3:36, 3:18). And it is impossible to conceive that the benefit of faith is the remission of sins unless we also conceive that the damage of unbelief is the retention of those same sins.

But then, someone may ask, what was the point of the apostles, and the church's pastors after them, meeting together to agree on what doctrine should be taught, both for faith and for conduct, if no one was obliged to observe their decrees? The answer: the apostles and elders of that council were obliged — by their very entrance into it — to teach the doctrine concluded and decreed there, so far as no prior law they owed obedience to stood against it. But all other Christians were not obliged to observe what they taught. For though the members of the council could deliberate about what each of them should teach, they could not deliberate about what others should do — unless their assembly had legislative power, which none can have but civil sovereigns. For though God is the sovereign of all the world, we are not bound to take for his law whatever any man propounds in his name, nor anything contrary to the civil law, which God has expressly commanded us to obey.

Since, then, the acts of the council of the apostles were not laws at the time, but counsels, much less are the acts of any other doctors or councils since then laws — if those councils assembled without the authority of the civil sovereign. Consequently, the books of the New Testament, though the most perfect rules of Christian doctrine, could not be made laws by any authority other than that of kings or sovereign assemblies.

The first council that made the Scriptures we now have into the canon is not extant. The collection attributed to the first Bishop of Rome after St. Peter is open to question: though the canonical books are listed there, the words "Sint vobis omnibus Clericis et Laicis Libri venerandi" — "Let these books be venerated by all of you, clergy and laity" — contain a distinction between clergy and laity that was not in use so near St. Peter's time. The first surviving council that settled the canonical Scripture is that of Laodicea (canon 59), which forbids the reading of any other books in the churches. But that is a mandate addressed not to every Christian, but only to those who had the authority to read anything publicly in church — that is, to churchmen only.

Who Appointed Church Officers in the Time of the Apostles

Among church officers in the time of the apostles, some were magisterial, some ministerial. Magisterial were the offices of preaching the gospel of the kingdom of God to unbelievers; of administering the sacraments and divine service; and of teaching the rules of faith and conduct to the converted. Ministerial was the office of deacons — those appointed to manage the worldly necessities of the church, in the days when Christians lived on a common fund of money raised from the voluntary contributions of the faithful.

Among the magisterial officers, the first and principal were the apostles. At first there were only twelve, chosen and appointed by our Savior himself; and their office was not only to preach, teach, and baptize, but also to be martyrs — witnesses of our Savior's resurrection. This testimony was the specific, essential mark distinguishing the apostleship from every other church office. To be an apostle, a man had either to have seen our Savior after his resurrection, or to have kept company with him before it and seen his works and the other evidence of his divinity, so that he could count as a sufficient witness. That is why, at the election of a new apostle to replace Judas Iscariot, St. Peter says: "Of these men that have companied with us, all the time that the Lord Jesus went in and out among us, beginning from the baptism of John unto that same day that he was taken up from us, must one be ordained to be a witness with us of his resurrection" (Acts 1:21-22). That word "must" implies a necessary property of an apostle: to have kept company with the first and original apostles during the time our Savior showed himself in the flesh.

Matthias: Made an Apostle by the Congregation

The first apostle not appointed by Christ during his time on earth was Matthias, and he was chosen in this manner. About a hundred and twenty Christians were assembled in Jerusalem (Acts 1:15). They nominated two men, Joseph the Just and Matthias (verse 23), and had lots drawn; "and the lot fell upon Matthias, and he was numbered with the apostles" (verse 26). So here we see that the ordination of this apostle was the act of the congregation — not of St. Peter, nor of the eleven, except as members of the assembly.

Paul and Barnabas: Made Apostles by the Church of Antioch

After Matthias, no other apostles were ever ordained except Paul and Barnabas, and that was done (as we read in Acts 13:1-3) in this manner: "There were in the church that was at Antioch certain prophets and teachers; as Barnabas, and Simeon that was called Niger, and Lucius of Cyrene, and Manaen, which had been brought up with Herod the Tetrarch, and Saul. As they ministered unto the Lord, and fasted, the Holy Ghost said, 'Separate me Barnabas and Saul for the work whereunto I have called them.' And when they had fasted and prayed, and laid their hands on them, they sent them away."

From this it is clear that though they were called by the Holy Spirit, their calling was declared to them, and their mission authorized, by the particular church of Antioch. That this calling was to the apostleship is shown by the fact that both of them are called apostles (Acts 14:14). And that it was by virtue of this act of the church of Antioch that they were apostles, St. Paul declares plainly (Romans 1:1), for he uses the very word the Holy Spirit used at his calling: he styles himself "an apostle separated unto the gospel of God," alluding to the words of the Holy Spirit, "Separate me Barnabas and Saul."

But since the work of an apostle was to be a witness of the resurrection of Christ, someone may ask how St. Paul — who never kept company with our Savior before his passion — could know that he had risen. The answer is easy: our Savior himself appeared to him on the road to Damascus, from heaven, after his ascension, "and chose him for a vessel to bear his name before the Gentiles, and kings, and the children of Israel." Having seen the Lord after his passion, Paul was a competent witness of his resurrection. As for Barnabas, he was a disciple before the passion. It is therefore evident that Paul and Barnabas were apostles — and yet they were chosen and authorized not by the first apostles alone, but by the church of Antioch, just as Matthias was chosen and authorized by the church of Jerusalem.

Which Offices in the Church Are Magisterial

Bishop is a word formed in our language out of the Greek episcopus, which means an overseer or superintendent of any business — and in particular a pastor, or shepherd. From there it was taken up by metaphor, not only among the Jews (who were originally shepherds), but among the pagans too, to mean the office of a king or any other ruler or guide of a people, whether he ruled by laws or by doctrine. In this sense the apostles were the first Christian bishops, instituted by Christ himself — which is why the apostleship of Judas is called his bishopric (Acts 1:20). Later, when elders were appointed in the Christian churches with the charge of guiding Christ's flock by their doctrine and advice, these elders were also called bishops. Timothy was an elder (and in the New Testament, "elder" is the name of an office, not just of an age), yet he was also a bishop. And bishops in those days were content with the title of elder. Even St. John himself, the apostle our Lord loved, begins his second epistle with the words "The Elder to the Elect Lady."

From all this it is plain that bishop, pastor, elder, and doctor — that is, teacher — were just so many different names for the same office in the time of the apostles. For there was then no government by coercion, but only by doctrine and persuasion. The kingdom of God was still to come, in a new world; so there could be no authority to compel in any church until the commonwealth had embraced the Christian faith. And that means there could be no diversity of authority, though there was diversity of employments.

Besides these magisterial employments in the church — namely apostles, bishops, elders, pastors, and doctors, whose calling was to proclaim Christ to the Jews and the unbelievers, and to direct and teach those who believed — we read of no others in the New Testament. The names evangelist and prophet signify no office at all, but particular gifts by which particular men were useful to the church.
