Evangelists were useful by writing the life and acts of our Savior — such as the apostles St. Matthew and St. John, and the disciples St. Mark and St. Luke, and whoever else wrote on that subject (as St. Thomas and St. Barnabas are said to have done, though the Church has not received the books that have gone under their names). Prophets were useful by the gift of interpreting the Old Testament, and sometimes by declaring their special revelations to the church. But neither these gifts, nor the gift of languages, nor the gift of casting out devils or curing diseases, nor anything else made a man an officer in the church. Only one thing did that: the due calling and election to the charge of teaching.

The Ordination of Teachers

The apostles Matthias, Paul, and Barnabas were not made apostles by our Savior himself. They were elected by the Church — that is, by the assembly of Christians: Matthias by the church of Jerusalem, and Paul and Barnabas by the church of Antioch. In the same way, the presbyters and pastors in other cities were elected by the churches of those cities.

For proof, consider first how St. Paul proceeded in the ordination of presbyters in the cities where he had converted men to the Christian faith, immediately after he and Barnabas had received their apostleship. We read (Acts 14:23) that "they ordained elders in every church" — which at first glance might be taken as an argument that the apostles themselves chose the elders and gave them their authority. But if we look at the original text, it becomes clear that the elders were authorized and chosen by the assembly of the Christians of each city. The Greek words there are cheirotonesantes autois presbuterous kat ekklesian — that is, "when they had ordained them elders by the holding up of hands in every congregation." Now it is well known that in all those cities, the way of choosing magistrates and officers was by majority vote; and because the ordinary way of telling the yes votes from the no votes was by the holding up of hands, to "ordain" an officer in any of those cities was nothing more than to bring the people together and elect him by majority — whether by a majority of raised hands, or of voices, or of balls, or beans, or small stones, each man casting one into a vessel marked for yes or for no. Different cities had different customs on that point.

So it was the assembly that elected its own elders. The apostles were only presidents of the assembly: they called it together for the election, pronounced the winners elected, and gave them the blessing that is now called consecration. That is why those who presided over the assemblies — as the elders did in the absence of the apostles — were called proestotes, and in Latin antistites: words that mean the principal person of the assembly, whose office was to count the votes, declare who was chosen, and, when the votes were tied, decide the question by adding his own. That is simply the office of a president in a council.

And because all the churches had their presbyters ordained in this same way, wherever the word is "constitute" — as in Titus 1:5, ina katasteses kata polin presbuterous, "For this cause I left you in Crete, that you should constitute elders in every city" — we are to understand the same thing: that Titus should call the faithful together and ordain them presbyters by majority vote. It would have been a strange thing if, in a town where men had perhaps never seen a magistrate chosen any other way than by an assembly, the townspeople, on becoming Christians, had so much as thought of any other way of electing their teachers and guides — that is, their presbyters, otherwise called bishops — than this election by majority vote, which is exactly what St. Paul's word cheirotonesantes implies (Acts 14:23). Nor was there ever any choosing of bishops, before the emperors found it necessary to regulate them in order to keep the peace among them, except by the assemblies of the Christians in every separate town.

The same point is confirmed by the continual practice, even to this day, in the election of the bishops of Rome. For if the bishop of any place had the right to choose another man to succeed to the pastoral office in any city when he left it to plant the faith somewhere else, then surely he would have had all the more right to appoint his successor in the place where he last lived and died. And yet we never find that any bishop of Rome appointed his own successor. For a long time they were chosen by the people — as we can see from the riot raised over the election between Damasus and Ursinus, which Ammianus Marcellinus says was so violent that Juventius the prefect, unable to keep the peace between the factions, was forced to leave the city, and more than a hundred men were found dead on that occasion in the church itself. And though afterward the popes were chosen first by the whole clergy of Rome, and later by the cardinals, no pope was ever appointed to the succession by his predecessor. If, then, the popes never claimed the right to appoint their own successors, I think I may reasonably conclude they had no right to appoint themselves any new power — a power that no one could take from the Church and bestow on them except someone with lawful authority not only to teach the Church but to command it. And no one could do that but the civil sovereign.

What the Ministers of the Church Are

The word minister, in the original diakonos, means someone who voluntarily does another man's business. A minister differs from a servant only in this: servants are obliged by their condition to do whatever they are commanded, while ministers are obliged only by what they have undertaken — and so are bound to no more than they have undertaken. By this measure, both those who teach the Word of God and those who manage the secular affairs of the Church are ministers; but they are ministers of different persons. The pastors of the Church, called "the ministers of the Word" (Acts 6:4), are ministers of Christ, whose Word it is. But the ministry of a deacon — called "serving of tables" in verse 2 of the same chapter — is a service done to the church, that is, to the congregation. So neither any one man nor the whole church could ever say of their pastor that he was their minister. But of a deacon — whether his charge was to serve tables, or to distribute support to the Christians when they lived in each city on a common stock or on collections (as they did in the earliest times), or to take care of the house of prayer, or of the revenue, or of any other worldly business of the church — the whole congregation could properly call him their minister.

Their employment as deacons was to serve the congregation; though when the occasion arose they did not fail to preach the Gospel and uphold the doctrine of Christ, each according to his gifts — as St. Stephen did — and even to preach and baptize, as Philip did. For the Philip who preached the Gospel at Samaria (Acts 8:5) and baptized the eunuch (verse 38) was Philip the deacon, not Philip the apostle. This is clear from verse 1: when Philip preached in Samaria, the apostles were at Jerusalem; and (verse 14) "when they heard that Samaria had received the Word of God, they sent Peter and John to them." It was by the laying on of Peter and John's hands that those who had been baptized received the Holy Spirit (verse 15) — which by Philip's baptism alone they had not received. For the conferring of the Holy Spirit, their baptism had to be administered or confirmed by a minister of the Word, not by a minister of the church. And so, to confirm the baptism of those Philip the deacon had baptized, the apostles sent two from their own number, Peter and John, from Jerusalem to Samaria; and they conferred on those who until then were merely baptized the graces that were the signs of the Holy Spirit, which at that time accompanied all true believers.

What those signs were, we can learn from what St. Mark says (Mark 16:17): "These signs shall follow them that believe in my name: they shall cast out devils; they shall speak with new tongues; they shall take up serpents; and if they drink any deadly thing, it shall not hurt them; they shall lay hands on the sick, and they shall recover." This was what Philip could not give but the apostles could — and, as this passage shows, actually did give to every man who truly believed and was baptized by a minister of Christ himself. From which it follows that either Christ's ministers in our own age cannot confer this power, or else there are very few true believers — or Christ has very few ministers.

And How They Were Chosen

That the first deacons were chosen not by the apostles but by a congregation of the disciples — that is, by Christian men of every sort — is clear from Acts 6. There we read that the Twelve, after the number of disciples had multiplied, called them together; and having told them it was not fitting that the apostles should leave the Word of God to serve tables, said to them (verse 3), "Brethren, look out among you seven men of honest report, full of the Holy Spirit and of wisdom, whom we may appoint over this business." It is plain here that though the apostles declared them elected, the congregation chose them — which verse 5 says even more expressly: "the saying pleased the multitude, and they chose seven," and so on.

Church Revenue Under the Law of Moses

Under the Old Testament, only the tribe of Levi was eligible for the priesthood and the other lesser offices of the Church. The land was divided among the other tribes (Levi excepted), which — by the subdivision of the tribe of Joseph into Ephraim and Manasseh — still numbered twelve. The tribe of Levi was assigned certain cities to live in, with the surrounding land for their cattle; but for their portion, they were to have a tenth of the fruits of the land of their brethren. The priests, in turn, had for their maintenance a tenth of that tenth, together with part of the offerings and sacrifices. For God had said to Aaron (Numbers 18:20), "You shall have no inheritance in their land, neither shall you have any part among them: I am your part and your inheritance among the children of Israel."

God was then king; and having appointed the tribe of Levi to be his public ministers, he allowed them for their maintenance the public revenue — that is, the part God had reserved to himself, which was the tithes and offerings. That is what is meant where God says, "I am your inheritance." And for this reason the Levites might fittingly be given the name of clergy, from kleros, which means "lot" or "inheritance" — not because they were heirs of the kingdom of God any more than other men, but because God's inheritance was their maintenance. Now, seeing that in this period God himself was their king, and Moses, Aaron, and the succeeding high priests were his lieutenants, it is plain that the right of tithes and offerings was established by the civil power.

After the Jews rejected God by demanding a king, the Levites still enjoyed the same revenue; but their right to it now rested on the fact that the kings never took it from them. For the public revenue was at the disposal of whoever was the public person — and until the Captivity, that was the king. And again, after the return from the Captivity, the people paid their tithes to the priest as before. Up to this point, then, church livings were determined by the civil sovereign.

In Our Savior's Time, and After

Of the maintenance of our Savior and his apostles, we read only that they had a purse (carried by Judas Iscariot); that those apostles who were fishermen sometimes worked at their trade; and that when our Savior sent the twelve apostles out to preach, he forbade them "to carry gold, and silver, and brass in their purses, for the workman is worthy of his hire" (Matthew 10:9-10). From this it is likely that their ordinary maintenance was not out of keeping with their employment — for their employment was "freely to give, because they had freely received" (verse 8), and their maintenance was the free gift of those who believed the good news they carried about the coming of the Messiah, their Savior. To this we may add what was contributed out of gratitude by those our Savior had healed of diseases — among whom are mentioned (Luke 8:2-3) "certain women which had been healed of evil spirits and infirmities: Mary Magdalene, out of whom went seven devils; and Joanna the wife of Chuza, Herod's steward; and Susanna, and many others, which ministered unto him of their substance."

After our Savior's ascension, the Christians of every city lived in common (Acts 4:34), on the money raised from the sale of their lands and possessions and laid down at the apostles' feet — out of good will, not duty. For as St. Peter said to Ananias (Acts 5:4), "While the land remained, was it not yours? And after it was sold, was it not in your power?" — which shows that Ananias had no need to save his land or his money by lying, since he was not bound to contribute anything at all unless he pleased. And as in the time of the apostles, so all the way down until after Constantine the Great, we find that the maintenance of the bishops and pastors of the Christian Church was nothing but the voluntary contribution of those who had embraced their doctrine. There was still no mention of tithes. But by the time of Constantine and his sons, such was the affection of Christians toward their pastors that — as Ammianus Marcellinus says, describing the riot between Damasus and Ursinus over the bishopric — the office was worth fighting over: the bishops of those times, thanks to the generosity of their flocks, and especially of wealthy matrons, lived splendidly, were carried about in coaches, and were lavish in their food and dress.

The Ministers of the Gospel Lived on the Generosity of Their Flocks

Here someone may ask whether the pastor in those days was bound to live on voluntary contribution, as on alms. "For who," says St. Paul (1 Corinthians 9:7), "goes to war at his own expense? Or who feeds a flock and does not eat of the milk of the flock?" And again (1 Corinthians 9:13), "Do you not know that they which minister about holy things live of the things of the Temple, and they which wait at the altar partake with the altar?" — that is to say, have part of what is offered at the altar for their maintenance. And then he concludes, "Even so has the Lord appointed, that they which preach the Gospel should live of the Gospel."

From this passage we may indeed infer that the pastors of the Church ought to be maintained by their flocks — but not that the pastors were to determine the amount or the kind of their own allowance, and carve out their own portions, so to speak. Their allowance, then, had to be determined either by the gratitude and generosity of each individual member of the flock, or by the whole congregation. It could not be by the whole congregation, because in those days the congregation's acts were not laws. Therefore the maintenance of pastors, before emperors and civil sovereigns had made laws to settle it, was nothing but benevolence. Those who served at the altar lived on what was offered. In what court could they have sued for more, when they had no tribunals? Or if they had arbitrators among themselves, who would have executed their judgments, when they had no power to arm their officers? It remains, therefore, that no fixed maintenance could be assigned to any pastors of the Church except by the whole congregation — and then only when its decrees came to have the force not merely of canons but of laws; and such laws could be made by no one but emperors, kings, or other civil sovereigns. Nor could the right of tithes in Moses' law be applied to the ministers of the Gospel of that age — because Moses and the high priests were the civil sovereigns of the people under God, whose kingdom among the Jews was then present, whereas the kingdom of God by Christ is yet to come.

So far, then, we have shown what the pastors of the Church are; what the points of their commission are (to preach, to teach, to baptize, to preside in their several congregations); what ecclesiastical censure — excommunication — is (namely: where Christianity was forbidden by the civil laws, the Christians removing themselves from the company of the excommunicated person, and where Christianity was commanded by the civil law, the excommunicated person being put out of the congregations of Christians); who elected the pastors and ministers of the Church (the congregation); who consecrated and blessed them (the pastor); and what their due revenue was (none but their own possessions, their own labor, and the voluntary contributions of devout and grateful Christians). We are now to consider what office belongs to those persons who, being civil sovereigns, have also embraced the Christian faith.

A Christian Civil Sovereign Has the Right to Appoint Pastors

First, we must remember that the right of judging which doctrines are fit for peace and fit to be taught to the subjects is, in all commonwealths, inseparably attached to the sovereign civil power, whether that power lies in one man or in one assembly of men — as has already been proved in chapter 18. For it is obvious to the meanest understanding that men's actions flow from the opinions they hold about the good or evil those actions will bring upon themselves. Consequently, once men are possessed of the opinion that obeying the sovereign power will hurt them more than disobeying it, they will disobey the laws — and thereby overthrow the commonwealth and bring in confusion and civil war, the very things all civil government was ordained to avoid. That is why, in all the commonwealths of the pagans, the sovereigns bore the name of pastors of the people: because no subject could lawfully teach the people except by their permission and authority.

This right of the pagan kings cannot be thought to have been taken from them by their conversion to the faith of Christ — who never ordained that kings, for believing in him, should be deposed (that is, subjected to anyone but himself), or, which comes to the same thing, be stripped of the power necessary for preserving peace among their subjects and defending them against foreign enemies. Therefore Christian kings are still the supreme pastors of their people, and have the power to ordain whatever pastors they please to teach the Church — that is, to teach the people committed to their charge.

Again, suppose the right of choosing pastors remains (as it was before the conversion of kings) in the Church — for so it was in the time of the apostles themselves, as has already been shown in this chapter. Even so, the right will still belong to the Christian civil sovereign. For insofar as he is a Christian, he allows the teaching; and insofar as he is the sovereign — which is as much as to say, the Church by representation — the teachers he elects are elected by the Church. And when an assembly of Christians chooses its pastor in a Christian commonwealth, it is the sovereign who elects him, because it is done by his authority — just as when a town chooses its mayor, it is the act of the one who holds the sovereign power. For every act done is the act of him without whose consent it would be invalid. And therefore, whatever examples may be drawn out of history of pastors being elected by the people or by the clergy, they are no argument against the right of any civil sovereign, because those who elected them did it by his authority.

Since, then, in every Christian commonwealth the civil sovereign is the supreme pastor, to whose charge the whole flock of his subjects is committed, and since it is consequently by his authority that all other pastors are made and have power to teach and to perform all other pastoral offices, it follows that it is from the civil sovereign that all other pastors derive their right of teaching, preaching, and every other function belonging to that office — and that they are nothing but his ministers. They are his ministers in the same way that town magistrates, judges in courts of justice, and commanders of armies are all ministers of him who is the magistrate of the whole commonwealth, the judge of all causes, and the commander of the whole militia — which is always the civil sovereign. And the reason for this is not that the teachers are his subjects, but that the learners are.

For suppose a Christian king were to hand over the authority of ordaining pastors in his dominions to another king — as various Christian kings allow that power to the Pope. He does not thereby set up a pastor over himself, nor a sovereign pastor over his people. To do that would be to strip himself of the civil power itself — which, since it depends on the opinion men have of their duty to him and the fear they have of punishment in another world, would then depend also on the skill and loyalty of teachers of religion, who are no less subject to ambition, and to ignorance, than any other sort of men. So wherever a foreigner has authority to appoint teachers, that authority is given to him by the sovereign in whose dominions he teaches. Christian doctors are our schoolmasters in Christianity; but kings are the fathers of families, and they may receive schoolmasters for their subjects on the recommendation of a foreigner, but not on his command — especially when teaching the subjects badly would work to the great and obvious profit of the one recommending them. Nor can kings be obliged to keep such teachers any longer than it serves the public good — the care of which remains in their charge for as long as they retain any other essential right of the sovereignty.

Only the Sovereign's Pastoral Authority Is Jure Divino; All Other Pastors Hold Theirs Jure Civili

If a man should ask a pastor, in the execution of his office, the question the chief priests and elders of the people asked our Savior (Matthew 21:23) — "By what authority do you do these things, and who gave you this authority?" — he can give no other honest answer than that he does it by the authority of the commonwealth, given him by the king or the assembly that represents it. All pastors, except the supreme one, execute their charges in the right — that is, by the authority — of the civil sovereign: that is, jure civili, by civil right. But the king, and every other sovereign, executes his office of supreme pastor by immediate authority from God — that is to say, in God's right, or jure divino, by divine right. And that is why none but kings may put into their titles the mark of their submission to God alone: Dei Gratia Rex — "King by the grace of God," and so forth. Bishops ought to say at the beginning of their official documents, "By the favor of the King's Majesty, Bishop of such-and-such a diocese," or, as civil ministers, "In his Majesty's name." For when they write Divina Providentia — "by Divine Providence," which is just Dei Gratia in disguise — they deny that they received their authority from the civil state, and slyly slip the collar of their civil subjection off their necks, contrary to the unity and defense of the commonwealth.

Christian Kings Have the Power to Perform Every Pastoral Function

But if every Christian sovereign is the supreme pastor of his own subjects, it would seem he also has the authority not only to preach (which perhaps no one will deny), but also to baptize, to administer the sacrament of the Lord's Supper, and to consecrate both temples and pastors to God's service. Most men deny this — partly because sovereigns do not customarily do these things, and partly because the administration of sacraments and the consecration of persons and places to holy uses is thought to require the laying on of hands by men who, through a chain of such impositions reaching back to the apostles, have themselves been ordained to that ministry. So to prove that Christian kings have the power to baptize and to consecrate, I must explain two things: why they do not customarily do it, and how, without the ordinary ceremony of laying on of hands, they are nevertheless qualified to do it whenever they please.

There is no doubt that any king, if he happened to be skilled in the sciences, could by the very right of his office deliver lectures in them himself — the same right by which he authorizes others to deliver them in the universities. Nevertheless, because the care of the whole business of the commonwealth takes up his entire time, it would not be practical for him to apply himself in person to that particular task. A king may also, if he pleases, sit in judgment and hear and decide all manner of cases, just as well as give others the authority to do it in his name; but the burden of command and government that lies on him forces him to stay continually at the helm and commit the ministerial offices to others under him. In just the same way our Savior — who surely had the power to baptize — baptized no one himself, but sent his apostles and disciples to baptize (John 4:2). So also St. Paul, pressed by the necessity of preaching in many far-distant places, baptized few: among all the Corinthians he baptized only Crispus, Gaius, and Stephanas (1 Corinthians 1:14, 16), and the reason was that his principal charge was to preach (1 Corinthians 1:17). From this it is clear that the greater charge — such as the government of the Church — is a dispensation from the lesser. The reason Christian kings do not customarily baptize is therefore obvious, and it is the same reason why, to this day, few people are baptized by bishops — and by the Pope, fewer still.

As for the laying on of hands — whether it is needed to authorize a king to baptize and consecrate — we may consider the matter as follows.

The laying on of hands was a very ancient public ceremony among the Jews, by which the person or thing intended in a man's prayer, blessing, sacrifice, consecration, condemnation, or other speech was designated and made certain. So Jacob, in blessing the children of Joseph (Genesis 48:14), "laid his right hand on Ephraim the younger, and his left hand on Manasseh the firstborn" — and he did this deliberately (though the boys were presented to him by Joseph in the opposite order, so that he had to stretch his arms crosswise to do it) in order to mark which one he intended the greater blessing for. So also in the sacrificing of the burnt offering, Aaron is commanded (Exodus 29:10) "to lay his hands on the head of the bullock," and (verse 15) "to lay his hand on the head of the ram." The same is said again in Leviticus 1:4 and 8:14. Likewise, when Moses ordained Joshua to be captain of the Israelites — that is, consecrated him to God's service — he "laid his hands upon him and gave him his charge" (Numbers 27:23), designating and making certain who it was they were to obey in war. And in the consecration of the Levites (Numbers 8:10), God commanded that "the children of Israel should put their hands upon the Levites." And in the condemnation of the man who had blasphemed the Lord (Leviticus 24:14), God commanded that "all that heard him should lay their hands on his head, and that all the congregation should stone him." Why should only those who heard him lay their hands on him, rather than a priest, a Levite, or some other minister of justice? Because no one else was able to designate and demonstrate to the eyes of the congregation who it was that had blasphemed and deserved to die. To point a man out — or anything else — by the hand to the eye is less subject to mistake than to do it by a name to the ear.

So carefully was this ceremony observed that even in blessing the whole congregation at once — which cannot be done by laying on of hands — "Aaron lifted up his hand toward the people when he blessed them" (Leviticus 9:22). And we read of a similar ceremony in the consecration of temples among the pagans: the priest laid his hands on some post of the temple the whole time he was speaking the words of consecration. So natural is it, in matters of God's public service, to designate any individual thing by the hand, to assure the eyes, rather than by words, to inform the ear.

This ceremony, then, was nothing new in our Savior's time. Jairus, whose daughter was sick (Mark 5:23), begged our Savior — not simply to heal her, but — "to lay his hands upon her, that she might be healed." And (Matthew 19:13) "they brought unto him little children, that he should put his hands on them and pray."

Following this ancient rite, the apostles, the presbyters, and the presbytery itself laid hands on those they ordained as pastors, praying at the same time that they might receive the Holy Spirit — and not only once, but sometimes more than once, whenever a new occasion arose. But the purpose was always the same: a precise and religious designation of the person, ordained either to the pastoral charge in general or to a particular mission. So "the apostles prayed and laid their hands" on the seven deacons (Acts 6:6) — which was done not to give them the Holy Spirit (for they were full of the Holy Spirit before they were chosen, as appears just before, in verse 3), but to designate them to that office. And after Philip the deacon had converted certain persons in Samaria, Peter and John went down "and laid their hands on them, and they received the Holy Spirit" (Acts 8:17). Nor was this power confined to apostles: a presbyter had it too. For St. Paul advises Timothy (1 Timothy 5:22), "Lay hands suddenly on no man" — that is, designate no man rashly to the office of a pastor. The whole presbytery laid their hands on Timothy, as we read in 1 Timothy 4:14; but this is to be understood as meaning that some did it by the appointment of the presbytery — most likely their proestos, or presiding officer, who may well have been St. Paul himself. For in his second epistle to Timothy (verse 6) he says to him, "Stir up the gift of God which is in thee, by the laying on of my hands." Note in passing that by "the Holy Spirit" here is meant not the third Person of the Trinity, but the gifts necessary for the pastoral office. We read also that St. Paul himself received the laying on of hands twice: once from Ananias at Damascus (Acts 9:17-18), at the time of his baptism, and again at Antioch (Acts 13:3), when he was first sent out to preach.

The use of this ceremony in the ordination of pastors, then, was to designate the person to whom the power was being given. But suppose there had been, in those days, a Christian who already possessed the power of teaching before his baptism. Baptizing him — that is, making him a Christian — would have given him no new power; it would only have directed him to preach true doctrine, that is, to use the power he already had rightly. So the laying on of hands would have been unnecessary; baptism itself would have been sufficient. Now, every sovereign, before Christianity existed, had the power of teaching and of ordaining teachers. Christianity therefore gave sovereigns no new right; it only directed them in the way of teaching truth. And consequently they needed no laying on of hands — beyond the one performed in baptism — to authorize them to exercise any part of the pastoral function, such as baptizing and consecrating. And in the Old Testament, though only the priest had the right to consecrate during the time when the sovereignty was in the high priest, it was not so when the sovereignty was in the king. For we read (1 Kings 8) that Solomon blessed the people, consecrated the Temple, and pronounced the public prayer that is the pattern to this day for the consecration of all Christian churches and chapels. From this it appears that he possessed not only the right of ecclesiastical government, but also the right of exercising ecclesiastical functions.

The Civil Sovereign, If a Christian, Is Head of the Church in His Own Dominions

From this consolidation of political right and ecclesiastical right in Christian sovereigns, one thing is evident: they have every kind of power over their subjects that can possibly be given to a man for governing people's external actions, in politics and in religion alike. They may make whatever laws they judge best for governing their own subjects — both as those subjects make up the commonwealth and as they make up the church. For state and church are the same men.

So if they please, Christian sovereigns may do what many Christian kings now do: hand the government of their subjects in matters of religion over to the Pope. But in that case the Pope is, on that point, subordinate to them. He exercises that charge inside another's dominion jure civili — by civil right, the right of the civil sovereign — not jure divino — by God's right. And he may therefore be dismissed from that office whenever the sovereign judges it necessary for the good of his subjects.

They may also, if they please, commit the care of religion to one supreme pastor, or to an assembly of pastors, and give them whatever power over the church — or over one another — they think most convenient, and whatever titles of honor they like: bishops, archbishops, priests, or presbyters. They may make whatever laws they please for the clergy's upkeep, whether by tithes or by other means — provided they do it out of a sincere conscience, of which God alone is the judge.

It is the civil sovereign who appoints the judges and interpreters of canonical Scripture, for it is he who makes them laws. It is he, too, who gives force to excommunication — which, without laws and punishments to humble obstinate freethinkers and bring them back into union with the rest of the church, would simply be shrugged off.

In sum: the sovereign holds the supreme power in all causes, ecclesiastical as well as civil, as far as actions and words are concerned. For only actions and words can be known, and only they can be accused. As for what cannot be accused, there is no judge at all but God, who alone knows the heart.

And these rights belong to all sovereigns, whether monarchs or assemblies. For those who represent a Christian people represent the church — because a church and a commonwealth of Christian people are the same thing.

Cardinal Bellarmine's Books De Summo Pontifice Considered

Now, what I have said here and elsewhere in this book seems to me clear enough to establish that supreme ecclesiastical power belongs to Christian sovereigns. But the Pope of Rome's claim to that power over all the world has been defended — chiefly, and I think as strongly as it can be defended — by Cardinal Bellarmine, in his treatise De Summo Pontifice — "On the Supreme Pontiff." So I have thought it necessary to examine, as briefly as I can, the grounds and the strength of his argument.

The First Book

He wrote five books on this subject. The first takes up three questions. First: which is simply the best government — monarchy, aristocracy, or democracy? He concludes: none of them, but rather a government mixed of all three. Second: which of these is the best government for the church? He concludes: the mixed kind again, but with the largest share of monarchy in it. Third: in this mixed monarchy, did St. Peter hold the place of the monarch?

As for his first conclusion, I have already sufficiently proved (chapter 18) that all governments which men are bound to obey are simple and absolute. In a monarchy there is only one man supreme; everyone else who holds any kind of power in the state holds it by his commission, during his pleasure, and exercises it in his name. In an aristocracy or a democracy there is only one supreme assembly, with the very same power that belongs to the monarch in a monarchy. That is not a mixed sovereignty but an absolute one.

And as for which of the three forms is best — that is not a question to be debated anywhere one of them is already established. The present government always ought to be preferred, maintained, and counted the best, because it is against both the law of nature and God's positive law to do anything tending to its overthrow.

Besides, the question of which government is best contributes nothing to the power of any pastor (unless he also holds the civil sovereignty). For a pastor's calling is not to govern men by command, but to teach them and persuade them by arguments, and then leave it to them to consider whether they will embrace or reject the doctrine taught. Monarchy, aristocracy, and democracy mark out for us three sorts of sovereigns — not three sorts of pastors. Or, as we might put it: three sorts of masters of families, not three sorts of schoolmasters for their children.

For the same reason, his second conclusion — about the best form of government for the church — has nothing to do with the question of the Pope's power outside his own dominions. For in every other commonwealth, his power (if he has any at all) is only the power of the schoolmaster, not of the master of the family.

As for the third conclusion — that St. Peter was monarch of the church — his chief argument is the passage in Matthew (Matthew 16:18-19): "Thou art Peter, and upon this rock I will build my Church... And I will give thee the keys of Heaven; whatsoever thou shalt bind on Earth shall be bound in Heaven, and whatsoever thou shalt loose on Earth shall be loosed in Heaven."

Considered carefully, this passage proves no more than this: that the Church of Christ has one single article for its foundation — namely the article which Peter, professing it in the name of all the Apostles, gave our Savior the occasion to speak the words quoted here. To understand this clearly, consider that our Savior — in his own person, through John the Baptist, and through his Apostles — preached nothing but this one article of faith: that Jesus is the Christ. Every other article requires faith only insofar as it rests on that one.

John began it first (Matthew 3:2), preaching only this: "The kingdom of God is at hand." Then our Savior himself preached the same (Matthew 4:17). And when he gave his twelve Apostles their commission (Matthew 10:7), there is no mention of preaching any other article but that. This was the fundamental article — the foundation of the church's faith.

Afterward, when the Apostles had returned to him, he asked them all (Matthew 16:13) — not Peter only — who men said he was. They answered that some said John the Baptist, some Elijah, others Jeremiah or one of the prophets. Then (verse 15) he asked them all again — not Peter only — "Whom say ye that I am?" And Peter answered, for them all: "Thou art Christ, the Son of the living God." That, as I said, is the foundation of the faith of the whole church. And from it our Savior takes the occasion to say, "Upon this stone I will build my Church." Which makes it plain that by the foundation-stone of the church was meant the fundamental article of the church's faith.

But then why, someone will object, does our Savior slip in the words "Thou art Peter"? If the original of this text had been strictly translated, the reason would be obvious. The Apostle Simon was surnamed Stone — that is what the Syriac word Cephas means, and the Greek word Petrus too. So after Peter confessed that fundamental article, our Savior, playing on his name, said — as if it were in English — "Thou art Stone, and upon this stone I will build my Church." Which is as much as to say: this article, that "I am the Christ," is the foundation of all the faith I require in those who are to be members of my church. There is nothing unusual about punning on a name in ordinary speech. But it would have been a strange and obscure way of talking if our Savior, intending to build his church on the person of St. Peter, had said "thou art a stone, and upon this stone I will build my Church" — when he could so easily and unambiguously have said "I will build my Church on thee," and kept the very same play on the name.

As for the words that follow — "I will give thee the keys of Heaven," and so on — this is no more than what our Savior also gave to all the rest of his disciples (Matthew 18:18): "Whatsoever ye shall bind on Earth shall be bound in Heaven, and whatsoever ye shall loose on Earth shall be loosed in Heaven." But however the passage is interpreted, there is no doubt the power granted here belongs to all supreme pastors — and that is what all Christian civil sovereigns are, in their own dominions. So much so that if St. Peter, or our Savior himself, had converted one of those sovereigns to believe in him and acknowledge his kingdom, then — because his kingdom is not of this world — he would have left the supreme care of converting that sovereign's subjects to no one but the sovereign himself. Otherwise he would have had to strip him of the sovereignty, to which the right of teaching is inseparably attached.

And that is enough in refutation of his first book, in which he tries to prove St. Peter was the universal monarch of the church — that is, of all the Christians in the world.

The Second Book

The second book has two conclusions: one, that St. Peter was Bishop of Rome and died there; the other, that the Popes of Rome are his successors. Others have disputed both points. But suppose them both true. Even so, if by "Bishop of Rome" we mean either the monarch of the church or its supreme pastor, then that bishop was not Sylvester but Constantine — the first Christian emperor. And as with Constantine, so with all the other Christian emperors: they were by right the supreme bishops of the Roman Empire. I say of the Roman Empire — not of all Christendom. For other Christian sovereigns held the same right in their own territories, as an office essentially attached to their sovereignty. And that will serve as answer to his second book.

The Third Book

In the third book he takes up the question of whether the Pope is Antichrist. For my part, I see no argument that proves he is — not in the sense in which Scripture uses the name. Nor will I draw any argument from the character of Antichrist to contradict the authority the Pope exercises, or has in the past exercised, in the dominions of any other prince or state.

It is evident that the prophets of the Old Testament foretold, and the Jews expected, a Messiah — that is, a Christ — who would re-establish among them the kingdom of God, which they had rejected in the time of Samuel, when they demanded a king after the manner of the other nations. This expectation of theirs left them exposed to the imposture of anyone who had both the ambition to attempt the kingdom and the skill to deceive the people — by counterfeit miracles, by a hypocritical life, or by plausible speeches and doctrine. So our Savior and his Apostles warned men against false prophets and false Christs.

False Christs are those who pretend to be the Christ but are not. They are properly called Antichrists, in the same sense in which, when a schism in the church produces the election of two Popes, each calls the other Antipapa — the false Pope. So Antichrist, in the proper meaning of the word, has two essential marks. One: he denies that Jesus is the Christ. The other: he professes himself to be the Christ. The first mark is set down by St. John (1 John 4:3): "Every spirit that confesseth not that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh is not of God; and this is the spirit of Antichrist." The other mark is expressed in the words of our Savior (Matthew 24:5): "Many shall come in my name, saying, I am Christ" — and again, "If any man shall say unto you, Lo, here is Christ, there is Christ, believe it not." So Antichrist must be a false Christ — one of those who pretend that they themselves are Christ. And from these two marks — denying that Jesus is the Christ, and affirming oneself to be the Christ — it follows that he must also be an adversary of the true Christ, which is another usual meaning of the word Antichrist.

Now, among these many Antichrists there is one special one: O Antichristos — THE Antichrist, definitely, one particular person; not indefinitely, an Antichrist. And seeing that the Pope of Rome neither claims to be the Christ nor denies that Jesus is the Christ, I do not see how he can be called Antichrist. That word does not mean someone who falsely pretends to be Christ's lieutenant or vicar-general — it means someone who pretends to be Christ himself.

There is also a mark of the time of this special Antichrist (Matthew 24:15): when that abominable destroyer spoken of by Daniel (Daniel 9:27) shall stand in the holy place, and there shall be such tribulation as has not been since the beginning of the world, nor ever shall be again — so severe that if it were to last long (verse 22), "no flesh could be saved; but for the elect's sake those days shall be shortened" — made fewer. But that tribulation has not yet come. For it is to be followed immediately (verse 29) by a darkening of the sun and moon, a falling of the stars, a shaking of the heavens, and the glorious coming again of our Savior in the clouds. Therefore The Antichrist has not yet come — whereas many Popes have both come and gone.

It is true that the Pope, in taking upon himself to give laws to all Christian kings and nations, usurps a kingdom in this world which Christ did not take on himself. But he does it not as Christ — he does it as standing in for Christ. And in that there is nothing of the Antichrist.

The Fourth Book

In the fourth book, to prove that the Pope is the supreme judge in all questions of faith and manners — which amounts to being the absolute monarch of all the Christians in the world — he brings three propositions: first, that his judgments are infallible; second, that he can make laws, and punish those who do not observe them; third, that our Savior conferred all ecclesiastical jurisdiction on the Pope of Rome.

Texts for the Infallibility of the Pope's Judgment in Points of Faith

For the infallibility of his judgments, he cites the Scriptures — and first, Luke 22:31: "Simon, Simon, Satan hath desired you, that he may sift you as wheat; but I have prayed for thee, that thy faith fail not; and when thou art converted, strengthen thy brethren." According to Bellarmine's exposition, Christ here gave Simon Peter two privileges: one, that his faith should never fail; the other, that neither he nor any of his successors should ever define any point of faith or manners wrongly, or contrary to a former Pope's definition. Which is a strange — a very strained — interpretation.

But whoever reads that chapter with attention will find there is no passage in the whole of Scripture that makes more against the Pope's authority than this very one. Here is the scene. The priests and scribes were seeking to kill our Savior at the Passover, and Judas was already resolved to betray him. The day for killing the Passover lamb having come, our Savior celebrated it with his Apostles — saying he would do so no more until the kingdom of God was come — and told them, besides, that one of them was to betray him. At that, they fell to questioning which of them it would be. And then — since the next Passover their Master celebrated would be when he was king — they slid into a quarrel over who would then be the greater man.

So our Savior told them: the kings of the nations have dominion over their subjects, and are called by a name that (in Hebrew) means Bountiful; but I cannot be that to you. You must strive to serve one another. I ordain you a kingdom — but it is such a kingdom as my Father has ordained me: a kingdom I am now about to purchase with my blood, and shall not possess until my second coming. Then you shall eat and drink at my table, and sit on thrones, judging the twelve tribes of Israel. And then, turning to St. Peter, he says: Simon, Simon, Satan seeks — by dangling present domination before you — to weaken your faith in the future; but I have prayed for thee, that thy faith shall not fail. Thou therefore (note this), once converted, and understanding my kingdom as a kingdom of another world, confirm that same faith in thy brethren. To which St. Peter answered — as a man who no longer expected any authority in this world — "Lord, I am ready to go with thee, not only to prison, but to death."

From this it is plain that St. Peter was not only given no jurisdiction in this world — he was given a charge to teach all the other Apostles that they should have none either. And as for the infallibility of St. Peter's definitive sentence in matters of faith, no more can be drawn from this text than that Peter would continue in the belief of this one point: that Christ would come again, and possess the kingdom at the day of judgment. The text did not give that to all his successors — for we see they claim the kingdom in the world that now is.

The second passage is Matthew 16: "Thou art Peter, and upon this rock I will build my Church, and the gates of Hell shall not prevail against it." By this — as I have already shown in this chapter — no more is proved than that the gates of hell shall not prevail against the confession of Peter, which gave occasion to that speech: namely, that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God.

The third text is John 21:16-17: "Feed my sheep." This contains no more than a commission of teaching. And if we grant that the rest of the Apostles are included in that word "sheep," then it is the supreme power of teaching — but only for the time when there were no Christian sovereigns already in possession of that supremacy. I have already proved that Christian sovereigns are the supreme pastors in their own dominions, instituted to that office by virtue of their being baptized — and without any other laying-on of hands. For such imposition of hands, being a ceremony for designating the person, is needless when the person is already designated to the power of teaching whatever doctrine he will, by his institution to absolute power over his subjects. As I have proved before, sovereigns are supreme teachers in general by their office, and therefore by their baptism they oblige themselves to teach the doctrine of Christ. And when they let others teach their people, they do it at the peril of their own souls — for it is at the hands of the heads of families that God will demand the account of how his children and servants were instructed. It is of Abraham himself, not of some hireling, that God says (Genesis 18:19), "I know him, that he will command his children and his household after him, that they keep the way of the Lord, and do justice and judgment."

The fourth passage is Exodus 28:30: "Thou shalt put in the breastplate of judgment the Urim and the Thummim" — which, he says, the Septuagint interprets as delosin kai aletheian, that is, "evidence and truth." From this he concludes that God had given evidence and truth — which is nearly infallibility — to the High Priest. Very well: whether what was given was evidence and truth itself, or merely an admonition to the priest to inform himself clearly and judge uprightly — either way, what was given to the High Priest was given to the civil sovereign. For in the commonwealth of Israel, the High Priest stood next under God. So this is actually an argument for evidence and truth — that is, for the ecclesiastical supremacy of civil sovereigns over their own subjects — and against the pretended power of the Pope.

These are all the texts he brings for the infallibility of the Pope's judgment in points of faith.

Texts for the Same in Point of Manners

For the infallibility of his judgment concerning manners, he brings one text: John 16:13, "When the Spirit of truth is come, he will lead you into all truth" — where, says Bellarmine, "all truth" means at least all truth necessary to salvation. But with that softening, he attributes no more infallibility to the Pope than to any man who professes Christianity and is not to be damned. For if any man errs on a point where not erring is necessary to salvation, it is impossible he should be saved — since "necessary to salvation" means precisely that without which being saved is impossible. What those points are, I shall set out from Scripture in the chapter that follows. Here I will say only this: even granting that the Pope could not possibly teach any error at all, that still would not entitle him to any jurisdiction in the dominions of another prince — unless we are also prepared to say that a man is bound in conscience to hire the best workman for every job, even when he has already promised the work to someone else.

Besides the text, he argues from reason, like this: if the Pope could err in things necessary, then Christ has not sufficiently provided for the church's salvation, because he has commanded the church to follow the Pope's directions. But this reasoning is worthless unless he can show when and where Christ commanded any such thing — or took any notice of a Pope at all. No: even granting that whatever was given to St. Peter was given to the Pope, still, since there is in Scripture no command to any man to obey St. Peter, no man can be acting justly who obeys him when his commands run contrary to those of his lawful sovereign.

Lastly, it has never been declared — not by the church, not by the Pope himself — that he is the civil sovereign of all the Christians in the world. Therefore all Christians are not bound to acknowledge his jurisdiction in point of manners. For civil sovereignty and supreme judicature in controversies of manners are the same thing. And the makers of civil laws are not merely the declarers but the makers of the justice and injustice of actions — for there is nothing in men's manners that makes them righteous or unrighteous except their conformity with the law of the sovereign. So when the Pope claims supremacy in controversies of manners, he is teaching men to disobey the civil sovereign — which is an erroneous doctrine, contrary to the many precepts of our Savior and his Apostles delivered to us in Scripture.

To prove the Pope has power to make laws, he cites many passages. First, Deuteronomy 17:12: "The man that will do presumptuously, and will not hearken unto the priest (that standeth to minister there before the Lord thy God, or unto the judge), even that man shall die, and thou shalt put away the evil from Israel." For the answer to this, remember that the High Priest — next and immediately under God — was the civil sovereign, and all judges were to be appointed by him. The words cited therefore amount to this: "The man who presumes to disobey the civil sovereign for the time being, or any of his officers in the execution of their offices, that man shall die," and so on. Which is clearly for civil sovereignty, and against the universal power of the Pope.

Second, he cites Matthew 16, "Whatsoever ye shall bind," and interprets that binding as the kind attributed (Matthew 23:4) to the scribes and Pharisees — "They bind heavy burdens, and grievous to be borne, and lay them on men's shoulders" — by which, he says, is meant the making of laws; and he concludes from this that the Pope can make laws. But this, too, argues only for the legislative power of civil sovereigns. For the scribes and Pharisees sat in Moses' chair — and Moses, next under God, was sovereign of the people of Israel. That is why our Savior commanded the people to do everything the scribes and Pharisees said, but not everything they did: obey their laws, but do not follow their example.

The third passage is John 21:16, "Feed my sheep" — which is not a power to make laws, but a command to teach. Making laws belongs to the lord of the family, who chooses, at his own discretion, his chaplain — and likewise a schoolmaster to teach his children.

The fourth passage, John 20:21, actually works against him. The words are: "As my Father sent me, so send I you." But our Savior was sent to redeem, by his death, such as should believe, and — by his own preaching and his Apostles' — to prepare them for their entrance into his kingdom. That kingdom, he himself says, is not of this world; he taught us to pray for its coming hereafter, though he declined (Acts 1:6-7) to tell his Apostles when it would come; and in that kingdom, when it comes, the twelve Apostles shall sit on twelve thrones — every one, perhaps, as high as St. Peter's — to judge the twelve tribes of Israel. Since, then, God the Father did not send our Savior to make laws in this present world, we may conclude from the text that neither did our Savior send St. Peter to make laws here. He sent him to persuade men to await his second coming with a steadfast faith — and in the meantime, if they were subjects, to obey their princes; and if they were princes, both to believe it themselves and to do their best to make their subjects do the same. Which is the office of a bishop. So this passage argues most strongly for joining the ecclesiastical supremacy to the civil sovereignty — the exact opposite of what Cardinal Bellarmine cites it for.

The fifth passage is Acts 15:28: "It hath seemed good to the Holy Spirit, and to us, to lay upon you no greater burden than these necessary things: that ye abstain from meats offered to idols, and from blood, and from things strangled, and from fornication." Here he seizes on the phrase "laying of burdens" as the legislative power. But who can read this text and say that the Apostles' style here could not just as properly be used in giving counsel as in making laws? The style of a law is "We command." But "We think good" is the ordinary style of those who merely give advice — and advisers do lay a burden of sorts, though a conditional one: binding only if those who receive the advice want to attain their ends. And that is exactly the kind of burden this is — abstaining from things strangled and from blood: not absolute, but binding only in case they do not wish to err. I have shown before (chapter 25) that law is distinguished from counsel in this: the reason of a law is drawn from the design and benefit of the one who prescribes it, but the reason of a counsel from the design and benefit of the one to whom the counsel is given. And here the Apostles aim only at the benefit of the converted Gentiles — namely their salvation — not at their own benefit; for having done their part, they shall have their reward whether they are obeyed or not. Therefore the acts of this council were not laws, but counsels.

The sixth passage is Romans 13: "Let every soul be subject to the higher powers, for there is no power but of God" — which, he says, is meant not only of secular princes but of ecclesiastical ones too. To which I answer, first, that there are no ecclesiastical princes except those who are also civil sovereigns — and their principalities do not extend one inch beyond the bounds of their civil sovereignty. Outside those bounds, they may be received as teachers, but they cannot be acknowledged as princes. For if the Apostle had meant that we should be subject both to our own princes and to the Pope, he would have been teaching us a doctrine Christ himself has told us is impossible — namely, "to serve two masters." And though the Apostle says in another place, "I write these things being absent, lest being present I should use sharpness, according to the power which the Lord hath given me," this does not mean he claimed a power to put any of them to death, or imprison, banish, whip, or fine them — which are punishments. He claimed only the power to excommunicate — which, without the civil power behind it, is nothing more than leaving a man's company and having no more to do with him than with a heathen or a tax collector. And on many occasions that might be a greater pain to the one doing the excommunicating than to the one excommunicated.

The seventh passage is 1 Corinthians 4:21: "Shall I come unto you with a rod, or in love, and the spirit of lenity?" But here again, the rod does not mean a magistrate's power to punish offenders. It means only the power of excommunication — which is not, by its own nature, a punishment, but only an announcement of the punishment Christ shall inflict when he is in possession of his kingdom, at the day of judgment. And even then it will not properly be a punishment — as upon a subject who has broken the law — but a revenge, as upon an enemy or a rebel who denies our Savior's right to the kingdom. So this proves no legislative power in any bishop who does not also hold the civil power.

The eighth passage is 1 Timothy 3:2: "A bishop must be the husband but of one wife, vigilant, sober," and so on — which, he says, was a law. I had thought no one could make a law in the church but the monarch of the church, St. Peter. But suppose this precept was made by St. Peter's authority. Even so, I see no reason to call it a law rather than a piece of advice — since Timothy was not St. Paul's subject but his disciple, and the flock under Timothy's charge were not his subjects in a kingdom but his students in the school of Christ. If every precept Paul gives Timothy is a law, why is this not a law too: "Drink no longer water, but use a little wine for thy health's sake"? And why aren't the prescriptions of good physicians so many laws? The answer is that it is not the imperative manner of speaking that makes a precept a law — it is absolute subjection to a person.

In the same way the ninth passage, 1 Timothy 5:19 — "Against an elder receive not an accusation, but before two or three witnesses" — is a wise precept, but not a law.

The tenth passage is Luke 10:16: "He that heareth you, heareth me; and he that despiseth you, despiseth me." No doubt about it: whoever despises the counsel of those who are sent by Christ despises the counsel of Christ himself. But who are those sent by Christ now, if not those who are ordained pastors by lawful authority? And who is lawfully ordained, who is not ordained by the sovereign pastor? And who is ordained by the sovereign pastor in a Christian commonwealth, who is not ordained by the authority of its sovereign? From this passage, then, it follows that he who hears his sovereign — his sovereign being a Christian — hears Christ; and he who despises the doctrine his Christian king authorizes despises the doctrine of Christ. Which is not what Bellarmine set out to prove here, but its exact contrary.

And in any case, none of this amounts to a law. More than that: a Christian king, as pastor and teacher of his subjects, does not thereby make his doctrines laws. He cannot oblige men to believe. As civil sovereign, he may indeed make laws to suit his doctrine, and those laws may oblige men to certain actions — sometimes to actions they would not otherwise do, and which he ought not to command. Yet when they are commanded, they are laws; and the external actions done in obedience to them, without inward approval, are the actions of the sovereign, not of the subject — who is, in that case, merely an instrument, with no motion of his own at all, because God has commanded obedience to sovereigns.

The eleventh is every passage where the Apostle uses, for counsel, some word men ordinarily use to signify command, or calls the following of his counsel by the name of obedience. So they cite 1 Corinthians 11:2: "I commend you for keeping my precepts as I delivered them to you." The Greek actually says: "I commend you for keeping those things I delivered to you, as I delivered them" — which is far from signifying that they were laws, or anything other than good counsel. And 1 Thessalonians 4:2: "You know what commandments we gave you" — where the Greek phrase, paraggelias edokamen, is equivalent to paredokamen — "what we delivered to you," just as in the passage before. This does not prove the traditions of the Apostles to be anything more than counsels — even though, as verse 8 says, "he that despiseth them, despiseth not man, but God." For our Savior himself came not to judge — that is, not to be king in this world — but to sacrifice himself for sinners, and to leave teachers in his church to lead men to Christ, not to drive them. For Christ never accepts forced actions, which are all that law produces; he accepts the inward conversion of the heart — which is not the work of laws, but of counsel and doctrine.

And then 2 Thessalonians 3:14: "If any man obey not our word by this Epistle, note that man, and have no company with him, that he may be ashamed." From the word "obey," Bellarmine would infer that this epistle was a law to the Thessalonians. Now, the epistles of the emperors were indeed laws. So if the epistle of St. Paul were also a law, the Thessalonians were to obey two masters. But the word "obey," as it stands in the Greek — upakouei — signifies hearkening to, or putting in practice: not only what is commanded by one who has the right to punish, but also what is delivered by way of counsel for our good. And notice that St. Paul does not bid them kill the man who disobeys, nor beat him, nor imprison him, nor fine him — all of which lawmakers may do. He bids them avoid his company, that he may be ashamed. From which it is evident that what the Christians stood in awe of was not the empire of an Apostle, but his reputation among the faithful.

The last passage is Hebrews 13:17: "Obey your leaders, and submit yourselves to them, for they watch for your souls, as those that must give account." Here too the obedience intended is a following of their counsel. For the reason for our obedience is not drawn from the will and command of our pastors, but from our own benefit — it is the salvation of our souls they watch for, not the exaltation of their own power and authority. If this verse meant that everything pastors teach is law, then not only the Pope but every pastor in his parish would have legislative power. Again: if men were bound to obey their pastors, they would have no power to examine their commands. And what then shall we say to St. John, who tells us (1 John 4:1) "not to believe every spirit, but to test the spirits, whether they are of God, because many false prophets have gone out into the world"?

It is plain, therefore, that we may dispute the doctrine of our pastors — but no man can dispute a law. The commands of civil sovereigns are granted on all sides to be laws. And if anyone besides the sovereign could make a law, then all commonwealth — and with it all peace and justice — must cease; which is contrary to all laws, both divine and human. Nothing, therefore, can be drawn from these or any other passages of Scripture to prove that the decrees of the Pope are laws anywhere he does not also hold the civil sovereignty.

The Question of Superiority Between the Pope and the Other Bishops

The last point he tries to prove is this: "that our Savior Christ committed ecclesiastical jurisdiction immediately to none but the Pope." Notice that here he is not handling the question of supremacy between the Pope and Christian kings, but between the Pope and the other bishops.

First, he says it is agreed that the jurisdiction of bishops is, at least in general, de jure divino — by divine right. For this he cites St. Paul (Ephesians 4:11), where he says that Christ, after his ascension into heaven, "gave gifts to men: some apostles, some prophets, and some evangelists, and some pastors, and some teachers." From this he infers that bishops do indeed hold their jurisdiction in God's right — but he will not grant that they have it immediately from God. It must come, he says, derived through the Pope.

But if a man can be said to hold his jurisdiction by divine right and yet not immediately, then what lawful jurisdiction, even a merely civil one, is there in a Christian commonwealth that is not also by divine right? Christian kings have their civil power from God immediately, and the magistrates under a king exercise their several offices by virtue of his commission. What those magistrates do is no less de jure divino mediato — by divine right at one remove — than what bishops do by virtue of the Pope's ordination. All lawful power is from God: immediately in the supreme governor, and mediately in those who hold authority under him. So he must either grant that every constable in the state holds his office by the right of God, or else admit that no bishop holds his office that way — except the Pope himself.

But this whole dispute — whether Christ left jurisdiction to the Pope only, or to the other bishops as well — is, outside the territories where the Pope holds the civil sovereignty, a contention de lana caprina — a quarrel over goat's wool; that is, over nothing. For where they are not sovereigns, none of them has any jurisdiction at all. Jurisdiction is the power of hearing and deciding cases between man and man; and it can belong to no one but the man who has the power to prescribe the rules of right and wrong — that is, to make laws — and to compel men with the sword of justice to obey his decisions, whether pronounced by himself or by the judges he appoints. And no one can lawfully do that but the civil sovereign.

So when he cites Luke 6 — that our Savior called his disciples together and chose twelve of them, whom he named apostles — he proves only that Christ elected them (all except Matthias, Paul, and Barnabas) and gave them power and command to preach. Not to judge cases between man and man; for that is a power Christ refused to take upon himself, saying, "Who made me a judge or a divider over you?" — and in another place, "My kingdom is not of this world." And he that has no power to hear and decide cases between man and man cannot be said to have any jurisdiction at all. None of this prevents our Savior from having given them power to preach and baptize in all parts of the world — supposing they were not forbidden by their own lawful sovereign. For Christ himself, and his apostles, have in many places expressly commanded us to be obedient to our own sovereigns in all things.

The arguments by which he would prove that bishops receive their jurisdiction from the Pope are therefore all in vain, since the Pope, in the dominions of other princes, has no jurisdiction himself. Yet because those same arguments prove the contrary — that all bishops, when they have jurisdiction at all, receive it from their civil sovereigns — I will not omit to run through them.

The first is from Numbers 11, where Moses, unable to bear alone the whole burden of administering the affairs of the people of Israel, was commanded by God to choose seventy elders; and God took part of the spirit of Moses and put it upon those seventy elders. This is to be understood not as God weakening the spirit of Moses — that would not have eased him at all — but as showing that the elders all had their authority derived from him. In this, Bellarmine interprets the passage truly and honestly. But since Moses held the entire sovereignty in the commonwealth of the Jews, what the passage plainly signifies is that they had their authority from the civil sovereign. So this passage proves that bishops in every Christian commonwealth have their authority from the civil sovereign — and from the Pope in his own territories only, not in the territories of any other state.

The second argument is from the nature of monarchy, in which all authority resides in one man, and in others only by derivation from him; and the government of the Church, he says, is monarchical. But this too works in favor of Christian monarchs. For they really are monarchs of their own people — that is, of their own church, since the church is the same thing as a Christian people. Whereas the power of the Pope, even if he were St. Peter himself, is not monarchy at all; it has nothing in it of the archical — ruling — or the cratical — commanding — but only of the didactical — teaching. For God accepts not a forced, but a willing obedience.

The third is that the See of St. Peter is called by St. Cyprian the Head, the Source, the Root, and the Sun, from which the authority of bishops is derived. But by the law of nature — which is a better principle of right and wrong than the word of any doctor who is but a man — the civil sovereign in every commonwealth is the head, the source, the root, and the sun from which all jurisdiction is derived. Therefore the jurisdiction of bishops is derived from the civil sovereign.

The fourth is taken from the inequality of bishops' jurisdictions. For if God (says he) had given jurisdiction to bishops immediately, he would have given them equality of jurisdiction along with equality of order. But we see that some are bishops of only one town, some of a hundred towns, and some of many whole provinces — differences that were never determined by any command of God. Their jurisdiction, therefore, is not from God but from man; one has a greater share and another a lesser, as it pleases the prince of the Church. Now this argument would have served his purpose — if he had first proved that the Pope holds a universal jurisdiction over all Christians. But that has not been proved. And it is notoriously well known that the Pope's large jurisdiction was given him by those who had it to give — namely, the emperors of Rome. (The Patriarch of Constantinople, on the very same title — being bishop of the capital city of the empire and seat of the emperor — claimed to be the Pope's equal.) It follows that all other bishops have their jurisdiction from the sovereigns of the places where they exercise it. And just as, for that reason, they do not hold their authority by divine right, neither does the Pope hold his by divine right — except only where he is also the civil sovereign.

His fifth argument is this: "If bishops have their jurisdiction immediately from God, the Pope could not take it from them, for he can do nothing contrary to God's ordination." This consequence is good, and well proved. "But," says he, "the Pope can do this, and has done it." This too is granted — provided he does it in his own dominions, or in the dominions of any other prince who has given him that power; but not universally, in right of the papacy. For that power belongs to every Christian sovereign within the bounds of his own empire, and is inseparable from the sovereignty. Before the people of Israel, by God's command to Samuel, set a king over themselves after the manner of the other nations, the high priest held the civil government, and none but he could make or depose an inferior priest. But afterward that power was in the king — as can be proved by this very argument of Bellarmine's. For if the priest (high priest or any other) had his jurisdiction immediately from God, then the king could not take it from him, "for he could do nothing contrary to God's ordinance." Yet it is certain that King Solomon deprived Abiathar the high priest of his office (1 Kings 2:26) and placed Zadok in his room (verse 35). Kings, therefore, may in the same way ordain and depose bishops as they see fit for the good governing of their subjects.

His sixth argument is this: if bishops have their jurisdiction de jure divino — that is, immediately from God — then those who maintain it should produce some word of God to prove it; but they can produce none. The argument is good; I have nothing to say against it. But it is an equally good argument to prove that the Pope himself has no jurisdiction in the dominion of any other prince.

Lastly, he brings as an argument the testimony of two Popes, Innocent and Leo. And I do not doubt that he might, with just as good reason, have cited the testimony of nearly every Pope since St. Peter. Considering the love of power naturally planted in mankind, whoever was made Pope would be tempted to uphold the same opinion. Nevertheless, all of them would only be doing what Innocent and Leo did: bearing witness on their own behalf — and therefore their witness is no good.

The Pope's Temporal Power

In his fifth book he has four conclusions. The first is "that the Pope is not lord of all the world." The second, "that the Pope is not lord of all the Christian world." The third, "that the Pope, outside his own territory, has no temporal jurisdiction DIRECTLY." These three conclusions are easily granted. The fourth is "that the Pope has, in the dominions of other princes, the supreme temporal power INDIRECTLY." This is denied — unless by "indirectly" he means that he has gotten it by indirect means; in which case that too is granted. But that is not what he means. When he says the Pope has it indirectly, he means that such temporal jurisdiction belongs to him by right, but that this right is merely a consequence of his pastoral authority, which he could not exercise without it. Therefore (so the doctrine runs) to the pastoral power — which he calls spiritual — the supreme civil power is necessarily annexed; and the Pope thereby has the right to change kingdoms, giving them to one and taking them from another, whenever he thinks it conduces to the salvation of souls.

Before I come to consider the arguments by which he would prove this doctrine, it will not be amiss to lay open its consequences — so that the princes and states who hold the civil sovereignty in their several commonwealths may consider for themselves whether it is convenient for them, and conducive to the good of their subjects, of whom they must give an account at the day of judgment, to admit it.

When it is said that the Pope does not hold the supreme civil power in the territories of other states directly, we are to understand that he does not claim it the way other civil sovereigns do — from the original submission of those who are to be governed. For it is evident, and has already been sufficiently demonstrated in this treatise, that the right of all sovereigns is derived originally from the consent of every one of those who are to be governed: whether those who choose a sovereign do it for their common defense against an enemy, as when they agree among themselves to appoint a man or an assembly of men to protect them, or whether they do it to save their lives, by submission to a conquering enemy. So when the Pope disclaims the supreme civil power over other states "directly," all he denies is that his right comes to him by that route. He does not, for all that, cease to claim it by another route — namely (without the consent of those who are to be governed), by a right given him by God in his elevation to the papacy; and this he calls "indirect." But by whatever route he pretends to it, the power is the same. And if it is granted to be his right, he may depose princes and states as often as it is necessary for the salvation of souls — that is, as often as he wants. For he also claims the sole power to judge whether something is for the salvation of men's souls or not.

And this is the doctrine not only that Bellarmine here, and many other doctors, teach in their sermons and books, but that some councils have decreed, and that Popes have, when occasion served them, put into practice. For the Fourth Lateran Council, held under Pope Innocent III, has this canon (in the third chapter, De Haereticis — on heretics): "If a king, at the Pope's admonition, does not purge his kingdom of heretics, and, being excommunicated for the same, does not make satisfaction within a year, his subjects are absolved of their obedience." And the practice of this doctrine has been seen on various occasions: in the deposing of Chilperic, King of France; in the transfer of the Roman Empire to Charlemagne; in the oppression of John, King of England; in the transferring of the Kingdom of Navarre; and, in recent years, in the League against Henry III of France — and in many more instances.
