I think there are few princes who do not consider this unjust and inconvenient. But I wish they would all resolve to be either kings or subjects. Men cannot serve two masters. Princes ought therefore to relieve their people, either by holding the reins of government wholly in their own hands, or by delivering them wholly into the hands of the Pope — so that the men who are willing to be obedient may be protected in their obedience. For this distinction of temporal and spiritual power is but words. Power is as really divided, and as dangerously for all purposes, by sharing it with an indirect power as with a direct one. But now to his arguments.

The first is this: "The civil power is subject to the spiritual. Therefore he that has the supreme spiritual power has the right to command temporal princes, and to dispose of their temporal goods in order to the spiritual end."

As for this distinction of temporal and spiritual, let us consider in what sense it can intelligibly be said that the temporal, or civil, power is subject to the spiritual. There are only two ways those words can be made into sense. When we say one power is subject to another power, we mean either that the holder of the one is subject to the holder of the other, or that the one power stands to the other as means to an end. For we cannot understand that one power has power over another power, or that one power can have right or command over another power. Subjection, command, right, and power are properties not of powers, but of persons. One power may be subordinate to another the way the art of a saddle-maker is subordinate to the art of a rider. Suppose we grant, then, that civil government is ordained as a means to bring us to spiritual happiness. It still does not follow that if a king has the civil power and the Pope the spiritual, the king is therefore bound to obey the Pope — any more than every saddle-maker is bound to obey every rider. Just as the subordination of an art does not imply the subjection of its practitioner, so the subordination of a government does not imply the subjection of the governor.

So when he says the civil power is subject to the spiritual, his meaning is that the civil sovereign is subject to the spiritual sovereign. And then the argument stands thus: "The civil sovereign is subject to the spiritual; therefore the spiritual prince may command temporal princes." The conclusion is the very thing he was supposed to prove as his premise.

To prove it, he first offers this reason: "Kings and Popes, clergy and laity, make but one commonwealth — that is, but one Church. And in all bodies the members depend on one another. But things spiritual do not depend on things temporal. Therefore things temporal depend on things spiritual, and so are subject to them." In this argument there are two gross errors.

One is the claim that all Christian kings, Popes, clergy, and all other Christian men make but one commonwealth. For it is evident that France is one commonwealth, Spain another, and Venice a third, and so on. These consist of Christians, and so they are several bodies of Christians — that is to say, several churches. And their several sovereigns represent them, which is what makes them capable of commanding and obeying, of doing and suffering, like a natural man. No general or universal church has that capacity until it has a representative — which it does not have on earth. If it had one, there is no doubt that all Christendom would be one commonwealth, whose sovereign would be that representative, in things spiritual and temporal alike. And the Pope, to make himself this representative, lacks three things our Savior has not given him: to command, to judge, and to punish — beyond the punishment of excommunication, which is only to withdraw from those who will not learn from him. For even if the Pope were Christ's only vicar, he cannot exercise his government until our Savior's second coming. And even then it is not the Pope, but St. Peter himself, together with the other apostles, who are to be judges of the world.

The other error in this first argument is his saying that the members of every commonwealth depend on one another, like the members of a natural body. It is true that they cohere together; but they depend only on the sovereign, who is the soul of the commonwealth. When the sovereign fails, the commonwealth dissolves into civil war — no one man so much as cohering with another, for want of a common dependence on a known sovereign — just as the members of a natural body dissolve into earth for want of a soul to hold them together. So there is nothing in this comparison from which to infer a dependence of the laity on the clergy, or of the temporal officers on the spiritual — only the dependence of both on the civil sovereign. And the sovereign ought indeed to direct his civil commands toward the salvation of souls; but he is not, for all that, subject to anyone but God himself. So now you see the labored fallacy of the first argument: it is built to deceive men who do not distinguish between the subordination of actions as means toward an end, and the subjection of persons to one another in the administration of those means. For the means to every end are determined by nature, or by God himself supernaturally; but the power to make men use the means is, in every nation, resigned (by the law of nature, which forbids men to violate the faith they have given) to the civil sovereign.

His second argument is this: "Every commonwealth — because it is supposed to be perfect and sufficient in itself — may command any other commonwealth not subject to it, and force it to change the administration of its government, and even depose its prince and set another in his place, if it cannot otherwise defend itself against the injuries that prince attempts against it. Much more, then, may a spiritual commonwealth command a temporal one to change the administration of its government, and may depose princes and institute others, when it cannot otherwise defend the spiritual good."

That a commonwealth, to defend itself against injuries, may lawfully do everything he says here is very true, and has already been sufficiently demonstrated earlier in this book. And if it were also true that there now exists in this world a spiritual commonwealth distinct from the civil commonwealth, then its prince might, upon injury done to him, or for want of security against injury to come, repair and secure himself by war — which is, in sum, deposing, killing, subduing, or doing any act of hostility. But by the same reasoning, it would be no less lawful for a civil sovereign, upon like injuries done or feared, to make war upon the spiritual sovereign — which, I believe, is more than Cardinal Bellarmine intended to have inferred from his own proposition.

But there is no spiritual commonwealth in this world. For a spiritual commonwealth is the same thing as the kingdom of Christ — which Christ himself says is not of this world, but shall be in the next world, at the resurrection, when those who have lived justly and believed that he was the Christ shall rise (though they died natural bodies) as spiritual bodies. Then it is that our Savior shall judge the world, conquer his adversaries, and make a spiritual commonwealth. In the meantime, seeing there are no men on earth whose bodies are spiritual, there can be no spiritual commonwealth among men who are still in the flesh — unless we call the preachers, who have a commission to teach and to prepare men for their reception into the kingdom of Christ at the resurrection, a commonwealth; and I have proved that they are none.

The third argument is this: "It is not lawful for Christians to tolerate an infidel or heretical king, if he tries to draw them into his heresy or unbelief. But it belongs to the Pope to judge whether a king is drawing his subjects to heresy or not. Therefore the Pope has the right to determine whether a prince is to be deposed or not deposed."

To this I answer that both assertions are false. For Christians — and men of whatever religion — who do not tolerate their king, whatever law he makes, even a law concerning religion, violate their faith, contrary to the divine law, both natural and positive. Nor is there any judge of heresy among subjects but their own civil sovereign. For heresy is nothing else but a private opinion, obstinately maintained, contrary to the opinion which the public person — that is, the representative of the commonwealth — has commanded to be taught. From which it is plain that an opinion publicly appointed to be taught cannot be heresy, and that the sovereign princes who authorize such opinions are not heretics. Heretics are none but private men who stubbornly defend some doctrine prohibited by their lawful sovereigns.

To prove that Christians are not to tolerate infidel or heretical kings, he cites a passage in Deuteronomy 17 where God forbids the Jews, when they set a king over themselves, to choose a foreigner; and from this he infers that it is unlawful for a Christian to choose a king who is not a Christian. And it is true that a Christian — that is, a man who has already pledged himself to receive our Savior, when he comes, as his king — would tempt God too far by choosing as his king in this world one whom he knows will try, by terror and by persuasion, to make him violate his faith. But, says he, it is the same danger to choose a non-Christian for king and then not to depose him once he is chosen. To this I say: the question is not about the danger of not deposing him; it is about the justice of deposing him. To choose him may, in some cases, be unjust. But to depose him, once he is chosen, is in no case just. For it is always a violation of faith, and consequently against the law of nature, which is the eternal law of God. Nor do we read that any such doctrine was accounted Christian in the time of the apostles, or in the time of the Roman emperors, until the Popes gained the civil sovereignty of Rome.

To this he has replied that the Christians of old did not depose Nero, or Diocletian, or Julian, or Valens (an Arian), for this one reason only: that they lacked the temporal forces. Perhaps so. But did our Savior — who, had he called for them, might have had twelve legions of immortal, invulnerable angels to assist him — lack the forces to depose Caesar, or at least Pilate, who unjustly, finding no fault in him, delivered him to the Jews to be crucified? Or if the apostles lacked temporal forces to depose Nero, was it therefore necessary for them, in their epistles to the newly made Christians, to teach them (as they did) to obey the powers constituted over them — of whom Nero, at that time, was one — and to obey them not for fear of their wrath, but for conscience's sake? Shall we say they not only obeyed, but also taught what they did not mean, for want of strength? It is not for want of strength, then, but for conscience's sake, that Christians are to tolerate their heathen princes, or princes who authorize the teaching of an error (for I cannot call anyone whose doctrine is the public doctrine a heretic).

And whereas, in support of the Pope's temporal power, he further alleges that St. Paul (1 Corinthians 6) appointed judges under the heathen princes of those times — judges not ordained by those princes — this is not true. St. Paul only advises them to take some of their brethren to settle their differences as arbitrators, rather than to go to law with one another before the heathen judges: a wholesome precept, full of charity, fit to be practiced in the best Christian commonwealths too. And as for the danger that may arise to religion from subjects tolerating a heathen or an erring prince — that is a point of which a subject is no competent judge. Or if he is, then the Pope's own temporal subjects may likewise judge of the Pope's doctrine. For every Christian prince, as I have proved before, is no less supreme pastor of his own subjects than the Pope is of his.

The fourth argument is taken from the baptism of kings: that in being made Christians, they submit their scepters to Christ, and promise to keep and defend the Christian faith. This is true; for Christian kings are no more than Christ's subjects. But they may, for all that, be the Pope's fellows. For they are supreme pastors of their own subjects; and the Pope is no more than king, and pastor, even in Rome itself.

The fifth argument is drawn from the words spoken by our Savior, "Feed my sheep" — by which, he says, was given all the power necessary for a pastor: the power to chase away wolves, such as heretics; the power to shut up rams, if they are mad or butt the other sheep with their horns, such as evil (though Christian) kings; and the power to give the flock convenient food. From this he infers that St. Peter had these three powers given him by Christ.

To this I answer that the last of these powers is nothing more than the power — or rather the command — to teach. As for the first, the power to chase away wolves, that is, heretics: the passage he quotes is Matthew 7:15, "Beware of false prophets, which come to you in sheep's clothing, but inwardly are ravening wolves." But heretics are not false prophets — they are not prophets at all. And even granting that heretics are the wolves meant there, the apostles were not commanded to kill them, or, if they were kings, to depose them — but to beware of them, flee from them, and avoid them. Nor was that counsel to beware of false prophets given to St. Peter, or to any of the apostles, but to the crowd of Jews who followed him up the mountain — men for the most part not yet converted. So if the passage confers a power of chasing away kings, it was given not only to private men, but to men who were not even Christians.

And as to the power of separating and shutting up furious rams — by which he means Christian kings who refuse to submit themselves to the Roman pastor — our Savior refused to take that power upon himself in this world, and advised letting the wheat and the weeds grow up together until the day of judgment. Much less did he give it to St. Peter; nor can St. Peter give it to the Popes. St. Peter, and all other pastors, are told to treat those Christians who disobey the Church — that is, who disobey the Christian sovereign — as heathen men and tax collectors. And since no one claims for the Pope any authority over heathen princes, they ought to claim none over princes who are to be treated as heathen.

But from the power to teach alone, he infers also a coercive power in the Pope over kings. The pastor, says he, must give his flock convenient food; therefore the Pope may, and ought to, compel kings to do their duty. From which it follows that the Pope, as pastor of Christian men, is King of Kings — which all Christian kings ought indeed either to confess, or else take upon themselves the supreme pastoral charge, every one in his own dominion.

His sixth and last argument is from examples. To which I answer, first, that examples prove nothing; and second, that the examples he cites do not establish so much as a probability of right. The act of Jehoiada in killing Athaliah (2 Kings 11) was done either by the authority of King Joash, or else it was a horrible crime in the high priest — who, ever after the election of King Saul, was a mere subject. The act of St. Ambrose in excommunicating the Emperor Theodosius (if it is true that he did so) was a capital crime. And as for the Popes — Gregory I, Gregory II, Zachary, and Leo III — their judgments are void, as judgments given in their own cause; and the acts done by them in conformity with this doctrine are the greatest crimes (especially that of Zachary) that human nature is capable of.

And this is as much as I have to say of ecclesiastical power. I would have been briefer, and spared the examination of these arguments of Bellarmine's, if they had been his as a private man — and not as the champion of the papacy against all other Christian princes and states.

Chapter 43: What Is Necessary for a Person to Be Received into the Kingdom of Heaven

The Difficulty of Obeying Both God and Man at Once

The most frequent pretext for sedition and civil war in Christian commonwealths has long come from a single difficulty, never yet sufficiently resolved: how to obey both God and man at the same time, when their commands contradict each other. One point is obvious enough. When a man receives two contrary commands, and knows that one of them is God's, he ought to obey that one and not the other — even if the other is the command of his lawful sovereign (whether a monarch or a sovereign assembly), or the command of his own father. The real difficulty is this: when men are commanded in the name of God, they often cannot tell, in many cases, whether the command actually comes from God, or whether the man giving it is merely abusing God's name for private ends of his own. For just as the Church of the Jews had many false prophets who built reputations with the people on invented dreams and visions, so the Church of Christ has had, in every age, false teachers who seek reputation with the people through fantastic and false doctrines — and who use that reputation (as is the nature of ambition) to govern them for their own private benefit.

The Difficulty Vanishes for Those Who Can Distinguish What Is, and What Is Not, Necessary to Salvation

But this difficulty of obeying both God and the civil sovereign on earth carries no weight at all for those who can distinguish between what is necessary and what is not necessary for their reception into the kingdom of God. If the civil sovereign's command is one that can be obeyed without forfeiting eternal life, then it is unjust not to obey it, and the apostle's precept applies: "Servants, obey your masters in all things," and "Children, obey your parents in all things" — along with our Savior's precept: "The scribes and Pharisees sit in Moses' chair; all therefore that they tell you to observe, observe and do." But if the command is one that cannot be obeyed without being condemned to eternal death, then it would be madness to obey it, and our Savior's counsel applies (Matthew 10:28): "Fear not those that kill the body, but cannot kill the soul." Everyone, therefore, who wants to avoid both kinds of punishment — the punishments inflicted in this world for disobedience to an earthly sovereign, and those to be inflicted in the world to come for disobedience to God — needs to be taught to distinguish well between what is, and what is not, necessary to eternal salvation.

All That Is Necessary to Salvation Is Contained in Faith and Obedience

All that is necessary to salvation is contained in two virtues: faith in Christ, and obedience to laws. The second of these, if it were perfect, would be enough by itself. But because we are all guilty of disobedience to God's law — not only originally, in Adam, but also actually, by our own transgressions — something more is now required of us than obedience for the rest of our days. We also need remission of sins for the time past; and that remission is the reward of our faith in Christ. That nothing else is necessarily required for salvation is plain from this: the kingdom of heaven is shut to no one but sinners — that is, to the disobedient, the transgressors of the law — and not even to them, if they repent and believe all the articles of Christian faith that are necessary to salvation.

What Obedience Is Necessary

The obedience God requires of us — God, who in all our actions accepts the will for the deed — is a serious endeavor to obey him; and it goes by every name that signifies that endeavor. So obedience is sometimes called charity and love, because those imply a will to obey, and our Savior himself makes our love of God and of one another a fulfilling of the whole law. Sometimes it is called righteousness, for righteousness is nothing but the will to give everyone his own — that is to say, the will to obey the laws. And sometimes it is called repentance, because to repent implies a turning away from sin, which is the same thing as the will returning to obedience. Whoever sincerely desires to fulfill the commandments of God, or truly repents of his transgressions, or loves God with all his heart and his neighbor as himself, has all the obedience necessary for his reception into the kingdom of God. For if God required perfect innocence, no flesh could be saved.

And Obedience to What Laws

But which commandments are the ones God has given us? Are all the laws given to the Jews by the hand of Moses the commandments of God? If they are, why aren't Christians taught to obey them? If they are not, what laws are God's, besides the law of nature? For our Savior Christ did not give us new laws, but counsel to observe the laws we are already subject to — that is, the laws of nature and the laws of our several sovereigns. Even in the Sermon on the Mount he made no new law for the Jews; he only expounded the laws of Moses, to which they were subject already. The laws of God, therefore, are none other than the laws of nature — chief among them the law that we must not violate our faith: that is, a commandment to obey the civil sovereigns we set up over ourselves by mutual pact with one another. And this law of God, which commands obedience to the civil law, commands by consequence obedience to all the precepts of the Bible — which (as I proved in the previous chapter) is law only where the civil sovereign has made it law, and everywhere else is only counsel, which a man may refuse to obey at his own peril, without injustice.

In a Christian's Faith, Who Is the Person Believed?

Knowing now what obedience is necessary to salvation, and to whom it is owed, we must consider faith next: whom we believe, and why; and what articles, or points, must necessarily be believed by those who are to be saved. First, the person whom we believe. Since it is impossible to believe any person before we know what he says, the person believed must be someone we have heard speak. The person whom Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Moses, and the prophets believed, then, was God himself, who spoke to them supernaturally. And the person whom the apostles and disciples who lived alongside Christ believed was our Savior himself. But of those to whom neither God the Father nor our Savior ever spoke, it cannot be said that the person they believed was God. They believed the apostles, and after them the pastors and doctors of the Church, who recommended to their faith the history of the Old and New Testaments. So the faith of Christians, ever since our Savior's time, has had for its foundation, first, the reputation of their pastors, and afterward the authority of those who made the Old and New Testaments be received as the rule of faith — and none could do that but Christian sovereigns, who are therefore the supreme pastors, and the only persons whom Christians now hear speak from God; except for any to whom God still speaks supernaturally in these days. But because many false prophets "are gone out into the world," other men must examine such spirits (as St. John advises us, 1 John 4:1) "whether they be of God, or not." And so, since the examination of doctrines belongs to the supreme pastor, the person whom everyone without special revelation is to believe is, in every commonwealth, the supreme pastor — that is to say, the civil sovereign.

The Causes of Christian Faith

The causes that lead men to believe any Christian doctrine are various; for faith is the gift of God, and he works it in each man by whatever means seem good to himself. The most ordinary immediate cause of our belief in any point of Christian faith is that we believe the Bible to be the word of God. But why we believe the Bible to be the word of God is much disputed — as every question must be that is badly framed. For people frame the question not as "Why do we believe it?" but as "How do we know it?" — as if believing and knowing were the same thing. And so one side grounds its supposed knowledge on the infallibility of the Church, and the other side on the testimony of the private spirit, and neither side proves what it claims. For how shall a man know the infallibility of the Church, except by first knowing the infallibility of the Scripture? Or how shall a man know that his own private spirit is anything more than a belief grounded on the authority and arguments of his teachers — or on a presumption about his own gifts? Besides, there is nothing in Scripture from which the infallibility of the Church can be inferred; much less the infallibility of any particular church; and least of all the infallibility of any particular man.

Faith Comes by Hearing

It is plain, therefore, that Christian men do not know, but only believe, the Scripture to be the word of God; and the means God is ordinarily pleased to use to make men believe it follows the way of nature — that is to say, it comes from their teachers. This is St. Paul's doctrine concerning Christian faith in general (Romans 10:17): "Faith cometh by hearing" — that is, by hearing our lawful pastors. He also says (Romans 10:14-15): "How shall they believe in him of whom they have not heard? And how shall they hear without a preacher? And how shall they preach, except they be sent?" From this it is evident that the ordinary cause of believing that the Scriptures are the word of God is the same as the cause of believing every other article of our faith: namely, hearing those whom the law allows and appoints to teach us — our parents in their houses, and our pastors in the churches. Experience makes this even plainer. What other reason can be given why, in Christian commonwealths, everyone believes — or at least professes — the Scripture to be the word of God, while in other commonwealths scarcely anyone does, except that in Christian commonwealths people are taught it from infancy, and in other places they are taught otherwise?

But if teaching is the cause of faith, why doesn't everyone believe? It is certain, then, that faith is the gift of God, and he gives it to whom he will. Nevertheless, because he gives it — to those he gives it to — by means of teachers, the immediate cause of faith is hearing. In a school where many are taught, some profit and others do not; the cause of learning in those who profit is the master — yet no one can infer from this that learning is not the gift of God. All good things proceed from God; yet not everyone who has them can say he is inspired, for that implies a supernatural gift and the immediate hand of God — and whoever claims that, claims to be a prophet, and is subject to the examination of the Church.

But whether men know, or believe, or merely grant the Scriptures to be the word of God: if, from passages of Scripture that contain no obscurity, I can show which articles of faith are necessary for salvation — and the only ones necessary — then those men must of necessity know, believe, or grant the same.

The Only Necessary Article of Christian Faith — the Unum Necessarium

The only article of faith that Scripture makes simply necessary to salvation — the unum necessarium, the one thing needed — is this: that JESUS IS THE CHRIST. By the name "Christ" is understood the king whom God had promised, through the prophets of the Old Testament, to send into the world: to reign under him eternally over the Jews, and over those of other nations who would believe in him; and to give them that eternal life which was lost by the sin of Adam. When I have proved this out of Scripture, I will go on to show when, and in what sense, certain other articles may also be called necessary.

Proved from the Aim of the Evangelists

For proof that belief in this one article — Jesus is the Christ — is all the faith required for salvation, my first argument is from the aim of the evangelists, which was, by describing the life of our Savior, to establish that one article. The sum of St. Matthew's gospel is this: that Jesus was of the line of David, and born of a virgin — which are the marks of the true Christ; that the Magi came to worship him as King of the Jews; that Herod, for the same reason, sought to kill him; that John the Baptist proclaimed him; that he preached, by himself and through his apostles, that he was that king; that he taught the law not as a scribe, but as a man with authority; that he cured diseases by his word alone, and did many other miracles that the Christ was foretold to do; that he was hailed as king when he entered Jerusalem; that he warned his followers to beware of all others who would claim to be Christ; that he was arrested, accused, and put to death for saying that he was king; and that the charge of his condemnation, written on the cross, was JESUS OF NAZARETH, THE KING OF THE JEWS. All of this points to one end and no other: that men should believe that Jesus is the Christ. Such, then, was the aim of St. Matthew's gospel. And the aim of all the evangelists, as anyone may see by reading them, was the same. Therefore the aim of the whole gospel was the establishing of that one article. And St. John expressly makes it his conclusion (John 20:31): "These things are written, that you may know that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of the living God."

From the Sermons of the Apostles

My second argument is taken from the subject of the apostles' sermons, both while our Savior lived on earth and after his ascension. In our Savior's time, the apostles were sent (Luke 9:2) to preach the kingdom of God; for neither there, nor in Matthew 10:7, does he give them any commission other than this: "As ye go, preach, saying, the kingdom of heaven is at hand" — that is, that Jesus is the Messiah, the Christ, the king who was to come. That their preaching after his ascension was the same is plain from Acts 17:6: "They drew Jason and certain brethren unto the rulers of the city" (says St. Luke), "crying, These that have turned the world upside down are come hither also, whom Jason hath received. And these all do contrary to the decrees of Caesar, saying that there is another king, one Jesus." And from verses 2 and 3 of the same chapter, where it is said that St. Paul, "as his manner was, went in unto them, and three sabbath days reasoned with them out of the Scriptures, opening and alleging that Christ must needs have suffered, and risen again from the dead, and that this Jesus, whom he preached, is Christ."

From the Easiness of the Doctrine

The third argument is from those places of Scripture that declare all the faith required for salvation to be easy. For if an inward assent of the mind to all the doctrines of Christian faith now taught — most of which are disputed — were necessary to salvation, there would be nothing in the world so hard as being a Christian. The thief on the cross, though repentant, could not have been saved for saying, "Lord, remember me when thou comest into thy kingdom" — words that testify to no other article than this: that Jesus was the king. Nor could it be said (Matthew 11:30) that "Christ's yoke is easy, and his burden light." Nor that "little children believe in him," as in Matthew 18:6. Nor could St. Paul have said (1 Corinthians 1:21), "It pleased God by the foolishness of preaching to save them that believe." Nor could St. Paul himself have been saved — much less become so great a doctor of the Church so suddenly — when he had perhaps never once thought of transubstantiation, or purgatory, or many of the other articles now forced on believers.

From Plain and Clear Texts

The fourth argument is taken from texts that are express, and admit no controversy of interpretation. First, John 5:39: "Search the Scriptures, for in them ye think ye have eternal life; and they are they that testify of me." Our Savior here speaks only of the Scriptures of the Old Testament, for the Jews at that time could not search the Scriptures of the New Testament, which were not yet written. But the Old Testament contains nothing of Christ except the marks by which men might know him when he came: that he would descend from David, be born at Bethlehem, and of a virgin; that he would do great miracles, and the like. Therefore to believe that this Jesus was he was sufficient for eternal life. But more than sufficient is not necessary — and consequently no other article is required.

Second (John 11:26): "Whosoever liveth and believeth in me shall not die eternally." Therefore to believe in Christ is faith sufficient for eternal life, and consequently no more faith than that is necessary. And to believe in Jesus, and to believe that Jesus is the Christ, are one and the same thing, as the verses immediately following make clear. For when our Savior asks Martha (verse 26), "Believest thou this?" she answers (verse 27), "Yea, Lord: I believe that thou art the Christ, the Son of God, which should come into the world." Therefore this article alone is faith sufficient for eternal life, and more than sufficient is not necessary.

Third, John 20:31: "These things are written that ye might believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that believing ye might have life through his name." There, to believe that Jesus is the Christ is faith sufficient for obtaining life; and therefore no other article is necessary.

Fourth, 1 John 4:2: "Every spirit that confesseth that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh is of God." And 1 John 5:1: "Whosoever believeth that Jesus is the Christ is born of God." And verse 5: "Who is he that overcometh the world, but he that believeth that Jesus is the Son of God?"

Fifth, Acts 8:36-37: "See," says the eunuch, "here is water; what doth hinder me to be baptized? And Philip said, If thou believest with all thy heart, thou mayest. And he answered and said, I believe that Jesus Christ is the Son of God." Therefore this article believed — Jesus is the Christ — is sufficient for baptism, that is to say, for our reception into the kingdom of God; and by consequence, it is the only article necessary. And generally, wherever our Savior says to anyone, "Thy faith hath saved thee," the occasion for saying it is some confession that directly, or by consequence, implies a belief that Jesus is the Christ.

From Its Being the Foundation of All Other Articles

The last argument is from the places where this article is made the foundation of faith — for he that holds the foundation shall be saved. The first such place is Matthew 24:23-24: "If any man shall say unto you, Lo, here is Christ, or there, believe it not; for there shall arise false Christs and false prophets, and shall show great signs and wonders," and so on. Here we see that this article — Jesus is the Christ — must be held even if the man who teaches the contrary works great miracles.

The second place is Galatians 1:8: "Though we, or an angel from heaven, preach any other gospel unto you than that we have preached unto you, let him be accursed." But the gospel which Paul and the other apostles preached was only this article, that Jesus is the Christ. Therefore, for the belief of this article, we are to reject the authority of an angel from heaven — much more of any mortal man — if he teaches the contrary. This, then, is the fundamental article of Christian faith.

A third place is 1 John 4:1-2: "Beloved, believe not every spirit. Hereby ye shall know the Spirit of God: every spirit that confesseth that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh is of God." From this it is evident that this article is the measure and rule by which all other articles are to be weighed and examined — and is therefore the only fundamental one.

A fourth is Matthew 16:18, where, after St. Peter had professed this article, saying to our Savior, "Thou art Christ, the Son of the living God," our Savior answered, "Thou art Peter, and upon this rock I will build my church." From which I infer that this article is the rock on which all the other doctrines of the Church are built, as on their foundation.

A fifth is 1 Corinthians 3:11-15: "Other foundation can no man lay than that which is laid: Jesus is the Christ. Now if any man build upon this foundation gold, silver, precious stones, wood, hay, stubble; every man's work shall be made manifest; for the Day shall declare it, because it shall be revealed by fire, and the fire shall try every man's work, of what sort it is. If any man's work abide which he hath built thereupon, he shall receive a reward; if any man's work shall be burnt, he shall suffer loss — but he himself shall be saved, yet so as by fire." These words are partly plain and easy to understand, and partly allegorical and difficult. From the plain part this much may be inferred: pastors who teach this foundation — that Jesus is the Christ — may still be saved even if they draw false consequences from it (something all men are sometimes prone to). And much more may those be saved who are not pastors but hearers, and who believe what their lawful pastors teach them. Therefore belief in this article is sufficient; and by consequence, no other article of faith is necessarily required for salvation.

Now for the allegorical part — that "the fire shall try every man's work," and that "they shall be saved, but so as by fire," or "through fire" (the original Greek is dia puros) — it takes nothing away from the conclusion I have drawn from the plain words. Nevertheless, because this passage has been used as an argument to prove the fire of purgatory, I will offer here my own conjecture about the meaning of this trial of doctrines, and this saving of men as if by fire. The apostle here seems to allude to the words of the prophet Zechariah (Zechariah 13:8-9), who, speaking of the restoration of the kingdom of God, says: "Two parts therein shall be cut off, and die, but the third shall be left therein; and I will bring the third part through the fire, and will refine them as silver is refined, and will try them as gold is tried; they shall call on the name of the Lord, and I will hear them." The day of judgment is the day of the restoration of the kingdom of God; and on that day, St. Peter tells us (2 Peter 3:7, 10, 12), will come the conflagration of the world, in which the wicked shall perish — but the remnant whom God will save shall pass through that fire unhurt, and in it be tried and refined of their idolatry (as silver and gold are refined by fire from their dross), and be brought to call on the name of the true God. Alluding to this, St. Paul says here that the Day — that is, the day of judgment, the great day of our Savior's coming to restore the kingdom of God in Israel — shall try every man's doctrine, judging which are gold, silver, precious stones, and which are wood, hay, stubble. And then those who have built false consequences on the true foundation shall see their doctrines condemned; nevertheless they themselves shall be saved, pass unhurt through this universal fire, and live eternally, calling on the name of the true and only God. Taken in this sense, there is nothing here that does not agree with the rest of Holy Scripture — and not so much as a glimpse of the fire of purgatory.

In What Sense Other Articles May Be Called Necessary

But here a man may ask whether it is not just as necessary to salvation to believe that God is omnipotent; that he is creator of the world; that Jesus Christ is risen; and that all other men shall rise again from the dead at the last day — as it is to believe that Jesus is the Christ. To which I answer: it is; and so are many more articles. But they are articles contained in this one, and they can be deduced from it with more or less difficulty. For who does not see that those who believe Jesus to be the Son of the God of Israel, and that the Israelites had for their God the omnipotent creator of all things, thereby also believe that God is the omnipotent creator of all things? Or how can a man believe that Jesus is the king who shall reign eternally, unless he also believes that Jesus rose again from the dead? A dead man cannot exercise the office of a king. In sum: he that holds this foundation — Jesus is the Christ — holds expressly all that he sees rightly deduced from it, and implicitly all that follows from it, even if he lacks the skill to work out the consequences himself. And so it still stands good that belief in this one article is faith sufficient to obtain remission of sins for the penitent, and consequently to bring them into the kingdom of heaven.

Faith and Obedience Are Both Necessary to Salvation

Now I have shown that all the obedience required for salvation consists in the will to obey the law of God — that is to say, in repentance — and that all the faith required for it is comprehended in the belief of this article: Jesus is the Christ. I will now cite the places in the gospel that prove that all that is necessary to salvation is contained in these two joined together.

The men to whom St. Peter preached on the day of Pentecost, just after our Savior's ascension, asked him and the rest of the apostles (Acts 2:37), "Men and brethren, what shall we do?" St. Peter answered, in the next verse, "Repent, and be baptized every one of you, for the remission of sins, and ye shall receive the gift of the Holy Ghost." Therefore repentance and baptism — that is, believing that Jesus is the Christ — is all that is necessary to salvation.

Again, when a certain ruler asked our Savior (Luke 18:18), "What shall I do to inherit eternal life?" he answered (verse 20), "Thou knowest the commandments: Do not commit adultery, Do not kill, Do not steal, Do not bear false witness, Honor thy father and thy mother." And when the ruler said he had kept all these, our Savior added, "Sell all thou hast, give it to the poor, and come and follow me" — which was as much as to say: rely on me, who am the king. Therefore to fulfill the law, and to believe that Jesus is the king, is all that is required to bring a man to eternal life.

Third, St. Paul says (Romans 1:17), "The just shall live by faith" — not everyone, but the just. Therefore faith and justice (that is, the will to be just, or repentance) are all that is necessary to eternal life.

And in Mark 1:15 our Savior preached, saying, "The time is fulfilled, and the kingdom of God is at hand: repent, and believe the gospel" — that is, the good news that the Christ had come. Therefore to repent, and to believe that Jesus is the Christ, is all that is required for salvation.

What Each of Them Contributes to Salvation

Since, then, it is necessary that faith and obedience (implied in the word "repentance") both work together for our salvation, the question of which of the two justifies us is pointlessly disputed. Still, it will not be pointless to make clear in what manner each of them contributes, and in what sense it is said that we are justified by the one and by the other.

First: if by "righteousness" is understood the justice of the works themselves, then no man can be saved, for there is no one who has not transgressed the law of God. So when we are said to be justified by works, it must be understood of the will — which God always accepts for the work itself, in good men and in evil men alike. In this sense, and this sense only, a man is called just or unjust; and his justice justifies him in this sense: it gives him, in God's acceptance, the title of "just," and makes him capable of living by his faith, which before he was not. So justice justifies in the sense in which "to justify" means to declare a man just — not in the sense of discharging the law, which would make the punishment of his sins unjust.

But a man is also said to be justified when his plea, though insufficient in itself, is accepted — as when we plead our will, our endeavor to fulfill the law, and our repentance for our failings, and God accepts the plea in place of the performance itself. And because God accepts the will for the deed only in the faithful, it is faith that makes our plea good; and in this sense it is that faith alone justifies. So faith and obedience are both necessary to salvation; yet each of them, in a different sense, is said to justify.

Obedience to God and to the Civil Sovereign Do Not Conflict — When the Sovereign Is a Christian

Having now shown what is necessary to salvation, it is not hard to reconcile our obedience to God with our obedience to the civil sovereign — who is either a Christian or an infidel. If he is a Christian, he allows the belief of this article, that Jesus is the Christ, and of all the articles contained in it, or evidently deduced from it — which is all the faith necessary to salvation. And because he is a sovereign, he requires obedience to all his own laws — that is, to all the civil laws — in which are also contained all the laws of nature, that is, all the laws of God. For besides the laws of nature and the laws of the Church, which are part of the civil law (since the church that can make laws is the commonwealth), there are no other divine laws. Whoever obeys his Christian sovereign, therefore, is hindered neither from believing God nor from obeying him.

But suppose a Christian king, starting from this foundation — Jesus is the Christ — should draw some false consequences from it; that is, build some superstructure of hay or stubble; and command it to be taught. Since St. Paul says that such a builder shall be saved, much more shall the man be saved who teaches it by the king's command; and much more still the man who does not teach it at all, but only believes his lawful teacher. And if a subject is forbidden by the civil sovereign to profess some of his own opinions — on what grounds can he disobey? Christian kings may err in deducing a consequence; but who shall judge? Shall a private man judge, when the question is his own obedience? Or shall anyone judge but the one appointed to it by the Church — that is, by the civil sovereign who represents it? And if the Pope, or an apostle, judges — may not he too err in deducing a consequence? Did not one of the two, St. Peter or St. Paul, err in a superstructure, when St. Paul withstood St. Peter to his face? There can therefore be no contradiction between the laws of God and the laws of a Christian commonwealth.

Or When the Sovereign Is an Infidel

And when the civil sovereign is an infidel, any one of his own subjects who resists him sins against the laws of God (for the laws of nature are God's laws), and rejects the counsel of the apostles, who admonish all Christians to obey their princes, and all children and servants to obey their parents and masters in all things. As for their faith, it is internal and invisible. They have the license that Naaman had — the Syrian who bowed in the house of Rimmon at his master's side — and they need not put themselves in danger for it. But if they do, they ought to expect their reward in heaven, not complain of their lawful sovereign — much less make war upon him. For a man who is not glad of any just occasion of martyrdom does not have the faith he professes; he only pretends it, to put some color on his own defiance. And besides, what infidel king is so unreasonable that — knowing he has a subject who waits for the second coming of Christ after the present world is burned, and who intends then to obey him (which is what believing that Jesus is the Christ amounts to), and who in the meantime thinks himself bound to obey the laws of that infidel king (as all Christians are obliged in conscience to do) — he would put such a subject to death, or persecute him?

And this much shall suffice concerning the kingdom of God and church government. In all of it I claim to advance no position of my own, but only to show the consequences that seem to me deducible from the principles of Christian politics — which are the holy Scriptures — in confirmation of the power of civil sovereigns and the duty of their subjects. In citing Scripture, I have tried to avoid texts of obscure or disputed interpretation, and to cite none except in the sense that is plainest and most agreeable to the harmony and aim of the whole Bible, which was written for the re-establishment of the kingdom of God in Christ. For it is not the bare words, but the aim of the writer, that gives the true light by which any writing is to be interpreted. Those who insist on single texts, without considering the main design, can derive nothing from them clearly. Instead, by throwing atoms of Scripture like dust into men's eyes, they make everything more obscure than it is — the usual trick of those who seek not the truth, but their own advantage.

Part IV: Of the Kingdom of Darkness

Chapter 44: Spiritual Darkness from the Misinterpretation of Scripture

What the Kingdom of Darkness Is

Besides the sovereign powers I have discussed so far — divine and human — Scripture mentions another power: that of "the rulers of the darkness of this world" (Ephesians 6:12), "the kingdom of Satan" (Matthew 12:26), and "the principality of Beelzebub over demons" (Matthew 9:34) — that is, over phantasms that appear in the air. For this reason Satan is also called "the prince of the power of the air" (Ephesians 2:2); and, because he rules in the darkness of this world, "the prince of this world" (John 16:11). And accordingly, those under his dominion — in opposition to the faithful, who are the children of the light — are called the children of darkness. Since Beelzebub is prince of phantasms, the inhabitants of his dominion of air and darkness, the children of darkness and these demons, phantasms, or spirits of illusion all signify, allegorically, the same thing.

With this in mind, the KINGDOM OF DARKNESS, as it is set out in these and other places of Scripture, is nothing else but a confederacy of deceivers who, in order to obtain dominion over men in this present world, work by dark and erroneous doctrines to extinguish in them the light both of nature and of the gospel — and so to leave them unprepared for the kingdom of God to come.

The Church Is Not Yet Fully Free of Darkness

Men who have been utterly deprived of bodily sight from birth have no idea of light at all; and no man can imagine any greater light than the greatest he has at some time actually perceived with his outward senses. The same holds for the light of the gospel and the light of the understanding: no one can conceive of a greater degree of it than the degree he has already attained. And so the only means men have of recognizing their own darkness is to reason backward from the unforeseen disasters that befall them along their way.

The darkest part of Satan's kingdom is the part outside the Church of God — among those who do not believe in Jesus Christ. But we cannot say that the Church therefore enjoys, like the land of Goshen, all the light necessary for the work God has assigned us. If it is not night among us, or at least a mist, then how has it come about that in Christendom, almost from the time of the apostles, there has been such jostling of one another out of their places, by foreign war and civil war alike? Such stumbling at every little roughness in their own fortune, and every little eminence in another man's? Such a diversity of roads taken in running toward the same goal — happiness? We are, therefore, still in the dark.

The Four Causes of Spiritual Darkness

The Enemy has been here in the night of our natural ignorance and sown the tares — the weeds — of spiritual error. He did it, first, by abusing and putting out the light of the Scriptures; for we err by not knowing the Scriptures. Second, by introducing the demonology of the heathen poets — their fabulous doctrine concerning demons, which are nothing but idols, phantasms of the brain, with no real nature of their own distinct from human imagination: dead men's ghosts, and fairies, and the other stuff of old wives' tales. Third, by mixing into Scripture various relics of the religion of the Greeks, and much of their vain and erroneous philosophy — especially Aristotle's. Fourth, by mingling with both of these false or uncertain traditions, and feigned or uncertain history.

And so we come to err by "giving heed to seducing spirits," and to the demonology of those "that speak lies in hypocrisy" — or, as it reads in the original (1 Timothy 4:1-2), "of those that play the part of liars" — "with a seared conscience"; that is, against their own knowledge. Concerning the first of these — the seducing of men by the abuse of Scripture — I intend to speak briefly in this chapter.

Errors About the Kingdom of God, from Misinterpreting the Scriptures

The greatest and main abuse of Scripture — the one to which nearly all the rest are either consequences or servants — is the wresting of it to prove that the kingdom of God, mentioned so often in Scripture, is the present Church: the multitude of Christians now living, or now living together with those who, being dead, are to rise again at the last day. Whereas in fact the kingdom of God was first instituted by the ministry of Moses, over the Jews only — who were for that reason called his peculiar people — and it ceased afterward in the election of Saul, when they refused to be governed by God any longer and demanded a king after the manner of the nations; and God himself consented to it, as I proved at length in Chapter 35. After that time there was no other kingdom of God in the world by any covenant. God, of course, always was, is, and shall be king of all men and all creatures in another sense — governing according to his will, by his infinite power.

Nevertheless, he promised through his prophets to restore his government to them again, when the time he has appointed in his secret counsel has fully come, and when they turn back to him in repentance and amendment of life. And not only that: he also invited the Gentiles to come in and enjoy the happiness of his reign, on the same conditions of conversion and repentance. And he promised to send his Son into the world, to expiate the sins of them all by his death, and to prepare them by his teaching to receive him at his second coming. Since that second coming has not yet happened, the kingdom of God is not yet come; and we are now under no kings by covenant except our civil sovereigns — with this one qualification, that Christians are already in the kingdom of grace, inasmuch as they already have the promise of being received at his coming again.

The Error That the Kingdom of God Is the Present Church

Watch what follows from this error — that the present Church is Christ's kingdom. If it were true, there would have to be some one man or assembly by whose mouth our Savior, now in heaven, speaks and gives law, and who represents his person to all Christians; or else several men or assemblies who do the same for the several parts of Christendom. This regal power under Christ is claimed universally by the Pope, and within particular commonwealths by assemblies of the local pastors — when Scripture gives it to none but civil sovereigns. And the claim comes to be disputed so passionately that it puts out the light of nature, and causes so great a darkness in men's understanding that they cannot see who it is they have pledged their obedience to.

And That the Pope Is His Vicar General

From the Pope's claim to be Vicar General of Christ in the present Church — supposed to be that very kingdom of Christ to which the gospel directs us — follows the doctrine that a Christian king must receive his crown from a bishop; as if it were from that ceremony that he derives the phrase Dei gratia — "by the grace of God" — in his title; as if he were made king by God's favor only when he is crowned by the authority of God's universal viceregent on earth. And it follows likewise that every bishop, whoever his sovereign may be, takes at his consecration an oath of absolute obedience to the Pope.

From the same claim follows the doctrine of the Fourth Lateran Council, held under Pope Innocent III (Chapter 3, De Haereticis — "On Heretics"): that if a king, at the Pope's admonition, does not purge his kingdom of heresies, and, being excommunicated for it, does not give satisfaction within a year, his subjects are absolved of the bond of their obedience. Where by "heresies" are understood all opinions the Church of Rome has forbidden to be maintained. And by this means, whenever the political designs of the Pope clash with those of other Christian princes — as they very often do — there arises such a mist among their subjects that they cannot tell a stranger who has thrust himself into the throne of their lawful prince from the man they themselves placed there. And in this darkness of mind they are made to fight one against another, unable to tell their enemies from their friends, under the conduct of another man's ambition.

And That the Pastors Are the Clergy

From the same opinion — that the present Church is the kingdom of God — it comes about that pastors, deacons, and all other ministers of the Church take to themselves the name of "the clergy," and give all other Christians the name of "laity," that is, simply "the people." For "clergy" signifies those whose maintenance is that revenue which God, having reserved it to himself during his reign over the Israelites, assigned to the tribe of Levi to be their inheritance — they were to be his public ministers, and had no portion of land set out for them to live on, as their brethren had. The Pope, therefore, pretending the present Church to be, like the realm of Israel, the kingdom of God, claimed for himself and his subordinate ministers the same kind of revenue, as the inheritance of God; and the name "clergy" suited that claim. And that is why tithes and other tributes, paid to the Levites as God's right among the Israelites, have for a long time been demanded and taken from Christians by ecclesiastics, jure divino — that is, "in God's right."

By this means the people everywhere were obliged to pay a double tribute: one to the State, another to the clergy. And the clergy's share — a tenth of their revenue — is double what a certain king of Athens, a man counted a tyrant, exacted from his subjects to cover all public expenses. He demanded no more than a twentieth part, and maintained the commonwealth abundantly with it. And in the kingdom of the Jews, during the priestly reign of God, the tithes and offerings were the entire public revenue.

From the same mistaking of the present Church for the kingdom of God came the distinction between the civil and the canon laws: the civil law being the acts of sovereigns in their own dominions, and the canon law being the acts of the Pope in those same dominions. These canons, though they were only canons — that is, rules proposed, and received voluntarily by Christian princes — became rules commanded once the Empire passed to Charlemagne and the Pope's power increased; and the emperors themselves, to avoid the greater mischiefs a blinded people might be led into, were forced to let them pass for laws.

Hence it is that in all dominions where the Pope's ecclesiastical power is fully received, Jews, Turks, and pagans are tolerated by the Roman Church in their religion, so far as they do not offend against the civil power in exercising and professing it; whereas for a Christian, even a foreigner, not to be of the Roman religion is a capital offense — because the Pope claims that all Christians are his subjects. Otherwise it would be just as much against the law of nations to persecute a Christian stranger for professing the religion of his own country as to persecute an infidel — or rather more so, since those who are not against Christ are with him.

From the same source it comes that in every Christian state there are certain men exempt, by "ecclesiastical liberty," from the taxes and the tribunals of the civil state. So are the secular clergy, besides the monks and friars — who in many places make up so great a proportion of the common people that, if the need arose, an army could be raised out of them alone, sufficient for any war the Church militant might choose to employ them in, against their own princes or anyone else's.

The Error of Turning Consecration into Conjuration

A second general abuse of Scripture is the turning of CONSECRATION into CONJURATION, or enchantment. To consecrate, in Scripture, is to offer, give, or dedicate a man or any other thing to God, in pious and decent language and gesture, by separating it from common use — that is, to sanctify it, to make it God's, to be used only by those whom God has appointed to be his public ministers (as I have already proved at length in Chapter 35). Consecration changes not the thing consecrated, but only the use of it: from profane and common, to holy and set apart for God's service. But when such words are claimed to change the very nature or quality of the thing itself, that is not consecration. It is either an extraordinary work of God, or a vain and impious conjuration. And since they claim a change of nature in their consecrations constantly — far too frequently for it to be counted an extraordinary work — it is nothing other than a conjuration or incantation, by which they would have men believe in an alteration of nature that is not there, against the testimony of a man's own sight and of all the rest of his senses.

Take the example. In the sacrament of the Lord's Supper, the priest's task is to consecrate bread and wine to God's particular service — which is nothing but a separating of them from common use, to signify, that is, to put men in mind of, their redemption by the passion of Christ, whose body was broken and blood shed upon the cross for our transgressions. Instead, the priest pretends that by speaking the words of our Savior — "Take, eat: this is my body," and "This is my blood" — the nature of bread is no longer there at all, but Christ's very body; even though nothing appears to the sight, or to any other sense of the receiver, that did not appear before the consecration.

The Egyptian conjurers who are said to have turned their rods into serpents and the water into blood are thought only to have deluded the spectators' senses with a false show of things — and yet they are counted enchanters. But what should we have thought of them if there had appeared in their rods nothing like a serpent, and in the enchanted water nothing like blood, nor like anything else but water — and they had simply faced down the king, insisting that these were serpents that looked like rods, and that it was blood that seemed to be water? That would have been both enchantment and lying. And yet in this daily act of the priest, they do the very same thing. They turn the holy words into the manner of a charm — a charm which now produces nothing at all to the senses — and then they face us down that it has turned the bread into a man; no, more than that: into a god; and they require men to worship it as if it were our Savior himself, present as God and man, and thereby to commit the grossest idolatry. For if it is enough to clear the practice of idolatry to say, "it is no longer bread, but God" — why should not the same excuse have served the Egyptians, supposing they had had the face to say that the leeks and onions they worshipped were not really leeks and onions, but a divinity hidden under their "species," or likeness?

The words "This is my body" are equivalent to "This signifies, or represents, my body." That is an ordinary figure of speech. To take it literally is an abuse; and even taken literally, it can extend no further than to the bread which Christ himself, with his own hands, consecrated. For he never said that whatever bread any priest whatsoever pronounces the words "This is my body" or "This is Christ's body" over should instantly be transubstantiated. Nor did the Church of Rome ever establish this transubstantiation until the time of Innocent III — not much more than five hundred years ago, when the power of the Popes was at its highest, and the darkness of the times had grown so thick that men could no longer make out the bread that was given them to eat; especially when it was stamped with the figure of Christ upon the cross — as if they would have men believe it was transubstantiated not only into the body of Christ, but into the wood of his cross as well, and that they ate both together in the sacrament.

Incantation in the Ceremonies of Baptism

The same incantation, in place of consecration, is used also in the sacrament of baptism. There the abuse of God's name in each separate person, and in the whole Trinity, with the sign of the cross at each name, makes up the charm. First, in making the holy water, the priest says: "I conjure thee, thou creature of water, in the name of God the Father Almighty, and in the name of Jesus Christ his only Son our Lord, and in virtue of the Holy Ghost, that thou become conjured water, to drive away all the powers of the Enemy, and to eradicate and supplant the Enemy," and so on. The same in the blessing of the salt to be mingled with it: "That thou become conjured salt, that all phantasms and knavery of the Devil's fraud may fly and depart from the place wherein thou art sprinkled, and every unclean spirit be conjured by Him that shall come to judge the quick and the dead." The same in the blessing of the oil: "That all the power of the Enemy, all the host of the Devil, all assaults and phantasms of Satan, may be driven away by this creature of oil."

And the infant who is to be baptized is subject to many charms of his own. First, at the church door, the priest blows three times in the child's face and says, "Go out of him, unclean spirit, and give place to the Holy Ghost the Comforter" — as if all children, until blown on by the priest, were demoniacs. Again, before the child enters the church, the priest says as before, "I conjure thee, etc., to go out and depart from this servant of God." And the same exorcism is repeated once more before the baptism itself. These and certain other incantations and consecrations fill the administration of the sacraments of baptism and the Lord's Supper — in which everything that serves those holy men, except the unhallowed spittle of the priest, has its own set form of exorcism.

In Marriage, in Visitation of the Sick, and in Consecration of Places

Nor are the other rites — marriage, extreme unction, visitation of the sick, the consecrating of churches and churchyards, and the like — exempt from charms; for they too involve the use of enchanted oil and water, together with the abuse of the cross and of David's holy words, Asperges me Domine hyssopo — "Purge me with hyssop, O Lord" — used as things with power in themselves to drive away phantasms and imaginary spirits.

Errors About Eternal Life and Everlasting Death

Another general error comes from misinterpreting the words "eternal life," "everlasting death," and "the second death." Read Holy Scripture plainly and you find this. God created Adam in a condition of living forever — but conditionally; that is, on condition that he did not disobey the commandment. That immortality was not essential to human nature; it was a consequence of the virtue of the tree of life, which Adam was free to eat from as long as he had not sinned. And after he sinned, he was thrust out of paradise precisely so that he would not eat of that tree and live forever. And Christ's passion is a discharge of sin to all who believe in him — and therefore a restoration of eternal life to all the faithful, and to them only.

Yet the doctrine now — and for a long time past — has been far otherwise: namely, that every man has eternal life by nature, because his soul is immortal. On that view, the flaming sword at the entrance of paradise may keep a man from reaching the tree of life, but it does not keep him from the immortality God took from him for his sin; nor does it leave him needing the sacrifice of Christ to recover it. And the consequence follows: not only the faithful and righteous, but the wicked too, and the heathen, shall enjoy eternal life, without any death at all — much less a second and everlasting death. To patch this up, they say that by "second and everlasting death" is meant a second and everlasting life — but in torments: a figure of speech never used anywhere but in this very case.

All of this doctrine is founded only on some of the obscurer places of the New Testament — places which, when the whole scope of Scripture is considered, are clear enough in a different sense, and unnecessary to the Christian faith. For suppose that when a man dies, nothing remains of him but his carcass. Cannot God, who raised inanimate dust and clay into a living creature by his word, just as easily raise a dead carcass to life again by another word, and continue him alive forever — or make him die again? The "soul," in Scripture, always signifies either the life, or the living creature; and "body and soul" together signify the body alive. On the fifth day of creation, God said, let the water produce reptile animae viventis — "the creeping thing that hath in it a living soul"; the English translates it, "that hath life." Again, God created whales et omnem animam viventem, which in the English is "every living creature." And likewise of man: God made him of the dust of the earth, and breathed in his face the breath of life, et factus est homo in animam viventem — that is, "and man was made a living creature." And after Noah came out of the ark, God says he will no more smite omnem animam viventem — that is, "every living creature." And Deuteronomy 12:23: "Eat not the blood, for the blood is the soul" — that is, "the life."

If "soul" in these places meant an incorporeal substance, with an existence separate from the body, then the same could be inferred just as well of any other living creature as of man. No: the souls of the faithful remain in their bodies, from the resurrection to all eternity, not by their own nature, but by God's special grace — which I think I have already sufficiently proved out of the Scriptures in Chapter 38. And as for the places of the New Testament where it is said that a man shall be cast body and soul into hell fire, that means no more than body and life; that is to say, they shall be cast alive into the perpetual fire of Gehenna.

Hence the Doctrines of Purgatory, Exorcisms, and the Invocation of Saints

This is the window that lets in the dark doctrine, first, of eternal torments; and after that, of purgatory; and consequently of the walking abroad — especially in consecrated, solitary, or dark places — of the ghosts of the dead; and from there, the pretenses of exorcism and the conjuring of phantasms; and likewise the invocation of dead men; and the doctrine of indulgences — that is, of exemption, for a time or forever, from the fire of purgatory, in which these incorporeal substances are supposedly cleansed by burning and made fit for heaven. Here is how it happened. Before the time of our Savior, men were generally possessed — by contagion from the demonology of the Greeks — of the opinion that the souls of men were substances distinct from their bodies; and therefore that when the body died, the soul of every man, godly or wicked, had to subsist somewhere by virtue of its own nature, without acknowledging any supernatural gift of God in the matter. The doctors of the Church doubted for a long time what place these souls were to wait in, until they should be reunited with their bodies at the resurrection — supposing, for a while, that they lay under the altars. But afterward the Church of Rome found it more profitable to build for them this place of purgatory; which some other churches, in this later age, have demolished.

The Texts Cited for These Doctrines Have Been Answered Already

Let us now consider which texts of Scripture seem most to confirm these three general errors I have touched on here. As for the texts Cardinal Bellarmine alleges in favor of the present kingdom of God administered by the Pope — and none make a better show of proof — I have answered them already, and made it evident that the kingdom of God instituted by Moses ended in the election of Saul. After that time, the priest never, by his own authority, deposed any king. What the high priest did to Athaliah was done not in his own right, but in the right of the young king Joash, her son. Solomon, on the other hand, in his own right deposed the high priest Abiathar, and set up another in his place.

The most difficult passage of all that can be brought to prove that the kingdom of God through Christ is already in this world is alleged not by Bellarmine, nor by anyone else of the Church of Rome, but by Beza — who would have the kingdom begin at the resurrection of Christ. Whether he intends by this to entitle the presbytery to the supreme ecclesiastical power in the commonwealth of Geneva (and consequently every presbytery in every other commonwealth), or to entitle princes and other civil sovereigns to it, I do not know. For the presbytery has claimed the power to excommunicate its own kings, and to be the supreme moderator in religion wherever that form of church government exists, no less than the Pope claims it universally.

Answer to the Text from Which Beza Infers That the Kingdom of Christ Began at the Resurrection

The words are these (Mark 9:1): "Verily I say unto you, that there be some of them that stand here, which shall not taste of death, till they have seen the kingdom of God come with power." Taken grammatically, these words make one of two things certain: either some of the men who stood by Christ at that time are still alive, or else the kingdom of God must already be in this present world.

But then there is another, harder place. When the apostles, after our Savior's resurrection and immediately before his ascension, asked him (Acts 1:6), "Wilt thou at this time restore again the kingdom to Israel?" — he answered them: "It is not for you to know the times and the seasons, which the Father hath put in his own power; but ye shall receive power by the coming of the Holy Ghost upon you, and ye shall be my (martyrs) witnesses both in Jerusalem, and in all Judaea, and in Samaria, and unto the uttermost part of the earth." Which is as much as to say: my kingdom is not yet come, and you shall not know in advance when it will come, for it will come like a thief in the night; but I will send you the Holy Ghost, and by him you shall have power to bear witness to all the world, by your preaching, of my resurrection, of the works I have done, and of the doctrine I have taught, so that men may believe in me and expect eternal life at my coming again. How does that square with Christ's kingdom coming at the resurrection?

And there is what St. Paul says (1 Thessalonians 1:9-10): "that they turned from idols, to serve the living and true God, and to wait for his Son from heaven." To wait for his Son from heaven is to wait for his coming to be king in power — which would not be necessary if his kingdom were present already. Again: if the kingdom of God began at the resurrection, as Beza on that place (Mark 9:1) would have it, what reason have Christians had, ever since the resurrection, to say in their prayers, "Thy kingdom come"? It is therefore plain that the words of St. Mark are not to be interpreted that way. "There be some of them that stand here," says our Savior, "that shall not taste of death till they have seen the kingdom of God come in power." If this kingdom were to come at the resurrection of Christ, why does he say "some of them," rather than all? For they all lived until after Christ had risen.

Explaining the Passage in Mark 9:1

But those who demand an exact interpretation of this text should first interpret the similar words of our Savior to St. Peter concerning St. John (John 21:22): "If I will that he tarry till I come, what is that to thee?" On those words was grounded a report that John would never die; yet the truth of that report was neither confirmed as well grounded, nor refuted as ill grounded, by those words. It was simply left as a saying not understood. The same difficulty stands in the place in St. Mark. And if it is lawful to make a guess at the meaning from what immediately follows — both here and in St. Luke, where the same words are repeated — it is not improbable that they refer to the Transfiguration, which is described in the very next verses. There it is said that "after six days Jesus taketh with him Peter, and James, and John" — not all, but some of his disciples — "and leadeth them up into an high mountain apart by themselves: and he was transfigured before them. And his raiment became shining, exceeding white as snow; so as no fuller on earth can white them. And there appeared unto them Elias with Moses: and they were talking with Jesus," and so forth. So they saw Christ in glory and majesty, as he is to come — so much so that "they were sore afraid." And thus the promise of our Savior was fulfilled by way of vision. For it was a vision, as may probably be inferred from St. Luke, who recites the same story (Luke 9:28) and says that Peter and those with him were heavy with sleep; and most certainly from Matthew 17:9, where the same is related again, for our Savior charged them, saying, "Tell no man the vision, until the Son of man be risen from the dead." However it may be, no argument can be drawn from this passage to prove that the kingdom of God begins before the day of judgment.

Other Texts Abused to Defend the Pope's Power

Besides Bellarmine's arguments, there are some other texts pressed into service to prove the Pope's power over civil sovereigns. For instance: the two swords that Christ and his apostles had among them are said to be the spiritual sword and the temporal sword, both supposedly given to St. Peter by Christ. And the two great lights in the sky? The greater one signifies the Pope, they say, and the lesser one the king.

By that method of reading, one might just as well conclude from the first verse of the Bible that "heaven" means the Pope and "earth" means the king. This is not arguing from Scripture. It is wanton insult flung at princes — a fashion that came in after the Popes had grown so secure in their greatness that they could treat all Christian kings with contempt. Treading on the necks of emperors, they mocked both the emperors and the Scripture at once, using the words of Psalm 91: "Thou shalt tread upon the lion and the adder; the young lion and the dragon thou shalt trample under thy feet."

Consecration in Scripture Used No Exorcisms

As for the rites of consecration: these depend, for the most part, on the discretion and judgment of the governors of the Church, not on Scripture. But even so, those governors are bound by one obvious standard — the nature of the action itself. The ceremonies, words, and gestures must be decent and meaningful, or at least appropriate to what is being done.

Look at how it was actually done in Scripture. When Moses consecrated the tabernacle, the altar, and their vessels (Exodus 40), he anointed them with the oil God had commanded to be made for that purpose — and they were holy. Nothing was exorcised. No phantasms were driven away. The same Moses — the civil sovereign of Israel — when he consecrated Aaron the high priest and his sons, washed them with water (not exorcised water), put their garments on them, and anointed them with oil. And they were sanctified to minister to the Lord in the priest's office. It was a simple, decent cleansing and adorning of them before he presented them to God to be his servants.
