When King Solomon — the civil sovereign of Israel — consecrated the temple he had built (2 Kings 8), he stood before the whole congregation of Israel. He blessed them. He gave thanks to God for putting it into his father's heart to build the temple, and for giving him the grace to finish it. Then he prayed: first, that God would accept that house, though it was not remotely adequate to his infinite greatness; second, that God would hear the prayers of his servants who prayed in it, or — if they were far away — toward it. And last, he offered a sacrifice of peace offerings, and the house was dedicated.

Notice what was missing. No procession — the king stood still in his place. No exorcised water. No Asperges me — "sprinkle me," words lifted from a psalm and applied where they do not belong — nor any other irrelevant recycling of words spoken on some other occasion. Just a decent, rational speech, exactly suited to the occasion of presenting God with his newly built house.

We never read that St. John exorcised the water of the Jordan, or that Philip exorcised the river where he baptized the eunuch. Nor did any pastor in the apostles' time take his own spittle, put it to the nose of the person being baptized, and say In odorem suavitatis — "for a sweet savor unto the Lord." No human authority can justify that ceremony: not the spittle, which is unclean; not the application of that Scripture, which is frivolous.

The Soul's Immortality Comes from Grace, Not Nature

Now to the foundation under all of this. Some maintain that the soul, separated from the body, lives eternally — and not only the souls of the elect, by special grace, by the restoration of the eternal life Adam lost through sin and our Savior bought back for the faithful by the sacrifice of himself. No: they claim the souls of the reprobate live forever too, as a property that naturally belongs to the essence of mankind, requiring no grace of God beyond what is given universally to everyone.

There are various passages that at first glance seem to support this. But when I compare them with the passage from Job 14 that I cited back in chapter 38, they seem to me far more open to a different interpretation than the words of Job are.

Take them in order. First, the words of Solomon (Ecclesiastes 12:7): "Then shall the dust return to dust, as it was, and the spirit shall return to God that gave it." If no other text stood directly against it, this could perfectly well mean that God alone knows — and man does not — what becomes of a man's spirit when he dies. And the same Solomon, in the same book (Ecclesiastes 3:20-21), delivers the same thought in exactly that sense: "All go (man and beast) to the same place; all are of the dust, and all turn to dust again; who knoweth that the spirit of man goeth upward, and the spirit of the beast goeth downward to the earth?" That is: nobody knows but God. And it is hardly an unusual way of speaking — we still say of things we do not understand, "God knows what" and "God knows where."

Then there is Genesis 5:24: "Enoch walked with God, and he was not; for God took him." The epistle to the Hebrews expounds it: "He was translated, that he should not die; and was not found, because God had translated him. For before his translation, he had this testimony, that he pleased God." Notice that this passage argues for the immortality of the body just as much as of the soul. And what it actually proves is that this translation was peculiar to those who please God — not common to them and the wicked alike — and that it depended on grace, not on nature.

But on the other side, what interpretation can we possibly give — other than the literal one — to Solomon's words in Ecclesiastes 3:19? "That which befalleth the sons of men, befalleth beasts, even one thing befalleth them; as the one dieth, so doth the other; yea, they have all one breath (one spirit); so that a man hath no preeminence above a beast, for all is vanity." Taken literally, there is no natural immortality of the soul here — and yet nothing that contradicts the eternal life the elect shall enjoy by grace. And again (Ecclesiastes 4:3): "Better is he that hath not yet been, than both they" — better, that is, than those who live or have lived. If the souls of all who have ever lived were immortal, that would be a hard saying, for then having an immortal soul would be worse than having no soul at all. And again (Ecclesiastes 9:5): "The living know they shall die, but the dead know not any thing" — that is, naturally, and before the resurrection of the body.

Another passage that seems to support a natural immortality of the soul is where our Savior says that Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob are living. But this is said of the promise of God, and of their certainty of rising again — not of a life then actual. It is in the same sense that God told Adam that on the day he ate the forbidden fruit he would certainly die: from that moment forward Adam was a dead man by sentence, though not by execution until almost a thousand years later. So Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob were alive by promise when Christ spoke, but they are not actually alive until the resurrection. And the story of Dives and Lazarus makes nothing against this, if we take it — as it is — for a parable.

But there are other places in the New Testament where an immortality seems to be attributed directly to the wicked. It is evident that they shall all rise to judgment. And it is said in many places that they shall go into "everlasting fire, everlasting torments, everlasting punishments," and that "the worm of conscience never dieth." All of this gets bundled into the phrase "everlasting death" — which is then ordinarily interpreted as everlasting life in torments. And yet I can find nowhere in Scripture that any man shall live in torments everlastingly.

It also seems hard to say that God — the Father of mercies; who does in heaven and earth all that he wills; who has the hearts of all men at his disposal; who works in men both the doing and the willing; without whose free gift a man has neither any inclination to good nor any repentance of evil — should punish men's transgressions without any end of time, and with every extremity of torture that men can imagine, and more. So we must consider what "everlasting fire" and the other phrases like it actually mean.

I have shown already that the kingdom of God by Christ begins at the day of judgment. That on that day the faithful shall rise again with glorious and spiritual bodies, and be his subjects in that kingdom, which shall be eternal. That they shall neither marry nor be given in marriage, nor eat and drink as they did in their natural bodies, but live forever in their individual persons, with no need for the eternity-of-the-species that comes by reproduction. That the reprobate also shall rise again, to receive punishment for their sins. And that those of the elect still alive in their earthly bodies on that day shall have their bodies suddenly changed and made spiritual and immortal.

But here is what Scripture nowhere says: that the bodies of the reprobate — who make up the kingdom of Satan — shall also be glorious or spiritual bodies; or that they shall be like the angels of God, neither eating, nor drinking, nor reproducing; or that their life shall be eternal in their individual persons, the way the life of every faithful man is, or the way Adam's life would have been had he not sinned. There is no passage of Scripture to prove any of it — except those passages about eternal torments, which can be interpreted otherwise.

From this we may infer the symmetry of the whole matter. As the elect, after the resurrection, shall be restored to the state Adam was in before he sinned, so the reprobate shall be in the state Adam and his posterity were in after the sin was committed — with this difference: God promised a Redeemer to Adam, and to those of his seed who should trust in him and repent, but not to those who die in their sins, as the reprobate do.

What "Eternal Torments" Means

With all that on the table, the texts that mention eternal fire, eternal torments, or the worm that never dies do not contradict the doctrine of a second and everlasting death, taking the word "death" in its proper and natural sense.

The fire or torments prepared for the wicked — in Gehenna, in Tophet, or wherever — may continue forever. And there may never be a shortage of wicked men to be tormented in them. But that does not mean every wicked man, or any one wicked man, is tormented eternally. For the wicked, being left in the state they were in after Adam's sin, may at the resurrection live as they lived before: marry, give in marriage, have gross and corruptible bodies as all mankind now has — and consequently may reproduce perpetually after the resurrection, as they did before. There is no passage of Scripture against it.

For when St. Paul speaks of the resurrection (1 Corinthians 15), he means only the resurrection to eternal life, not the resurrection to punishment. Of the first he says that the body is "sown in corruption, raised in incorruption; sown in dishonor, raised in honor; sown in weakness, raised in power; sown a natural body, raised a spiritual body." Nothing of the sort can be said of the bodies of those who rise to punishment.

The key text is Luke 20:34-36 — a fertile text. "The children of this world marry, and are given in marriage; but they that shall be counted worthy to obtain that world, and the resurrection from the dead, neither marry, nor are given in marriage: neither can they die any more; for they are equal to the angels, and are the children of God, being the children of the resurrection."

Read it closely. The children of this world — those left in the state Adam left them in — shall marry and be given in marriage; that is, they shall be corruptible and generate one generation after another. That is an immortality of the kind, but not of the persons of men. They are not counted worthy to obtain the next world and an absolute resurrection from the dead; they get only a short time as lodgers in that world, and only in order to receive the punishment their defiance has earned. The elect are the only children of the resurrection — that is, the sole heirs of eternal life. They alone can die no more. It is they who are equal to the angels and are the children of God — not the reprobate.

For the reprobate there remains, after the resurrection, a second and eternal death. Between their resurrection and their second and eternal death there is only a time of punishment and torment — a fire kept burning by the succession of sinners coming into it, lasting as long as mankind endures by propagation, which is eternally.

The Texts Alleged for Purgatory, Answered

On this doctrine of the natural eternity of separated souls is founded, as I said, the doctrine of purgatory. For if eternal life comes by grace only, then there is no life but the life of the body, and no immortality until the resurrection — and purgatory has nowhere to exist and no one to hold.

So let us examine the texts Bellarmine alleges for purgatory, starting with those from the canonical Scripture of the Old Testament.

First, the fasting of David for Saul and Jonathan (2 Kings 1:12), and again for the death of Abner (2 Samuel 3:35). This fasting of David, Bellarmine says, was to obtain something for them at God's hands after their death — because when David fasted to procure the recovery of his own child, he called for food the moment he learned the child was dead. The argument runs: the soul has an existence separate from the body; nothing can be obtained by fasting for souls already in heaven or in hell; therefore there must be some souls of dead men that are neither in heaven nor in hell; therefore they must be in some third place — which must be purgatory.

That is hard straining. He has wrested these passages into a proof of purgatory when the real explanation is plain. The ceremonies of mourning and fasting, when used for the death of men whose life was of no profit to the mourners, are used out of honor to their persons. When done for the death of men by whose life the mourners benefited, they proceed from the mourners' own particular loss. So David honored Saul and Abner with his fasting; and at the death of his own child, he comforted himself by taking his ordinary food.

As for the other places he alleges out of the Old Testament, there is not so much as a show or shadow of proof in them. His method is simple: he brings in every text containing the word "anger," or "fire," or "burning," or "purging," or "cleansing," provided any of the Fathers ever rhetorically applied it in a sermon to the doctrine of purgatory — a doctrine already believed before the text was consulted. Take the first verse of Psalm 37: "O Lord, rebuke me not in thy wrath, nor chasten me in thy hot displeasure." What would this have to do with purgatory, if Augustine had not assigned the "wrath" to the fire of hell and the "displeasure" to the fire of purgatory? And what does Psalm 66:12 have to do with purgatory — "We went through fire and water, and thou broughtest us to a moist place"? These and other texts like them — which the doctors of those times reached for to decorate or stretch out their sermons and commentaries — have been hauled to their purposes by sheer force of wit.

The New Testament Passages for Purgatory, Answered

But Bellarmine alleges other places, from the New Testament, that are not so easy to answer.

The first is Matthew 12:32: "Whosoever speaketh a word against the Son of man, it shall be forgiven him; but whosoever speaketh against the Holy Ghost, it shall not be forgiven him, neither in this world, nor in the world to come." Here he insists that "the world to come" is purgatory — a place where some sins may be forgiven that were not forgiven in this world.

But it is manifest that there are only three worlds. One, from the creation to the flood, destroyed by water, and called in Scripture the old world. Another, from the flood to the day of judgment, which is the present world, and shall be destroyed by fire. And the third, from the day of judgment forward, everlasting, which is called the world to come — and in which, by everyone's agreement, there shall be no purgatory. So "the world to come" and purgatory are inconsistent. The very text he builds on excludes the thing he builds.

What, then, can our Savior's words mean? I confess they are very hard to reconcile with all the doctrines now unanimously received. And it is no shame to confess that the depth of Scripture is too great to be sounded by the shortness of human understanding. Nevertheless, I can propose, for the consideration of more learned divines, what the text itself suggests.

First: since to speak against the Holy Ghost — the third person of the Trinity — is to speak against the Church, in which the Holy Ghost resides, it seems the comparison is between the gentleness of our Savior in bearing with offenses done to him while he was on earth, and the severity of the pastors after him toward those who would deny their authority, which came from the Holy Ghost. As if he had said: You that deny my power — even you that shall crucify me — shall be pardoned by me as often as you turn to me in repentance. But if you deny the power of those who teach you hereafter by virtue of the Holy Ghost, they shall be inexorable. They shall not forgive you. They shall persecute you in this world and leave you without absolution (though you turn to me, unless you turn to them as well), consigned — as far as lies in their power — to the punishments of the world to come. On this reading, the words may be taken as a prophecy, a prediction of the times — and the history of the Christian Church has borne it out.

Or, if that is not the meaning (for I am not dogmatic about such difficult places), perhaps there may be room left, after the resurrection, for the repentance of some sinners. There is another passage that seems to agree with this. Considering the words of St. Paul (1 Corinthians 15:29) — "What shall they do which are baptized for the dead, if the dead rise not at all? why also are they baptized for the dead?" — a man may plausibly infer, as some have, that in St. Paul's time there was a custom of receiving baptism for the dead: standing in for the persons of one's deceased friends — much as believers today are sureties and undertakers for the faith of infants who are not yet capable of believing — and undertaking that they should be ready to obey and receive our Savior as their king at his coming again. If so, then the forgiveness of sins in the world to come has no need of a purgatory.

But there is so much paradox in both of these interpretations that I do not trust them. I propose them to those thoroughly versed in the Scripture, to inquire whether some clearer passage contradicts them. This much, however, I do see in evident Scripture: there is neither the word "purgatory" nor the thing itself, not in this text nor in any other. Nor is there anything that can prove a necessity of a place for the soul without the body — not for the soul of Lazarus during the four days he was dead, nor for the souls the Roman Church claims are being tormented in purgatory right now. For God, who could give life to a piece of clay, has the same power to give life again to a dead man, and to renew his lifeless, rotten carcass into a glorious, spiritual, and immortal body.

Another place is 1 Corinthians 3, where it is said that those who build stubble, hay, and the like on the true foundation shall see their work perish, but "they themselves shall be saved; yet so as by fire." This fire, Bellarmine will have to be the fire of purgatory. But the words, as I have said before, are an allusion to Zechariah 13:9: "I will bring the third part through the fire, and refine them as silver is refined, and will try them as gold is tried" — which is spoken of the coming of the Messiah in power and glory, that is, at the day of judgment and the conflagration of the present world. In that fire the elect shall not be consumed but refined: they shall shed their erroneous doctrines and traditions, have them singed off, as it were, and afterward call on the name of the true God. In the same way the Apostle says of those who hold the foundation — Jesus is the Christ — but build erroneous doctrines on it, that they shall not be consumed in the fire that renews the world, but shall pass through it to salvation: yet in such a way as to see and abandon their former errors. The builders are the pastors; the foundation, that Jesus is the Christ; the stubble and hay, the false conclusions drawn from it through ignorance or frailty; the gold, silver, and precious stones are their true doctrines; and the refining or purging is the abandoning of their errors. In all of this there is no shadow of support for the burning of incorporeal — that is to say, impassible — souls: souls incapable of suffering anything at all.

How "Baptism for the Dead" Should Be Understood

A third place is that of 1 Corinthians 15, already mentioned, concerning baptism for the dead. From it Bellarmine concludes, first, that prayers for the dead are not unprofitable, and from that, that there is a fire of purgatory. Neither conclusion follows.

Of the many interpretations of the word "baptism," he ranks first the one that takes baptism (metaphorically) as a baptism of penance — so that men are "baptized" when they fast, and pray, and give alms; and thus baptism for the dead and prayer for the dead are the same thing. But this is a metaphor without one single example, either in Scripture or in any other use of language — and it clashes with the harmony and drift of Scripture besides. The word "baptism" is used (Mark 10:38; Luke 12:59) for being dipped in one's own blood, as Christ was on the cross and as most of the apostles were in bearing witness to him. But it is hard to say that prayer, fasting, and alms bear any resemblance to dipping. The word is also used (Matthew 3:11) — and this might seem to do something for purgatory — for a purging with fire. But it is evident that the fire and purging mentioned there are the same the prophet Zechariah speaks of (Zechariah 13:9): "I will bring the third part through the fire, and will refine them," and so on. And St. Peter after him (1 Peter 1:7): "That the trial of your faith, which is much more precious than of gold that perisheth, though it be tried with fire, might be found unto praise, and honour, and glory at the appearing of Jesus Christ." And St. Paul (1 Corinthians 3:13): "The fire shall try every man's work, of what sort it is." But St. Peter and St. Paul speak of the fire that shall come at the second appearing of Christ, and the prophet Zechariah of the day of judgment. So the place in St. Matthew may be interpreted the same way — and then there is no necessity for the fire of purgatory.

The other interpretation of baptism for the dead is the one I mentioned above, which Bellarmine ranks second in probability — and from it he likewise infers the usefulness of prayer for the dead. For if, after the resurrection, those who never heard of Christ or never believed in him may be received into Christ's kingdom, then it is not in vain for their friends to pray for them after their death, until they should rise. Grant all of it. Grant that God, at the prayers of the faithful, may convert to himself some of those who never heard Christ preached and so cannot have rejected him; grant that men's charity in that point cannot be blamed. It still concludes nothing for purgatory. Because to rise from death to life is one thing; to rise from purgatory to life is quite another — that would be a rising from life to life, from a life in torments to a life in joy.

A fourth place is Matthew 5:25: "Agree with thine adversary quickly, whilest thou art in the way with him, lest at any time the adversary deliver thee to the officer, and thou be cast into prison. Verily I say unto thee, thou shalt by no means come out thence, till thou hast paid the uttermost farthing." In this allegory, the offender is the sinner; both the adversary and the judge are God; the way is this life; the prison is the grave; the officer is death. And from that prison the sinner shall not rise again to eternal life, but to a second death — unless the uttermost farthing is paid, or Christ pays it for him by his passion, which is a full ransom for every kind of sin, lesser sins and greater crimes alike, both made equally forgivable by the passion of Christ. No purgatory: a grave, and a ransom.

The fifth place is Matthew 5:22: "Whosoever is angry with his brother without a cause, shall be guilty in judgment. And whosoever shall say to his brother, Racha" — an Aramaic insult, roughly "worthless" — "shall be guilty in the council. But whosoever shall say, Thou fool, shall be guilty to hell fire." From these words Bellarmine infers three sorts of sins and three sorts of punishments, with only the last punished by hellfire — and consequently, that after this life there is punishment of lesser sins in purgatory.

There is no shadow of support for that inference in any interpretation ever given of these words. Shall there be, after this life, a system of separate courts of justice, like the ones the Jews had in our Savior's time to hear and determine different sorts of crimes — the judges here, the council there? Shall not all judgment belong to Christ and his apostles? To understand the text, we must not read it in isolation but together with the words before and after it. In this chapter our Savior is interpreting the law of Moses, which the Jews thought they had fulfilled so long as they had not violated its grammatical sense — however thoroughly they had violated the intent of the Legislator. They thought the sixth commandment was broken only by killing a man, and the seventh only when a man lay with a woman not his wife. So our Savior tells them: the inward anger of a man against his brother, if it is without just cause, is homicide. You have heard the law of Moses — "Thou shalt not kill," and "Whosoever shall kill shall be condemned before the judges," or before the session of the seventy. But I say to you: to be angry with one's brother without cause, or to call him Racha, or fool, is homicide — and shall be punished at the day of judgment, at the session of Christ and his apostles, with hellfire.

So the words were never meant to distinguish between different crimes, different courts, and different punishments. They were meant to rebuke the Jews' way of ranking sin against sin — drawn not from the difference of the will in obeying God, but from the difference of their temporal courts — and to show them that whoever has the will to hurt his brother, even if the effect goes no further than an insult, or never shows at all, shall be cast into hellfire by the judges and the session, which at the day of judgment shall be one and the same court, not different ones. With all this considered, what can be drawn from this text to support purgatory, I cannot imagine.

The sixth place is Luke 16:9: "Make ye friends of the unrighteous Mammon, that when ye fail, they may receive you into everlasting tabernacles." This he alleges to prove the invocation of departed saints. But the sense is plain: we should use our riches to make friends of the poor, and thereby obtain their prayers while they live. "He that giveth to the poor, lendeth to the Lord."

The seventh is Luke 23:42: "Lord, remember me when thou comest into thy kingdom." Therefore, says Bellarmine, there is remission of sins after this life. But the inference does not hold. Our Savior forgave him then and there; and at his coming again in glory, he will remember to raise him again to eternal life.

The eighth is Acts 2:24, where St. Peter says of Christ "that God had raised him up, and loosed the pains of death, because it was not possible he should be holden of it." This he interprets as a descent of Christ into purgatory, to loose some souls there from their torments. But it is manifest that it was Christ who was loosed; it was Christ who could not be held by death, or the grave — not the souls in purgatory. And if what Beza says in his notes on this place is well observed — that instead of "pains" the word should be "bands" — then there is no further reason to go hunting for purgatory in this text at all.

Chapter 45: Demonology and Other Relics of Pagan Religion

Where Demonology Came From

When light strikes the organs of sight — whether it comes straight from a luminous body, or bounces off an opaque one, or bends as it passes through something transparent — it produces, in any living creature God has equipped with such organs, an imagination of the object the light came from. That imagination is what we call sight. And it does not feel like a mere imagination; it feels like the body itself, out there, outside us. It is exactly like what happens when a man presses hard on his own eye: a light appears outside him and in front of him, a light no one else can see — because there is in fact nothing out there at all, only a motion in the inner organs, pressing outward by resistance, that makes him think there is. And when that motion continues after the object that caused it is gone, we call it imagination, and memory, and — in sleep, or when the organs are badly disordered by sickness or violence — a dream. I covered all of this briefly back in Chapters 2 and 3.

The ancient pretenders to natural knowledge never discovered this nature of sight; still less did ordinary people, who do not bother with things so remote from daily use. So it was hard for men to think of those images in the imagination and the senses as anything other than things really existing outside us. Some — because the images vanish away, they know not where or how — insist they are absolutely incorporeal, that is, immaterial: forms without matter; color and shape without any colored or shaped body; able to put on airy bodies, like a garment, whenever they wish to make themselves visible to our bodily eyes. Others say they are bodies, and living creatures, but made of air or some other finer, more ethereal matter, which condenses when they want to be seen. But both camps agree on one general name for them: DEMONS. As if the dead people they dreamed of were not inhabitants of their own brains, but of the air, or of heaven, or hell — not phantasms, but ghosts. They had exactly as much reason for this as a man would have for saying he saw his own ghost in a mirror, or the ghosts of the stars in a river, or for calling the ordinary appearance of the sun — about a foot wide, as it looks — the demon, or ghost, of that great sun that lights the whole visible world.

And having invented these demons, men feared them as things of unknown — which is to say, unlimited — power to do them good or harm. That fear gave the governors of the pagan commonwealths their opening: they regulated it by establishing DEMONOLOGY (a field in which the poets, as the principal priests of pagan religion, were specially employed and revered), and they aimed it at the public peace, and at the obedience of subjects necessary to that peace. They sorted the demons into good ones and evil ones — the good as a spur to keep men observing the laws, the evil as reins to hold them back from breaking them.

What the Ancients' Demons Were

What sort of things they were that got the name of demons appears partly in the genealogy of their gods written by Hesiod, one of the most ancient of the Greek poets, and partly in other histories — some of which I noted earlier, in Chapter 12 of this book.

How the Doctrine Spread

The Greeks, through their colonies and conquests, carried their language and writings into Asia, Egypt, and Italy — and with them, by necessary consequence, their demonology, or (as St. Paul calls it) their "doctrines of devils." By that route the contagion reached the Jews as well, both those of Judaea and those of Alexandria and the other places where they were scattered. But the Jews did not apply the name "demon," as the Greeks did, to good spirits and evil ones alike. They reserved it for the evil only. The good demons they called the Spirit of God, and they considered anyone such a spirit entered to be a prophet. In short: anything extraordinary, if good, they credited to the Spirit of God; if evil, to some demon — but always a kakodaimon, an evil demon, that is, a devil.

And so they used the word "demoniacs" — people possessed by the devil — for those we would call madmen or lunatics; or for those who had the falling sickness — epilepsy; or for anyone who said something that they, for lack of understanding, thought absurd. Of a notoriously unclean person they would say he had an unclean spirit; of a mute man, that he had a dumb devil. Of John the Baptist they said, because of his extraordinary fasting, that he had a devil (Matthew 11:18). And of our Savior, because he said that whoever keeps his sayings shall not see death in aeternum — forever — they said, "Now we know you have a devil; Abraham is dead, and the prophets are dead" (John 8:52). And again, because he said they were trying to kill him, the people answered, "You have a devil; who is trying to kill you?" (John 7:20). All of which makes it plain that the Jews held the same opinion about phantasms that the Greeks did — namely, that they were not phantasms at all, not idols of the brain, but real things, independent of the imagination.

Why Our Savior Did Not Correct It

If this doctrine is false, someone may ask, why did our Savior not contradict it and teach the contrary? Indeed, why does he on various occasions use forms of speech that seem to confirm it?

To this I answer: first, where Christ says "a spirit has not flesh and bone," he shows that spirits exist, but he does not deny that they are bodies. And where St. Paul says "we shall rise spiritual bodies," he acknowledges the nature of spirits — but as bodily spirits. That is not hard to understand. Air and many other things are bodies, though they are not flesh and bone or any other gross body the eye can pick out.

But when our Savior speaks to the devil and commands him to come out of a man — if by "the devil" is meant a disease, such as frenzy or lunacy, or else a corporeal spirit, isn't the speech improper? Can a disease hear? And can there be a corporeal spirit in a body of flesh and bone that is already full of vital and animal spirits? Doesn't it follow that there must be spirits that neither have bodies nor are mere imaginations?

To the first objection I answer that addressing a command to the madness or lunacy he is curing is no more improper than his rebuking of the fever, or of the wind and the sea — for those do not hear either. Nor is it more improper than God's command to the light, to the firmament, to the sun and stars, when he commanded them to be — for they could not hear before they existed. Those speeches are not improper, because they signify the power of God's word. And so it is no more improper to command madness or lunacy — under the name of devils, which is how everyone then commonly understood those conditions — to depart out of a man's body. To the second objection, concerning spirits being incorporeal: I have not yet found any place in Scripture from which it can be gathered that any man was ever possessed by any corporeal spirit except his own — the one by which his body is naturally moved.

Scripture Does Not Teach That Spirits Are Incorporeal

Immediately after the Holy Spirit descended on him in the form of a dove, our Savior is said by St. Matthew (Matthew 4:1) to have been "led up by the Spirit into the wilderness"; and the same event is told by St. Luke (Luke 4:1) in these words: "Jesus, being full of the Holy Spirit, was led in the Spirit into the wilderness." It is evident that "the Spirit" there means the Holy Spirit. And this cannot be interpreted as a possession — for Christ and the Holy Spirit are one and the same substance, and one substance cannot be "possessed" by itself.

In the verses that follow, he is said to have been "taken up by the devil into the holy city, and set upon a pinnacle of the temple." Shall we conclude from this that he was possessed by the devil, or carried there by force? And again, he was "carried from there by the devil onto an exceedingly high mountain, who showed him from it all the kingdoms of the world." Here we are not to believe he was either possessed or forced by the devil — and no mountain is high enough, taken literally, to show a man one whole hemisphere, let alone all the kingdoms of the world. What, then, can this passage mean, except that he went into the wilderness of his own accord, and that this carrying of him up and down — from the wilderness to the city, and from there onto a mountain — was a vision? St. Luke's phrasing agrees with this: he says Jesus was led into the wilderness not by, but in, the Spirit; while concerning the mountain and the pinnacle of the temple he speaks as St. Matthew does. All of which suits the nature of a vision.

Again, where St. Luke says of Judas Iscariot that "Satan entered into him," and that he thereupon went and conferred with the chief priests and captains about how to betray Christ to them — it may be answered that by the entering of Satan (that is, the Enemy) is meant his hostile and traitorous intention of selling his Lord and Master. For just as "the Holy Spirit" in Scripture frequently means the graces and good inclinations the Holy Spirit gives, so "the entering of Satan" may mean the wicked thoughts and designs of the adversaries of Christ and his disciples. It is hard to say the devil entered Judas before he had any such hostile design; and it is pointless to say he was first Christ's enemy in his heart and that the devil only entered him afterward. The entering of Satan and his wicked purpose were one and the same thing.

But if there is no immaterial spirit, and no possession of men's bodies by any corporeal spirit, the question comes back again: why did our Savior and his apostles not teach the people so, in words so clear that no one could doubt it any longer? My answer is that questions like these are more curious than necessary for a Christian man's salvation. One might as well ask why Christ, who could have given faith, piety, and every moral virtue to all men, gave them to some only and not to all; or why he left the search for natural causes and the sciences to the natural reason and industry of men, instead of revealing them supernaturally to everyone, or to anyone; and many other such questions. And yet probable and pious reasons can be offered. When God brought the Israelites into the Promised Land, he did not secure them in it by subduing all the nations around them; he left many of them there, as thorns in their sides, to awaken the Israelites' piety and industry from time to time. In the same way our Savior, in conducting us toward his heavenly kingdom, did not clear away every difficulty in natural questions; he left them standing, to exercise our industry and reason. The whole scope of his preaching was to show us the plain and direct way to salvation — namely, belief in this article: that he was the Christ, the Son of the living God, sent into the world to sacrifice himself for our sins, and at his coming again to reign gloriously over his elect and save them from their enemies eternally. The opinion that spirits or phantasms can possess people is no obstacle on that road — though for some it is an occasion to wander off the road and follow their own inventions.

If we demand that Scripture settle every question that might be raised to trouble us in performing God's commands, we may as well complain of Moses for not having set down the time when such spirits were created, alongside the creation of the earth and sea and of men and beasts. To conclude: I find in Scripture that there are angels and spirits, good and evil. I do not find that they are incorporeal, like the apparitions men see in the dark, or in a dream, or in a vision — the things the Latins called spectra, and took for demons. I find that there are corporeal spirits, though subtle and invisible; but not that any man's body was ever possessed or inhabited by them. And I find that the bodies of the saints shall be of that kind — spiritual bodies, as St. Paul calls them.

The Power of Casting Out Devils Is Not What It Was in the Early Church

Nevertheless, the contrary doctrine — that there are incorporeal spirits — has prevailed in the Church so far that the practice of exorcism (that is, the ejection of devils by conjuration) is built upon it; and though it is rarely and faintly practiced now, it has not yet been given up entirely. That there were many demoniacs in the early Church and few madmen and other such singular diseases, whereas in our times we hear of and see many madmen and few demoniacs, comes not from any change in nature, but from a change in names.

But how it came about that the apostles, and for a time the pastors of the Church after them, cured those singular diseases, which now they are not seen to do; and likewise why it is not in the power of every true believer now to do everything the faithful did then — that is, as we read (Mark 16:17), in Christ's name to cast out devils, to speak with new tongues, to take up serpents, to drink deadly poison without harm, and to cure the sick by the laying on of their hands, all with no other words than "in the name of Jesus" — that is another question. And the probable answer is this: those extraordinary gifts were given to the Church for no longer than the time men trusted wholly in Christ and looked for their happiness only in his kingdom to come. When instead they began to seek authority and riches, and to trust their own cunning to win a kingdom of this world, these supernatural gifts of God were taken from them again.

Another Relic of Paganism: Image Worship — Left in the Church, Not Brought Into It

Another relic of paganism is the worship of images. It was instituted neither by Moses in the Old Testament nor by Christ in the New; nor was it imported from the pagans — it was simply left among them, still in place, after they had given their names to Christ. Before our Savior preached, the general religion of the pagans was to worship as gods the appearances that remain in the brain from the impression of external bodies on the organs of sense — the things commonly called ideas, idols, phantasms, conceits. These are representations of the external bodies that cause them, and they have no more reality in them than the things that seem to stand before us in a dream. That is why St. Paul says, "We know that an idol is nothing." Not that he thought an image of metal, stone, or wood was nothing — but that the thing men honored or feared in the image, and held for a god, was a mere figment, with no place, no habitation, no motion, no existence at all, except in the motions of the brain.

The worship of these figments with divine honor is what Scripture calls idolatry, and rebellion against God. For God was King of the Jews, and his lieutenant was first Moses and afterward the High Priest. If the people had been permitted to worship and pray to images — which are representations of their own imaginations — they would have had no further dependence on the true God, of whom there can be no likeness, nor on his prime ministers, Moses and the High Priests. Every man would have governed himself according to his own appetite, to the utter overthrow of the commonwealth and their own destruction for want of union. And therefore the first law of God was that they should not take for gods alienos deos — the gods of other nations — but only that one true God who deigned to commune with Moses, and through him to give them laws and directions for their peace and for their salvation from their enemies. And the second law was that they should not make for themselves any image of their own invention to worship. For it is the same deposing of a king to submit to another king, whether he is set up by a neighboring nation or by ourselves.

Answering Texts That Seem to Support Images

The places of Scripture claimed to support setting up images to worship them — or setting them up at all in the places where God is worshipped — are these. First, two examples: the cherubim over the Ark of God, and the bronze serpent. Second, certain texts in which we are commanded to worship certain creatures for their relation to God — as, to worship his footstool. And last, certain other texts said to authorize a religious honoring of holy things. But before I examine the force of these passages to prove what is claimed, I must first explain what is to be understood by worshipping, and what by images and idols.

What Worship Is

I have already shown, earlier in this book, that to honor is to value highly the power of a person, and that such value is measured by comparing him with others. But because nothing can be compared with God in power, we do not honor him — we dishonor him — by any valuation less than infinite. Honor, properly speaking, is by its own nature secret, internal, in the heart. But the inward thoughts of men, when they appear outwardly in words and actions, are the signs of our honoring; and these go by the name of worship — in Latin, cultus. Therefore, to pray to someone, to swear by him, to obey him, to be diligent and attentive in serving him — in short, all words and actions that show fear of offending or desire to please — is worship, whether those words and actions are sincere or feigned. And because they appear as signs of honoring, they are ordinarily also called honor.

The Difference Between Divine and Civil Worship

The worship we offer to those we believe to be merely men — kings, and men in authority — is civil worship. But the worship we offer to what we think to be God, whatever the words, ceremonies, gestures, or other actions may be, is divine worship. To fall prostrate before a king, by a man who thinks him only a man, is merely civil worship. But a man who so much as takes off his hat in church, because he thinks it the house of God, worships with divine worship.

Those who try to ground the distinction between divine and civil worship not in the intention of the worshipper, but in the Greek words douleia and latreia, deceive themselves. There were two sorts of servants. One sort were those absolutely in the power of their masters — slaves taken in war, and their children — whose bodies were not their own, whose lives depended on their masters' will so completely as to be forfeited at the least disobedience, and who were bought and sold like beasts. These were called douloi, properly "slaves," and their service douleia. The other sort were those who served voluntarily, for hire or in hope of benefit from their masters. These were called thetes — domestic servants — to whose service the masters had no further right than was contained in the covenants made between them. These two kinds of servants have this much in common: their labor is appointed for them by another, whether they serve as a slave or as a voluntary servant. And the word latris is the general name for both, meaning anyone who works for another, slave or volunteer. So latreia signifies service in general, while douleia signifies the service of bondmen only, and the condition of slavery. And in Scripture, both words are used for our service of God interchangeably: douleia, because we are God's slaves; latreia, because we serve him. And in every kind of service is contained not only obedience but also worship — that is, the actions, gestures, and words that signify honor.

What an Image Is; Phantasms

An IMAGE, in the strictest sense of the word, is the resemblance of something visible. In this sense, the phantastical forms, apparitions, or seemings of visible bodies to the sight are nothing but images: the appearance of a man or some other thing in the water, by reflection or refraction; or of the sun or the stars seen directly through the air. These appearances are nothing real in the things seen, nor in the place where they seem to be. Their sizes and shapes are not the same as the object's, but change with the variation of the organs of sight, or with lenses. They are often present in our imagination and in our dreams when the object is absent, or changed into other colors and shapes — things that depend only on the imagination. These are the images that were originally and most properly called ideas and IDOLS, words derived from the language of the Greeks, in which eido means "to see." They are also called PHANTASMS, which in the same language means "apparitions." And it is from these images that one of the faculties of man's nature is called the imagination. From all this it is plain that there neither is, nor can be, any image of a thing that is invisible.

It is equally evident that there can be no image of a thing that is infinite. All the images and phantasms made by the impression of visible things have a shape; but shape is a quantity bounded in every direction. Therefore there can be no image of God, nor of the soul of man, nor of spirits — only of visible bodies, that is, bodies that have light in themselves or are lit by bodies that do.

Fictions; Material Images

A man can, of course, imagine shapes he never saw — making up a figure out of the parts of various creatures, as the poets make their centaurs, chimeras, and other monsters never seen. And he can give matter to those shapes too, and make them in wood, clay, or metal. These are also called images — not for their resemblance to any corporeal thing, but for their resemblance to some phantastical inhabitant of the brain of their maker. But between these idols as they exist originally in the brain, and as they are painted, carved, molded, or cast in matter, there is a likeness of the one to the other; and for that likeness, the material body made by art may be said to be the image of the phantastical idol made by nature.

But in a broader use of the word, "image" also includes any representation of one thing by another. So an earthly sovereign may be called the image of God, and an inferior magistrate the image of an earthly sovereign. And in the idolatry of the pagans, there was often little regard for whether the material idol resembled the idol in their imagination — yet it was still called its image. An unhewn stone has been set up for Neptune, along with various other shapes far different from the shapes men conceived of their gods. And at this day we see many images of the Virgin Mary and other saints, unlike one another and corresponding to no one man's imagination, that nevertheless serve well enough for the purpose they were set up for — which was no more than to represent, by their names alone, the persons mentioned in the history; to which every man applies a mental image of his own making, or none at all. And so an image, in the largest sense, is either the resemblance, or the representation, of something visible — or both together, as it most often happens.

But Scripture stretches the name of idol even further. It covers the sun, or a star, or any other creature, visible or invisible, whenever that creature is worshipped as a god.

What Idolatry Is

Having shown what worship is, and what an image is, I will now put the two together and examine what that IDOLATRY is which the Second Commandment, and other places of Scripture, forbid.

To worship an image is voluntarily to perform those external acts which are signs of honoring one of three things: the material of the image — the wood, stone, or metal, or some other visible creature; or the phantasm in the brain that the material was shaped and figured to resemble; or both together, as one living body composed of the matter and the phantasm, the way a man is composed of body and soul.

Now compare that with civil honor. To remove your hat before a man of power and authority, or before the throne of a prince, or in whatever other places he appoints for that purpose in his absence, is to worship that man or prince with civil worship. It is a sign of honoring not the chair, and not the place, but the person — and that is not idolatry. But if the man who does it supposed the soul of the prince to be in the chair, or presented a petition to the chair, that would be divine worship, and idolatry.

To pray to a king for things he is actually able to do for us — even if we prostrate ourselves before him — is only civil worship, because we acknowledge no power in him beyond the human. But voluntarily to pray to him for fair weather, or for anything only God can do, is divine worship, and idolatry.

On the other side, if a king compels a man to such worship by the terror of death or other severe bodily punishment, it is not idolatry. The worship that a sovereign commands to be paid to himself through the terror of his laws is not a sign that the man obeying inwardly honors him as a god. It is a sign that the man wants to save himself from death, or from a miserable life. And what is no sign of internal honor is no worship — and therefore no idolatry. Nor can it be said that such a man scandalizes his brother, or lays a stumbling block in his path. However wise or learned the man worshipping in that manner may be, no one else can conclude from his behavior that he approves of it — only that he does it out of fear, and that it is not his act, but the act of the sovereign.

To worship God in some particular place, or turning your face toward an image or a fixed spot, is not to worship or honor the place or the image. It is to acknowledge it as holy — that is, to acknowledge that the image or place has been set apart from common use. That is all the word holy means here. It implies no new quality in the place or image, only a new relation: appropriation to God. And therefore it is not idolatry — no more than it was idolatry to worship God before the bronze serpent; or for the Jews, when they were out of their own country, to turn their faces toward the Temple of Jerusalem when they prayed; or for Moses to take off his shoes before the burning bush on the ground belonging to Mount Sinai. That place was the one God had chosen to appear in and to give his laws to the people of Israel, and so it was holy ground — not by any inherent sanctity, but by being set apart to God's use. Just so, it is no idolatry for Christians to worship in churches once solemnly dedicated to God for that purpose by the authority of the king, or whoever else truly represents the Church.

But to worship God as animating or inhabiting such an image or place — that is, to worship an infinite substance in a finite place — is idolatry. Such finite gods are mere idols of the brain, nothing real, and Scripture commonly calls them by their right names: vanity, and lies, and nothing. It is also idolatry to worship God — not as present in the place or image, but simply to be reminded of him or of some of his works — in a place or before an image dedicated or set up by private authority, without the authority of those who are our sovereign pastors. For the Commandment is: "You shall not make to yourself any graven image." God commanded Moses to set up the bronze serpent; Moses did not make it to himself; so it was not against the Commandment. But the making of the golden calf by Aaron and the people, being done without any authority from God, was idolatry — not only because they held it for God, but also because they made it for a religious use without warrant either from God their sovereign, or from Moses, who was his lieutenant.

The pagans of the past worshipped as gods Jupiter and the rest — who in life were perhaps men who had done great and glorious deeds. And they worshipped as children of God various men and women, supposing them to have been begotten between an immortal deity and a mortal human. This was idolatry, because they made these gods for themselves, with no authority from God — neither in his eternal law of reason, nor in his positive and revealed will. But though our Savior was a man, whom we also believe to be God immortal and the Son of God, this is no idolatry; because we build that belief not on our own fancy or judgment, but on the Word of God revealed in the Scriptures.

As for the adoration of the Eucharist: if the words of Christ, "This is my body," really mean that he himself, and the seeming bread in his hand — and not only that, but every seeming morsel of bread that has ever since been or shall ever hereafter be consecrated by priests — are so many bodies of Christ, and yet all of them only one body, then that is no idolatry, because our Savior authorized it. But if the text does not mean that — and no other text can be brought forward for it — then, because it is a worship of human institution, it is idolatry. It is not enough to say that God can transubstantiate the bread into Christ's body. The pagans also held God to be omnipotent, and on that ground they could just as easily have excused their idolatry by claiming the transubstantiation of their wood and stone into God Almighty.

There are also those who claim divine inspiration is a supernatural entering of the Holy Spirit into a man, rather than an acquisition of God's grace through doctrine and study. I think those people are caught in a very dangerous dilemma. If they do not worship the men they believe to be so inspired, they fall into impiety — failing to adore God's supernatural presence. But if they do worship them, they commit idolatry — for the Apostles would never permit themselves to be worshipped like that. The safest course, therefore, is to believe that the descending of the dove upon the Apostles, and Christ's breathing on them when he gave them the Holy Spirit, and the giving of it by laying on of hands, were the signs God was pleased to use, or ordain to be used, of his promise to assist those men in their study to preach his kingdom, and in their conduct of life — so that it might not be scandalous, but edifying to others.

Scandalous Worship of Images

Besides the idolatrous worship of images, there is also a scandalous worship of them — which is also a sin, but not idolatry. Idolatry is worship by signs of an internal and real honor. Scandalous worship is only seeming worship. It may sometimes go along with an inward, hearty detestation both of the image and of the imaginary demon or idol it is dedicated to, and proceed only from the fear of death or other grievous punishment. Even so, it is a sin in those who worship this way, if they are men whose actions others look to as guiding lights. For those who follow their example cannot help but stumble and fall in the way of religion. Whereas the example of people we pay no attention to does not work on us at all; it leaves us to our own diligence and caution, and so is no cause of our falling.

If, therefore, a pastor lawfully called to teach and direct others — or anyone else whose knowledge is held in high regard — pays external honor to an idol out of fear, then unless he makes his fear and his unwillingness as evident as the worship itself, he scandalizes his brother by seeming to approve idolatry. His brother, reasoning from the action of his teacher, or of a man whose knowledge he respects, concludes that the thing is lawful in itself. That scandal is sin, and a scandal given.

But if someone who is no pastor, and has no eminent reputation for knowledge of Christian doctrine, does the same thing, and another follows him — that is no scandal given, for the follower had no reason to follow such an example. It is only a pretense of scandal, which he takes up himself as an excuse before men. For an unlearned man in the power of an idolatrous king or state, commanded on pain of death to worship before an idol: if he detests the idol in his heart, he does well — though if he had the fortitude to suffer death rather than worship it, he would do better. But if a pastor — who, as Christ's messenger, has undertaken to teach Christ's doctrine to all nations — should do the same, it would be not only a sinful scandal with respect to other Christians' consciences, but a treacherous desertion of his post.

Here, then, is the sum of everything I have said so far about the worship of images. Whoever worships, in an image or in any creature, either the matter of it, or some fancy of his own which he thinks dwells in it, or both together — or believes that such things hear his prayers or see his devotions, without ears or eyes — commits idolatry. Whoever counterfeits such worship out of fear of punishment, if he is a man whose example carries weight among his brethren, commits a sin. But whoever worships the Creator of the world before such an image, or in such a place, as he has not made or chosen for himself, but has taken from the commandment of God's Word — as the Jews did in worshipping God before the cherubim, and before the bronze serpent for a time, and in or toward the Temple of Jerusalem, which was also only for a time — commits no idolatry.

Now, as for the worship of saints, and images, and relics, and the other things practiced today in the Church of Rome, I say this: they are not allowed by the Word of God, and they were not brought into the Church of Rome by the doctrine taught there. They were partly left in it at the first conversion of the pagans — and afterwards countenanced, confirmed, and enlarged by the Bishops of Rome.

The Argument from the Cherubim and the Bronze Serpent, Answered

As for the proofs alleged out of Scripture — those examples of images that God himself appointed to be set up — they were not set up for the people, or for anyone, to worship. They were set up so that the people should worship God himself before them: before the cherubim over the Ark, and before the bronze serpent. We never read that the priest, or anyone else, worshipped the cherubim. On the contrary, we read (2 Kings 18:4) that Hezekiah broke the bronze serpent that Moses had set up into pieces — because the people were burning incense to it.

Besides, those examples were not given for our imitation, as if we too should set up images under the pretense of worshipping God before them. The words of the second Commandment — "You shall not make to yourself any graven image" — draw a line between the images God commanded to be set up and those we set up for ourselves. And therefore the argument from the cherubim or the bronze serpent to images of man's devising — from the worship commanded by God to the will-worship of men — is no good argument at all.

Consider this too: just as Hezekiah broke the bronze serpent in pieces because the Jews worshipped it, so that they would do so no more, so also Christian sovereigns ought to break down the images their subjects have grown accustomed to worshipping, so there is no further occasion for such idolatry. For at this day, where images are worshipped, the ignorant people really do believe there is a divine power in the images. Their pastors tell them that some of these images have spoken, and have bled, and that miracles have been done by them — which the people understand as done by the saint, who they think either is the image itself or is inside it. The Israelites, when they worshipped the calf, thought they were worshipping the God who brought them out of Egypt; and yet it was idolatry, because they thought the calf either was that God or had him in its belly.

Some may think it impossible for people to be so stupid as to take the image for God, or for a saint, or to worship it under that notion. But Scripture plainly says otherwise. When the golden calf was made, the people said (Exodus 32:2), "These are your gods, O Israel." And the images of Laban (Genesis 31:30) are called his gods. And we see by daily experience, in all sorts of people, that men who study nothing but their food and their ease are content to believe any absurdity rather than trouble themselves to examine it. They hold their faith the way a family holds an entailed estate — unalienable, except by an express and new law.

Painting What You Imagine Is Not Idolatry — Worshipping It Is

But they infer from certain other passages that it is lawful to paint angels, and even God himself: from God's walking in the garden, from Jacob's seeing God at the top of the ladder, and from other visions and dreams. But visions and dreams, whether natural or supernatural, are only phantasms. The man who paints an image of any of them is making an image not of God, but of his own phantasm — which is the making of an idol. I do not say that drawing a picture from your imagination is a sin. But once it is drawn, to hold it up as a representation of God is against the second Commandment, and can serve no purpose but worship.

The same goes for images of angels, and of the dead — unless they stand as monuments of friends, or of men worth remembering. That use of an image is not worship of the image, but a civil honoring of the person — not the person who is, but the person who was. But when it is done to the image we make of a saint, for no other reason than that we think he hears our prayers and is pleased with the honor we pay him — when he is dead and without sense — then we attribute to him more than human power, and that is idolatry.

So then: there is no authority, in the Law of Moses or in the Gospel, for the religious worship of images or other representations of God which men set up for themselves, nor for the worship of the image of any creature in heaven, or earth, or under the earth. And Christian kings — who are God's living representatives — are not to be worshipped by their subjects with any act signifying a greater esteem of their power than mortal human nature can carry. Given all that, it cannot be imagined that the religious worship now in use was brought into the Church through a misunderstanding of Scripture. The only remaining explanation is that it was left in the Church — because, when the pagans who worshipped the images were converted, the images themselves were never destroyed.

How Idolatry Was Left in the Church

And here is how that happened. The images were held in immoderate esteem and carried high prices as works of craftsmanship. So their owners — though converted from worshipping them religiously, as they once had, for demons — kept them in their houses anyway, on the pretense of doing it in honor of Christ, of the Virgin Mary, of the Apostles, and of the other pastors of the primitive Church. It was easy: just give them new names. What had perhaps been called the image of Venus and Cupid became an image of the Virgin Mary and her Son our Savior. A Jupiter became a Barnabas; a Mercury, a Paul; and so on. And as worldly ambition crept by degrees into the pastors, it drew them to try to please these newly made Christians — and also to develop a liking for this kind of honor themselves, which they could hope to receive after their own deaths, just like those who had already gained it. So the worshipping of the images of Christ and his Apostles grew more and more idolatrous. True, somewhat after the time of Constantine, various emperors and bishops and general councils noticed the unlawfulness of it, and opposed it — but too late, or too weakly.

The Canonization of Saints

The canonizing of saints is another relic of pagan religion. It is neither a misunderstanding of Scripture nor a new invention of the Roman Church, but a custom as ancient as the commonwealth of Rome itself. The first man ever canonized at Rome was Romulus — and that on the story of Julius Proculus, who swore before the Senate that he had spoken with Romulus after his death, and had it from his own mouth that he dwelt in heaven, was called there Quirinius, and would look kindly on the fortunes of their new city. On the strength of that, the Senate gave public testimony of his sanctity. Julius Caesar, and other emperors after him, received the same testimony — that is, they were canonized as saints. For that is what canonization, now properly defined, amounts to: it is the same thing as the apotheosis — the god-making — of the heathen.

The Name Pontifex

It is also from the Roman heathen that the Popes have received the name and power of PONTIFEX MAXIMUS. This was the title of the man who, in the ancient commonwealth of Rome, held the supreme authority — under the Senate and people — for regulating all ceremonies and doctrines concerning their religion. And when Augustus Caesar turned the state into a monarchy, he took to himself no more than this office and that of tribune of the people — which is to say, the supreme power in both state and religion. The succeeding emperors enjoyed the same.

But in the days of the Emperor Constantine — the first emperor to profess and authorize the Christian religion — it suited his profession to have religion regulated, under his authority, by the Bishop of Rome. Not that the bishops appear to have taken the name of Pontifex that early; rather, the later bishops assumed it on their own, to dress up the power they were exercising over the bishops of the Roman provinces. For it was never any privilege of St. Peter that gave them such authority over other bishops. It was the privilege of the city of Rome, which the emperors were always willing to uphold. That is plain from this: the Bishop of Constantinople, when the emperor made that city the seat of the empire, claimed to be equal to the Bishop of Rome. In the end — not without a fight — the Pope carried the point and became the Pontifex Maximus. But he held it only by right of the emperor; and not beyond the bounds of the empire; and nowhere at all after the emperor had lost his power in Rome — even though it was the Pope himself who took that power from him. From this we may observe, in passing, that there is no ground for the Pope's superiority over other bishops, except in territories where he is himself the civil sovereign, or where an emperor holding sovereign civil power has expressly chosen the Pope as chief pastor under himself over his Christian subjects.

Processions of Images

The carrying of images about in procession is another relic of the religion of the Greeks and Romans. They too carried their idols from place to place in a kind of chariot specially dedicated to that use, which the Latins called the thensa, the vehiculum deorum — the carriage of the gods. The image rode in a frame or shrine called the ferculum. And what they called the pompa is exactly what is now called a procession. Accordingly, among the divine honors the Senate granted to Julius Caesar was this: that in the pompa — the procession — at the Circaean games, he should have a thensa and ferculum, a sacred chariot and a shrine; which was as much as to be carried up and down as a god. Just as the Popes, at this day, are carried about by their Swiss Guards under a canopy.

Wax Candles and Lighted Torches

Part of those processions, too, was the bearing of burning torches and candles before the images of the gods, among both the Greeks and the Romans. Later, the emperors of Rome received the same honor. We read of Caligula that at his reception to the empire he was carried from Misenum to Rome through a throng of people, the roads lined with altars, beasts for sacrifice, and burning torches. And of Caracalla, that he was received into Alexandria with incense, with the scattering of flowers, and with dadouchiai — that is, with torches; for the dadouchoi were the men who, among the Greeks, carried lighted torches in the processions of their gods. In the course of time, the devout but ignorant people began honoring their bishops with the same pomp of wax candles, and paying it constantly to the images of our Savior and the saints in the church itself. And that is how wax candles came into use — a use afterwards established by some of the ancient councils.

The heathens also had their aqua lustralis — that is to say, holy water. The Church of Rome imitates them in its holy days as well. They had their Bacchanalia, and we have our wakes, which answer to them. They had their Saturnalia, and we our carnivals and the Shrove Tuesday liberty of servants. They had their procession of Priapus; we our fetching in, setting up, and dancing around maypoles — and dancing is one kind of worship. They had their procession called the Ambarvalia; and we our procession around the fields in Rogation Week.

Nor do I think these are all the ceremonies left in the Church from the first conversion of the pagans. They are only all that I can presently call to mind. If a man were to look carefully at what the histories tell us about the religious rites of the Greeks and Romans, I have no doubt he would find many more of these old empty bottles of paganism — bottles which the doctors of the Roman Church, whether by negligence or by ambition, have filled up again with the new wine of Christianity, which will not fail, in time, to break them.

Chapter 46: Darkness from Vain Philosophy and Fabulous Traditions

What Philosophy Is

By PHILOSOPHY is understood the knowledge acquired by reasoning — reasoning from the way a thing is generated to its properties, or from its properties to some possible way of generating it — with the aim of being able to produce, as far as matter and human power permit, the effects that human life requires. So the geometrician, by reasoning from the construction of figures, discovers many of their properties; and from those properties, new ways of constructing them — all so that he can measure land and water, and for countless other uses. So the astronomer, from the rising, setting, and movement of the sun and stars in different parts of the heavens, discovers the causes of day and night and of the different seasons of the year, and by this keeps an account of time. And the same goes for the other sciences.

Prudence Is Not Philosophy

From this definition it is evident that we should not count as any part of philosophy that original knowledge called experience, which is what prudence consists of. Prudence is not attained by reasoning. It is found in brute beasts as well as in man, and it is nothing but a memory of how events have followed one another in the past — where leaving out any little circumstance that changes the outcome defeats the expectation of even the most prudent. Whereas right reasoning produces nothing but general, eternal, and unchanging truth.

False Doctrine Is Not Philosophy

Nor should we give the name philosophy to any false conclusions. For whoever reasons rightly, in words he understands, can never conclude an error.

Neither Is Supernatural Revelation

Nor to what any man knows by supernatural revelation — because that is not acquired by reasoning.

Nor Learning Taken on the Authority of Books

Nor to what is gotten by reasoning from the authority of books — because that is not reasoning from cause to effect, nor from effect to cause; and it is not knowledge, but faith.

The Beginnings and Progress of Philosophy

Since the faculty of reasoning follows from the use of speech, it could not be otherwise: some general truths must have been found out by reasoning almost as long ago as language itself. The indigenous peoples of the Americas, as Hobbes' century knew of them, are not without some good moral sayings; they also have a little arithmetic, enough to add and divide numbers that are not too large. But that does not make them philosophers. Think of it this way. There were scattered plants of grain and grapevine growing in small quantities in the fields and woods before men knew their value, or used them for food, or planted them apart in fields and vineyards — and in that time men fed on acorns and drank water. In the same way, there have been various true, general, and profitable speculations from the beginning, since they are the natural plants of human reason. But at first they were few. Men lived on crude experience. There was no method — that is, no sowing or planting of knowledge by itself, set apart from the weeds and common plants of error and guesswork. And the cause of this was the lack of leisure: men were too busy getting the necessities of life and defending themselves against their neighbors. Until great commonwealths were erected, it could not be otherwise.

Leisure is the mother of philosophy, and commonwealth is the mother of peace and leisure. Where there were first great and flourishing cities, there was first the study of philosophy. The Gymnosophists of India, the Magi of Persia, and the priests of Chaldea and Egypt are counted the most ancient philosophers — and those countries were the most ancient kingdoms. Philosophy had not yet risen among the Greeks and the other peoples of the West, whose commonwealths — no bigger, perhaps, than Lucca or Geneva — never had peace except when their fears of one another were evenly matched, and never had the leisure to observe anything but each other. At length, when war had united many of these smaller Greek cities into fewer and greater ones, then seven men from various parts of Greece began to get the reputation of being wise — the Seven Sages of Greece — some of them for moral and political sayings, others for the learning of the Chaldeans and Egyptians, which was astronomy and geometry. But we still hear nothing of any schools of philosophy.

The Schools of Philosophy in Athens

After the Athenians, by defeating the Persian armies, had won dominion of the sea — and with it all the islands and coastal cities of the Aegean, in Asia as well as Europe — and had grown wealthy, the men who had no employment, at home or abroad, had little else to do with themselves but (as St. Luke says, Acts 17:21) "telling and hearing news," or holding forth on philosophy in public to the young men of the city. Every master took some spot for the purpose. Plato taught in certain public walks called the Academia, after one Academus. Aristotle taught in the walk of the temple of Pan, called the Lyceum. Others taught in the Stoa — a covered walkway where the merchants' goods were brought ashore. Others taught in other places, where they spent their leisure time teaching or disputing their opinions — and some in any place at all where they could gather the youth of the city to hear them talk. This is exactly what Carneades did at Rome, when he was there as an ambassador — which led Cato to advise the Senate to send him home quickly, for fear of corrupting the morals of the young men who delighted in hearing him say what they took to be fine things.

This is how the place where any of them taught and disputed came to be called schola, which in their language means leisure, and their disputations diatribae — that is to say, passing the time. The philosophers themselves got the names of their sects, in some cases, from their schools. The followers of Plato's doctrine were called Academics; the followers of Aristotle, Peripatetics, from the walk where he taught; and those Zeno taught, Stoics, from the Stoa. It is as if we were to name men after Moorfields, after St. Paul's church, and after the Exchange, because they often meet there to chatter and loiter.

Nevertheless, men were so taken with this custom that in time it spread over all of Europe and the best part of Africa, so that schools were publicly erected and maintained for lectures and disputations in almost every commonwealth.

The Schools of the Jews

There were also schools, in ancient times, both before and after the time of our Savior, among the Jews — but they were schools of their Law. For though they were called synagogues, that is to say, congregations of the people, yet since the Law was read, expounded, and disputed in them every Sabbath, they differed from public schools in name only, not in nature. And they existed not only in Jerusalem but in every Gentile city where the Jews lived. There was such a school at Damascus, which Paul entered to persecute. There were others at Antioch, Iconium, and Thessalonica, which he entered to dispute in. And such was the synagogue of the Libertines, Cyrenians, Alexandrians, Cilicians, and those of Asia — that is to say, the school of the Libertines and of Jews who were foreigners in Jerusalem. It was members of this school who disputed with St. Stephen.

The Greek Schools Were Useless

But what good have those schools ever done? What science do we possess today that was acquired by their readings and disputings? The geometry we have — which is the mother of all natural science — we do not owe to the schools. Plato, the best philosopher of the Greeks, forbade entrance into his school to anyone who was not already something of a geometrician. Many men studied that science to the great advantage of mankind; but there is no mention of their schools, there was never any sect of geometricians, and they did not pass under the name of philosophers.

The natural philosophy of those schools was rather a dream than science, and it was set forth in senseless and insignificant language — which cannot be avoided by anyone who tries to teach philosophy without first attaining real knowledge of geometry. For nature works by motion, and the ways and degrees of motion cannot be known without knowing the proportions and properties of lines and figures.

Their moral philosophy is nothing but a description of their own passions.
