The rule of conduct, where there is no civil government, is the law of nature; and where there is civil government, it is the civil law — that is what determines what is honest and dishonest, just and unjust, and in general what is good and evil. But these philosophers made the rules of good and bad according to their own likes and dislikes. And with so great a diversity of taste, the result is that nothing is generally agreed on; everyone does (as far as he dares) whatever seems good in his own eyes — to the subversion of the commonwealth.

Their logic, which should be the method of reasoning, is nothing but word-traps: tricks invented to stump anyone who tries to pin them down.

To sum up: there is nothing so absurd that some of the old philosophers have not maintained it — as Cicero says, and he was one of them. And I believe that scarcely anything can be more absurdly said in natural philosophy than what is now called Aristotle's Metaphysics; nor anything more repugnant to government than much of what he said in his Politics; nor anything more ignorant than a great part of his Ethics.

The Jewish Schools Were Useless Too

The school of the Jews was originally a school of the Law of Moses, who commanded (Deuteronomy 31:10) that at the end of every seventh year, at the Feast of Tabernacles, the Law should be read to all the people, so that they might hear it and learn it. So the reading of the Law every Sabbath (a practice in use after the Captivity) ought to have had no purpose but acquainting the people with the commandments they were to obey, and expounding to them the writings of the prophets. But it is plain, from our Savior's many rebukes of them, that they corrupted the text of the Law with their false commentaries and vain traditions, and understood the prophets so little that they recognized neither Christ nor the works he did — the very works the prophets had prophesied. So by their lectures and disputations in their synagogues, they turned the doctrine of their Law into a fantastical kind of philosophy about the incomprehensible nature of God and of spirits. They cooked this up out of the vain philosophy and theology of the Greeks, mixed with their own fancies drawn from the obscurer passages of Scripture — the ones most easily twisted to their purpose — and from the fabulous traditions of their ancestors.

What a University Is

What is now called a university is a joining together — an incorporation under one government — of many public schools in one and the same town or city. In a university, the principal schools were established for the three professions: Roman religion, Roman law, and the art of medicine. As for the study of philosophy, it has no place there except as a handmaid to the Roman religion. And since the authority of Aristotle is the only currency accepted there, that study is not properly philosophy (whose nature does not depend on authors) but Aristotelity. As for geometry, until very recently it had no place at all — since it serves nothing but rigid truth. And if any man, by the ingenuity of his own nature, attained any degree of perfection in it, he was commonly thought a magician, and his art diabolical.

Errors Brought into Religion from Aristotle's Metaphysics

Now let us descend to the particular tenets of vain philosophy that were handed down to the universities, and from there into the Church — derived partly from Aristotle, partly from sheer blindness of understanding. I shall first consider their principles.

There is a certain philosophia prima — first philosophy — on which all other philosophy ought to depend. It consists principally in correctly limiting the meanings of the most universal names of all. These limitations serve to avoid ambiguity and equivocation in reasoning, and are commonly called definitions: definitions of body, time, place, matter, form, essence, subject, substance, accident, power, act, finite, infinite, quantity, quality, motion, action, passion, and various others necessary for explaining a man's conceptions about the nature and generation of bodies.

The explication — that is, the settling of the meaning — of these and similar terms is commonly called, in the universities, metaphysics, this being part of the philosophy of Aristotle that bears that title. But the title means something different there. In Aristotle it signifies only "the books written, or placed, after his natural philosophy." The Scholastics, however, take them for books of supernatural philosophy — for the word "metaphysics" will bear both senses. And in truth, what is written there is for the most part so far beyond any possibility of being understood, and so repugnant to natural reason, that whoever thinks there is anything in it to be understood must necessarily conclude that it is supernatural.

Errors About Abstract Essences

From this metaphysics, mixed with Scripture to make what they call School divinity, we are told that there exist in the world certain essences separated from bodies, which they call "abstract essences" and "substantial forms." To interpret this jargon, more than ordinary attention is needed here. I also beg the pardon of those who are not used to this kind of discussion, because here I am addressing those who are.

The world — and I mean not just the earth, which gives the lovers of it the name "worldly men," but the universe, the whole mass of all things that exist — is corporeal. That is to say: body. It has the dimensions of magnitude, namely length, breadth, and depth. Every part of body is likewise body, and has the same dimensions. Consequently, every part of the universe is body, and whatever is not body is no part of the universe. And because the universe is everything, whatever is no part of it is nothing — and consequently, nowhere.

It does not follow from this that spirits are nothing. Spirits have dimensions, and are therefore really bodies — even though, in common speech, the name "body" is given only to bodies that are visible or touchable, that is, bodies with some degree of opacity. As for spirits, men call them incorporeal — which is a name of greater honor, and may therefore with more piety be attributed to God himself. For when we speak of God, we are not considering which attribute best expresses his nature, which is incomprehensible, but which best expresses our desire to honor him.

Now, to know on what grounds they say there are "abstract essences" or "substantial forms," we must consider what those words properly signify.

The use of words is to register our thoughts and conceptions to ourselves, and to make them known to others. Of these words, some are names of the things conceived — like the names of all sorts of bodies that work on the senses and leave an impression in the imagination. Others are names of the imaginations themselves — that is, of the ideas or mental images we have of all the things we see or remember. Others again are names of names, or of different kinds of speech: "universal," "plural," "singular," "negation," "true," "false," "syllogism," "interrogation," "promise," "covenant" — these are names of certain forms of speech. And still others serve to show the connection, or the contradiction, between one name and another. When someone says "a man is a body," he means that the name "body" necessarily follows from the name "man," since they are simply different names of the same thing — man. And that connection is signified by coupling the two names together with the word "is."

As we use the verb "is," so the Latins used their verb est, and the Greeks their esti through all its forms. Whether all the other nations of the world have a word answering to it in their various languages, I cannot say — but I am sure they have no need of one. Simply placing two names side by side could serve to signify their connection, if that were the custom (for custom is what gives words their force), just as well as the words "is," or "be," or "are," and the like.

And suppose there were a language with no verb answering to est, or "is," or "be." The men who used it would be not one bit less capable of inferring, concluding, and every kind of reasoning than the Greeks and Latins were. But then what would become of these terms — "entity," "essence," "essential," "essentially" — which are derived from that verb, along with the many more that depend on them, applied the way they most commonly are? They are not names of things at all. They are signs by which we make known that we conceive one name or attribute as following from another. When we say "a man is a living body," we do not mean that the man is one thing, the living body another, and the "is" or "being" a third. We mean that the man and the living body are the same thing — because the inference "if he is a man, he is a living body" is a true inference, and that is what the word "is" signifies. Therefore "to be a body," "to walk," "to be speaking," "to live," "to see," and similar infinitives — and likewise "corporeity," "walking," "speaking," "life," "sight," and the like, which signify just the same — are the names of nothing. I have explained this more fully elsewhere.

But someone may ask: what is the point of such fine-grained analysis in a work like this, where I claim to deal with nothing but what is necessary to the doctrine of government and obedience? The point is this: that men may no longer let themselves be abused by those who, using this doctrine of "separated essences" built on the vain philosophy of Aristotle, would frighten them away from obeying the laws of their country with empty names — the way men frighten birds from the corn with an empty coat, a hat, and a crooked stick.

For it is on this ground that they say, when a man is dead and buried, that his soul (that is, his life) can walk around separated from his body, and is seen by night among the graves. On the same ground they say that the shape, color, and taste of a piece of bread have a being in a place where, they tell us, there is no bread. And on the same ground they say that faith, and wisdom, and other virtues are sometimes "poured" into a man, sometimes "blown" into him from heaven — as if the virtuous and their virtues could exist apart — along with a great many other things that serve to weaken the dependence of subjects on the sovereign power of their country. For who will make any effort to obey the laws, if he expects obedience to be poured or blown into him? Who will not obey a priest who can make God, rather than obey his sovereign — rather, indeed, than obey God himself? And who that is afraid of ghosts will not show great respect to those who can make the holy water that drives the ghosts away?

Let this serve as one example of the errors brought into the Church from the "entities" and "essences" of Aristotle. It may be that Aristotle himself knew it was false philosophy, but wrote it as something agreeable to, and supportive of, the religion of his time — fearing the fate of Socrates.

Once fallen into this error of "separated essences," they are necessarily entangled in many other absurdities that follow from it. Since they insist these forms are real, they are obliged to assign them some place. But because they hold them to be incorporeal, without any dimension of quantity — and everyone knows that place is dimension, and can be filled only by what is corporeal — they are driven to prop up their credibility with a distinction: the forms are not anywhere "circumscriptively," but "definitively." These terms are mere words, and in this case insignificant; they pass current only in Latin, so that their emptiness can be concealed. For the circumscription of a thing is nothing other than the determination — the defining — of its place; both terms of the distinction mean exactly the same thing. And regarding the essence of a man in particular, which they say is his soul, they affirm that all of it is in his little finger, and all of it in every other part of his body, however small — and yet there is no more soul in the whole body than in any one of those parts. Can any man think God is served by such absurdities? And yet all of this must be believed by anyone who will believe in the existence of an incorporeal soul separated from the body.

And when they are asked to explain how an incorporeal substance can be capable of pain, and be tormented in the fire of hell or purgatory, they have no answer at all — except that it cannot be known how fire can burn souls.

Again: motion is change of place, and incorporeal substances are not capable of occupying any place. So the Scholastics are hard pressed to make it seem possible how a soul can leave here and go — without its body — to Heaven, Hell, or Purgatory; and how the ghosts of men (and, I may add, the ghosts of the clothes they appear in) can walk by night in churches, churchyards, and other burial places. I do not know what they can answer to this, unless they want to say that ghosts walk "definitively, not circumscriptively," or "spiritually, not temporally." Such magnificent distinctions are equally applicable to any difficulty whatsoever — which is exactly what is wrong with them.

The Nunc-Stans

Then there is the meaning of eternity. They refuse to let eternity be an endless succession of time, because then they could not explain how God's will and his preordaining of things to come would not have to exist before his foreknowledge of those same things — the way an efficient cause comes before its effect, or an agent before its action — nor could they defend many of their other bold opinions about the incomprehensible nature of God. So instead, they teach us that eternity is the standing still of the present time, a nunc-stans — "a standing now," as the Schools call it. Neither they nor anyone else understands this, any more than anyone would understand a hic-stans — "a standing here" — for an infinite greatness of place.

One Body in Many Places, and Many Bodies in One Place at Once

When we divide a body in our thoughts, we do it by counting its parts; and in counting the parts, we also count the parts of the place that body filled. We cannot make many parts without making just as many places for those parts. No mind on earth can conceive of more parts, or fewer parts, than there are places for them. Yet they would have us believe that, by the almighty power of God, one body can be in many places at one and the same time, and many bodies in one place at one and the same time — as if it were a tribute to divine power to say that what is, is not; or that what has been, has not been.

And these are only a small sample of the absurdities they are forced into by insisting on disputing philosophically about God, instead of admiring and adoring his divine and incomprehensible nature — whose attributes cannot tell us what he is, but ought to express our desire to honor him with the best names we can think of. Those who venture to reason about God's nature from these attributes of honor lose their understanding at the very first step, and then tumble from one blunder into another, without end and without number. They are like a man ignorant of the ceremonies of court who comes into the presence of someone far greater than he is used to addressing: he stumbles at the entrance; to keep from falling, he lets slip his cloak; to recover his cloak, he drops his hat; and with one disorder after another, he reveals his bewilderment and his bad breeding.

Absurdities in Natural Philosophy, Such as Gravity as the Cause of Heaviness

Then there is their physics — the knowledge of the subordinate, secondary causes of natural events. Here they offer no causes at all, only empty words.

If you want to know why some kinds of bodies sink naturally down toward the earth while others naturally rise away from it, the Schools will tell you, out of Aristotle, that the bodies that sink downward are heavy, and that this heaviness is what causes them to descend. But if you ask what they mean by heaviness, they will define it as an endeavor to go to the center of the earth. So the cause of things sinking downward is — an endeavor to be below. Which is as much as to say that bodies descend, or ascend, because they do.

Or they will tell you that the center of the earth is the place of rest and preservation for heavy things, and that is why heavy things endeavor to be there. As if stones and metals had desires; or could discern the place they want to be in, the way a man does; or loved rest, which man does not; or as if a pane of glass were less safe sitting in the window than falling into the street.

Quantity Poured into a Body Already Made

If we want to know why the same body seems greater at one time than another, without anything being added to it, they say: when it seems less, it is condensed; when greater, rarefied. And what are "condensed" and "rarefied"? Condensed is when there is less quantity in the very same matter than before; rarefied, when there is more. As if there could be matter without some determinate quantity — when quantity is nothing but the determination of matter, that is, of body: the measure by which we say one body is greater or less than another, by this much or that much. As if a body could be made without any quantity at all, and then more or less quantity poured into it afterward, depending on how dense the body is intended to be.

The Pouring In of Souls

For the cause of the soul of man, they say: Creatur infundendo, and Creando infunditur — that is, "It is created by being poured in," and "poured in by being created." That is the entire explanation.

The Ubiquity of Apparitions

For the cause of sense, they offer a ubiquity of "species" — that is, of the shows or apparitions of objects: which, when they are apparitions to the eye, is sight; when to the ear, hearing; to the palate, taste; to the nostril, smelling; and to the rest of the body, feeling.

The Will as the Cause of Willing

For the cause of the will to do any particular action — which is called volitio — they assign the faculty, that is, the general capacity that men have to will sometimes one thing and sometimes another, which is called voluntas. In other words, they make the power the cause of the act. That is like naming, as the cause of men's good or evil deeds, their ability to do them.

Ignorance Dressed Up as an Occult Cause

And on many occasions they put forward, as the cause of natural events, their own ignorance — disguised in other words. As when they say that fortune is the cause of contingent things; that is, of things whose cause they do not know. Or when they attribute many effects to "occult qualities" — that is, qualities unknown to them, and therefore (so they think) unknown to everyone else too. Or to "sympathy," "antipathy," "antiperistasis," "specifical qualities," and other such terms — which signify neither the agent that produces the effect, nor the operation by which it is produced.

If metaphysics and physics like this are not vain philosophy, then there never was any, and St. Paul need not have warned us to avoid it.

One Maker for the Things, Another for Their Incongruity

As for their moral and civil philosophy, it has the same absurdities, or greater. If a man commits an act of injustice — that is, an action contrary to the law — they say God is the prime cause of the law, and also the prime cause of that action and all other actions, but no cause at all of the injustice, which is the failure of the action to conform to the law. This is vain philosophy. One might as well say that one man makes both a straight line and a crooked one, and a different man makes their failure to match. Such is the philosophy of all men who settle on their conclusions before they know their premises; who pretend to comprehend what is incomprehensible; and who turn attributes of honor into attributes of nature — as this particular distinction was invented to prop up the doctrine of free will, that is, of a human will not subject to the will of God.

Private Appetite as the Measure of Public Good

Aristotle and other heathen philosophers define good and evil by human appetite — and fairly enough, so long as we consider each man governed by no law but his own. In the condition of men who have no law but their own appetites, there can be no general rule of good and evil actions. But in a commonwealth this measure is false. It is not the appetite of private men, but the law — which is the will and appetite of the state — that is the measure.

And yet this doctrine is still practiced. Men judge the goodness or wickedness of their own actions, and of other men's actions, and of the actions of the commonwealth itself, by their own passions; and no man calls anything good or evil except what is so in his own eyes, without any regard at all to the public laws — except only monks and friars, who are bound by vow to that simple obedience to their superior which every subject ought to consider himself bound to render to the civil sovereign by the law of nature. This private measure of good is a doctrine that is not merely vain, but pernicious to the public state.

And That Lawful Marriage Is Unchastity

It is also vain and false philosophy to say that the work of marriage is repugnant to chastity or continence, and so to make them moral vices — as they do who plead chastity and continence as the ground for denying marriage to the clergy. For they admit it is nothing more than a regulation of the Church, requiring that those in holy orders who continually attend the altar and administer the Eucharist practice continual abstinence from women, under the name of continual chastity, continence, and purity. By this they call the lawful use of wives a lack of chastity and continence, and so make marriage a sin — or at least a thing so impure and unclean that it renders a man unfit for the altar. But follow the logic. If the law was made because the use of wives is incontinence and contrary to chastity, then all marriage is vice. If it was made because marriage is too impure and unclean for a man consecrated to God, then other natural, necessary, daily functions that all men perform should disqualify men from the priesthood far more, since they are more unclean.

But the secret foundation of this prohibition of priests' marriage was probably not laid so carelessly as on such errors in moral philosophy. Nor was it laid on the preference of the single life over the married state — a preference that came from the wisdom of St. Paul, who saw how inconvenient it was for the preachers of the Gospel in those times of persecution, forced to flee from one country to another, to be weighed down with the care of a wife and children. No: it was laid on the design of the popes and priests of later times to make themselves "the clergy" — that is, the sole heirs of the kingdom of God in this world. And for that purpose it was necessary to take the use of marriage away from them; because our Savior says that at the coming of his kingdom the children of God shall "neither marry, nor be given in marriage, but shall be as the angels in heaven" — that is to say, spiritual. Having taken on themselves the name of spiritual men, to have allowed themselves wives (when there was no need) would have been an incongruity.

And That All Government Except Popular Government Is Tyranny

From Aristotle's civil philosophy they have learned to call every kind of commonwealth except the popular kind (which was the state of Athens in his day) by the name of tyranny. All kings they called tyrants; and the aristocracy of the Thirty Governors, set up in Athens by the Spartans who had conquered them, they called the Thirty Tyrants. Likewise they learned to call the condition of the people under a democracy liberty.

Now, a tyrant originally meant nothing more than a monarch, plain and simple. But after that kind of government was abolished in most parts of Greece, the name came to signify not only what it had meant before, but along with it the hatred the popular states bore toward monarchy — just as the name of king became odious in Rome after the kings were deposed. It is natural to all men to assume that some great fault is signified by any label that is hurled in spite at a great enemy. And when these same men become displeased with those who administer a democracy or an aristocracy, they do not have to hunt for disgraceful names to vent their anger: they readily call the one anarchy and the other oligarchy, the tyranny of a few.

And what is it that actually offends the people? Nothing but this: that they are governed not as each one of them would govern himself, but as the public representative — whether one man or an assembly of men — thinks fit. That is, by an arbitrary government. For this they give evil names to their superiors, never knowing (until perhaps a little while after a civil war) that without such arbitrary government, war must be perpetual; and that it is men and arms, not words and promises, that make the force and power of the laws.

That Not Men, but Law Governs

And therefore this is another error of Aristotle's politics: that in a well-ordered commonwealth, not men but the laws should govern. What man with his natural senses intact — even if he can neither read nor write — does not find himself governed by those he fears, those he believes can kill or hurt him if he disobeys? Who believes that the law itself can hurt him — that is, words and paper, without the hands and swords of men? This error belongs on the list of pernicious ones, for it leads men, whenever they dislike their governors, to attach themselves to those who call the governors tyrants, and to think it lawful to raise war against them. And yet this doctrine is many times nurtured from the pulpit, by the clergy.

Laws Over the Conscience

There is another error in their civil philosophy, and this one they never learned from Aristotle, nor Cicero, nor any other of the heathens: to extend the power of the law — which is the rule of actions only — to the very thoughts and consciences of men, by examination and inquisition into what they hold in their hearts, even when their speech and actions conform. By this practice, men are either punished for answering with the truth of their thoughts, or forced to answer with a lie for fear of punishment.

It is true that a civil magistrate, intending to employ a minister as a teacher, may ask him whether he is willing to preach such and such doctrines, and may deny him the job if he refuses. But to force a man to accuse himself of opinions, when his actions are not forbidden by law, is against the law of nature. And it is especially monstrous in those who teach that a man will be damned to eternal and extreme torments if he dies holding a false opinion about an article of the Christian faith. For who is there who, knowing there is such great danger in an error, and whom the natural care of himself compels, would not rather hazard his soul on his own judgment than on the judgment of some other man who has no stake whatsoever in his damnation?

Private Interpretation of Law

For a private man, without the authority of the commonwealth — that is, without permission from its representative — to interpret the law by his own spirit, is another error in politics; but this one is not drawn from Aristotle, nor from any other heathen philosopher. None of them denied that the power of making laws includes the power of explaining them when there is need. And are not the Scriptures, in every place where they are law, made law by the authority of the commonwealth — and consequently a part of the civil law?

It is an error of the same kind when anyone but the sovereign restrains in any man a power that the commonwealth has not restrained — as they do who claim the preaching of the Gospel as the exclusive property of one particular order of men, in places where the laws have left it free. If the state gives me leave to preach or teach — that is, if it does not forbid me — then no man can forbid me. Suppose I find myself among the idolaters of America. Shall I, a Christian, though not ordained, think it a sin to preach Jesus Christ until I have received orders from Rome? And once I have preached, shall I not answer their doubts, and expound the Scriptures to them — that is, shall I not teach? Some may say that in such a case, as also for administering the sacraments to them, the necessity is to be counted a sufficient commission — which is true. But this is also true: wherever a dispensation would be due because of necessity, no dispensation is needed at all when there is no law that forbids the thing. Therefore to deny these functions to those whom the civil sovereign has not denied them is to take away a lawful liberty — which is contrary to the doctrine of civil government.

The Language of the School-Divines

More examples of vain philosophy brought into religion by the doctors of School-divinity could be produced, but other men may observe them for themselves if they please. I shall only add this: the writings of the School-divines are, for the most part, nothing but meaningless trains of strange and barbarous words — or words used otherwise than in the ordinary use of the Latin tongue — such as would stump Cicero, and Varro, and all the grammarians of ancient Rome. If anyone wants this proved, let him (as I have said once before) try whether he can translate any School-divine into any of the modern tongues — French, English, or any other language rich in words. For whatever cannot be made intelligible in most of these languages is not intelligible in the Latin either. This meaninglessness of language I cannot quite mark down as false philosophy; but it has a quality of its own: it not only hides the truth, but makes men think they already have it — and so they stop searching.

Errors from Tradition

Lastly, consider the errors brought in from false or uncertain history. What is the whole legend of fictitious miracles in the lives of the saints; and all the histories of apparitions and ghosts alleged by the doctors of the Roman Church to shore up their doctrines of hell and purgatory, the power of exorcism, and other doctrines that have no warrant in either reason or Scripture; and all those traditions they call the unwritten word of God — what is all this, but old wives' fables?

It is true that scraps of such things can be found scattered in the writings of the ancient Fathers. But those Fathers were men, and men who might too easily believe false reports. Producing their opinions as testimony to the truth of what they believed carries no force with anyone who — following the counsel of St. John (1 John 4:1) — examines the spirits. On the contrary: in everything that concerns the power of the Roman Church (whose abuse they either did not suspect, or profited by), their testimony is discredited by their too-hasty belief in reports. The most sincere men, when they lack great knowledge of natural causes (as the Fathers did), are usually the most prone to such credulity; for it is natural that the best men are the least suspicious of fraudulent purposes. Pope Gregory and St. Bernard have something about apparitions of ghosts who said they were in purgatory; and so does our own Bede. But nowhere, I believe, except by report from others. And if they, or anyone else, should relate such stories as matters of their own firsthand knowledge, they would not thereby make such vain reports any more credible; they would only expose their own weakness, or their own fraud.

The Suppression of Reason

To the introduction of false philosophy we may add the suppression of true philosophy — by men who, having neither lawful authority nor sufficient study, are not competent judges of the truth. Our own voyages of navigation make it plain, and all men learned in the human sciences now acknowledge, that there are antipodes — people standing on the opposite side of the globe. And every day it appears more and more clearly that the years and the days are determined by the motions of the earth. Nevertheless, men who have done no more in their writings than suppose such doctrine, as an occasion to lay out the reasons for and against it, have been punished for it by ecclesiastical authority.

And what reason is there for that? Is it because such opinions are contrary to true religion? That cannot be, if they are true. Let the truth first be examined by competent judges, or refuted by those who claim to know the contrary. Is it because they are contrary to the established religion? Then let them be silenced by the laws of those to whom their teachers are subject — that is, by the civil laws; for disobedience may lawfully be punished even in those who teach true philosophy against the law. Is it because they tend toward disorder in government, by encouraging rebellion or sedition? Then let them be silenced, and their teachers punished, by the power of the one to whom the care of the public peace is committed — which is the civil authority. For whatever power churchmen take upon themselves in their own right, in any place where they are subject to the state — though they call it God's right — is nothing but usurpation.

Chapter 47: Of the Benefit That Comes from This Darkness, and Who Collects It

Whoever Benefits from a Deed Is Presumed to Be Its Author

Cicero makes honorable mention of one of the Cassii, a stern judge among the Romans, for a custom he kept in criminal cases. When the testimony of the witnesses was not sufficient, he would ask the accusers: Cui bono? — who benefits? What profit, honor, or other satisfaction did the accused gain, or expect to gain, from the deed? For among all presumptions, none points to the author of an action so plainly as the benefit that comes of it.

By that same rule I intend, in this place, to examine who it can be that has kept the people of this part of Christendom possessed for so long by these doctrines — doctrines contrary to the peaceful societies of mankind.

The Church of Rome First Taught That the Church on Earth Is the Kingdom of God

Begin with the first error: that the present church, still militant here on earth, is the kingdom of God — meaning the kingdom of glory, the Promised Land itself, and not the kingdom of grace, which is only a promise of the land. To this error are attached the following worldly benefits.

First, the pastors and teachers of the church are entitled by it, as God's public ministers, to a right of governing the church — and consequently, since the church and the commonwealth are the same persons, to be rectors and governors of the commonwealth. It was by this title that the Pope prevailed upon the subjects of all Christian princes to believe that to disobey him was to disobey Christ himself, and, in every quarrel between him and other princes, to abandon their lawful sovereigns — charmed by the words "spiritual power." Which is, in effect, a universal monarchy over all Christendom.

For the popes were first invested with the right of being supreme teachers of Christian doctrine by Christian emperors, and under them, within the limits of the Roman Empire — as they themselves acknowledge — under the title of Pontifex Maximus, the chief priest of Rome, an officer subject to the civil state. But after the empire was divided and dissolved, it was not hard to press upon a people already subject to them another title: the right of St. Peter — not only to keep their pretended power intact, but to extend it over those same Christian provinces, even though they were no longer united in the empire of Rome.

This benefit of a universal monarchy — considering how much men desire to rule — is presumption enough that the popes who claimed it, and who enjoyed it for a long time, were the authors of the doctrine by which it was obtained: namely, that the church now on earth is the kingdom of Christ. For grant that, and it must follow that Christ has some lieutenant among us, through whom we are to be told what his commandments are.

After certain churches renounced this universal power of the Pope, you would reasonably expect the civil sovereigns in all those churches to have recovered the portion of it that had been their own right, in their own hands, all along — before they so carelessly let it go. And in England that is, in effect, what happened — except that the men through whom the kings administered the government of religion, by insisting that they held their employment by God's own right, seemed to claim, if not supremacy, then at least independence from the civil power. And they only seemed to claim it, since they acknowledged the King's right to strip them of the exercise of their functions whenever he pleased.

And the Presbyterians Kept Teaching It

But in those places where the presbytery took over that office, many other doctrines of the Church of Rome were indeed forbidden to be taught — yet this one doctrine, that the kingdom of Christ has already come, and that it began at the resurrection of our Savior, was retained. But cui bono? What profit did they expect from it? The same profit the popes expected: sovereign power over the people.

For what does it mean for men to excommunicate their lawful king, if not to bar him from every place of God's public service in his own kingdom — and to resist him with force when he tries with force to correct them? And what does it mean to excommunicate any person without authority from the civil sovereign, if not to take away his lawful liberty — that is, to usurp an unlawful power over their brethren? The authors of this darkness in religion, therefore, are the Roman and the Presbyterian clergy.

Infallibility

Under this same head I place all the doctrines that serve them to keep possession of this spiritual sovereignty once it has been gotten. First, that the Pope, in his public capacity, cannot err. For who is there that believes this to be true, and will not readily obey him in whatever he commands?

The Subjection of Bishops

Secondly, that all other bishops, in whatever commonwealth they live, hold their right neither immediately from God nor mediately from their civil sovereigns, but from the Pope. This is a doctrine by which every Christian commonwealth comes to contain many powerful men — for that is what bishops are — who depend on the Pope and owe obedience to him, even though he is a foreign prince. By this means he is able, as he has done many times, to raise a civil war against any state that will not submit to be governed according to his pleasure and interest.

Exemption of the Clergy

Thirdly, the exemption of these bishops, and of all other priests, and of all monks and friars, from the power of the civil laws. By this means a great part of every commonwealth enjoys the benefit of the laws and the protection of the civil state, yet pays no share of the public expense, and is not liable, as other subjects are, to the penalties due for its crimes. Consequently these men stand in fear of no one but the Pope, and adhere to him alone, to uphold his universal monarchy.

The Names "Sacerdotes" and "Sacrifices"

Fourthly, giving their priests the name of sacerdotes — that is, sacrificers — when in the New Testament they are nothing more than presbyters, that is, elders. "Sacrificer" was the title of the civil sovereign and his public ministers among the Jews, in the days when God was their king. Likewise, making the Lord's Supper a sacrifice serves to make the people believe the Pope has the same power over all Christians that Moses and Aaron had over the Jews — that is to say, all power, both civil and ecclesiastical, just as the High Priest had then.

Making Marriage a Sacrament

Fifthly, teaching that matrimony is a sacrament gives the clergy the judging of which marriages are lawful — and thereby of which children are legitimate — and consequently of the right of succession to hereditary kingdoms.

The Celibacy of Priests

Sixthly, the denial of marriage to priests serves to secure this power of the Pope over kings. For if a king is a priest, he cannot marry and pass his kingdom to his posterity. And if he is not a priest, then the Pope claims this ecclesiastical authority over him, and over his people.

Auricular Confession

Seventhly, from auricular confession — confession whispered privately into the priest's ear — they obtain, for the security of their power, better intelligence of the designs of princes and great persons in the civil state than those princes can ever have of the designs of the ecclesiastical state.

Canonizing Saints and Declaring Martyrs

Eighthly, by the canonization of saints, and by declaring who are martyrs, they secure their power by leading simple men into obstinacy against the laws and commands of their civil sovereigns — obstinacy even to the death — whenever the Pope's excommunication declares those sovereigns heretics or enemies of the church; that is, as they interpret it, enemies of the Pope.

Transubstantiation, Penance, Absolution

Ninthly, they secure the same power by what they ascribe to every priest: the power of making Christ; the power of ordaining penance; and the power of remitting and retaining sins.

Purgatory, Indulgences, External Works

Tenthly, by the doctrine of purgatory, of justification by external works, and of indulgences, the clergy is enriched.

Demonology and Exorcism

Eleventhly, by their demonology, and the use of exorcism, and the other things belonging to it, they keep the people — or think they keep them — in greater awe of their power.

Scholastic Theology

Lastly, the metaphysics, ethics, and politics of Aristotle, and the frivolous distinctions, barbarous terms, and obscure language of the Scholastics, taught in the universities — all of which were erected and regulated by the Pope's authority — serve them to keep these errors from being detected, and to make men mistake the ignis fatuus — the fool's fire, the will-o'-the-wisp — of vain philosophy for the light of the Gospel.

Who the Authors of Spiritual Darkness Are

If these did not suffice, other dark doctrines of theirs could be added, whose profit plainly flows toward the same ends: toward setting up an unlawful power over the lawful sovereigns of Christian people; or toward sustaining that power once it is set up; or toward the worldly riches, honor, and authority of those who sustain it. And therefore, by the rule of cui bono stated above, we may justly pronounce the authors of all this spiritual darkness: the Pope, and the Roman clergy, and all those besides who labor to settle in the minds of men this erroneous doctrine — that the church now on earth is the kingdom of God spoken of in the Old and New Testament.

But the emperors, and the other Christian sovereigns, under whose government these errors and the like encroachments of churchmen upon their office first crept in — to the disturbance of their possessions and of the peace of their subjects — are not blameless either. Though they suffered it through lack of foresight of the consequences, and lack of insight into the designs of their teachers, they may nevertheless be counted accessories to their own damage, and to the public's. For without their authority, no seditious doctrine could have been publicly preached in the first place. I say they could have stopped it at the beginning.

But once the people were possessed by those spiritual men, no human remedy remained that anyone could invent. As for the remedies God provides — and he never fails, in his good time, to destroy all the machinations of men against the truth — for those we must wait upon his good pleasure. Many times he allows the prosperity of his enemies, together with their ambition, to grow to such a height that its very violence opens the eyes that the caution of their predecessors had sealed shut, and makes men, by grasping too much, lose their hold on everything — as Peter's net was broken by the struggling of too great a multitude of fish. Whereas the impatience of those who tried to resist such encroachment before their subjects' eyes were opened only increased the power they were resisting.

I do not, therefore, blame the Emperor Frederick for holding the stirrup for our countryman Pope Adrian. Such was the disposition of his subjects at the time that if he had not done it, he was not likely to have succeeded in the empire. But I do blame those who, in the beginning, when their power was still whole, allowed such doctrines to be forged in the universities of their own dominions — and so held the stirrup for all the succeeding popes, while they mounted into the thrones of all Christian sovereigns, to ride and tire both them and their people at their pleasure.

But as the inventions of men are woven, so also they are unraveled: the path is the same, but the order is reversed. The web begins at the first elements of power — wisdom, humility, sincerity, and the other virtues of the apostles, whom the people they converted obeyed out of reverence, not obligation. Their consciences were free, and their words and actions were subject to none but the civil power.

Afterwards, as the flocks of Christ increased, the presbyters assembled to consider what they should teach; and by binding themselves to teach nothing against the decrees of their assemblies, they made it be thought that the people were bound to follow their doctrine too — and when anyone refused, they refused to keep him company. That was what excommunication then meant: the man was shunned not as an infidel, but as disobedient. And this was the first knot upon Christian liberty.

Then, as the number of presbyters grew, the presbyters of the chief city or province got themselves authority over the parish presbyters, and appropriated to themselves the name of bishops. And this was the second knot on Christian liberty.

Lastly, the Bishop of Rome, on the strength of the imperial city, took upon himself an authority over all the other bishops of the empire — partly by the will of the emperors themselves and by the title of Pontifex Maximus, and at last, when the emperors had grown weak, by the privileges of St. Peter. And this was the third and last knot, and the whole synthesis and construction of the pontifical power.

And therefore the analysis — the untying — proceeds by the same path, but beginning with the knot that was tied last. We can watch it happen in the dissolution of the praeterpolitical church government in England — the church government that stood outside, and above, the civil order.

First, the power of the popes was dissolved totally by Queen Elizabeth; and the bishops, who before had exercised their functions in the right of the Pope, afterwards exercised them in the right of the Queen and her successors — though by keeping the phrase jure divino — "by divine right" — they were thought to claim them by immediate right from God. And so the first knot was untied: the one that had been tied last.

After this, the Presbyterians in England lately obtained the putting down of episcopacy. And so the second knot was dissolved.

And almost at the same time, the power was taken from the Presbyterians too. And so we are reduced to the independency of the primitive Christians: to follow Paul, or Cephas, or Apollos, every man as he likes best. Which — if it comes without contention, and without measuring the doctrine of Christ by our affection for the person of his minister (the fault the Apostle rebuked in the Corinthians) — is perhaps the best arrangement of all. First, because there ought to be no power over the consciences of men but the power of the Word itself, working faith in every one of us — not always according to the purpose of those who plant and water, but according to the purpose of God himself, who gives the increase. And secondly, because it is unreasonable for those who teach that there is danger in every little error to demand that a man endowed with reason of his own follow the reason of some other man, or the majority vote of many other men — which is little better than staking his salvation on a game of cross and pile: a coin toss.

Nor should those teachers be displeased at this loss of their ancient authority. For no one should know better than they that power is preserved by the same virtues by which it is acquired — by wisdom, humility, clearness of doctrine, and sincerity of life. Not by suppressing the natural sciences and the morality of natural reason. Not by obscure language. Not by claiming more knowledge than they let appear. Not by pious frauds. Nor by the other faults that, in the pastors of God's church, are not merely faults but scandals — stumbling blocks apt to make men trip, one time or another, into throwing off their authority altogether.

Comparing the Papacy with the Kingdom of Fairies

But after this doctrine — "that the church now militant is the kingdom of God spoken of in the Old and New Testament" — was received in the world, the ambition and campaigning for the offices that belong to it, and especially for that great office of being Christ's lieutenant, and the pomp of those who obtained the principal public charges in it, became by degrees so obvious that men lost the inward reverence due to the pastoral function. So much so that the wisest men, among those who had any power in the civil state, needed nothing but the authority of their princes to refuse them any further obedience.

For from the time the Bishop of Rome got himself acknowledged as universal bishop, by pretense of succession to St. Peter, their whole hierarchy — their kingdom of darkness — may be compared, not unfittingly, to the kingdom of fairies: that is, to the old wives' tales of England about ghosts and spirits, and the tricks they play in the night. And if a man considers the origin of this great ecclesiastical dominion, he will easily perceive that the Papacy is no other than the ghost of the deceased Roman Empire, sitting crowned upon the grave thereof. For so the Papacy started up, all of a sudden, out of the ruins of that heathen power.

The language they use, too, both in their churches and in their public acts, is Latin — which is not the common speech of any nation now in the world. What is it but the ghost of the old Roman language?

The fairies, in whatever nation they keep company, have but one universal king, whom some of our poets call King Oberon — but whom Scripture calls Beelzebub, prince of demons. The churchmen likewise, in whatever dominions they are found, acknowledge but one universal king: the Pope.

The churchmen are spiritual men and ghostly fathers. The fairies are spirits and ghosts. Fairies and ghosts inhabit darkness, solitudes, and graves. The churchmen walk in obscurity of doctrine, in monasteries, churches, and churchyards.

The churchmen have their cathedral churches, which, in whatever town they are erected, have the power — by virtue of holy water, and certain charms called exorcisms — to make those towns cities, that is to say, seats of empire. The fairies also have their enchanted castles, and certain gigantic ghosts that lord it over all the regions round about them.

The fairies cannot be seized and brought to answer for the harm they do. So also the churchmen vanish away from the tribunals of civil justice.

The churchmen take from young men the use of reason, by certain charms compounded of metaphysics, and miracles, and traditions, and abused Scripture — after which they are good for nothing else but to execute what the churchmen command them. The fairies likewise are said to take young children out of their cradles and change them into natural fools — which the common people therefore call elves, and which are prone to mischief.

In what shop or workshop the fairies make their enchantments, the old wives have not determined. But the workshops of the clergy are well enough known: they are the universities, which received their discipline from papal authority.

When the fairies are displeased with somebody, they are said to send their elves to pinch him. The churchmen, when they are displeased with any civil state, likewise make their elves — that is, their superstitious, enchanted subjects — pinch their princes, by preaching sedition; or they use one prince, enchanted with promises, to pinch another.

The fairies do not marry; but there are among them incubi, which nevertheless couple with flesh and blood. The priests also do not marry.

The churchmen skim the cream of the land, by the donations of ignorant men who stand in awe of them, and by tithes. So also it is in the fable of the fairies, that they slip into the dairies and feast upon the cream, which they skim from the milk.

What kind of money is current in the kingdom of fairies, the story does not record. But the churchmen, in what they take in, accept the same money we do — though when they have any payment to make, they pay it in canonizations, indulgences, and masses.

To these and other such resemblances between the Papacy and the kingdom of fairies, one more may be added: that just as the fairies have no existence except in the fancies of ignorant people, rising out of the traditions of old wives or old poets, so the spiritual power of the Pope — outside the bounds of his own civil dominion — consists only in the fear that seduced people stand in of his excommunication, upon hearing false miracles, false traditions, and false interpretations of Scripture.

It was not, therefore, a very difficult matter for Henry VIII to cast them out by his exorcism, nor for Queen Elizabeth by hers. But who knows whether this spirit of Rome — now gone out, and walking by missions through the dry places of China, Japan, and the Indies, which yield him little fruit — may not return? Or rather, whether an assembly of spirits worse than he may not enter and inhabit this clean-swept house, and make the end of it worse than the beginning? For the Roman clergy are not the only ones who claim that the kingdom of God is of this world, and who claim, on that ground, a power in it distinct from the power of the civil state.

And this is all I had a design to say concerning the doctrine of POLITICS. When I have reviewed it, I shall willingly expose it to the censure of my country.

A Review and Conclusion

Some people have argued, from the way certain natural faculties of the mind work against each other — and certain passions against other passions — that no one man could ever be fit for every kind of civil duty. Severity of judgment, they say, makes men harsh critics, slow to pardon the errors and weaknesses of others. Quickness of imagination, on the other side, makes the thoughts less steady than they need to be to tell right from wrong exactly. Again, in every deliberation and every courtroom argument, solid reasoning is necessary — without it, men's decisions are rash and their verdicts unjust. And yet without powerful eloquence to win attention and consent, reason accomplishes almost nothing. But these two are contrary faculties: reasoning is grounded on principles of truth, while eloquence is grounded on opinions already received, true or false, and on the passions and interests of men, which are different and always shifting.

And among the passions, courage — by which I mean contempt for wounds and violent death — inclines men toward private revenge, and sometimes toward unsettling the public peace; while timidity often disposes them to desert the public defense. Both of these, they say, cannot live in the same person.

And when you consider how much men's opinions and manners differ in general, it is impossible, they say, to keep up a steady civil friendship with everyone the business of the world forces us to deal with — a business that consists in almost nothing else but a perpetual contest for honor, riches, and authority.

To this I answer: these are indeed great difficulties, but not impossibilities. By education and discipline they can be reconciled, and sometimes are. Judgment and imagination can both have a place in the same man — but by turns, as the goal he is aiming at requires. Just as the Israelites in Egypt were sometimes tied to their labor of making bricks, and at other times ranged abroad to gather straw, so the judgment can sometimes be fixed on one particular question, and the imagination at another time go wandering about the world. So too reason and eloquence can stand very well together — perhaps not in the natural sciences, but certainly in the moral ones. For wherever there is room to dress up and promote error, there is far more room to dress up and promote truth, if you have truth to dress. Nor is there any contradiction between fearing the laws and not fearing a public enemy; nor between refraining from injury yourself and pardoning it in others. So there is no such incompatibility between human nature and civil duties as some people think. I have known clearness of judgment and largeness of imagination, strength of reason and graceful speech, courage for war and fear of the laws, all eminently combined in one man — and that was my most noble and honored friend, Mr. Sidney Godolphin: who, hating no man and hated by none, was unfortunately killed at the start of the late civil war, in the public quarrel, by an undiscerned and undiscerning hand.

To the laws of nature set out in Chapter 15, I would have this one added: that every man is bound by nature, as far as in him lies, to protect in war the authority by which he is himself protected in time of peace. For a man who claims a right of nature to preserve his own body cannot claim a right of nature to destroy the very power by whose strength he is preserved. That is a plain contradiction of himself. He is obliged to protect his protector. And though this law can be deduced from some of those already listed there, the times require that it be drummed in and remembered.

And because I find from various English books lately printed that the civil wars have not yet sufficiently taught men at what point in time a subject becomes obliged to a conqueror — nor what conquest is, nor how it comes about that conquest obliges men to obey the conqueror's laws — I will say it plainly, for their further satisfaction. The point in time at which a man becomes the subject of a conqueror is the point at which, having the liberty to submit to him, he consents — either by express words or by some other sufficient sign — to be his subject. As for when a man has the liberty to submit, I showed that already at the end of Chapter 21: for a man who has no obligation to his former sovereign beyond that of an ordinary subject, it is when the means of his life lie within the guards and garrisons of the enemy. For at that point he no longer has protection from his old sovereign, but is protected by the opposing side in exchange for his contribution. The end of obedience is protection. And since such contribution is everywhere treated as lawful, as an unavoidable thing — even though it is assistance to the enemy — total submission, which is nothing more than assistance to the enemy, cannot be treated as unlawful. Besides, consider this: those who submit assist the enemy with only part of their estates, while those who refuse assist him with the whole of theirs. So there is no reason to call their submission, or their settlement with the conqueror, assistance to the enemy. It is rather a loss to him.

But if a man, on top of his obligation as a subject, has taken on the new obligation of a soldier, then he does not have the liberty to submit to a new power as long as the old one keeps the field and provides him the means of subsistence, either in its armies or in its garrisons. In that case he cannot complain of lacking protection and the means to live as a soldier. But when that fails too, a soldier also may seek his protection wherever he has the most hope of finding it, and may lawfully submit himself to his new master. So much for the time when he may do it lawfully, if he chooses. And if he does do it, he is without question bound to be a true subject: for a contract lawfully made cannot lawfully be broken.

From this a man can also understand when people may be said to be conquered, and what the nature of conquest and the right of a conqueror consist in — for this act of submission contains them all. Conquest is not the victory itself, but the acquisition, by victory, of a right over the persons of men. The man who is killed is overcome, but not conquered. The man who is captured and put in prison or in chains is not conquered, though he is overcome — for he is still an enemy, and may save himself if he can. But the man who, on a promise of obedience, is allowed his life and liberty — he is then conquered, and a subject, and not before. The Romans used to say that their general had "pacified" such-and-such a province — that is to say, in plain English, conquered it — and that the country was pacified by victory when its people had promised imperata facere — to do what the Roman people commanded them. That was what it meant to be conquered. But this promise may be either express or tacit: express, by an actual promise; tacit, by other signs. For example, a man who has never been called on to make an express promise (perhaps because his power is not considerable enough to matter) is nevertheless understood to submit himself to the government if he lives openly under its protection. But if he lives there secretly, he is liable to anything that may be done to a spy and enemy of the state. I do not say he commits any injustice — acts of open hostility do not bear that name — but I do say he may justly be put to death. Likewise, if a man is out of his country when it is conquered, he is not conquered, and not a subject; but if on his return he submits to the government, he is bound to obey it. So conquest, to define it, is the acquiring of the right of sovereignty by victory. And that right is acquired in the people's submission, by which they contract with the victor, promising obedience in exchange for life and liberty.

In Chapter 29 I set down, as one of the causes of the dissolution of commonwealths, their imperfect generation — their lacking an absolute and unconditional legislative power, for want of which the civil sovereign is forced to handle the sword of justice waveringly, as if it were too hot to hold. One reason for this, which I did not mention there, is that sovereigns will all insist on justifying the war by which their power was first gotten, as if their right depended on the goodness of that old cause and not on their present possession. As if, for example, the right of the kings of England depended on the goodness of William the Conqueror's cause, and on their lineal and most direct descent from him — by which standard there would perhaps be no tie of obedience binding any subject to any sovereign anywhere in the world today. In needlessly trying to justify themselves this way, they justify every successful rebellion that ambition shall ever raise against them and their successors. Therefore I set this down as one of the most effective seeds of the death of any state: that conquerors demand not only a submission of men's actions for the future, but also an approval of all their own actions past — when there is scarcely a commonwealth in the world whose beginnings can in good conscience be justified.

And because the name of tyranny signifies nothing more and nothing less than the name of sovereignty — whether in one man or many — except that those who use the word "tyranny" are understood to be angry at the men they call tyrants, I think that tolerating a professed hatred of tyranny is tolerating a hatred of commonwealth in general, and is another evil seed, not much different from the first. For to justify the cause of a conqueror, men generally find it necessary to vilify the cause of the conquered — but neither the one nor the other is necessary for the obligation of the conquered. And this much I have thought fit to say on review of the first and second parts of this discourse.

In Chapter 35 I showed sufficiently from Scripture that in the commonwealth of the Jews, God himself was made the sovereign by pact with the people — who were therefore called his peculiar people, to distinguish them from the rest of the world, over whom God reigned not by their consent but by his own power — and that in this kingdom Moses was God's lieutenant on earth, and it was he who told them what laws God had appointed. But I did not then deal with who was to carry out those laws, especially in capital punishments — not thinking it then a matter requiring as much consideration as I have since found it does. We know that in commonwealths generally, the execution of corporal punishments was either assigned to the guards or other soldiers of the sovereign power, or given to men in whom poverty, contempt for honor, and hardness of heart combined to make them apply for such an office. But among the Israelites it was a positive law of God their sovereign that whoever was convicted of a capital crime should be stoned to death by the people — and that the witnesses should cast the first stone, and after the witnesses, the rest of the people. This was a law that designated who the executioners were to be. It did not allow anyone to throw a stone before conviction and sentence, where the congregation was the judge. The witnesses still had to be heard before the execution proceeded, unless the deed had been committed in the presence of the congregation itself, or in the sight of the lawful judges — for then no other witnesses were needed but the judges themselves. Nevertheless, because this procedure has not been thoroughly understood, it has given occasion to a dangerous opinion: that any man may kill another, in some cases, by a "right of zeal" — as if the executions done upon offenders in the kingdom of God in ancient times proceeded not from the sovereign's command, but from the authority of private zeal. If we look at the very texts that seem to favor this idea, they say quite the contrary.

First, when the Levites fell upon the people who had made and worshipped the golden calf, and killed three thousand of them, it was by the commandment of Moses, from the mouth of God — as is plain in (Exodus 32:27). And when the son of an Israelite woman blasphemed God, those who heard it did not kill him, but brought him before Moses, who put him in custody until God should give sentence against him — as appears in (Leviticus 24:11-12). Again, in (Numbers 25:6-7), when Phinehas killed Zimri and Cozbi, it was not by right of private zeal. Their crime was committed in the sight of the whole assembly, so no witness was needed; the law was known; Phinehas was heir apparent to the sovereignty; and — the principal point — the lawfulness of his act depended entirely on a subsequent ratification by Moses, of which he had no cause to doubt. And this presumption of a future ratification is sometimes necessary to the safety of a commonwealth: in a sudden rebellion, any man who can suppress it by his own power, in the place where it begins, may lawfully do so, and arrange to have his act ratified or pardoned while it is being done, or after it is done. Also, (Numbers 35:30) says expressly, "Whosoever shall kill the murderer shall kill him upon the word of witnesses" — but witnesses presuppose a formal court, and so this text condemns the whole pretense of jus zelotarum — the supposed right of zealots. The law of Moses concerning a man who entices others to idolatry — that is to say, in the kingdom of God, to a renouncing of their allegiance — forbids concealing him (Deuteronomy 13:8) and commands the accuser to have him put to death and to cast the first stone at him: but not to kill him before he is condemned. And in (Deuteronomy 17:4-6) the legal process against idolatry is set down exactly. God there speaks to the people as judge, and commands them, when a man is accused of idolatry, to inquire diligently into the fact; and if they find it true, then to stone him — but still the hand of the witness throws the first stone. This is not private zeal; it is public condemnation. In the same way, when a father has a rebellious son, the law (Deuteronomy 21:18) is that he shall bring him before the judges of the town, and all the people of the town shall stone him. Lastly, it was under pretense of these laws that St. Stephen was stoned — not under any pretense of private zeal: for before he was carried away to execution, he had pleaded his cause before the High Priest. There is nothing in all this, nor in any other part of the Bible, to support executions by private zeal — which, being most of the time nothing but a combination of ignorance and passion, is against both the justice and the peace of a commonwealth.

In Chapter 36 I said that it is not declared in what manner God spoke supernaturally to Moses. I did not mean that he never spoke to Moses by dreams and visions and by a supernatural voice, as he did to other prophets. The manner in which God spoke to him from the mercy-seat is set down expressly in (Numbers 7:89), in these words: "From that time forward, when Moses entered into the Tabernacle of the Congregation to speak with God, he heard a Voice which spake unto him from over the Mercy-Seat, which is over the Ark of the Testimony; from between the Cherubims he spake unto him." But what is not declared is what made the manner of God's speaking to Moses superior to his speaking to other prophets — to Samuel, say, or to Abraham, to whom he also spoke by a voice, that is, by vision — unless the difference lay in the clearness of the vision. For "face to face" and "mouth to mouth" cannot be understood literally of the infiniteness and incomprehensibility of the divine nature.

As for the doctrine of this book as a whole, I still cannot see anything wrong with it: the principles of it are true and proper, and the reasoning solid. For I ground the civil right of sovereigns, and both the duty and the liberty of subjects, on the known natural inclinations of mankind and on the articles of the law of nature — things no man ought to be ignorant of who claims even reason enough to govern his own household. And as for the ecclesiastical power of those same sovereigns, I ground it on texts that are both evident in themselves and in harmony with the aim of the whole of Scripture. And therefore I am persuaded that whoever reads this book with no other purpose than to be informed, will be informed by it. But those who by their writing, or public speaking, or prominent actions have already committed themselves to maintaining the contrary opinions will not be so easily satisfied. In such cases it is natural for men to do two things at once: to keep reading, and to lose their attention in hunting for objections to what they read before. And in a time when men's interests have changed — since much of the doctrine that serves to establish a new government must necessarily run contrary to the doctrine that helped dissolve the old — there cannot help but be very many such objections.

In the part that deals with a Christian commonwealth there are some new doctrines which, in a state where the contrary had already been fully settled, it would perhaps be a fault for a subject to publish without permission — that would be usurping the place of a teacher. But in this time, when men are calling not only for peace but also for truth, to offer such doctrines as I think true — doctrines that plainly tend toward peace and loyalty — for the consideration of those who are still deliberating, is nothing more than offering new wine to be put into new casks, so that both may be preserved together. And I suppose that when novelty can breed no trouble or disorder in a state, men are not generally so devoted to the reverence of antiquity as to prefer ancient errors over new and well-proved truth.

There is nothing I distrust more than my own style — which nevertheless I am confident is not obscure, apart from the accidents of the printing press. As for my having neglected the fashionable ornament of quoting ancient poets, orators, and philosophers, contrary to the custom of recent times — whether I have done well or badly in it — that came from my own judgment, grounded on many reasons. First, all truth of doctrine depends either on reason or on Scripture, both of which lend credibility to many writers but never receive credibility from any. Second, the matters in question here are not matters of fact but matters of right, in which there is no place for witnesses. Third, there is scarcely one of those old writers who does not sometimes contradict both himself and the others, which makes their testimony insufficient. Fourth, opinions taken purely on the credit of antiquity are not really the judgment of those who cite them, but words that pass — like yawning — from mouth to mouth. Fifth, men often have a fraudulent design when they stud their corrupt doctrine with the cloves of other men's wit. Sixth, I do not find that the ancients they cite considered it an ornament to do the same with those who wrote before them. Seventh, it is a sign of indigestion when Greek and Latin sentences come back up unchewed, as they usually do — unchanged. Lastly, though I revere those men of ancient times who either wrote the truth clearly or set us on a better path to find it out for ourselves, I think nothing is owed to antiquity itself. For if we are going to revere age, the present is the oldest age there is. And if it is the age of the writer we revere — I am not sure that the men generally given such honor were any more ancient when they wrote than I am, who am writing now. But consider it carefully, and the praise of ancient authors proceeds not from reverence for the dead, but from the competition and mutual envy of the living.

To conclude: there is nothing in this whole discourse, nor in what I wrote before on the same subject in Latin, that I can perceive to be contrary either to the Word of God or to good morals, or that tends to disturb the public tranquility. Therefore I think it may profitably be printed, and even more profitably taught in the universities — if those to whom the judgment of such things belongs agree. For since the universities are the fountains of civil and moral doctrine, from which the preachers and the gentry draw such water as they find there and sprinkle it on the people (both from the pulpit and in their conversation), there certainly ought to be great care taken to keep that water pure — pure both from the venom of heathen politicians and from the incantation of deceiving spirits. By that means most men, knowing their duties, will be less liable to serve the ambition of a few discontented persons in their schemes against the state; they will be less aggrieved by the taxes necessary for their peace and defense; and the governors themselves will have less cause to maintain, at the common expense, any larger army than is necessary to secure the public liberty against the invasions and encroachments of foreign enemies.

And so I have brought to an end my discourse of civil and ecclesiastical government, occasioned by the disorders of the present time — without partiality, without aiming at any person, and with no other design than to set before men's eyes the mutual relation between protection and obedience, of which the condition of human nature and the laws of God, both natural and positive, require an inviolable observance. And though in the upheaval of states there can be no very favorable constellation for truths of this kind to be born under — facing the angry glare of those who are dissolving an old government, and seeing only the backs of those who are erecting a new one — still I cannot think this book will be condemned at this time, either by the public judge of doctrine or by anyone who desires the continuance of the public peace. And in that hope I return to my interrupted speculation on natural bodies, in which — if God gives me the health to finish it — I hope the novelty will please as much as the novelty in this doctrine of the artificial body has tended to offend. For such truth as opposes no man's profit, nor his pleasure, is to all men welcome.
